packages.name: algebra
packages.synopsis: Package algebra is a small computer-algebra system (CAS) written entirely with the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package algebra is a small computer-algebra system (CAS) written entirely
with the Go standard library. It represents mathematical expressions as
immutable trees, manipulates them symbolically, and prints them back as
readable infix strings. It is a from-scratch, spiritual port of a tiny
subset of Python's SymPy.

# Expression model

Every expression implements the [Expr] interface. The concrete node kinds
are:

  - [Symbol]   — a named variable such as x.
  - [Integer]  — an arbitrary-precision integer (backed by math/big).
  - [Rational] — an exact fraction with denominator > 1 (backed by math/big).
  - [Float]    — an inexact float64 literal.
  - [Constant] — a named mathematical constant; [Pi], [E], the imaginary
    unit [I] and the infinities [Inf]/[NegInf] are provided.
  - internal Add, Mul and Pow nodes for sums, products and powers.
  - internal function nodes for the elementary, trigonometric, hyperbolic and
    special functions listed below.

Expressions are value-based and immutable: constructors never mutate their
arguments and the big.Int/big.Rat payloads are never modified after
construction. Structural equality is available through [Expr.Equal].

# Building expressions

Expressions can be built with the constructors [Sym], [Int], [Rat], [Flt],
[Add], [Mul], [Pow], [Sin], [Cos], [Tan], [Exp], [Log] and [Sqrt], with the
fluent builder methods [Expr.Add], [Expr.Mul] and [Expr.Pow], or by parsing
a string with [Parse]:

	x := Sym("x")
	e := x.Pow(Int(2)).Add(x.Mul(Int(2)), Int(1)) // x^2 + 2*x + 1
	e, _ = Parse("x^2 + 2*x + 1")                  // same thing

The [Add], [Mul] and [Pow] constructors are canonicalizing: they flatten
nested sums/products, fold numeric constants, apply the algebraic identities
(x+0, x*1, x*0, x^1, x^0), combine like terms, combine repeated powers and
sort arguments into a deterministic canonical order. As a result equal
expressions compare equal with [Expr.Equal] and print identically.

# Operations

The package provides [Simplify], [Expand], [Diff] (symbolic
differentiation), [Subs] (substitution), [Eval] and [Evalf] (numeric
evaluation), [Integrate] (symbolic integration of a documented subset),
[Solve] (polynomial equations) and the bonus [Factor] and [Collect] helpers
for univariate polynomials.

# Functions

Beyond the original sin/cos/tan/exp/log/sqrt, the package now provides the
reciprocal and inverse trigonometric functions ([Sec], [Csc], [Cot], [Asin],
[Acos], [Atan], [Acot], [Asec], [Acsc], [Atan2]); the hyperbolic functions
and their inverses ([Sinh], [Cosh], [Tanh], [Coth], [Sech], [Csch], [Asinh],
[Acosh], [Atanh]); and the special functions [Abs], [Sign], [Floor], [Ceil],
[Factorial], [Gamma], [Beta], [Erf] and [Erfc]. [Simplify] returns exact
values at the standard rational multiples of Pi and applies the Pythagorean
and double-angle identities.

# Complex numbers

The imaginary unit [I] participates in ordinary arithmetic (I^2 folds to -1
and clean Euler identities such as exp(I*Pi) -> -1 are recognised).
[Conjugate], [Re], [Im], [Abs] (modulus) and [Arg] decompose a complex value,
and [Evalc] evaluates any expression to a complex128.

# Calculus and solving

[Limit] computes limits (including L'Hôpital's rule and limits at infinity),
[Series] produces Taylor/Maclaurin expansions, and [Summation] and [Product]
find closed forms for polynomial, geometric and finite sums and products.
[Integrate] additionally handles the arctangent and arcsine forms, the
remaining trigonometric and hyperbolic antiderivatives, integration by parts
for polynomial×exp/sin/cos, and rational functions via partial fractions.
[Solve] handles polynomials of any degree — exact rational, quadratic (with
complex conjugate roots) and higher-degree factors, with a numeric fallback —
and [SolveSystem] solves linear systems by Gaussian elimination.

# Subpackages

Four companion packages build on the same standard-library-only philosophy:

  - matrix — symbolic and numeric linear algebra over [Expr] values:
    [matrix.Matrix] and [matrix.Vector] with exact determinant, inverse,
    reduced row echelon form, rank, adjugate, transpose, Kronecker product,
    matrix powers, characteristic polynomial and eigenvalues, plus
    [matrix.Solve] for Ax=b and the vector dot, cross and norm operations.
  - ntheory — number theory and combinatorics on int64/math/big: GCD/LCM and
    the extended Euclid, deterministic Miller–Rabin primality, prime sieves
    and factorization, Euler's totient, the Möbius function, modular
    arithmetic (modular exponentiation and inverse, CRT, the Legendre/Jacobi
    symbols, discrete logarithm), the binomial/multinomial coefficients and
    the Fibonacci, Lucas, Catalan, Bernoulli and partition sequences.
  - stats — descriptive statistics (mean, median, variance, quantiles,
    skewness, kurtosis, correlation and linear regression) and eight
    probability distributions (Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Uniform,
    Exponential, Student's t, chi-squared and Gamma) with PDF/PMF, CDF and
    moment methods.
  - physics — the SI-2019/CODATA physical constants (enumerable via
    [physics.Constants] and [physics.Lookup]), unit conversions across
    length, mass, time, temperature, energy and angle, and common formulas
    for kinematics, relativity, waves and electromagnetism.

Correctness is favoured over coverage: an operation that does not know how
to transform an expression returns it unchanged (or an explicit unevaluated
node) rather than producing a wrong answer.

vars.doc:
Inf is positive infinity (∞), used as a target for [Limit] and produced by
evaluations that diverge. NegInf is negative infinity (-∞).

vars.signature: var E = newConst("E", math.E)
vars.doc: E is Euler's number, the base of the natural logarithm.

vars.signature: var I = newConst("I", math.NaN())
vars.doc:
I is the imaginary unit, satisfying I^2 == -1. It participates in ordinary
[Add], [Mul] and [Pow] arithmetic; integer powers of I fold (I^2 -> -1) and
clean Euler identities such as exp(I*Pi) -> -1 are recognised by [Simplify].

vars.signature: var Pi = newConst("pi", math.Pi)
vars.doc: Pi is the mathematical constant pi.

types.name: Constant
types.doc: Constant is a named mathematical constant such as pi or E.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the canonical sum of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Diff
methods.doc: Diff returns the derivative of the receiver with respect to v.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether o is the same named constant.

methods.name: Expand
methods.doc: Expand distributes products over sums and expands integer powers.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the canonical product of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc: Pow returns the receiver raised to exp.

methods.name: Simplify
methods.doc: Simplify returns a canonicalized, simplified copy of the receiver.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders a named constant.

methods.name: Subs
methods.signature: func (b Constant) Subs(sym, val Expr) Expr
methods.doc: Subs substitutes every occurrence of sym with val in the receiver.

types.name: Expr
types.doc:
Expr is the interface implemented by every node in an expression tree.

Expr values are immutable and value-based. In addition to the reflective
helpers below (String, Equal), Expr carries fluent builder methods so that
expressions can be assembled left to right, e.g. x.Pow(Int(2)).Add(Int(1)).

funcs.name: Abs
funcs.doc:
Abs returns the absolute value |x|. Numeric arguments fold exactly, |I| folds
to 1, and a numeric coefficient is pulled out as |c|*Abs(rest). For a complex
number a+b*I the modulus sqrt(a^2+b^2) is returned.

funcs.name: Acos
funcs.doc:
Acos returns the inverse cosine arccos(x), folding the exact values at
0, 1/2, -1 and 1.

funcs.name: Acosh
funcs.doc: Acosh returns the inverse hyperbolic cosine arcosh(x), folding acosh(1) to 0.

funcs.name: Acot
funcs.doc:
Acot returns the inverse cotangent arccot(x), folding acot(0) to Pi/2 and
acot(1) to Pi/4.

funcs.name: Acsc
funcs.doc:
Acsc returns the inverse cosecant arccsc(x), folding acsc(1) to Pi/2 and
acsc(-1) to -Pi/2.

funcs.name: Add
funcs.doc:
Add returns the canonical sum of its arguments. It flattens nested sums,
drops zero terms, folds numeric constants, combines like terms (so that
x + x becomes 2*x) and sorts the result. Add() with no arguments is 0.

funcs.name: ApartExpr
funcs.signature: func ApartExpr(e Expr, v Expr) (Expr, error)
funcs.doc:
ApartExpr computes the partial-fraction decomposition of the rational
expression e in the symbol v and rebuilds it as an [Expr]. It splits e into
numerator and denominator, forms the corresponding polynomials with
[PolyFrom], applies [PartialFractions] and reassembles the result through
[Add], [Mul] and [Pow]. It returns an error if e is not a rational function of
v or if the decomposition cannot be carried out. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: Arg
funcs.doc:
Arg returns the argument (phase) of e in the range (-Pi, Pi]. Numeric inputs
fold to an exact angle where possible and otherwise to a [Float].

funcs.name: Asec
funcs.doc:
Asec returns the inverse secant arcsec(x), folding asec(1) to 0 and
asec(-1) to Pi.

funcs.name: Asin
funcs.doc:
Asin returns the inverse sine arcsin(x), folding the exact values at
0, ±1/2 and ±1.

funcs.name: Asinh
funcs.doc: Asinh returns the inverse hyperbolic sine arsinh(x), folding asinh(0) to 0.

funcs.name: Atan
funcs.doc:
Atan returns the inverse tangent arctan(x), folding the exact values at
0 and ±1.

funcs.name: Atan2
funcs.doc:
Atan2 returns the two-argument arctangent atan2(y, x), the angle of the point
(x, y) measured from the positive x-axis in the range (-Pi, Pi]. Numeric
arguments are folded to a [Float]; otherwise an unevaluated node is kept.

funcs.name: Atanh
funcs.doc:
Atanh returns the inverse hyperbolic tangent artanh(x), folding atanh(0)
to 0.

funcs.name: Beta
funcs.doc:
Beta returns the beta function B(a, b) = Γ(a)Γ(b)/Γ(a+b) as a symbolic node
that folds to a [Float] when both arguments are numeric.

funcs.name: Ceil
funcs.doc: Ceil returns the least integer not less than x for numeric arguments.

funcs.name: Collect
funcs.doc:
Collect rewrites e as a canonical polynomial in the symbol v, grouping like
powers of v. If e is not polynomial in v it is simplified and returned.

funcs.name: Conjugate
funcs.doc:
Conjugate returns the complex conjugate of e. For a+b*I it returns a-b*I;
free symbols are treated as real.

funcs.name: Cos
funcs.doc:
Cos returns cos(x), folding cos(0) to 1 and returning exact values at the
standard rational multiples of Pi.

funcs.name: Cosh
funcs.doc: Cosh returns the hyperbolic cosine cosh(x), folding cosh(0) to 1.

funcs.name: Cot
funcs.doc:
Cot returns cot(x) = cos(x)/sin(x), returning exact values at the standard
angles where sin is non-zero.

funcs.name: Coth
funcs.doc: Coth returns the hyperbolic cotangent coth(x) = cosh(x)/sinh(x).

funcs.name: Csc
funcs.doc:
Csc returns csc(x) = 1/sin(x), returning the exact reciprocal of sin at the
standard angles where sin is non-zero.

funcs.name: Csch
funcs.doc: Csch returns the hyperbolic cosecant csch(x) = 1/sinh(x).

funcs.name: Diff
funcs.doc:
Diff returns the symbolic derivative of e with respect to the symbol v.
It implements the sum, product, power, quotient and chain rules and the
derivatives of sin, cos, tan, exp, log and sqrt. The result is returned in
canonical (simplified) form. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: Erf
funcs.doc:
Erf returns the error function erf(x), folding erf(0) to 0 and numeric
[Float] arguments to their value.

funcs.name: Erfc
funcs.doc:
Erfc returns the complementary error function erfc(x) = 1 - erf(x), folding
erfc(0) to 1 and numeric [Float] arguments to their value.

funcs.name: Exp
funcs.doc:
Exp returns e^x, folding exp(0) to 1, recognising exp(log(u)) -> u and the
clean Euler values exp(I*Pi*k) for integer or half-integer k (for example
exp(I*Pi) -> -1 and exp(I*Pi/2) -> I).

funcs.name: Expand
funcs.doc:
Expand distributes products over sums and expands non-negative integer
powers of sums (binomial/multinomial expansion), then re-simplifies.

funcs.name: Factor
funcs.doc:
Factor factors a univariate polynomial e in the symbol v of degree 1 or 2
into a leading coefficient times monic linear factors, when its roots are
rational or expressible with a single sqrt. If e cannot be factored this
way it is returned unchanged (canonicalized). This is a convenience helper,
not a general factorization algorithm.

funcs.name: Factorial
funcs.doc:
Factorial returns x! for a non-negative integer x (folded exactly). For other
arguments an unevaluated node is returned; it evaluates numerically as
Gamma(x+1).

funcs.name: Floor
funcs.doc: Floor returns the greatest integer not exceeding x for numeric arguments.

funcs.name: Flt
funcs.doc: Flt returns the inexact float literal f.

funcs.name: Gamma
funcs.doc:
Gamma returns the gamma function Γ(x). Positive integers fold to a factorial
(Γ(n) = (n-1)!) and positive half-integers fold to a rational multiple of
sqrt(pi).

funcs.name: Im
funcs.doc:
Im returns the imaginary part of e (the real coefficient of I), treating free
symbols as real.

funcs.name: Int
funcs.doc: Int returns the integer expression for v.

funcs.name: IntBig
funcs.doc:
IntBig returns the integer expression for the given big.Int; the value is
copied so later mutation of v does not affect the expression.

funcs.name: Integrate
funcs.doc:
Integrate returns a symbolic antiderivative of e with respect to the symbol
v (the constant of integration is omitted).

Coverage: constants; powers x^n for any integer n (including 1/x -> log x);
sums (term by term); constant multiples (constants are pulled out); the
elementary functions exp, sin and cos; and, via a linear substitution, any
of the above whose argument or base is linear in v (a*v+b). Integrands
outside this set are returned as an unevaluated [Integral] node.

funcs.name: Limit
funcs.signature: func Limit(e, sym, to Expr) Expr
funcs.doc:
Limit returns the limit of e as sym approaches to. It evaluates finite limits
by substitution, applies L'Hôpital's rule to 0/0 and ∞/∞ indeterminate
forms, and handles limits at ±∞ ([Inf], [NegInf]) of rational functions by
comparing degrees, with a numeric fallback. When no value can be determined
an unevaluated Limit node is returned. sym must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: Log
funcs.doc:
Log returns the natural logarithm log(x), folding log(1) to 0, log(E) to 1
and recognising log(exp(u)) -> u.

funcs.name: Mul
funcs.doc:
Mul returns the canonical product of its arguments. It flattens nested
products, folds numeric constants, applies x*0 and x*1, combines repeated
bases (so that x*x becomes x^2) and sorts the result. Mul() with no
arguments is 1.

funcs.name: MustParse
funcs.doc:
MustParse is like [Parse] but panics on error. It is convenient for tests
and package-level variables built from constant strings.

funcs.name: Parse
funcs.doc:
Parse parses a mathematical expression written in ordinary infix notation
and returns its expression tree. It understands:

  - the binary operators + - * / ^ (^ is right-associative),
  - unary plus and minus,
  - parentheses for grouping,
  - integer and decimal-float literals,
  - symbols (identifiers), the constants pi and E,
  - function calls sin, cos, tan, exp, log, ln and sqrt, and
  - implicit multiplication, so "2x", "2(x+1)" and "x y" all parse as
    products.

The returned expression is canonicalized by the constructors as it is built.

funcs.name: Pow
funcs.doc:
Pow returns base raised to exp in canonical form, applying x^0, x^1, 1^x,
0^x, folding numeric powers with integer exponents and collapsing nested
powers with integer outer exponents.

funcs.name: Product
funcs.signature: func Product(e, sym, lo, hi Expr) Expr
funcs.doc:
Product returns the product of e over sym running from lo to hi inclusive. It
finds closed forms for constant summands (e^count) and for the summand k
(giving hi!/(lo-1)!), and evaluates finite numeric bounds directly.
Unresolved products return an unevaluated Product node. sym must be a
[Symbol].

funcs.name: Rat
funcs.doc: Rat returns the exact rational a/b in lowest terms. It panics if b is zero.

funcs.name: Re
funcs.doc: Re returns the real part of e, treating free symbols as real.

funcs.name: Sec
funcs.doc:
Sec returns sec(x) = 1/cos(x), folding sec(0) to 1 and returning the exact
reciprocal of cos at the standard angles where cos is non-zero.

funcs.name: Sech
funcs.doc: Sech returns the hyperbolic secant sech(x) = 1/cosh(x), folding sech(0) to 1.

funcs.name: Series
funcs.signature: func Series(e, sym, at Expr, n int) Expr
funcs.doc:
Series returns the Taylor expansion of e about sym = at, truncated to n terms
(degrees 0 through n-1). With at = 0 this is the Maclaurin series. The result
is a polynomial in (sym - at); no remainder term is attached. sym must be a
[Symbol] and n must be positive.

funcs.name: Sign
funcs.doc:
Sign returns the sign of x: -1, 0 or +1 for numeric inputs. A leading numeric
factor contributes its sign, so Sign(-3*x) folds to -Sign(x).

funcs.name: Simplify
funcs.doc:
Simplify returns a canonicalized copy of e. Because the [Add], [Mul] and
[Pow] constructors already canonicalize, Simplify simply rebuilds the tree
bottom-up through them: this folds numeric arithmetic, applies the algebraic
identities, combines like terms in sums, combines repeated powers in
products and evaluates function identities such as sin(0)=0.

funcs.name: Sin
funcs.doc:
Sin returns sin(x), folding sin(0) to 0 and returning exact values at
rational multiples of Pi that land on the standard unit-circle angles
(multiples of Pi/6 and Pi/4).

funcs.name: Sinh
funcs.doc: Sinh returns the hyperbolic sine sinh(x), folding sinh(0) to 0.

funcs.name: Solve
funcs.doc:
Solve returns the solutions of the equation e == 0 for the symbol v.

It handles polynomials of any degree by first extracting all rational roots
exactly (via the rational-root theorem and synthetic division) and then
resolving the remaining factor: a linear or quadratic factor is solved
exactly with the quadratic formula (complex conjugate roots a±b*I are
returned when the discriminant is negative), and an irreducible factor of
degree three or higher is solved numerically with the Durand–Kerner method,
yielding correct real and complex roots. Repeated roots are returned once.
Non-polynomial equations return an error. v must be a [Symbol].

To solve lhs == rhs, pass Solve(lhs.Add(rhs.Mul(Int(-1))), v), i.e. move
everything to one side.

funcs.name: SolveSystem
funcs.doc:
SolveSystem solves a system of linear equations eqs (each read as eq == 0)
for the given syms, using Gaussian elimination over the rationals. It returns
the solution values aligned with syms. The number of equations must equal the
number of unknowns and the system must be linear with a unique solution;
otherwise an error is returned. Each entry of syms must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: Sqrt
funcs.doc:
Sqrt returns the square root of x. Non-negative integers have their largest
square factor pulled out of the radical, so sqrt(9) folds to 3 and sqrt(8)
to 2*sqrt(2).

funcs.name: Subs
funcs.signature: func Subs(e, sym, val Expr) Expr
funcs.doc:
Subs replaces every occurrence of the symbol sym with val and rebuilds the
expression through the canonicalizing constructors. sym must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: Summation
funcs.signature: func Summation(e, sym, lo, hi Expr) Expr
funcs.doc:
Summation returns the sum of e over sym running from lo to hi inclusive. It
finds closed forms for sums whose summand is constant, polynomial (via power
sums up to degree three) or geometric, and evaluates finite numeric bounds
directly. Unresolved sums return an unevaluated Sum node. sym must be a
[Symbol].

funcs.name: Sym
funcs.doc: Sym returns the symbol (named variable) with the given name.

funcs.name: Tan
funcs.doc:
Tan returns tan(x), folding tan(0) to 0 and returning exact values at the
standard rational multiples of Pi where the tangent is finite.

funcs.name: Tanh
funcs.doc: Tanh returns the hyperbolic tangent tanh(x), folding tanh(0) to 0.

types.name: Float
types.doc: Float is an inexact floating-point literal.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the canonical sum of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Diff
methods.doc: Diff returns the derivative of the receiver with respect to v.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether o is the same float.

methods.name: Expand
methods.doc: Expand distributes products over sums and expands integer powers.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the canonical product of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc: Pow returns the receiver raised to exp.

methods.name: Simplify
methods.doc: Simplify returns a canonicalized, simplified copy of the receiver.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders a float using the shortest round-tripping representation.

methods.name: Subs
methods.signature: func (b Float) Subs(sym, val Expr) Expr
methods.doc: Subs substitutes every occurrence of sym with val in the receiver.

types.name: Integer
types.doc: Integer is an arbitrary-precision integer.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the canonical sum of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Diff
methods.doc: Diff returns the derivative of the receiver with respect to v.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether o is the same integer.

methods.name: Expand
methods.doc: Expand distributes products over sums and expands integer powers.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the canonical product of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc: Pow returns the receiver raised to exp.

methods.name: Simplify
methods.doc: Simplify returns a canonicalized, simplified copy of the receiver.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders an integer in base 10.

methods.name: Subs
methods.signature: func (b Integer) Subs(sym, val Expr) Expr
methods.doc: Subs substitutes every occurrence of sym with val in the receiver.

types.name: ODEError
types.doc:
ODEError reports that no implemented method could solve the differential
equation, or that its inputs were malformed.

methods.name: Error
methods.doc: Error returns the human-readable reason the ODE could not be solved.

types.name: ODESolution
types.doc:
ODESolution is the result of symbolically solving an ordinary differential
equation. It mirrors the information returned by a computer-algebra dsolve.

funcs.name: SolveODE1
funcs.signature: func SolveODE1(rhs, x, y Expr) (*ODESolution, error)

funcs.name: SolveODE2Const
funcs.signature: func SolveODE2Const(a, b, c, g, x, y Expr) (*ODESolution, error)
funcs.doc:
SolveODE2Const solves the second-order linear constant-coefficient equation

	a*y'' + b*y' + c*y = g(x)

where a, b and c are numeric [Expr] and x is the independent-variable
[Symbol]. The homogeneous part is built from the characteristic roots of
a*r^2 + b*r + c, covering the distinct-real, repeated-real and
complex-conjugate cases with arbitrary constants C1 and C2. A particular
solution is found by undetermined coefficients when g is a sum of terms of
the form (polynomial)*exp(alpha*x)*{1, cos(omega*x), sin(omega*x)} with
rational alpha and omega; resonance with the homogeneous solution is handled
by the usual multiplication by a power of x. If g falls outside that family
an *[ODEError] is returned.

types.name: PFTerm
types.doc:
PFTerm is a single term Coeff/(Denom^Power) of a partial-fraction
decomposition, as returned by [PartialFractions]. Coeff is an expression whose
degree in the variable is strictly less than the degree of Denom, and Denom is
an irreducible (linear or quadratic) monic polynomial.

types.name: Poly
types.doc:
Poly is a dense univariate polynomial in a single named variable over the
exact rationals. The coefficient of Var^i is stored at index i, using
arbitrary-precision math/big rationals internally, so all arithmetic is exact.
The zero polynomial has degree -1. Poly values are immutable once returned:
every method builds and returns a fresh Poly rather than mutating the receiver.

funcs.name: NewPoly
funcs.doc:
NewPoly returns the polynomial in varName whose coefficient of x^i is
coeffs[i]. Each coefficient must be a numeric integer or rational expression;
NewPoly panics if any coefficient is not exactly rational. Trailing zero
coefficients are trimmed automatically, so NewPoly("x", Int(0)) is the zero
polynomial.

funcs.name: PolyFrom
funcs.signature: func PolyFrom(e Expr, v Expr) (*Poly, error)
funcs.doc:
PolyFrom extracts the polynomial in the symbol v from an arbitrary expression
e. It returns an error if e is not polynomial in v (for example if v appears
inside a function or with a negative or non-integer power) or if any
coefficient is not exactly rational. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: PolyGCD
funcs.doc:
PolyGCD returns the monic greatest common divisor of a and b, computed by the
Euclidean algorithm over the rationals. The GCD of two zero polynomials is the
zero polynomial.

funcs.name: PolyLCM
funcs.doc:
PolyLCM returns the monic least common multiple of a and b, computed as
(a*b)/gcd(a,b). If either argument is the zero polynomial the result is the
zero polynomial.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns the sum p + q. The result variable is taken from whichever operand
is non-constant; Add panics if both operands are non-constant in different
variables.

methods.name: Coeff
methods.doc:
Coeff returns the coefficient of x^i as an exact integer or rational
expression. Indices outside the stored range (including negative indices)
yield Int(0).

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc: Degree returns the degree of the polynomial, or -1 for the zero polynomial.

methods.name: Derivative
methods.doc: Derivative returns the formal derivative of the polynomial.

methods.name: Discriminant
methods.doc:
Discriminant returns the discriminant of the polynomial, defined as
(-1)^{n(n-1)/2} / a_n * Resultant(p, p'), where n is the degree and a_n is the
leading coefficient. Polynomials of degree less than one yield Int(0).

methods.name: DivMod
methods.doc:
DivMod returns the quotient and remainder of Euclidean division of p by q over
the rationals, so that p == quo*q + rem with deg(rem) < deg(q). It returns an
error if q is the zero polynomial.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval returns the value of the polynomial at x, computed by Horner's method and
returned as a canonical [Expr]. For a numeric x the result folds to a number.

methods.name: Expr
methods.doc:
Expr rebuilds an [Expr] equal to the polynomial, as a canonical sum of
coefficient-times-power terms assembled through [Add], [Mul] and [Pow]. The
zero polynomial yields Int(0).

methods.name: Factor
methods.doc:
Factor returns a factorization of the polynomial over the rationals: the
product of Base^Mult reproduces the polynomial. Factoring proceeds by
square-free decomposition followed by exact extraction of all rational roots
(giving monic linear factors) and identification of irreducible quadratic
factors; any remaining higher-degree factor with no rational root is returned
as-is. A non-unit leading coefficient is returned as a leading constant
factor. The result is deterministic.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether the polynomial is identically zero.

methods.name: LeadingCoeff
methods.doc:
LeadingCoeff returns the coefficient of the highest power as an exact integer
or rational expression, or Int(0) for the zero polynomial.

methods.name: Monic
methods.doc:
Monic returns the polynomial divided by its leading coefficient, yielding a
monic polynomial with the same roots. The zero polynomial is returned
unchanged.

methods.name: MulP
methods.doc:
MulP returns the product p * q. The result variable is chosen as in
[Poly.Add].

methods.name: Resultant
methods.doc:
Resultant returns the resultant of p and q, computed exactly over the
rationals via the Euclidean remainder sequence. The resultant is zero exactly
when p and q share a non-constant common factor.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc:
Scale returns the polynomial with every coefficient multiplied by the scalar
c, which must be an exact integer or rational; Scale panics otherwise.

methods.name: SquareFree
methods.doc:
SquareFree returns the square-free decomposition of the polynomial using Yun's
algorithm: a list of pairwise-coprime square-free factors, each tagged with
the multiplicity to which it divides the polynomial, so that the product of
Base^Mult reproduces the polynomial. A non-unit leading coefficient is
returned as a leading constant factor of multiplicity one. The zero polynomial
yields no factors.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the polynomial as a readable expression such as
"x^2 - 2*x + 1", with terms ordered from the highest power down and the zero
polynomial rendered as "0".

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc:
Sub returns the difference p - q. The result variable is chosen as in
[Poly.Add].

methods.name: Var
methods.doc: Var returns the name of the polynomial's variable.

types.name: PolyFactor
types.doc:
PolyFactor is one factor of a polynomial together with its multiplicity, as
returned by [Poly.SquareFree] and [Poly.Factor]. The factored polynomial
equals the product of Base raised to Mult over all returned factors.

types.name: Rational
types.doc:
Rational is an exact fraction whose reduced denominator is greater than one.
Fractions that reduce to whole numbers are represented as [Integer] instead.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the canonical sum of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Diff
methods.doc: Diff returns the derivative of the receiver with respect to v.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether o is the same rational.

methods.name: Expand
methods.doc: Expand distributes products over sums and expands integer powers.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the canonical product of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc: Pow returns the receiver raised to exp.

methods.name: Simplify
methods.doc: Simplify returns a canonicalized, simplified copy of the receiver.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders a rational as numerator/denominator.

methods.name: Subs
methods.signature: func (b Rational) Subs(sym, val Expr) Expr
methods.doc: Subs substitutes every occurrence of sym with val in the receiver.

types.name: Solution
types.doc:
Solution is a complete worked solution: a Title, the ordered list of every
[Step] taken (with no intermediate work skipped), and the final Result.

funcs.name: CompleteSquareSteps
funcs.doc:
CompleteSquareSteps returns a [Solution] that rewrites the quadratic e in the
symbol v in completed-square form a*(v + h)^2 + k, showing the factor-out,
half-the-linear-coefficient and add-and-subtract steps. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: DifferentiateSteps
funcs.doc:
DifferentiateSteps returns a [Solution] that differentiates e with respect to
the symbol v, showing every rule applied at every node of the expression
tree: the constant, variable, sum, product, constant-multiple, power,
exponential and chain rules, and the derivative of each elementary function.
Leaf nodes emit their base-case step and each internal node emits a combining
step, so no intermediate work is skipped. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: ExpandSteps
funcs.doc:
ExpandSteps returns a [Solution] that expands e, showing each distribution of
a product over a sum and each expansion of an integer power of a sum as its
own step. The final result is produced by [Expand].

funcs.name: FactorSteps
funcs.doc:
FactorSteps returns a [Solution] that factors the univariate quadratic e in
the symbol v: it computes the discriminant, finds the two roots and writes e
as a*(v - r1)*(v - r2). For non-quadratic input it reports the factored form
from [Factor] in a single step. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: IntegrateSteps
funcs.doc:
IntegrateSteps returns a [Solution] that integrates e with respect to the
symbol v, naming the technique used at each stage: the sum rule, the
constant-multiple rule, the power and logarithm rules, the standard
antiderivatives, integration by parts, partial fractions and substitution.
The final antiderivative is produced by [Integrate], so it is always correct;
the steps narrate how it is reached. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: LimitSteps
funcs.doc:
LimitSteps returns a [Solution] that evaluates the limit of e as the symbol v
approaches to. It shows the direct-substitution attempt, detects the 0/0
indeterminate form and applies l'Hopital's rule, and handles limits at
infinity by degree comparison. The final value is produced by [Limit]. v must
be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: PartialFractionSteps
funcs.doc:
PartialFractionSteps returns a [Solution] that decomposes the rational
expression e in the symbol v into partial fractions: it factors the
denominator, extracts any polynomial part, and reports each residue term.
The final decomposition is produced by [ApartExpr]. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: SeriesSteps
funcs.signature: func SeriesSteps(e, v, at Expr, n int) *Solution
funcs.doc:
SeriesSteps returns a [Solution] that builds the Taylor expansion of e about
the symbol v = at to n terms (degrees 0 through n-1), showing, for each k,
the k-th derivative, its value at the expansion point, the division by k! and
the resulting term. The final polynomial is produced by [Series]. v must be a
[Symbol] and n must be positive.

funcs.name: SimplifySteps
funcs.doc:
SimplifySteps returns a [Solution] that simplifies e, showing each place in
the expression tree where an identity is applied (combining like terms,
folding numeric constants, combining repeated powers, evaluating a function
at a known value) as its own step. The final result is produced by
[Simplify].

funcs.name: SolveCubicSteps
funcs.doc:
SolveCubicSteps returns a [Solution] that solves the cubic equation e == 0 for
the symbol v: it searches for a rational root (rational-root theorem),
divides it out by synthetic division to leave a quadratic factor, and solves
that quadratic with the quadratic formula. When no rational root exists the
roots are reported from the numeric solver. v must be a [Symbol].

funcs.name: SolveLinearSteps
funcs.doc:
SolveLinearSteps returns a [Solution] that solves the linear equation e == 0
for the symbol v, showing the isolate-the-variable-term and
divide-by-the-coefficient steps explicitly. v must be a [Symbol]; if e is not
linear in v a single explanatory step is returned instead.

funcs.name: SolveQuadraticSteps
funcs.doc:
SolveQuadraticSteps returns a [Solution] that solves the quadratic equation
e == 0 for the symbol v: it identifies the coefficients, states the quadratic
formula, computes the discriminant, and reports both roots (a repeated root
once, complex conjugate roots when the discriminant is negative). v must be a
[Symbol]; if e is not quadratic in v a single explanatory step is returned.

funcs.name: SolveSystemSteps
funcs.doc:
SolveSystemSteps returns a [Solution] that solves the square linear system
eqs (each read as eq == 0) for the unknowns syms by Gaussian elimination,
recording every row operation — pivot selection, row swaps, pivot
normalization and elimination — as its own step, then reading off each
unknown. Each entry of syms must be a [Symbol].

methods.name: LaTeX
methods.doc:
LaTeX renders the whole solution as a complete LaTeX derivation inside a
single aligned environment. Each step contributes an explanation line
(numbered, as a \text{...} group) followed by its rendered before/after
mathematics; the final line shows the result. The output is deterministic and
depends only on the standard library.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the whole solution as numbered, human-readable text: the
title, then each step in order with its explanation and its before/after
mathematics, then the final result. Every step is shown.

types.name: Step
types.doc:
Step is a single, clearly-defined move in a worked solution. It records what
was done (Explanation, in plain language), the name of the rule or identity
applied (Rule), and the mathematics immediately before and after the move
(Before and After, as ordinary [Expr] values). A step whose Before and After
are structurally equal represents a "state" line — the starting expression or
a restatement — rather than a transformation.

methods.name: LaTeX
methods.doc:
LaTeX renders the step as a self-contained LaTeX fragment combining its
plain-language explanation (as a \text{...} group) with its before/after
mathematics rendered by the package's expression renderer.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the step as a single human-readable line of the form
"[rule] explanation: before -> after" (the arrow and after are omitted when
the step does not change the expression).

types.name: Symbol
types.doc: Symbol is a named variable such as x or y.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the canonical sum of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Diff
methods.doc: Diff returns the derivative of the receiver with respect to v.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports structural equality of two expressions. Because the [Add],
[Mul] and [Pow] constructors canonicalize their inputs, mathematically equal
expressions built through the public API compare equal here.

methods.name: Expand
methods.doc: Expand distributes products over sums and expands integer powers.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the canonical product of the receiver and the arguments.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc: Pow returns the receiver raised to exp.

methods.name: Simplify
methods.doc: Simplify returns a canonicalized, simplified copy of the receiver.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders a symbol as its name.

methods.name: Subs
methods.signature: func (b Symbol) Subs(sym, val Expr) Expr
methods.doc: Subs substitutes every occurrence of sym with val in the receiver.

funcs.name: Eval
funcs.signature: func Eval(e Expr, env map[string]float64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Eval numerically evaluates e to a float64 using env to look up symbol
values. It returns an error if e references an unbound symbol or an
operation it cannot evaluate numerically.

funcs.name: EvalComplex
funcs.signature: func EvalComplex(e Expr, env map[string]complex128) (complex128, error)
funcs.doc: EvalComplex is Evalc with an environment binding symbols to complex values.

funcs.name: Evalc
funcs.doc:
Evalc numerically evaluates a symbol-free expression to a complex128,
interpreting I as the imaginary unit. Use it when an expression may take a
complex value (for example sqrt(-1) or exp(I*Pi/3)).

funcs.name: Evalf
funcs.doc:
Evalf numerically evaluates a fully numeric expression (one with no free
symbols) to a float64. When the real evaluator cannot proceed (for example
because the imaginary unit I appears) it falls back to a complex128
evaluation and returns the real part if the value is real; genuinely complex
values return an error and should be evaluated with [Evalc].

funcs.name: GenerateLaTeX
funcs.doc:
GenerateLaTeX returns the complete LaTeX derivation of the solution sol,
identical to sol.LaTeX(). It is the explicit package-level entry point for
turning a worked [Solution] into a renderable LaTeX string.

funcs.name: LaTeX
funcs.doc:
LaTeX renders e as a LaTeX math fragment suitable for inclusion between
dollar signs or in an equation environment.

Rationals and negative or reciprocal powers become \frac{..}{..}; square
roots and half powers become \sqrt{..}; every supported elementary function
maps to its conventional LaTeX command (\sin, \cos, \Gamma, \psi, ...);
the constants pi, E, I and oo render as \pi, e, i and \infty; a \cdot is
inserted only where two factors would otherwise run together, and it is
suppressed between a numeric coefficient and a symbol. Auto-sized
\left(\right) delimiters wrap sums that appear under products or powers.
The output is deterministic.

funcs.name: LaTeXEq
funcs.doc:
LaTeXEq renders the equation lhs = rhs as a LaTeX fragment, joining the two
sides with " = ".

funcs.name: MathML
funcs.doc:
MathML renders e as a presentation MathML fragment wrapped in a <math>
element. Fractions become <mfrac>, powers <msup>, square roots <msqrt> and
grouped subexpressions <mrow>, covering the same node set as LaTeX and
Pretty. The output is deterministic.

funcs.name: PartialFractions
funcs.signature: func PartialFractions(num, den *Poly) (poly *Poly, terms []PFTerm, err error)
funcs.doc:
PartialFractions computes the partial-fraction decomposition of num/den over
the rationals. It returns the polynomial (improper) part from Euclidean
division and a list of proper terms Coeff/(Denom^Power), one per distinct
irreducible factor of den and each of its powers with a non-zero numerator.
The denominator is factored with [Poly.Factor] and the residues are solved as
an exact rational linear system. It returns an error if den is the zero
polynomial or if den has an irreducible factor of degree three or higher that
cannot be reduced.

funcs.name: Pretty
funcs.doc:
Pretty renders e as a multi-line Unicode drawing in the style of a computer
algebra system's pretty printer: fractions are stacked over a horizontal
bar, exponents are raised onto the line above, square roots are drawn under
a radical, and the terms of a sum are aligned on a common baseline. The
result is the rows joined by newlines, with no trailing newline. The output
is deterministic.

funcs.name: SolutionLaTeX
funcs.doc:
SolutionLaTeX is an alias for [GenerateLaTeX]: it returns the complete LaTeX
derivation of the solution sol.

funcs.name: VerifyODE1
funcs.signature: func VerifyODE1(sol *ODESolution, rhs, x, y Expr) bool
funcs.doc:
VerifyODE1 checks a first-order solution by substitution: for an explicit
solution y = sol.General it forms the residual y' - rhs (with y replaced by
the solution) and reports whether it simplifies exactly to zero. It is a
best-effort exact check and returns false for implicit solutions (whose
General still mentions y) or when the residual cannot be reduced to zero.

examples.name: Conjugate
examples.code:
z := Add(Int(2), Mul(Int(3), I))
fmt.Println(Conjugate(z))

examples.name: Diff
examples.code:
x := Sym("x")
fmt.Println(Diff(MustParse("x^2"), x))
fmt.Println(Diff(MustParse("sin(x^2)"), x))

examples.name: Expand
examples.code: fmt.Println(Expand(MustParse("(x + 1)^2")))

examples.name: Integrate (byParts)
examples.code:
x := Sym("x")
fmt.Println(Integrate(MustParse("x*exp(x)"), x))

examples.name: Limit
examples.code:
x := Sym("x")
fmt.Println(Limit(MustParse("sin(x)/x"), x, Int(0)))

examples.name: Parse
examples.code:
e, _ := Parse("x^2 + 2*x + 1")
fmt.Println(e)

examples.name: Series
examples.code:
x := Sym("x")
fmt.Println(Series(MustParse("exp(x)"), x, Int(0), 5))

examples.name: Simplify
examples.code:
x := Sym("x")
fmt.Println(Simplify(Add(x, x, Int(3), Int(4))))

examples.name: Solve
examples.code:
roots, _ := Solve(MustParse("x^2 - 5*x + 6"), Sym("x"))
fmt.Println(roots[0], roots[1])

examples.name: SolveSystem
examples.code:
x, y := Sym("x"), Sym("y")
sol, _ := SolveSystem([]Expr{MustParse("x + y - 3"), MustParse("x - y - 1")}, []Expr{x, y})
fmt.Println(sol[0], sol[1])

examples.name: Summation
examples.code:
k, n := Sym("k"), Sym("n")
fmt.Println(Summation(k, k, Int(1), n))

packages.name: approxtheory
packages.synopsis: Package approxtheory implements the core tools of classical approximation theory using nothing but the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package approxtheory implements the core tools of classical approximation
theory using nothing but the Go standard library.

The package is organised around several themes:

  - Chebyshev series: computing interpolation coefficients from function
    samples (a discrete cosine transform), evaluating a series with the
    numerically stable Clenshaw recurrence, differentiating and integrating
    in coefficient space, finding the real roots on an interval and doing
    ordinary arithmetic on series.

  - Remez exchange: computing the best (minimax) polynomial approximation of
    a continuous function on an interval, together with the equioscillating
    error.

  - Pade approximants: turning a truncated Taylor series into a rational
    function that usually reproduces the original function far better than
    the polynomial partial sum.

  - Polynomial fitting: ordinary and weighted least-squares fits together
    with a discrete minimax (Chebyshev) fit obtained by an exchange
    algorithm.

  - Rational interpolation: Thiele's continued-fraction interpolant built
    from reciprocal differences.

  - Bernstein polynomials and Bezier evaluation, degree elevation and the
    Bernstein approximation operator.

  - Error analysis: Lebesgue functions and constants, node polynomials and
    the associated interpolation error bounds, and assorted error norms.

Unless otherwise noted, polynomials are represented in the monomial basis by
a slice of coefficients in ascending order, so that coeffs[i] multiplies
x**i. Chebyshev series carry their own domain [A, B]; all other routines act
on the interval the caller supplies.

The implementations favour clarity and numerical soundness over raw speed
and depend only on the packages math, sort, errors, fmt and strings.

vars.signature: var ErrDimensionMismatch = errors.New("approxtheory: dimension mismatch")
vars.doc:
ErrDimensionMismatch is returned when input slices that must have equal
length do not.

vars.signature: var ErrEmptyInput = errors.New("approxtheory: empty input")
vars.doc:
ErrEmptyInput is returned when a routine that requires at least one point or
coefficient is given none.

vars.signature: var ErrSingular = errors.New("approxtheory: singular linear system")
vars.doc:
ErrSingular is returned when a linear system encountered during a fit or
solve is singular (or numerically so) and cannot be solved.

types.name: Barycentric
types.doc:
Barycentric holds a polynomial interpolant in the second (true) barycentric
form, storing the nodes, the values at those nodes and the barycentric
weights.

funcs.name: ChebyshevInterpolant
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevInterpolant(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) *Barycentric
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevInterpolant builds a barycentric polynomial interpolant of f at the
n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto nodes on [a, b], using the stable closed-form
weights. This is the recommended way to interpolate at Chebyshev points.

funcs.name: NewBarycentric
funcs.doc:
NewBarycentric builds a barycentric interpolant through (xs, ys) with weights
derived from the nodes. The xs must be distinct.

funcs.name: NewBarycentricWeighted
funcs.doc:
NewBarycentricWeighted builds a barycentric interpolant with caller-supplied
weights (for example the closed-form Chebyshev weights). The three slices
must have equal length.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval evaluates the barycentric interpolant at x. When x coincides with a
node the exact node value is returned.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the interpolant at every point in xs.

types.name: BernsteinPoly
types.doc:
BernsteinPoly represents a polynomial in the Bernstein (Bezier) form with
control values Coeffs on the interval [A, B]. The degree equals
len(Coeffs)-1.

funcs.name: BernsteinApprox
funcs.signature: func BernsteinApprox(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) *BernsteinPoly
funcs.doc:
BernsteinApprox returns the degree-n Bernstein polynomial approximation of f
on [a, b], whose control values are the samples f(a + k/n (b-a)). The
Bernstein operator converges uniformly to any continuous f.

funcs.name: NewBernsteinPoly
funcs.doc:
NewBernsteinPoly builds a Bernstein-form polynomial with the given control
values on [a, b].

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns a deep copy of the Bernstein polynomial.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc: Degree returns the polynomial degree of the Bernstein form.

methods.name: Derivative
methods.doc:
Derivative returns the Bernstein form of the derivative of p, correctly
scaled for the domain [A, B].

methods.name: Elevate
methods.doc:
Elevate returns an equivalent Bernstein polynomial of degree n+1 (degree
elevation), leaving the represented polynomial unchanged.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval evaluates the Bernstein polynomial at x in [A, B] using the numerically
stable de Casteljau algorithm.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the Bernstein polynomial at every point in xs.

methods.name: Integral
methods.doc:
Integral returns the definite integral of the Bernstein polynomial over its
whole domain [A, B]. Every Bernstein basis of degree n integrates to
(B-A)/(n+1), so the integral is the mean of the control values times (B-A).

methods.name: ToMonomial
methods.doc:
ToMonomial converts the Bernstein form (assumed on [0, 1]) into monomial
coefficients in ascending order.

types.name: ChebSeries
types.doc:
ChebSeries represents a finite Chebyshev series

	f(x) = sum_{k=0}^{n} Coeffs[k] * T_k(t),   t = (2x-(A+B))/(B-A),

on the interval [A, B]. T_k is the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind.

funcs.name: ChebFit
funcs.signature: func ChebFit(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) *ChebSeries
funcs.doc:
ChebFit returns the degree-n Chebyshev interpolant of f on [a, b], sampling f
at the n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points and transforming to coefficients.

funcs.name: ChebFitGauss
funcs.signature: func ChebFitGauss(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) *ChebSeries
funcs.doc:
ChebFitGauss returns the degree n-1 Chebyshev approximation of f built from
samples at the n Chebyshev-Gauss points (roots of T_n). The coefficients are
the discrete Chebyshev transform associated with Gauss quadrature.

funcs.name: ChebFromMonomial
funcs.doc:
ChebFromMonomial builds a ChebSeries on [a, b] from a monomial polynomial
defined on that same interval (the polynomial is first rescaled to [-1, 1]).

funcs.name: ChebLeastSquares
funcs.signature: func ChebLeastSquares(xs, ys []float64, degree int, a, b float64) (*ChebSeries, error)
funcs.doc:
ChebLeastSquares fits a Chebyshev series of the given degree to sampled data
(xs, ys) on [a, b] by least squares in the Chebyshev basis. It is more
numerically stable than a monomial fit for higher degrees.

funcs.name: ChebProduct
funcs.doc:
ChebProduct returns the Chebyshev series of the product of two series sharing
the same domain. The product is computed exactly in the Chebyshev basis via
2 T_i T_j = T_{i+j} + T_{|i-j|}.

funcs.name: NewChebSeries
funcs.doc:
NewChebSeries builds a ChebSeries from raw coefficients on the interval
[a, b]. The coefficient slice is copied.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the sum of two series. They must share the same domain.

methods.name: Antiderivative
methods.doc:
Antiderivative returns a Chebyshev series whose derivative is s and whose
value at A is zero (an indefinite integral pinned to the left endpoint),
correctly scaled for the domain [A, B].

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns a deep copy of the series.

methods.name: Coefficient
methods.doc:
Coefficient returns the k-th Chebyshev coefficient, or 0 when k is out of
range.

methods.name: Coefficients
methods.doc: Coefficients returns a copy of the series coefficients.

methods.name: DefiniteIntegral
methods.doc: DefiniteIntegral returns the integral of the series from x0 to x1.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree returns the polynomial degree of the series (len(Coeffs)-1), or 0
when empty.

methods.name: Derivative
methods.doc:
Derivative returns the Chebyshev series of the derivative of s, correctly
scaled for the domain [A, B].

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the interval endpoints [A, B] of the series.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the value of the series at x in [A, B].

methods.name: EvalDerivative
methods.doc: EvalDerivative evaluates the derivative of the Chebyshev series at x.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the series at every point in xs.

methods.name: Integral
methods.doc: Integral returns the integral of the series over its whole domain [A, B].

methods.name: L2Norm
methods.doc:
L2Norm returns the L2 norm of the series over its domain, sqrt(int f^2 dx),
computed exactly from the coefficients via the Chebyshev product.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of coefficients in the series.

methods.name: MaxAbs
methods.doc:
MaxAbs returns the maximum absolute value of the series over its domain,
estimated on a fine grid.

methods.name: MaxCoeff
methods.doc: MaxCoeff returns the largest absolute coefficient of the series.

methods.name: Remap
methods.doc:
Remap returns a new series with the same coefficients reinterpreted on the
interval [a, b]; the underlying polynomial shape in the unit variable is
preserved but its domain changes.

methods.name: Roots
methods.doc:
Roots returns the real roots of the Chebyshev series lying in [A, B], sorted
ascending. Roots are located by scanning a fine grid for sign changes and
refining each bracket with a hybrid bisection/Newton iteration.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns the series multiplied by the constant c.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the difference s-o of two series sharing the same domain.

methods.name: TailNorm
methods.doc:
TailNorm returns the sum of absolute values of the coefficients beyond index
k, a convenient a-posteriori estimate of the truncation error.

methods.name: ToMonomial
methods.doc:
ToMonomial converts the Chebyshev series into monomial coefficients
(ascending) of the polynomial in the original variable x on [A, B].

methods.name: Truncate
methods.doc:
Truncate returns a copy of the series with trailing coefficients whose
absolute value is at most tol removed. At least the constant term is kept.

types.name: PadeResult
types.doc:
PadeResult holds a Pade approximant P(x)/Q(x) with the numerator and
denominator given as monomial coefficients in ascending order. By
convention Q[0] = 1.

funcs.name: PadeApprox
funcs.signature: func PadeApprox(taylor []float64, m, n int) (*PadeResult, error)
funcs.doc:
PadeApprox computes the [m/n] Pade approximant of the power series whose
Taylor coefficients (about 0) are given in taylor, taylor[k] being the
coefficient of x**k. It requires len(taylor) >= m+n+1. The numerator has
degree m and the denominator degree n with Q(0)=1.

funcs.name: PadeCos
funcs.doc: PadeCos returns the [m/n] Pade approximant of cos about 0.

funcs.name: PadeExp
funcs.doc:
PadeExp returns the [m/n] Pade approximant of exp about 0, a convenience
wrapper around PadeApprox with the exponential Taylor series.

funcs.name: PadeLog1p
funcs.doc: PadeLog1p returns the [m/n] Pade approximant of log(1+x) about 0.

funcs.name: PadeSin
funcs.doc: PadeSin returns the [m/n] Pade approximant of sin about 0.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the Pade approximant at x.

types.name: PolyFitResult
types.doc:
PolyFitResult holds the result of a polynomial fit: the monomial
coefficients (ascending), the root-mean-square residual, the maximum
absolute residual and the coefficient of determination R^2.

funcs.name: PolyFit
funcs.signature: func PolyFit(xs, ys []float64, degree int) (*PolyFitResult, error)
funcs.doc:
PolyFit computes the ordinary least-squares polynomial of the given degree
through the data (xs, ys) by solving the normal equations. It returns the
coefficients and residual statistics.

funcs.name: WeightedPolyFit
funcs.signature: func WeightedPolyFit(xs, ys, weights []float64, degree int) (*PolyFitResult, error)
funcs.doc:
WeightedPolyFit computes the weighted least-squares polynomial of the given
degree. When weights is nil every point has unit weight; otherwise weights
must match the length of xs and ys and are treated as multipliers on the
squared residuals.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the fitted polynomial at x.

types.name: RemezResult
types.doc:
RemezResult holds the outcome of a Remez minimax approximation: the monomial
coefficients of the best polynomial (ascending order), the equioscillating
error magnitude, the number of exchange iterations performed and whether the
iteration converged to the requested tolerance.

funcs.name: RemezPoly
funcs.signature: func RemezPoly(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64, maxIter int, tol float64) (*RemezResult, error)
funcs.doc:
RemezPoly computes the degree-n minimax (best uniform) polynomial
approximation of f on [a, b] using the Remez exchange algorithm. maxIter
bounds the number of exchanges and tol is the relative leveling tolerance on
the extrema. A degree-n polynomial has n+1 coefficients and the reference
carries n+2 points.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the minimax polynomial at x.

types.name: Thiele
types.doc:
Thiele holds a rational interpolant built from Thiele's continued fraction.
Xs are the abscissae and Rho are the continued-fraction coefficients
(the leading reciprocal differences).

funcs.name: NewThiele
funcs.doc:
NewThiele builds Thiele's continued-fraction rational interpolant through
the points (xs, ys). It computes the reciprocal-difference table and returns
an interpolant that reproduces every data value. The xs must be distinct.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree returns the number of interpolation nodes, one more than the depth of
the continued fraction.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the Thiele continued-fraction interpolant at x.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the interpolant at every point in xs.

funcs.name: BarycentricWeights
funcs.doc:
BarycentricWeights computes the barycentric weights w_j = 1 / prod_{k!=j}
(x_j - x_k) for an arbitrary set of distinct nodes.

funcs.name: BernsteinBasis
funcs.signature: func BernsteinBasis(k, n int, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BernsteinBasis evaluates the k-th Bernstein basis polynomial of degree n,
b_{k,n}(t) = C(n,k) t^k (1-t)^{n-k}, at t in [0, 1].

funcs.name: BernsteinBasisInterval
funcs.signature: func BernsteinBasisInterval(k, n int, x, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BernsteinBasisInterval evaluates the k-th Bernstein basis polynomial of
degree n on the interval [a, b] at x, by mapping x to [0, 1].

funcs.name: BernsteinControlPoints
funcs.signature: func BernsteinControlPoints(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
BernsteinControlPoints returns the control values f(a + k/n (b-a)) used by
the Bernstein approximation operator.

funcs.name: BernsteinFromMonomial
funcs.doc:
BernsteinFromMonomial returns the Bernstein control values of degree n for a
monomial polynomial defined on [0, 1]. It requires n >= degree of the input.

funcs.name: BezierEval
funcs.doc:
BezierEval evaluates a Bezier curve (Bernstein polynomial on [0,1]) with the
given control values at parameter t. It is an alias for DeCasteljau.

funcs.name: Binomial
funcs.doc:
Binomial returns the binomial coefficient C(n, k) as a float64, computed
multiplicatively to avoid overflow for moderate arguments.

funcs.name: ChebClenshaw
funcs.doc:
ChebClenshaw evaluates the Chebyshev series with coefficients c (index k
multiplies T_k) at t in [-1, 1] using Clenshaw's recurrence.

funcs.name: ChebCoeffsFromValues
funcs.doc:
ChebCoeffsFromValues computes the Chebyshev interpolation coefficients from
samples of a function taken at the n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points in the
order returned by ChebPoints (that is, values[i] is the sample at the i-th
point running from a to b). It performs a discrete cosine transform.

funcs.name: ChebGaussPoints
funcs.signature: func ChebGaussPoints(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
ChebGaussPoints returns the n Chebyshev-Gauss points (roots of T_n) on
[a, b], ordered from a to b.

funcs.name: ChebPoints
funcs.signature: func ChebPoints(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
ChebPoints returns the n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points on [a, b], ordered
from a to b. These are the extrema of T_n mapped to the interval and are the
natural sampling grid for ChebFit.

funcs.name: ChebT
funcs.signature: func ChebT(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebT evaluates the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind T_n at x in
[-1, 1] using the stable three-term recurrence. For |x| > 1 the polynomial
recurrence is still applied.

funcs.name: ChebTValues
funcs.doc: ChebTValues returns the vector [T_0(x), T_1(x), ..., T_n(x)].

funcs.name: ChebU
funcs.signature: func ChebU(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc: ChebU evaluates the Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind U_n at x.

funcs.name: ChebValuesFromCoeffs
funcs.doc:
ChebValuesFromCoeffs is the inverse of ChebCoeffsFromValues: it evaluates the
Chebyshev series at the n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto nodes (ordered a..b via
ChebPoints on [-1,1]) and returns those sample values.

funcs.name: ChebyshevBarycentricWeights
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevBarycentricWeights returns the closed-form barycentric weights for
the n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto nodes (ordered a..b as in ChebPoints):
w_j = (-1)^j delta_j with the endpoints halved. These weights are equal in
magnitude and independent of the interval.

funcs.name: ChebyshevNodes
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevNodes(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevNodes returns the n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto nodes on [a, b],
ascending. It is an alias for ChebPoints kept for readability in error
analysis code.

funcs.name: ChebyshevQuadrature
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevQuadrature(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevQuadrature integrates f over the domain of a Chebyshev fit by first
forming the degree-n interpolant and integrating it exactly. This is a
spectrally accurate alternative to the explicit quadrature rules.

funcs.name: ChebyshevToMonomial
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevToMonomial converts Chebyshev coefficients on [-1, 1] into the
coefficients of the equivalent polynomial in the monomial basis (ascending
order). It uses the recurrence T_{k+1} = 2x T_k - T_{k-1} on coefficient
vectors.

funcs.name: ChebyshevTruncationError
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevTruncationError estimates the uniform error of truncating a
Chebyshev series after index k as the sum of the absolute values of the
discarded coefficients (a standard a-posteriori bound).

funcs.name: ClenshawCurtisQuadrature
funcs.signature: func ClenshawCurtisQuadrature(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ClenshawCurtisQuadrature approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using
n+1 point Clenshaw-Curtis quadrature at the Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto nodes.

funcs.name: ClenshawCurtisWeights
funcs.signature: func ClenshawCurtisWeights(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
ClenshawCurtisWeights returns the n+1 Clenshaw-Curtis quadrature weights on
[a, b] associated with the Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto nodes (ordered a..b as in
ChebPoints). The weights integrate polynomials up to degree n exactly.

funcs.name: ContinuedFractionEval
funcs.doc:
ContinuedFractionEval evaluates the continued fraction

	a[0] + b[1]/(a[1] + b[2]/(a[2] + ... ))

with the given partial numerators b (b[0] is ignored) and denominators a,
using the backward recurrence. len(a) must equal len(b).

funcs.name: ConvergenceOrder
funcs.signature: func ConvergenceOrder(h1, e1, h2, e2 float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ConvergenceOrder estimates the observed order of convergence from two step
sizes and their associated errors: log(e1/e2) / log(h1/h2).

funcs.name: DeCasteljau
funcs.doc:
DeCasteljau evaluates a Bezier curve with the given control values at
parameter t in [0, 1] using the de Casteljau algorithm. It is the
interval-free core used by BernsteinPoly.Eval.

funcs.name: DiscreteMinimaxPoly
funcs.signature: func DiscreteMinimaxPoly(xs, ys []float64, n, maxIter int) ([]float64, float64, error)
funcs.doc:
DiscreteMinimaxPoly computes a degree-n polynomial minimising the maximum
absolute residual over the discrete data set (xs, ys) using a single-point
exchange (Remez-type) algorithm. It returns the monomial coefficients and
the achieved maximum error.

funcs.name: EconomizeChebyshev
funcs.signature: func EconomizeChebyshev(mono []float64, a, b, tol float64) ([]float64, float64)
funcs.doc:
EconomizeChebyshev reduces the degree of a monomial polynomial on [a, b] by
converting to the Chebyshev basis, discarding the highest-order coefficients
whose cumulative absolute value does not exceed tol, and converting back. It
returns the economized monomial polynomial on [a, b] and the discarded
coefficient mass (an upper bound on the introduced uniform error).

funcs.name: EquispacedNodes
funcs.signature: func EquispacedNodes(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc: EquispacedNodes returns n+1 equispaced nodes spanning [a, b].

funcs.name: Factorial
funcs.doc: Factorial returns k! as a float64.

funcs.name: FejerNodes
funcs.signature: func FejerNodes(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FejerNodes returns the n Fejer nodes (Chebyshev points of the first kind,
the roots of T_n) on [a, b], ascending.

funcs.name: FejerQuadrature
funcs.signature: func FejerQuadrature(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
FejerQuadrature approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using Fejer's
first rule with n nodes.

funcs.name: FejerWeights
funcs.signature: func FejerWeights(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FejerWeights returns the n weights of Fejer's first quadrature rule on
[a, b], associated with the roots of T_n.

funcs.name: GaussChebyshev2Nodes
funcs.signature: func GaussChebyshev2Nodes(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshev2Nodes returns the n Gauss-Chebyshev nodes of the second kind,
x_k = cos(k*pi/(n+1)), mapped to [a, b] in ascending order.

funcs.name: GaussChebyshev2Quadrature
funcs.signature: func GaussChebyshev2Quadrature(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshev2Quadrature approximates the plain integral of f over [a, b]
using n-point Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature of the second kind, folding the
weight sqrt(1-t^2) back in.

funcs.name: GaussChebyshev2Weights
funcs.signature: func GaussChebyshev2Weights(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshev2Weights returns the n weights of Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature of
the second kind on [a, b], w_k = pi/(n+1) sin^2(k*pi/(n+1)) scaled by
(b-a)/2. These integrate f(x)*sqrt(1-t^2).

funcs.name: GaussChebyshevNodes
funcs.signature: func GaussChebyshevNodes(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshevNodes returns the n Gauss-Chebyshev nodes (roots of T_n) on
[a, b] in ascending order. These are the nodes of Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature
of the first kind.

funcs.name: GaussChebyshevQuadrature
funcs.signature: func GaussChebyshevQuadrature(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshevQuadrature approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using
n-point Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature of the first kind, folding the Chebyshev
weight back in so the plain (unweighted) integral is returned.

funcs.name: GaussChebyshevWeights
funcs.signature: func GaussChebyshevWeights(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshevWeights returns the n weights of Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature of
the first kind on [a, b]. Each weight equals pi/n scaled by (b-a)/2. The
quadrature integrates f(x) against the weight 1/sqrt(1-t^2), so it estimates
the integral of f divided by that weight; see GaussChebyshevQuadrature for
the plain integral.

funcs.name: Horner
funcs.doc:
Horner is an alias for Polyval; it evaluates a monomial polynomial with
Horner's method.

funcs.name: InterpErrorBound
funcs.signature: func InterpErrorBound(nodes []float64, derivBound, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
InterpErrorBound returns the standard bound on the polynomial interpolation
error, |f(x)-p(x)| <= (M / (n+1)!) * max|w(x)|, where n+1 is the number of
nodes, derivBound is a bound on |f^{(n+1)}| over the interval and nodes are
the interpolation abscissae.

funcs.name: L2Error
funcs.signature: func L2Error(f, g func(float64) float64, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
L2Error returns the discrete root-integral L2 error of f-g over [a, b],
estimated by the composite trapezoidal rule on a fine grid.

funcs.name: LagrangeBasis
funcs.signature: func LagrangeBasis(xs []float64, j int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LagrangeBasis evaluates the j-th Lagrange cardinal polynomial for the nodes
xs at x.

funcs.name: LagrangeEval
funcs.signature: func LagrangeEval(xs, ys []float64, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LagrangeEval evaluates, at x, the Lagrange interpolating polynomial through
(xs, ys) directly (O(n^2) per point). It is a simple reference companion to
the barycentric form.

funcs.name: LebesgueConstant
funcs.signature: func LebesgueConstant(nodes []float64, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LebesgueConstant returns the Lebesgue constant, the maximum of the Lebesgue
function over [a, b], estimated on a fine grid. It bounds how much larger the
interpolation error can be than the best-approximation error.

funcs.name: LebesgueConstantChebyshevAsymptotic
funcs.doc:
LebesgueConstantChebyshevAsymptotic returns the classical asymptotic estimate
(2/pi) log(n+1) + 0.9625 for the Lebesgue constant of n+1 Chebyshev points.

funcs.name: LebesgueConstantEquispacedAsymptotic
funcs.doc:
LebesgueConstantEquispacedAsymptotic returns the classical asymptotic growth
estimate 2^{n+1} / (e * n * ln n) of the Lebesgue constant for n+1
equispaced nodes (valid for large n).

funcs.name: LebesgueFunction
funcs.doc:
LebesgueFunction evaluates the Lebesgue function lambda(x) = sum_j
|L_j(x)| for the interpolation nodes at x, where L_j are the Lagrange
cardinal polynomials.

funcs.name: Linspace
funcs.signature: func Linspace(a, b float64, n int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Linspace returns n equally spaced points spanning the closed interval
[a, b]. For n == 1 it returns the midpoint.

funcs.name: MaxAbsError
funcs.doc: MaxAbsError returns the maximum absolute difference between paired samples.

funcs.name: MaxError
funcs.signature: func MaxError(f, g func(float64) float64, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
MaxError returns the maximum of |f(x) - g(x)| over [a, b], estimated on a
fine grid. It is the standard uniform (sup-norm) error estimate.

funcs.name: MaxNodePolynomial
funcs.signature: func MaxNodePolynomial(nodes []float64, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
MaxNodePolynomial returns the maximum of |w(x)| = |prod_i (x-nodes[i])| over
[a, b], estimated on a fine grid.

funcs.name: MeanAbsError
funcs.doc: MeanAbsError returns the mean absolute difference between paired samples.

funcs.name: MinimaxError
funcs.signature: func MinimaxError(f func(float64) float64, coeffs []float64, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
MinimaxError returns the achieved uniform error of a monomial polynomial p
against f on [a, b], sampled on a fine grid.

funcs.name: MonomialToChebyshev
funcs.doc:
MonomialToChebyshev converts monomial coefficients of a polynomial on
[-1, 1] into Chebyshev coefficients. It expresses each power x^k in the
Chebyshev basis and accumulates, using x*T_j = (T_{|j+1|}+T_{|j-1|})/2 with
the convention T_{-1} = T_1.

funcs.name: NevilleEval
funcs.signature: func NevilleEval(xs, ys []float64, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NevilleEval interpolates (xs, ys) at x using Neville's algorithm and returns
the interpolated value.

funcs.name: NewtonDividedDifferences
funcs.doc:
NewtonDividedDifferences returns the divided-difference coefficients of the
Newton interpolating polynomial through (xs, ys).

funcs.name: NewtonEval
funcs.signature: func NewtonEval(coeffs, xs []float64, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NewtonEval evaluates the Newton form with the given divided-difference
coefficients and nodes at x using Horner-like nesting.

funcs.name: NodePolynomial
funcs.doc:
NodePolynomial evaluates the node polynomial w(x) = prod_i (x - nodes[i]) at
x. Its size governs the interpolation error.

funcs.name: PolyAdd
funcs.doc:
PolyAdd returns the sum of two monomial polynomials. The result has the
length of the longer input.

funcs.name: PolyCompose
funcs.doc:
PolyCompose returns the coefficients of the composition p(q(x)) for two
monomial polynomials.

funcs.name: PolyDegree
funcs.doc:
PolyDegree returns the degree of a monomial polynomial, ignoring trailing
(high-order) coefficients that are exactly zero. The zero polynomial has
degree 0.

funcs.name: PolyDeriv
funcs.doc: PolyDeriv returns the derivative of a monomial polynomial.

funcs.name: PolyEqual
funcs.signature: func PolyEqual(a, b []float64, tol float64) bool
funcs.doc:
PolyEqual reports whether two monomial polynomials are equal within the
absolute tolerance tol, ignoring trailing zero coefficients.

funcs.name: PolyFromRoots
funcs.doc:
PolyFromRoots returns the monic monomial polynomial whose roots are exactly
the supplied values, i.e. product over i of (x - roots[i]).

funcs.name: PolyInt
funcs.doc:
PolyInt returns an antiderivative of a monomial polynomial whose constant
term equals the supplied value.

funcs.name: PolyIsZero
funcs.doc: PolyIsZero reports whether every coefficient is exactly zero.

funcs.name: PolyLeadingCoeff
funcs.doc:
PolyLeadingCoeff returns the coefficient of the highest-order nonzero term,
or 0 for the zero polynomial.

funcs.name: PolyMul
funcs.doc: PolyMul returns the product of two monomial polynomials by convolution.

funcs.name: PolyNormalize
funcs.doc:
PolyNormalize returns a copy of coeffs with trailing zero coefficients
removed; the zero polynomial is returned as a single zero coefficient.

funcs.name: PolyScale
funcs.doc: PolyScale returns the polynomial a scaled by the constant s.

funcs.name: PolyShift
funcs.doc: PolyShift returns the coefficients of p(x + h).

funcs.name: PolyString
funcs.doc:
PolyString renders a monomial polynomial as a human readable expression in
the variable x, e.g. "1 + 2*x - 3*x^2".

funcs.name: PolySub
funcs.doc: PolySub returns the difference a-b of two monomial polynomials.

funcs.name: Polyval
funcs.doc:
Polyval evaluates the polynomial with the given monomial coefficients
(ascending order, coeffs[i] multiplies x**i) at x using Horner's method.

funcs.name: PolyvalSlice
funcs.doc:
PolyvalSlice evaluates a monomial polynomial at every point in xs and
returns the resulting slice.

funcs.name: RMSError
funcs.doc: RMSError returns the root-mean-square difference between paired samples.

funcs.name: RSquared
funcs.doc:
RSquared returns the coefficient of determination for the fit of a monomial
polynomial to the data (xs, ys).

funcs.name: RelativeError
funcs.signature: func RelativeError(approx, exact, eps float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RelativeError returns |approx-exact| / max(|exact|, eps) guarding against
division by zero with the small floor eps.

funcs.name: RungeFunction
funcs.doc:
RungeFunction evaluates Runge's function 1/(1+25 x^2), the classic example
where high-degree equispaced interpolation diverges.

funcs.name: TaylorAtan
funcs.doc: TaylorAtan returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of atan about 0.

funcs.name: TaylorCos
funcs.doc: TaylorCos returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of cos about 0.

funcs.name: TaylorCosh
funcs.doc: TaylorCosh returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of cosh about 0.

funcs.name: TaylorExp
funcs.doc: TaylorExp returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of exp about 0.

funcs.name: TaylorExpm1
funcs.doc: TaylorExpm1 returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of exp(x)-1 about 0.

funcs.name: TaylorGeometric
funcs.doc:
TaylorGeometric returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of 1/(1-x), all
equal to one.

funcs.name: TaylorLog1p
funcs.doc: TaylorLog1p returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of log(1+x) about 0.

funcs.name: TaylorSin
funcs.doc: TaylorSin returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of sin about 0.

funcs.name: TaylorSinh
funcs.doc: TaylorSinh returns the first n+1 Taylor coefficients of sinh about 0.

funcs.name: ThieleInterp
funcs.signature: func ThieleInterp(xs, ys []float64, x float64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
ThieleInterp is a convenience wrapper that builds a Thiele interpolant from
(xs, ys) and evaluates it at a single point x.

examples.name: ClenshawCurtisQuadrature
examples.code:
got := ClenshawCurtisQuadrature(math.Exp, 20, -1, 1)
fmt.Printf("%.10f\n", got)
examples.doc: Example integrates exp over [-1,1] with Clenshaw-Curtis quadrature.

examples.name: ChebFit
examples.code:
s := ChebFit(math.Exp, 15, -1, 1)
fmt.Printf("%.10f\n", s.Eval(0.5))
examples.doc: Example demonstrates fitting exp with a Chebyshev series and evaluating it.

examples.name: PadeApprox
examples.code:
p, _ := PadeApprox(TaylorExp(2), 1, 1)
fmt.Println(p.Num, p.Den)
examples.doc: Example shows a [1/1] Pade approximant of the exponential function.

examples.name: RemezPoly
examples.code:
r, _ := RemezPoly(func(x float64) float64 { return x * x }, 1, -1, 1, 100, 1e-6)
fmt.Printf("%.4f %.4f\n", r.Coeffs[0], r.Error)
examples.doc: Example computes the best degree-1 minimax polynomial of x^2 on [-1,1].

packages.name: autodiff
packages.synopsis: Package autodiff implements automatic differentiation in pure Go using only the standard library.
packages.doc:
Package autodiff implements automatic differentiation in pure Go using only
the standard library.

Automatic differentiation (AD) evaluates the exact derivative of a function
specified by ordinary program code, up to floating-point round-off. Unlike
finite differences it introduces no truncation error and needs no step size,
and unlike symbolic differentiation it does not build an expression graph
that grows with the algebra. The package provides three complementary
engines:

  - Forward mode via [Dual] numbers. A dual number carries a value together
    with its derivative along a chosen direction. Evaluating a function on a
    seeded dual number yields the value and the directional derivative in a
    single pass. Forward mode is efficient when the number of inputs is small
    relative to the number of outputs and underlies [Derivative], [Gradient],
    [Jacobian], [DirectionalDerivative] and [PartialDerivative].

  - Second-order forward mode via [HyperDual] numbers. A hyper-dual number
    carries a value, two first-order derivative slots and one mixed
    second-order slot, so a single evaluation returns both first and second
    derivatives with no cancellation error. This underlies [SecondDerivative],
    [Hessian], [GradientHessian] and [HessianVectorProduct].

  - Reverse mode via a small [Tape]. The tape records every elementary
    operation as it executes, then propagates adjoints backward from the
    output to every input in one sweep, yielding the whole gradient of a
    scalar function at the cost of roughly one function evaluation regardless
    of the number of inputs.

All routines are deterministic and free of global mutable state (each [Tape]
is independent), so results are reproducible across runs and safe to use from
distinct goroutines that operate on distinct tapes.

# Seeding

Forward-mode derivatives are obtained by seeding: the derivative slot of the
independent variable is set to one (see [Variable]) while constants keep a
zero slot (see [Constant]). The chain rule then transports the seed through
every elementary operation, and the derivative appears in the output's slot.

types.name: Dual
types.doc:
Dual is a forward-mode dual number of the form Val + Der*ε, where ε is a
nilpotent infinitesimal satisfying ε² = 0. Evaluating a function f on the
dual number x = a + ε yields f(a) + f'(a)*ε, so the Der field of the result
