SqrtCFExpand returns the first count partial quotients of the continued
fraction of sqrt(n).

funcs.name: SqrtTwoCF
funcs.doc:
SqrtTwoCF returns the first n partial quotients of the continued fraction of
sqrt(2) = [1; 2, 2, 2, ...].

methods.name: Canonical
methods.doc:
Canonical returns the canonical form of the continued fraction: a trailing
term equal to one is absorbed into the preceding term ([..., x, 1] becomes
[..., x+1]), so that finite expansions have a unique representative (except
for the single-term expansion [1]).

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of c.

methods.name: Compare
methods.doc: Compare compares the values of c and d, returning -1, 0 or +1.

methods.name: Convergent
methods.doc:
Convergent returns the k-th convergent (0-indexed) as a reduced [Frac]. It
panics if k is out of range.

methods.name: Convergents
methods.doc:
Convergents returns all convergents p0/q0, p1/q1, ... of the continued
fraction as reduced fractions.

methods.name: ConvergentsBig
methods.doc:
ConvergentsBig returns all convergents as *big.Rat, correct for expansions of
any length.

methods.name: Denominators
methods.doc: Denominators returns the denominators of the successive convergents.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether c and d have identical partial quotients.

methods.name: EqualValue
methods.doc:
EqualValue reports whether c and d denote the same rational number, comparing
their canonical forms.

methods.name: Frac
methods.doc:
Frac evaluates the continued fraction exactly to a reduced [Frac] via the
convergent recurrence. Overflow is possible for very long expansions; use
[CF.Rat] for arbitrary precision.

methods.name: IsValid
methods.doc:
IsValid reports whether c is a well-formed expansion: it is non-empty and
every partial quotient after the first is a positive integer.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of partial quotients.

methods.name: Numerators
methods.doc: Numerators returns the numerators of the successive convergents.

methods.name: Rat
methods.doc:
Rat evaluates the continued fraction exactly to a *big.Rat, correct for
expansions of any length.

methods.name: Reverse
methods.doc:
Reverse returns the continued fraction with its partial quotients in reverse
order. Reversal leaves the numerator of the final convergent unchanged and is
a classical symmetry of continuants.

methods.name: Semiconvergents
methods.doc:
Semiconvergents returns the sequence of intermediate fractions generated
along the way to the value: for each partial quotient a_i and each
m = 1, ..., a_i the fraction (m*h_{i-1}+h_{i-2})/(m*k_{i-1}+k_{i-2}). These
are the best rational approximations of the first kind and trace the
Stern-Brocot path to the value.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the continued fraction in the usual bracket notation
"[a0; a1, a2, ...]" (or "[a0]" for a single term).

methods.name: Terms
methods.doc: Terms returns a copy of the underlying slice of partial quotients.

methods.name: Truncate
methods.doc:
Truncate returns the continued fraction consisting of the first n partial
quotients. If n exceeds the length the whole CF is returned.

methods.name: Value
methods.doc:
Value evaluates the continued fraction as a float64 using stable backward
recurrence, which avoids the overflow that forward convergent recurrence can
suffer for long expansions.

types.name: Frac
types.doc:
Frac is an exact rational number with an int64 numerator and denominator.
The zero value Frac{} is 0/0 and is not a valid number; use [NewFrac] or the
package constructors to build values. Most methods assume a non-zero
denominator and, for canonical results, a reduced representation with a
positive denominator (which [NewFrac] and [Frac.Reduce] guarantee).

funcs.name: BestApproximationFrac
funcs.signature: func BestApproximationFrac(x float64, maxDen int64) Frac
funcs.doc: BestApproximationFrac returns [BestApproximation] as a reduced [Frac].

funcs.name: BestApproximations
funcs.doc:
BestApproximations returns the sequence of convergents of x whose
denominators do not exceed maxDen. Each convergent is a best rational
approximation of the second kind, so the list gives successively better
approximations with growing denominators.

funcs.name: Convergent
funcs.signature: func Convergent(cf CF, k int) Frac
funcs.doc: Convergent is a free-function form of [CF.Convergent].

funcs.name: Convergents
funcs.doc: Convergents is a free-function form of [CF.Convergents].

funcs.name: EApprox
funcs.doc:
EApprox returns the n-th convergent of e (using n partial quotients) as a
single best rational approximation.

funcs.name: EConvergents
funcs.doc: EConvergents returns the first n convergents of e as reduced fractions.

funcs.name: FareyNeighbors
funcs.signature: func FareyNeighbors(a, b, n int64) (prev, next Frac)
funcs.doc:
FareyNeighbors returns the predecessor and successor of a/b in F_n. If a/b is
an endpoint (0/1 or 1/1) the missing neighbour is returned as the endpoint
itself.

funcs.name: FareyNext
funcs.signature: func FareyNext(a, b, c, d, n int64) Frac
funcs.doc:
FareyNext returns the term after c/d given the two consecutive Farey terms
a/b and c/d of F_n, using the standard mediant recurrence
k = floor((n+b)/d), next = (k*c-a)/(k*d-b).

funcs.name: FareyPredecessor
funcs.signature: func FareyPredecessor(a, b, n int64) Frac
funcs.doc:
FareyPredecessor returns the term immediately before a/b in the Farey
sequence F_n. The fraction a/b must be reduced with 0 < a/b <= 1 and b <= n.
It panics if a/b == 0/1, which has no predecessor.

funcs.name: FareySequence
funcs.doc:
FareySequence returns the Farey sequence F_n: all reduced fractions in the
closed interval [0, 1] with denominator at most n, listed in ascending order
from 0/1 to 1/1. It panics for n < 1.

funcs.name: FareySuccessor
funcs.signature: func FareySuccessor(a, b, n int64) Frac
funcs.doc:
FareySuccessor returns the term immediately after a/b in the Farey sequence
F_n. The fraction a/b must be reduced with 0 <= a/b < 1 and b <= n. It panics
if a/b == 1/1, which has no successor in F_n.

funcs.name: FracFromFloat
funcs.doc:
FracFromFloat returns the exact Frac whose value equals x, provided x is a
dyadic rational representable in an int64 numerator and denominator, together
with ok. When x cannot be represented exactly (it needs more than 62 bits or
is non-finite), ok is false. For a best approximation with a bounded
denominator use [BestApproximationFrac] instead.

funcs.name: MediantFrac
funcs.doc:
MediantFrac returns the mediant of two fractions as a [Frac] (not reduced,
mirroring the Stern-Brocot construction).

funcs.name: NewFrac
funcs.doc:
NewFrac returns the fraction n/d reduced to lowest terms with a positive
denominator. It panics if d == 0.

funcs.name: PiApprox
funcs.doc:
PiApprox returns the continued-fraction convergent of pi obtained from n
partial quotients as a single best rational approximation.

funcs.name: PiConvergents
funcs.doc:
PiConvergents returns the first n convergents of pi as reduced fractions,
including the classical 22/7 and 355/113.

funcs.name: Rationalize
funcs.signature: func Rationalize(x float64, maxDen int64) Frac
funcs.doc:
Rationalize is an alias for [BestApproximationFrac]: it returns the simplest
good rational approximation of x with denominator at most maxDen.

funcs.name: SmallestDenominatorWithin
funcs.doc:
SmallestDenominatorWithin returns the fraction with the smallest possible
denominator whose value lies within eps of x. It searches increasing
denominator bounds using the continued-fraction best-approximation routine.
eps must be positive.

funcs.name: SqrtConvergent
funcs.signature: func SqrtConvergent(n int64, k int) Frac
funcs.doc: SqrtConvergent returns the k-th convergent (0-indexed) of sqrt(n).

funcs.name: SqrtConvergents
funcs.doc:
SqrtConvergents returns the first count convergents of sqrt(n) as reduced
fractions.

funcs.name: SternBrocotAncestors
funcs.doc:
SternBrocotAncestors returns the fractions on the path from the root down to
(but not including) p/q, in order from the root.

funcs.name: SternBrocotChildren
funcs.signature: func SternBrocotChildren(p, q int64) (left, right Frac)
funcs.doc: SternBrocotChildren returns both children (left, right) of p/q.

funcs.name: SternBrocotFromPath
funcs.doc:
SternBrocotFromPath returns the fraction reached by following the given
L/R path from the root of the Stern-Brocot tree. Invalid characters are
ignored. The empty path yields 1/1.

funcs.name: SternBrocotLeftChild
funcs.doc: SternBrocotLeftChild returns the left child of p/q in the Stern-Brocot tree.

funcs.name: SternBrocotParent
funcs.doc:
SternBrocotParent returns the parent of p/q in the Stern-Brocot tree. The
parent of the root 1/1 is itself.

funcs.name: SternBrocotRightChild
funcs.doc: SternBrocotRightChild returns the right child of p/q in the Stern-Brocot tree.

funcs.name: UnitFraction
funcs.doc: UnitFraction returns the unit fraction 1/d as a [Frac]. It panics if d == 0.

methods.name: Abs
methods.doc: Abs returns |f|.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the reduced sum f + g.

methods.name: Ceil
methods.doc: Ceil returns the least integer not less than f.

methods.name: Cmp
methods.doc:
Cmp compares f and g, returning -1 if f < g, 0 if they are equal and +1 if
f > g. The comparison is exact for inputs whose cross products fit in int64.

methods.name: Div
methods.doc: Div returns the reduced quotient f / g. It panics if g is zero.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether f and g represent the same rational number.

methods.name: Float
methods.doc: Float returns the value of f as a float64.

methods.name: Floor
methods.doc: Floor returns the greatest integer not exceeding f.

methods.name: Inv
methods.doc: Inv returns the reciprocal 1/f. It panics if f is zero.

methods.name: IsInteger
methods.doc: IsInteger reports whether f is an integer.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether f == 0.

methods.name: Less
methods.doc: Less reports whether f < g.

methods.name: Mediant
methods.doc:
Mediant returns the mediant (f.Num+g.Num)/(f.Den+g.Den) of f and g. The
mediant of two reduced fractions lies strictly between them.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the reduced product f * g.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns -f.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns f raised to the integer power n (which may be negative). It
panics for a negative power of zero.

methods.name: Rat
methods.doc: Rat returns f as a *big.Rat.

methods.name: Reduce
methods.doc:
Reduce returns an equivalent fraction in lowest terms with a positive
denominator. It panics if the denominator is zero.

methods.name: Round
methods.doc:
Round returns f rounded to the nearest integer, rounding halves away from
zero.

methods.name: Sign
methods.doc:
Sign returns -1, 0 or +1 according to the sign of f (assuming a positive
denominator, as produced by the constructors).

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders f as "p/q", or just "p" when the denominator is 1.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the reduced difference f - g.

types.name: PeriodicCF
types.doc:
PeriodicCF is an eventually periodic continued fraction consisting of a
non-repeating Head followed by an infinitely repeated Period. When Period is
empty the value is the rational number denoted by Head.

funcs.name: SqrtCF
funcs.doc:
SqrtCF returns the continued fraction of sqrt(n) for n >= 0 as a PeriodicCF.
For a perfect square the period is empty. Otherwise the head is the single
term floor(sqrt(n)) and the period is the repeating tail, which is known to
be a palindrome followed by 2*floor(sqrt(n)). It panics for n < 0.

methods.name: Convergents
methods.doc: Convergents returns the first n convergents of the expansion.

methods.name: Expand
methods.doc:
Expand returns the first n partial quotients of the (infinite) continued
fraction: the head followed by as many copies of the period as needed.

methods.name: HeadLength
methods.doc: HeadLength returns the length of the non-repeating prefix.

methods.name: IsPurelyPeriodic
methods.doc:
IsPurelyPeriodic reports whether the expansion has an empty head and a
non-empty period, so the whole continued fraction repeats from the start.

methods.name: IsRational
methods.doc: IsRational reports whether the expansion terminates (empty period).

methods.name: PeriodLength
methods.doc: PeriodLength returns the length of the repeating block (0 for a rational).

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the expansion as "[h0; h1, ..., (p0, p1, ...)]" with the
period parenthesised.

methods.name: Surd
methods.doc:
Surd returns the quadratic surd represented by the periodic continued
fraction, obtained by solving the quadratic fixed-point equation of the
period and then folding in the head terms. For a rational expansion the
result has D == 0. It panics if the expansion is neither rational nor
eventually periodic (which cannot happen for values built by this package).

methods.name: Terms
methods.doc: Terms is an alias for Expand returning a plain []int64.

methods.name: Value
methods.doc:
Value returns the numeric value of the periodic continued fraction as a
float64. For a rational (empty period) it is exact up to float rounding; for
a quadratic irrational it is accurate to full float64 precision.

types.name: QuadraticSurd
types.doc:
QuadraticSurd represents the real number (P + sqrt(D))/Q with integer P, Q, D
and D >= 0, Q != 0. Every quadratic irrational (and every rational) can be
written in this form, and by Lagrange's theorem its continued fraction is
eventually periodic; see [QuadraticSurd.CF].

funcs.name: NewSurd
funcs.signature: func NewSurd(P, Q, D int64) QuadraticSurd
funcs.doc:
NewSurd returns the quadratic surd (P + sqrt(D))/Q. It panics if Q == 0 or
D < 0.

methods.name: CF
methods.doc:
CF returns the eventually periodic continued fraction of the surd. If the
surd is rational the returned PeriodicCF has an empty period.

methods.name: Conjugate
methods.doc:
Conjugate returns the algebraic conjugate (P - sqrt(D))/Q, written in the
canonical form with a non-negative radical by negating both P and Q.

methods.name: Floor
methods.doc:
Floor returns the greatest integer not exceeding the surd, computed exactly
with integer arithmetic (no floating-point rounding error).

methods.name: IsRational
methods.doc:
IsRational reports whether the surd is actually a rational number, i.e. D is a
perfect square.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the surd as "(P + sqrt(D))/Q".

methods.name: Value
methods.doc: Value returns the surd as a float64.

funcs.name: AreFareyNeighbors
funcs.signature: func AreFareyNeighbors(a, b, c, d int64) bool
funcs.doc:
AreFareyNeighbors reports whether a/b and c/d are adjacent in some Farey
sequence, i.e. |b*c - a*d| == 1.

funcs.name: BestApproximation
funcs.signature: func BestApproximation(x float64, maxDen int64) (int64, int64)
funcs.doc:
BestApproximation returns the rational p/q closest to x whose denominator q
satisfies 1 <= q <= maxDen. Among equally close fractions the one with the
smaller denominator is returned. It uses the continued-fraction algorithm, so
the answer is always a convergent or a best semiconvergent of x. maxDen must
be at least 1.

funcs.name: BestApproximationRat
funcs.doc:
BestApproximationRat returns the rational closest to the target *big.Rat whose
denominator does not exceed maxDen, computed exactly from the continued
fraction of the target.

funcs.name: CFToPath
funcs.doc:
CFToPath converts a continued fraction of a positive value into the
corresponding Stern-Brocot L/R path. The moves alternate R, L, R, ... with
run lengths given by the partial quotients, the final term being decremented
by one (so [1; 2] -> "RL"). Both representations of the value (with the last
term reduced or not) map to the same path.

funcs.name: Continuant
funcs.doc:
Continuant returns the continuant K(a0, a1, ..., a_{n-1}), the polynomial
that equals the numerator of the convergent of the continued fraction with
those partial quotients. The empty continuant is 1 and K(a0) is a0.

funcs.name: ContinuantBig
funcs.doc: ContinuantBig is the arbitrary-precision form of [Continuant].

funcs.name: ConvergentError
funcs.signature: func ConvergentError(cf CF, k int) float64
funcs.doc:
ConvergentError returns the absolute difference between the value of the full
continued fraction and its k-th convergent, evaluated in floating point.

funcs.name: ECFTerm
funcs.doc:
ECFTerm returns the k-th partial quotient (0-indexed) of the continued
fraction of Euler's number e = [2; 1, 2, 1, 1, 4, 1, 1, 6, 1, 1, 8, ...].
After the leading 2 the terms follow the pattern 1, 2m, 1 for m = 1, 2, 3, ...

funcs.name: EgyptianFraction
funcs.doc:
EgyptianFraction returns the denominators of a sum of distinct unit fractions
equal to the proper fraction p/q (with 0 < p/q < 1) using Fibonacci's greedy
algorithm. Each returned denominator d contributes 1/d, and the denominators
are strictly increasing. It returns an error if p/q is not a proper fraction.

funcs.name: EgyptianFractionBig
funcs.doc:
EgyptianFractionBig returns the denominators of a distinct-unit-fraction
decomposition of the proper fraction r using the greedy algorithm, with
arbitrary-precision denominators so it never overflows. It returns an error
if r is not in the open interval (0, 1).

funcs.name: EgyptianFractionFrac
funcs.doc: EgyptianFractionFrac is the [Frac] form of [EgyptianFraction].

funcs.name: EulerPhi
funcs.doc:
EulerPhi returns Euler's totient of n for n >= 1: the count of integers in
[1, n] coprime to n. EulerPhi of a non-positive number is 0.

funcs.name: Evaluate
funcs.doc: Evaluate is a free-function form of [CF.Value].

funcs.name: EvaluateRat
funcs.doc: EvaluateRat is a free-function form of [CF.Rat].

funcs.name: FareyLength
funcs.doc:
FareyLength returns the number of terms in the Farey sequence F_n, which is
1 + sum_{k=1}^{n} phi(k). It panics for n < 1.

funcs.name: GCD
funcs.doc:
GCD returns the non-negative greatest common divisor of a and b using the
Euclidean algorithm. GCD(0, 0) is 0.

funcs.name: GoldenRatio
funcs.doc: GoldenRatio returns the golden ratio (1 + sqrt(5))/2 as a float64.

funcs.name: IsPellSolution
funcs.signature: func IsPellSolution(D, x, y, n int64) bool
funcs.doc: IsPellSolution reports whether (x, y) satisfies x^2 - D*y^2 == n.

funcs.name: IsPerfectSquare
funcs.doc:
IsPerfectSquare reports whether n is a perfect square. Negative numbers are
never perfect squares.

funcs.name: IsPeriodPalindrome
funcs.doc:
IsPeriodPalindrome reports whether the given period (excluding its final
element) reads the same forwards and backwards, the classical structure of
the period of sqrt(n). An empty or single-element period is trivially a
palindrome.

funcs.name: IsUnitFraction
funcs.doc: IsUnitFraction reports whether p/q reduces to 1/n for some positive integer n.

funcs.name: Isqrt
funcs.doc:
Isqrt returns the integer square root floor(sqrt(n)) for n >= 0 using a
Newton iteration that is exact for every non-negative int64. It panics for
negative n.

funcs.name: LCM
funcs.doc:
LCM returns the least common multiple of a and b as a non-negative value.
LCM(a, 0) and LCM(0, b) are 0.

funcs.name: Mediant
funcs.signature: func Mediant(a, b, c, d int64) (int64, int64)
funcs.doc:
Mediant returns the mediant (a+c)/(b+d) of the fractions a/b and c/d as an
unreduced numerator and denominator. The mediant of two fractions lies
strictly between them when they are distinct.

funcs.name: MediantRat
funcs.doc: MediantRat returns the mediant of two *big.Rat values.

funcs.name: PellFundamental
funcs.signature: func PellFundamental(D int64) (x, y *big.Int, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
PellFundamental returns the fundamental (smallest positive) solution (x, y) of
the Pell equation x^2 - D*y^2 = 1 for a non-square D > 1, together with ok. If
D is a perfect square or D <= 0 there is no non-trivial solution and ok is
false. The solution is derived from the continued fraction of sqrt(D).

funcs.name: PellFundamentalInt
funcs.signature: func PellFundamentalInt(D int64) (x, y int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
PellFundamentalInt returns the fundamental solution of x^2 - D*y^2 = 1 as
int64 values when they fit, together with ok. ok is false when D admits no
solution or the fundamental solution overflows int64.

funcs.name: PellNegative
funcs.signature: func PellNegative(D int64) (x, y *big.Int, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
PellNegative returns the fundamental solution (x, y) of the negative Pell
equation x^2 - D*y^2 = -1, together with ok. Such a solution exists precisely
when the period of the continued fraction of sqrt(D) has odd length; ok is
false otherwise.

funcs.name: PellNegativeSolvable
funcs.doc:
PellNegativeSolvable reports whether x^2 - D*y^2 = -1 has a solution, i.e. the
period of sqrt(D) has odd length.

funcs.name: PellNthSolution
funcs.signature: func PellNthSolution(D int64, n int) (x, y *big.Int, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
PellNthSolution returns the n-th positive solution (n >= 1) of
x^2 - D*y^2 = 1, with n == 1 the fundamental solution. It returns ok == false
when D admits no solution or n < 1.

funcs.name: PellSolutions
funcs.doc:
PellSolutions returns the first count positive solutions of x^2 - D*y^2 = 1,
generated from the fundamental solution by the recurrence
x_{k+1} = x1*x_k + D*y1*y_k, y_{k+1} = x1*y_k + y1*x_k. It returns nil when D
admits no solution.

funcs.name: PiFloat
funcs.doc:
PiFloat returns pi computed to roughly the requested number of decimal digits
as a *big.Float. digits must be positive.

funcs.name: ReduceFraction
funcs.doc:
ReduceFraction returns p/q in lowest terms with a positive denominator.
It panics if q == 0.

funcs.name: SqrtCFPeriod
funcs.doc:
SqrtCFPeriod returns just the repeating block of the continued fraction of
sqrt(n) (nil for a perfect square).

funcs.name: SqrtCFPeriodLength
funcs.doc:
SqrtCFPeriodLength returns the length of the period of the continued fraction
of sqrt(n) (0 for a perfect square).

funcs.name: SternBrocotDepth
funcs.doc:
SternBrocotDepth returns the depth of p/q in the Stern-Brocot tree (the root
1/1 has depth 0).

funcs.name: SternBrocotPath
funcs.doc:
SternBrocotPath returns the path from the root 1/1 of the Stern-Brocot tree
to the positive reduced fraction p/q, encoded as a string of 'L' (left) and
'R' (right) moves. The empty string denotes the root itself. It panics if
p/q is not positive.

funcs.name: SumUnitFractions
funcs.doc:
SumUnitFractions returns the exact sum of the unit fractions 1/d for each d in
dens as a *big.Rat. A denominator of zero is skipped.

examples.name: BestApproximation
EgyptianFraction
PellFundamental
SternBrocotPath
FromRational
PiCF
FareySequence
SqrtCF

packages.name: controltheory
packages.synopsis: Package controltheory provides classical linear control-systems primitives implemented with the Go standard library only.
packages.doc:
Package controltheory provides classical linear control-systems primitives
implemented with the Go standard library only.

The package models single-input single-output (SISO) linear time-invariant
systems in two equivalent representations:

  - Transfer functions G(s) = N(s)/D(s), where N and D are real polynomials
    stored as [Poly] values.
  - State-space realizations (A, B, C, D) stored as [StateSpace] values.

On top of these representations it offers block-diagram algebra
(series, parallel, feedback), pole/zero extraction, time-domain
step and impulse responses computed by numerical integration,
controllability and observability analysis, Routh-Hurwitz stability
testing, frequency-domain Bode and Nyquist sampling with gain and phase
margins, and a discrete PID controller.

Polynomials use the ascending-power convention: a [Poly] value p has
p[i] as the coefficient of s^i, so Poly{2, 3, 1} represents 2 + 3s + s^2.

Every routine is deterministic and depends on nothing outside the standard
library.

types.name: BodePoint
types.doc: BodePoint is a single sample of a Bode plot at one angular frequency.

types.name: NyquistPoint
types.doc: NyquistPoint is a single sample of a Nyquist plot at one angular frequency.

types.name: PIDController
types.doc:
PIDController is a discrete-time proportional-integral-derivative controller
with configurable gains. It maintains internal state (the accumulated
integral and the previous error) so successive calls to Update implement the
standard incremental control law.

funcs.name: NewPIDController
funcs.doc:
NewPIDController returns a PIDController with the given proportional,
integral, and derivative gains and zero internal state.

funcs.name: ZieglerNicholsPID
funcs.doc:
ZieglerNicholsPID returns PID gains tuned by the classical Ziegler-Nichols
ultimate-sensitivity rule from the ultimate gain ku (the proportional gain at
which the loop sustains oscillation) and the ultimate period pu (the
oscillation period, in seconds). The returned controller uses
Kp = 0.6·ku, Ki = Kp/(0.5·pu), Kd = Kp·0.125·pu.

methods.name: Integral
methods.doc: Integral returns the current accumulated integral of the error.

methods.name: Reset
methods.doc:
Reset clears the accumulated integral term and derivative history so the
controller behaves as if freshly constructed.

methods.name: TransferFunction
methods.doc:
TransferFunction returns the ideal continuous-time transfer function of the
controller, C(s) = Kp + Ki/s + Kd·s = (Kd·s^2 + Kp·s + Ki) / s.

methods.name: Update
methods.doc:
Update advances the controller by one time step of length dt given the
current error (setpoint minus measurement) and returns the control output

	u = Kp·e + Ki·∫e dt + Kd·de/dt

The integral is accumulated with the rectangle rule and the derivative uses a
backward difference. On the first call after construction or Reset the
derivative term is taken as zero. It panics if dt is not positive.

types.name: Poly
types.signature: type Poly []float64
types.doc:
Poly represents a real polynomial in the Laplace variable s using the
ascending-power convention: element i is the coefficient of s^i. For example
Poly{6, 5, 1} is the polynomial 6 + 5s + s^2. The zero-length polynomial and
a polynomial of all zeros both represent the constant 0.

funcs.name: NewPoly
funcs.doc:
NewPoly returns a Poly built from the given ascending-power coefficients.
coeffs[i] becomes the coefficient of s^i. The slice is copied so later
mutation of the argument does not affect the result.

funcs.name: PolyFromRoots
funcs.doc:
PolyFromRoots returns the monic real polynomial whose roots are the given
complex values. Roots are expected to appear in conjugate pairs so the
resulting coefficients are real; any residual imaginary parts from rounding
are discarded.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the sum p + q as a new polynomial.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree returns the degree of the polynomial, i.e. the highest power of s
with a nonzero coefficient. The zero polynomial has degree 0 by convention.

methods.name: Derivative
methods.doc: Derivative returns the derivative dp/ds as a new polynomial.

methods.name: DivMod
methods.signature: func (p Poly) DivMod(q Poly) (quotient, remainder Poly)
methods.doc:
DivMod divides p by q and returns the quotient and remainder polynomials
such that p = quotient*q + remainder with degree(remainder) < degree(q).
It panics if q is the zero polynomial.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the polynomial at the real point x using Horner's method.

methods.name: EvalComplex
methods.doc:
EvalComplex evaluates the polynomial at the complex point z using Horner's
method, which is the form needed for frequency-response calculations.

methods.name: LeadingCoeff
methods.doc:
LeadingCoeff returns the coefficient of the highest nonzero power of s.
For the zero polynomial it returns 0.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the product p*q. Polynomial multiplication is the convolution of
the two coefficient sequences.

methods.name: Roots
methods.doc:
Roots returns all complex roots of the polynomial using the Durand-Kerner
(Weierstrass) iteration with deterministic starting points. Real roots are
returned with a zero (or negligible) imaginary part. The zero polynomial and
nonzero constants have no roots and return an empty slice.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns the polynomial with every coefficient multiplied by k.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the difference p - q as a new polynomial.

types.name: RouthResult
types.doc:
RouthResult holds the outcome of a Routh-Hurwitz stability analysis of a
characteristic polynomial.

funcs.name: RouthHurwitz
funcs.doc:
RouthHurwitz performs the Routh-Hurwitz stability test on the given
characteristic polynomial (ascending-power [Poly]). It builds the Routh
array, counts sign changes in the first column, and reports stability. A
zero appearing in the first column is replaced by a small positive epsilon so
the tabulation can proceed. The polynomial must have degree at least 1.

types.name: StateSpace
types.doc:
StateSpace is a SISO continuous-time state-space realization

	x' = A x + B u
	y  = C x + D u

where A is n×n, B is n×1, C is 1×n, and D is a scalar. Matrices are stored
as row-major slices of slices.

funcs.name: NewStateSpace
funcs.signature: func NewStateSpace(a [][]float64, b, c []float64, d float64) StateSpace
funcs.doc:
NewStateSpace constructs a StateSpace from the given matrices. The slices are
copied. It panics if the dimensions are inconsistent (A must be square and
B, C must match its size).

funcs.name: TransferFunctionToStateSpace
funcs.doc:
TransferFunctionToStateSpace converts a proper transfer function into a
state-space realization in controllable canonical form. Any direct
feedthrough (when the numerator and denominator have equal degree) is placed
in the D term. It panics if the transfer function is not proper or the
denominator is the zero polynomial.

methods.name: CharacteristicPolynomial
methods.doc:
CharacteristicPolynomial returns the characteristic polynomial det(sI - A) of
the state matrix as an ascending-power monic [Poly], computed with the
Faddeev-LeVerrier algorithm.

methods.name: ControllabilityMatrix
methods.doc:
ControllabilityMatrix returns the controllability matrix
[B, AB, A^2 B, ..., A^(n-1) B], an n×n matrix for a SISO system.

methods.name: ControllabilityRank
methods.doc: ControllabilityRank returns the rank of the controllability matrix.

methods.name: ImpulseResponse
methods.doc:
ImpulseResponse returns the unit-impulse response C·e^{At}·B of the
state-space system sampled at the given time points, obtained by simulating
the autonomous dynamics from initial state B. Any direct feedthrough D
contributes only at t=0 (a Dirac impulse) and is not included in the samples.

methods.name: IsControllable
methods.doc:
IsControllable reports whether the system is completely state controllable,
i.e. the controllability matrix has full rank n.

methods.name: IsObservable
methods.doc:
IsObservable reports whether the system is completely observable, i.e. the
observability matrix has full rank n.

methods.name: ObservabilityMatrix
methods.doc:
ObservabilityMatrix returns the observability matrix
[C; CA; CA^2; ...; CA^(n-1)], an n×n matrix for a SISO system.

methods.name: ObservabilityRank
methods.doc: ObservabilityRank returns the rank of the observability matrix.

methods.name: Order
methods.doc: Order returns the number of states n, the dimension of the A matrix.

methods.name: Poles
methods.doc:
Poles returns the poles of the realization, i.e. the eigenvalues of A, found
as the roots of the characteristic polynomial.

methods.name: Simulate
methods.signature: func (s StateSpace) Simulate(times []float64, x0 []float64, input func(t float64) float64) []float64
methods.doc:
Simulate integrates the state-space system forward from initial state x0
over the given monotonically increasing time points using the classical
fourth-order Runge-Kutta method. The scalar input at time t is supplied by
the function input. It returns the output y sampled at each time point.
The returned slice has the same length as times.

methods.name: StepResponse
methods.doc:
StepResponse returns the unit-step response of the state-space system from
rest, sampled at the given time points.

methods.name: TransferFunction
methods.doc:
TransferFunction converts the state-space realization to an equivalent SISO
transfer function G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D using the Faddeev-LeVerrier
algorithm.

types.name: TransferFunction
types.doc:
TransferFunction is a SISO continuous-time transfer function
G(s) = Num(s) / Den(s) with real polynomial numerator and denominator stored
in the ascending-power convention of [Poly].

funcs.name: Feedback
funcs.signature: func Feedback(g, h TransferFunction, sign int) TransferFunction
funcs.doc:
Feedback returns the closed-loop transfer function of forward path g with
feedback path h. When sign is -1 (negative feedback) the result is
G/(1+G·H); when sign is +1 (positive feedback) it is G/(1-G·H).

funcs.name: NewTransferFunction
funcs.doc:
NewTransferFunction builds a TransferFunction from ascending-power numerator
and denominator coefficient slices. Both slices are copied.

funcs.name: Parallel
funcs.doc:
Parallel returns the parallel connection G(s)+H(s) of two transfer
functions, whose outputs are summed for a common input.

funcs.name: SecondOrderSystem
funcs.doc:
SecondOrderSystem returns the canonical second-order transfer function
wn^2 / (s^2 + 2·zeta·wn·s + wn^2) for natural frequency wn (rad/s) and
damping ratio zeta.

funcs.name: Series
funcs.doc: Series returns the cascade connection G(s)·H(s) of two transfer functions.

funcs.name: UnityFeedback
funcs.doc:
UnityFeedback returns the closed-loop transfer function of forward path g
with unity negative feedback, G/(1+G).

methods.name: Bode
methods.doc:
Bode returns the Bode-plot samples (magnitude in dB and phase in degrees) of
the transfer function at each supplied angular frequency. Phase is computed
with a continuous unwrap so successive samples do not jump by 2π.

methods.name: DCGain
methods.doc:
DCGain returns the steady-state gain G(0) = Num(0)/Den(0). It returns
+Inf when the denominator constant term is zero (a pole at the origin).

methods.name: Evaluate
methods.doc:
Evaluate returns the complex value of G(s) at the complex point s. It returns
a value with infinite magnitude when s is a pole (the denominator vanishes).

methods.name: FinalValue
methods.doc:
FinalValue returns the steady-state value of the step response predicted by
the final-value theorem, lim_{t->inf} y(t) = G(0), when the closed system is
stable. It equals the DC gain of the transfer function.

methods.name: FrequencyResponse
methods.doc:
FrequencyResponse returns G(jω), the complex response at angular frequency
omega (in radians per second).

methods.name: GainCrossoverFrequency
methods.doc:
GainCrossoverFrequency returns the lowest angular frequency in the supplied
grid at which the open-loop magnitude |G(jω)| crosses unity (0 dB), refined
by linear interpolation in the log-magnitude versus log-frequency plane. The
boolean result reports whether such a crossing was found within the grid.

methods.name: GainMargin
methods.doc:
GainMargin returns the gain margin in decibels, defined as -20·log10|G(jω)|
at the phase crossover frequency (where the phase is -180 degrees), evaluated
over the supplied frequency grid. The boolean result reports whether a phase
crossover was found. A positive gain margin indicates a stable closed loop
under unity negative feedback.

methods.name: ImpulseResponse
methods.doc:
ImpulseResponse returns the unit-impulse response of a strictly proper
transfer function sampled at the given monotonically increasing time points.
For a strictly proper realization the impulse response equals C·e^{At}·B, so
it is computed by simulating the autonomous system with initial state B and
zero input. The transfer function must be strictly proper.

methods.name: IsProper
methods.doc:
IsProper reports whether the numerator degree does not exceed the
denominator degree, the condition for a physically realizable system.

methods.name: IsStable
methods.doc:
IsStable reports whether every pole has a strictly negative real part, the
condition for asymptotic stability of a continuous-time system.

methods.name: IsStrictlyProper
methods.doc:
IsStrictlyProper reports whether the numerator degree is strictly less than
the denominator degree.

methods.name: Nyquist
methods.doc:
Nyquist returns the Nyquist-plot samples (real and imaginary parts of G(jω))
of the transfer function at each supplied angular frequency.

methods.name: Order
methods.doc:
Order returns the order of the system, i.e. the degree of the denominator
polynomial.

methods.name: PhaseCrossoverFrequency
methods.doc:
PhaseCrossoverFrequency returns the lowest angular frequency in the supplied
grid at which the open-loop phase crosses -180 degrees, refined by linear
interpolation. The boolean result reports whether such a crossing was found.

methods.name: PhaseMargin
methods.doc:
PhaseMargin returns the phase margin in degrees, defined as 180 plus the
open-loop phase at the gain crossover frequency, evaluated over the supplied
frequency grid. The boolean result reports whether a gain crossover was
found. A positive phase margin indicates a stable closed loop under unity
negative feedback.

methods.name: Poles
methods.doc: Poles returns the poles of the system, i.e. the roots of the denominator.

methods.name: Series
methods.doc:
Series returns the transfer function of g followed by h connected in series
(cascade): the product G(s)·H(s).

methods.name: StepResponse
methods.doc:
StepResponse returns the unit-step response of the transfer function sampled
at the given monotonically increasing time points. The system starts from
rest (zero initial state) and the input is held at 1 for all t >= 0. The
transfer function must be proper.

methods.name: Zeros
methods.doc: Zeros returns the zeros of the system, i.e. the roots of the numerator.

funcs.name: DampingRatio
funcs.doc:
DampingRatio returns the damping ratio of a complex pole, defined as
-Re(p)/|p|. It returns 0 for a pole at the origin.

funcs.name: IsHurwitzStable
funcs.doc:
IsHurwitzStable reports whether the polynomial is Hurwitz stable, i.e. all of
its roots lie strictly in the left half-plane, as determined by the
Routh-Hurwitz test.

funcs.name: LogSpace
funcs.signature: func LogSpace(startExp, endExp float64, n int) []float64
funcs.doc:
LogSpace returns n logarithmically spaced values between 10^startExp and
10^endExp inclusive. It is convenient for generating frequency grids for
Bode and Nyquist sampling. It returns a single-element slice when n is 1 and
an empty slice when n is less than 1.

funcs.name: MagnitudeDB
funcs.doc: MagnitudeDB returns 20·log10(|value|) in decibels.

funcs.name: NaturalFrequency
funcs.doc:
NaturalFrequency returns the undamped natural frequency of a complex pole,
which is its magnitude |p|.

funcs.name: NumRightHalfPlaneRoots
funcs.doc:
NumRightHalfPlaneRoots returns the number of roots of the polynomial with
strictly positive real part, equal to the number of sign changes in the
first column of the Routh array.

funcs.name: PhaseDeg
funcs.doc: PhaseDeg returns the phase angle of value in degrees.

packages.name: crypto
packages.synopsis: Package crypto implements the mathematical primitives that underpin classical public-key cryptography, built exclusively on top of math/big.Int and the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package crypto implements the mathematical primitives that underpin
classical public-key cryptography, built exclusively on top of
math/big.Int and the Go standard library.

The package is deliberately educational and self-contained: it exposes the
number-theoretic machinery directly rather than hiding it behind opaque,
hardened implementations. Nothing here draws on crypto/rand, crypto/rsa or
any other package under crypto/*; every routine is expressed in terms of
arbitrary-precision integer arithmetic so that the algorithms can be read,
tested against closed-form values and reused for teaching.

The following families are covered:

  - modular arithmetic: fast modular exponentiation (ModExp), modular
    inverse, ModMul/ModAdd/ModSub, GCD/LCM, the extended Euclidean
    algorithm and the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT, CRTPair);
  - quadratic residues: the Jacobi and Legendre symbols, a Tonelli-Shanks
    modular square root, multiplicative order and primitive roots;
  - primality: the Fermat and Miller-Rabin tests (both probabilistic and a
    deterministic witness set for moderate inputs), trial division, the
    sieve of Eratosthenes, next/previous prime and random (safe) prime
    generation;
  - factoring: Pollard's rho (classic and Brent variants), full integer
    factorization, Euler's totient and the Carmichael function;
  - discrete logarithms: the baby-step giant-step algorithm;
  - protocols: RSA key generation, encryption, decryption (with a CRT fast
    path) and signatures; Diffie-Hellman key agreement; ElGamal
    encryption; and Shamir's threshold secret sharing.

Randomness. Routines that need randomness accept an explicit *math/rand.Rand
source. This keeps the whole package deterministic and reproducible: seeding
the source identically always yields identical keys, primes and shares. Such
determinism is invaluable for tests and demonstrations but means the package
is NOT suitable for producing real-world secrets. Do not use it to protect
anything of value.

Conventions. Moduli are required to be positive. Functions that cannot
return a meaningful result for out-of-domain integer inputs (a non-positive
modulus, a negative factorial argument and so on) panic with a message
prefixed by "crypto:"; functions whose failure depends on runtime values the
caller cannot cheaply check in advance (a non-invertible element, a discrete
log that does not exist) return an error instead.

vars.signature: var ErrNoDiscreteLog = errors.New("crypto: no discrete logarithm exists")
vars.doc:
ErrNoDiscreteLog is returned when a discrete logarithm does not exist for the
given base, target and modulus.

vars.signature: var ErrNotInvertible = errors.New("crypto: element is not invertible modulo m")
vars.doc:
ErrNotInvertible is returned by ModInverse and related routines when the
element has no multiplicative inverse modulo the given modulus (that is, when
the element and modulus are not coprime).

types.name: DHParams
types.doc:
DHParams holds the public Diffie-Hellman domain parameters shared by both
parties: a prime modulus P and a generator G of a (large) subgroup of the
multiplicative group modulo P.

funcs.name: GenerateDHParams
funcs.doc:
GenerateDHParams produces Diffie-Hellman domain parameters using a freshly
generated safe prime P of the requested bit length and the generator G = 2,
which generates the prime-order subgroup of a safe-prime group. Randomness is
drawn from rng. bits must be at least 3.

methods.name: DHGeneratePrivate
methods.doc:
DHGeneratePrivate returns a random Diffie-Hellman private exponent in the
range [2, P-2] drawn from rng. Each party keeps this value secret.

methods.name: DHPublicKey
methods.doc:
DHPublicKey computes the public value G^priv mod P that a party transmits to
its peer.

methods.name: DHSharedSecret
methods.doc:
DHSharedSecret computes the shared secret peerPub^priv mod P from this
party's private exponent and the peer's public value. Both parties arrive at
the same secret. It returns an error if the peer's public value is out of the
range [1, P-1].

types.name: ElGamalPrivateKey
types.doc:
ElGamalPrivateKey is an ElGamal private key: the secret exponent X together
with the matching public key.

funcs.name: GenerateElGamalKey
funcs.doc:
GenerateElGamalKey builds an ElGamal key pair over a fresh safe-prime group
of the requested bit length, using generator G = 2 and a random secret
exponent drawn from rng. bits must be at least 3.

types.name: ElGamalPublicKey
types.doc:
ElGamalPublicKey is an ElGamal public key over the multiplicative group
modulo the prime P with generator G, where Y = G^x mod P for the secret x.

types.name: Factor
types.doc:
Factor represents a prime power in an integer's factorization: Prime raised
to the given positive Exponent.

funcs.name: Factorization

types.name: RSAPrivateKey
types.signature:
type RSAPrivateKey struct {
	Public RSAPublicKey
	D      *big.Int // private exponent, the inverse of E modulo λ(N)
	P      *big.Int // first prime factor of N
	Q      *big.Int // second prime factor of N
	Dp     *big.Int // D mod (P-1)
	Dq     *big.Int // D mod (Q-1)
	Qinv   *big.Int // Q^-1 mod P
}
types.doc:
RSAPrivateKey is an RSA private key. It embeds the matching public key and
stores the private exponent D together with the secret primes P and Q and
precomputed Chinese-Remainder-Theorem values (Dp, Dq, Qinv) that accelerate
decryption via RSADecryptCRT.

funcs.name: GenerateRSAKey
funcs.signature: func GenerateRSAKey(bits int, e *big.Int, rng *rand.Rand) (*RSAPrivateKey, error)
funcs.doc:
GenerateRSAKey generates an RSA private key whose modulus has approximately
the requested bit length, using the public exponent e (65537 is standard and
used when e is nil) and drawing randomness from rng. bits must be at least 4
so two distinct primes of bits/2 each can be produced. It returns an error if
a suitable key cannot be formed with the chosen exponent (for example if e
shares a factor with λ(N)); callers may simply retry. The determinism of rng
makes generated keys reproducible.

methods.name: PublicKey
methods.doc: PublicKey returns the public half of the private key.

types.name: RSAPublicKey
types.doc:
RSAPublicKey is an RSA public key: the modulus N = p*q and the public
exponent E. Encryption and signature verification use only these values.

types.name: Share
types.doc:
Share is a single point (X, Y) on the secret-sharing polynomial produced by
SplitSecret. Distributing shares to participants lets any threshold-sized
subset reconstruct the secret while smaller subsets learn nothing.

funcs.name: SplitSecret
funcs.signature: func SplitSecret(secret *big.Int, threshold, participants int, prime *big.Int, rng *rand.Rand) ([]Share, error)
funcs.doc:
SplitSecret splits secret into shares participants using Shamir's threshold
scheme over the prime field Z/prime. Any threshold shares can reconstruct the
secret; any fewer reveal nothing about it. The secret must satisfy
0 <= secret < prime, and 2 <= threshold <= participants. Coefficients above
the constant term are drawn from rng, so identical seeding reproduces the
same shares. Shares are evaluated at X = 1, 2, ..., participants. It returns
an error for invalid parameters.

funcs.name: BabyStepGiantStep
funcs.signature: func BabyStepGiantStep(g, h, p, order *big.Int) (*big.Int, error)
funcs.doc:
BabyStepGiantStep solves the discrete logarithm g^x ≡ h (mod p) for x in
[0, order), using Shanks' baby-step giant-step algorithm in O(sqrt(order))
time and space. order must be a positive multiple of the multiplicative order
of g (for a prime modulus p, passing order = p-1 always works). It returns
the least such non-negative x, or ErrNoDiscreteLog if none exists. The
modulus p must be positive.

funcs.name: CRT
funcs.doc:
CRT solves a system of simultaneous congruences x ≡ residues[i] (mod
moduli[i]) via the Chinese Remainder Theorem and returns the unique solution
x in [0, M), where M is the product of all moduli. The two slices must be the
same non-zero length and every modulus must be positive. It returns an error
if the moduli are not pairwise coprime (in which case no unique solution
exists over their product).

funcs.name: CRTPair
funcs.signature: func CRTPair(a1, n1, a2, n2 *big.Int) (*big.Int, error)
funcs.doc:
CRTPair solves the pair of congruences x ≡ a1 (mod n1) and x ≡ a2 (mod n2)
and returns the unique solution in [0, n1*n2). Both moduli must be positive
and coprime; otherwise an error is returned. It is a convenience wrapper
around CRT for the common two-modulus case.

funcs.name: CarmichaelLambda
funcs.doc:
CarmichaelLambda returns the Carmichael function λ(n): the smallest positive
integer m such that a^m ≡ 1 (mod n) for every a coprime to n. It is the least
common multiple of λ over each prime-power factor. It panics if n < 1; λ(1) =
1.

funcs.name: CombineShares
funcs.doc:
CombineShares reconstructs the secret from a set of shares using Lagrange
interpolation at x = 0 over the prime field Z/prime. At least threshold
shares are required and their X coordinates must be distinct and non-zero;
otherwise an error is returned. Passing exactly the correct shares recovers
the original secret; supplying more than the threshold also works.

funcs.name: DiscreteLog
funcs.doc:
DiscreteLog solves g^x ≡ h (mod p) for a prime modulus p, searching the full
range [0, p-1). It is a convenience wrapper around BabyStepGiantStep with
order = p-1. It returns ErrNoDiscreteLog if no solution exists.

funcs.name: ElGamalDecrypt
funcs.doc:
ElGamalDecrypt recovers the message m from a ciphertext pair (c1, c2) using
the private key, computing m = c2 · (c1^x)^-1 mod P. It returns an error if
c1 is not invertible modulo P.

funcs.name: ElGamalEncrypt
funcs.signature: func ElGamalEncrypt(pub ElGamalPublicKey, m *big.Int, rng *rand.Rand) (c1, c2 *big.Int, err error)
funcs.doc:
ElGamalEncrypt encrypts the integer message m (0 <= m < P) under the public
key, using the ephemeral randomness k drawn from rng. It returns the
ciphertext pair (c1, c2) = (G^k, m·Y^k) mod P. Because k is random the same
message encrypts to different ciphertexts on each call (unless rng is
re-seeded identically). It returns an error if m is out of range.

funcs.name: EulerTotient
funcs.doc:
EulerTotient returns Euler's totient φ(n): the count of integers in [1, n]
that are coprime to n. It is computed from the prime factorization as
n · ∏ (1 - 1/p). It panics if n < 1; φ(1) = 1.

funcs.name: EvaluatePolynomial
funcs.doc:
EvaluatePolynomial evaluates the polynomial whose coefficients are given in
ascending order (coeffs[0] is the constant term) at the point x modulo m,
using Horner's method. The modulus m must be positive.

funcs.name: ExtendedGCD
funcs.doc:
ExtendedGCD returns the greatest common divisor g of a and b together with
Bézout coefficients x and y satisfying a*x + b*y = g. The returned g is
non-negative.

funcs.name: FermatTest
funcs.signature: func FermatTest(n *big.Int, rounds int, rng *rand.Rand) bool
funcs.doc:
FermatTest runs the Fermat probable-prime test on n using the given number of
random bases a drawn from rng, checking a^(n-1) ≡ 1 (mod n). It returns true
if n passes every round. Note that Carmichael numbers pass the Fermat test to
all coprime bases, so a positive result is weaker than Miller-Rabin. It
panics if rounds < 1.

funcs.name: GCD
funcs.doc:
GCD returns the non-negative greatest common divisor of a and b. GCD(0, 0)
is 0. The arguments may be negative; the result is always non-negative.

funcs.name: GenerateSafePrime
funcs.doc:
GenerateSafePrime returns a random safe prime p (one for which (p-1)/2 is
also prime) with exactly the requested bit length, drawing randomness from
rng. bits must be at least 3. Because safe primes are comparatively rare this
may test many candidates.

funcs.name: IsPrime
funcs.doc:
IsPrime reports whether n is prime. Small n are decided by trial division
against the primes below 100; the rest is settled by the deterministic
Miller-Rabin witness set, which is exact for all n < 3.3 * 10^24 and an
exceptionally strong test beyond. It needs no randomness and is the
recommended general-purpose primality predicate in this package.

funcs.name: IsPrimitiveRoot
funcs.doc:
IsPrimitiveRoot reports whether g is a primitive root modulo the odd prime p,
i.e. whether its multiplicative order is exactly p-1. It panics if p <= 2 and
is only meaningful for prime p.

funcs.name: IsProbablePrime
funcs.signature: func IsProbablePrime(n *big.Int, rounds int, rng *rand.Rand) bool
funcs.doc:
IsProbablePrime reports whether n is a probable prime, combining fast trial
division by small primes with the deterministic Miller-Rabin witness set and
then rounds additional random Miller-Rabin bases drawn from rng for extra
assurance on very large inputs. rounds may be zero, in which case it reduces
to IsPrime. It panics if rounds < 0.

funcs.name: IsQuadraticResidue
funcs.doc:
IsQuadraticResidue reports whether a is a quadratic residue modulo the odd
prime p, i.e. whether there exists x with x^2 ≡ a (mod p). Zero is treated as
a residue (0 = 0^2). It panics under the same conditions as Legendre.

funcs.name: IsSafePrime
funcs.doc:
IsSafePrime reports whether p is a safe prime, that is a prime for which
(p-1)/2 is also prime. Safe primes are preferred for Diffie-Hellman because
the corresponding group has a large prime-order subgroup.

funcs.name: Jacobi
funcs.doc:
Jacobi returns the Jacobi symbol (a/n) for an odd positive integer n. The
value is one of -1, 0 or +1. When n is an odd prime the Jacobi symbol equals
the Legendre symbol. Jacobi panics if n is even or non-positive.

funcs.name: LCM
funcs.doc:
LCM returns the non-negative least common multiple of a and b. LCM(x, 0) and
LCM(0, x) are 0 by convention.

funcs.name: Legendre
funcs.doc:
Legendre returns the Legendre symbol (a/p) for an odd prime p: +1 if a is a
non-zero quadratic residue mod p, -1 if it is a non-residue, and 0 if p
divides a. The result is computed via Euler's criterion a^((p-1)/2) mod p.
Legendre panics if p is not an odd prime candidate (even or <= 2); it does
not itself verify primality, so the result is only meaningful for prime p.

funcs.name: MillerRabin
funcs.signature: func MillerRabin(n *big.Int, rounds int, rng *rand.Rand) bool
funcs.doc:
MillerRabin runs the probabilistic Miller-Rabin primality test on n using the
given number of independently chosen random bases (rounds) drawn from rng. It
returns true if n is a probable prime and false if n is definitely composite.
The probability that a composite passes all rounds is at most 4^-rounds.
Inputs n <= 3 and even n are handled directly. It panics if rounds < 1 or rng
is nil for n large enough to require random bases.

funcs.name: MillerRabinDeterministic
funcs.doc:
MillerRabinDeterministic performs a deterministic Miller-Rabin test using a
fixed set of small prime bases. Its verdict is provably correct for every
n < 3.3 * 10^24; for larger n it is an extremely strong probable-prime test
(a composite would have to be a strong pseudoprime to all twelve of the first
prime bases). It requires no randomness.

funcs.name: ModAdd
funcs.doc: ModAdd returns a+b mod m. The modulus m must be positive.

funcs.name: ModExp
funcs.doc:
ModExp returns base**exp mod m using right-to-left binary
(square-and-multiply) exponentiation. The modulus m must be positive.

A negative exponent is supported provided base is invertible modulo m: the
result is then (base^-1)^(-exp) mod m. base itself may be negative or larger
than m; it is reduced into the range [0, m) first. ModExp panics if m <= 0
and if exp < 0 but base has no inverse modulo m.

funcs.name: ModInverse
funcs.doc:
ModInverse returns the multiplicative inverse of a modulo m, i.e. the unique
x in [0, m) with a*x ≡ 1 (mod m). The modulus m must be positive. It returns
ErrNotInvertible when gcd(a, m) != 1.

funcs.name: ModMul
funcs.doc:
ModMul returns a*b mod m. The modulus m must be positive; a and b may be
negative and are reduced into [0, m).

funcs.name: ModSqrt
funcs.doc:
ModSqrt returns a square root r of a modulo the odd prime p (so that r^2 ≡ a
(mod p)), using the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm. When a solution exists the
smaller of the two roots r and p-r is returned. It returns an error if a is a
quadratic non-residue modulo p. It panics if p is even or <= 2.

funcs.name: ModSub
funcs.doc:
ModSub returns a-b mod m, normalised into [0, m). The modulus m must be
positive.

funcs.name: MultiplicativeOrder
funcs.doc:
MultiplicativeOrder returns the multiplicative order of a modulo n, that is
the smallest positive integer k with a^k ≡ 1 (mod n). It requires gcd(a, n)
= 1 and n > 1, returning an error otherwise. The order is found by reducing
the Carmichael function λ(n) (a known multiple of every element's order) over
its prime factors.

funcs.name: NextPrime
funcs.doc:
NextPrime returns the smallest prime strictly greater than n. For n < 2 it
returns 2. The search uses the deterministic primality test.

funcs.name: PollardRho
funcs.doc:
PollardRho returns a non-trivial factor of the composite integer n using
Pollard's rho algorithm with the iteration x -> x^2 + c. It returns 2 for
even n. If n is prime a copy of n itself is returned, since it has no proper
factor. It panics if n <= 1. The polynomial constant c is advanced
deterministically on failure, so the routine needs no randomness and always
returns the same factor for the same input.

funcs.name: PollardRhoBrent
funcs.doc:
PollardRhoBrent returns a non-trivial factor of the composite integer n using
Brent's improvement to Pollard's rho, which reduces the number of gcd
computations. It behaves like PollardRho with respect to even and prime
inputs and is deterministic. It panics if n <= 1.

funcs.name: PrevPrime
funcs.doc:
PrevPrime returns the largest prime strictly less than n. It returns nil when
no such prime exists (n <= 2).

funcs.name: PrimeFactors
funcs.doc:
PrimeFactors returns the distinct-with-multiplicity prime factors of n in
ascending order (each prime appears as many times as it divides n). For
example PrimeFactors(360) yields [2 2 2 3 3 5]. It panics if n < 1;
PrimeFactors(1) is the empty slice.

funcs.name: PrimesUpTo
funcs.doc:
PrimesUpTo is an alias for SieveOfEratosthenes returning every prime not
exceeding limit.

funcs.name: PrimitiveRoot
funcs.doc:
PrimitiveRoot returns the smallest primitive root modulo the odd prime p,
i.e. the least generator g of the cyclic multiplicative group (Z/pZ)* whose
multiplicative order equals p-1. It panics if p <= 2. The result is only
meaningful when p is prime.

funcs.name: RSADecrypt
funcs.doc:
RSADecrypt performs the raw RSA decryption primitive m = c^D mod N using the
private exponent directly. The ciphertext c must satisfy 0 <= c < N.

funcs.name: RSADecryptCRT
funcs.doc:
RSADecryptCRT performs RSA decryption using the Chinese Remainder Theorem,
exponentiating separately modulo P and Q and recombining. It is roughly four
times faster than RSADecrypt for the same key and yields an identical result.
The ciphertext c must satisfy 0 <= c < N.

funcs.name: RSAEncrypt
funcs.doc:
RSAEncrypt performs the raw (textbook) RSA encryption primitive c = m^E mod
N. The message m, interpreted as an integer, must satisfy 0 <= m < N;
otherwise an error is returned. This is the pure mathematical operation with
no padding and must not be used to encrypt real messages directly.

funcs.name: RSASign
funcs.doc:
RSASign produces a raw RSA signature s = m^D mod N over the integer message m
(0 <= m < N). It is the textbook signing primitive without hashing or
padding; RSAVerify inverts it.

funcs.name: RSAVerify
funcs.signature: func RSAVerify(pub RSAPublicKey, m, s *big.Int) bool
funcs.doc:
RSAVerify checks a raw RSA signature by computing s^E mod N and reporting
whether it equals the original message m. It returns true exactly when the
signature is valid for m under the given public key.

funcs.name: RandomPrime
funcs.doc:
RandomPrime returns a random prime with exactly the requested bit length
(both the top and bottom bits are forced to 1, guaranteeing an odd number of
the requested size), using rng as the randomness source. bits must be at
least 2. The determinism of rng makes the result reproducible.

funcs.name: SieveOfEratosthenes
funcs.doc:
SieveOfEratosthenes returns all primes p with 2 <= p <= limit as int64
values, computed with the classic sieve. It panics if limit < 0. For limit <
2 the result is empty.

funcs.name: SmallestPrimeFactor
funcs.doc: SmallestPrimeFactor returns the least prime factor of n. It panics if n < 2.

funcs.name: TrialDivision
funcs.doc:
TrialDivision reports whether n is prime by trial division against every
candidate up to sqrt(n). It is exact for all n but only practical for small
inputs; use IsPrime for anything large. n < 2 is not prime.

packages.name: diffgeo
packages.synopsis: Package diffgeo implements classical differential geometry of curves and surfaces in three-dimensional Euclidean space using only the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package diffgeo implements classical differential geometry of curves and
surfaces in three-dimensional Euclidean space using only the Go standard
library.

The package is organized around two functional descriptions of geometric
objects:

  - A [Curve] is a function t ↦ (x, y, z) tracing a parametric space curve.
    From it the package computes the Frenet apparatus: unit tangent, principal
    normal and binormal ([FrenetFrame]), signed [Curvature] and [Torsion], the
    curvature vector, radius of curvature and [ArcLength].

  - A [Surface] is a function (u, v) ↦ (x, y, z) describing a parametric
    patch. From it the package computes the [FirstForm] and [SecondForm]
    (the first and second fundamental forms), the unit [SurfaceNormal],
    [GaussianCurvature], [MeanCurvature], [PrincipalCurvatures],
    [NormalCurvature], the [AreaElement] and total [SurfaceArea], the
    [Christoffel] symbols of the second kind, and numerically integrated
    geodesics ([GeodesicPath]) and [ParallelTransport].

Derivatives are approximated by central finite differences with carefully
chosen step sizes, so every routine works for an arbitrary caller-supplied
parametrization without symbolic differentiation. Ready-made
parametrizations are provided as constructors ([Line], [Circle], [Ellipse],
[Helix], [Sphere], [Cylinder], [Torus], [Graph], [SurfacePlane]) so that
results can be checked against the well-known closed-form curvatures of these
standard shapes.

All computation is done in float64. Routines are deterministic: identical
inputs yield identical outputs. Approximate predicates take an explicit
epsilon so the caller controls the robustness/strictness trade-off.

consts.signature: const Eps = 1e-9
consts.doc:
Eps is the default absolute tolerance used by this package's approximate
predicates and degeneracy checks. It sits comfortably above double-precision
rounding noise for quantities of moderate magnitude.

types.name: Christoffel
types.doc:
Christoffel holds the Christoffel symbols of the second kind Γᵏᵢⱼ at a point,
indexed as Gamma[k][i][j] with parameter indices 0 = u and 1 = v. They are
symmetric in the lower indices (Γᵏᵢⱼ = Γᵏⱼᵢ) and are the intrinsic connection
coefficients governing geodesics and parallel transport.

funcs.name: ChristoffelSymbols
funcs.signature: func ChristoffelSymbols(s Surface, u, v float64) Christoffel
funcs.doc:
ChristoffelSymbols returns the [Christoffel] symbols of the second kind at
(u, v). They are derived purely from the first fundamental form and its first
partial derivatives via Γᵏᵢⱼ = ½ gᵏˡ(∂ᵢgⱼˡ + ∂ⱼgᵢˡ − ∂ˡgᵢⱼ), where g is the
metric [FirstForm] written as [[E,F],[F,G]] and gᵏˡ its inverse. The metric
derivatives are taken by central differences of [FirstFundamental].

methods.name: At
methods.signature: func (c Christoffel) At(k, i, j int) float64
methods.doc: At returns the symbol Γᵏᵢⱼ = Gamma[k][i][j].

types.name: Curve
types.signature: type Curve func(t float64) Vec3
types.doc:
Curve is a parametric space curve: a function mapping a scalar parameter t to
a point r(t) in three-dimensional space. Derivatives are taken with respect
to t by central finite differences, so any smooth parametrization works.

funcs.name: Circle
funcs.doc:
Circle returns the planar circle of the given radius in the xy-plane,
r(t) = (radius·cos t, radius·sin t, 0). Its curvature is 1/radius and its
torsion is zero.

funcs.name: Ellipse
funcs.doc:
Ellipse returns the planar ellipse r(t) = (a·cos t, b·sin t, 0) with the
given semi-axes a and b.

funcs.name: Helix
funcs.doc:
Helix returns the circular helix r(t) = (radius·cos t, radius·sin t, rise·t).
It has constant curvature radius/(radius²+rise²) and constant torsion
rise/(radius²+rise²).

funcs.name: Line
funcs.doc:
Line returns the straight-line curve r(t) = p + t·d through point p with
direction d. Its curvature and torsion are identically zero.

types.name: FirstForm
types.doc:
FirstForm is the first fundamental form (metric) of a surface at a point,
I = E du² + 2F du dv + G dv², with coefficients E = rᵤ·rᵤ, F = rᵤ·rᵥ and
G = rᵥ·rᵥ. It encodes lengths, angles and areas intrinsic to the surface.

funcs.name: FirstFundamental
funcs.signature: func FirstFundamental(s Surface, u, v float64) FirstForm
funcs.doc: FirstFundamental returns the [FirstForm] coefficients at (u, v).

methods.name: Determinant
methods.doc:
Determinant returns EG − F², the squared area of the parallelogram spanned by
rᵤ and rᵥ. It is strictly positive at a regular point.

types.name: Frame
types.doc:
Frame is an orthonormal Frenet frame at a point of a curve: the unit tangent
T, principal normal N and binormal B, forming a right-handed basis with
T×N = B.

funcs.name: FrenetFrame
funcs.signature: func FrenetFrame(c Curve, t float64) Frame
funcs.doc:
FrenetFrame returns the [Frame] {T, N, B} at parameter t. The tangent is
r'/|r'|, the binormal is (r'×r”)/|r'×r”|, and the normal is B×T. Where the
curvature vanishes the normal and binormal are not well defined and are
returned as zero vectors.

types.name: GeodesicState
types.doc:
GeodesicState is a point on a curve in a surface's parameter domain together
with its parametric velocity: the parameters (U, V) and their rates of change
(DU, DV) with respect to the curve parameter.

funcs.name: GeodesicPath
funcs.signature: func GeodesicPath(s Surface, start GeodesicState, tEnd float64, steps int) []GeodesicState
funcs.doc:
GeodesicPath integrates a geodesic on surface s from the initial
[GeodesicState] start over a curve-parameter length of tEnd, using steps
equal-sized RK4 steps. It returns the sequence of states, of length steps+1,
beginning with start. A geodesic is the surface analogue of a straight line:
on a sphere the paths are great circles, on a plane they are straight lines.

funcs.name: GeodesicStep
funcs.signature: func GeodesicStep(s Surface, st GeodesicState, h float64) GeodesicState
funcs.doc:
GeodesicStep advances one geodesic [GeodesicState] by a step h of the curve
parameter using the classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta method.

funcs.name: ParallelTransport
funcs.signature: func ParallelTransport(s Surface, start GeodesicState, w0, w1, tEnd float64, steps int) ([]GeodesicState, [][2]float64)
funcs.doc:
ParallelTransport transports the tangent vector with parameter-space
components (w0, w1) along the geodesic starting at start on surface s, over a
curve-parameter length tEnd using steps RK4 steps. It returns the geodesic
[GeodesicPath] and, aligned index-for-index with it, the transported vector
components at each state.

Parallel transport preserves the metric inner product; in particular the
length √(E w0² + 2F w0 w1 + G w1²) of the transported vector is invariant
along the path, a useful correctness check.

types.name: SecondForm
types.doc:
SecondForm is the second fundamental form of a surface at a point,
II = L du² + 2M du dv + N dv², with coefficients L = rᵤᵤ·n, M = rᵤᵥ·n and
N = rᵥᵥ·n, where n is the unit [SurfaceNormal]. It measures how the surface
curves away from its tangent plane.

funcs.name: SecondFundamental
funcs.signature: func SecondFundamental(s Surface, u, v float64) SecondForm
funcs.doc:
SecondFundamental returns the [SecondForm] coefficients at (u, v). Its sign
convention matches the orientation of [SurfaceNormal].

types.name: Surface
types.signature: type Surface func(u, v float64) Vec3
types.doc:
Surface is a parametric surface patch: a function mapping parameters (u, v)
to a point r(u, v) in three-dimensional space. Partial derivatives are taken
by central finite differences, so any smooth parametrization works.

funcs.name: Cylinder
funcs.doc:
Cylinder returns the parametrization of a circular cylinder of the given
radius aligned with the z-axis: r(u,v) = (radius·cos u, radius·sin u, v). It
is a developable surface with zero Gaussian curvature.

funcs.name: Graph
funcs.signature: func Graph(f func(u, v float64) float64) Surface
funcs.doc:
Graph returns the Monge patch r(u,v) = (u, v, f(u,v)) of the height function
f, representing the surface z = f(x, y).

funcs.name: Sphere
funcs.doc:
Sphere returns the parametrization of a sphere of the given radius by
longitude u and latitude v: r(u,v) = radius·(cos v cos u, cos v sin u, sin v),
with u in [0, 2π) and v in (−π/2, π/2). Its Gaussian curvature is
1/radius² everywhere.

funcs.name: SurfacePlane
funcs.signature: func SurfacePlane(origin, du, dv Vec3) Surface
funcs.doc:
SurfacePlane returns the affine plane r(u,v) = origin + u·du + v·dv spanned by
the direction vectors du and dv through origin. Provided the spanning vectors
are independent, its Gaussian and mean curvatures are zero everywhere.

funcs.name: Torus
funcs.doc:
Torus returns the parametrization of a torus with center-circle radius major
and tube radius minor: r(u,v) = ((major+minor·cos v)cos u,
(major+minor·cos v)sin u, minor·sin v). Its Gaussian curvature is
cos v /(minor·(major+minor·cos v)).

types.name: Vec3
types.doc:
Vec3 is a vector in three-dimensional Euclidean space. It doubles as a point
(a position) and as a displacement or direction, depending on context.

funcs.name: Acceleration
funcs.signature: func Acceleration(c Curve, t float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: Acceleration returns the second derivative r”(t) by a central difference.

funcs.name: Binormal
funcs.signature: func Binormal(c Curve, t float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
Binormal returns the unit binormal B(t) = T×N = (r'×r”)/|r'×r”|, the normal
to the osculating plane.

funcs.name: CurvatureVector
funcs.signature: func CurvatureVector(c Curve, t float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
CurvatureVector returns dT/ds, the derivative of the unit tangent with
respect to arc length. Its magnitude is the [Curvature] and its direction is
the principal normal. It is computed as (a − (a·T)T)/|v|² with v = r'(t),
a = r”(t) and T = v/|v|.

funcs.name: Jerk
funcs.signature: func Jerk(c Curve, t float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
Jerk returns the third derivative r”'(t), used by [Torsion], via a five-point
central difference.

funcs.name: NewVec3
funcs.signature: func NewVec3(x, y, z float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: NewVec3 returns the vector with the given components.

funcs.name: PartialU
funcs.signature: func PartialU(s Surface, u, v float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: PartialU returns the first partial derivative rᵤ = ∂r/∂u at (u, v).

funcs.name: PartialUU
funcs.signature: func PartialUU(s Surface, u, v float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: PartialUU returns the second partial derivative rᵤᵤ = ∂²r/∂u² at (u, v).

funcs.name: PartialUV
funcs.signature: func PartialUV(s Surface, u, v float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
PartialUV returns the mixed second partial derivative rᵤᵥ = ∂²r/∂u∂v at
(u, v), computed by the four-point central difference.

funcs.name: PartialV
funcs.signature: func PartialV(s Surface, u, v float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: PartialV returns the first partial derivative rᵥ = ∂r/∂v at (u, v).

funcs.name: PartialVV
funcs.signature: func PartialVV(s Surface, u, v float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: PartialVV returns the second partial derivative rᵥᵥ = ∂²r/∂v² at (u, v).

funcs.name: PrincipalNormal
funcs.signature: func PrincipalNormal(c Curve, t float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
PrincipalNormal returns the unit principal normal N(t), the direction in
which the curve is turning. It is the normalized [CurvatureVector].

funcs.name: SurfaceNormal
funcs.signature: func SurfaceNormal(s Surface, u, v float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
SurfaceNormal returns the unit normal vector n = (rᵤ×rᵥ)/|rᵤ×rᵥ| at (u, v).
The orientation follows the right-hand rule from the parameter ordering
(u, v). At a degenerate point where rᵤ and rᵥ are parallel it returns the
zero vector.

funcs.name: UnitTangent
funcs.signature: func UnitTangent(c Curve, t float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
UnitTangent returns the unit tangent vector T(t) = r'(t)/|r'(t)|. At a point
where the velocity vanishes it returns the zero vector.

funcs.name: Velocity
funcs.signature: func Velocity(c Curve, t float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
Velocity returns the first derivative r'(t), the velocity vector, computed by
a central difference. Its direction is tangent to the curve and its magnitude
is the parametric [Speed].

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the component-wise sum v+w.

methods.name: Angle
methods.doc:
Angle returns the unsigned angle in radians between v and w, in [0, π].
If either operand is the zero vector it returns 0.

methods.name: Cross
methods.doc: Cross returns the cross product v×w, a vector orthogonal to both operands.

methods.name: Distance
methods.doc: Distance returns the Euclidean distance between the points v and w.

methods.name: Dot
methods.doc: Dot returns the Euclidean inner product v·w.

methods.name: Equal
methods.signature: func (v Vec3) Equal(w Vec3, eps float64) bool
methods.doc: Equal reports whether v and w agree component-wise within the tolerance eps.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether every component of v is within eps of zero.

methods.name: Lerp
methods.signature: func (v Vec3) Lerp(w Vec3, t float64) Vec3
methods.doc: Lerp returns the linear interpolation (1-t)·v + t·w.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns the additive inverse -v.

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc: Norm returns the Euclidean length |v|.

methods.name: Norm2
methods.doc: Norm2 returns the squared Euclidean length |v|², avoiding the square root.

methods.name: Normalize
methods.doc:
Normalize returns the unit vector pointing along v. If v is the zero vector
(within [Eps]) it returns the zero vector unchanged.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns v with every component multiplied by the scalar s.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the component-wise difference v-w.

funcs.name: ArcLength
funcs.signature: func ArcLength(c Curve, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
ArcLength returns the arc length of the curve between parameters a and b,
computed as the integral of [Speed] using composite Simpson's rule with n
subintervals. n is rounded up to the next even number and forced to at least
2. The result is the exact geometric length in the limit of large n.

funcs.name: AreaElement
funcs.signature: func AreaElement(s Surface, u, v float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AreaElement returns the scalar surface area element √(EG − F²) = |rᵤ×rᵥ| at
(u, v), the local factor relating parameter area to surface area.

funcs.name: Curvature
funcs.signature: func Curvature(c Curve, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Curvature returns the (unsigned) curvature κ(t) = |r'×r”| / |r'|³, the
reciprocal of the radius of the osculating circle. For a straight line it is
zero; for a circle of radius R it is 1/R.

funcs.name: GaussianCurvature
funcs.signature: func GaussianCurvature(s Surface, u, v float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussianCurvature returns the Gaussian curvature K = (LN − M²)/(EG − F²) at
(u, v). It is an intrinsic invariant (Gauss's Theorema Egregium): positive on
dome-like regions, negative on saddles, zero on developable surfaces such as
planes and cylinders.

funcs.name: GeodesicAcceleration
funcs.signature: func GeodesicAcceleration(s Surface, st GeodesicState) (accU, accV float64)
funcs.doc:
GeodesicAcceleration returns the parameter-space acceleration (d²u, d²v)
dictated by the geodesic equations at state st on surface s:

	d²u = −(Γ⁰₀₀ u'² + 2Γ⁰₀₁ u'v' + Γ⁰₁₁ v'²)
	d²v = −(Γ¹₀₀ u'² + 2Γ¹₀₁ u'v' + Γ¹₁₁ v'²)

where the Γ are the [Christoffel] symbols at (U, V). A curve satisfying these
equations is a geodesic: it has zero tangential acceleration within the
surface.

funcs.name: GeodesicLength
funcs.doc:
GeodesicLength returns the arc length in space of the geodesic sampled by
[GeodesicPath], measured as the total length of the polyline through the
image points s(U, V) of the path states. With enough steps it approaches the
true intrinsic length between the endpoints.

funcs.name: MeanCurvature
funcs.signature: func MeanCurvature(s Surface, u, v float64) float64
funcs.doc:
MeanCurvature returns the mean curvature H = (EN + GL − 2FM)/(2(EG − F²)) at
(u, v), the average of the two [PrincipalCurvatures]. Its sign depends on the
orientation of [SurfaceNormal]; a minimal surface has H = 0 everywhere.

funcs.name: NormalCurvature
funcs.signature: func NormalCurvature(s Surface, u, v, du, dv float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NormalCurvature returns the normal curvature at (u, v) in the tangent
direction (du, dv), that is II(du,dv)/I(du,dv) =
(L du² + 2M du dv + N dv²)/(E du² + 2F du dv + G dv²). The direction need not
be a unit vector; only its ratio matters.

funcs.name: PrincipalCurvatures
funcs.signature: func PrincipalCurvatures(s Surface, u, v float64) (k1, k2 float64)
funcs.doc:
PrincipalCurvatures returns the principal curvatures k1 ≥ k2 at (u, v), the
extreme values of the [NormalCurvature] over all tangent directions. They are
the eigenvalues of the shape operator, k = H ± √(H² − K), where H is the
[MeanCurvature] and K the [GaussianCurvature].

funcs.name: RadiusOfCurvature
funcs.signature: func RadiusOfCurvature(c Curve, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RadiusOfCurvature returns 1/κ(t), the radius of the osculating circle. It
returns +Inf where the curvature vanishes.

funcs.name: Speed
funcs.signature: func Speed(c Curve, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Speed returns the parametric speed |r'(t)|, the rate at which arc length
accumulates per unit parameter.

funcs.name: SurfaceArea
funcs.signature: func SurfaceArea(s Surface, u0, u1, v0, v1 float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
SurfaceArea returns the area of the patch over the parameter rectangle
[u0,u1]×[v0,v1], the double integral of the [AreaElement] evaluated by a
tensor-product composite Simpson's rule with n subintervals in each
direction. n is rounded up to the next even number and forced to at least 2.

funcs.name: Torsion
funcs.signature: func Torsion(c Curve, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Torsion returns the torsion τ(t) = ((r'×r”)·r”') / |r'×r”|², which measures
how sharply the curve twists out of its osculating plane. It is zero for any
planar curve. Where the curvature vanishes the osculating plane is undefined
and Torsion returns 0.

packages.name: discretemath
packages.synopsis: Package discretemath implements a broad collection of discrete-mathematics primitives built entirely on the Go standard library.

types.name: Pair
types.doc:
Pair is an ordered two-element tuple, used as the element type of a Cartesian
product of two (possibly heterogeneous) slices.

funcs.name: CartesianProduct
funcs.signature: func CartesianProduct[T, U any](a []T, b []U) []Pair[T, U]
funcs.doc:
CartesianProduct returns the Cartesian product of a and b: every ordered pair
(x, y) with x drawn from a and y from b. The pairs are ordered with a's index
varying slowest.

types.name: Run
types.doc:
Run is a single maximal run of an equal value produced by run-length encoding:
Value repeated Count times.

funcs.name: RunLengthEncode
funcs.doc:
RunLengthEncode compresses a slice into a sequence of runs, each recording a
value and the number of times it repeats consecutively. Encoding an empty
slice yields an empty result.

types.name: Set
types.signature: type Set[T comparable] map[T]struct{}
types.doc:
Set is a mathematical set of distinct comparable elements, represented as a
map whose keys are the members. The zero value is not usable; construct sets
with NewSet.

funcs.name: Difference
funcs.signature: func Difference[T comparable](a, b Set[T]) Set[T]
funcs.doc:
