DihedralIdentity returns the identity element of every dihedral group, the
rotation by zero.

funcs.name: DihedralInverse
funcs.signature: func DihedralInverse(n int, a DihedralElement) DihedralElement
funcs.doc:
DihedralInverse returns the inverse of a in D_n. Reflections are their own
inverse; the inverse of r^k is r^(n-k). The argument n must be positive.

funcs.name: DihedralReflection
funcs.doc:
DihedralReflection returns the reflection s·r^k in D_n with its exponent
reduced modulo n. The argument n must be positive.

funcs.name: DihedralRotation
funcs.doc:
DihedralRotation returns the rotation r^k in D_n with its exponent reduced
modulo n. The argument n must be positive.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders a dihedral element as "r^k" for rotations and "s·r^k" for
reflections, abbreviating the exponent-zero cases as "e", "r", "s".

types.name: FiniteGroup
types.doc:
FiniteGroup is an abstract finite group whose elements are the integer
indices 0, 1, ..., Order()-1 and whose binary operation is supplied as a
function on those indices. It supports Cayley-table generation, element and
group orders, subgroup and coset enumeration, and structural tests such as
abelian/cyclic detection.

funcs.name: CyclicGroupZn
funcs.doc:
CyclicGroupZn returns the additive cyclic group Z/nZ as a FiniteGroup: the
elements are 0..n-1, the operation is addition modulo n, and the identity is
0. The argument n must be positive.

funcs.name: NewFiniteGroup
funcs.signature: func NewFiniteGroup(n, identity int, op func(a, b int) int) *FiniteGroup
funcs.doc:
NewFiniteGroup builds a FiniteGroup on the elements {0, ..., n-1} with the
given operation and identity index. The operation must return an index in
[0, n) for every pair of arguments. n must be positive and identity must be
a valid index. Use [FiniteGroup.IsValid] to check the closure, associativity
and inverse axioms of the resulting structure.

funcs.name: SymmetricGroupTable
funcs.doc:
SymmetricGroupTable returns the symmetric group S_n as a FiniteGroup whose
element indices enumerate the permutations in the lexicographic order of
[SymmetricGroup]. The operation composes permutations and the identity is
index 0. n must be non-negative and small. Use [SymmetricGroup] to recover
the permutation for a given index.

methods.name: CayleyTable
methods.doc:
CayleyTable returns the group's multiplication table as an n×n matrix where
entry [a][b] holds the index of a·b.

methods.name: Center
methods.doc:
Center returns the sorted indices of the center Z(G): the elements that
commute with every element of the group.

methods.name: Cosets
methods.doc:
Cosets returns the partition of the group into distinct left cosets of the
given subgroup, each coset sorted and the cosets ordered by their smallest
element. By Lagrange's theorem these cosets have equal size and partition the
group.

methods.name: ElementOrder
methods.doc:
ElementOrder returns the order of a: the smallest k > 0 with a^k equal to the
identity. The argument a must be a valid index.

methods.name: Elements
methods.doc: Elements returns the slice {0, 1, ..., Order()-1} of all element indices.

methods.name: GeneratedSubgroup
methods.doc:
GeneratedSubgroup returns the sorted set of elements of the subgroup
generated by the given generators, computed by closing the generator set
under the group operation. An empty generator list yields the trivial
subgroup {identity}.

methods.name: Identity
methods.doc: Identity returns the index of the group's identity element.

methods.name: Index
methods.doc:
Index returns the index [G:H] of the subgroup H in the group G, the number of
distinct left cosets. For a valid subgroup this equals |G|/|H|.

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse returns the index of the inverse of a. It panics if a has no inverse
(which cannot happen in a valid group). The argument a must be a valid
index.

methods.name: IsAbelian
methods.doc:
IsAbelian reports whether the group is commutative: a·b == b·a for every
pair of elements.

methods.name: IsCyclic
methods.doc:
IsCyclic reports whether the group is cyclic, i.e. some single element
generates all n elements.

methods.name: IsSubgroup
methods.doc:
IsSubgroup reports whether the given set of element indices forms a subgroup:
it must be non-empty, contain the identity, and be closed under the operation
and under inverses. Duplicate entries are tolerated.

methods.name: IsValid
methods.doc:
IsValid reports whether the structure satisfies the group axioms over its n
indices: closure, the declared identity acting as a two-sided identity,
existence of inverses, and associativity. This is an O(n^3) check intended
for validating hand-built tables.

methods.name: LeftCoset
methods.doc:
LeftCoset returns the sorted left coset g·H = {g·h : h in subgroup} for the
element index x. The subgroup argument is treated as a set of indices.

methods.name: Op
methods.doc: Op returns the product a·b of two element indices under the group operation.

methods.name: Order
methods.doc: Order returns the number of elements in the group.

methods.name: RightCoset
methods.doc:
RightCoset returns the sorted right coset H·g = {h·g : h in subgroup} for the
element index x.

types.name: Perm
types.signature: type Perm []int
types.doc:
Perm is a permutation of {0, 1, ..., n-1} in one-line notation: p[i] is the
image of i under p. A valid Perm of length n contains each value in [0, n)
exactly once.

funcs.name: AlternatingGroup
funcs.doc:
AlternatingGroup returns all even permutations of degree n (the alternating
group A_n) in lexicographic order. n must be non-negative and small.

funcs.name: Compose
funcs.doc:
Compose returns the composition p∘q, the permutation that applies q first
and then p, so (Compose(p, q))[i] == p[q[i]]. Both permutations must have
the same degree.

funcs.name: Identity
funcs.doc:
Identity returns the identity permutation of degree n, mapping every i to
itself. The degree n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: PermFromCycles
funcs.doc:
PermFromCycles builds a permutation of degree n from disjoint cycles given
as slices of points. Points not mentioned are fixed. It panics if n < 0, if
any point is out of range, or if a point appears more than once across the
cycles.

funcs.name: SymmetricGroup
funcs.doc:
SymmetricGroup returns all n! permutations of degree n in lexicographic
order of their one-line notation. n must be non-negative and small.

funcs.name: Transposition
funcs.signature: func Transposition(n, i, j int) Perm
funcs.doc:
Transposition returns the permutation of degree n that swaps i and j and
fixes every other point. It panics if i or j is out of range.

methods.name: Apply
methods.doc:
Apply returns the image p[i] of the point i under the permutation p. It
panics if i is out of range.

methods.name: CycleType
methods.doc:
CycleType returns the multiset of cycle lengths of p sorted in
non-increasing order (a partition of the degree n). For example the cycle
type of a product of a 3-cycle and a transposition in S5 is [3, 2].

methods.name: Cycles
methods.doc:
Cycles returns the disjoint-cycle decomposition of p. Each cycle is a slice
of points listed starting from its smallest element, and the cycles are
ordered by their smallest element. Fixed points appear as length-one cycles.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree returns the size n of the set {0, ..., n-1} that p permutes, i.e.
len(p).

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether p and q are the same permutation (equal degree and
equal images).

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse returns the inverse permutation p^-1, satisfying
Compose(p, p.Inverse()) == Identity(p.Degree()).

methods.name: IsEven
methods.doc: IsEven reports whether p is an even permutation (sign +1).

methods.name: IsIdentity
methods.doc: IsIdentity reports whether p fixes every point, i.e. p[i] == i for all i.

methods.name: NumInversions
methods.doc:
NumInversions returns the number of inversions of p: pairs i < j with
p[i] > p[j]. The parity of this count matches [Perm.Sign].

methods.name: Order
methods.doc:
Order returns the order of p in the symmetric group: the smallest k > 0 with
p^k the identity. It equals the least common multiple of the cycle lengths.
The identity permutation has order 1.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns p raised to the integer power k. Negative k uses the inverse of
p, and Pow(0) is the identity of the same degree.

methods.name: Sign
methods.doc:
Sign returns the sign (parity) of p: +1 if p is an even permutation and -1
if it is odd. The sign equals (-1)^(n - c) where n is the degree and c is
the number of disjoint cycles including fixed points.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders p in disjoint-cycle notation, e.g. "(0 2 1)(3 4)". The
identity permutation renders as "()". Fixed points are omitted.

types.name: Poly
types.signature: type Poly []float64
types.doc:
Poly is a univariate polynomial over the real (rational) field, stored in
coefficient slice form from lowest to highest degree: Poly{c0, c1, c2}
represents c0 + c1·x + c2·x². The zero polynomial is represented by an empty
(or all-zero) slice. Polynomials form a Euclidean domain, so the Euclidean
algorithm yields a greatest common divisor via [PolyGCD].

funcs.name: PolyAdd
funcs.doc: PolyAdd returns the sum a + b.

funcs.name: PolyDerivative
funcs.doc: PolyDerivative returns the formal derivative a'(x) of the polynomial a.

funcs.name: PolyDivMod
funcs.signature: func PolyDivMod(a, b Poly) (q, r Poly)
funcs.doc:
PolyDivMod returns the quotient q and remainder r of polynomial long
division a = q·b + r with deg(r) < deg(b). The divisor b must be non-zero; it
panics otherwise.

funcs.name: PolyGCD
funcs.doc:
PolyGCD returns the monic greatest common divisor of a and b via the
Euclidean algorithm over polynomials. PolyGCD of two zero polynomials is the
zero polynomial.

funcs.name: PolyMod
funcs.doc: PolyMod returns the remainder of a divided by b (the r from [PolyDivMod]).

funcs.name: PolyMonic
funcs.doc:
PolyMonic returns a scaled to be monic (leading coefficient 1). The zero
polynomial is returned unchanged.

funcs.name: PolyMul
funcs.doc: PolyMul returns the product a·b via the convolution of coefficient slices.

funcs.name: PolyScale
funcs.signature: func PolyScale(a Poly, c float64) Poly
funcs.doc:
PolyScale returns the polynomial a with every coefficient multiplied by the
scalar c.

funcs.name: PolySub
funcs.doc: PolySub returns the difference a - b.

funcs.name: PolyTrim
funcs.doc:
PolyTrim returns a copy of a with trailing (highest-degree) coefficients that
are effectively zero removed, giving the canonical representation. The zero
polynomial trims to an empty slice.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree returns the degree of a: the index of its highest non-zero
coefficient. The zero polynomial has degree -1 by convention.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc:
IsZero reports whether a is the zero polynomial (every coefficient within
tolerance of zero).

methods.name: LeadingCoeff
methods.doc:
LeadingCoeff returns the coefficient of the highest-degree term of a, or 0
for the zero polynomial.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders a in conventional descending-degree notation, e.g.
"2x^2 + 3x - 1". The zero polynomial renders as "0".

funcs.name: AlternatingGroupOrder
funcs.doc:
AlternatingGroupOrder returns |A_n|, the order of the alternating group on n
points: n!/2 for n >= 2 and 1 for n in {0, 1}. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Coprime
funcs.doc: Coprime reports whether a and b are relatively prime, i.e. gcd(a, b) == 1.

funcs.name: CyclicOrder
funcs.doc:
CyclicOrder returns the order (number of elements) of the additive cyclic
group Z/nZ, which is n. The argument n must be positive.

funcs.name: DihedralElementOrder
funcs.signature: func DihedralElementOrder(n int, a DihedralElement) int
funcs.doc:
DihedralElementOrder returns the order of a in D_n: 1 for the identity, 2 for
every reflection, and n/gcd(k, n) for the rotation r^k. The argument n must
be positive.

funcs.name: DihedralOrder
funcs.doc:
DihedralOrder returns the order of the dihedral group D_n, which is 2n. The
argument n must be positive.

funcs.name: ElementOrderZn
funcs.doc:
ElementOrderZn returns the additive order of a in the cyclic group Z/nZ,
that is the smallest k > 0 with k*a ≡ 0 (mod n). This equals n/gcd(a, n).
The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: EulerTotient
funcs.doc:
EulerTotient returns Euler's totient φ(n): the number of integers in
[1, n] that are coprime to n, equivalently the order of the unit group
(Z/nZ)*. The argument n must be positive.

funcs.name: ExtendedGcd
funcs.signature: func ExtendedGcd(a, b int) (g, x, y int)
funcs.doc:
ExtendedGcd returns g = gcd(a, b) together with Bézout coefficients x and y
satisfying a*x + b*y == g. The returned g is non-negative.

funcs.name: Factorial
funcs.doc:
Factorial returns n! as an int. n must be non-negative and small enough that
n! fits in an int; Factorial(0) == 1.

funcs.name: GFAdd
funcs.signature: func GFAdd(a, b, p int) int
funcs.doc: GFAdd returns a + b in the prime field GF(p). p must be prime.

funcs.name: GFDiv
funcs.signature: func GFDiv(a, b, p int) int
funcs.doc:
GFDiv returns a / b in the prime field GF(p). It panics if b ≡ 0 (mod p). p
must be prime.

funcs.name: GFInv
funcs.doc:
GFInv returns the multiplicative inverse of a in the prime field GF(p). It
panics if a ≡ 0 (mod p), which has no inverse. p must be prime.

funcs.name: GFMul
funcs.signature: func GFMul(a, b, p int) int
funcs.doc: GFMul returns a * b in the prime field GF(p). p must be prime.

funcs.name: GFNeg
funcs.doc:
GFNeg returns the additive inverse -a in the prime field GF(p). p must be
prime.

funcs.name: GFPow
funcs.signature: func GFPow(a, e, p int) int
funcs.doc:
GFPow returns a raised to the power e in the prime field GF(p). The exponent
e must be non-negative and p must be prime.

funcs.name: GFSub
funcs.signature: func GFSub(a, b, p int) int
funcs.doc: GFSub returns a - b in the prime field GF(p). p must be prime.

funcs.name: Gcd
funcs.doc:
Gcd returns the non-negative greatest common divisor of a and b using the
Euclidean algorithm. Gcd(0, 0) == 0 and the result is invariant under the
sign of either argument.

funcs.name: GcdMany
funcs.doc:
GcdMany returns the greatest common divisor of all supplied integers.
GcdMany with no arguments returns 0.

funcs.name: IsPermutation
funcs.doc:
IsPermutation reports whether p is a valid permutation: a bijection of
{0, ..., len(p)-1} with every value appearing exactly once.

funcs.name: IsPrime
funcs.doc:
IsPrime reports whether n is a prime number using trial division. Values
less than 2 are not prime.

funcs.name: IsPrimitiveRoot
funcs.doc:
IsPrimitiveRoot reports whether g is a primitive root modulo n, i.e. g
generates the whole unit group (Z/nZ)*. Equivalently the multiplicative
order of g equals φ(n). The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: IsUnitModN
funcs.doc:
IsUnitModN reports whether a is a unit modulo n, i.e. gcd(a mod n, n) == 1.
The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: Lcm
funcs.doc:
Lcm returns the non-negative least common multiple of a and b. Lcm with a
zero argument is 0.

funcs.name: LcmMany
funcs.doc:
LcmMany returns the least common multiple of all supplied integers.
LcmMany with no arguments returns 1 (the empty product). If any argument is
0 the result is 0.

funcs.name: Mod
funcs.doc:
Mod returns the representative of a in Z/nZ lying in [0, n). The modulus n
must be positive; unlike Go's built-in % operator the result is never
negative. It panics if n <= 0.

funcs.name: ModAdd
funcs.signature: func ModAdd(a, b, n int) int
funcs.doc: ModAdd returns (a + b) mod n in [0, n). The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: ModInverse
funcs.doc:
ModInverse returns the multiplicative inverse of a modulo n in [0, n) and
true when gcd(a, n) == 1. When a is not a unit modulo n it returns (0,
false). The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: ModMul
funcs.signature: func ModMul(a, b, n int) int
funcs.doc:
ModMul returns (a * b) mod n in [0, n) computed without intermediate
overflow for operands whose reduced values fit in an int. The modulus n must
be positive.

funcs.name: ModNeg
funcs.doc: ModNeg returns (-a) mod n in [0, n). The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: ModPow
funcs.signature: func ModPow(base, exp, n int) int
funcs.doc:
ModPow returns base^exp mod n in [0, n) via binary exponentiation. The
exponent exp must be non-negative and the modulus n must be positive. It
panics if exp < 0.

funcs.name: ModSub
funcs.signature: func ModSub(a, b, n int) int
funcs.doc: ModSub returns (a - b) mod n in [0, n). The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: MultiplicativeOrder
funcs.doc:
MultiplicativeOrder returns the multiplicative order of a modulo n, the
smallest k > 0 with a^k ≡ 1 (mod n), together with true. It returns (0,
false) when gcd(a, n) != 1, since only units have a finite multiplicative
order. The modulus n must be positive.

funcs.name: PolyEqual
funcs.doc:
PolyEqual reports whether a and b are equal as polynomials, comparing
coefficients within tolerance after trimming.

funcs.name: PolyEval
funcs.signature: func PolyEval(a Poly, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
PolyEval returns the value a(x) evaluated at the point x using Horner's
method.

funcs.name: PolyModAdd
funcs.doc:
PolyModAdd returns the sum a + b of two polynomials over GF(p), with
coefficients reduced into [0, p). p must be prime.

funcs.name: PolyModDegree
funcs.doc:
PolyModDegree returns the degree of the polynomial a over GF(p): the index of
its highest non-zero coefficient after reduction mod p, or -1 for the zero
polynomial. p must be prime.

funcs.name: PolyModDivMod
funcs.signature: func PolyModDivMod(a, b []int, p int) (q, r []int)
funcs.doc:
PolyModDivMod returns the quotient q and remainder r of dividing a by b over
GF(p), satisfying a = q·b + r with deg(r) < deg(b). The divisor b must be
non-zero mod p; it panics otherwise. p must be prime.

funcs.name: PolyModEval
funcs.signature: func PolyModEval(a []int, x, p int) int
funcs.doc:
PolyModEval returns the value a(x) mod p evaluated at x over GF(p) using
Horner's method. p must be prime.

funcs.name: PolyModGCD
funcs.doc:
PolyModGCD returns the monic greatest common divisor of a and b over GF(p)
via the Euclidean algorithm. The GCD of two zero polynomials is the zero
polynomial. p must be prime.

funcs.name: PolyModMonic
funcs.doc:
PolyModMonic returns a scaled so its leading coefficient is 1 over GF(p). The
zero polynomial is returned unchanged. p must be prime.

funcs.name: PolyModMul
funcs.doc:
PolyModMul returns the product a·b of two polynomials over GF(p). p must be
prime.

funcs.name: PolyModSub
funcs.doc:
PolyModSub returns the difference a - b of two polynomials over GF(p). p must
be prime.

funcs.name: PrimitiveRoots
funcs.doc:
PrimitiveRoots returns every primitive root modulo n in [1, n) in increasing
order. The slice is empty when no primitive root exists (which happens
unless n is 1, 2, 4, p^k, or 2·p^k for an odd prime p). The argument n must
be positive.

funcs.name: SymmetricGroupOrder
funcs.doc:
SymmetricGroupOrder returns |S_n| = n!, the order of the symmetric group on
n points. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: UnitsModN
funcs.doc:
UnitsModN returns the elements of the multiplicative group (Z/nZ)* in
increasing order: every a in [1, n) with gcd(a, n) == 1. For n == 1 it
returns the single element {0} representing the trivial group. The argument
n must be positive.

packages.name: hypercomplex
packages.synopsis: Package hypercomplex implements arithmetic over several hypercomplex number systems that extend the real and complex numbers.
packages.doc:
Package hypercomplex implements arithmetic over several hypercomplex number
systems that extend the real and complex numbers.

The package provides:

  - Quaternion: Hamilton's quaternions, the four-dimensional normed division
    algebra. Includes multiplication, conjugation, norm, inverse, the
    exponential and logarithm maps, power, spherical linear interpolation
    (Slerp), rotation of 3-vectors, and conversions to and from axis-angle,
    Euler angles, and rotation matrices.
  - DualQuaternion: the tensor of the dual numbers with the quaternions,
    used to represent rigid-body transforms (screw motions) in a single
    algebraic object.
  - Octonion: the eight-dimensional non-associative normed division algebra,
    built from quaternions by the Cayley-Dickson construction.
  - SplitComplex: the split-complex (hyperbolic) numbers x + y*j with
    j*j = +1, the algebra underlying Lorentz boosts in 1+1 dimensions.

Every routine uses only the Go standard library and is deterministic. Angles
are expressed in radians throughout, and 3-vectors are represented by the
[Vec3] type defined in this package.

types.name: DualQuaternion
types.doc:
DualQuaternion is a dual quaternion Real + Dual*epsilon, where epsilon is the
dual unit with epsilon^2 = 0 and Real and Dual are ordinary [Quaternion]
values. A unit dual quaternion encodes a rigid-body transform: its real part
carries the rotation and its dual part carries the translation.

funcs.name: DualQuat
funcs.doc: DualQuat constructs a dual quaternion from its real and dual quaternion parts.

funcs.name: DualQuatFromRotationTranslation
funcs.signature: func DualQuatFromRotationTranslation(rot Quaternion, t Vec3) DualQuaternion
funcs.doc:
DualQuatFromRotationTranslation returns the unit dual quaternion for the rigid
transform that first rotates by the (unit) quaternion rot and then translates
by t. The rotation is normalized internally.

funcs.name: DualQuatFromTranslation
funcs.doc:
DualQuatFromTranslation returns the unit dual quaternion representing a pure
translation by t with no rotation.

funcs.name: IdentityDualQuat
funcs.doc:
IdentityDualQuat returns the identity dual quaternion (identity rotation, zero
translation).

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the component-wise sum of the two dual quaternions.

methods.name: Conjugate
methods.doc:
Conjugate returns the quaternion conjugate applied to both parts,
(Real*, Dual*). This is the conjugate used to transform points via the
sandwich product.

methods.name: DualNumberConjugate
methods.doc:
DualNumberConjugate returns the dual-number conjugate (Real, -Dual), which
negates the dual part while leaving the real part unchanged.

methods.name: Equal
methods.signature: func (d DualQuaternion) Equal(e DualQuaternion, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether d and e agree in both parts to within the absolute
tolerance tol.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the dual quaternion product d*e. Because epsilon^2 = 0, the
product is (Rd*Re) + (Rd*De + Dd*Re)*epsilon. Composing two rigid transforms
corresponds to multiplying their unit dual quaternions.

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc:
Norm returns the norm of a unit dual quaternion's real part, |Real|. For a
valid rigid transform this equals 1.

methods.name: Normalize
methods.doc:
Normalize returns d rescaled so that its real part has unit norm, dividing
both parts by |Real|. If the real part is zero, d is returned unchanged.

methods.name: Rotation
methods.doc:
Rotation returns the rotation component of d as a unit quaternion (its
normalized real part).

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns d with both parts multiplied by the real factor s.

methods.name: ScrewAngle
methods.doc:
ScrewAngle returns the rotation angle in radians of the rigid transform
encoded by d, in the range [0, pi]. It is a convenience wrapper over the
axis-angle decomposition of the rotation part.

methods.name: TransformPoint
methods.doc:
TransformPoint applies the rigid transform encoded by the unit dual
quaternion d to the point p, returning the rotated-then-translated point.

methods.name: Translation
methods.doc:
Translation returns the translation vector encoded by the unit dual
quaternion d, recovered as 2 * Dual * Real*.

types.name: Octonion
types.doc:
Octonion is an element of the eight-dimensional real algebra of octonions,
stored as the coefficients E0..E7 of the basis units e0=1, e1..e7. The
octonions form a normed division algebra that is neither commutative nor
associative, but is alternative (any two elements generate an associative
subalgebra).

funcs.name: Associator
funcs.signature: func Associator(o, p, q Octonion) Octonion
funcs.doc:
Associator returns the associator (o*p)*q - o*(p*q), a measure of the failure
of associativity. It vanishes whenever any two of the three arguments are
equal (the alternative-algebra property) but is generally non-zero otherwise.

funcs.name: Commutator
funcs.doc:
Commutator returns the commutator o*p - p*o, which vanishes exactly when o
and p commute. It is generally non-zero because octonion multiplication is
non-commutative.

funcs.name: IdentityOct
funcs.doc: IdentityOct returns the multiplicative identity octonion e0 = 1.

funcs.name: Oct
funcs.signature: func Oct(e0, e1, e2, e3, e4, e5, e6, e7 float64) Octonion
funcs.doc: Oct constructs an octonion from its eight real coefficients.

funcs.name: OctFromScalar
funcs.doc:
OctFromScalar returns the octonion whose real part is s and whose other seven
coefficients are zero.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the component-wise sum o + p.

methods.name: Conj
methods.doc:
Conj returns the octonion conjugate, which negates all seven imaginary
coefficients and leaves the real part unchanged.

methods.name: Dot
methods.doc: Dot returns the Euclidean dot product of o and p treated as 8-vectors.

methods.name: Equal
methods.signature: func (o Octonion) Equal(p Octonion, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether o and p agree in every coefficient to within the
absolute tolerance tol.

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse returns the multiplicative inverse conj(o)/|o|^2. A zero octonion is
returned unchanged.

methods.name: IsUnit
methods.doc: IsUnit reports whether o has unit norm to within the absolute tolerance tol.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the octonion product o*p computed by the Cayley-Dickson formula
(a,b)(c,d) = (a*c - conj(d)*b, d*a + b*conj(c)) over the quaternions.
Multiplication is neither commutative nor associative.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns the additive inverse -o.

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc: Norm returns the Euclidean norm |o| = sqrt(NormSq).

methods.name: NormSq
methods.doc:
NormSq returns the squared norm, the sum of the squares of the eight
coefficients, equal to the real part of o * conj(o).

methods.name: Real
methods.doc: Real returns the real (scalar) part E0 of o.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns o with every coefficient multiplied by the real factor s.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the component-wise difference o - p.

types.name: Quaternion
types.doc:
Quaternion is a Hamilton quaternion q = W + X*i + Y*j + Z*k, where i, j and k
satisfy i*i = j*j = k*k = i*j*k = -1. W is the scalar (real) part and
(X, Y, Z) is the vector (imaginary) part.

funcs.name: IdentityQuat
funcs.doc: IdentityQuat returns the multiplicative identity quaternion 1 + 0i + 0j + 0k.

funcs.name: Quat
funcs.signature: func Quat(w, x, y, z float64) Quaternion
funcs.doc:
Quat constructs a quaternion from its scalar part w and vector part
(x, y, z).

funcs.name: QuatFromAxisAngle
funcs.signature: func QuatFromAxisAngle(axis Vec3, angle float64) Quaternion
funcs.doc:
QuatFromAxisAngle returns the unit quaternion representing a rotation of angle
radians about axis. The axis is normalized internally; a zero axis yields the
identity quaternion.

funcs.name: QuatFromEuler
funcs.signature: func QuatFromEuler(roll, pitch, yaw float64) Quaternion
funcs.doc:
QuatFromEuler returns the unit quaternion for the intrinsic Tait-Bryan
rotation with the given roll (about X), pitch (about Y) and yaw (about Z),
applied in the aerospace Z-Y-X order (yaw, then pitch, then roll). Angles are
in radians.

funcs.name: QuatFromRotationMatrix
funcs.doc:
QuatFromRotationMatrix returns the unit quaternion corresponding to the
row-major 3x3 rotation matrix m (nine elements m00, m01, m02, m10, ...). The
implementation uses the numerically stable trace method and normalizes the
result.

funcs.name: QuatFromScalar
funcs.doc:
QuatFromScalar returns the quaternion whose scalar part is s and whose vector
part is zero.

funcs.name: QuatFromVector
funcs.doc:
QuatFromVector returns the pure quaternion 0 + v (a quaternion with zero
scalar part), used to embed a 3-vector into the quaternions.

funcs.name: Slerp
funcs.signature: func Slerp(q0, q1 Quaternion, t float64) Quaternion
funcs.doc:
Slerp returns the spherical linear interpolation between the unit quaternions
q0 and q1 at parameter t in [0, 1]. The interpolation follows the shortest
arc (choosing the sign of q1 that minimizes the angle) and falls back to
normalized linear interpolation when the endpoints are nearly parallel.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the component-wise sum q + r.

methods.name: Conj
methods.doc: Conj returns the conjugate q* = W - X*i - Y*j - Z*k.

methods.name: Dot
methods.doc: Dot returns the Euclidean dot product of q and r treated as 4-vectors.

methods.name: Equal
methods.signature: func (q Quaternion) Equal(r Quaternion, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether q and r agree component-wise to within the absolute
tolerance tol.

methods.name: Exp
methods.doc:
Exp returns the quaternion exponential exp(q). Writing q = w + v with v the
vector part and theta = |v|, exp(q) = e^w (cos theta + (v/theta) sin theta).

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse returns the multiplicative inverse q^-1 = q* / |q|^2. A zero
quaternion is returned unchanged.

methods.name: IsUnit
methods.doc: IsUnit reports whether q has unit norm to within the absolute tolerance tol.

methods.name: Log
methods.doc:
Log returns the principal quaternion logarithm log(q). Writing q = |q|
(cos phi + n sin phi) with n the unit vector part, log(q) = ln|q| + n*phi,
where phi = atan2(|v|, w). A zero quaternion is returned unchanged.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the Hamilton product q*r. Quaternion multiplication is
associative but not commutative.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns the additive inverse -q.

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc: Norm returns the Euclidean norm |q| = sqrt(W^2 + X^2 + Y^2 + Z^2).

methods.name: NormSq
methods.doc:
NormSq returns the squared norm W^2 + X^2 + Y^2 + Z^2, equal to the scalar
part of q * q*.

methods.name: Normalize
methods.doc:
Normalize returns q scaled to unit norm. A zero quaternion is returned
unchanged.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns q raised to the real power t, computed as exp(t*log(q)). For a
unit quaternion this scales the rotation angle by t.

methods.name: RotateVector
methods.doc:
RotateVector returns the image of v under the rotation represented by q,
computed as the vector part of q * (0,v) * q^-1. The quaternion need not be a
unit quaternion; any non-zero scaling cancels.

methods.name: Scalar
methods.doc: Scalar returns the scalar (real) part W of q.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns q with every component multiplied by the real factor s.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the component-wise difference q - r.

methods.name: ToAxisAngle
methods.signature: func (q Quaternion) ToAxisAngle() (axis Vec3, angle float64)
methods.doc:
ToAxisAngle returns the rotation axis (a unit vector) and angle in radians
(in the range [0, pi]) represented by q. The quaternion is normalized
internally. For the identity rotation the axis defaults to (1, 0, 0).

methods.name: ToEuler
methods.signature: func (q Quaternion) ToEuler() (roll, pitch, yaw float64)
methods.doc:
ToEuler returns the roll (about X), pitch (about Y) and yaw (about Z) angles
in radians that reproduce the rotation of q under the aerospace Z-Y-X
convention (the inverse of [QuatFromEuler]). The pitch is clamped to
[-pi/2, pi/2].

methods.name: ToRotationMatrix
methods.doc:
ToRotationMatrix returns the row-major 3x3 rotation matrix (nine elements
m00, m01, m02, m10, ...) equivalent to the rotation represented by q. The
quaternion is normalized internally.

methods.name: Vector
methods.doc: Vector returns the vector (imaginary) part (X, Y, Z) of q as a [Vec3].

types.name: SplitComplex
types.doc:
SplitComplex is a split-complex (hyperbolic) number z = Re + Im*j, where the
unit j satisfies j*j = +1 (in contrast to the ordinary imaginary unit).
Split-complex numbers model Lorentz boosts in 1+1 spacetime dimensions.

funcs.name: Split
funcs.doc: Split constructs a split-complex number Re + Im*j.

funcs.name: SplitFromLightCone
funcs.doc:
SplitFromLightCone reconstructs a split-complex number from its null-basis
coordinates (u, v), returning ((u+v)/2) + ((u-v)/2)*j.

funcs.name: SplitFromModulusArgument
funcs.doc:
SplitFromModulusArgument returns the time-like split-complex number with the
given non-negative modulus r and rapidity phi, namely
r*(cosh phi + j sinh phi).

methods.name: Abs
methods.doc:
Abs returns sqrt(|ModulusSq|), a non-negative magnitude that is multiplicative
in absolute value.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the sum z + w.

methods.name: Argument
methods.doc:
Argument returns the hyperbolic argument (rapidity) atanh(Im/Re) of z. It is
well defined only in the right time-like sector Re > |Im|; elsewhere the
result may be non-finite.

methods.name: Conj
methods.doc: Conj returns the conjugate Re - Im*j.

methods.name: Equal
methods.signature: func (z SplitComplex) Equal(w SplitComplex, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether z and w agree in both parts to within the absolute
tolerance tol.

methods.name: Exp
methods.doc: Exp returns the split-complex exponential exp(z) = e^Re (cosh Im + j sinh Im).

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse returns the multiplicative inverse conj(z)/ModulusSq, together with a
boolean that is false when z is a zero divisor (ModulusSq = 0), in which case
the inverse does not exist and the zero element is returned.

methods.name: IsZeroDivisor
methods.doc:
IsZeroDivisor reports whether z lies on the null cone Re = ±Im (with z not the
zero element) to within the absolute tolerance tol, in which case z has no
multiplicative inverse.

methods.name: LightCone
methods.doc:
LightCone returns the coordinates (u, v) of z in the null (diagonal) basis,
with u = Re + Im and v = Re - Im. In this basis multiplication acts
component-wise: (u1,v1)*(u2,v2) = (u1*u2, v1*v2).

methods.name: ModulusSq
methods.doc:
ModulusSq returns the split-complex squared modulus Re^2 - Im^2, equal to the
real part of z * conj(z). It is the quadratic form preserved by
split-complex multiplication and may be negative or zero.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the product z*w. Using j*j = +1,
(a+bj)(c+dj) = (ac+bd) + (ad+bc)j. Multiplication is commutative.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns the additive inverse -z.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns z with both parts multiplied by the real factor s.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the difference z - w.

types.name: Vec3
types.doc:
Vec3 is a three-dimensional real vector. It is used for the vector (imaginary)
part of quaternions and as the argument and result of rotations.

funcs.name: V3
funcs.signature: func V3(x, y, z float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: V3 constructs a [Vec3] from its three components.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the component-wise sum v + w.

methods.name: Cross
methods.doc: Cross returns the cross product v×w.

methods.name: Dot
methods.doc: Dot returns the Euclidean dot product v·w.

methods.name: Equal
methods.signature: func (v Vec3) Equal(w Vec3, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether v and w agree component-wise to within the absolute
tolerance tol.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns the additive inverse -v.

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc: Norm returns the Euclidean length |v|.

methods.name: Normalize
methods.doc:
Normalize returns v scaled to unit length. If v is the zero vector it is
returned unchanged.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns the vector v scaled by the real factor s.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the component-wise difference v - w.

packages.name: infotheory
packages.synopsis: Package infotheory implements classical information theory: entropy measures, divergences, discrete channel models and error-control codes.
packages.doc:
Package infotheory implements classical information theory: entropy
measures, divergences, discrete channel models and error-control codes.

The package is organised around discrete probability distributions
represented as plain []float64 slices whose entries are non-negative and
sum to one, and around joint distributions represented as row-major
[][]float64 matrices whose entries sum to one. Unless stated otherwise all
entropic quantities are expressed in bits (base-2 logarithms); companion
routines suffixed with "Nat" report the same quantity in nats (natural
logarithms), and BitsToNats / NatsToBits convert between the two.

The following families are covered:

  - entropy: Entropy (Shannon), EntropyBase, EntropyNat, BinaryEntropy,
    Surprisal, Perplexity, NormalizedEntropy, Redundancy, GiniImpurity and
    the Renyi family (RenyiEntropy, CollisionEntropy, MinEntropy,
    HartleyEntropy, TsallisEntropy);
  - joint and relative measures: JointEntropy, ConditionalEntropyYgivenX,
    ConditionalEntropyXgivenY, MutualInformation, NormalizedMutualInformation,
    VariationOfInformation, KLDivergence, CrossEntropy,
    JensenShannonDivergence and JensenShannonDistance;
  - channels: the binary symmetric channel (BSC), binary erasure channel
    (BEC), the general discrete memoryless Channel, and the Blahut-Arimoto
    algorithm for numerically computing channel capacity;
  - source coding: Huffman coding (BuildHuffmanTree, HuffmanCodes,
    HuffmanEncode, HuffmanDecode) and ShannonFanoCodes;
  - error-control coding: Hamming(7,4) encode/decode with single-error
    correction, Hamming weight and distance helpers;
  - inequalities and rates: the Kraft-McMillan inequality (KraftSum,
    KraftInequality, KraftEquality, McMillanInequality), the Fano
    inequality bound and code-rate helpers.

All routines are deterministic and depend only on the Go standard library.

vars.signature: var ErrChannelShape = errors.New("infotheory: inconsistent channel shape")
vars.doc:
ErrChannelShape is returned when a channel transition matrix or an input
distribution has an inconsistent or empty shape.

vars.signature: var ErrHamming = errors.New("infotheory: mismatched lengths")
vars.doc:
ErrHamming is returned by the Hamming distance helpers when their operands
have mismatched lengths.

vars.signature: var ErrHuffman = errors.New("infotheory: invalid huffman input")
vars.doc:
ErrHuffman is returned by the Huffman routines when their input is empty or
otherwise malformed (for example encoding a symbol with no codeword or
decoding an incomplete bit string).

vars.signature: var ErrNotNormalizable = errors.New("infotheory: weights sum to a non-positive value")
vars.doc:
ErrNotNormalizable is returned when a set of weights or counts cannot be
turned into a probability distribution because their total is not positive.

types.name: BEC
types.doc:
BEC models a binary erasure channel: each transmitted bit is independently
erased (replaced by an erasure symbol) with probability Erasure, which must
lie in [0,1], and is otherwise received correctly.

methods.name: Capacity
methods.doc:
Capacity returns the channel capacity of the binary erasure channel in bits
per channel use, C = 1 - e, where e is the erasure probability. It is
achieved by a uniform input.

methods.name: Channel
methods.doc:
Channel returns the discrete-memoryless Channel representation of the binary
erasure channel. The two inputs map to three outputs {0, 1, erasure} with
transition rows [1-e, 0, e] and [0, 1-e, e].

types.name: BSC
types.doc:
BSC models a binary symmetric channel: each transmitted bit is independently
flipped with crossover probability Crossover, which must lie in [0,1].

methods.name: Capacity
methods.doc:
Capacity returns the channel capacity of the binary symmetric channel in
bits per channel use, C = 1 - H_b(p), where p is the crossover probability
and H_b is the binary entropy function. It is achieved by a uniform input.

methods.name: Channel
methods.doc:
Channel returns the discrete-memoryless Channel representation of the binary
symmetric channel, a 2x2 transition matrix with rows [1-p, p] and [p, 1-p].

types.name: Channel
types.doc:
Channel is a discrete memoryless channel described by its transition
probability matrix. Transition[i][j] is P(Y=j | X=i); each row must be a
probability distribution over the output alphabet.

methods.name: ConditionalEntropy
methods.doc:
ConditionalEntropy returns the conditional entropy H(Y|X) in bits for the
given input distribution, H(Y|X) = sum_i input_i H(Transition[i]). It returns
ErrChannelShape for a mismatched input distribution.

methods.name: MutualInformation
methods.doc:
MutualInformation returns the mutual information I(X;Y) in bits between the
channel input, distributed as input, and the channel output. It equals
sum_i input_i sum_j T_ij log2(T_ij / py_j) with py the induced output
distribution. It returns ErrChannelShape for a mismatched input.

methods.name: NumInputs
methods.doc: NumInputs returns the size of the channel's input alphabet.

methods.name: NumOutputs
methods.doc:
NumOutputs returns the size of the channel's output alphabet, or zero for a
channel with no inputs.

methods.name: OutputDistribution
methods.doc:
OutputDistribution returns the distribution of the output Y induced by the
input distribution input, that is P(Y=j) = sum_i input_i Transition[i][j]. It
returns ErrChannelShape if input does not have one entry per channel input.

types.name: HuffmanNode
types.doc:
HuffmanNode is a node of a Huffman code tree. Leaf nodes carry a Symbol and
its Weight; internal nodes carry the summed Weight of their subtree and have
non-nil Left and Right children. The empty Symbol is used for internal nodes.

funcs.name: BuildHuffmanTree
funcs.doc:
BuildHuffmanTree builds an optimal prefix-code tree for the symbols in
weights, whose keys are symbols and values are non-negative weights
(probabilities or frequencies). Symbols are processed in ascending key order
and ties in weight are broken deterministically, so the tree is reproducible.
It returns the root of the tree, or ErrHuffman if weights is empty.

methods.name: Codewords
methods.doc:
Codewords returns the binary codeword for every symbol in the tree rooted at
n, assigning "0" to each left branch and "1" to each right branch. A tree
consisting of a single leaf assigns that symbol the codeword "0". The
returned map is keyed by symbol.

methods.name: IsLeaf
methods.doc: IsLeaf reports whether the node is a leaf (has no children).

funcs.name: AverageCodeLength
funcs.signature: func AverageCodeLength(codes map[string]string, probs map[string]float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AverageCodeLength returns the expected codeword length sum_s p_s len(code_s)
for the given codewords under the probability distribution probs, both keyed
by symbol. Symbols present in probs but absent from codes are ignored.

funcs.name: BECCapacity
funcs.doc:
BECCapacity returns the capacity in bits of a binary erasure channel with
erasure probability e. It is a convenience wrapper for BEC{e}.Capacity.

funcs.name: BSCCapacity
funcs.doc:
BSCCapacity returns the capacity in bits of a binary symmetric channel with
crossover probability p. It is a convenience wrapper for BSC{p}.Capacity.

funcs.name: BinaryEntropy
funcs.doc:
BinaryEntropy returns the binary entropy function
H_b(p) = -p log2 p - (1-p) log2 (1-p), the entropy in bits of a Bernoulli
random variable with success probability p. It is defined for p in [0,1],
with H_b(0) = H_b(1) = 0, and peaks at H_b(0.5) = 1.

funcs.name: BitsToNats
funcs.doc:
BitsToNats converts an information quantity expressed in bits (base-2 units)
to nats (natural-logarithm units) by multiplying by ln 2.

funcs.name: BlahutArimoto
funcs.signature: func BlahutArimoto(ch Channel, tol float64, maxIter int) (capacity float64, input []float64, err error)
funcs.doc:
BlahutArimoto computes the capacity of the discrete memoryless channel ch by
the Blahut-Arimoto algorithm. It returns the capacity in bits and the
capacity-achieving input distribution. Iteration stops when the gap between
the upper and lower capacity bounds falls below tol or after maxIter
iterations, whichever comes first; a non-positive maxIter defaults to 1000
and a non-positive tol to 1e-10. It returns ErrChannelShape for an empty
channel.

funcs.name: ChannelCapacity
funcs.doc:
ChannelCapacity returns the capacity in bits of the discrete memoryless
channel ch, computed via BlahutArimoto with default tolerance and iteration
limit. The capacity-achieving input distribution is discarded.

funcs.name: CodeRate
funcs.doc:
CodeRate returns the rate k/n of a block code that maps k information symbols
to n coded symbols. It returns zero for a non-positive n.

funcs.name: CodingEfficiency
funcs.doc:
CodingEfficiency returns the efficiency of a code, the ratio of the source
entropy in bits to the average codeword length: H / avgLength. A value of one
indicates an optimal code that attains the entropy bound. It returns zero for
a non-positive average length.

funcs.name: CollisionEntropy
funcs.doc:
CollisionEntropy returns the Renyi entropy of order two,
-log2(sum_i p_i^2), measured in bits. It is the negative log of the
collision (repeat) probability of two independent samples.

funcs.name: ConditionalEntropyXgivenY
funcs.doc:
ConditionalEntropyXgivenY returns the conditional entropy H(X|Y) =
H(X,Y) - H(Y) of the joint distribution joint, measured in bits.

funcs.name: ConditionalEntropyYgivenX
funcs.doc:
ConditionalEntropyYgivenX returns the conditional entropy H(Y|X) =
H(X,Y) - H(X) of the joint distribution joint, measured in bits. It is the
average remaining uncertainty about the column variable Y once the row
variable X is known.

funcs.name: CrossEntropy
funcs.doc:
CrossEntropy returns the cross entropy H(p,q) = -sum_i p_i log2 q_i between a
true distribution p and a model distribution q, measured in bits. It equals
H(p) + D(p||q) and is infinite if some outcome has p_i > 0 while q_i == 0.
p and q must have equal length; a mismatch returns NaN.

funcs.name: CrossEntropyNat
funcs.doc: CrossEntropyNat returns the cross entropy H(p,q) measured in nats.

funcs.name: EmpiricalDistribution
funcs.doc:
EmpiricalDistribution returns the empirical probability distribution of the
samples together with the slice of distinct values, sorted ascending, that
index it. Entry i of the returned distribution is the fraction of samples
equal to the i-th distinct value. It returns ErrNotNormalizable when samples
is empty.

funcs.name: Entropy
funcs.doc:
Entropy returns the Shannon entropy H(p) = -sum_i p_i log2 p_i of the
discrete probability distribution p, measured in bits. Zero-probability
outcomes contribute nothing (0 log 0 = 0). The distribution is assumed to be
normalised; entries that are non-positive are ignored.

funcs.name: EntropyBase
funcs.doc:
EntropyBase returns the Shannon entropy of p measured in units of the given
logarithm base (for example base 2 for bits, math.E for nats, base 10 for
bans/hartleys). The base must be greater than one.

funcs.name: EntropyNat
funcs.doc:
EntropyNat returns the Shannon entropy of p measured in nats (natural
logarithm units): H(p) = -sum_i p_i ln p_i.

funcs.name: EntropyRate
funcs.doc:
EntropyRate returns the entropy rate H(p)/L in bits per symbol of a source
with per-block entropy H(p) emitting blocks of length L symbols. It returns
zero for a non-positive L.

funcs.name: FanoInequalityBound
funcs.signature: func FanoInequalityBound(errorProb float64, alphabet int) float64
funcs.doc:
FanoInequalityBound returns the Fano inequality's upper bound on the
conditional entropy H(X|Y) in bits, given the probability of error errorProb
of an estimator of X and the size alphabet of the alphabet of X:
H_b(P_e) + P_e log2(|X|-1). Any decoder achieving error probability errorProb
must have conditional entropy no greater than this bound. errorProb must lie
in [0,1] and alphabet must be at least one.

funcs.name: GiniImpurity
funcs.doc:
GiniImpurity returns the Gini impurity 1 - sum_i p_i^2 of the distribution p,
the probability that two independent samples fall in different categories. It
ranges from 0 (a point mass) to 1 - 1/n (the uniform distribution over n
outcomes).

funcs.name: Hamming74Decode
funcs.signature: func Hamming74Decode(code byte) (data byte, errorPos int, corrected bool)
funcs.doc:
Hamming74Decode decodes a 7-bit Hamming(7,4) codeword held in the low seven
bits of code, correcting any single-bit error. It returns the recovered four
data bits in the low nibble, the 1-indexed position of the corrected error
(0 if no error was detected), and a flag reporting whether a correction was
applied. Because Hamming(7,4) has minimum distance three it corrects any
single-bit error but cannot reliably detect two-bit errors.

funcs.name: Hamming74Encode
funcs.doc:
Hamming74Encode encodes the four data bits in the low nibble of data (bit 0
is d1, bit 1 is d2, bit 2 is d3, bit 3 is d4) into a 7-bit Hamming(7,4)
codeword returned in the low seven bits of a byte. The bit positions 1..7
are laid out as p1, p2, d1, p4, d2, d3, d4 with parity bits
p1 = d1^d2^d4, p2 = d1^d3^d4 and p4 = d2^d3^d4. Only the low four bits of
data are used.

funcs.name: Hamming74Syndrome
funcs.doc:
Hamming74Syndrome returns the error-position syndrome of a 7-bit Hamming(7,4)
codeword held in the low seven bits of code. A result of zero indicates no
detected error; a non-zero result in 1..7 is the 1-indexed bit position at
which a single-bit error is located.

funcs.name: HammingDistanceBits
funcs.doc:
HammingDistanceBits returns the Hamming distance between a and b, the number
of bit positions in which their binary representations differ.

funcs.name: HammingDistanceBytes
funcs.doc:
HammingDistanceBytes returns the bit-level Hamming distance between two equal
length byte slices, the total number of differing bits across all bytes. It
returns ErrHamming if the slices differ in length.

funcs.name: HammingDistanceStrings
funcs.doc:
HammingDistanceStrings returns the symbol-level Hamming distance between two
equal-length strings, the number of positions at which the corresponding
bytes differ. It returns ErrHamming if the strings differ in length.

funcs.name: HammingWeight
funcs.doc:
HammingWeight returns the Hamming weight of x, the number of 1-bits in its
binary representation.

funcs.name: HartleyEntropy
funcs.doc:
HartleyEntropy returns the Hartley (max-) entropy log2(k), where k is the
number of outcomes with strictly positive probability in p, measured in
bits. It is the Renyi entropy of order zero and depends only on the support.

funcs.name: HuffmanCodes
funcs.doc:
HuffmanCodes builds a Huffman tree for weights and returns the map from each
symbol to its binary codeword. It is a convenience wrapper combining
BuildHuffmanTree and Codewords, and returns ErrHuffman for empty input.

funcs.name: HuffmanDecode
funcs.doc:
HuffmanDecode decodes a bit string of '0' and '1' characters back into the
sequence of symbols, walking the tree rooted at root one bit at a time ('0'
goes left, '1' goes right). It returns ErrHuffman if the tree is nil, a bit
other than '0' or '1' is encountered, or the bit string ends in the middle of
a codeword.

funcs.name: HuffmanEncode
funcs.doc:
HuffmanEncode concatenates the codewords for the given sequence of symbols
into a single bit string of '0' and '1' characters. It returns ErrHuffman if
any symbol has no codeword in codes.

funcs.name: IsPrefixFree
funcs.doc:
IsPrefixFree reports whether the set of binary codewords is prefix-free: no
codeword is a prefix of another. A prefix-free code is uniquely and
instantaneously decodable. Duplicate codewords make the code not prefix-free.

funcs.name: IsProbabilityDistribution
funcs.doc:
IsProbabilityDistribution reports whether p consists of non-negative entries
summing to one within the absolute tolerance tol. A negative entry always
makes the report false.

funcs.name: JensenShannonDistance
funcs.doc:
JensenShannonDistance returns the square root of the JensenShannonDivergence
of p and q. This quantity is a true metric satisfying the triangle
inequality, measured in sqrt-bits.

funcs.name: JensenShannonDivergence
funcs.doc:
JensenShannonDivergence returns the Jensen-Shannon divergence between
distributions p and q, measured in bits: JSD = 0.5 D(p||m) + 0.5 D(q||m)
with m = (p+q)/2. Unlike the KL divergence it is symmetric, always finite,
and bounded in [0,1] bits. p and q must have equal length; a mismatch returns
NaN.

funcs.name: JointEntropy
funcs.doc:
JointEntropy returns the joint Shannon entropy H(X,Y) = -sum_ij p_ij log2
p_ij of the joint distribution joint, measured in bits.

funcs.name: KLDivergence
funcs.doc:
KLDivergence returns the Kullback-Leibler divergence D(p||q) =
sum_i p_i log2(p_i/q_i) of distribution p from reference distribution q,
measured in bits. It is non-negative and zero exactly when p equals q. If any
outcome has p_i > 0 while q_i == 0 the divergence is infinite. p and q must
have equal length; a mismatch returns NaN.

funcs.name: KLDivergenceNat
funcs.doc:
KLDivergenceNat returns the Kullback-Leibler divergence D(p||q) measured in
nats (natural-logarithm units).

funcs.name: KraftEquality
funcs.doc:
KraftEquality reports whether the codeword lengths make the Kraft sum equal to
one within a small tolerance, the condition characterising a complete (no
wasted codeword) prefix code such as an optimal Huffman code.

funcs.name: KraftInequality
funcs.doc:
KraftInequality reports whether the codeword lengths satisfy the Kraft
inequality sum_i base^{-l_i} <= 1, the necessary and sufficient condition for
a prefix code with those lengths to exist over an alphabet of the given base.
A small tolerance absorbs floating-point round-off.

funcs.name: KraftSum
funcs.doc:
KraftSum returns the Kraft sum sum_i base^{-l_i} of a code whose codeword
lengths are l_i, over an alphabet of the given base (2 for binary). Lengths
that are non-positive are treated as zero-length and contribute base^0 = 1.
The base must be at least two.

funcs.name: MarginalX
funcs.doc:
MarginalX returns the marginal distribution of the row variable X of the
joint distribution given as a row-major matrix, where joint[i][j] is the
probability P(X=i, Y=j). The result has one entry per row, each the sum of
that row.

funcs.name: MarginalY
funcs.doc:
MarginalY returns the marginal distribution of the column variable Y of the
joint distribution joint, where joint[i][j] is P(X=i, Y=j). The result has
one entry per column, each the sum of that column. It returns nil for an
empty joint distribution.

funcs.name: McMillanInequality
funcs.doc:
McMillanInequality reports whether the codeword lengths satisfy the
McMillan inequality sum_i base^{-l_i} <= 1. By McMillan's theorem this is the
same numerical condition as the Kraft inequality, but it is the necessary and
sufficient condition for a uniquely decodable (not merely prefix) code with
those lengths to exist.

funcs.name: MinEntropy
funcs.doc:
MinEntropy returns the min-entropy -log2(max_i p_i) of the distribution p,
measured in bits. It is the Renyi entropy in the limit of infinite order and
gives a worst-case (most conservative) measure of uncertainty.

funcs.name: MutualInformation
funcs.doc:
MutualInformation returns the mutual information I(X;Y) =
H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) of the joint distribution joint, measured in bits. It is
non-negative, symmetric in X and Y, and zero exactly when X and Y are
independent.

funcs.name: NatsToBits
funcs.doc:
NatsToBits converts an information quantity expressed in nats to bits by
dividing by ln 2.

funcs.name: NormalizeCounts
funcs.doc:
NormalizeCounts returns a probability distribution proportional to the
supplied integer counts, dividing each count by their total. It returns
ErrNotNormalizable if the counts do not sum to a positive value.

funcs.name: NormalizeWeights
funcs.doc:
NormalizeWeights returns a probability distribution proportional to the
supplied non-negative weights, that is each weight divided by their total. It
returns ErrNotNormalizable if the weights do not sum to a positive value.

funcs.name: NormalizedEntropy
funcs.doc:
NormalizedEntropy returns the entropy efficiency H(p)/log2(n), where n is the
number of outcomes len(p). The result lies in [0,1], reaching one for the
uniform distribution. For n < 2 it returns zero, the maximum entropy being
itself zero.

funcs.name: NormalizedMutualInformation
funcs.doc:
NormalizedMutualInformation returns the mutual information of joint scaled to
[0,1] by the geometric mean of the marginal entropies:
I(X;Y) / sqrt(H(X) H(Y)). If either marginal entropy is zero the value is
defined to be zero.

funcs.name: Parity
funcs.doc:
Parity returns the parity bit of x: 1 if x has an odd number of 1-bits and 0
otherwise (even parity).

funcs.name: Perplexity
funcs.doc:
Perplexity returns the perplexity 2^H(p) of the distribution p, where H is
the Shannon entropy in bits. It equals the effective number of equally likely
outcomes and reaches its maximum, len(p), for the uniform distribution.

funcs.name: Redundancy
funcs.doc:
Redundancy returns 1 - NormalizedEntropy(p), the fraction of the maximum
entropy that is unused by the distribution p. It lies in [0,1] and is zero
for the uniform distribution.

funcs.name: RenyiEntropy
funcs.doc:
RenyiEntropy returns the Renyi entropy of order alpha of the distribution p,
measured in bits. For alpha != 1 it is 1/(1-alpha) * log2(sum_i p_i^alpha).
Special orders are handled by their limits: alpha == 1 yields the Shannon
Entropy, alpha == 0 yields the HartleyEntropy (log2 of the support size), and
alpha == +Inf yields the MinEntropy. alpha must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ShannonFanoCodes
funcs.doc:
ShannonFanoCodes returns a Shannon-Fano prefix code for the symbols in
weights, keyed by symbol. Symbols are sorted by descending weight (ties
broken by symbol) and recursively split into two groups of as-equal-as-
possible total weight, the upper group receiving a '0' bit and the lower a
'1'. Shannon-Fano codes are prefix-free but, unlike Huffman codes, are not
always optimal. It returns ErrHuffman for empty input.

funcs.name: Surprisal
funcs.doc:
Surprisal returns the self-information (surprisal) -log2 p of an outcome with
probability p, measured in bits. An impossible outcome (p <= 0) has infinite
surprisal.

funcs.name: SurprisalBase
funcs.doc:
SurprisalBase returns the self-information of an outcome with probability p
measured in units of the given logarithm base. The base must exceed one.

funcs.name: SurprisalNat
funcs.doc:
SurprisalNat returns the self-information of an outcome with probability p
measured in nats: -ln p.

funcs.name: TsallisEntropy
funcs.doc:
TsallisEntropy returns the Tsallis entropy of order q of the distribution p,
S_q = (1 - sum_i p_i^q) / (q - 1). For q == 1 it reduces, by its limit, to
the Shannon entropy expressed in nats. The Tsallis entropy is a
non-logarithmic generalisation of Shannon entropy used in non-extensive
statistics.

funcs.name: UniformDistribution
funcs.doc:
UniformDistribution returns the uniform probability distribution over n
outcomes, a slice of length n whose every entry is 1/n. It returns nil for
n <= 0.

funcs.name: VariationOfInformation
funcs.doc:
VariationOfInformation returns the variation of information
VI(X;Y) = H(X|Y) + H(Y|X) = 2 H(X,Y) - H(X) - H(Y) of the joint distribution
joint, measured in bits. It is a true metric on the space of partitions and
is zero exactly when X and Y determine each other.

packages.name: interp
packages.synopsis: Package interp implements interpolation and approximation of one- and multi-dimensional sampled data.
packages.doc:
Package interp implements interpolation and approximation of one- and
multi-dimensional sampled data.

The one-dimensional constructors take a strictly increasing set of abscissae
x together with matching ordinates y and return an interpolant object whose
Eval method reconstructs a continuous function passing through (or, for the
approximating variants, near) the samples. The package covers:

  - Piecewise-linear interpolation (LinearInterp), together with the raw
    scalar helpers Lerp, InverseLerp and LinearAt.
  - Nearest-neighbour (NearestInterp) and piecewise-constant step
    interpolation (StepInterp).
  - Cubic splines with natural, clamped and not-a-knot boundary conditions
    (CubicSpline), each supporting evaluation, the first three derivatives
    and definite integration in closed form.
  - The Akima spline (AkimaSpline), which suppresses the spurious
    oscillations a global cubic spline can exhibit near sharp features.
  - Monotone piecewise cubic Hermite interpolation (PCHIP), which preserves
    the shape and monotonicity of the underlying data.
  - Bilinear and trilinear interpolation on rectilinear grids (BilinearGrid,
    TrilinearGrid) with the scalar helpers BilinearAt and TrilinearAt.

Every routine is implemented with the Go standard library only, is
deterministic, and validates its results against closed-form references in
the accompanying tests.

vars.doc: Sentinel errors returned by the constructors in this package.

types.name: AkimaSpline
types.doc:
AkimaSpline is a piecewise-cubic Hermite interpolant using Akima's local
slope estimates. Because each slope depends only on nearby samples, the
spline resists the spurious oscillations that a global cubic spline can
produce near abrupt changes in the data.

funcs.name: NewAkimaSpline
funcs.doc:
NewAkimaSpline builds an Akima spline through the samples (x[i], y[i]). x must
be strictly increasing and hold at least three points.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the first and last abscissae of the samples.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the spline value at x.

methods.name: EvalDerivative
methods.doc: EvalDerivative returns the first derivative of the spline at x.

methods.name: EvalSecondDerivative
methods.doc: EvalSecondDerivative returns the second derivative of the spline at x.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice returns the spline evaluated at every point of xs.

methods.name: Integral
methods.doc:
Integral returns the definite integral of the spline from a to b, with limits
clamped to the sampled domain.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of samples.

methods.name: Xs
methods.doc: Xs returns a copy of the sample abscissae.

methods.name: Ys
methods.doc: Ys returns a copy of the sample ordinates.

types.name: BSpline
types.doc: BSpline is a reusable vector-valued B-spline curve.

funcs.name: NewBSpline
funcs.signature: func NewBSpline(degree int, knots []float64, ctrl [][]float64) (*BSpline, error)
funcs.doc:
NewBSpline builds a B-spline of the given degree from the knot vector and
control points. The knot vector length must equal len(ctrl) + degree + 1, the
degree must be at least one, there must be at least degree+1 control points,
and all control points must share a dimension.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc: Degree reports the polynomial degree of the B-spline.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the valid parameter range [lo, hi] of the curve.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the point on the curve at parameter t, clamped to the domain.

types.name: BarycentricInterpolator
types.doc:
BarycentricInterpolator is a reusable interpolant that evaluates in linear
time via precomputed barycentric weights.

funcs.name: NewBarycentricInterpolator
funcs.doc:
NewBarycentricInterpolator builds a BarycentricInterpolator from the samples
(x[i], y[i]). It requires at least one point, matching lengths and distinct
abscissae.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the interpolating polynomial evaluated at xq.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the interpolant at every element of xs.

methods.name: Weights
methods.doc: Weights returns a copy of the barycentric weights.

types.name: Bezier
types.doc: Bezier is a reusable vector-valued Bezier curve of arbitrary degree.

funcs.name: NewBezier
funcs.doc:
NewBezier builds a Bezier curve from the given control points. It requires at
least one control point and a consistent dimension across all of them.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree reports the polynomial degree of the curve, one less than the number
of control points.

methods.name: Derivative
methods.doc: Derivative returns the derivative vector of the curve at parameter t.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the point on the curve at parameter t in [0, 1].

types.name: BilinearGrid
types.doc:
BilinearGrid is a bilinear interpolant on a rectilinear grid. The value at
grid point (x[i], y[j]) is z[i][j]. Queries outside the grid extrapolate
along the boundary cells unless EvalClamped is used.

funcs.name: NewBilinearGrid
funcs.doc:
NewBilinearGrid builds a bilinear grid interpolant. x and y must each be
strictly increasing with at least two entries, and z must have shape
len(x) by len(y) with z[i][j] the value at (x[i], y[j]).

methods.name: Dims
methods.doc: Dims returns the number of grid points along the x and y axes.

methods.name: Domain
methods.signature: func (g *BilinearGrid) Domain() (xMin, xMax, yMin, yMax float64)
methods.doc: Domain returns the grid extent as (xMin, xMax, yMin, yMax).

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the bilinearly interpolated value at (x, y).

methods.name: EvalClamped
methods.doc:
EvalClamped returns the interpolated value at (x, y) with the query point
first clamped to the grid extent, so no extrapolation occurs.

types.name: ChebyshevInterpolator
types.doc:
ChebyshevInterpolator approximates a function on an interval [a, b] by
truncated Chebyshev series obtained from samples at the Chebyshev roots.

funcs.name: NewChebyshevInterpolator
funcs.signature: func NewChebyshevInterpolator(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) (*ChebyshevInterpolator, error)
funcs.doc:
NewChebyshevInterpolator constructs a ChebyshevInterpolator of order n for f
on [a, b] by sampling f at the n Chebyshev roots. It requires n >= 1 and
a != b.

methods.name: Coefficients
methods.doc: Coefficients returns a copy of the Chebyshev coefficients.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval returns the Chebyshev approximation at xq. Values of xq are mapped from
[a, b] to [-1, 1] before the series is summed.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the approximation at every element of xs.

types.name: CubicSpline
types.doc:
CubicSpline is a C2-continuous piecewise-cubic interpolant. It is produced by
one of the constructors NewNaturalCubicSpline, NewClampedCubicSpline or
NewNotAKnotCubicSpline, which differ only in the boundary conditions imposed
at the two ends of the data.

funcs.name: NewClampedCubicSpline
funcs.signature: func NewClampedCubicSpline(x, y []float64, dLeft, dRight float64) (*CubicSpline, error)
funcs.doc:
NewClampedCubicSpline builds a cubic spline whose first derivative equals
dLeft at the first node and dRight at the last node. x must be strictly
increasing and hold at least two points.

funcs.name: NewNaturalCubicSpline
funcs.doc:
NewNaturalCubicSpline builds a cubic spline with natural boundary conditions,
that is with zero second derivative at both ends. x must be strictly
increasing and hold at least two points.

funcs.name: NewNotAKnotCubicSpline
funcs.doc:
NewNotAKnotCubicSpline builds a cubic spline with not-a-knot boundary
conditions, requiring the third derivative to be continuous across the first
and last interior knots. The interpolant reproduces any cubic polynomial
exactly. x must be strictly increasing and hold at least four points.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the first and last abscissae of the samples.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval returns the spline value at x. Queries outside the sampled range are
extrapolated with the boundary cubic.

methods.name: EvalClamped
methods.doc:
EvalClamped returns the spline value at x with x first clamped to the sampled
domain, so no extrapolation occurs.

methods.name: EvalDerivative
methods.doc: EvalDerivative returns the first derivative of the spline at x.

methods.name: EvalSecondDerivative
methods.doc: EvalSecondDerivative returns the second derivative of the spline at x.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice returns the spline evaluated at every point of xs.

methods.name: EvalThirdDerivative
methods.doc:
EvalThirdDerivative returns the third derivative of the spline at x, constant
within each interval.

methods.name: Integral
methods.doc:
Integral returns the definite integral of the spline from a to b, with the
limits clamped to the sampled domain.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of samples.

methods.name: Xs
methods.doc: Xs returns a copy of the sample abscissae.

methods.name: Ys
methods.doc: Ys returns a copy of the sample ordinates.

types.name: HermiteInterpolator
types.doc:
HermiteInterpolator is a reusable osculating polynomial matching values and
first derivatives at each node.

funcs.name: NewHermiteInterpolator
funcs.doc:
NewHermiteInterpolator builds a HermiteInterpolator matching the values y and
first derivatives dy at the abscissae x. All three slices must share a length
of at least one, and x must contain distinct abscissae.

methods.name: Coefficients
methods.doc:
Coefficients returns a copy of the Newton coefficients over the doubled node
sequence.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the osculating polynomial evaluated at xq.

methods.name: EvalDerivative
methods.doc:
EvalDerivative returns the first derivative of the osculating polynomial at
xq.

types.name: LagrangeInterpolator
types.doc:
LagrangeInterpolator is a reusable interpolant in Lagrange form built from a
fixed set of samples.

funcs.name: NewLagrangeInterpolator
funcs.doc:
NewLagrangeInterpolator builds a LagrangeInterpolator from the samples
(x[i], y[i]). It requires at least one point, matching slice lengths and
distinct abscissae.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree reports the degree of the interpolating polynomial, which is one less
than the number of samples.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the interpolating polynomial evaluated at xq.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the interpolant at every element of xs.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len reports the number of samples used to build the interpolant.

types.name: LinearInterp
types.doc:
LinearInterp is a piecewise-linear interpolant through a set of samples.
Evaluation outside the sampled range extrapolates along the end segments.

funcs.name: NewLinearInterp
funcs.doc:
NewLinearInterp builds a piecewise-linear interpolant through the samples
(x[i], y[i]). x must be strictly increasing and hold at least two points.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the first and last abscissae of the samples.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the interpolated value at x.

methods.name: EvalDerivative
methods.doc: EvalDerivative returns the slope of the segment containing x.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice returns the interpolant evaluated at every point of xs.

methods.name: Integral
methods.doc:
Integral returns the definite integral from a to b, with limits clamped to
the sampled domain.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of samples.

methods.name: Xs
methods.doc: Xs returns a copy of the sample abscissae.

methods.name: Ys
methods.doc: Ys returns a copy of the sample ordinates.

types.name: NearestInterp
types.doc:
NearestInterp is a nearest-neighbour interpolant: it returns the ordinate of
whichever sample abscissa is closest to the query point, breaking ties toward
the lower index.

funcs.name: NewNearestInterp
funcs.doc:
NewNearestInterp builds a nearest-neighbour interpolant. x must be strictly
increasing and hold at least two points.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the first and last abscissae of the samples.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the ordinate of the sample nearest to x.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of samples.

methods.name: Xs
methods.doc: Xs returns a copy of the sample abscissae.

methods.name: Ys
methods.doc: Ys returns a copy of the sample ordinates.

types.name: NewtonInterpolator
types.doc:
NewtonInterpolator is a reusable interpolant in Newton form that also
supports incremental extension via AddPoint.

funcs.name: NewNewtonInterpolator
funcs.doc:
NewNewtonInterpolator builds a NewtonInterpolator from the samples
(x[i], y[i]). It requires at least one point, matching lengths and distinct
abscissae.

methods.name: AddPoint
methods.doc:
AddPoint extends the interpolant with one additional sample (xi, yi) in
O(len) time, updating the Newton coefficients in place. The new abscissa must
differ from every existing one.

methods.name: Coefficients
methods.doc: Coefficients returns a copy of the Newton coefficients.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the interpolating polynomial evaluated at xq.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the interpolant at every element of xs.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len reports the number of samples currently held by the interpolant.

types.name: PCHIP
types.doc:
PCHIP is a monotone piecewise cubic Hermite interpolant using the
Fritsch-Carlson slope limiter. It preserves the monotonicity of the data
between samples and never overshoots, at the cost of only C1 continuity.

funcs.name: NewPCHIP
funcs.doc:
NewPCHIP builds a monotone piecewise cubic Hermite interpolant through the
samples (x[i], y[i]). x must be strictly increasing and hold at least two
points.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the first and last abscissae of the samples.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the interpolated value at x.

methods.name: EvalDerivative
methods.doc: EvalDerivative returns the first derivative of the interpolant at x.

methods.name: EvalSecondDerivative
methods.doc:
EvalSecondDerivative returns the second derivative of the interpolant at x.
Note that a PCHIP is only C1, so this is generally discontinuous at the nodes.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice returns the interpolant evaluated at every point of xs.

methods.name: Integral
methods.doc:
Integral returns the definite integral of the interpolant from a to b, with
limits clamped to the sampled domain.

methods.name: IsMonotone
methods.doc:
IsMonotone reports whether the underlying sample ordinates are monotone
(entirely non-decreasing or entirely non-increasing), the condition under
which a PCHIP is itself monotone.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of samples.

methods.name: Xs
methods.doc: Xs returns a copy of the sample abscissae.

methods.name: Ys
methods.doc: Ys returns a copy of the sample ordinates.

types.name: PolynomialFit
types.doc:
PolynomialFit bundles the ascending coefficients of a least-squares fit with
convenient evaluation.

funcs.name: NewPolynomialFit
funcs.signature: func NewPolynomialFit(x, y []float64, degree int) (*PolynomialFit, error)
funcs.doc:
NewPolynomialFit builds a PolynomialFit of the given degree from the samples
(x[i], y[i]) by ordinary least squares.

methods.name: Coefficients
methods.doc: Coefficients returns a copy of the ascending fit coefficients.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc: Degree reports the degree of the fitted polynomial.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the fitted polynomial at x.

types.name: StepInterp
types.doc:
StepInterp is a piecewise-constant (zero-order-hold) interpolant. A previous
step holds the value of the nearest sample at or below the query point; a
next step holds the value of the nearest sample at or above it.

funcs.name: NewNextStepInterp
funcs.doc:
NewNextStepInterp builds a piecewise-constant interpolant that holds the
value of the nearest sample at or above the query point. x must be strictly
increasing and hold at least two points.

funcs.name: NewPreviousStepInterp
funcs.doc:
NewPreviousStepInterp builds a piecewise-constant interpolant that holds the
value of the nearest sample at or below the query point (a causal
zero-order hold). x must be strictly increasing and hold at least two points.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the first and last abscissae of the samples.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the piecewise-constant value at x.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of samples.

methods.name: Xs
methods.doc: Xs returns a copy of the sample abscissae.

methods.name: Ys
methods.doc: Ys returns a copy of the sample ordinates.

types.name: ThieleInterpolator
types.doc:
ThieleInterpolator is a reusable rational interpolant in Thiele
continued-fraction form.

funcs.name: NewThieleInterpolator
funcs.doc:
NewThieleInterpolator builds a ThieleInterpolator from the samples
(x[i], y[i]). It requires at least one point, matching lengths and distinct
abscissae.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the rational interpolant evaluated at xq.

methods.name: EvalSlice
methods.doc: EvalSlice evaluates the interpolant at every element of xs.

types.name: TrigInterpolator
types.doc:
TrigInterpolator is a reusable trigonometric interpolant of equally spaced
periodic samples.

funcs.name: NewTrigInterpolator
funcs.doc:
NewTrigInterpolator builds a TrigInterpolator from samples y taken at the
nodes x_j = 2*pi*j/len(y). It requires at least one sample.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the trigonometric interpolant evaluated at xq.

types.name: TrilinearGrid
types.doc:
TrilinearGrid is a trilinear interpolant on a rectilinear grid. The value at
grid point (x[i], y[j], z[k]) is v[i][j][k]. Queries outside the grid
extrapolate along the boundary cells unless EvalClamped is used.

funcs.name: NewTrilinearGrid
funcs.signature: func NewTrilinearGrid(x, y, z []float64, v [][][]float64) (*TrilinearGrid, error)
funcs.doc:
NewTrilinearGrid builds a trilinear grid interpolant. x, y and z must each be
strictly increasing with at least two entries, and v must have shape
len(x) by len(y) by len(z) with v[i][j][k] the value at (x[i], y[j], z[k]).

methods.name: Dims
methods.doc: Dims returns the number of grid points along the x, y and z axes.

methods.name: Domain
methods.signature: func (g *TrilinearGrid) Domain() (xMin, xMax, yMin, yMax, zMin, zMax float64)
methods.doc: Domain returns the grid extent as (xMin, xMax, yMin, yMax, zMin, zMax).

methods.name: Eval
methods.signature: func (g *TrilinearGrid) Eval(x, y, z float64) float64
methods.doc: Eval returns the trilinearly interpolated value at (x, y, z).

methods.name: EvalClamped
methods.signature: func (g *TrilinearGrid) EvalClamped(x, y, z float64) float64
methods.doc:
EvalClamped returns the interpolated value at (x, y, z) with the query point
first clamped to the grid extent, so no extrapolation occurs.

funcs.name: BSplineBasis
funcs.signature: func BSplineBasis(i, degree int, knots []float64, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BSplineBasis returns the value at t of the i-th B-spline basis function of
the given degree over the knot vector knots, computed with the Cox-de Boor
recurrence.

funcs.name: BarycentricEval
funcs.signature: func BarycentricEval(x, y, w []float64, xq float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BarycentricEval evaluates the interpolating polynomial through (x[i], y[i])
at xq using the second (true) barycentric formula with the supplied weights.
