If xq coincides with a node, the corresponding y value is returned exactly.

funcs.name: BarycentricWeights
BernsteinBasis
funcs.signature: func BernsteinBasis(n, i int, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BernsteinBasis returns the value at t of the i-th Bernstein basis polynomial
of degree n, C(n, i) t^i (1-t)^{n-i}.

funcs.name: BezierCurve
funcs.doc:
BezierCurve evaluates the vector-valued Bezier curve at every parameter in ts
and returns the resulting points in order.

funcs.name: BezierDerivative
funcs.doc:
BezierDerivative returns the derivative vector dC/dt of the Bezier curve at
parameter t. The derivative is itself a degree n-1 Bezier curve whose control
points are n (P_{i+1} - P_i).

funcs.name: BezierElevate
funcs.doc:
BezierElevate returns the control points of the degree-elevated Bezier curve.
The returned curve has one extra control point but describes exactly the same
curve as the input.

funcs.name: BezierPoint
funcs.doc:
BezierPoint evaluates the vector-valued Bezier curve with the given control
points at parameter t in [0, 1] using de Casteljau's algorithm. Each control
point is a coordinate slice and all must share the same dimension; the
returned point has that dimension.

funcs.name: BilinearAt
funcs.signature: func BilinearAt(x0, x1, y0, y1, q00, q10, q01, q11, x, y float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BilinearAt returns the bilinear interpolation at (x, y) of the four corner
values of the rectangle [x0, x1] x [y0, y1]: q00 at (x0, y0), q10 at
(x1, y0), q01 at (x0, y1) and q11 at (x1, y1).

funcs.name: ChebyshevCoefficients
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevCoefficients(f func(float64) float64, n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevCoefficients samples f at the n Chebyshev roots on [a, b] and
returns the n coefficients c of the Chebyshev series approximation
f(x) ~= sum_{j=0}^{n-1} c[j] T_j(t) - c[0]/2, where t maps [a, b] to [-1, 1].

funcs.name: ChebyshevNodes
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevNodes returns the n roots of the degree-n Chebyshev polynomial of
the first kind on [-1, 1], namely cos((2k+1)pi / (2n)) for k = 0..n-1. The
nodes are returned in increasing order.

funcs.name: ChebyshevNodesInterval
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevNodesInterval(n int, a, b float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevNodesInterval returns the n Chebyshev roots mapped affinely from
[-1, 1] onto [a, b], in increasing order.

funcs.name: ChebyshevNodesSecondKind
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevNodesSecondKind returns the n Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points
cos(k*pi / (n-1)) for k = 0..n-1 on [-1, 1] (the extrema of T_{n-1}),
returned in increasing order. It requires n >= 2.

funcs.name: ChebyshevT
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevT(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevT returns the value of the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind of
degree n at x, computed by the stable three-term recurrence.

funcs.name: Clamp
funcs.signature: func Clamp(v, lo, hi float64) float64
funcs.doc: Clamp constrains v to the closed interval [lo, hi].

funcs.name: ClenshawEval
funcs.doc:
ClenshawEval evaluates the Chebyshev series with coefficients c at the point
y in [-1, 1] using Clenshaw recurrence, following the convention
sum_{j} c[j] T_j(y) - c[0]/2.

funcs.name: CubicHermiteSegment
funcs.signature: func CubicHermiteSegment(p0, m0, p1, m1, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
CubicHermiteSegment evaluates a single cubic Hermite segment on the unit
interval at parameter t in [0, 1], given endpoint values p0, p1 and endpoint
tangents m0, m1. It uses the standard Hermite basis functions.

funcs.name: DFTReal
funcs.doc:
DFTReal returns the discrete Fourier transform of the real input y, with
re[k] and im[k] holding the real and imaginary parts of
X_k = sum_j y_j exp(-2*pi*i*j*k/N).

funcs.name: DeBoor
funcs.signature: func DeBoor(degree int, knots []float64, ctrl [][]float64, t float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
DeBoor evaluates the vector-valued B-spline curve of the given degree with
knot vector knots and control points ctrl at parameter t using de Boor's
algorithm. t is clamped to the valid parameter range.

funcs.name: DeCasteljau
funcs.doc:
DeCasteljau evaluates the scalar Bezier curve with the given control ordinates
at parameter t in [0, 1] using de Casteljau's algorithm.

funcs.name: DividedDifferenceTable
funcs.doc:
DividedDifferenceTable returns the full lower-triangular table of divided
differences. Entry table[i][j] holds f[x_i, ..., x_{i+j}] and is defined for
i+j < len(x); unused upper entries are left at zero. The leading column
table[i][0] equals y[i] and the top row's j-th entry table[0][j] is the
Newton coefficient c[j].

funcs.name: DividedDifferences
funcs.doc:
DividedDifferences returns the Newton coefficients c of the interpolating
polynomial written as c[0] + c[1](x-x0) + c[2](x-x0)(x-x1) + ... The inputs
must have equal length and distinct abscissae.

funcs.name: HermiteEval
funcs.signature: func HermiteEval(x, y, dy []float64, xq float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermiteEval evaluates, at xq, the osculating (Hermite) polynomial that
matches both the values y and the first derivatives dy at the abscissae x.
The three slices must share a common length and x must be distinct.

funcs.name: InverseLerp
funcs.signature: func InverseLerp(a, b, v float64) float64
funcs.doc:
InverseLerp returns the parameter t for which Lerp(a, b, t) == v, that is
(v-a)/(b-a). It returns 0 when a == b.

funcs.name: LagrangeBasis
funcs.signature: func LagrangeBasis(x []float64, i int, xq float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LagrangeBasis returns the value at xq of the i-th Lagrange basis polynomial
L_i built from the abscissae x. L_i equals one at x[i] and zero at every
other node. The abscissae must be distinct.

funcs.name: LagrangeEval
funcs.signature: func LagrangeEval(x, y []float64, xq float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LagrangeEval evaluates, at xq, the unique polynomial of degree < len(x) that
passes through the samples (x[i], y[i]) using the Lagrange form. The two
slices must have equal length and x must contain distinct abscissae.

funcs.name: LeastSquaresLine
funcs.signature: func LeastSquaresLine(x, y []float64) (slope, intercept float64, err error)
funcs.doc:
LeastSquaresLine returns the slope and intercept of the ordinary
least-squares line y = slope*x + intercept fitting the samples. It requires
at least two points with non-degenerate abscissae.

funcs.name: Lerp
funcs.signature: func Lerp(a, b, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Lerp returns the linear interpolation between a and b at parameter t, that is
a + t*(b-a). t need not lie in [0, 1]; values outside extrapolate.

funcs.name: LinearAt
funcs.signature: func LinearAt(x0, y0, x1, y1, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LinearAt returns the value at x of the straight line through the points
(x0, y0) and (x1, y1).

funcs.name: LinearInterpolate
funcs.signature: func LinearInterpolate(x, y []float64, xq float64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
LinearInterpolate returns the piecewise-linear interpolation of the samples
(x[i], y[i]) at the single query point xq. It is a convenience wrapper around
NewLinearInterp for one-off evaluations.

funcs.name: NearestInterpolate
funcs.signature: func NearestInterpolate(x, y []float64, xq float64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
NearestInterpolate returns the nearest-neighbour interpolation of the samples
(x[i], y[i]) at the single query point xq.

funcs.name: NevilleEval
funcs.signature: func NevilleEval(x, y []float64, xq float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NevilleEval evaluates, at xq, the interpolating polynomial through the
samples (x[i], y[i]) using Neville's iterated linear interpolation. The
slices must share a length and x must be distinct.

funcs.name: NevilleTable
funcs.doc:
NevilleTable returns the full Neville tableau evaluated at xq. Row j (for
0 <= j < len(x)) has length len(x)-j; entry table[j][i] is the value at xq of
the polynomial through the points i..i+j. Thus table[0] equals y and the
single entry table[len(x)-1][0] is the final interpolated value.

funcs.name: NevilleWithError
funcs.signature: func NevilleWithError(x, y []float64, xq float64) (value, errEst float64)
funcs.doc:
NevilleWithError evaluates the interpolating polynomial at xq and also
returns the magnitude of the last correction, a heuristic estimate of the
interpolation error. It follows the diagonal path of the Neville tableau
nearest xq.

funcs.name: NewtonEval
funcs.signature: func NewtonEval(x, coeffs []float64, xq float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NewtonEval evaluates the Newton form with nodes x and coefficients coeffs at
xq using Horner's scheme. len(coeffs) must not exceed len(x).

funcs.name: OpenUniformKnots
funcs.doc:
OpenUniformKnots returns a clamped (open uniform) knot vector on [0, 1] for a
B-spline of the given degree with numCtrl control points, so that the curve
interpolates its first and last control points. It requires
numCtrl >= degree + 1.

funcs.name: PolyDerivativeCoeffs
funcs.doc:
PolyDerivativeCoeffs returns the ascending coefficients of the derivative of
the polynomial given by the ascending coefficients coeffs.

funcs.name: PolyFit
funcs.signature: func PolyFit(x, y []float64, degree int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
PolyFit returns the ascending coefficients of the degree-`degree`
least-squares polynomial that best fits the samples (x[i], y[i]) in the
ordinary (unweighted) sense. It requires at least degree+1 samples.

funcs.name: PolyFitWeighted
funcs.signature: func PolyFitWeighted(x, y, w []float64, degree int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
PolyFitWeighted returns the ascending coefficients of the degree-`degree`
weighted least-squares polynomial fit, where w[i] is the weight of sample i.

funcs.name: PolyIntegralCoeffs
funcs.doc:
PolyIntegralCoeffs returns the ascending coefficients of an antiderivative of
the polynomial given by coeffs, using constant of integration c.

funcs.name: PolyVal
funcs.doc:
PolyVal evaluates the polynomial with ascending coefficients coeffs (so
coeffs[0] is the constant term) at x using Horner's method.

funcs.name: RMSE
funcs.doc:
RMSE returns the root-mean-square error of the polynomial with ascending
coefficients coeffs against the samples (x[i], y[i]).

funcs.name: RSquared
funcs.doc:
RSquared returns the coefficient of determination R^2 of the polynomial with
ascending coefficients coeffs against the samples (x[i], y[i]).

funcs.name: RationalInterpEval
funcs.signature: func RationalInterpEval(x, y []float64, xq float64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
RationalInterpEval performs Bulirsch-Stoer diagonal rational function
interpolation of the samples (x[i], y[i]) at xq, returning the interpolated
value. It returns errPolyPole if the query point sits at a pole of the
interpolant.

funcs.name: SearchInterval
funcs.doc:
SearchInterval returns the index i such that xs[i] <= x <= xs[i+1], clamped so
that i lies in [0, len(xs)-2]. It requires len(xs) >= 2 and reports the
containing interval for values outside the sampled range as the nearest
boundary interval.

funcs.name: SmoothStep
funcs.signature: func SmoothStep(edge0, edge1, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
SmoothStep returns the cubic Hermite smoothstep 3u^2 - 2u^3 where u is x
mapped from [edge0, edge1] to [0, 1] and clamped. It is 0 for x <= edge0 and
1 for x >= edge1, with zero slope at both ends.

funcs.name: SmootherStep
funcs.signature: func SmootherStep(edge0, edge1, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
SmootherStep returns Perlin's quintic smoothstep 6u^5 - 15u^4 + 10u^3 where u
is x mapped from [edge0, edge1] to [0, 1] and clamped. It has zero first and
second derivatives at both ends.

funcs.name: ThieleEval
funcs.signature: func ThieleEval(x, a []float64, xq float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ThieleEval evaluates Thiele's continued fraction with nodes x and
coefficients a (as returned by ThieleReciprocalDifferences) at xq.

funcs.name: ThieleReciprocalDifferences
funcs.doc:
ThieleReciprocalDifferences returns the coefficients a of Thiele's continued
fraction for the samples (x[i], y[i]):

	R(x) = a[0] + (x-x0)/(a[1] + (x-x1)/(a[2] + ...)).

The abscissae must be distinct and the reciprocal differences must be finite.

funcs.name: TrigCoefficients
funcs.doc:
TrigCoefficients returns the real trigonometric interpolation coefficients
a and b (each of length N/2+1) for samples y taken at the equally spaced
nodes x_j = 2*pi*j/N on [0, 2*pi). See TrigInterpEval for the reconstruction
convention.

funcs.name: TrigInterpEval
funcs.doc:
TrigInterpEval evaluates, at xq, the trigonometric polynomial that
interpolates the samples y taken at the equally spaced nodes
x_j = 2*pi*j/len(y) on [0, 2*pi). The interpolant is real and, for an even
number of samples, uses the standard half-weight on the Nyquist term.

funcs.name: TrilinearAt
funcs.signature: func TrilinearAt(x0, x1, y0, y1, z0, z1, c000, c100, c010, c110, c001, c101, c011, c111, x, y, z float64) float64
funcs.doc:
TrilinearAt returns the trilinear interpolation at (x, y, z) of the eight
corner values of the box [x0, x1] x [y0, y1] x [z0, z1]. Corner cijk sits at
(x0 or x1, y0 or y1, z0 or z1) as selected by i, j, k in that order.

funcs.name: UniformKnots
funcs.doc:
UniformKnots returns a uniform (periodic-style) knot vector 0, 1, ..., m for a
B-spline of the given degree with numCtrl control points. Its length is
numCtrl + degree + 1 and its valid parameter range is [degree, numCtrl].

funcs.name: VandermondeSolve
funcs.doc:
VandermondeSolve returns the ascending coefficients of the unique polynomial
of degree len(x)-1 that passes exactly through the samples (x[i], y[i]),
obtained by solving the Vandermonde system. The abscissae must be distinct.

packages.name: interval
packages.synopsis: Package interval implements rigorous interval arithmetic over IEEE-754 double precision using outward directed rounding.
packages.doc:
Package interval implements rigorous interval arithmetic over IEEE-754
double precision using outward directed rounding.

An [Interval] represents the set of real numbers {x : Lo <= x <= Hi}. Every
operation returns an enclosure: an interval that is guaranteed to contain the
true result of the corresponding real operation applied to every point of the
inputs. This makes the package suitable for verified numerics, where a
computed answer must come with a mathematically sound error bound rather than
a heuristic estimate.

# Directed rounding

The Go standard library does not expose the hardware rounding-mode control
registers, so this package obtains outward rounding portably with
[math.Nextafter]. After a floating-point operation whose exact result is r,
the computed value fl(r) differs from r by at most half an ULP for the
correctly rounded primitives (+, -, *, /, sqrt). Nudging the lower bound one
ULP toward -Inf and the upper bound one ULP toward +Inf therefore yields an
interval that provably contains r. Elementary transcendental functions
(exp, log, sin, ...) are not guaranteed correctly rounded, so their results
are inflated by a small fixed number of ULPs to remain rigorous. The bounds
are wider than strictly necessary but never unsound.

# Special values

The empty set is represented by [Empty] and reported by [Interval.IsEmpty].
The whole real line is [Entire]. Operations propagate emptiness: any
operation with an empty operand returns [Empty]. Unbounded intervals use
infinities as their bounds.

# Contents

  - Interval arithmetic: [Interval.Add], [Interval.Sub], [Interval.Mul],
    [Interval.Div] and scalar variants, all outward rounded.
  - Elementary functions with enclosures: [Interval.Exp], [Interval.Log],
    [Interval.Sqrt], [Interval.Sin], [Interval.Cos], [Interval.Tan],
    [Interval.Sinh], [Interval.Cosh], [Interval.Tanh], [Interval.Atan],
    [Interval.IntPow] and [Interval.Pow].
  - Set operations and measures: [Interval.Width], [Interval.Midpoint],
    [Interval.Radius], [Interval.Intersect], [Interval.Hull],
    [Interval.Contains].
  - Verified root finding: [Newton] and [Bisect] using the interval Newton
    operator.
  - Interval matrices: [Matrix] with [Matrix.Add], [Matrix.Mul],
    [Matrix.MulVec] and related operations.

All results are deterministic and depend only on the inputs; the package uses
nothing outside the Go standard library.

vars.signature: var ErrDimension = errors.New("interval: incompatible dimensions")
vars.doc:
ErrDimension reports that the shapes of the interval matrices or vectors in
an operation are incompatible.

types.name: Interval
types.doc:
Interval is a closed real interval [Lo, Hi] interpreted as the set of real
numbers x with Lo <= x <= Hi. The zero value [Interval]{} is the degenerate
interval [0, 0] containing only zero. Use [Empty] for the empty set and
[Entire] for the whole real line.

funcs.name: Add
funcs.doc:
Add returns an enclosure of a + b. It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Add].

funcs.name: Bisect
funcs.doc:
Bisect splits x at its midpoint and returns the closed left and right halves,
which share the midpoint as a common endpoint. An empty interval yields two
empty intervals.

funcs.name: Cos
funcs.doc:
Cos returns an enclosure of cos(a). It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Cos].

funcs.name: Div
funcs.doc:
Div returns an enclosure of a / b. It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Div].

funcs.name: DotVec
funcs.doc:
DotVec returns an outward rounded enclosure of the dot product of interval
vectors x and y. It returns [ErrDimension] if their lengths differ.

funcs.name: Empty
funcs.doc:
Empty returns the empty interval, which contains no real numbers. It is
encoded with Lo = +Inf and Hi = -Inf so that any Lo > Hi test recognises it.

funcs.name: Entire
funcs.doc: Entire returns the interval [-Inf, +Inf] representing the whole real line.

funcs.name: Exp
funcs.doc:
Exp returns an enclosure of e**a. It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Exp].

funcs.name: Hull
funcs.doc:
Hull returns the convex hull of a and b. It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Hull].

funcs.name: Intersect
funcs.doc:
Intersect returns the intersection of a and b. It is the free-function form
of [Interval.Intersect].

funcs.name: Log
funcs.doc:
Log returns an enclosure of the natural logarithm of a. It is the
free-function form of [Interval.Log].

funcs.name: Mul
funcs.doc:
Mul returns an enclosure of a * b. It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Mul].

funcs.name: New
funcs.doc:
New returns the interval [lo, hi]. If lo > hi (and neither is NaN) the bounds
are swapped so the result is always a well-formed interval. If either bound
is NaN the result is [Empty].

funcs.name: Newton
funcs.signature: func Newton(f, df func(Interval) Interval, x Interval, tol float64, maxIter int) (Interval, bool)
funcs.doc:
Newton runs the interval Newton iteration to enclose a root of f within the
starting interval x. The function df must return an enclosure of the
derivative of f over any input interval; both f and df must be inclusion
isotonic interval extensions of the real function and its derivative.

At each step the Newton operator N(X) = m - f(m)/f'(X), evaluated at the
midpoint m of X, is intersected with X to discard root-free regions. When the
derivative enclosure straddles 0 the step falls back to bisection so progress
continues. Iteration stops when the enclosure narrows to within tol or after
maxIter steps.

The second return value is true only when existence and uniqueness of a root
in the returned interval were rigorously verified, which happens when N(X) is
contained in X while f'(X) excludes 0. A false result with a non-empty
interval means the enclosure is a plausible but unverified bracket; an empty
interval proves f has no root in the original x.

funcs.name: Point
funcs.doc:
Point returns the degenerate interval [x, x] containing only the single value
x. If x is NaN the result is [Empty].

funcs.name: Pow
funcs.signature: func Pow(a Interval, y float64) Interval
funcs.doc:
Pow returns an enclosure of a**y for a real exponent y. It is the
free-function form of [Interval.Pow].

funcs.name: Sin
funcs.doc:
Sin returns an enclosure of sin(a). It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Sin].

funcs.name: Sqrt
funcs.doc:
Sqrt returns an enclosure of the square root of a. It is the free-function
form of [Interval.Sqrt].

funcs.name: Sub
funcs.doc:
Sub returns an enclosure of a - b. It is the free-function form of
[Interval.Sub].

methods.name: Abs
methods.doc:
Abs returns the interval of absolute values {|x| : x in a}. If a straddles 0
the lower bound is 0.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns an enclosure of a + b, with the lower bound rounded down and the
upper bound rounded up so the result rigorously contains every sum x + y for
x in a and y in b.

methods.name: AddFloat
methods.doc: AddFloat returns an enclosure of a + f, outward rounded.

methods.name: Atan
methods.doc:
Atan returns an enclosure of the arctangent of a. atan is monotone
increasing.

methods.name: Contains
methods.doc:
Contains reports whether the real number x lies within the interval. NaN is
never contained.

methods.name: ContainsInterval
methods.doc:
ContainsInterval reports whether b is a subset of a (every point of b lies in
a). The empty interval is a subset of every interval.

methods.name: Cos
methods.doc:
Cos returns an enclosure of the cosine of a. It accounts for the interior
extrema of cosine (the points k*pi where cos is +-1) so the enclosure is
tight, not merely [-1, 1].

methods.name: Cosh
methods.doc:
Cosh returns an enclosure of the hyperbolic cosine of a. cosh is even and
convex with minimum 1 at 0, so the lower bound comes from the mignitude and
the upper bound from the magnitude.

methods.name: Div
methods.doc:
Div returns an enclosure of a / b, outward rounded. Division by an interval
containing 0 follows the same conventions as [Interval.Recip].

methods.name: DivFloat
methods.doc:
DivFloat returns an enclosure of a / f, outward rounded. Division by 0
follows [Interval.Div].

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether a and b denote exactly the same set. Two empty
intervals are equal regardless of their encoded bounds.

methods.name: Exp
methods.doc:
Exp returns an enclosure of e raised to the power of a. Because exp is
monotone increasing the bounds come directly from the endpoints.

methods.name: Hull
methods.doc:
Hull returns the convex hull (union enclosure) of a and b, the smallest
interval containing both. The hull with an empty interval is the other
interval.

methods.name: IntPow
methods.doc:
IntPow returns an enclosure of a raised to the integer power n. It exploits
the monotonicity of odd powers and the convex, even shape of positive even
powers to produce a tight enclosure. For n == 0 the result is [1, 1]; for
n < 0 it is the reciprocal of the positive power.

methods.name: Intersect
methods.doc:
Intersect returns the intersection of a and b, the largest interval contained
in both. If they do not overlap the result is [Empty].

methods.name: IsBounded
methods.doc:
IsBounded reports whether both bounds of the interval are finite. The empty
interval is considered bounded.

methods.name: IsEmpty
methods.doc: IsEmpty reports whether the interval is empty, i.e. contains no real numbers.

methods.name: IsEntire
methods.doc: IsEntire reports whether the interval is the whole real line [-Inf, +Inf].

methods.name: IsPoint
methods.doc:
IsPoint reports whether the interval is degenerate, containing exactly one
finite value (Lo == Hi and finite).

methods.name: Log
methods.doc:
Log returns an enclosure of the natural logarithm of a. Only the positive
part of a is in the domain: if Hi <= 0 the result is [Empty], and if Lo <= 0
the lower bound is -Inf.

methods.name: Mag
methods.doc:
Mag returns the magnitude of the interval, the supremum of |x| over all x in
the interval (also called the norm). An empty interval returns NaN.

methods.name: Max
methods.doc: Max returns the elementwise maximum enclosure {max(x, y) : x in a, y in b}.

methods.name: Midpoint
methods.doc:
Midpoint returns a representable point near the centre (Lo + Hi) / 2. For an
empty interval it returns NaN; for [Entire] it returns 0; for a half-bounded
interval it returns the appropriate signed infinity.

methods.name: Mig
methods.doc:
Mig returns the mignitude of the interval, the infimum of |x| over all x in
the interval. It is 0 when the interval contains 0. An empty interval returns
NaN.

methods.name: Min
methods.doc: Min returns the elementwise minimum enclosure {min(x, y) : x in a, y in b}.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns an enclosure of a * b, outward rounded. It evaluates the four
endpoint products and takes their extremes, then inflates by one ULP to
cover the rounding of the selected product.

methods.name: MulFloat
methods.doc: MulFloat returns an enclosure of a * f, outward rounded.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc:
Neg returns -a, the reflection of the interval through the origin. It is
exact and requires no rounding.

methods.name: Overlaps
methods.doc: Overlaps reports whether a and b share at least one common point.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns an enclosure of a raised to the real power y, defined for a with
positive lower bound via exp(y * log(a)). If a is not strictly positive the
result is [Empty]. For integer exponents on intervals that may include
non-positive values, use [Interval.IntPow].

methods.name: Radius
methods.doc:
Radius returns half the width of the interval, rounded up. An empty interval
has radius 0.

methods.name: Recip
methods.doc:
Recip returns an enclosure of 1 / a. If a contains 0 in its interior the
result is [Entire]; if a is exactly [0, 0] the result is [Empty].

methods.name: Sin
methods.doc:
Sin returns an enclosure of the sine of a. As with [Interval.Cos] it accounts
for the interior extrema at pi/2 + k*pi so the enclosure is tight.

methods.name: Sinh
methods.doc:
Sinh returns an enclosure of the hyperbolic sine of a. sinh is monotone
increasing.

methods.name: Sqrt
methods.doc:
Sqrt returns an enclosure of the square root of a. The negative part of a is
discarded (sqrt is undefined there); if a is entirely negative the result is
[Empty].

methods.name: Square
methods.doc:
Square returns an enclosure of a*a. It is tighter than [Interval.Mul] with a
itself because it exploits x^2 >= 0: the lower bound is the squared
mignitude and the upper bound the squared magnitude.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String formats the interval as "[Lo, Hi]" using the shortest decimal that
round-trips each bound. The empty interval formats as "[empty]".

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns an enclosure of a - b, outward rounded.

methods.name: SubFloat
methods.doc: SubFloat returns an enclosure of a - f, outward rounded.

methods.name: Tan
methods.doc:
Tan returns an enclosure of the tangent of a, computed as Sin(a) / Cos(a).
Where the cosine enclosure contains 0 the tangent is unbounded and the result
is [Entire].

methods.name: Tanh
methods.doc:
Tanh returns an enclosure of the hyperbolic tangent of a. tanh is monotone
increasing.

methods.name: Width
methods.doc:
Width returns Hi - Lo, the length of the interval, rounded up so the result
never underestimates the true width. An empty interval has width 0; an
unbounded interval has width +Inf.

types.name: Matrix
types.doc:
Matrix is a dense matrix whose entries are intervals. It is used for verified
linear algebra: each entry encloses the corresponding real value, so matrix
operations propagate rigorous bounds. The zero value is not usable; construct
matrices with [NewMatrix], [MatrixFromFloat] or [Identity].

funcs.name: Identity
funcs.doc:
Identity returns the n-by-n interval identity matrix, with degenerate 1s on
the diagonal and 0s elsewhere.

funcs.name: MatrixFromFloat
funcs.signature: func MatrixFromFloat(rows, cols int, values []float64) (*Matrix, error)
funcs.doc:
MatrixFromFloat builds a rows-by-cols interval matrix whose entries are the
degenerate (point) intervals of the given row-major values. It returns
[ErrDimension] if len(values) != rows*cols.

funcs.name: NewMatrix
funcs.doc:
NewMatrix returns a rows-by-cols interval matrix with every entry set to the
degenerate interval [0, 0]. It panics if rows or cols is negative.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns the entrywise sum m + n as a new matrix, each entry an outward
rounded interval sum. It returns [ErrDimension] if the shapes differ.

methods.name: At
methods.doc:
At returns the interval stored at row i, column j (both 0-indexed). It panics
if the indices are out of range.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc:
Clone returns a deep copy of the matrix that shares no storage with the
receiver.

methods.name: Cols
methods.doc: Cols returns the number of columns in the matrix.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether m and n have the same shape and every corresponding
entry denotes the same interval.

methods.name: MaxWidth
methods.doc:
MaxWidth returns the largest entry width in the matrix, a scalar measure of
how tight the enclosure is. An empty matrix returns 0.

methods.name: Midpoint
methods.doc:
Midpoint returns the real matrix of entrywise midpoints as a row-major slice
of length Rows*Cols.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the matrix product m * n as a new matrix. Each entry is an
outward rounded interval dot product, so the result encloses the true product
of any real matrices contained in m and n. It returns [ErrDimension] if the
inner dimensions do not match.

methods.name: MulVec
methods.doc:
MulVec returns the matrix-vector product m * x as a new interval vector. It
returns [ErrDimension] if len(x) != m.Cols().

methods.name: Rows
methods.doc: Rows returns the number of rows in the matrix.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns a new matrix with every entry multiplied by the interval s.

methods.name: Set
methods.signature: func (m *Matrix) Set(i, j int, v Interval)
methods.doc:
Set stores the interval v at row i, column j (both 0-indexed). It panics if
the indices are out of range.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc:
Sub returns the entrywise difference m - n as a new matrix. It returns
[ErrDimension] if the shapes differ.

methods.name: Transpose
methods.doc: Transpose returns a new matrix that is the transpose of m.

packages.name: knottheory
packages.synopsis: Package knottheory implements combinatorial knot and braid theory using only the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package knottheory implements combinatorial knot and braid theory using only
the Go standard library.

The package provides self-contained, mathematically correct implementations
of the classical objects and invariants of low-dimensional topology as they
appear in a combinatorial (diagrammatic) setting:

  - Laurent polynomials over the integers (the Laurent type), the coefficient
    ring in which every polynomial knot invariant lives, with a full set of
    ring operations, evaluation, substitution and normalisation helpers.
  - Permutations (the Permutation type): composition, inversion, cycle
    decomposition, order and sign, used to describe the underlying
    permutation and closure of a braid.
  - Braid groups (the Braid type): Artin generators and relations, free
    reduction, exponent sum (writhe), the induced permutation, mirror and
    reverse, torus braids, the full twist and the Garside half twist, and the
    reduced Burau representation over the Laurent ring.
  - Knot and link diagrams described by signed Gauss codes (the GaussCode and
    Diagram types): validation, crossing number, writhe, self-writhe and the
    Gauss linking number between components.
  - Planar-diagram (PD) codes (the PDCode type): the Kauffman bracket via a
    union-find state sum, the writhe-normalised bracket, and the Jones
    polynomial for small diagrams, both in the standard variable t and in the
    variable t^{1/2} for links.
  - The Alexander polynomial, computed two independent ways: from the reduced
    Burau matrix of a braid, and directly from a diagram via the Alexander
    (Fox-derivative) matrix, together with the knot determinant and
    p-colourability counts.
  - Reidemeister-move recognisers (types I, II and III) that operate
    combinatorially on Gauss codes.
  - Torus-knot invariants T(p, q): crossing number, Seifert genus, bridge and
    unknotting numbers, determinant, and the closed forms of the Alexander and
    Jones polynomials.

Unless documented otherwise polynomials are represented by the Laurent type,
braids by words of non-zero integers (generator i is +i, its inverse is -i),
and diagrams by Gauss or PD codes whose crossings are labelled by consecutive
integers starting at one.

types.name: Braid
types.doc:
Braid is an element of the Artin braid group B_n on n strands, represented by
a word in the standard generators. The word is a slice of non-zero integers:
the value +i denotes the generator sigma_i (the i-th strand crossing over the
(i+1)-th) and -i denotes its inverse sigma_i^{-1}, for 1 <= i <= n-1.

funcs.name: FullTwist
funcs.doc:
FullTwist returns the full twist braid (sigma_1 ... sigma_{n-1})^n on n
strands, the generator of the centre of B_n.

funcs.name: GarsideHalfTwist
funcs.doc:
GarsideHalfTwist returns the Garside half-twist braid Delta_n on n strands,
the positive half twist whose square is the full twist. It is the product
(sigma_1)(sigma_2 sigma_1)(sigma_3 sigma_2 sigma_1)...

funcs.name: Generator
funcs.doc:
Generator returns the braid consisting of the single generator sigma_i on n
strands.

funcs.name: GeneratorInv
funcs.doc:
GeneratorInv returns the braid consisting of the single inverse generator
sigma_i^{-1} on n strands.

funcs.name: IdentityBraid
funcs.doc: IdentityBraid returns the trivial (empty) braid on n strands.

funcs.name: MustBraid
funcs.doc:
MustBraid is like NewBraid but panics on error. It is convenient for
constructing constant braids in tests and examples.

funcs.name: NewBraid
funcs.doc:
NewBraid returns the braid on the given number of strands whose word is the
supplied sequence of generators. It returns an error if the strand count is
less than one or if any generator index is zero or out of range.

funcs.name: TorusBraid
funcs.doc:
TorusBraid returns the braid (sigma_1 sigma_2 ... sigma_{p-1})^q on p strands
whose closure is the torus link T(p, q). It returns an error if p < 1.

funcs.name: TorusKnotBraid
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotBraid returns a braid on |p| strands whose closure is T(p, q),
namely (sigma_1 ... sigma_{p-1})^q.

methods.name: AlexanderPolynomial
methods.doc:
AlexanderPolynomial returns the Alexander polynomial of the braid closure,
computed from the reduced Burau matrix via the Burau formula

	det(ReducedBurau - I) = ± t^k (1 + t + ... + t^{n-1}) / (1 - t) * Delta(t),

implemented as det(ReducedBurau - I) divided by 1 + t + ... + t^{n-1} and then
normalised to the symmetric representative with Delta(1) = 1. It returns an
error if the closure is not a knot or if the division is not exact.

methods.name: ClosureCrossingNumber
methods.doc:
ClosureCrossingNumber returns the number of crossings in the standard closed
braid diagram, which is the braid word length.

methods.name: ClosureIsKnot
methods.doc:
ClosureIsKnot reports whether the closure of the braid is a knot (a single
component).

methods.name: Concat
methods.doc:
Concat returns the product b*other formed by concatenating the two braid
words. It returns an error if the strand counts differ.

methods.name: Conjugate
methods.doc:
Conjugate returns the braid g * b * g^{-1}. Conjugate braids have isotopic
closures, so all closure invariants are preserved. It returns an error if the
strand counts differ.

methods.name: ExponentSum
methods.doc:
ExponentSum returns the algebraic sum of the generator signs, that is the
number of positive generators minus the number of negative ones. It equals
the writhe of the braid closure and is a braid invariant.

methods.name: FreeReduce
methods.doc:
FreeReduce returns the braid obtained by repeatedly cancelling adjacent
inverse pairs sigma_i sigma_i^{-1}. The result is freely reduced but is not a
solution of the braid word problem; distinct freely reduced words can still
represent the same braid.

methods.name: Generators
methods.doc:
Generators returns the sorted list of distinct generator indices that occur
in the braid word (ignoring sign).

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse returns the inverse braid, whose word is the reversed word with every
generator sign flipped.

methods.name: IsFreelyReduced
methods.doc:
IsFreelyReduced reports whether the braid word contains no adjacent inverse
pair.

methods.name: IsNegative
methods.doc: IsNegative reports whether every generator in the word is negative.

methods.name: IsPositive
methods.doc: IsPositive reports whether every generator in the word is positive.

methods.name: IsPureBraid
methods.doc:
IsPureBraid reports whether the braid induces the identity permutation, so
that every strand returns to its starting position.

methods.name: IsTrivial
methods.doc: IsTrivial reports whether the braid word is empty.

methods.name: Length
methods.doc:
Length returns the number of letters (generators) in the braid word, which
equals the number of crossings in the associated braid diagram.

methods.name: Mirror
methods.doc:
Mirror returns the braid obtained by flipping every crossing, that is by
negating every generator. Its closure is the mirror image of the original
link.

methods.name: NumComponents
methods.doc:
NumComponents returns the number of link components of the braid closure,
which equals the number of cycles of the underlying permutation.

methods.name: Permutation
methods.doc:
Permutation returns the underlying permutation of the braid: strand starting
at position i ends at position Permutation()[i]. Generator sigma_i acts as the
transposition (i, i+1) on positions numbered from 0.

methods.name: Power
methods.doc:
Power returns the braid raised to the integer power k. Negative powers use the
inverse braid and k==0 gives the identity braid.

methods.name: ReducedBurau
methods.doc:
ReducedBurau returns the reduced Burau matrix of the braid, an (n-1)x(n-1)
matrix of Laurent polynomials in t obtained as the ordered product of the
generator matrices. For the identity braid it is the identity matrix.

methods.name: Reverse
methods.doc:
Reverse returns the braid whose word is read right to left (the reverse or
"review" of the braid), keeping every generator sign.

methods.name: Strands
methods.doc: Strands returns the number of strands of the braid.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the braid word using sigma notation, for example
"s1 s2^-1 s1".

methods.name: Word
methods.doc: Word returns a copy of the generator word of the braid.

methods.name: Writhe
methods.doc:
Writhe returns the writhe of the closure of the braid, which equals the
exponent sum.

types.name: Diagram
types.doc:
Diagram is a knot or link diagram given as one signed Gauss code per
component.

funcs.name: NewDiagram
funcs.doc:
NewDiagram validates and returns a Diagram from its component Gauss codes.
Each crossing label must appear exactly twice across all components combined,
once over and once under, with a consistent sign.

methods.name: CrossingNumber
methods.doc: CrossingNumber returns the number of crossings in the diagram.

methods.name: IsKnot
methods.doc: IsKnot reports whether the diagram has exactly one component.

methods.name: IsLink
methods.doc: IsLink reports whether the diagram has more than one component.

methods.name: LinkingNumber
methods.signature: func (d Diagram) LinkingNumber(i, j int) (int, error)
methods.doc:
LinkingNumber returns the Gauss linking number of components i and j, defined
as half the sum of the signs of the crossings between the two components. It
returns an error if the indices are out of range or equal.

methods.name: NumComponents
methods.doc: NumComponents returns the number of components of the diagram.

methods.name: SelfWrithe
methods.doc:
SelfWrithe returns the writhe contributed only by self-crossings of the given
component (crossings both of whose strands lie on that component).

methods.name: TotalLinkingNumber
methods.doc:
TotalLinkingNumber returns the sum of the pairwise linking numbers over all
unordered pairs of distinct components.

methods.name: Writhe
methods.doc:
Writhe returns the writhe of the whole diagram, the sum of all crossing
signs.

types.name: GaussCode
types.doc:
GaussCode is the signed Gauss code of a single knot component: the cyclic
sequence of crossing encounters met while traversing the knot once.

funcs.name: NewGaussCode
funcs.doc:
NewGaussCode validates and returns a GaussCode. Every crossing label must
occur exactly twice, once as an over-pass and once as an under-pass, with the
same sign in both occurrences.

methods.name: CrossingLabels
methods.doc: CrossingLabels returns the sorted list of distinct crossing labels.

methods.name: CrossingNumber
methods.doc: CrossingNumber returns the number of crossings of the diagram.

methods.name: IsReduced
methods.doc:
IsReduced reports whether the diagram admits neither a type I kink nor a
reducible type II bigon, a necessary (though not sufficient) condition for the
diagram to be minimal.

methods.name: IsReidemeisterIIIApplicable
methods.doc:
IsReidemeisterIIIApplicable reports whether the diagram contains a candidate
type III triangle.

methods.name: IsReidemeisterIIReducible
methods.doc: IsReidemeisterIIReducible reports whether a reducible type II bigon exists.

methods.name: IsReidemeisterIReducible
methods.doc:
IsReidemeisterIReducible reports whether the diagram contains at least one
type I kink.

methods.name: Length
methods.doc:
Length returns the number of crossing encounters, which is twice the number
of crossings.

methods.name: Mirror
methods.doc:
Mirror returns the Gauss code of the mirror image, obtained by exchanging
over and under at every crossing and negating every sign.

methods.name: ReidemeisterIIITriangles
methods.doc:
ReidemeisterIIITriangles returns the triples of distinct crossing labels that
are pairwise cyclically adjacent in the Gauss code. Such a triangle of chords
is the combinatorial footprint required for a Reidemeister III move, so the
returned triples are the candidate locations at which a type III move may be
applied. Each triple is sorted in increasing order.

methods.name: ReidemeisterIIReducible
methods.doc:
ReidemeisterIIReducible returns the pairs of crossing labels that form a
reducible Reidemeister II bigon. Such a pair {a,b} occurs when, cyclically,
the two crossings appear adjacent as an all-over arc (both encounters over)
in one place and as an all-under arc (both encounters under) in another. Each
returned slice has length two with the smaller label first.

methods.name: ReidemeisterIKinks
methods.doc:
ReidemeisterIKinks returns the crossing labels that form a Reidemeister I
kink, that is a crossing whose two encounters are cyclically adjacent in the
Gauss code. Such a crossing can be removed by a type I move without changing
the knot type.

methods.name: RemoveReidemeisterII
methods.signature: func (gc GaussCode) RemoveReidemeisterII(a, b int) (GaussCode, bool)
methods.doc:
RemoveReidemeisterII returns the Gauss code with the two crossings of a
reducible bigon removed. It returns ok=false when {a,b} is not a reducible
type II pair.

methods.name: RemoveReidemeisterIKink
methods.doc:
RemoveReidemeisterIKink returns the Gauss code with the kink at the given
crossing removed. It returns the original code and ok=false if that crossing
is not a type I kink.

methods.name: ReverseOrientation
methods.doc:
ReverseOrientation returns the Gauss code obtained by traversing the knot in
the opposite direction. The writhe is unchanged.

methods.name: ToDiagram
methods.doc: ToDiagram wraps a single-component Gauss code as a one-component Diagram.

methods.name: Validate
methods.doc:
Validate checks the structural consistency of the Gauss code and returns a
descriptive error if it is malformed.

methods.name: Writhe
methods.doc: Writhe returns the writhe of the diagram, the sum of the crossing signs.

types.name: GaussEntry
types.doc:
GaussEntry is a single crossing encounter recorded while traversing a knot or
link component. Crossing is the crossing label (a positive integer), Over is
true when this pass is along the over-strand, and Sign is the sign of the
crossing (+1 or -1) determined by the right-hand rule.

types.name: Laurent
types.doc:
Laurent is a Laurent polynomial in a single variable with integer
coefficients, that is a finite sum of terms c*X^e where e may be any integer
(positive, negative or zero). The zero polynomial is represented by an empty
coefficient slice. A Laurent value is always kept trimmed so that neither the
leading nor the trailing coefficient is zero.

funcs.name: LaurentConst
funcs.doc: LaurentConst returns the constant Laurent polynomial c (that is c*X^0).

funcs.name: LaurentProduct
funcs.doc: LaurentProduct returns the product of all the supplied Laurent polynomials.

funcs.name: LaurentSum
funcs.doc: LaurentSum returns the sum of all the supplied Laurent polynomials.

funcs.name: Monomial
funcs.doc: Monomial returns the single-term Laurent polynomial coeff*X^exp.

funcs.name: NewLaurent
funcs.doc:
NewLaurent builds a Laurent polynomial whose coefficient of X^(min+i) is
coeffs[i]. The slice is copied and the result is trimmed.

funcs.name: OneLaurent
funcs.doc: OneLaurent returns the constant Laurent polynomial 1.

funcs.name: SkeinDelta
funcs.doc:
SkeinDelta returns the loop value delta = -A^2 - A^{-2} of the Kauffman
bracket skein relation, as a Laurent polynomial in A.

funcs.name: TorusKnotAlexander
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotAlexander returns the Alexander polynomial of the torus knot
T(p, q) with coprime p, q >= 2, given by the closed form

	Delta(t) = (t^{pq} - 1)(t - 1) / ((t^p - 1)(t^q - 1)),

normalised to the symmetric representative with Delta(1) = 1. It returns an
error if p and q are not coprime.

funcs.name: TorusKnotJones
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotJones returns the Jones polynomial of the torus knot T(p, q) with
coprime p, q >= 2, in the variable t, using the closed form

	V(t) = t^{(p-1)(q-1)/2} (1 - t^{p+1} - t^{q+1} + t^{p+q}) / (1 - t^2).

It returns an error if p and q are not coprime.

funcs.name: ZeroLaurent
funcs.doc: ZeroLaurent returns the zero Laurent polynomial.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns L+other.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of L.

methods.name: Coeff
methods.doc: Coeff returns the coefficient of X^exp in L.

methods.name: CoeffSlice
methods.doc:
CoeffSlice returns the minimum exponent and a copy of the dense coefficient
slice of L, so that coeffs[i] is the coefficient of X^(min+i).

methods.name: ConstantTerm
methods.doc: ConstantTerm returns the coefficient of X^0.

methods.name: Content
methods.doc:
Content returns the greatest common divisor of the coefficients of L (the
integer content), and 0 for the zero polynomial.

methods.name: Derivative
methods.doc: Derivative returns the formal derivative dL/dX, itself a Laurent polynomial.

methods.name: DivExact
methods.signature: func (L Laurent) DivExact(other Laurent) (q Laurent, ok bool)
methods.doc:
DivExact returns the quotient L/other when the division is exact, and reports
ok=false otherwise. Division is performed in the Laurent ring: the result is
the unique Laurent polynomial q with q*other == L when one exists.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether L and other are the same polynomial.

methods.name: EqualUpToShift
methods.doc:
EqualUpToShift reports whether L and other agree after shifting one of them by
a power of X, that is whether they are equal as polynomials up to
multiplication by a monomial X^k. This is the natural equality for the
Alexander polynomial, which is only defined up to units.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval evaluates L at the real number x. Negative exponents use 1/x; Eval
panics only if x is zero and a negative exponent is present.

methods.name: EvalUnit
methods.doc:
EvalUnit evaluates L at an integer root of unity argument, meaning it returns
the integer value L(x) for x equal to +1 or -1, where all powers stay
integral. EvalUnit panics for any other x.

methods.name: IsConstant
methods.doc:
IsConstant reports whether L is a constant (a single term of degree zero, or
the zero polynomial).

methods.name: IsOne
methods.doc: IsOne reports whether L is the constant polynomial 1.

methods.name: IsPalindromic
methods.doc:
IsPalindromic reports whether L(X) equals L(X^{-1}) up to multiplication by a
power of X. The Alexander polynomial of a knot is always palindromic.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether L is the zero polynomial.

methods.name: LeadingCoeff
methods.doc:
LeadingCoeff returns the coefficient of the highest-degree term, or 0 for the
zero polynomial.

methods.name: MaxAbsCoeff
methods.doc:
MaxAbsCoeff returns the largest absolute value among the coefficients of L,
and 0 for the zero polynomial.

methods.name: MaxDegree
methods.doc:
MaxDegree returns the largest exponent that occurs with a non-zero
coefficient. It returns 0 for the zero polynomial.

methods.name: MinDegree
methods.doc:
MinDegree returns the smallest exponent that occurs with a non-zero
coefficient. It returns 0 for the zero polynomial.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the product L*other.

methods.name: MulMonomial
methods.signature: func (L Laurent) MulMonomial(c, e int) Laurent
methods.doc: MulMonomial returns L multiplied by the single term c*X^e.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns -L.

methods.name: NumTerms
methods.doc: NumTerms returns the number of non-zero terms of L.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns L raised to the non-negative power n. Pow panics if n is
negative because a general Laurent polynomial has no polynomial inverse; use
a monomial and ShiftExp for negative powers of a variable.

methods.name: Reverse
methods.doc:
Reverse returns L(X^{-1}), the polynomial obtained by replacing X with its
inverse. A palindromic polynomial (many knot invariants are) is fixed by
Reverse up to a shift.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns k*L.

methods.name: ShiftExp
methods.doc: ShiftExp returns X^d * L, that is L with every exponent shifted by d.

methods.name: SpanWidth
methods.doc:
SpanWidth returns MaxDegree-MinDegree, the breadth of the polynomial. The
span of the Jones polynomial is a lower bound for the crossing number.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders L in the variable X using conventional notation, for example
"X^-2 - X + 3". The zero polynomial renders as "0".

methods.name: StringVar
methods.doc: StringVar renders L using the supplied variable name.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns L-other.

methods.name: SubstitutePow
methods.doc:
SubstitutePow returns L with the variable X replaced by X^k, mapping every
term c*X^e to c*X^{e*k}. k must be non-zero.

methods.name: Terms
methods.doc: Terms returns the non-zero terms of L sorted by increasing exponent.

methods.name: TrailingCoeff
methods.doc:
TrailingCoeff returns the coefficient of the lowest-degree term, or 0 for the
zero polynomial.

types.name: LaurentMatrix
types.doc:
LaurentMatrix is a dense matrix whose entries are Laurent polynomials. It is
used for the Burau representation of a braid and for the Alexander matrix of a
diagram. Entries are stored in row-major order.

funcs.name: IdentityLaurentMatrix
funcs.doc:
IdentityLaurentMatrix returns the n-by-n identity matrix over the Laurent
ring.

funcs.name: NewLaurentMatrix
funcs.doc:
NewLaurentMatrix returns an r-by-c matrix with every entry the zero
polynomial.

methods.name: At
methods.doc: At returns the entry in row i, column j.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the matrix.

methods.name: Cols
methods.doc: Cols returns the number of columns of the matrix.

methods.name: Determinant
methods.doc:
Determinant returns the determinant of a square matrix computed by Laplace
(cofactor) expansion over the Laurent ring. It panics for a non-square
matrix. The method is intended for the small matrices that arise from braids
and diagrams of modest crossing number.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the matrix product m*other. It panics if the shapes are
incompatible.

methods.name: Rows
methods.doc: Rows returns the number of rows of the matrix.

methods.name: Set
methods.signature: func (m *LaurentMatrix) Set(i, j int, v Laurent)
methods.doc: Set stores v in row i, column j.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the entrywise difference m-other.

types.name: LaurentTerm
types.doc: LaurentTerm is a single monomial c*X^e of a Laurent polynomial.

types.name: PDCode
types.doc:
PDCode is a knot or link diagram in planar-diagram notation together with a
count of any crossingless unknotted circles. Edges are labelled by positive
integers, each of which appears exactly twice across all crossings.

funcs.name: CinquefoilPD
funcs.doc:
CinquefoilPD returns the (2,5) torus knot 5_1 (Solomon's seal knot) with
writhe +5.

funcs.name: FigureEightPD
funcs.doc:
FigureEightPD returns the figure-eight knot (4_1) diagram, an amphichiral knot
with writhe 0.

funcs.name: HopfLinkPD
funcs.doc:
HopfLinkPD returns a Hopf link diagram. When positive is true both crossings
are positive (writhe +2); otherwise both are negative (writhe -2).

funcs.name: LeftTrefoilPD
funcs.doc: LeftTrefoilPD returns the left-handed trefoil, the mirror image of TrefoilPD.

funcs.name: TrefoilPD
funcs.doc: TrefoilPD returns a right-handed trefoil (3_1) diagram with writhe +3.

funcs.name: UnknotPD
funcs.doc: UnknotPD returns the standard crossingless diagram of the unknot.

funcs.name: UnlinkPD
funcs.doc:
UnlinkPD returns the diagram of the n-component unlink (n crossingless
circles).

methods.name: AlexanderMatrix
methods.doc:
AlexanderMatrix returns the Alexander matrix of the diagram over the Laurent
ring in the variable t. It has one row per crossing and one column per arc;
each crossing contributes the Fox relation among its over-arc and its two
under-arcs.

methods.name: AlexanderPolynomial
methods.doc:
AlexanderPolynomial returns the Alexander polynomial of the diagram computed
from its Alexander matrix as the determinant of the minor obtained by
deleting the last row and last column, normalised to the symmetric
representative with value +1 at t=1. It returns an error for a diagram with
fewer than one crossing (use the unknot value 1 directly).

methods.name: ArcCount
methods.doc:
ArcCount returns the number of arcs of the diagram (maximal over-strands
between under-crossings).

methods.name: ArfInvariant
methods.doc:
ArfInvariant returns the Arf invariant (0 or 1) of the knot, computed from the
determinant: it is 0 when the determinant is congruent to +/-1 modulo 8 and 1
when it is congruent to +/-3 modulo 8.

methods.name: IsPColorable
methods.doc:
IsPColorable reports whether the diagram admits a non-trivial Fox p-colouring,
which for a prime p is equivalent to p dividing the determinant.

methods.name: IsThreeColorable
methods.doc: IsThreeColorable reports whether the diagram is Fox 3-colourable.

methods.name: JonesPolynomial
methods.doc:
JonesPolynomial returns the Jones polynomial in the variable t. It returns an
error when the polynomial contains genuine half-integer powers of t (which
happens for links with an even number of components); use JonesPolynomialSqrt
in that case.

methods.name: JonesPolynomialSqrt
methods.doc:
JonesPolynomialSqrt returns the Jones polynomial expressed in the variable
u = t^(1/2), so that a term c*u^k denotes c*t^(k/2). Every exponent is an
integer, which makes this form valid for links as well as knots.

methods.name: JonesSpan
methods.doc:
JonesSpan returns the breadth of the Jones polynomial (in the variable
t^{1/2}), a lower bound of twice the crossing number for a reduced
alternating diagram by the Kauffman-Murasugi-Thistlethwaite theorem.

methods.name: KauffmanBracket
methods.doc:
KauffmanBracket returns the Kauffman bracket polynomial of the diagram as a
Laurent polynomial in the variable A. It is computed by the state sum

	<D> = sum over states A^(a-b) * (-A^2 - A^-2)^(loops-1),

where a and b count A- and B-smoothings and loops is the number of circles in
the smoothed state. The bracket of the unknot is 1.

methods.name: KnotDeterminant
methods.doc:
KnotDeterminant returns the determinant of the diagram, the absolute value of
the Alexander polynomial evaluated at t = -1. It equals the order of the first
homology of the double branched cover.

methods.name: Mirror
methods.doc:
Mirror returns the PD code of the mirror image, obtained by swapping the
over- and under-arcs (A,B,C,D -> B,C,D,A) and negating every sign.

methods.name: NormalizedBracket
methods.doc:
NormalizedBracket returns the writhe-normalised Kauffman bracket
(-A^3)^(-w) <D>, an ambient-isotopy invariant (in the variable A) from which
the Jones polynomial is obtained by the substitution A = t^(-1/4).

methods.name: NumCrossings
methods.doc: NumCrossings returns the number of crossings of the diagram.

methods.name: SignSequence
methods.doc: SignSequence returns the signs of the crossings of the PD diagram in order.

methods.name: Validate
methods.doc:
Validate checks that every edge label occurs exactly twice and that every
sign is +1 or -1. A diagram with only circles and no crossings is valid.

methods.name: Writhe
methods.doc: Writhe returns the writhe of the diagram, the sum of the crossing signs.

types.name: PDCrossing
types.doc:
PDCrossing is one crossing of a planar-diagram (PD) code. The four edge
labels A, B, C, D are listed counter-clockwise starting from the incoming
under-strand: A is the incoming under-arc, C the outgoing under-arc, and B, D
the over-arc. Sign is the writhe contribution of the crossing (+1 or -1).

types.name: Permutation
types.signature: type Permutation []int
types.doc:
Permutation is a bijection of the set {0, 1, ..., n-1} represented by the
slice of images: p[i] is the image of i. It is used to describe the
underlying permutation of a braid and the number of components of its
closure.

funcs.name: IdentityPermutation
funcs.doc: IdentityPermutation returns the identity permutation on n points.

funcs.name: NewPermutation
funcs.doc:
NewPermutation returns the permutation with the given image slice after
checking that it is a genuine bijection of {0, ..., n-1}. It returns an error
if the slice is not a permutation.

funcs.name: TranspositionPermutation
funcs.signature: func TranspositionPermutation(n, i, j int) (Permutation, error)
funcs.doc:
TranspositionPermutation returns the permutation on n points that swaps i and
j and fixes everything else. It returns an error if i or j is out of range.

methods.name: Apply
methods.doc: Apply returns the image of i under the permutation.

methods.name: Compose
methods.doc:
Compose returns the permutation p∘q defined by (p∘q)(i) = p(q(i)). Both
permutations must act on the same number of points.

methods.name: Cycles
methods.doc:
Cycles returns the cycle decomposition of p as a slice of cycles, each cycle
being the list of points visited starting from its smallest element. Fixed
points appear as singleton cycles.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether p and q are the same permutation.

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc: Inverse returns the inverse permutation.

methods.name: IsIdentity
methods.doc: IsIdentity reports whether p fixes every point.

methods.name: NumCycles
methods.doc:
NumCycles returns the number of cycles in the decomposition of p, counting
fixed points. For the permutation of a braid this equals the number of
components of the braid closure.

methods.name: Order
methods.doc:
Order returns the multiplicative order of p, that is the least positive k with
p^k the identity. It equals the least common multiple of the cycle lengths.

methods.name: Power
methods.doc: Power returns p raised to the integer power k, which may be negative.

methods.name: Sign
methods.doc:
Sign returns +1 if p is an even permutation and -1 if it is odd. The sign is
(-1)^(n - number of cycles).

methods.name: Size
methods.doc: Size returns the number of points the permutation acts on.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders p in one-line notation, for example "[1 2 0]".

methods.name: TranspositionCount
methods.doc:
TranspositionCount returns the minimum number of transpositions needed to
express the permutation, equal to size minus the number of cycles.

methods.name: Transpositions
methods.doc:
Transpositions returns a decomposition of the permutation into transpositions.
Reading the returned list left to right and composing with Compose reproduces
the permutation.

funcs.name: BraidGroupRank
funcs.doc: BraidGroupRank returns the number of Artin generators of B_n, namely n-1.

funcs.name: IsTorusLink
funcs.doc:
IsTorusLink reports whether T(p, q) is a genuine link of more than one
component (gcd(|p|,|q|) > 1).

funcs.name: IsTrivialTorusKnot
funcs.doc:
IsTrivialTorusKnot reports whether T(p, q) is the unknot, which happens iff
|p| <= 1 or |q| <= 1 (and the pair is coprime).

funcs.name: IsValidBraidWord
funcs.doc:
IsValidBraidWord reports whether word is a legal word for B_n, that is every
entry is non-zero and has absolute value at most n-1.

funcs.name: TorusIsKnot
funcs.doc: TorusIsKnot reports whether T(p, q) is a knot, that is gcd(|p|,|q|) = 1.

funcs.name: TorusKnotArf
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotArf returns the Arf invariant of the torus knot T(p, q) with coprime
p, q, derived from its determinant.

funcs.name: TorusKnotBraidWord
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotBraidWord returns the generator word of the torus braid whose closure
is T(p, q).

funcs.name: TorusKnotBridgeNumber
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotBridgeNumber returns the bridge number of T(p, q) with coprime
p, q >= 2, which is min(|p|, |q|). The unknot has bridge number 1.

funcs.name: TorusKnotCrossingNumber
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotCrossingNumber returns the crossing number of the torus knot
T(p, q) with coprime p, q >= 2, namely min(p(q-1), q(p-1)). It returns 0 for
the unknot.

funcs.name: TorusKnotDeterminant
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotDeterminant returns the determinant of the torus knot T(p, q), the
absolute value of its Alexander polynomial at t = -1. For coprime p, q it is
1 when both are odd and p (equivalently q) when exactly one is even.

funcs.name: TorusKnotGenus
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotGenus returns the Seifert genus of the torus knot T(p, q) with
coprime p, q, which is (p-1)(q-1)/2.

funcs.name: TorusKnotSeifertGenus
funcs.doc: TorusKnotSeifertGenus is an alias for TorusKnotGenus.

funcs.name: TorusKnotUnknottingNumber
funcs.doc:
TorusKnotUnknottingNumber returns the unknotting number of the torus knot
T(p, q) with coprime p, q, equal to its genus (p-1)(q-1)/2 by the resolution
of the Milnor conjecture.

funcs.name: TorusLinkComponents
funcs.doc:
TorusLinkComponents returns the number of components of T(p, q), which is
gcd(|p|, |q|).

examples.name: trefoilInvariants
examples.code:
pd := TrefoilPD()
jones, _ := pd.JonesPolynomial()
alex, _ := pd.AlexanderPolynomial()
fmt.Println("writhe:", pd.Writhe())
fmt.Println("jones:", jones.StringVar("t"))
fmt.Println("alexander:", alex.StringVar("t"))
fmt.Println("determinant:", pd.KnotDeterminant())
fmt.Println("3-colorable:", pd.IsThreeColorable())
examples.output:
writhe: 3
jones: -t^4 + t^3 + t
alexander: t - 1 + t^-1
determinant: 3
3-colorable: true
examples.doc:
Example_trefoilInvariants computes several invariants of the right-handed
trefoil and prints them.

examples.name: Braid (AlexanderPolynomial)
examples.code:
b := MustBraid(3, 1, -2, 1, -2)
alex, _ := b.AlexanderPolynomial()
fmt.Println("components:", b.NumComponents())
fmt.Println("alexander:", alex.StringVar("t"))
examples.doc:
ExampleBraid_AlexanderPolynomial recovers the Alexander polynomial of the
figure-eight knot from a braid whose closure is that knot.

packages.name: lattices
packages.synopsis: Package lattices implements algorithms for integer and real lattices.
packages.doc:
Package lattices implements algorithms for integer and real lattices.

A lattice is the set of all integer linear combinations of a set of
linearly independent basis vectors b_0, ..., b_{m-1} living in R^n. This
package represents a basis as an ordered list of row vectors ([Basis]) and
provides the classical toolbox of lattice algorithms:

  - Gram matrices, Gram determinants and lattice covolume (determinant).
  - Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization with the mu coefficient matrix.
  - LLL (Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovasz) basis reduction and standalone size
    reduction.
  - Shortest-vector (SVP) and closest-vector (CVP) search by
    Fincke-Pohst enumeration.
  - Babai's rounding and nearest-plane approximate CVP solvers.
  - Minkowski and Hermite bounds and successive-minima estimates.
  - The dual (reciprocal) lattice.
  - Hermite normal form of integer matrices.

Vectors and dense matrices come in three flavours: [Vec] and [Matrix] use
float64 for speed and are used by the numeric reduction and enumeration
routines; [RatVec] and [RatMatrix] use math/big.Rat for exact rational
arithmetic (exact Gram determinants, exact inverses, exact duals); and
[IntMatrix] uses math/big.Int for exact integer work such as the Hermite
normal form. Everything is implemented with the Go standard library only.

Conventions: bases are lists of rows, so a basis of m vectors in R^n is an
m-element [Basis] whose entries are n-dimensional [Vec] values. The Gram
matrix of a basis is G with G[i][j] = <b_i, b_j>, and the lattice
determinant (covolume) is sqrt(det G).

consts.signature: const DefaultDelta = 0.75
consts.doc:
DefaultDelta is the standard Lovasz parameter used by LLL reduction. Values
in the open interval (1/4, 1) guarantee termination; 3/4 is the classical
choice.

vars.signature: var ErrBadParameter = errors.New("lattices: parameter out of range")
vars.doc:
ErrBadParameter is returned when a numeric parameter is outside its valid
range (for example an LLL delta outside (1/4, 1]).

vars.signature: var ErrDimMismatch = errors.New("lattices: dimension mismatch")
vars.doc:
ErrDimMismatch is returned when two vectors or matrices have incompatible
dimensions for the requested operation.

vars.signature: var ErrEmpty = errors.New("lattices: empty input")
vars.doc:
ErrEmpty is returned when an operation requires a non-empty basis, vector or
matrix but received an empty one.

vars.signature: var ErrNoSolution = errors.New("lattices: no solution found")
vars.doc:
ErrNoSolution is returned when an enumeration or search finds no vector
satisfying the requested constraints.

vars.signature: var ErrNotFullRank = errors.New("lattices: basis is not full rank")
vars.doc:
ErrNotFullRank is returned when a basis is expected to be full rank (its
vectors linearly independent) but is rank deficient.

vars.signature: var ErrNotSquare = errors.New("lattices: matrix is not square")
vars.doc:
ErrNotSquare is returned when a square matrix is required but the argument is
rectangular.

vars.signature: var ErrRankMismatch = errors.New("lattices: basis rank does not match ambient dimension")
vars.doc:
ErrRankMismatch is returned when the number of basis vectors does not match
the ambient dimension for an operation that requires a square basis.

vars.signature: var ErrSingular = errors.New("lattices: matrix is singular")
vars.doc:
ErrSingular is returned when a matrix is singular and therefore cannot be
inverted or used to solve a linear system.

types.name: Basis
types.signature: type Basis []Vec
types.doc:
Basis is a lattice basis: an ordered list of row vectors that generate the
lattice by integer linear combination. The vectors should be linearly
independent; the number of vectors is the lattice rank and the length of each
vector is the ambient dimension.

funcs.name: BasisFromMatrix
funcs.doc: BasisFromMatrix builds a Basis whose vectors are the rows of m.

funcs.name: BasisFromRows
funcs.doc: BasisFromRows builds a Basis from a slice of float64 row slices.

funcs.name: IdentityBasis
funcs.doc: IdentityBasis returns the standard basis of Z^n (the n rows of the identity).

funcs.name: NewBasis
funcs.doc:
NewBasis returns a Basis containing copies of the given row vectors. It
panics if the rows have differing lengths.

methods.name: BabaiCoeffs
methods.doc:
BabaiCoeffs returns the integer coordinate vector produced by Babai rounding
of target, so that b.Point(result) equals BabaiRound(target). It panics on a
dimension mismatch.

methods.name: BabaiNearestPlane
methods.doc:
BabaiNearestPlane returns an approximate closest lattice vector to target
using Babai's nearest-plane algorithm, which walks the Gram-Schmidt frame
from the last vector to the first. It typically produces a closer vector than
BabaiRound. It panics if the dimension of target does not match the ambient
dimension.

methods.name: BabaiRound
methods.doc:
BabaiRound returns an approximate closest lattice vector to target using
Babai's rounding technique: express the target in basis coordinates (in a
least-squares sense) and round each coordinate to the nearest integer. It is
fast but only reliable for reasonably orthogonal bases. It panics if the
dimension of target does not match the ambient dimension.

methods.name: Biorthogonality
methods.doc:
Biorthogonality returns the matrix M[i][j] = <d_i, b_j> where d is the dual
basis; it should equal the identity matrix up to numerical error. It returns
ErrEmpty or ErrNotFullRank as appropriate.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent deep copy of the basis.

methods.name: ClosestVector
methods.doc:
ClosestVector returns the lattice vector nearest to target using enumeration
seeded by Babai's nearest-plane approximation for the search radius. The
returned Norm2 is the squared distance to the target. It returns ErrEmpty,
ErrDimMismatch or ErrNotFullRank as appropriate.

methods.name: Coordinates
methods.doc:
Coordinates returns the coefficient vector x such that b represents v, that
is sum_i x[i]*b_i = v, by solving the (possibly overdetermined) system in a
least-squares sense via the normal equations G x = B v. It returns
ErrNotFullRank when the Gram matrix is singular.

methods.name: Determinant
methods.doc:
Determinant returns the lattice determinant (covolume) sqrt(det G). It is the
volume of a fundamental parallelepiped and is invariant under change of
basis.

methods.name: Dim
methods.doc:
Dim returns the ambient dimension (length of each vector), or 0 for an empty
basis.

methods.name: Dual
methods.doc:
Dual returns the dual (reciprocal) lattice basis d_0, ..., d_{n-1}, the unique
vectors in the span of the basis satisfying <d_i, b_j> = 1 if i == j and 0
otherwise. It is computed as G^{-1} B, where G is the Gram matrix and B the
basis rows. It returns ErrEmpty for an empty basis and ErrNotFullRank when the
Gram matrix is singular.

methods.name: DualDeterminant
methods.doc:
DualDeterminant returns the covolume of the dual lattice, which equals the
reciprocal of the covolume of the primal lattice. It returns 0 for a
degenerate lattice.

methods.name: DualGramDeterminantRat
methods.doc:
DualGramDeterminantRat returns the exact determinant of the dual Gram matrix,
which equals the reciprocal of the primal Gram determinant.

methods.name: DualRat
methods.doc:
DualRat returns the exact rational dual basis, computed with exact rational
arithmetic. It returns ErrEmpty or ErrNotFullRank as appropriate.

methods.name: EnumerateVectors
methods.signature: func (b Basis) EnumerateVectors(radius float64, limit int) ([]EnumResult, bool, error)
methods.doc:
EnumerateVectors returns all nonzero lattice vectors whose Euclidean norm is
at most radius, each as an EnumResult, together with a completeness flag that
is false if the internal limit on the number of returned vectors was reached.
The search uses Fincke-Pohst enumeration. It returns ErrEmpty or
ErrNotFullRank as appropriate.

methods.name: FirstMinimum
methods.doc:
FirstMinimum returns lambda_1, the length of the shortest nonzero lattice
vector, computed exactly by enumeration. It returns 0 with an error for an
empty or rank-deficient basis.

methods.name: GaussianHeuristicBasis
methods.doc:
GaussianHeuristicBasis returns the Gaussian heuristic evaluated for the
basis.

methods.name: Gram
methods.doc: Gram returns the Gram matrix G with G[i][j] = <b_i, b_j>.

methods.name: GramDeterminant
methods.doc:
GramDeterminant returns det(G), the determinant of the Gram matrix. For a
full-rank basis this equals the square of the lattice covolume.

methods.name: GramDeterminantRat
methods.doc: GramDeterminantRat returns the exact determinant of the Gram matrix.

methods.name: GramInverseRat
methods.doc:
GramInverseRat returns the exact inverse of the rational Gram matrix, the
Gram matrix of the dual basis. It returns ErrEmpty or ErrNotFullRank as
appropriate.

methods.name: GramRat
methods.doc: GramRat returns the exact rational Gram matrix of the basis.

methods.name: GramSchmidtNorms
methods.doc: GramSchmidtNorms returns the squared norms of the Gram-Schmidt vectors b*_i.

methods.name: HadamardRatio
methods.doc:
HadamardRatio returns covolume / prod_i |b_i|, a value in (0, 1]. Values near
1 indicate a nearly orthogonal ("good") basis.

methods.name: HermiteBoundBasis
methods.doc:
HermiteBoundBasis returns HermiteBound evaluated at the rank and determinant
of the basis.

methods.name: HermiteNormalForm
methods.doc:
HermiteNormalForm returns the row Hermite normal form of an integer basis. It
returns ErrBadParameter if the basis has non-integer entries and ErrEmpty for
an empty basis.

methods.name: IsDualTo
methods.signature: func (b Basis) IsDualTo(other Basis, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
IsDualTo reports whether other is (numerically, within tol) the dual basis of
b, i.e. whether <other_i, b_j> is the identity matrix within tol.

methods.name: IsFullRank
methods.doc:
IsFullRank reports whether the basis vectors are linearly independent, i.e.
the Gram determinant is nonzero within the tolerance tol.

methods.name: IsLLLReduced
methods.signature: func (b Basis) IsLLLReduced(delta, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
IsLLLReduced reports whether the basis is LLL reduced for parameter delta
within tolerance tol: it must be size reduced and satisfy the Lovasz
condition |b*_k|^2 >= (delta - mu[k][k-1]^2) |b*_{k-1}|^2 for every k.

methods.name: IsSizeReduced
methods.doc:
IsSizeReduced reports whether every Gram-Schmidt coefficient Mu[i][j] with
j < i has absolute value at most 1/2 + tol, the size-reduced condition.

methods.name: IsSquare
methods.doc: IsSquare reports whether the number of vectors equals the ambient dimension.

methods.name: KissingNumber
methods.doc:
KissingNumber returns the number of shortest nonzero lattice vectors, that is
the number of lattice vectors of length exactly lambda_1 (which always comes
in plus/minus pairs). It returns an error for an empty or rank-deficient
basis.

methods.name: LLL
methods.doc:
LLL returns an LLL-reduced basis for the same lattice using the given Lovasz
parameter delta, which must lie in (1/4, 1]. It applies the classical
Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovasz algorithm: repeated size reduction interleaved with
swaps whenever the Lovasz condition fails. It returns ErrBadParameter for an
out-of-range delta and ErrEmpty for an empty basis.

methods.name: LLLDefault
methods.doc: LLLDefault returns an LLL-reduced basis using DefaultDelta (3/4).

methods.name: LLLGramDefect
methods.doc:
LLLGramDefect returns the orthogonality defect of the LLL-reduced basis, a
convenient quality measure after reduction.

methods.name: Matrix
methods.doc: Matrix returns the basis as a Matrix whose rows are the basis vectors.

methods.name: MaxNorm
methods.doc: MaxNorm returns the largest Euclidean norm among the basis vectors.

methods.name: MinkowskiBoundBasis
methods.doc:
MinkowskiBoundBasis returns MinkowskiBound evaluated at the rank and
determinant of the basis.

methods.name: MuMatrix
methods.doc:
MuMatrix returns the strictly lower-triangular matrix of Gram-Schmidt
coefficients (with ones on the diagonal) as a Matrix.

methods.name: OrthogonalNormal
methods.doc:
OrthogonalNormal returns the Gram-Schmidt vectors normalized to unit length,
forming an orthonormal basis of the span. Zero vectors are left unchanged.

methods.name: OrthogonalityDefect
methods.doc:
OrthogonalityDefect returns the ratio prod_i |b_i| / covolume, a measure of
how far the basis is from orthogonal. It equals 1 exactly for an orthogonal
basis and is at least 1 otherwise (Hadamard's inequality).

methods.name: Orthogonalize
methods.doc:
Orthogonalize computes the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization of the basis
without normalizing the resulting vectors. The i-th orthogonal vector is
b_i minus its projection onto the span of the earlier orthogonal vectors.

methods.name: Point
methods.doc:
Point returns the lattice point sum_i coeffs[i]*b_i for integer coefficients.
It panics if len(coeffs) != Rank.

methods.name: PointFloat
methods.doc:
PointFloat returns sum_i coeffs[i]*b_i for real coefficients (a point in the
real span of the lattice). It panics if len(coeffs) != Rank.

methods.name: ProductOfStarNorms
methods.doc:
ProductOfStarNorms returns prod_i |b*_i|, which for a full-rank basis equals
the lattice covolume exactly (Gram-Schmidt preserves volume).

methods.name: Rank
methods.doc: Rank returns the number of vectors in the basis.

methods.name: Row
methods.doc: Row returns the i-th basis vector (the stored slice, not a copy).

methods.name: ShortestBasisVector
methods.doc:
ShortestBasisVector returns the index and Euclidean norm of the shortest
nonzero vector currently in the basis (not the lattice minimum). It returns
-1 and 0 for an empty basis.

methods.name: ShortestVector
methods.doc:
ShortestVector returns the shortest nonzero vector of the lattice by
Fincke-Pohst enumeration. The basis is LLL reduced first to obtain a good
starting radius, so the returned Norm2 is the squared first minimum
lambda_1^2. It returns ErrEmpty for an empty basis and ErrNotFullRank when
the basis is rank deficient.

methods.name: SizeReduced
methods.doc:
SizeReduced returns a copy of the basis that is size reduced: every
Gram-Schmidt coefficient mu[i][j] with j < i is made to satisfy
|mu[i][j]| <= 1/2 by subtracting integer multiples of earlier vectors. Size
reduction does not reorder the basis and leaves the lattice unchanged.

methods.name: StarBasis
methods.doc: StarBasis returns the orthogonalized vectors b*_i as a Basis.

methods.name: SuccessiveMinima
methods.doc:
SuccessiveMinima returns the successive minima lambda_1 <= ... <= lambda_n of
the lattice, computed exactly by enumerating short vectors and greedily
collecting linearly independent ones. It returns ErrEmpty or ErrNotFullRank
as appropriate and ErrNoSolution if the enumeration limit is exhausted before
n independent vectors are found.

methods.name: Swap
methods.doc: Swap exchanges basis vectors i and j in place.

methods.name: Volume
methods.doc: Volume is an alias for Determinant: the covolume of the lattice.

types.name: EnumResult
types.doc:
EnumResult holds the outcome of a lattice enumeration search: the lattice
Vector found, its integer coordinate vector Coeffs in the given basis, and
the squared Euclidean quantity Norm2 that was minimized (the squared norm for
a shortest-vector search or the squared distance to the target for a
closest-vector search).

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc: Norm returns the Euclidean norm (or distance) sqrt(Norm2) of the result.

types.name: GramSchmidt
types.doc:
GramSchmidt holds the result of Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization of a basis:
the orthogonal vectors Star (the b*_i), the strictly lower-triangular matrix
of Gram-Schmidt coefficients Mu (Mu[i][j] = <b_i, b*_j>/<b*_j, b*_j> for
j < i), and the squared norms Norm2 of the orthogonal vectors.

types.name: IntMatrix
types.doc:
IntMatrix is a dense matrix of exact integers (*big.Int) stored row-major. It
