form and the result is returned in normal form; the exponentiation itself runs
entirely in the Montgomery domain via square-and-multiply over [Montgomery.MulMont].

methods.name: ToMont
methods.doc:
ToMont maps a into the Montgomery domain, returning a*R mod n. a is reduced
modulo n first.

types.name: PrimePower
types.doc: PrimePower pairs a prime with its exponent in a factorization.

funcs.name: FactorList
funcs.doc:
FactorList returns the prime factorization of |n| as a slice of
[PrimePower] values sorted by ascending prime. It is the ordered counterpart
of [Factorize]. FactorList(0) and FactorList(±1) return nil.

types.name: PrimePowerBig
types.doc:
PrimePowerBig pairs an arbitrary-precision prime with its exponent in a
factorization. It is the [math/big] analogue of [PrimePower].

funcs.name: FactorizeBig
funcs.doc:
FactorizeBig returns the prime factorization of |n| as a slice of
[PrimePowerBig] values sorted by ascending prime. The sign of n is ignored.

It trial-divides by the cached small primes from sieve.go (up to
[ntheoryBigTrialLimit]), tests the residual with [IsProbablePrimeBig], and
splits any remaining composite with [PollardRhoBig], recursing until only
primes remain. It is the arbitrary-precision counterpart of [FactorList] and
returns nil for |n| < 2.

types.name: PrimePowerU64
types.doc:
PrimePowerU64 pairs a prime with its exponent in a uint64 factorization. It is
the unsigned counterpart of [PrimePower].

funcs.name: FactorListUint64
funcs.doc:
FactorListUint64 returns the prime factorization of n as a slice of
[PrimePowerU64] sorted by ascending prime. It is the ordered counterpart of
[FactorizeU64]. FactorListUint64(0) and FactorListUint64(1) return nil.

types.name: PrimeSieve
types.doc:
PrimeSieve is a stateful generator that yields the primes in ascending order,
one per call to [PrimeSieve.Next], without ever holding them all in memory. It
advances through fixed-size segments lazily, reusing a single mod-30 wheel
window and the shared base-prime cache. The zero value is not usable; obtain
one from [NewPrimeSieve]. A PrimeSieve is not safe for concurrent use.

funcs.name: NewPrimeSieve
funcs.doc:
NewPrimeSieve returns a PrimeSieve positioned before the first prime, so the
first call to Next returns 2.

methods.name: Next
methods.doc:
Next returns the next prime in ascending order, advancing to a further
segment only when the current one is exhausted. Successive calls yield
2, 3, 5, 7, 11, ... deterministically.

funcs.name: AddModU64
funcs.signature: func AddModU64(a, b, m uint64) uint64
funcs.doc:
AddModU64 returns (a + b) mod m without overflowing, even when a + b does not
fit in a uint64. m must be positive; AddModU64 panics if m == 0. The result
lies in [0, m).

funcs.name: AllSqrtModComposite
funcs.doc:
AllSqrtModComposite returns every square root of a modulo the composite
modulus m, sorted ascending. The modulus is factored with [FactorListUint64],
each prime-power component is solved for its complete set of roots, and the
component roots are recombined across all combinations with [CRT]. The result
is empty when a is a non-residue modulo some component (and therefore has no
square root modulo m).

m must be greater than 0; it panics when m == 0. a is reduced modulo m before
use.

funcs.name: Bernoulli
funcs.doc:
Bernoulli returns the n-th Bernoulli number Bₙ as an exact rational
(math/big.Rat), using the convention B₁ = -1/2. All odd-indexed Bernoulli
numbers beyond B₁ are zero. n must be non-negative.

It is computed from the recurrence Σ_{k=0}^{n} C(n+1, k)·Bₖ = 0.

funcs.name: Binomial
funcs.doc:
Binomial returns the binomial coefficient C(n, k) = n! / (k!·(n-k)!) as an
arbitrary-precision integer. It returns 0 when k < 0 or k > n. n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: CRT
funcs.signature: func CRT(r, n []int64) (x, mod int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
CRT solves a system of simultaneous congruences via the Chinese Remainder
Theorem. Given residues r and moduli n of equal length, it returns the unique
x in [0, N) with x ≡ r[i] (mod n[i]) for all i, where N is the least common
multiple of the moduli, together with N and ok == true.

The moduli need not be pairwise coprime; if the congruences are inconsistent
(no solution exists) it returns (0, 0, false). Every modulus must be
positive, and r and n must have the same non-zero length.

funcs.name: Carmichael
funcs.doc:
Carmichael returns λ(n), the Carmichael function: the exponent of the
multiplicative group modulo n, i.e. the smallest m > 0 with a^m ≡ 1 (mod n)
for every a coprime to n.

It is computed from the prime factorization ([FactorizeU64]) as the least
common multiple of λ over the prime powers of n, where λ(2) = 1, λ(4) = 2,
λ(2^k) = 2^(k−2) for k ≥ 3, and λ(p^k) = p^(k−1)(p−1) for odd primes p.
By convention λ(1) = 1 (and λ(0) = 0). n is unsigned, so its sign is
irrelevant.

funcs.name: CatalanNumber
funcs.doc:
CatalanNumber returns the n-th Catalan number Cₙ = C(2n, n) / (n+1) as an
arbitrary-precision integer. n must be non-negative; CatalanNumber(0) == 1.

funcs.name: ContinuedFraction
funcs.doc:
ContinuedFraction returns the finite continued-fraction expansion
[a0; a1, a2, ...] of the rational p/q (q != 0) as a slice of int64 partial
quotients via the Euclidean algorithm. The expansion is the canonical one:
a0 = floor(p/q) may be negative or zero, while every later partial quotient is
positive. It panics if q == 0.

funcs.name: ContinuedFractionRat
funcs.doc:
ContinuedFractionRat is the big.Rat form of ContinuedFraction for exact large
rationals. It returns the canonical finite expansion [a0; a1, a2, ...] of r as
a slice of int64 partial quotients, computed with the Euclidean algorithm on
arbitrary-precision integers. r must be non-nil.

funcs.name: Convergents
funcs.doc:
Convergents returns the successive convergents of the partial-quotient slice
cf as reduced big.Rat values. The i-th element is the value of the truncated
continued fraction [cf[0]; cf[1], ..., cf[i]]. They are computed with the
classic h/k recurrence

	h_i = a_i*h_{i-1} + h_{i-2},  k_i = a_i*k_{i-1} + k_{i-2},

seeded by h_{-1}=1, h_{-2}=0, k_{-1}=0, k_{-2}=1, so each convergent is
obtained from the previous two without re-multiplying whole fractions. The
result has len(cf) entries; an empty cf yields an empty slice.

funcs.name: CountDivisors
funcs.doc:
CountDivisors returns τ(n), the number of positive divisors of n.
The sign of n is ignored. CountDivisors(0) == 0.

funcs.name: CountDivisorsBig
funcs.doc:
CountDivisorsBig returns tau(|n|), the number of positive divisors of |n|. The
sign of n is ignored. CountDivisorsBig(0) == 0 and CountDivisorsBig(1) == 1.
It is the arbitrary-precision counterpart of [CountDivisors] and derives from
[FactorizeBig] via the product tau(n) = prod (e + 1) over the exponents e in
the factorization.

funcs.name: DiscreteLog
funcs.signature: func DiscreteLog(g, h, m int64) (x int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
DiscreteLog returns the smallest non-negative x with g**x ≡ h (mod m), found
via the baby-step giant-step algorithm, together with ok == true. If no such
x below m exists it returns (0, false). m must be positive and g should be a
unit modulo m for a solution to be guaranteed searchable; the search space is
bounded by m.

funcs.name: DivisorSigma
funcs.doc:
DivisorSigma returns σ_k(n), the sum of the k-th powers of the positive
divisors of n: Σ_{d | n} d^k. The sign of n is ignored.

Special cases: σ_0(n) is the number of divisors τ(n), and σ_1(n) is the sum of
divisors σ(n). k must be non-negative. DivisorSigma(k, 0) == 0, and for n >= 1
the value is computed from the prime factorization via the multiplicative
formula σ_k(p^a) = (p^(k(a+1)) - 1)/(p^k - 1) for k > 0, and (a+1) for k == 0.

funcs.name: Divisors
funcs.doc:
Divisors returns all positive divisors of n in ascending order.

The sign of n is ignored. Divisors(0) returns nil because zero has
infinitely many divisors.

funcs.name: DoubleFactorial
funcs.doc:
DoubleFactorial returns n!! = n·(n-2)·(n-4)·…, the product of every second
integer down to 1 or 2. By convention DoubleFactorial(0) ==
DoubleFactorial(-1) == 1. n must be >= -1.

funcs.name: EulerPhi
funcs.doc:
EulerPhi returns Euler's totient φ(n): the count of integers in [1, n] that
are coprime to n. The sign of n is ignored. By convention EulerPhi(0) == 0
and EulerPhi(1) == 1.

funcs.name: EulerPhiBig
funcs.doc:
EulerPhiBig returns Euler's totient phi(|n|): the count of integers in [1, |n|]
that are coprime to |n|. The sign of n is ignored. By convention
EulerPhiBig(0) == 0 and EulerPhiBig(1) == 1. It is the arbitrary-precision
counterpart of [EulerPhi] and derives from [FactorizeBig] via the product
phi(n) = prod p^(e-1) * (p - 1) over the prime powers p^e dividing n.

funcs.name: ExtendedGCD
funcs.signature: func ExtendedGCD(a, b int64) (g, x, y int64)
funcs.doc:
ExtendedGCD returns g = GCD(a, b) together with Bézout coefficients x and y
satisfying a*x + b*y == g. The returned g is non-negative.

funcs.name: Factorial
funcs.doc:
Factorial returns n! as an arbitrary-precision integer. n must be
non-negative; Factorial(0) == 1.

funcs.name: Factorize
funcs.doc:
Factorize returns the prime factorization of |n| as a map from each prime
factor to its exponent. Factorize(0) and Factorize(±1) return an empty
(non-nil) map. The sign of n is ignored.

See [FactorList] for a deterministically ordered slice representation.

funcs.name: FactorizeU64
funcs.doc:
FactorizeU64 returns the complete prime factorization of n as a map from each
prime factor to its (positive) multiplicity.

It first strips small primes by trial division, tests the residual with
[IsPrimeU64], and splits any remaining composite with [PollardBrentU64],
recursing until only primes remain. This makes it subexponential and
dramatically faster than the pure trial-division [Factorize] on large
semiprimes, while covering the full uint64 range. FactorizeU64(0) and
FactorizeU64(1) return an empty (non-nil) map.

funcs.name: Fibonacci
funcs.doc:
Fibonacci returns the n-th Fibonacci number Fₙ, with F₀ = 0, F₁ = 1 and
Fₙ = Fₙ₋₁ + Fₙ₋₂, as an arbitrary-precision integer. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: GCD
funcs.doc:
GCD returns the greatest common divisor of a and b.

The result is always non-negative. By convention GCD(0, 0) == 0 and
GCD(n, 0) == GCD(0, n) == |n|. Signs of the inputs are ignored.

funcs.name: IsPerfect
funcs.doc:
IsPerfect reports whether n is a perfect number, i.e. a positive integer
equal to the sum of its proper divisors (σ(n) == 2n). The smallest perfect
numbers are 6, 28, 496 and 8128.

funcs.name: IsPrime
funcs.doc:
IsPrime reports whether n is prime using a deterministic Miller-Rabin test.

The chosen set of witness bases makes the test provably correct for every
value representable by an int64, so the answer is exact (never merely
probable). Negative numbers, 0 and 1 are not prime. Carmichael numbers such
as 561 are correctly reported as composite.

funcs.name: IsPrimeBig
funcs.doc:
IsPrimeBig reports whether n is (very probably) prime using
math/big.Int.ProbablyPrime with 20 Miller-Rabin rounds plus a Baillie-PSW
test. No composite number is known to pass, making it suitable for the
arbitrary-precision case where a deterministic int64 test does not apply.
Values below 2 are not prime.

funcs.name: IsPrimeU64
funcs.doc:
IsPrimeU64 reports whether n is prime. It is a deterministic test that is
exact for every value of a uint64 (never merely probable).

It first trial-divides by the primes below 64, which rejects most composites
immediately, then runs strong-probable-prime tests to the fixed base set
[ntheoryU64Witnesses], which is proven to give the correct answer for all
n < 2^64. The inner powering uses Montgomery multiplication from fastmod.go
whenever the modulus is odd. Values below 2 are not prime.

This is the uint64 counterpart of the int64-only [IsPrime]; use it when
operands can exceed 2^63.

funcs.name: IsPrimitiveRoot
funcs.doc:
IsPrimitiveRoot reports whether g is a primitive root modulo m, i.e. whether
the multiplicative order of g modulo m equals φ(m). g must be coprime to m for
the result to be true. m must be positive; IsPrimitiveRoot(g, 1) == true.

Rather than compute the order outright, it applies the fast criterion: with
gcd(g, m) = 1, g is a primitive root iff g^(φ(m)/q) ≠ 1 (mod m) for every
prime q dividing φ(m) ([FactorizeU64] and [ModPowU64]). This also correctly
reports false when the group is not cyclic, since then no g attains order
φ(m).

funcs.name: IsProbablePrimeBig
funcs.doc:
IsProbablePrimeBig reports whether the arbitrary-precision n is (very
probably) prime using a Baillie-PSW test: a strong probable-prime test to
base 2 together with a strong Lucas-Selfridge test, then additional
Miller-Rabin rounds delegated to [math/big.Int.ProbablyPrime]. No composite
is known to pass, and because none of the steps use randomness the result is
deterministic for a given n. Values below 2 are not prime.

funcs.name: IsQuadraticResidue
funcs.doc:
IsQuadraticResidue reports whether a is a quadratic residue modulo the prime
p, i.e. whether some x satisfies x**2 ≡ a (mod p). Zero counts as a residue
(x == 0). p must be prime.

funcs.name: IsSquare
funcs.doc:
IsSquare reports whether n is a perfect square (n == k*k for some integer k).
Negative numbers are never perfect squares; 0 and 1 are.

funcs.name: IsqrtBig
funcs.doc:
IsqrtBig returns the integer square root of n: the greatest integer r with
r*r <= n, using math/big's exact Sqrt. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: JacobiSymbol
funcs.doc:
JacobiSymbol returns the Jacobi symbol (a/n) for an odd positive integer n.
It generalizes the Legendre symbol to composite n; note that a result of +1
does not by itself prove a is a quadratic residue when n is composite. n must
be odd and positive.

funcs.name: LCM
funcs.doc:
LCM returns the least common multiple of a and b.

The result is non-negative and LCM(a, 0) == LCM(0, b) == 0. The caller is
responsible for ensuring the true least common multiple fits in an int64.

funcs.name: LegendreSymbol
funcs.doc:
LegendreSymbol returns the Legendre symbol (a/p) for an odd prime p:

	 0 if a ≡ 0 (mod p),
	+1 if a is a non-zero quadratic residue modulo p,
	-1 if a is a non-residue.

p must be an odd prime; the behavior is otherwise undefined.

funcs.name: Lucas
funcs.doc:
Lucas returns the n-th Lucas number Lₙ, with L₀ = 2, L₁ = 1 and
Lₙ = Lₙ₋₁ + Lₙ₋₂, as an arbitrary-precision integer. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: MertensFunction
funcs.doc:
MertensFunction returns M(n) = Σ_{k=1}^{n} μ(k), the Mertens function, the
running sum of the Möbius function up to n. MertensFunction(0) == 0. It sieves
the Möbius values once and accumulates them. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: MillerRabinU64
funcs.doc:
MillerRabinU64 runs a single strong-probable-prime (Miller-Rabin) test of n
to base a and reports whether n passes it. A return of false proves n is
composite; a return of true means n is a strong probable prime to base a
(definitely prime only after enough well-chosen bases pass — see
[IsPrimeU64]).

The base is reduced modulo n; a base that is a multiple of n carries no
information and is reported as passing. n < 2 fails and n = 2 passes; every
other even n fails. The inner powering is Montgomery-accelerated, so the test
is cheap enough to reuse from factoring code such as factor.go.

funcs.name: MobiusMu
funcs.doc:
MobiusMu returns the Möbius function μ(n): 0 if n is divisible by a square
greater than 1, otherwise (-1)^k where k is the number of distinct prime
factors. The sign of n is ignored and μ(1) == 1. MobiusMu(0) == 0.

funcs.name: MobiusSieve
funcs.doc:
MobiusSieve returns a slice mu of length n+1 where mu[i] == μ(i), the Möbius
function of i, for every 0 <= i <= n. By convention mu[0] == 0 and mu[1] == 1.

All values are produced in one sieve pass using smallest-prime factorization,
which is much faster than evaluating [MobiusMu] at each i separately. n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: ModInverse
funcs.signature: func ModInverse(a, m int64) (inv int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
ModInverse returns the modular multiplicative inverse of a modulo m: the
unique x in [0, m) with a*x ≡ 1 (mod m), together with ok == true. If a and m
are not coprime no inverse exists and it returns (0, false). m must be
positive.

funcs.name: ModPow
funcs.signature: func ModPow(base, exp, m int64) int64
funcs.doc:
ModPow returns base**exp mod m computed with math/big internally so no
intermediate value overflows. The result is normalized to the range [0, m).

m must be positive. A negative exp is supported when base is invertible
modulo m: the inverse of base is raised to |exp|; if base is not invertible
modulo m, ModPow panics.

funcs.name: ModPowU64
funcs.signature: func ModPowU64(base, exp, m uint64) uint64
funcs.doc:
ModPowU64 returns base**exp mod m by square-and-multiply. base is reduced
modulo m first. m must be positive; ModPowU64 panics if m == 0, and returns 0
when m == 1.

When m is odd it multiplies through a Montgomery context (division-free REDC
in the inner loop); for even m it falls back to square-and-multiply over
[MulModU64]. The result lies in [0, m).

funcs.name: MulModU64
funcs.signature: func MulModU64(a, b, m uint64) uint64
funcs.doc:
MulModU64 returns a*b mod m computed from the full 128-bit product with
math/bits (bits.Mul64 followed by bits.Div64), so no intermediate value
overflows and no math/big allocation is required.

m must be positive; MulModU64 panics if m == 0. The high word of the product
is reduced modulo m first (so it is strictly less than m) to keep bits.Div64
from overflowing. The result lies in [0, m).

funcs.name: Multinomial
funcs.doc:
Multinomial returns the multinomial coefficient (Σkᵢ)! / (k₀!·k₁!·…) as an
arbitrary-precision integer. Every kᵢ must be non-negative. Multinomial with
no arguments returns 1.

funcs.name: MultiplicativeOrder
funcs.doc:
MultiplicativeOrder returns the multiplicative order of a modulo m — the
smallest k > 0 with a^k ≡ 1 (mod m) — together with ok == true. It returns
(0, false) when gcd(a, m) ≠ 1, since a then has no order. m must be positive.

It is computed the fast way: the order necessarily divides λ(m), so it starts
from ord = [Carmichael](m) and, for each prime q dividing λ(m), divides q out
of ord as long as a^(ord/q) ≡ 1 (mod m) still holds ([ModPowU64]). This tests
only the prime-quotient candidates rather than enumerating every divisor, so
it is asymptotically far cheaper than the divisor-scanning [Order].

funcs.name: NextPrime
funcs.doc: NextPrime returns the smallest prime strictly greater than n.

funcs.name: NextPrimeBig
funcs.doc:
NextPrimeBig returns the least prime strictly greater than n, using
[IsProbablePrimeBig] to test candidates and a mod-30 wheel to skip multiples
of 2, 3 and 5. For n < 2 it returns 2. The returned value is a fresh
*big.Int; n is not modified.

funcs.name: NextPrimeU64
funcs.doc:
NextPrimeU64 returns the least prime strictly greater than n.

Unlike the int64 [NextPrime] it works correctly for values near and above
2^63 without overflow. Candidate stepping uses a mod-30 wheel so only the
eight residues coprime to 30 are tested. If no prime greater than n is
representable in a uint64 (that is, n is at least the largest uint64 prime
18446744073709551557), it returns 0.

funcs.name: NthPrime
funcs.doc:
NthPrime returns the n-th prime, 1-indexed, so NthPrime(1) == 2,
NthPrime(2) == 3 and so on. It estimates an upper bound with
n(ln n + ln ln n), warms the base-prime cache up to the square root of that
bound, then streams a segmented sieve until the n-th prime appears. It panics
if n < 1.

funcs.name: Order
funcs.signature: func Order(a, m int64) (ord int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
Order returns the multiplicative order of a modulo m: the smallest positive
integer k with a**k ≡ 1 (mod m), together with ok == true. If a and m are not
coprime the order does not exist and it returns (0, false). m must be
positive.

funcs.name: Partition
funcs.doc:
Partition returns p(n), the number of distinct ways to write the
non-negative integer n as a sum of positive integers where order does not
matter, as an arbitrary-precision integer. Partition(0) == 1.

It uses Euler's pentagonal number theorem recurrence, which is exact.

funcs.name: PellFundamental
funcs.doc:
PellFundamental returns the fundamental solution (x, y) of the Pell equation
x^2 - n*y^2 = 1 as arbitrary-precision integers, derived from the convergents
of the periodic continued-fraction expansion of sqrt(n). If the period length
L is even, the convergent with index L-1 is the fundamental solution;
otherwise the convergent with index 2L-1 is used. The convergents are built
with the exact h/k recurrence, so the result never overflows. It panics if n
is a perfect square, for which the equation has no nontrivial solution.

funcs.name: Permutations
funcs.doc:
Permutations returns the number of ordered k-permutations of n objects,
nPr = n! / (n-k)!, as an arbitrary-precision integer. It returns 0 when k < 0
or k > n. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: PollardBrentU64
funcs.doc:
PollardBrentU64 returns a non-trivial factor of the composite integer n using
Brent's improved cycle-finding variant of Pollard's rho algorithm. The
returned factor is strictly between 1 and n but is not necessarily prime.

If n is prime it returns n itself, and for an even n it returns 2 (so
PollardBrentU64(2) == 2 consistently reports the prime). Values below 2 are
returned unchanged.

The iteration f(x) = x^2 + c mod n runs entirely in the Montgomery domain via
[Montgomery.MulMont] from fastmod.go, and the differences |x - y| are
accumulated into a running product whose greatest common divisor with n is
taken only once per batch, so a hardware division is avoided on the hot path.
The search restarts with the next constant c on failure. The seed schedule is
fixed (c = 1, 2, 3, ... with x0 = 2), making the output reproducible.

funcs.name: PollardRhoBig
funcs.doc:
PollardRhoBig returns a non-trivial factor of the composite integer n using
Brent's variant of Pollard's rho over [math/big], with the greatest common
divisor taken periodically over a batched product of differences. The sign of
n is ignored and the returned factor lies strictly between 1 and |n|.

If |n| is (probably) prime it returns a copy of |n|; for an even |n| it
returns 2. The polynomial constant follows the fixed schedule c = 1, 2, 3,
... with start point x0 = 2, so the result is deterministic. Primality is
checked with [IsProbablePrimeBig].

funcs.name: PrevPrime
funcs.signature: func PrevPrime(n int64) (prime int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
PrevPrime returns the greatest prime strictly less than n together with
ok == true. When no prime is smaller than n (that is, n <= 2) it returns
(0, false). It is the int64 counterpart of [NextPrime] and delegates to
[PrevPrimeU64].

funcs.name: PrevPrimeBig
funcs.doc:
PrevPrimeBig returns the greatest prime strictly less than n together with
ok == true, using [IsProbablePrimeBig] and a descending mod-30 wheel. When no
prime is smaller than n (that is, n <= 2) it returns (nil, false). The
returned value is a fresh *big.Int; n is not modified.

funcs.name: PrevPrimeU64
funcs.doc:
PrevPrimeU64 returns the greatest prime strictly less than n together with
ok == true. When no prime is smaller than n (that is, n <= 2) it returns
(0, false).

This fills the "previous prime" gap (SymPy's prevprime) that the package
otherwise lacked entirely. Candidate stepping uses the descending mod-30
wheel for speed.

funcs.name: PrimePi
funcs.doc: PrimePi returns π(n), the number of primes p with p <= n.

funcs.name: PrimePiRange
funcs.doc:
PrimePiRange counts the primes p with lo <= p <= hi without materializing
them, sieving one segment at a time and tallying survivors. Its memory use is
bounded by a single window regardless of hi, making it suitable for a
prime-counting function over huge ranges. It returns 0 when hi < 2 or
lo > hi.

funcs.name: PrimesInRange
funcs.doc:
PrimesInRange returns all primes in the half-open interval [lo, hi) in
ascending order. It is a convenience wrapper over [SegmentedSieve] with an
exclusive upper bound. It returns nil when the interval contains no primes.

funcs.name: PrimesUpTo
funcs.doc:
PrimesUpTo returns all primes p with p <= n in ascending order, computed with
the sieve of Eratosthenes. It returns nil for n < 2.

funcs.name: PrimitiveRoot
funcs.doc:
PrimitiveRoot returns the smallest primitive root modulo m, together with
ok == true. It returns (0, false) when no primitive root exists; primitive
roots exist exactly for m in {1, 2, 4}, m = p^k with p an odd prime, and
m = 2·p^k. By convention PrimitiveRoot(1) == (0, true). m must be positive.

Existence is decided structurally, then candidates g are tested with the fast
prime-quotient criterion of [IsPrimitiveRoot]: g is a primitive root iff
g^(λ(m)/q) ≠ 1 (mod m) for every prime q dividing λ(m). Because a primitive
root exists only when the group is cyclic (λ(m) = φ(m)), this reuses
[FactorizeU64] of φ(m) and never scans all divisors.

funcs.name: PrimitiveRoots
funcs.doc:
PrimitiveRoots returns all primitive roots modulo m in ascending order, or nil
when none exist. When the group is cyclic there are exactly φ(φ(m)) of them.

It finds the smallest primitive root g with [PrimitiveRoot] and generates the
rest as the powers g^k for which gcd(k, φ(m)) = 1, k ranging over [1, φ(m)];
these powers are precisely the generators of the cyclic group. The results are
sorted before being returned so the slice is deterministic and ascending.

funcs.name: Radical
funcs.doc:
Radical returns the radical of n: the product of its distinct prime factors.
The sign of n is ignored. Radical(0) == 0 and Radical(1) == 1.

funcs.name: RatFromContinuedFraction
funcs.doc:
RatFromContinuedFraction reconstructs the exact big.Rat value of the
partial-quotient slice cf, i.e. the value of [cf[0]; cf[1], ..., cf[n-1]]. It
evaluates the expansion from the innermost quotient outward using exact
rational arithmetic. An empty cf yields 0.

funcs.name: SegmentedSieve
funcs.doc:
SegmentedSieve returns all primes p with lo <= p <= hi in ascending order.

It uses a segmented sieve of Eratosthenes: the base primes up to sqrt(hi) are
computed once and memoized in a package-level cache, then each fixed-size
window of the interval is sieved into a reusable mod-30 wheel array holding
only 8 flags per 30 integers. Because it never allocates an array spanning
[0, hi], it handles ranges whose upper bound is far beyond what a single n+1
boolean sieve could fit in memory. lo and hi are treated as non-negative;
the result is deterministic. It returns nil when the interval contains no
primes, including when hi < 2 or lo > hi.

funcs.name: SqrtContinuedFraction
funcs.doc:
SqrtContinuedFraction returns the continued-fraction expansion of the square
root of n for a positive n. The result is the pair (a0, period), where
a0 = floor(sqrt(n)) is the leading term and period is the repeating block, so
that sqrt(n) = [a0; period[0], period[1], ..., a0+period repeated]. It uses
the standard integer (m, d, a) recurrence

	m_{i+1} = d_i*a_i - m_i,
	d_{i+1} = (n - m_{i+1}^2) / d_i,
	a_{i+1} = floor((a0 + m_{i+1}) / d_{i+1}),

which is exact with no floating-point rounding; the period terminates when a
term equal to 2*a0 is reached. If n is a perfect square, sqrt(n) is the
integer a0 and period is nil.

funcs.name: SqrtMod
funcs.signature: func SqrtMod(a, p int64) (x int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
SqrtMod returns a square root x in [0, p) of a modulo the prime p, i.e. an x
with x*x ≡ a (mod p), together with ok == true. When a is a quadratic
non-residue modulo p (no square root exists) it returns (0, false).

The residue is validated with [LegendreSymbol]. For p ≡ 3 (mod 4) it uses the
direct closed form x = a^((p+1)/4), avoiding the Tonelli-Shanks inner loop;
for the remaining odd primes it runs the full Tonelli-Shanks algorithm. All
powering is performed with [ModPow] so no intermediate value overflows. The
special cases a ≡ 0 (returning (0, true)) and p == 2 are handled directly.

p must be an odd prime or 2; a is reduced modulo p before use. When the two
roots x and p-x both exist the smaller one is returned, so the result is
deterministic. The behavior is undefined when p is not prime.

funcs.name: SqrtModBig
funcs.doc:
SqrtModBig returns a square root of a modulo the prime p using the
Tonelli-Shanks algorithm over math/big, suitable for primes far larger than
int64. It returns (nil, false) when a is a quadratic non-residue modulo p.

Residue membership is decided with the Euler criterion a^((p-1)/2) ≡ 1
(mod p), evaluated with [big.Int.Exp] (the same square-and-multiply used by
[JacobiSymbol]-style checks). For p ≡ 3 (mod 4) the direct form
a^((p+1)/4) is used. The cases a ≡ 0 (returning 0) and p == 2 are handled
directly. a is reduced modulo p, and the smaller of the two roots is
returned, making the result deterministic. p must be an odd prime or 2.

funcs.name: SqrtModPrimePower
funcs.signature: func SqrtModPrimePower(a, p int64, k int) (x int64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
SqrtModPrimePower returns a square root x in [0, p^k) of a modulo the prime
power p^k, together with ok == true, or (0, false) when a is a non-residue.
A root modulo p is first obtained with [SqrtMod] and then lifted to modulus
p^k by Hensel lifting (Newton iteration on f(x) = x^2 - a). The p == 2 branch
and the non-unit cases (where p divides a) are handled by the same complete
prime-power solver used by [AllSqrtModComposite], and the smallest root is
returned for determinism.

p must be prime and k must be >= 1. The behavior is undefined when p is not
prime, and it panics when k < 1.

funcs.name: StirlingSecond
funcs.doc:
StirlingSecond returns the Stirling number of the second kind S(n, k): the
number of ways to partition a set of n labeled elements into k non-empty
unlabeled subsets, as an arbitrary-precision integer. n and k must be
non-negative. S(0, 0) == 1 and S(n, 0) == 0 for n > 0.

funcs.name: SubModU64
funcs.signature: func SubModU64(a, b, m uint64) uint64
funcs.doc:
SubModU64 returns (a - b) mod m without underflowing. m must be positive;
SubModU64 panics if m == 0. The result lies in [0, m).

funcs.name: SumDivisors
funcs.doc:
SumDivisors returns σ(n), the sum of all positive divisors of n
(including 1 and n itself). The sign of n is ignored. SumDivisors(0) == 0.

funcs.name: TotientSieve
funcs.doc:
TotientSieve returns a slice phi of length n+1 where phi[i] == φ(i), Euler's
totient of i, for every 0 <= i <= n. By convention phi[0] == 0 and phi[1] == 1.

It computes all values in a single sieve pass, which is far faster than
calling [EulerPhi] on each i independently when the whole table is needed.
n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Tribonacci
funcs.doc:
Tribonacci returns the n-th Tribonacci number Tₙ, with T₀ = 0, T₁ = 1,
T₂ = 1 and Tₙ = Tₙ₋₁ + Tₙ₋₂ + Tₙ₋₃, as an arbitrary-precision integer. n must
be non-negative.

examples.name: Bernoulli
Binomial
CRT
CatalanNumber
Divisors
EulerPhi
ExtendedGCD
Factorial
Factorize
Fibonacci
GCD
IsPerfect
IsPrime
LCM
MobiusMu
ModInverse
ModPow
Partition
PrimePi
PrimesUpTo
SumDivisors

packages.name: numint
packages.synopsis: Package numint implements numerical integration (quadrature) of functions of one and several variables using only the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package numint implements numerical integration (quadrature) of functions
of one and several variables using only the Go standard library.

The package collects the classical fixed-order Newton-Cotes rules
(trapezoid, midpoint, Simpson 1/3, Simpson 3/8, Boole, Weddle), their
composite forms, and higher-accuracy schemes: Romberg extrapolation,
adaptive Simpson and adaptive Gauss-Kronrod, fixed Gauss-Legendre rules
from 2 to 10 points plus an n-point routine for arbitrary order,
Gauss-Lobatto rules, Clenshaw-Curtis and Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature, and a
tanh-sinh (double-exponential) rule for integrands with endpoint
singularities. Sample-based helpers integrate tabulated data, including
cumulative integrals, and tensor-product routines extend the fixed rules to
two and three dimensions. A small deterministic Monte-Carlo family covers
higher-dimensional volumes.

All routines are deterministic and depend only on the standard library.

types.name: Field2
types.signature: type Field2 func(x, y float64) (u, v float64)
types.doc: Field2 is a two-dimensional vector field (x, y) -> (u, v).

types.name: Field3
types.signature: type Field3 func(x, y, z float64) (u, v, w float64)
types.doc: Field3 is a three-dimensional vector field (x, y, z) -> (u, v, w).

types.name: Func
types.signature: type Func func(x float64) float64
types.doc: Func is a real-valued function of a single real variable.

types.name: Func2
types.signature: type Func2 func(x, y float64) float64
types.doc: Func2 is a real-valued function of two real variables.

types.name: Func3
types.signature: type Func3 func(x, y, z float64) float64
types.doc: Func3 is a real-valued function of three real variables.

types.name: FuncND
types.signature: type FuncND func(x []float64) float64
types.doc: FuncND is a real-valued function of a vector of real variables.

types.name: Path2
types.signature: type Path2 func(t float64) (x, y float64)
types.doc: Path2 is a parametric plane curve t -> (x(t), y(t)).

types.name: Path3
types.signature: type Path3 func(t float64) (x, y, z float64)
types.doc: Path3 is a parametric space curve t -> (x(t), y(t), z(t)).

types.name: QuadResult
types.doc:
QuadResult bundles the estimated value of an integral with an estimate of
the absolute error and the number of integrand evaluations performed.

funcs.name: AdaptiveGaussKronrod
funcs.signature: func AdaptiveGaussKronrod(f Func, a, b, tol float64) QuadResult
funcs.doc:
AdaptiveGaussKronrod approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using
globally adaptive 15-point Gauss-Kronrod quadrature with an absolute
tolerance tol. The returned QuadResult carries the value, an error estimate,
and the number of integrand evaluations.

funcs.name: MonteCarlo2DWithError
funcs.signature: func MonteCarlo2DWithError(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, n int, seed int64) QuadResult
funcs.doc:
MonteCarlo2DWithError estimates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] together with a standard-error estimate.

funcs.name: MonteCarloWithError
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloWithError(f Func, a, b float64, n int, seed int64) QuadResult
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloWithError estimates the integral of f over [a, b] together with a
standard-error estimate and the evaluation count.

funcs.name: AdaptiveSimpson
funcs.signature: func AdaptiveSimpson(f Func, a, b, tol float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AdaptiveSimpson approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using adaptive
Simpson quadrature with an absolute tolerance tol.

funcs.name: AdaptiveSimpsonMaxDepth
funcs.signature: func AdaptiveSimpsonMaxDepth(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxDepth int) float64
funcs.doc:
AdaptiveSimpsonMaxDepth approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using
adaptive Simpson quadrature with an absolute tolerance tol and a bound
maxDepth on the recursion depth.

funcs.name: AdaptiveTrapezoid
funcs.signature: func AdaptiveTrapezoid(f Func, a, b, tol float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AdaptiveTrapezoid approximates the integral of f over [a, b] by recursively
bisecting subintervals until the trapezoid estimate on each meets the
requested absolute tolerance tol.

funcs.name: ArcLength2D
funcs.signature: func ArcLength2D(p Path2, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
ArcLength2D approximates the arc length of the plane curve p over the
parameter interval [a, b] using n Simpson panels.

funcs.name: ArcLength3D
funcs.signature: func ArcLength3D(p Path3, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
ArcLength3D approximates the arc length of the space curve p over the
parameter interval [a, b] using n Simpson panels.

funcs.name: ArcLengthPolyline2D
funcs.doc:
ArcLengthPolyline2D returns the total length of the polyline through the
given vertices.

funcs.name: AreaBetween
funcs.signature: func AreaBetween(ylo, yhi Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
AreaBetween returns the area of the region enclosed between the curves
y = ylo(x) and y = yhi(x) for x in [a, b].

funcs.name: Boole
funcs.signature: func Boole(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Boole approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the composite Boole
rule; n is the number of panels and is rounded up to a multiple of four.

funcs.name: BooleRule
funcs.signature: func BooleRule(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc: BooleRule applies the five-point Boole rule to f over [a, b].

funcs.name: CentroidRegion
funcs.signature: func CentroidRegion(ylo, yhi Func, a, b float64, n int) (xbar, ybar float64)
funcs.doc:
CentroidRegion returns the centroid (xbar, ybar) of the region between the
curves y = ylo(x) and y = yhi(x) over [a, b].

funcs.name: Circulation2D
funcs.signature: func Circulation2D(field Field2, p Path2, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Circulation2D approximates the circulation of the vector field around the
closed plane curve p, i.e. the line integral of F dot dr over [a, b].

funcs.name: ClenshawCurtis
funcs.signature: func ClenshawCurtis(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
ClenshawCurtis approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using
Clenshaw-Curtis quadrature with n+1 Chebyshev extreme points; n must be a
positive even number and is rounded up when odd.

funcs.name: CompositeGaussLegendre
funcs.signature: func CompositeGaussLegendre(f Func, a, b float64, panels, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
CompositeGaussLegendre approximates the integral of f over [a, b] by
splitting the interval into panels equal subintervals and applying the
n-point Gauss-Legendre rule to each.

funcs.name: CumulativeSimpson
funcs.doc:
CumulativeSimpson returns the cumulative integral of tabulated data
(xs, ys). The returned slice has the same length as xs and its first element
is zero. Interior points use a local (possibly non-uniform) Simpson rule;
the first panel uses the trapezoid rule.

funcs.name: CumulativeTrapezoid
funcs.doc:
CumulativeTrapezoid returns the cumulative integral of tabulated data
(xs, ys) evaluated at each xs. The returned slice has the same length as xs;
its first element is zero and element i holds the integral from xs[0] to
xs[i].

funcs.name: CumulativeTrapezoidUniform
funcs.doc:
CumulativeTrapezoidUniform returns the cumulative integral of equally spaced
samples ys with spacing h. The returned slice has the same length as ys and
its first element is zero.

funcs.name: DoubleAverage
funcs.signature: func DoubleAverage(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleAverage returns the mean value of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by], i.e. its double integral divided by the area.

funcs.name: DoubleBoole
funcs.signature: func DoubleBoole(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleBoole approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the composite Boole rule.

funcs.name: DoubleExponential
funcs.signature: func DoubleExponential(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleExponential is a synonym for TanhSinh, using the alternative name for
the double-exponential quadrature rule.

funcs.name: DoubleGaussLegendre
funcs.signature: func DoubleGaussLegendre(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleGaussLegendre approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using an n-by-n tensor Gauss-Legendre rule.

funcs.name: DoubleIntegral
funcs.signature: func DoubleIntegral(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleIntegral approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using a fine composite Simpson rule with sensible
default resolution.

funcs.name: DoubleIntegralRegion
funcs.signature: func DoubleIntegralRegion(f Func2, a, b float64, ylo, yhi Func, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleIntegralRegion approximates the integral of f over the region
{ (x, y) : a <= x <= b, ylo(x) <= y <= yhi(x) } using the composite Simpson
rule in the outer variable and an ny-panel inner Simpson rule.

funcs.name: DoubleIntegralRegionGL
funcs.signature: func DoubleIntegralRegionGL(f Func2, a, b float64, ylo, yhi Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleIntegralRegionGL approximates the integral of f over the region
{ (x, y) : a <= x <= b, ylo(x) <= y <= yhi(x) } using an n-point
Gauss-Legendre rule in each direction.

funcs.name: DoubleMidpoint
funcs.signature: func DoubleMidpoint(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleMidpoint approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the composite midpoint rule.

funcs.name: DoubleSimpson
funcs.signature: func DoubleSimpson(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleSimpson approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the composite Simpson rule.

funcs.name: DoubleTrapezoid
funcs.signature: func DoubleTrapezoid(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
DoubleTrapezoid approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the composite trapezoidal rule with nx and ny
subintervals along each axis.

funcs.name: ExpectationNormal
funcs.signature: func ExpectationNormal(f Func, mean, stddev float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
ExpectationNormal approximates E[f(X)] for a normal random variable X with
the given mean and standard deviation, using n-point Gauss-Hermite
quadrature.

funcs.name: Flux2D
funcs.signature: func Flux2D(field Field2, p Path2, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Flux2D approximates the outward flux of the vector field across the plane
curve p over [a, b], i.e. the integral of F dot n ds for a counterclockwise
parametrization.

funcs.name: GaussChebyshev
funcs.signature: func GaussChebyshev(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshev approximates the weighted integral of f over [-1, 1] with the
Chebyshev weight 1/sqrt(1-x^2), that is the integral of f(x)/sqrt(1-x^2),
using the n-point Gauss-Chebyshev rule of the first kind.

funcs.name: GaussHermite
funcs.signature: func GaussHermite(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussHermite approximates the integral of exp(-x^2) * f(x) over
(-inf, inf) using n-point Gauss-Hermite quadrature.

funcs.name: GaussHermiteNodes
funcs.doc:
GaussHermiteNodes returns the n nodes and weights of Gauss-Hermite
quadrature for the weight exp(-x^2) on (-inf, inf).

funcs.name: GaussKronrod15
funcs.signature: func GaussKronrod15(f Func, a, b float64) (value, errEst float64)
funcs.doc:
GaussKronrod15 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the 15-point
Gauss-Kronrod rule. It returns the Kronrod estimate together with a
conservative estimate of the absolute error derived from the difference
between the Kronrod and embedded 7-point Gauss estimates.

funcs.name: GaussKronrod15Nodes
funcs.doc:
GaussKronrod15Nodes returns the positive-half abscissae of the 15-point
Gauss-Kronrod rule on [-1, 1], the corresponding Kronrod weights, and the
four embedded 7-point Gauss weights. The full rule is symmetric about zero.

funcs.name: GaussLaguerre
funcs.signature: func GaussLaguerre(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLaguerre approximates the integral of exp(-x) * f(x) over (0, inf)
using n-point Gauss-Laguerre quadrature.

funcs.name: GaussLaguerreGen
funcs.signature: func GaussLaguerreGen(f Func, alpha float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLaguerreGen approximates the integral of x^alpha * exp(-x) * f(x) over
(0, inf) using n-point generalized Gauss-Laguerre quadrature.

funcs.name: GaussLaguerreGenNodes
funcs.signature: func GaussLaguerreGenNodes(n int, alpha float64) (nodes, weights []float64)
funcs.doc:
GaussLaguerreGenNodes returns the n nodes and weights of generalized
Gauss-Laguerre quadrature for the weight x^alpha * exp(-x) on (0, inf).

funcs.name: GaussLaguerreNodes
funcs.doc:
GaussLaguerreNodes returns the n nodes and weights of Gauss-Laguerre
quadrature for the weight exp(-x) on (0, inf).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre10
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre10(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre10 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
ten-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 19).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre2
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre2(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre2 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
two-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 3).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre2D
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre2D(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre2D approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the tensor product of the n-point Gauss-Legendre
rule in each direction.

funcs.name: GaussLegendre3
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre3(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre3 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
three-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 5).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre3D
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre3D(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre3D approximates the triple integral of f over the box
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] x [az, bz] using the tensor product of the n-point
Gauss-Legendre rule in each direction.

funcs.name: GaussLegendre4
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre4(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre4 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
four-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 7).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre5
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre5(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre5 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
five-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 9).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre6
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre6(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre6 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
six-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 11).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre7
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre7(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre7 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
seven-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 13).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre8
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre8(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre8 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
eight-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 15).

funcs.name: GaussLegendre9
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendre9(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre9 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
nine-point Gauss-Legendre rule (exact for polynomials up to degree 17).

funcs.name: GaussLegendreN
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendreN(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendreN approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the n-point
Gauss-Legendre rule for any n >= 1.

funcs.name: GaussLegendreNodes
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendreNodes returns the abscissae and weights of the n-point
Gauss-Legendre rule on the reference interval [-1, 1]. Tabulated values are
used for 2 <= n <= 10; higher orders are computed by Newton iteration on the
Legendre polynomials.

funcs.name: GaussLobatto3
funcs.signature: func GaussLobatto3(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLobatto3 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
three-point Gauss-Lobatto rule (Simpson's rule), which includes both
endpoints.

funcs.name: GaussLobatto4
funcs.signature: func GaussLobatto4(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLobatto4 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
four-point Gauss-Lobatto rule, which includes both endpoints and the
interior nodes at +/- 1/sqrt(5).

funcs.name: GaussLobatto5
funcs.signature: func GaussLobatto5(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLobatto5 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the
five-point Gauss-Lobatto rule, which includes both endpoints, the centre,
and the interior nodes at +/- sqrt(3/7).

funcs.name: HaltonPoint
funcs.doc:
HaltonPoint returns the Halton point at the given index using one base per
coordinate.

funcs.name: HaltonPrimes
funcs.doc:
HaltonPrimes returns the first k prime numbers, suitable as Halton bases for
a k-dimensional sequence.

funcs.name: HaltonSequence
funcs.doc:
HaltonSequence returns count Halton points (indices 1..count) using the given
per-coordinate bases.

funcs.name: Integrate
funcs.signature: func Integrate(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Integrate approximates the integral of f over [a, b] to roughly full
double-precision accuracy using globally adaptive Gauss-Kronrod quadrature.

funcs.name: Integrate2D
funcs.signature: func Integrate2D(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Integrate2D approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] to high accuracy using an 8-point Gauss-Legendre tensor
product refined over a modest grid of panels.

funcs.name: IntegrateAnnulus
funcs.signature: func IntegrateAnnulus(f Func2, cx, cy, rin, rout float64, nr, nt int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateAnnulus approximates the integral of f over the annulus centered at
(cx, cy) with inner radius rin and outer radius rout.

funcs.name: IntegrateBall
funcs.signature: func IntegrateBall(f Func3, cx, cy, cz, r float64, nr, nphi, ntheta int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateBall approximates the volume integral of f over the solid ball of
radius r centered at (cx, cy, cz) using spherical coordinates.

funcs.name: IntegrateCylinder
funcs.signature: func IntegrateCylinder(f Func3, cx, cy, r, z0, z1 float64, nr, ntheta, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateCylinder approximates the volume integral of f over the cylinder of
radius r centered on the axis x = cx, y = cy and spanning z in [z0, z1],
using cylindrical coordinates.

funcs.name: IntegrateDisk
funcs.signature: func IntegrateDisk(f Func2, cx, cy, r float64, nr, nt int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateDisk approximates the integral of f over the disk of radius r
centered at (cx, cy), using polar coordinates with nr radial and nt angular
subintervals.

funcs.name: IntegrateExpWeighted
funcs.signature: func IntegrateExpWeighted(f Func, rate float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateExpWeighted approximates the integral of exp(-rate*x) * f(x) over
(0, inf) using n-point Gauss-Laguerre quadrature; rate must be positive.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussianWeighted
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussianWeighted(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussianWeighted approximates the integral of exp(-x^2/2) * f(x)
over (-inf, inf) using n-point Gauss-Hermite quadrature.

funcs.name: IntegrateInfinite
funcs.signature: func IntegrateInfinite(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateInfinite approximates the integral of f over the whole real line
using the substitution x = t/(1-t^2) with n-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature.

funcs.name: IntegrateLowerInfinite
funcs.signature: func IntegrateLowerInfinite(f Func, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateLowerInfinite approximates the integral of f over (-inf, b] using
the substitution x = b - u/(1-u) with n-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature.

funcs.name: IntegrateND
funcs.signature: func IntegrateND(f FuncND, lower, upper []float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateND approximates the integral of f over the axis-aligned box defined
by the lower and upper bounds using an n-point Gauss-Legendre tensor product
in each dimension. The two bound slices must have equal length equal to the
dimensionality.

funcs.name: IntegratePolar
funcs.signature: func IntegratePolar(g Func2, r0, r1, t0, t1 float64, nr, nt int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegratePolar approximates the integral of g(r, theta) * r over the polar
rectangle r in [r0, r1], theta in [t0, t1]; the Jacobian factor r is applied
automatically so g is the integrand expressed in polar coordinates.

funcs.name: IntegrateSingularEndpoint
funcs.signature: func IntegrateSingularEndpoint(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateSingularEndpoint approximates the integral of f over [a, b] when f
has an integrable singularity at the lower endpoint a, using the
substitution x = a + t^2 to tame the singularity together with an n-point
Gauss-Legendre rule whose nodes never touch the endpoint.

funcs.name: IntegrateSphereSurface
funcs.signature: func IntegrateSphereSurface(f Func3, cx, cy, cz, r float64, nphi, ntheta int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateSphereSurface approximates the surface integral of f over the
sphere of radius r centered at (cx, cy, cz), with nphi polar and ntheta
azimuthal subintervals.

funcs.name: IntegrateTriangle
funcs.signature: func IntegrateTriangle(f Func2, x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3 float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateTriangle approximates the integral of f over the triangle with the
given vertices using an n-point Gauss-Legendre rule under a Duffy transform.

funcs.name: IntegrateUpperInfinite
funcs.signature: func IntegrateUpperInfinite(f Func, a float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateUpperInfinite approximates the integral of f over [a, inf) using the
substitution x = a + t/(1-t) with n-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature.

funcs.name: LaplaceTransform
funcs.signature: func LaplaceTransform(f Func, s float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
LaplaceTransform approximates the Laplace transform of f evaluated at s,
that is the integral of exp(-s*t) * f(t) over (0, inf), using n-point
Gauss-Laguerre quadrature; s must be positive.

funcs.name: LineIntegralPolyline2D
funcs.signature: func LineIntegralPolyline2D(f Func2, xs, ys []float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LineIntegralPolyline2D approximates the scalar line integral of f along the
polyline through the given vertices, using the midpoint of each segment.

funcs.name: LineIntegralScalar2D
funcs.signature: func LineIntegralScalar2D(f Func2, p Path2, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
LineIntegralScalar2D approximates the scalar line integral of f along the
plane curve p over [a, b], i.e. the integral of f ds.

funcs.name: LineIntegralScalar3D
funcs.signature: func LineIntegralScalar3D(f Func3, p Path3, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
LineIntegralScalar3D approximates the scalar line integral of f along the
space curve p over [a, b], i.e. the integral of f ds.

funcs.name: LineIntegralVector2D
funcs.signature: func LineIntegralVector2D(field Field2, p Path2, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
LineIntegralVector2D approximates the line integral of the vector field along
the plane curve p over [a, b], i.e. the work integral of F dot dr.

funcs.name: LineIntegralVector3D
funcs.signature: func LineIntegralVector3D(field Field3, p Path3, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
LineIntegralVector3D approximates the line integral of the vector field along
the space curve p over [a, b], i.e. the work integral of F dot dr.

funcs.name: Midpoint
funcs.signature: func Midpoint(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Midpoint approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the composite
midpoint rule with n panels.

funcs.name: Midpoint2D
funcs.signature: func Midpoint2D(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
Midpoint2D approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the composite midpoint rule with nx panels in x
and ny panels in y.

funcs.name: MidpointRule
funcs.signature: func MidpointRule(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc: MidpointRule applies the one-point open midpoint rule to f over [a, b].

funcs.name: MidpointSamples
funcs.doc:
MidpointSamples integrates samples ys taken at the midpoints of n panels of
width h using the composite midpoint rule.

funcs.name: MonteCarlo
funcs.signature: func MonteCarlo(f Func, a, b float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarlo approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using n uniform random
samples drawn from a generator seeded with seed, making the result
deterministic for a given seed.

funcs.name: MonteCarlo2D
funcs.signature: func MonteCarlo2D(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarlo2D approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using n uniform random samples from a generator seeded
with seed.

funcs.name: MonteCarlo3D
funcs.signature: func MonteCarlo3D(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarlo3D estimates the triple integral of f over the box
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] x [az, bz] by averaging n uniform random samples drawn
from the given seed.

funcs.name: MonteCarloAntithetic
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloAntithetic(f Func, a, b float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloAntithetic estimates the integral of f over [a, b] using n
antithetic sample pairs, which reduces variance for monotone integrands.

funcs.name: MonteCarloBall
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloBall(f Func3, cx, cy, cz, r float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloBall estimates the integral of f over the solid ball of radius r
centered at (cx, cy, cz) by averaging n uniform samples over the ball.

funcs.name: MonteCarloDisk
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloDisk(f Func2, cx, cy, r float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloDisk estimates the integral of f over the disk of radius r centered
at (cx, cy) by averaging n samples drawn uniformly over the disk.

funcs.name: MonteCarloImportance
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloImportance(f Func, sample func(rng *rand.Rand) float64, pdf Func, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloImportance estimates the integral of f using importance sampling:
sample draws a point from a density proportional to pdf, and the estimator
averages f(x)/pdf(x) over n samples.

funcs.name: MonteCarloND
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloND(f FuncND, lower, upper []float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloND approximates the integral of f over the axis-aligned box
defined by lower and upper using n uniform random samples from a generator
seeded with seed. The two bound slices must have equal length.

funcs.name: MonteCarloRegion
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloRegion(f Func2, a, b float64, ylo, yhi Func, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloRegion estimates the integral of f over the region
{ (x, y) : a <= x <= b, ylo(x) <= y <= yhi(x) } using n random samples.

funcs.name: MonteCarloStratified
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloStratified(f Func, a, b float64, strata, perStratum int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloStratified estimates the integral of f over [a, b] using stratified
sampling with the given number of equal strata and samples per stratum.

funcs.name: MonteCarloVolume
funcs.signature: func MonteCarloVolume(pred func(x []float64) bool, lower, upper []float64, n int, seed int64) float64
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloVolume estimates the volume of the subset of the box [lower, upper]
for which pred reports true, by sampling n uniform random points.

funcs.name: QuasiMonteCarlo
funcs.signature: func QuasiMonteCarlo(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
QuasiMonteCarlo estimates the integral of f over [a, b] using n points of the
base-2 Halton (van der Corput) sequence.

funcs.name: QuasiMonteCarlo2D
funcs.signature: func QuasiMonteCarlo2D(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
QuasiMonteCarlo2D estimates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using n points of the Halton sequence with bases 2 and 3.

funcs.name: QuasiMonteCarloND
funcs.signature: func QuasiMonteCarloND(f FuncND, lower, upper []float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
QuasiMonteCarloND estimates the integral of f over the box [lower, upper]
using n Halton points whose bases are the first len(lower) primes.

funcs.name: RichardsonExtrapolate
funcs.signature: func RichardsonExtrapolate(coarse, fine, order float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RichardsonExtrapolate combines a coarse and a fine estimate of the same
quantity, computed with step sizes differing by a factor of two, into a
higher-order estimate, given the leading error order p. It returns
(2^p * fine - coarse) / (2^p - 1).

funcs.name: Romberg
funcs.signature: func Romberg(f Func, a, b float64, maxLevel int) float64
funcs.doc:
Romberg approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using Romberg
integration with maxLevel levels of Richardson extrapolation on the
composite trapezoid rule. A typical value for maxLevel is 5 to 8.

funcs.name: RombergTable
funcs.signature: func RombergTable(f Func, a, b float64, maxLevel int) (float64, [][]float64)
funcs.doc:
RombergTable performs Romberg integration of f over [a, b] with maxLevel
levels and returns the best estimate (the last diagonal entry) together with
the full lower-triangular Romberg table, indexed table[i][j].

funcs.name: RombergTol
funcs.signature: func RombergTol(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxLevel int) float64
funcs.doc:
RombergTol approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using Romberg
integration, stopping once two successive diagonal estimates differ by less
than tol in absolute value or maxLevel levels have been used. It returns the
best available estimate.

funcs.name: Simpson
funcs.signature: func Simpson(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Simpson approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the composite
Simpson 1/3 rule; n is the number of panels and is rounded up to an even
number when odd.

funcs.name: Simpson2D
funcs.signature: func Simpson2D(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
Simpson2D approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the composite Simpson rule with nx panels in x and
ny panels in y (each rounded up to an even number).

funcs.name: Simpson38
funcs.signature: func Simpson38(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Simpson38 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the composite
Simpson 3/8 rule; n is the number of panels and is rounded up to a multiple
of three when necessary.

funcs.name: Simpson38Rule
funcs.signature: func Simpson38Rule(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc: Simpson38Rule applies the four-point Simpson 3/8 rule to f over [a, b].

funcs.name: Simpson3D
funcs.signature: func Simpson3D(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
Simpson3D approximates the triple integral of f over the box
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] x [az, bz] using the composite Simpson rule with the
given number of panels in each direction (each rounded up to an even number).

funcs.name: SimpsonRule
funcs.signature: func SimpsonRule(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc: SimpsonRule applies the three-point Simpson 1/3 rule to f over [a, b].

funcs.name: SimpsonSamples
funcs.doc:
SimpsonSamples integrates equally spaced samples ys with spacing h using the
composite Simpson 1/3 rule. It requires an odd number of samples (an even
number of panels); when given an even number of samples it integrates the
leading odd-length block with Simpson and closes the final panel with the
trapezoid rule.

funcs.name: TanhSinh
funcs.signature: func TanhSinh(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
TanhSinh approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the tanh-sinh
(double-exponential) rule with 2n+1 abscissae per level. The rule places
nodes that cluster near the endpoints, making it well suited to integrands
with integrable endpoint singularities. Non-finite integrand samples in the
negligible tail are skipped.

funcs.name: TanhSinhTol
funcs.signature: func TanhSinhTol(f Func, a, b, tol float64) float64
funcs.doc:
TanhSinhTol approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the tanh-sinh
rule, refining the abscissa spacing until two successive level estimates
differ by less than tol in absolute value or a level cap is reached.

funcs.name: Trapezoid
funcs.signature: func Trapezoid(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Trapezoid approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the composite
trapezoid rule with n panels.

funcs.name: Trapezoid2D
funcs.signature: func Trapezoid2D(f Func2, ax, bx, ay, by float64, nx, ny int) float64
funcs.doc:
Trapezoid2D approximates the double integral of f over the rectangle
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] using the composite trapezoid rule with nx panels in x
and ny panels in y.

funcs.name: Trapezoid3D
funcs.signature: func Trapezoid3D(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
Trapezoid3D approximates the triple integral of f over the box
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] x [az, bz] using the composite trapezoid rule with the
given number of panels in each direction.

funcs.name: TrapezoidData
funcs.doc:
TrapezoidData integrates tabulated data (xs, ys) with arbitrary, not
necessarily uniform, spacing using the trapezoid rule. xs must be sorted and
the same length as ys.

funcs.name: TrapezoidRule
funcs.signature: func TrapezoidRule(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc: TrapezoidRule applies the two-point trapezoid rule to f over [a, b].

funcs.name: TrapezoidSamples
funcs.doc:
TrapezoidSamples integrates equally spaced samples ys with spacing h using
the composite trapezoid rule.

funcs.name: TripleAverage
funcs.signature: func TripleAverage(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleAverage returns the mean value of f over the box, i.e. its triple
integral divided by the volume.

funcs.name: TripleGaussLegendre
funcs.signature: func TripleGaussLegendre(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleGaussLegendre approximates the triple integral of f over the box using
an n-by-n-by-n tensor Gauss-Legendre rule.

funcs.name: TripleIntegral
funcs.signature: func TripleIntegral(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleIntegral approximates the triple integral of f over the box using a
composite Simpson rule with sensible default resolution.

funcs.name: TripleMidpoint
funcs.signature: func TripleMidpoint(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleMidpoint approximates the triple integral of f over the box using the
composite midpoint rule.

funcs.name: TripleSimpson
funcs.signature: func TripleSimpson(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleSimpson approximates the triple integral of f over the box using the
composite Simpson rule.

funcs.name: TripleTrapezoid
funcs.signature: func TripleTrapezoid(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleTrapezoid approximates the triple integral of f over the box
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] x [az, bz] using the composite trapezoidal rule.

funcs.name: VanDerCorput
funcs.doc:
VanDerCorput returns the radical-inverse (van der Corput) value of index in
the given base, a number in [0, 1).

funcs.name: Weddle
funcs.signature: func Weddle(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
Weddle approximates the integral of f over [a, b] using the composite Weddle
rule; n is the number of panels and is rounded up to a multiple of six.

funcs.name: WeddleRule
funcs.signature: func WeddleRule(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
WeddleRule applies the seven-point Weddle (Newton-Cotes degree six) rule to
f over [a, b].

packages.name: numpde
packages.synopsis: Package numpde provides finite-difference solvers and supporting utilities for numerically approximating solutions to partial differential equations (PDEs) in one and two space dimensions.
packages.doc:
Package numpde provides finite-difference solvers and supporting utilities
for numerically approximating solutions to partial differential equations
(PDEs) in one and two space dimensions.

The package is organised around a handful of classic model problems:

  - The heat (diffusion) equation u_t = alpha * u_xx, solved with the
    explicit FTCS scheme, the fully implicit BTCS scheme, and the
    unconditionally stable Crank-Nicolson scheme, plus a 2D explicit
    solver and an alternating-direction-implicit (ADI) solver.
  - The wave equation u_tt = c^2 * u_xx, solved with the standard explicit
    leap-frog (central-time central-space) scheme in 1D and 2D.
  - The Laplace/Poisson equation, solved with the Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel and
    successive-over-relaxation (SOR) iterative methods, plus a direct
    tridiagonal solver for the 1D case.
  - The linear advection equation u_t + a * u_x = 0, solved with the
    first-order upwind, Lax-Friedrichs and second-order Lax-Wendroff
    schemes, all using periodic boundary conditions.

In addition the package exposes method-of-lines (MOL) helpers that convert a
spatial discretisation into a system of ordinary differential equations that
can be advanced with the supplied Euler, RK2 and RK4 time steppers, as well
as stencil builders and small dense/tridiagonal linear-algebra utilities.

All routines depend only on the Go standard library and are deterministic:
identical inputs always produce identical outputs. Grids are uniform, and
unless stated otherwise Dirichlet boundary conditions are imposed by holding
the first and last grid values fixed.

types.name: BCType
types.signature: type BCType int
types.doc: BCType enumerates the kinds of boundary condition understood by the solvers.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns a human-readable name for the boundary-condition type.

types.name: Field1D
types.signature: type Field1D func(x float64) float64
types.doc:
Field1D is a scalar function of a single spatial coordinate, x -> u(x).
It is commonly used to sample an initial condition onto a grid.

types.name: Field2D
types.signature: type Field2D func(x, y float64) float64
types.doc: Field2D is a scalar function of two spatial coordinates, (x, y) -> u(x, y).

types.name: Grid1D
types.doc:
Grid1D describes a uniform one-dimensional mesh of N points covering the
closed interval [X0, X1]. The points are x_i = X0 + i*Dx for i in [0, N-1]
with spacing Dx = (X1-X0)/(N-1).

funcs.name: NewGrid1D
funcs.signature: func NewGrid1D(x0, x1 float64, n int) Grid1D
funcs.doc:
NewGrid1D constructs a Grid1D over [x0, x1] with n points. It panics if
n < 2 or if x1 <= x0.

methods.name: Dx
methods.doc: Dx returns the uniform spacing between adjacent grid points.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of grid points.

methods.name: Points
methods.doc: Points returns a freshly allocated slice containing every grid coordinate.

methods.name: Sample
methods.doc: Sample evaluates f at every grid point and returns the resulting values.

methods.name: X
methods.doc: X returns the coordinate of the i-th grid point.

types.name: Grid2D
types.signature:
type Grid2D struct {
	X0 float64 // left x endpoint
	X1 float64 // right x endpoint
	Y0 float64 // bottom y endpoint
	Y1 float64 // top y endpoint
	Nx int     // number of grid points in x (must be at least 2)
	Ny int     // number of grid points in y (must be at least 2)
}
types.doc:
Grid2D describes a uniform two-dimensional mesh of Nx by Ny points covering
the rectangle [X0, X1] x [Y0, Y1]. Field values are stored in row-major
order as u[i][j] with i indexing x (0..Nx-1) and j indexing y (0..Ny-1).

funcs.name: NewGrid2D
funcs.signature: func NewGrid2D(x0, x1, y0, y1 float64, nx, ny int) Grid2D
funcs.doc:
NewGrid2D constructs a Grid2D over [x0,x1] x [y0,y1] with nx by ny points.
It panics if nx < 2, ny < 2, x1 <= x0 or y1 <= y0.

methods.name: Dx
methods.doc: Dx returns the uniform grid spacing in the x direction.

methods.name: Dy
methods.doc: Dy returns the uniform grid spacing in the y direction.

methods.name: Sample
methods.doc: Sample evaluates f at every grid node and returns the values as u[i][j].

methods.name: X
methods.doc: X returns the x coordinate of column index i.

methods.name: Y
methods.doc: Y returns the y coordinate of row index j.

types.name: IterResult
types.doc:
IterResult reports the outcome of an iterative linear solver. Solution holds
the final field, Iterations the number of sweeps performed, Residual the
final infinity-norm residual (or update norm), and Converged whether the
requested tolerance was met before the iteration cap.

funcs.name: LaplaceGaussSeidel
funcs.signature: func LaplaceGaussSeidel(u0 [][]float64, dx, dy, tol float64, maxIter int) IterResult
funcs.doc:
LaplaceGaussSeidel solves u_xx + u_yy = 0 with the Gauss-Seidel iteration,
using the border of u0 as fixed Dirichlet data.

funcs.name: LaplaceJacobi
funcs.signature: func LaplaceJacobi(u0 [][]float64, dx, dy, tol float64, maxIter int) IterResult
funcs.doc:
LaplaceJacobi solves the Laplace equation u_xx + u_yy = 0 with the Jacobi
iteration, using the border of u0 as fixed Dirichlet data. It is a
convenience wrapper around PoissonJacobi with a zero source term.

funcs.name: LaplaceSOR
funcs.signature: func LaplaceSOR(u0 [][]float64, dx, dy, omega, tol float64, maxIter int) IterResult
funcs.doc:
LaplaceSOR solves u_xx + u_yy = 0 with successive over-relaxation using factor
omega, using the border of u0 as fixed Dirichlet data.

funcs.name: PoissonGaussSeidel
funcs.signature: func PoissonGaussSeidel(u0, f [][]float64, dx, dy, tol float64, maxIter int) IterResult
funcs.doc:
PoissonGaussSeidel solves u_xx + u_yy = f with the Gauss-Seidel iteration,
which updates each node in place using the most recent neighbour values and
typically converges about twice as fast as Jacobi. Arguments and termination
mirror PoissonJacobi. The input matrices are not modified.

funcs.name: PoissonJacobi
funcs.signature: func PoissonJacobi(u0, f [][]float64, dx, dy, tol float64, maxIter int) IterResult
funcs.doc:
PoissonJacobi solves u_xx + u_yy = f with the Jacobi iteration. u0 supplies
the initial guess and the fixed Dirichlet boundary values; f is the source
term (pass nil for the Laplace equation). Iteration stops when the infinity
norm of the update between sweeps falls below tol or after maxIter sweeps. The
input matrices are not modified.

funcs.name: PoissonSOR
funcs.signature: func PoissonSOR(u0, f [][]float64, dx, dy, omega, tol float64, maxIter int) IterResult
funcs.doc:
PoissonSOR solves u_xx + u_yy = f with successive over-relaxation using the
relaxation factor omega (omega == 1 reduces to Gauss-Seidel; 1 < omega < 2
accelerates convergence). Arguments and termination mirror PoissonJacobi. The
input matrices are not modified.

types.name: ODEFunc
types.signature: type ODEFunc func(t float64, y []float64) []float64
types.doc:
ODEFunc is the right-hand side of an autonomous or non-autonomous system of
ordinary differential equations, y' = f(t, y). Implementations must return a
freshly allocated slice the same length as y and must not modify y.

funcs.name: MOLAdvection1DField
funcs.doc:
MOLAdvection1DField returns an ODEFunc that evaluates MOLAdvection1DRHS with
the given advection speed and grid spacing, suitable for MOLIntegrate.

funcs.name: MOLHeat1DField
funcs.doc:
MOLHeat1DField returns an ODEFunc that evaluates MOLHeat1DRHS with the given
diffusivity and grid spacing, suitable for passing to MOLIntegrate.

types.name: Stencil
types.doc:
Stencil holds the offset/weight pairs of a finite-difference approximation to
a derivative on a uniform grid. Offsets are integer multiples of the grid
spacing relative to the point being updated, and Weights are the matching
coefficients already divided by the appropriate power of the spacing, so that
the derivative estimate is sum_k Weights[k]*u[i+Offsets[k]].

funcs.name: BackwardFirstDerivativeStencil
funcs.doc:
BackwardFirstDerivativeStencil returns the first-order accurate backward
difference stencil for d/dx: weights [-1,1]/dx at offsets [-1,0].

funcs.name: CentralFirstDerivativeStencil
funcs.doc:
CentralFirstDerivativeStencil returns the second-order accurate central
difference stencil for d/dx on a grid of spacing dx: weights [-1,0,1]/(2dx)
at offsets [-1,0,1].

funcs.name: ForwardFirstDerivativeStencil
funcs.doc:
ForwardFirstDerivativeStencil returns the first-order accurate forward
difference stencil for d/dx: weights [-1,1]/dx at offsets [0,1].

funcs.name: SecondDerivativeStencil
funcs.doc:
SecondDerivativeStencil returns the second-order accurate central difference
stencil for d^2/dx^2: weights [1,-2,1]/dx^2 at offsets [-1,0,1].

methods.name: Apply
methods.signature: func (s Stencil) Apply(u []float64, i int) float64
methods.doc:
Apply evaluates the stencil at interior index i of the slice u. The caller
must ensure every i+Offsets[k] is a valid index; Apply performs no bounds
checking beyond what the Go runtime provides.

types.name: Stepper
types.signature: type Stepper func(f ODEFunc, t float64, y []float64, dt float64) []float64
types.doc:
Stepper advances an ODE system by one step of size dt from state y at time t
using the right-hand side f, returning the new state. EulerStep, RK2Step and
RK4Step all satisfy this signature.

funcs.name: Advection1DCourant
funcs.signature: func Advection1DCourant(a, dx, dt float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Advection1DCourant returns the (signed) Courant number nu = a*dt/dx. The
explicit schemes are stable for |nu| <= 1.

funcs.name: Advection1DLaxFriedrichs
funcs.signature: func Advection1DLaxFriedrichs(u0 []float64, a, dx, dt float64, steps int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Advection1DLaxFriedrichs solves u_t + a*u_x = 0 on a periodic grid with the
Lax-Friedrichs scheme, taking the given number of steps. It panics if the
Courant number exceeds 1 in magnitude. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: Advection1DLaxFriedrichsStep
funcs.doc:
Advection1DLaxFriedrichsStep advances one step of the Lax-Friedrichs scheme on
a periodic grid with Courant number nu. The scheme is first-order accurate and
quite diffusive but robust. A new slice is returned.

funcs.name: Advection1DLaxWendroff
funcs.signature: func Advection1DLaxWendroff(u0 []float64, a, dx, dt float64, steps int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Advection1DLaxWendroff solves u_t + a*u_x = 0 on a periodic grid with the
second-order Lax-Wendroff scheme, taking the given number of steps. It panics
if the Courant number exceeds 1 in magnitude. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: Advection1DLaxWendroffStep
funcs.doc:
Advection1DLaxWendroffStep advances one step of the second-order accurate
Lax-Wendroff scheme on a periodic grid with Courant number nu. A new slice is
returned.

funcs.name: Advection1DStableDt
funcs.doc:
Advection1DStableDt returns the largest stable time step dx/|a| for the
explicit advection schemes (the CFL limit). It panics if a is zero.

funcs.name: Advection1DUpwind
funcs.signature: func Advection1DUpwind(u0 []float64, a, dx, dt float64, steps int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Advection1DUpwind solves u_t + a*u_x = 0 on a periodic grid of spacing dx with
the first-order upwind scheme, taking the given number of steps of size dt.
It panics if the Courant number exceeds 1 in magnitude (unstable). The input
is not modified.

funcs.name: Advection1DUpwindStep
funcs.doc:
Advection1DUpwindStep advances one step of the first-order upwind scheme on a
periodic grid with Courant number nu = a*dt/dx. The upwind direction is
chosen from the sign of nu. A new slice is returned; u is left unmodified.

funcs.name: Clone
funcs.doc: Clone returns a copy of the slice u. The result never aliases u.

funcs.name: Clone2D
funcs.doc:
Clone2D returns a deep copy of the matrix u. Neither the outer slice nor any
row of the result aliases u.

funcs.name: EulerStep
funcs.signature: func EulerStep(f ODEFunc, t float64, y []float64, dt float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
EulerStep advances the ODE system y' = f(t, y) by one explicit (forward)
Euler step of size dt and returns the new state. It is first-order accurate.

funcs.name: FirstDerivativeMatrix
funcs.doc:
