FirstDerivativeMatrix returns the dense n by n central-difference
approximation of d/dx with spacing dx. Interior rows contain
[-1, 0, 1]/(2*dx); the first row uses a forward and the last row a backward
one-sided difference so the operator remains first-order accurate at the
boundaries.

funcs.name: FivePointLaplacianStencil
funcs.signature: func FivePointLaplacianStencil(dx, dy float64) (center, east, west, north, south float64)
funcs.doc:
FivePointLaplacianStencil returns the coefficients of the standard
second-order five-point discrete Laplacian on a grid with spacings dx and dy.
The returned values are the weights applied to the centre node and its four
axis-aligned neighbours: laplacian(u)[i][j] = center*u[i][j] +
east*u[i+1][j] + west*u[i-1][j] + north*u[i][j+1] + south*u[i][j-1].

funcs.name: Gradient1D
funcs.doc:
Gradient1D returns the discrete first derivative of u on a grid of spacing dx.
Interior points use the second-order central difference; the endpoints use
first-order one-sided differences so the result is defined everywhere. It
panics if len(u) < 2.

funcs.name: Heat1DCrankNicolson
funcs.signature: func Heat1DCrankNicolson(u0 []float64, alpha, dx, dt float64, steps int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Heat1DCrankNicolson solves u_t = alpha*u_xx with the Crank-Nicolson scheme,
taking the given number of steps of size dt on a grid of spacing dx. Boundary
values are held fixed. The scheme is second-order accurate in both space and
time and unconditionally stable. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: Heat1DCrankNicolsonStep
funcs.doc:
Heat1DCrankNicolsonStep advances u by one step of the second-order accurate,
unconditionally stable Crank-Nicolson scheme with mesh ratio r, holding the
boundary values fixed. A new slice is returned and u is left unchanged.

funcs.name: Heat1DExplicit
funcs.signature: func Heat1DExplicit(u0 []float64, alpha, dx, dt float64, steps int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Heat1DExplicit solves u_t = alpha*u_xx on the grid implied by u0 (spacing dx)
by taking the given number of explicit FTCS steps of size dt. The boundary
values u0[0] and u0[last] are held fixed for the whole simulation. The input
is not modified; the field at the final time is returned. It panics if the
scheme is unstable (mesh ratio above 1/2) so that silent garbage is never
produced.

funcs.name: Heat1DExplicitStep
funcs.doc:
Heat1DExplicitStep advances the field u by one time step of the explicit
forward-time centred-space (FTCS) scheme with mesh ratio r = alpha*dt/dx^2,
holding the two boundary values fixed (homogeneous or inhomogeneous
Dirichlet). It returns a new slice and leaves u unmodified. The scheme is
stable only for r <= 1/2.

funcs.name: Heat1DImplicit
funcs.signature: func Heat1DImplicit(u0 []float64, alpha, dx, dt float64, steps int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Heat1DImplicit solves u_t = alpha*u_xx with the unconditionally stable BTCS
scheme, taking the given number of steps of size dt on a grid of spacing dx.
Boundary values are held fixed. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: Heat1DImplicitStep
funcs.doc:
Heat1DImplicitStep advances u by one step of the fully implicit backward-time
centred-space (BTCS) scheme with mesh ratio r, holding the boundary values
fixed. The scheme is unconditionally stable for any r > 0. A new slice is
returned and u is left unchanged.

funcs.name: Heat1DMeshRatio
funcs.signature: func Heat1DMeshRatio(alpha, dx, dt float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Heat1DMeshRatio returns the dimensionless mesh (Fourier) ratio
r = alpha*dt/dx^2 that governs the stability and accuracy of the
finite-difference heat-equation schemes.

funcs.name: Heat1DStableDt
funcs.doc:
Heat1DStableDt returns the largest time step for which the explicit FTCS
scheme applied to u_t = alpha*u_xx is stable, namely dt = dx^2/(2*alpha).
Steps larger than this cause the explicit solver to blow up.

funcs.name: Heat2DADI
funcs.signature: func Heat2DADI(u0 [][]float64, alpha, dx, dy, dt float64, steps int) [][]float64
funcs.doc:
Heat2DADI solves u_t = alpha*(u_xx + u_yy) with the unconditionally stable
Peaceman-Rachford ADI scheme, taking the given number of steps of size dt on
a grid with spacings dx and dy. All boundary nodes are held fixed at their
initial values. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: Heat2DADIStep
funcs.signature: func Heat2DADIStep(u [][]float64, alpha, dx, dy, dt float64) [][]float64
funcs.doc:
Heat2DADIStep advances u by one Peaceman-Rachford alternating-direction
implicit (ADI) step for u_t = alpha*(u_xx + u_yy). Each step consists of two
half-steps: implicit in x then implicit in y, each solved with the Thomas
algorithm. The scheme is second-order accurate in time and unconditionally
stable. Boundary nodes are held fixed. A new matrix is returned.

funcs.name: Heat2DExplicit
funcs.signature: func Heat2DExplicit(u0 [][]float64, alpha, dx, dy, dt float64, steps int) [][]float64
funcs.doc:
Heat2DExplicit solves u_t = alpha*(u_xx + u_yy) on the grid implied by u0
(spacings dx, dy) with the explicit FTCS scheme, taking the given number of
steps of size dt. All boundary nodes are held fixed at their initial values.
It panics if the combined mesh ratio exceeds 1/2 (unstable). The input is not
modified.

funcs.name: Heat2DExplicitStep
funcs.doc:
Heat2DExplicitStep advances the field u (stored as u[i][j]) by one explicit
FTCS step for u_t = alpha*(u_xx + u_yy), holding every boundary node fixed.
rx = alpha*dt/dx^2 and ry = alpha*dt/dy^2 are the directional mesh ratios. A
new matrix is returned; u is left unmodified.

funcs.name: Heat2DMeshRatio
funcs.signature: func Heat2DMeshRatio(alpha, dx, dy, dt float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Heat2DMeshRatio returns the combined explicit stability parameter
alpha*dt*(1/dx^2 + 1/dy^2) for the 2D heat equation. The explicit scheme is
stable when this quantity does not exceed 1/2.

funcs.name: Heat2DStableDt
funcs.signature: func Heat2DStableDt(alpha, dx, dy float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Heat2DStableDt returns the largest stable explicit time step for
u_t = alpha*(u_xx + u_yy), namely dt = 1 / (2*alpha*(1/dx^2 + 1/dy^2)).

funcs.name: L2Norm
funcs.doc: L2Norm returns the Euclidean (2-norm) length of u, sqrt(sum u_i^2).

funcs.name: LInfNorm
funcs.doc:
LInfNorm returns the maximum-norm (infinity norm) of u, the largest absolute
element. It is an alias-free synonym for MaxAbs used where norm terminology
reads more clearly.

funcs.name: Laplacian1D
funcs.doc:
Laplacian1D returns the discrete second derivative of the field u on a grid
of spacing dx using the three-point central stencil at interior points. The
endpoints of the returned slice are set to zero, matching a homogeneous
Dirichlet treatment; adjust them afterwards if a different boundary is
required. It panics if len(u) < 3.

funcs.name: Laplacian2D
funcs.doc:
Laplacian2D returns the discrete Laplacian of the field u (stored as u[i][j])
using the five-point stencil with spacings dx and dy. Interior nodes receive
the standard approximation; boundary nodes are left at zero. It panics if the
grid is smaller than 3x3.

funcs.name: Linspace
funcs.signature: func Linspace(a, b float64, n int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Linspace returns n evenly spaced samples over the closed interval [a, b].
The first sample equals a and, when n >= 2, the last equals b. It panics if
n < 1.

funcs.name: MOLAdvection1DRHS
funcs.doc:
MOLAdvection1DRHS returns the semi-discrete right-hand side du/dt for the
linear advection equation u_t + a*u_x = 0 using the second-order central
difference for u_x on a periodic grid of spacing dx. The returned slice is
newly allocated.

funcs.name: MOLHeat1DRHS
funcs.doc:
MOLHeat1DRHS returns the semi-discrete right-hand side du/dt for the heat
equation u_t = alpha*u_xx obtained by replacing u_xx with the three-point
central difference on a grid of spacing dx. The endpoints are assigned a zero
time derivative so that Dirichlet boundary values remain fixed under any of
the time steppers. The returned slice is newly allocated.

funcs.name: MOLIntegrate
funcs.signature: func MOLIntegrate(f ODEFunc, y0 []float64, t0, dt float64, steps int, stepper Stepper) []float64
funcs.doc:
MOLIntegrate advances y0 through the given number of steps of size dt using
the supplied stepper, starting at time t0, and returns the final state. It is
the generic driver that turns a method-of-lines right-hand side into a full
time integration. The input y0 is not modified.

funcs.name: MatVec
funcs.doc:
MatVec multiplies the dense matrix m by the vector x and returns m*x. It
panics when the inner dimensions disagree.

funcs.name: MaxAbs
funcs.doc:
MaxAbs returns the largest absolute value among the elements of u, or zero
when u is empty.

funcs.name: MaxAbsDiff
funcs.doc:
MaxAbsDiff returns the infinity norm of the elementwise difference a-b. It
panics if the slices have different lengths.

funcs.name: MaxAbsDiff2D
funcs.doc:
MaxAbsDiff2D returns the infinity norm of the elementwise difference of two
equally shaped matrices. It panics on a shape mismatch.

funcs.name: NinePointLaplacianStencil
funcs.signature: func NinePointLaplacianStencil(h float64) (center, edge, corner float64)
funcs.doc:
NinePointLaplacianStencil returns the weights of the fourth-order accurate
nine-point discrete Laplacian on a square grid of spacing h. The order of the
returned weights is: centre, the four edge neighbours (E, W, N, S share the
value edge), and the four diagonal neighbours (share the value corner). The
operator is laplacian(u) = (corner*(sum of 4 diagonals) + edge*(sum of 4
edges) + center*u_ij)/(6 h^2) already folded into the returned weights.

funcs.name: OptimalSOROmega
funcs.doc:
OptimalSOROmega returns the theoretically optimal over-relaxation factor for
the SOR solution of the five-point Laplacian on an nx by ny Dirichlet grid
with equal spacing in both directions. It uses omega = 2/(1+sqrt(1-rho^2))
where rho is the spectral radius of the corresponding Jacobi iteration,
rho = (cos(pi/(nx-1)) + cos(pi/(ny-1)))/2. The result lies in (1, 2).

funcs.name: Poisson1D
funcs.doc:
Poisson1D directly solves the two-point boundary-value problem u_xx = f on a
uniform grid of spacing dx using the tridiagonal (Thomas) algorithm. The
boundary values u0[0] and u0[last] are imposed as Dirichlet data; f holds the
source term at every node (interior entries are used). The exact discrete
solution is returned in a new slice. It panics if len(u0) != len(f) or the
grid has fewer than three points.

funcs.name: RK2Step
funcs.signature: func RK2Step(f ODEFunc, t float64, y []float64, dt float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
RK2Step advances the ODE system by one step of the explicit midpoint method
(second-order Runge-Kutta) of size dt and returns the new state.

funcs.name: RK4Step
funcs.signature: func RK4Step(f ODEFunc, t float64, y []float64, dt float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
RK4Step advances the ODE system by one step of the classical fourth-order
Runge-Kutta method of size dt and returns the new state. This is the default
time integrator for the method-of-lines helpers.

funcs.name: RMSNorm
funcs.doc:
RMSNorm returns the root-mean-square norm of u, sqrt(mean(u_i^2)). It is the
discrete L2 norm normalised by the number of samples and is a good measure of
grid error that is insensitive to mesh refinement. It returns zero for empty
input.

funcs.name: SecondDerivativeMatrix
funcs.doc:
SecondDerivativeMatrix returns the dense n by n central-difference
approximation of the second-derivative operator d^2/dx^2 with grid spacing
dx and homogeneous Dirichlet treatment at the ends (the first and last rows
use the standard interior stencil truncated at the boundary). Interior rows
contain [1, -2, 1]/dx^2. It is primarily useful for small problems, teaching,
and assembling method-of-lines Jacobians.

funcs.name: ThomasSolve
funcs.doc:
ThomasSolve solves the tridiagonal linear system M x = d, where M has
sub-diagonal a, main diagonal b and super-diagonal c. Element a[0] and
c[n-1] are ignored. All slices must have equal length n >= 1. The inputs are
left unmodified; a freshly allocated solution slice is returned. It panics on
a length mismatch and returns a slice of NaNs is avoided by requiring
non-zero pivots (a zero pivot triggers a panic, indicating a singular or
non-diagonally-dominant system).

funcs.name: Wave1DCourant
funcs.signature: func Wave1DCourant(c, dx, dt float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Wave1DCourant returns the Courant number C = c*dt/dx for the 1D wave
equation. The explicit leap-frog scheme is stable for C <= 1.

funcs.name: Wave1DExplicit
funcs.signature: func Wave1DExplicit(u0, v0 []float64, c, dx, dt float64, steps int) []float64
funcs.doc:
Wave1DExplicit solves u_tt = c^2*u_xx with the explicit leap-frog (central
time, central space) scheme. u0 is the initial displacement and v0 the
initial velocity, both sampled on a grid of spacing dx. The solver performs
the given number of time steps of size dt using the standard second-order
accurate startup step and holds the boundary values of u0 fixed. It panics if
the Courant number exceeds 1 (unstable). The inputs are not modified.

funcs.name: Wave1DStableDt
funcs.doc:
Wave1DStableDt returns the largest stable explicit time step for the 1D wave
equation, dt = dx/c (the Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy limit).

funcs.name: Wave1DStep
funcs.doc:
Wave1DStep advances one leap-frog step of the 1D wave equation given the
solution at the previous two time levels. prev is u at time level n-1, curr
is u at level n, and c2 is the squared Courant number (c*dt/dx)^2. Boundary
values are copied from curr (fixed Dirichlet). A new slice with u at level
n+1 is returned.

funcs.name: Wave2DCourant
funcs.signature: func Wave2DCourant(c, dx, dy, dt float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Wave2DCourant returns the 2D Courant number c*dt*sqrt(1/dx^2 + 1/dy^2). The
explicit scheme is stable when this quantity does not exceed 1.

funcs.name: Wave2DExplicit
funcs.signature: func Wave2DExplicit(u0, v0 [][]float64, c, dx, dy, dt float64, steps int) [][]float64
funcs.doc:
Wave2DExplicit solves u_tt = c^2*(u_xx + u_yy) with the explicit leap-frog
scheme. u0 is the initial displacement and v0 the initial velocity, both
stored as [i][j] on a grid with spacings dx and dy. The solver performs the
given number of steps of size dt and holds all boundary nodes fixed. It
panics if the 2D Courant number exceeds 1. The inputs are not modified.

funcs.name: Zeros2D
funcs.doc: Zeros2D allocates an nx by ny matrix with every entry set to zero.

packages.name: optimize
packages.synopsis: Package optimize implements one-dimensional root finding and function optimization for real-valued functions of a single real variable.
packages.doc:
Package optimize implements one-dimensional root finding and function
optimization for real-valued functions of a single real variable.

The package gathers the classical numerical methods used to locate the
zeros and extrema of a scalar function. It is organised into several
families:

  - Bracketing solvers, which start from an interval [a, b] on which the
    function changes sign and are guaranteed to converge: Bisection,
    RegulaFalsi (with the Illinois, Pegasus and Anderson-Björck
    accelerations), Brent's method (zeroin), Ridders' method and Dekker's
    method.
  - Open solvers, which iterate from one or more starting guesses and
    converge quickly when close to a simple root: Secant, Newton, Halley,
    Schröder (for multiple roots), Steffensen, inverse quadratic
    interpolation, Muller's method and fixed-point iteration (optionally
    accelerated with Aitken's Δ² process).
  - Polynomial solvers, which return all real (or complex) roots of a
    polynomial: closed forms for linear, quadratic and cubic equations,
    and the iterative Durand-Kerner and Aberth-Ehrlich methods together
    with a companion-matrix constructor.
  - Minimizers, which locate a local minimum of a unimodal function:
    golden-section search, Brent's parabolic minimizer, ternary search and
    Newton/gradient descent on the derivative, plus routines that bracket a
    minimum.
  - Supporting numerical utilities: finite-difference derivatives, sign and
    bracketing predicates, and convergence helpers.

Polynomials are represented throughout as a slice of coefficients in
ascending order of degree: coeffs[i] is the coefficient of x^i, so that
coeffs = {c0, c1, c2} denotes c0 + c1*x + c2*x^2.

Every routine is deterministic and depends only on the Go standard library.

consts.signature: const DefaultLearningRate = 0.01
consts.doc:
DefaultLearningRate is a reasonable default step size for the first-order
descent methods when the caller has no specific requirement.

consts.signature: const DefaultMaxIter = 1000
consts.doc:
DefaultMaxIter is a reasonable default cap on the number of iterations
performed by the iterative multivariate routines.

consts.signature: const DefaultMaxIterations = 100
consts.doc:
DefaultMaxIterations is a reasonable default cap on the number of iterations
performed by the iterative routines before they give up.

consts.signature: const DefaultStep = 1e-6
consts.doc:
DefaultStep is the default finite-difference step used by the numeric
gradient and directional-derivative routines when the caller passes h <= 0.

consts.signature: const DefaultTol = 1e-8
consts.doc:
DefaultTol is a reasonable default convergence tolerance for the
multivariate routines.

consts.signature: const DefaultTolerance = 1e-10
consts.doc:
DefaultTolerance is a reasonable default convergence tolerance for the
iterative routines when the caller has no specific requirement.
GoldenRatio is the golden ratio phi = (1 + sqrt(5)) / 2.

InvGoldenRatio is the reciprocal of the golden ratio, 1/phi = phi - 1,
the shrink factor used by golden-section search.

vars.signature: var ErrDimensionMismatch = errors.New("optimize: vector or matrix dimensions do not match")
vars.doc:
ErrDimensionMismatch is returned when two vectors or a matrix and a vector
have incompatible lengths.

vars.signature: var ErrEmptyPoint = errors.New("optimize: starting point has zero length")
vars.doc:
ErrEmptyPoint is returned when a routine is called with an empty starting
point.

vars.signature: var ErrInvalidInterval = errors.New("optimize: invalid interval")
vars.doc:
ErrInvalidInterval is returned when an interval or set of starting points is
degenerate or otherwise unusable.

vars.signature: var ErrMaxIterations = errors.New("optimize: maximum number of iterations exceeded before convergence")
vars.doc:
ErrMaxIterations is returned when a routine exhausts its iteration budget
before satisfying the requested tolerance.

vars.signature: var ErrNoBracket = errors.New("optimize: interval does not bracket a root (endpoints share the same sign)")
vars.doc:
ErrNoBracket is returned by bracketing solvers when the supplied endpoints
do not straddle a root, i.e. the function has the same sign at both ends.

vars.signature: var ErrNonFinite = errors.New("optimize: encountered a non-finite value")
vars.doc: ErrNonFinite is returned when a computation produces a NaN or infinite value.

vars.signature: var ErrSingularMatrix = errors.New("optimize: matrix is singular")
vars.doc:
ErrSingularMatrix is returned by SolveLinearSystem and the Newton solver when
the coefficient matrix is (numerically) singular.

vars.signature: var ErrZeroDerivative = errors.New("optimize: derivative (or denominator) evaluated to zero")
vars.doc:
ErrZeroDerivative is returned when a method divides by a derivative or a
finite difference that has collapsed to zero.

types.name: Bracket
types.doc:
Bracket describes a closed interval [Lo, Hi] believed to enclose a root or a
minimum.

funcs.name: BracketSubdivide
funcs.signature: func BracketSubdivide(f Func, a, b float64, n int) []Bracket
funcs.doc:
BracketSubdivide divides [a, b] into n equal sub-intervals and returns every
sub-interval across which f changes sign, each as a Bracket. It is the tool
for isolating several roots of a function on a wide interval before refining
each with a bracketing solver.

funcs.name: FindBracket
funcs.signature: func FindBracket(f Func, a, b float64) (Bracket, bool)
funcs.doc:
FindBracket expands the interval [a, b] outward until it encloses a root and
returns the resulting Bracket together with a flag reporting success.

methods.name: Contains
methods.doc:
Contains reports whether x lies within the closed bracket, irrespective of
whether Lo or Hi is the larger endpoint.

methods.name: Midpoint
methods.doc: Midpoint returns the arithmetic midpoint of the bracket.

methods.name: Width
methods.doc: Width returns the (non-negative) length of the bracket.

types.name: Func
types.signature: type Func func(float64) float64
types.doc:
Func is a real-valued function of a single real variable, the fundamental
object operated on by the solvers and minimizers in this package.

types.name: GradFunc
types.signature: type GradFunc func(x []float64) []float64
types.doc:
GradFunc returns the gradient vector (vector of first partial derivatives)
of an objective evaluated at x. The returned slice has the same length as x.

types.name: HessFunc
types.signature: type HessFunc func(x []float64) [][]float64
types.doc:
HessFunc returns the Hessian matrix (matrix of second partial derivatives)
of an objective evaluated at x, as an n-by-n row-major slice of slices.

types.name: MultiFunc
types.signature: type MultiFunc func(x []float64) float64
types.doc:
MultiFunc is a scalar-valued objective function of a real vector argument.
It is the fundamental object minimized by the multivariate routines in this
file. Implementations must not mutate the slice they are handed.

types.name: Options
types.doc:
Options bundles the common tuning parameters shared by the iterative
multivariate minimizers. A zero Options is not usable directly; obtain a
populated value from DefaultOptions and adjust the fields you care about.

funcs.name: DefaultOptions
funcs.doc: DefaultOptions returns an Options value populated with sensible defaults.

types.name: Result
types.doc:
Result reports the outcome of a multivariate minimization: the located point
X, the objective value F at that point, the number of Iterations performed,
and whether the routine Converged to the requested tolerance.

funcs.name: BFGS
funcs.signature: func BFGS(f MultiFunc, grad GradFunc, x0 []float64, tol float64, maxIter int) Result
funcs.doc:
BFGS minimizes f by the BFGS quasi-Newton method starting from x0, maintaining
a dense approximation to the inverse Hessian that is refined by the rank-two
BFGS update after each Armijo-backtracked line search. If grad is nil the
gradient is estimated by central finite differences. Iteration stops when the
gradient norm falls below tol or after maxIter steps.

funcs.name: ConjugateGradient
funcs.signature: func ConjugateGradient(f MultiFunc, grad GradFunc, x0 []float64, tol float64, maxIter int) Result
funcs.doc:
ConjugateGradient minimizes f by the nonlinear conjugate-gradient method with
Fletcher-Reeves updates and Armijo backtracking line searches, starting from
x0. If grad is nil the gradient is estimated by central finite differences.
The search direction is reset to steepest descent every len(x0) iterations to
preserve convergence. Iteration stops when the gradient norm falls below tol
or after maxIter steps.

funcs.name: CoordinateDescent
funcs.signature: func CoordinateDescent(f MultiFunc, x0 []float64, window, tol float64, maxIter int) Result
funcs.doc:
CoordinateDescent minimizes f by cyclically minimizing along each coordinate
axis in turn. Each coordinate sub-problem is solved by BrentParabolic on the
window [x_i - window, x_i + window] centred on the current value. Iteration
stops when a full sweep moves the point by less than tol or after maxIter
sweeps. It is well suited to separable or mildly coupled convex objectives.

funcs.name: GradientDescentMomentum
funcs.signature: func GradientDescentMomentum(f MultiFunc, grad GradFunc, x0 []float64, rate, momentum, tol float64, maxIter int) Result
funcs.doc:
GradientDescentMomentum minimizes f by gradient descent with classical
(heavy-ball) momentum starting from x0. The velocity is updated as
v <- momentum*v - rate*grad and the point as x <- x + v. If grad is nil the
gradient is estimated by central finite differences. Iteration stops when the
gradient norm falls below tol or after maxIter steps.

funcs.name: GradientDescentNesterov
funcs.signature: func GradientDescentNesterov(f MultiFunc, grad GradFunc, x0 []float64, rate, momentum, tol float64, maxIter int) Result
funcs.doc:
GradientDescentNesterov minimizes f by gradient descent with Nesterov's
accelerated (look-ahead) momentum starting from x0. The gradient is evaluated
at the look-ahead point x + momentum*v. If grad is nil it is estimated by
central finite differences. Iteration stops when the gradient norm at x falls
below tol or after maxIter steps.

funcs.name: NelderMead
funcs.signature: func NelderMead(f MultiFunc, x0 []float64, step, tol float64, maxIter int) Result
funcs.doc:
NelderMead minimizes f by the Nelder-Mead downhill simplex method starting
from x0. An initial simplex is built by perturbing each coordinate of x0 by
step. The method uses the standard reflection, expansion, contraction and
shrink operations and requires no derivatives. Iteration stops when the spread
of objective values across the simplex falls below tol or after maxIter
iterations.

funcs.name: NewtonMultivariate
funcs.signature: func NewtonMultivariate(f MultiFunc, grad GradFunc, hess HessFunc, x0 []float64, tol float64, maxIter int) Result
funcs.doc:
NewtonMultivariate minimizes f by the damped Newton method starting from x0.
At each step it solves H*p = -g for the Newton direction p (where g and H are
the gradient and Hessian) and takes an Armijo-backtracked step along p. If
grad or hess is nil the corresponding quantity is estimated by finite
differences. If the Hessian is singular the method falls back to a steepest-
descent step. Iteration stops when the gradient norm falls below tol or after
maxIter steps.

funcs.name: SimulatedAnnealing
funcs.signature: func SimulatedAnnealing(f MultiFunc, x0 []float64, opts SAOptions, seed int64) Result
funcs.doc:
SimulatedAnnealing minimizes f by simulated annealing starting from x0.
Neighbours are proposed by adding independent Gaussian perturbations scaled by
opts.StepSize, and uphill moves are accepted with the Metropolis probability
exp(-Δf/T). The temperature starts at opts.InitialTemp and is multiplied by
opts.CoolingRate each step, floored at opts.MinTemp. All randomness is drawn
from a generator seeded by seed, so the result is fully deterministic for a
given seed. The best point ever visited is returned.

types.name: SAOptions
types.doc: SAOptions configures the SimulatedAnnealing minimizer.

funcs.name: DefaultSAOptions
funcs.doc: DefaultSAOptions returns an SAOptions value populated with sensible defaults.

types.name: ScalarFunc
types.signature: type ScalarFunc func(x float64) float64
types.doc:
ScalarFunc is a real-valued function of a single real variable, operated on
by the one-dimensional minimizers GoldenSection and BrentParabolic.

types.name: ScalarResult
types.doc:
ScalarResult reports the outcome of a one-dimensional minimization: the
located abscissa X, the objective value F there, the number of Iterations
performed, and whether the routine Converged.

funcs.name: BrentParabolic
funcs.signature: func BrentParabolic(f ScalarFunc, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) ScalarResult
funcs.doc:
BrentParabolic locates a minimum of the function f on the interval [a, b]
using Brent's method, which combines the reliability of golden-section search
with the fast convergence of successive parabolic interpolation. It stops
when the estimate is bracketed to within tol or after maxIter iterations.

funcs.name: GoldenSection
funcs.signature: func GoldenSection(f ScalarFunc, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) ScalarResult
funcs.doc:
GoldenSection locates a minimum of the unimodal function f on the interval
[a, b] by golden-section search, which shrinks the bracket by the constant
factor 1/phi each step. It stops when the bracket width falls below tol or
after maxIter iterations.

funcs.name: MinimizeScalar
funcs.signature: func MinimizeScalar(f ScalarFunc, a, b float64) ScalarResult
funcs.doc:
MinimizeScalar locates a minimum of f on [a, b] using BrentParabolic with the
DefaultTol and DefaultMaxIter. It is a convenience wrapper for the common
case where the caller does not need to tune the stopping rule.

types.name: VectorFunc
types.signature: type VectorFunc func(x []float64) []float64
types.doc:
VectorFunc is a vector-valued function of a vector argument, used by the
Jacobian utility. It maps an n-vector to an m-vector.

funcs.name: Aberth
funcs.signature: func Aberth(coeffs []float64, tol float64, maxIter int) []complex128
funcs.doc:
Aberth returns all n roots (real and complex) of the degree-n polynomial given
by coeffs (ascending order) using the Aberth-Ehrlich simultaneous iteration,
which incorporates the derivative for cubic local convergence and typically
needs fewer iterations than Durand-Kerner. Initial guesses are distributed on
a circle whose radius bounds the roots.

funcs.name: AbsoluteError
funcs.doc: AbsoluteError returns the absolute difference |a - b|.

funcs.name: AitkenDelta2
funcs.signature: func AitkenDelta2(x0, x1, x2 float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AitkenDelta2 applies a single step of Aitken's Δ² process to three
consecutive terms x0, x1, x2 of a linearly convergent sequence and returns
the accelerated estimate of the limit. When the second difference is zero it
returns x2 unchanged.

funcs.name: AndersonBjorck
funcs.signature: func AndersonBjorck(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
AndersonBjorck finds a root of f in [a, b] using the Anderson-Björck variant
of the method of false position, whose damping factor 1-fc/fb yields
asymptotic convergence close to that of the secant method while retaining the
bracketing guarantee.

funcs.name: ArmijoStep
funcs.signature: func ArmijoStep(f MultiFunc, x, dir, grad []float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ArmijoStep is a convenience wrapper around BacktrackingLineSearch using the
conventional parameters alpha0 = 1, c = 1e-4, rho = 0.5 and 50 reductions.

funcs.name: BacktrackingLineSearch
funcs.signature: func BacktrackingLineSearch(f MultiFunc, x, dir, grad []float64, alpha0, c, rho float64, maxIter int) float64
funcs.doc:
BacktrackingLineSearch returns a step length alpha along the descent
direction dir from x that satisfies the Armijo (sufficient decrease)
condition f(x + alpha*dir) <= f(x) + c*alpha*(grad . dir). Starting from
alpha0 it repeatedly multiplies the step by rho (0 < rho < 1) until the
condition holds or maxIter reductions have been made. grad must be the
gradient of f at x and dir must be a descent direction (grad . dir < 0) for
the guarantee to hold.

funcs.name: BackwardDifference
funcs.signature: func BackwardDifference(f Func, x, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BackwardDifference approximates f'(x) by the backward difference
(f(x)-f(x-h))/h, a first-order accurate one-sided estimate.

funcs.name: Bisection
funcs.signature: func Bisection(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Bisection finds a root of f in the bracketing interval [a, b] by repeated
interval halving. It converges linearly and is unconditionally reliable
whenever f is continuous and f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs. It returns
ErrNoBracket if the interval does not straddle a root and ErrMaxIterations if
the width tolerance is not reached within maxIter steps.

funcs.name: Booth
funcs.doc:
Booth is the two-dimensional convex test objective
(x0 + 2*x1 - 7)^2 + (2*x0 + x1 - 5)^2, with its unique minimum value 0 at
(1, 3). It panics if x does not have length 2.

funcs.name: BracketExpand
funcs.signature: func BracketExpand(f Func, a, b, factor float64, maxIter int) (float64, float64, bool)
funcs.doc:
BracketExpand geometrically expands the interval [a, b] outward (moving the
endpoint with the larger |f| by a factor of the current width) until f
changes sign across it or maxIter expansions have been tried. It reports the
possibly-widened endpoints and whether a bracket was found.

funcs.name: BracketMinimum
funcs.signature: func BracketMinimum(f Func, a, b float64) (float64, float64, float64)
funcs.doc:
BracketMinimum searches downhill from the initial points a and b to return a
triple (a, b, c) with a < b < c (or c < b < a) such that f(b) is less than
both f(a) and f(c), thereby bracketing a minimum. It implements the classic
mnbrak algorithm.

funcs.name: Brent
funcs.signature: func Brent(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Brent finds a root of f in [a, b] using Brent's method (the classic zeroin
algorithm), which combines the reliability of bisection with the speed of the
secant method and inverse quadratic interpolation. It is the recommended
general-purpose bracketing solver.

funcs.name: BrentMinimize
funcs.signature: func BrentMinimize(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, float64, error)
funcs.doc:
BrentMinimize returns the location and value of a minimum of f on [a, b] using
Brent's method, which blends golden-section search with parabolic
interpolation for fast, reliable convergence without requiring derivatives.
It returns (xmin, f(xmin), error).

funcs.name: Centroid
funcs.doc:
Centroid returns the arithmetic mean of a set of points, all assumed to have
the same dimension. It returns nil for an empty set.

funcs.name: Clamp
funcs.signature: func Clamp(x, lo, hi float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Clamp constrains x to the closed interval [lo, hi], swapping the bounds if
they are given in the wrong order.

funcs.name: CompanionMatrix
funcs.doc:
CompanionMatrix returns the companion matrix of the polynomial given by
coeffs (ascending order), a real n×n matrix whose characteristic polynomial
equals the given polynomial made monic. Its eigenvalues are the roots of the
polynomial. The matrix is returned in row-major order.

funcs.name: Converged
funcs.signature: func Converged(prev, cur, tol float64) bool
funcs.doc:
Converged reports whether successive iterates prev and cur satisfy a combined
relative/absolute convergence test with tolerance tol.

funcs.name: CubicRealRoots
funcs.signature: func CubicRealRoots(a, b, c, d float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
CubicRealRoots returns the real roots of a*x^3 + b*x^2 + c*x + d = 0 in
ascending order (with multiplicity), discarding any genuinely complex roots.

funcs.name: CubicRoots
funcs.signature: func CubicRoots(a, b, c, d float64) []complex128
funcs.doc:
CubicRoots returns all three roots of the cubic a*x^3 + b*x^2 + c*x + d = 0 as
complex numbers, using Cardano's formula together with the trigonometric
solution for the three-real-roots case. When a is zero it degenerates to the
quadratic case.

funcs.name: Dekker
funcs.signature: func Dekker(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Dekker finds a root of f in [a, b] using Dekker's method, the secant/bisection
hybrid that is the historical predecessor of Brent's method. It maintains a
bracketing contrapoint and falls back to bisection whenever the secant step
leaves the interval.

funcs.name: Derivative
funcs.signature: func Derivative(f Func, x, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Derivative approximates f'(x) by the central difference (f(x+h)-f(x-h))/(2h),
which has second-order accuracy in h.

funcs.name: DirectionalDerivative
funcs.signature: func DirectionalDerivative(f MultiFunc, x, dir []float64, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
DirectionalDerivative approximates the derivative of f at x along the unit-
scaled direction dir using a central finite difference with step h. If h <= 0
the DefaultStep is used. The direction need not be normalized; the returned
value is grad(f) . dir.

funcs.name: DurandKerner
funcs.signature: func DurandKerner(coeffs []float64, tol float64, maxIter int) []complex128
funcs.doc:
DurandKerner returns all n roots (real and complex) of the degree-n
polynomial given by coeffs (ascending order) using the Durand-Kerner
(Weierstrass) simultaneous iteration. The polynomial is made monic
internally; roots are refined until the largest update falls below tol or
maxIter iterations are reached.

funcs.name: FalsePosition
funcs.signature: func FalsePosition(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
FalsePosition is an alias for RegulaFalsi, the traditional English name for
the method of false position.

funcs.name: FixedPoint
funcs.signature: func FixedPoint(g Func, x0, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
FixedPoint finds a fixed point of g (a value x with g(x) = x) by the direct
iteration x_{n+1} = g(x_n) starting from x0. It converges linearly when g is a
contraction near the fixed point.

funcs.name: FixedPointAitken
funcs.signature: func FixedPointAitken(g Func, x0, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
FixedPointAitken finds a fixed point of g using Aitken's Δ² acceleration
(Steffensen's fixed-point method): it applies g twice and extrapolates,
turning linear convergence into quadratic convergence near the fixed point.

funcs.name: ForwardDifference
funcs.signature: func ForwardDifference(f Func, x, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ForwardDifference approximates f'(x) by the forward difference
(f(x+h)-f(x))/h, a first-order accurate one-sided estimate.

funcs.name: GoldenSectionMin
funcs.signature: func GoldenSectionMin(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
GoldenSectionMin returns the location of a minimum of the unimodal function f
on [a, b] using golden-section search, which narrows the bracket by the golden
ratio at each step and needs a single function evaluation per iteration.

funcs.name: GradientDescent
funcs.signature: func GradientDescent(df Func, x0, rate, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
GradientDescent locates a stationary point of a function by taking fixed-rate
steps against its derivative df from the starting point x0. It converges for a
sufficiently small rate and is included as a simple, dependency-free descent
method.

funcs.name: Halley
funcs.signature: func Halley(f, df, d2f Func, x0, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Halley finds a root of f using Halley's method, a third-order iteration that
uses the first derivative df and second derivative d2f. It converges cubically
near a simple root, roughly one iteration faster than Newton's method.

funcs.name: Himmelblau
funcs.doc:
Himmelblau is the two-dimensional test objective
(x0^2 + x1 - 11)^2 + (x0 + x1^2 - 7)^2, which has four equal local minima of
value 0, one of which is (3, 2). It panics if x does not have length 2.

funcs.name: Illinois
funcs.signature: func Illinois(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Illinois finds a root of f in [a, b] using the Illinois variant of the
method of false position, which halves the retained (stagnant) endpoint's
function value to break the one-sided convergence of RegulaFalsi. It keeps
the root bracketed and converges super-linearly.

funcs.name: InverseQuadratic
funcs.signature: func InverseQuadratic(f Func, x0, x1, x2, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
InverseQuadratic finds a root of f by inverse quadratic interpolation through
three starting points x0, x1 and x2: it fits x as a quadratic in f and
evaluates it at f = 0. Where the three function values are not distinct it
falls back to a secant step.

funcs.name: IsBracket
funcs.signature: func IsBracket(f Func, a, b float64) bool
funcs.doc: IsBracket reports whether [a, b] strictly brackets a sign change of f.

funcs.name: LinearRoot
funcs.doc:
LinearRoot returns the root of the linear equation a*x + b = 0. The boolean
result is false when a is zero and no unique root exists.

funcs.name: MatCopy
funcs.doc: MatCopy returns a deep copy of the matrix a.

funcs.name: MatIdentity
funcs.doc: MatIdentity returns the n-by-n identity matrix.

funcs.name: MatTranspose
funcs.doc: MatTranspose returns the transpose of the (rectangular) matrix a.

funcs.name: MatVec
funcs.doc:
MatVec returns the matrix-vector product a*x. It panics if the column count
of a does not equal len(x).

funcs.name: Muller
funcs.signature: func Muller(f func(complex128) complex128, x0, x1, x2 complex128, tol float64, maxIter int) (complex128, error)
funcs.doc:
Muller finds a (possibly complex) root of the complex function f using
Muller's method, which fits a parabola through three starting points and
takes the root of that parabola nearest the latest iterate. It is well suited
to polynomials because it can converge to complex roots from real starting
data.

funcs.name: Newton
funcs.signature: func Newton(f, df Func, x0, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Newton finds a root of f using the Newton-Raphson iteration, which requires
the derivative df and a single starting guess x0. It converges quadratically
near a simple root. It returns ErrZeroDerivative if df vanishes at an iterate.

funcs.name: NewtonComplex
funcs.signature: func NewtonComplex(f, df func(complex128) complex128, x0 complex128, tol float64, maxIter int) (complex128, error)
funcs.doc:
NewtonComplex finds a complex root of the complex function f with derivative
df using the Newton-Raphson iteration in the complex plane. It converges
quadratically near a simple root and is the natural tool for tracing the roots
of complex polynomials.

funcs.name: NewtonMinimize
funcs.signature: func NewtonMinimize(df, d2f Func, x0, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
NewtonMinimize locates a stationary point of a function by applying Newton's
method to its derivative, using the first derivative df and second derivative
d2f. Near a non-degenerate minimum it converges quadratically. It returns
ErrZeroDerivative if the second derivative vanishes.

funcs.name: NewtonSafe
funcs.signature: func NewtonSafe(f, df Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
NewtonSafe finds a root of f in the bracketing interval [a, b] using a
safeguarded Newton iteration (the rtsafe algorithm): it takes a Newton step
when that step stays inside the current bracket and is decreasing, and falls
back to bisection otherwise. It combines quadratic convergence with the
global reliability of bisection.

funcs.name: NumericGradient
funcs.doc:
NumericGradient approximates the gradient of f at x using central finite
differences. It is a convenience alias for NumericGradientCentral with the
DefaultStep.

funcs.name: NumericGradientCentral
funcs.signature: func NumericGradientCentral(f MultiFunc, x []float64, h float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
NumericGradientCentral approximates the gradient of f at x using central
finite differences with step h. If h <= 0 the DefaultStep is used. This is
the most accurate of the finite-difference gradient estimators.

funcs.name: NumericGradientForward
funcs.signature: func NumericGradientForward(f MultiFunc, x []float64, h float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
NumericGradientForward approximates the gradient of f at x using forward
finite differences with step h. If h <= 0 the DefaultStep is used. It uses
one fewer evaluation per coordinate than the central estimator at the cost of
accuracy.

funcs.name: NumericHessian
funcs.signature: func NumericHessian(f MultiFunc, x []float64, h float64) [][]float64
funcs.doc:
NumericHessian approximates the Hessian of f at x using central second
differences with step h. If h <= 0 a step of 1e-4 is used. The returned
matrix is symmetric by construction.

funcs.name: NumericJacobian
funcs.signature: func NumericJacobian(g VectorFunc, x []float64, h float64) [][]float64
funcs.doc:
NumericJacobian approximates the Jacobian of the vector-valued function g at
x using central finite differences with step h. If h <= 0 the DefaultStep is
used. The result is an m-by-n matrix whose (i, j) entry is d g_i / d x_j.

funcs.name: OppositeSign
funcs.doc: OppositeSign reports whether a and b have strictly opposite signs.

funcs.name: OuterProduct
funcs.doc:
OuterProduct returns the outer product a*b^T, an len(a)-by-len(b) matrix
whose (i, j) entry is a[i]*b[j].

funcs.name: ParabolicMinimum
funcs.signature: func ParabolicMinimum(x0, x1, x2, f0, f1, f2 float64) (float64, bool)
funcs.doc:
ParabolicMinimum fits a parabola through the three points (x0,f0), (x1,f1) and
(x2,f2) and returns the abscissa of its vertex. The boolean result is false
when the points are collinear and no unique vertex exists.

funcs.name: PartialDerivative
funcs.signature: func PartialDerivative(f MultiFunc, x []float64, i int, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
PartialDerivative approximates the partial derivative of f with respect to
coordinate i at x by a central finite difference with step h. If h <= 0 the
DefaultStep is used.

funcs.name: Pegasus
funcs.signature: func Pegasus(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Pegasus finds a root of f in [a, b] using the Pegasus variant of the method
of false position. Like Illinois it damps the stagnant endpoint, but scales
its function value by fb/(fb+fc), giving faster convergence in practice.

funcs.name: PolyComplexRoots
funcs.doc:
PolyComplexRoots returns all roots of the polynomial given by coeffs
(ascending order) as complex numbers, using Durand-Kerner with default
settings.

funcs.name: PolyDeflate
funcs.doc:
PolyDeflate divides the polynomial given by coeffs (ascending order) by the
linear factor (x - root) using synthetic division. It returns the
ascending-order quotient coefficients and the scalar remainder, which is zero
exactly when root is a root of the polynomial.

funcs.name: PolyDerivative
funcs.doc:
PolyDerivative returns the coefficients (ascending order) of the derivative of
the polynomial given by coeffs. A constant polynomial yields an empty slice.

funcs.name: PolyEval
funcs.doc:
PolyEval evaluates the polynomial with the given ascending-order coefficients
at x using Horner's scheme. An empty slice evaluates to zero.

funcs.name: PolyEvalComplex
funcs.doc:
PolyEvalComplex evaluates the real-coefficient polynomial (ascending order) at
a complex argument x using Horner's scheme.

funcs.name: PolyEvalDeriv
funcs.doc:
PolyEvalDeriv simultaneously evaluates the polynomial (ascending-order
coefficients) and its first derivative at x in a single Horner pass, returning
(value, derivative).

funcs.name: PolyIntegral
funcs.doc:
PolyIntegral returns the coefficients (ascending order) of an antiderivative
of the polynomial given by coeffs, using constant as the value of the
integration constant (the new degree-zero term).

funcs.name: PolyRoots
funcs.doc:
PolyRoots returns the real roots of the polynomial given by coeffs (ascending
order) in ascending order. A root is considered real when the magnitude of its
imaginary part is within tol (scaled by the root magnitude) of zero.

funcs.name: ProjectBox
funcs.doc:
ProjectBox returns a copy of x projected onto the axis-aligned box defined by
the per-coordinate bounds lo and hi. It panics if the lengths differ.

funcs.name: QuadraticRealRoots
funcs.doc:
QuadraticRealRoots returns the distinct real roots of a*x^2 + b*x + c = 0 in
ascending order. Complex roots are omitted, so the result has length 0, 1 or
2.

funcs.name: QuadraticRoots
funcs.signature: func QuadraticRoots(a, b, c float64) (complex128, complex128)
funcs.doc:
QuadraticRoots returns both roots of the quadratic a*x^2 + b*x + c = 0 as
complex numbers, using a numerically stable formulation that avoids
cancellation. When a is zero it degenerates to the linear (or empty) case.

funcs.name: QuarticRoots
funcs.signature: func QuarticRoots(a, b, c, d, e float64) []complex128
funcs.doc:
QuarticRoots returns all four roots of the quartic
a*x^4 + b*x^3 + c*x^2 + d*x + e = 0 as complex numbers, computed with the
Durand-Kerner iteration.

funcs.name: RegulaFalsi
funcs.signature: func RegulaFalsi(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
RegulaFalsi (the method of false position) finds a root of f in [a, b] by
linear interpolation between the bracketing endpoints. It keeps the root
bracketed at all times but can converge slowly when one endpoint stagnates;
see Illinois, Pegasus and AndersonBjorck for accelerated variants.

funcs.name: RelativeError
funcs.doc:
RelativeError returns the relative error of an approximation with respect to an
exact value. When exact is zero it falls back to the absolute magnitude of the
approximation.

funcs.name: RichardsonDerivative
funcs.signature: func RichardsonDerivative(f Func, x, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RichardsonDerivative approximates f'(x) by Richardson extrapolation of the
central difference at step sizes h and h/2, cancelling the leading error term
to yield a fourth-order accurate estimate.

funcs.name: Ridders
funcs.signature: func Ridders(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Ridders finds a root of f in [a, b] using Ridders' method, which fits an
exponential to the two endpoints and their midpoint to obtain a superlinearly
convergent, always-bracketed iterate. It is robust and requires only two
function evaluations per step.

funcs.name: Rosenbrock
funcs.doc:
Rosenbrock is the classic non-convex test objective
sum_i [100*(x_{i+1} - x_i^2)^2 + (1 - x_i)^2], with its global minimum value
0 at the all-ones point. It requires at least two coordinates.

funcs.name: RosenbrockGrad
funcs.doc: RosenbrockGrad returns the exact gradient of Rosenbrock at x.

funcs.name: SameSign
funcs.doc:
SameSign reports whether a and b are both strictly positive or both strictly
negative.

funcs.name: Schroeder
funcs.signature: func Schroeder(f, df Func, x0 float64, m int, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Schroeder finds a root of known multiplicity m using the modified Newton (or
Schröder) iteration x -= m*f/df, which restores quadratic convergence at a
root where the ordinary Newton method would degrade to linear convergence.
Passing m = 1 recovers the standard Newton method.

funcs.name: Secant
funcs.signature: func Secant(f Func, x0, x1, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Secant finds a root of f using the secant method, starting from two initial
guesses x0 and x1. It approximates the derivative by a finite difference of
successive iterates and converges super-linearly (order ≈ 1.618) near a
simple root. It returns ErrZeroDerivative if two successive function values
coincide.

funcs.name: SecondDerivative
funcs.signature: func SecondDerivative(f Func, x, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
SecondDerivative approximates f”(x) by the central difference
(f(x+h)-2f(x)+f(x-h))/h^2, which has second-order accuracy in h.

funcs.name: Sign
funcs.doc: Sign returns +1, -1 or 0 according to the sign of x.

funcs.name: SignChange
funcs.signature: func SignChange(f Func, a, b float64) bool
funcs.doc:
SignChange reports whether f takes opposite-signed values at a and b, i.e.
whether [a, b] is guaranteed (for continuous f) to contain a root.

funcs.name: SolveLinearSystem
funcs.doc:
SolveLinearSystem solves the linear system a*x = b for x using Gaussian
elimination with partial pivoting. The inputs are left unmodified. It returns
ErrSingularMatrix if the matrix is numerically singular and
ErrDimensionMismatch if the shapes are inconsistent.

funcs.name: Sphere
funcs.doc:
Sphere is the separable convex test objective sum_i x_i^2, with its unique
minimum value 0 at the origin.

funcs.name: SphereGrad
funcs.doc: SphereGrad returns the exact gradient 2x of Sphere at x.

funcs.name: Steffensen
funcs.signature: func Steffensen(f Func, x0, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Steffensen finds a root of f using Steffensen's method, a derivative-free
iteration that achieves quadratic convergence by estimating the local slope
from f(x) and f(x+f(x)). It needs only a single starting guess x0.

funcs.name: TernarySearch
funcs.signature: func TernarySearch(f Func, a, b, tol float64, maxIter int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
TernarySearch returns the location of a minimum of the unimodal function f on
[a, b] using ternary search, which discards one outer third of the interval at
each step.

funcs.name: VecAdd
funcs.doc: VecAdd returns the element-wise sum a + b. It panics if the lengths differ.

funcs.name: VecAxpy
funcs.doc:
VecAxpy returns the combination s*x + y (the classic "a x plus y"). It panics
if the lengths differ.

funcs.name: VecClamp
funcs.doc:
VecClamp returns a copy of x with every element confined to the scalar range
[lo, hi].

funcs.name: VecCopy
funcs.doc: VecCopy returns a fresh copy of x.

funcs.name: VecDistance
funcs.doc: VecDistance returns the Euclidean distance between a and b.

funcs.name: VecDot
funcs.doc:
VecDot returns the Euclidean inner product a . b. It panics if the lengths
differ.

funcs.name: VecFill
funcs.doc: VecFill returns a new vector of length n with every element set to v.

funcs.name: VecInfNorm
funcs.doc: VecInfNorm returns the maximum-absolute-value (L-infinity) norm of a.

funcs.name: VecLinComb
funcs.signature: func VecLinComb(a float64, x []float64, b float64, y []float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
VecLinComb returns the linear combination a*x + b*y. It panics if the lengths
differ.

funcs.name: VecNegate
funcs.doc: VecNegate returns -a.

funcs.name: VecNorm
funcs.doc: VecNorm returns the Euclidean (L2) norm |a|.

funcs.name: VecNormSquared
funcs.doc: VecNormSquared returns the squared Euclidean norm |a|^2.

funcs.name: VecScale
funcs.doc: VecScale returns the vector s*a.

funcs.name: VecSub
funcs.doc:
VecSub returns the element-wise difference a - b. It panics if the lengths
differ.

funcs.name: VecZeros
funcs.doc: VecZeros returns a new zero vector of length n.

funcs.name: WithinTolerance
funcs.signature: func WithinTolerance(a, b, tol float64) bool
funcs.doc:
WithinTolerance reports whether a and b differ by at most tol in absolute
value.

packages.name: orthopoly
packages.synopsis: Package orthopoly implements the classical families of orthogonal polynomials together with the tools needed to work with them: pointwise evaluation via numerically stable three-term recurrences, derivatives, weight functions, squared norms, the roots (zeros) of each family, and the Gaussian quadrature nodes and weights derived from those roots.
packages.doc:
Package orthopoly implements the classical families of orthogonal
polynomials together with the tools needed to work with them: pointwise
evaluation via numerically stable three-term recurrences, derivatives,
weight functions, squared norms, the roots (zeros) of each family, and the
Gaussian quadrature nodes and weights derived from those roots.

The families covered are Legendre, Chebyshev (first, second, third and
fourth kinds), Hermite (both the physicists' Hn and the probabilists' Hen
conventions), Laguerre and generalized (associated) Laguerre, along with the
Gegenbauer (ultraspherical) and Jacobi polynomials that unify them.

Evaluation routines are built on the forward recurrences for each family and
are accurate across the natural domain of the polynomial. Roots and Gaussian
quadrature nodes are computed either in closed form (Chebyshev) or by
Newton's method with well-conditioned starting estimates (Legendre, Hermite,
Laguerre). All routines use only the Go standard library and are
deterministic.

types.name: QuadratureRule
types.doc:
QuadratureRule bundles a set of Gaussian quadrature nodes with their weights
so that a weighted integral can be approximated as
sum_i Weights[i] f(Nodes[i]). The weight function is implied by the family
that produced the rule.

funcs.name: NewGaussHermiteRule
funcs.doc:
NewGaussHermiteRule returns the n-point Gauss-Hermite rule for weight
exp(-x^2) on the real line. n must be positive.

funcs.name: NewGaussLaguerreRule
funcs.doc:
NewGaussLaguerreRule returns the n-point Gauss-Laguerre rule for weight
exp(-x) on [0, ∞). n must be positive.

funcs.name: NewGaussLegendreRule
funcs.doc:
NewGaussLegendreRule returns the n-point Gauss-Legendre rule for unit weight
on [-1, 1]. The rule integrates polynomials up to degree 2n-1 exactly.
n must be positive.

funcs.name: NewGeneralizedLaguerreRule
funcs.signature: func NewGeneralizedLaguerreRule(n int, alpha float64) QuadratureRule
funcs.doc:
NewGeneralizedLaguerreRule returns the n-point generalized Gauss-Laguerre
rule for weight x^alpha exp(-x) on [0, ∞). alpha must exceed -1 and n must be
positive.

methods.name: Integrate
methods.signature: func (q QuadratureRule) Integrate(f func(float64) float64) float64
methods.doc:
Integrate approximates the weighted integral of f by evaluating f at each node
and forming the weighted sum sum_i Weights[i] f(Nodes[i]).

methods.name: Order
methods.doc: Order returns the number of nodes in the rule.

funcs.name: AssociatedLaguerre
funcs.signature: func AssociatedLaguerre(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AssociatedLaguerre evaluates the generalized Laguerre polynomial L_n^alpha(x)
using the recurrence
(n+1)*L_{n+1}^alpha = (2n+1+alpha-x)*L_n^alpha - (n+alpha)*L_{n-1}^alpha,
orthogonal with weight x^alpha*exp(-x) on [0, inf).

funcs.name: AssociatedLaguerreDerivative
funcs.signature: func AssociatedLaguerreDerivative(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc: AssociatedLaguerreDerivative returns d/dx L_n^alpha(x) = -L_{n-1}^{alpha+1}(x).

funcs.name: AssociatedLegendre
funcs.signature: func AssociatedLegendre(l, m int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AssociatedLegendre evaluates the associated Legendre function P_l^m(x) for
-l <= m <= l on the interval [-1, 1], including the Condon-Shortley phase
(-1)^m. Negative orders use the identity
P_l^{-m} = (-1)^m*(l-m)!/(l+m)!*P_l^m.

funcs.name: BernoulliNumber
funcs.doc:
BernoulliNumber returns the n-th Bernoulli number B_n in the convention with
B_1 = -1/2, computed from the standard recurrence.

funcs.name: BernoulliPolynomial
funcs.signature: func BernoulliPolynomial(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BernoulliPolynomial evaluates the Bernoulli polynomial B_n(x) via
B_n(x) = sum_{k=0}^n C(n,k)*B_{n-k}*x^k, where B_j are the Bernoulli numbers.

funcs.name: Bernstein
funcs.signature: func Bernstein(i, n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Bernstein evaluates the Bernstein basis polynomial
b_{i,n}(x) = C(n,i)*x^i*(1-x)^(n-i) for 0 <= i <= n.

funcs.name: BernsteinBasis
funcs.doc:
BernsteinBasis returns the full degree-n Bernstein basis
[b_{0,n}(x), ..., b_{n,n}(x)] evaluated at x.

funcs.name: BernsteinBezier
funcs.doc:
BernsteinBezier evaluates the Bezier curve with the given control ordinates at
parameter x in [0, 1] using the numerically stable de Casteljau algorithm.
The polynomial degree equals len(coeffs)-1.

funcs.name: BernsteinDerivative
funcs.signature: func BernsteinDerivative(i, n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
BernsteinDerivative returns the derivative of the Bernstein basis polynomial
b_{i,n}'(x) = n*(b_{i-1,n-1}(x) - b_{i,n-1}(x)).

funcs.name: ChebyshevFirst
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevFirst(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFirst returns the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind T_n(x),
which satisfies T_n(cos θ) = cos(nθ) and is orthogonal on [-1, 1] with weight
1/sqrt(1-x^2). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevFirstDerivative
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevFirstDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFirstDerivative returns the derivative T_n'(x) = n U_(n-1)(x), where
U is the Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevFirstExtrema
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFirstExtrema returns the n+1 extrema of T_n on [-1, 1],
x_k = cos(kπ/n) for k = 0..n, in ascending order. These are the
Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points. n must be positive.

funcs.name: ChebyshevFirstNorm
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFirstNorm returns the squared weighted L2 norm of T_n on [-1, 1],
namely π for n = 0 and π/2 for n > 0.

funcs.name: ChebyshevFirstRoots
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFirstRoots returns the n roots of T_n, x_k = cos((2k-1)π/(2n)), in
ascending order. These are the Chebyshev-Gauss nodes. n must be positive.

funcs.name: ChebyshevFirstValues
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFirstValues returns the slice [T_0(x), ..., T_n(x)] of first-kind
Chebyshev polynomials up to degree n. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevFirstWeight
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFirstWeight returns the orthogonality weight of the first-kind
Chebyshev polynomials, 1/sqrt(1-x^2), for x in (-1, 1). It returns +Inf at
x = ±1.

funcs.name: ChebyshevFourth
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevFourth(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevFourth returns the Chebyshev polynomial of the fourth kind W_n(x),
for which W_n(cos θ) = sin((n+1/2)θ)/sin(θ/2). It is orthogonal on [-1, 1]
with weight sqrt((1-x)/(1+x)). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevSecond
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevSecond(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevSecond returns the Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind U_n(x),
which satisfies U_n(cos θ) sin θ = sin((n+1)θ) and is orthogonal on [-1, 1]
with weight sqrt(1-x^2). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevSecondDerivative
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevSecondDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevSecondDerivative returns the derivative U_n'(x). The endpoints
x = ±1, where U_n'(±1) = (±1)^(n+1) n(n+1)(n+2)/3, are handled in closed
form. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevSecondNorm
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevSecondNorm returns the squared weighted L2 norm of U_n on [-1, 1],
which is π/2 for every n >= 0.

funcs.name: ChebyshevSecondRoots
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevSecondRoots returns the n roots of U_n, x_k = cos(kπ/(n+1)) for
k = 1..n, in ascending order. n must be positive.

funcs.name: ChebyshevSecondValues
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevSecondValues returns the slice [U_0(x), ..., U_n(x)] of second-kind
Chebyshev polynomials up to degree n. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevSecondWeight
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevSecondWeight returns the orthogonality weight of the second-kind
Chebyshev polynomials, sqrt(1-x^2), for x in [-1, 1].

funcs.name: ChebyshevT
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevT(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevT evaluates the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind T_n(x) using
the recurrence T_{n+1} = 2*x*T_n - T_{n-1}.

funcs.name: ChebyshevTBasis
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevTBasis returns the slice [T_0(x), T_1(x), ..., T_n(x)] evaluated in a
single pass of the recurrence.

funcs.name: ChebyshevTDerivative
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevTDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc: ChebyshevTDerivative returns T_n'(x) via the identity T_n'(x) = n*U_{n-1}(x).

funcs.name: ChebyshevTExtrema
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevTExtrema returns the n+1 Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points
x_k = cos(k*pi/n) for k = 0..n, the extrema of T_n, sorted in increasing
order.

funcs.name: ChebyshevTRoots
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevTRoots returns the n roots of T_n, x_k = cos((2k-1)*pi/(2n)) for
k = 1..n, sorted in increasing order.

funcs.name: ChebyshevTWeight
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevTWeight returns the weight 1/sqrt(1-x^2) for which the Chebyshev
polynomials of the first kind are orthogonal on (-1, 1).

funcs.name: ChebyshevThird
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevThird(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevThird returns the Chebyshev polynomial of the third kind V_n(x), for
which V_n(cos θ) = cos((n+1/2)θ)/cos(θ/2). It is orthogonal on [-1, 1] with
weight sqrt((1+x)/(1-x)). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: ChebyshevU
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevU(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevU evaluates the Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind U_n(x) using
the recurrence U_{n+1} = 2*x*U_n - U_{n-1}, with U_0 = 1 and U_1 = 2*x.

funcs.name: ChebyshevUDerivative
funcs.signature: func ChebyshevUDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevUDerivative returns U_n'(x). Interior points use
U_n'(x) = ((n+1)*T_{n+1}(x) - x*U_n(x))/(x^2-1); the endpoints x = +/-1 use
the closed forms U_n'(1) = n(n+1)(n+2)/3 and
U_n'(-1) = (-1)^(n+1)*n(n+1)(n+2)/3.

funcs.name: ChebyshevUWeight
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevUWeight returns the weight sqrt(1-x^2) for which the Chebyshev
polynomials of the second kind are orthogonal on [-1, 1].

funcs.name: ClenshawChebyshevFirst
funcs.doc:
ClenshawChebyshevFirst evaluates the Chebyshev series sum_{k} c[k] T_k(x)
using Clenshaw's recurrence, which is more stable than summing evaluated
polynomials. An empty coefficient slice yields 0.

funcs.name: EulerNumber
funcs.doc:
EulerNumber returns the n-th Euler number E_n = 2^n*E_n(1/2). The odd-indexed
Euler numbers are zero.

funcs.name: EulerPolynomial
funcs.signature: func EulerPolynomial(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
EulerPolynomial evaluates the Euler polynomial E_n(x) using its relation to
the Bernoulli polynomials,
E_n(x) = 2/(n+1) * (B_{n+1}(x) - 2^{n+1}*B_{n+1}(x/2)).

funcs.name: GaussChebyshevT
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshevT returns the n-point Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature of the first
kind on [-1, 1] with weight 1/sqrt(1-x^2). Nodes are x_k = cos((2k-1)*pi/(2n))
and every weight equals pi/n. The nodes are returned in increasing order.

funcs.name: GaussChebyshevU
funcs.doc:
GaussChebyshevU returns the n-point Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature of the second
kind on [-1, 1] with weight sqrt(1-x^2). Nodes are x_k = cos(k*pi/(n+1)) with
weights pi/(n+1)*sin^2(k*pi/(n+1)). The nodes are returned in increasing
order.

funcs.name: GaussGegenbauer
funcs.signature: func GaussGegenbauer(n int, alpha float64) (nodes, weights []float64)
funcs.doc:
GaussGegenbauer returns the n-point Gauss-Gegenbauer quadrature nodes and
weights on [-1, 1] with weight (1-x^2)^(alpha-1/2), for alpha > -1/2. It is
the symmetric Gauss-Jacobi rule with a = b = alpha - 1/2.

funcs.name: GaussHermite
funcs.doc:
GaussHermite returns the n-point Gauss-Hermite quadrature nodes and weights on
(-inf, inf) with weight exp(-x^2). The rule integrates polynomials up to
degree 2n-1 exactly.

funcs.name: GaussHermiteIntegrate
funcs.signature: func GaussHermiteIntegrate(f func(float64) float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussHermiteIntegrate approximates the integral of f(x)*exp(-x^2) over the
whole real line using the n-point Gauss-Hermite rule. f should be the factor
multiplying the Gaussian weight.

funcs.name: GaussJacobi
funcs.signature: func GaussJacobi(n int, a, b float64) (nodes, weights []float64)
funcs.doc:
GaussJacobi returns the n-point Gauss-Jacobi quadrature nodes and weights on
[-1, 1] with weight (1-x)^a*(1+x)^b, for a > -1 and b > -1.

funcs.name: GaussLaguerre
funcs.doc:
GaussLaguerre returns the n-point Gauss-Laguerre quadrature nodes and weights
on [0, inf) with weight exp(-x). The rule integrates polynomials up to degree
2n-1 exactly.

funcs.name: GaussLaguerreGeneralized
funcs.signature: func GaussLaguerreGeneralized(n int, alpha float64) (nodes, weights []float64)
funcs.doc:
GaussLaguerreGeneralized returns the n-point generalized Gauss-Laguerre
quadrature nodes and weights on [0, inf) with weight x^alpha*exp(-x), for
alpha > -1.

funcs.name: GaussLaguerreIntegrate
funcs.signature: func GaussLaguerreIntegrate(f func(float64) float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLaguerreIntegrate approximates the integral of f(x)*exp(-x) over
[0, inf) using the n-point Gauss-Laguerre rule. f should be the factor
multiplying the exponential weight.

funcs.name: GaussLegendre
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendre returns the n-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature nodes and weights
on [-1, 1] (weight function 1). The rule integrates polynomials up to degree
2n-1 exactly.

funcs.name: GaussLegendreIntegrate
funcs.signature: func GaussLegendreIntegrate(f func(float64) float64, lo, hi float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
GaussLegendreIntegrate approximates the integral of f over [lo, hi] using the
n-point Gauss-Legendre rule, mapping the standard interval [-1, 1] to
[lo, hi] with the affine change of variables.

funcs.name: Gegenbauer
funcs.signature: func Gegenbauer(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Gegenbauer evaluates the ultraspherical (Gegenbauer) polynomial C_n^alpha(x)
using the stable three-term recurrence

	n*C_n = 2*(n+alpha-1)*x*C_{n-1} - (n+2*alpha-2)*C_{n-2},

with C_0 = 1 and C_1 = 2*alpha*x.

funcs.name: GegenbauerC
funcs.signature: func GegenbauerC(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GegenbauerC returns the Gegenbauer (ultraspherical) polynomial C_n^(alpha)(x),
orthogonal on [-1, 1] with weight (1-x^2)^(alpha-1/2). It generalizes the
Legendre (alpha = 1/2) and second-kind Chebyshev (alpha = 1) polynomials and
satisfies n C_n = 2(n+alpha-1) x C_(n-1) - (n+2alpha-2) C_(n-2). alpha should
exceed -1/2 and n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: GegenbauerCDerivative
funcs.signature: func GegenbauerCDerivative(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GegenbauerCDerivative returns the derivative
d/dx C_n^(alpha)(x) = 2 alpha C_(n-1)^(alpha+1)(x). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: GegenbauerCValues
funcs.signature: func GegenbauerCValues(n int, alpha, x float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
GegenbauerCValues returns the slice [C_0^(alpha)(x), ..., C_n^(alpha)(x)] up
to degree n. alpha should exceed -1/2 and n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: GegenbauerDerivative
funcs.signature: func GegenbauerDerivative(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GegenbauerDerivative returns the derivative d/dx C_n^alpha(x), using the
identity d/dx C_n^alpha(x) = 2*alpha*C_{n-1}^{alpha+1}(x).

funcs.name: GegenbauerNorm
funcs.signature: func GegenbauerNorm(n int, alpha float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GegenbauerNorm returns the squared L2 norm of C_n^alpha under the Gegenbauer
weight, that is the value of the integral of (1-x^2)^(alpha-1/2)*C_n^alpha(x)^2
over [-1, 1]. It is valid for alpha > -1/2 and alpha != 0.

funcs.name: GegenbauerWeight
funcs.doc:
GegenbauerWeight returns the weight function (1-x^2)^(alpha-1/2) for which the
Gegenbauer polynomials are orthogonal on [-1, 1].

funcs.name: GeneralizedLaguerreL
funcs.signature: func GeneralizedLaguerreL(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedLaguerreL returns the generalized (associated) Laguerre polynomial
L_n^(alpha)(x), orthogonal on [0, ∞) with weight x^alpha exp(-x). It satisfies
(n+1) L_(n+1) = (2n+1+alpha-x) L_n - (n+alpha) L_(n-1). alpha must exceed -1
and n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: GeneralizedLaguerreLDerivative
funcs.signature: func GeneralizedLaguerreLDerivative(n int, alpha, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedLaguerreLDerivative returns the derivative
d/dx L_n^(alpha)(x) = -L_(n-1)^(alpha+1)(x). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: GeneralizedLaguerreLValues
funcs.signature: func GeneralizedLaguerreLValues(n int, alpha, x float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedLaguerreLValues returns the slice [L_0^(alpha)(x), ...,
L_n^(alpha)(x)] up to degree n. alpha must exceed -1 and n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: GeneralizedLaguerreNorm
funcs.signature: func GeneralizedLaguerreNorm(n int, alpha float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedLaguerreNorm returns the squared weighted L2 norm of L_n^(alpha)
on [0, ∞), namely Γ(n+alpha+1)/n!.

funcs.name: GeneralizedLaguerreRoots
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedLaguerreRoots returns the n roots of L_n^(alpha) in ascending
order. alpha must exceed -1 and n must be positive.

funcs.name: GeneralizedLaguerreWeight
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedLaguerreWeight returns the orthogonality weight x^alpha exp(-x) of
the generalized Laguerre polynomials, for x >= 0.

funcs.name: GeneralizedLaguerreWeights
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedLaguerreWeights returns the n generalized Gauss-Laguerre weights
corresponding to GeneralizedLaguerreRoots(n, alpha). alpha must exceed -1 and
n must be positive.

funcs.name: GolubWelsch
funcs.signature: func GolubWelsch(alpha, beta []float64, mu0 float64) (nodes, weights []float64)
funcs.doc:
GolubWelsch computes Gauss quadrature nodes and weights from the monic
three-term recurrence of an orthogonal polynomial family via the
Golub-Welsch algorithm. alpha holds the n diagonal recurrence coefficients
(a_0..a_{n-1}); beta holds the n subdiagonal coefficients where beta[i], for
i >= 1, is the monic recurrence coefficient beta_i (beta[0] is ignored). mu0
is the zeroth moment (the integral of the weight function). The returned
nodes are sorted in increasing order.

funcs.name: HermiteFunction
funcs.signature: func HermiteFunction(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermiteFunction returns the Hermite function psi_n(x) =
exp(-x^2/2) H_n(x) / sqrt(2^n n! sqrt(π)), the orthonormal eigenfunctions of
the quantum harmonic oscillator. It is evaluated stably via the normalized
recurrence, avoiding overflow of H_n for large x. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: HermiteH
funcs.signature: func HermiteH(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermiteH returns the physicists' Hermite polynomial H_n(x), orthogonal on
the real line with weight exp(-x^2) and satisfying the recurrence
H_(n+1) = 2x H_n - 2n H_(n-1). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: HermiteHDerivative
funcs.signature: func HermiteHDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermiteHDerivative returns the derivative H_n'(x) = 2n H_(n-1)(x).
n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: HermiteHNorm
funcs.doc:
HermiteHNorm returns the squared weighted L2 norm of H_n over the real line,
namely sqrt(π) 2^n n!.

funcs.name: HermiteHRoots
funcs.doc:
HermiteHRoots returns the n roots of the physicists' Hermite polynomial H_n
in ascending order (the Gauss-Hermite nodes). n must be positive.

funcs.name: HermiteHValues
funcs.doc:
HermiteHValues returns the slice [H_0(x), ..., H_n(x)] of physicists' Hermite
polynomials up to degree n. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: HermiteHWeight
funcs.doc:
HermiteHWeight returns the orthogonality weight exp(-x^2) of the physicists'
Hermite polynomials.

funcs.name: HermiteHWeights
funcs.doc:
HermiteHWeights returns the n Gauss-Hermite quadrature weights corresponding
to HermiteHRoots(n). n must be positive.

funcs.name: HermiteHe
funcs.signature: func HermiteHe(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermiteHe returns the probabilists' Hermite polynomial He_n(x), orthogonal
on the real line with weight exp(-x^2/2) and satisfying the recurrence
He_(n+1) = x He_n - n He_(n-1). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: HermiteHeDerivative
funcs.signature: func HermiteHeDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermiteHeDerivative returns the derivative He_n'(x) = n He_(n-1)(x).
n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: HermiteHeNorm
funcs.doc:
HermiteHeNorm returns the squared weighted L2 norm of He_n over the real
line, namely sqrt(2π) n!.

funcs.name: HermiteHeRoots
funcs.doc:
HermiteHeRoots returns the n roots of the probabilists' Hermite polynomial
He_n in ascending order. Because He_n(x) = 2^(-n/2) H_n(x/sqrt(2)), its roots
are the Gauss-Hermite nodes scaled by sqrt(2). n must be positive.

funcs.name: HermiteHeValues
funcs.doc:
HermiteHeValues returns the slice [He_0(x), ..., He_n(x)] of probabilists'
Hermite polynomials up to degree n. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: HermiteHeWeight
funcs.doc:
HermiteHeWeight returns the orthogonality weight exp(-x^2/2) of the
probabilists' Hermite polynomials.

funcs.name: HermitePhysicists
funcs.signature: func HermitePhysicists(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermitePhysicists evaluates the physicists' Hermite polynomial H_n(x) with the
recurrence H_{n+1} = 2*x*H_n - 2*n*H_{n-1}, orthogonal with weight exp(-x^2).

funcs.name: HermitePhysicistsBasis
funcs.doc:
HermitePhysicistsBasis returns [H_0(x), H_1(x), ..., H_n(x)] evaluated in a
single pass of the recurrence.

funcs.name: HermitePhysicistsDerivative
funcs.signature: func HermitePhysicistsDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc: HermitePhysicistsDerivative returns H_n'(x) = 2*n*H_{n-1}(x).

funcs.name: HermitePhysicistsNorm
funcs.doc:
HermitePhysicistsNorm returns the squared L2 norm of H_n against exp(-x^2),
namely sqrt(pi)*2^n*n!.

funcs.name: HermiteProbabilists
funcs.signature: func HermiteProbabilists(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HermiteProbabilists evaluates the probabilists' Hermite polynomial He_n(x)
with the recurrence He_{n+1} = x*He_n - n*He_{n-1}, orthogonal with weight
exp(-x^2/2).

funcs.name: HermiteProbabilistsDerivative
funcs.signature: func HermiteProbabilistsDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc: HermiteProbabilistsDerivative returns He_n'(x) = n*He_{n-1}(x).

funcs.name: HermiteWeight
funcs.doc:
HermiteWeight returns the weight exp(-x^2) for which the physicists' Hermite
polynomials are orthogonal on the whole real line.

funcs.name: Jacobi
funcs.signature: func Jacobi(n int, a, b, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Jacobi evaluates the Jacobi polynomial P_n^{a,b}(x) on [-1, 1] using the
classical three-term recurrence. The parameters a and b must be greater than
-1 for the polynomials to be orthogonal, but the recurrence is evaluated for
any real a, b.

funcs.name: JacobiDerivative
funcs.signature: func JacobiDerivative(n int, a, b, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
JacobiDerivative returns the derivative d/dx P_n^{a,b}(x), using the identity
d/dx P_n^{a,b}(x) = (n+a+b+1)/2 * P_{n-1}^{a+1,b+1}(x).

funcs.name: JacobiNorm
funcs.signature: func JacobiNorm(n int, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
JacobiNorm returns the squared L2 norm of P_n^{a,b} under the Jacobi weight,
i.e. the integral of (1-x)^a*(1+x)^b*P_n^{a,b}(x)^2 over [-1, 1]. Valid for
a > -1 and b > -1.

funcs.name: JacobiP
funcs.signature: func JacobiP(n int, a, b, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
JacobiP returns the Jacobi polynomial P_n^(a,b)(x), orthogonal on [-1, 1]
with weight (1-x)^a (1+x)^b. It generalizes the Legendre (a = b = 0),
Chebyshev, Gegenbauer and Laguerre limits. Both a and b must exceed -1 and n
must be non-negative.

funcs.name: JacobiPDerivative
funcs.signature: func JacobiPDerivative(n int, a, b, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
JacobiPDerivative returns the derivative
d/dx P_n^(a,b)(x) = (n+a+b+1)/2 * P_(n-1)^(a+1,b+1)(x). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: JacobiPValues
funcs.signature: func JacobiPValues(n int, a, b, x float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
JacobiPValues returns the slice [P_0^(a,b)(x), ..., P_n^(a,b)(x)] up to degree
n. Both a and b must exceed -1 and n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: JacobiWeight
funcs.signature: func JacobiWeight(a, b, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
JacobiWeight returns the weight function (1-x)^a*(1+x)^b for which the Jacobi
polynomials are orthogonal on [-1, 1].

funcs.name: Laguerre
funcs.signature: func Laguerre(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Laguerre evaluates the Laguerre polynomial L_n(x) using the recurrence
(n+1)*L_{n+1} = (2n+1-x)*L_n - n*L_{n-1}, orthogonal with weight exp(-x) on
[0, inf).

funcs.name: LaguerreBasis
funcs.doc:
LaguerreBasis returns [L_0(x), L_1(x), ..., L_n(x)] evaluated in a single pass
of the recurrence.

funcs.name: LaguerreDerivative
funcs.signature: func LaguerreDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc: LaguerreDerivative returns L_n'(x) = -L_{n-1}^1(x).

funcs.name: LaguerreL
funcs.signature: func LaguerreL(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LaguerreL returns the (ordinary) Laguerre polynomial L_n(x) = L_n^(0)(x),
orthogonal on [0, ∞) with weight exp(-x). n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LaguerreLDerivative
funcs.signature: func LaguerreLDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LaguerreLDerivative returns the derivative L_n'(x) = -L_(n-1)^(1)(x) of the
ordinary Laguerre polynomial. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LaguerreLValues
funcs.doc:
LaguerreLValues returns the slice [L_0(x), ..., L_n(x)] of ordinary Laguerre
polynomials up to degree n. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LaguerreNorm
funcs.doc:
LaguerreNorm returns the squared weighted L2 norm of L_n on [0, ∞), which is
1 for every n >= 0.

funcs.name: LaguerreRoots
funcs.doc:
LaguerreRoots returns the n roots of the ordinary Laguerre polynomial L_n in
ascending order (the Gauss-Laguerre nodes). n must be positive.

funcs.name: LaguerreWeight
funcs.doc:
