LaguerreWeight returns the weight x^alpha*exp(-x) for which the generalized
Laguerre polynomials are orthogonal on [0, inf).

funcs.name: LaguerreWeightFunction
funcs.doc:
LaguerreWeightFunction returns the orthogonality weight exp(-x) of the
ordinary Laguerre polynomials, for x >= 0.

funcs.name: LaguerreWeights
funcs.doc:
LaguerreWeights returns the n Gauss-Laguerre quadrature weights corresponding
to LaguerreRoots(n). n must be positive.

funcs.name: Legendre
funcs.signature: func Legendre(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Legendre evaluates the Legendre polynomial P_n(x) using Bonnet's recurrence
(n+1)*P_{n+1} = (2n+1)*x*P_n - n*P_{n-1}.

funcs.name: LegendreBasis
funcs.doc:
LegendreBasis returns the slice [P_0(x), P_1(x), ..., P_n(x)] evaluated in a
single stable pass of Bonnet's recurrence.

funcs.name: LegendreDerivative
funcs.signature: func LegendreDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LegendreDerivative returns the derivative P_n'(x). Interior points use the
identity (x^2-1)*P_n'(x) = n*(x*P_n(x) - P_{n-1}(x)); the endpoints x = +/-1
use the closed forms P_n'(1) = n(n+1)/2 and P_n'(-1) = (-1)^(n-1)*n(n+1)/2.

funcs.name: LegendreNorm
funcs.doc: LegendreNorm returns the squared L2 norm of P_n on [-1, 1], namely 2/(2n+1).

funcs.name: LegendreP
funcs.signature: func LegendreP(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LegendreP returns the Legendre polynomial P_n(x). The P_n are orthogonal on
[-1, 1] with unit weight. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LegendrePDerivative
funcs.signature: func LegendrePDerivative(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LegendrePDerivative returns the derivative P_n'(x) of the Legendre polynomial.
The endpoints x = ±1, where the closed form P_n'(±1) = (±1)^(n+1) n(n+1)/2
applies, are handled without cancellation. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LegendrePNorm
funcs.doc:
LegendrePNorm returns the squared L2 norm of P_n on [-1, 1], namely
integral of P_n(x)^2 dx = 2/(2n+1).

funcs.name: LegendrePValues
funcs.doc:
LegendrePValues returns the slice [P_0(x), P_1(x), ..., P_n(x)] of Legendre
polynomials up to degree n. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LegendreRoots
funcs.doc:
LegendreRoots returns the n roots of P_n(x), which coincide with the
Gauss-Legendre quadrature nodes on [-1, 1], sorted in increasing order.

funcs.name: LegendreWeight
funcs.doc:
LegendreWeight returns the (constant) weight function 1 for which the
Legendre polynomials are orthogonal on [-1, 1]. It is provided for API
symmetry with the other weighted families.

funcs.name: LegendreWeights
funcs.doc:
LegendreWeights returns the n Gauss-Legendre quadrature weights corresponding
to LegendreZeros(n). n must be positive.

funcs.name: LegendreZeros
funcs.doc:
LegendreZeros returns the n roots of the Legendre polynomial P_n in ascending
order (the Gauss-Legendre nodes). n must be positive.

funcs.name: NollToNM
funcs.doc:
NollToNM converts a 1-based Noll single index j to the Zernike degree n and
signed order m. Noll indexing assigns j = 1 to piston, j = 2, 3 to tilt, and
so on.

funcs.name: NormalizedAssociatedLegendre
funcs.signature: func NormalizedAssociatedLegendre(l, m int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NormalizedAssociatedLegendre returns the fully normalized associated Legendre
function, sqrt((2l+1)/2 * (l-|m|)!/(l+|m|)!) * P_l^{|m|}(x), whose square
integrates to 1 over [-1, 1] against the Legendre weight.

funcs.name: NormalizedHermiteH
funcs.signature: func NormalizedHermiteH(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NormalizedHermiteH returns the L2-orthonormal physicists' Hermite coefficient
H_n(x)/sqrt(sqrt(π) 2^n n!).

funcs.name: NormalizedHermiteHe
funcs.signature: func NormalizedHermiteHe(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NormalizedHermiteHe returns the L2-orthonormal probabilists' Hermite
coefficient He_n(x)/sqrt(sqrt(2π) n!).

funcs.name: NormalizedLegendreP
funcs.signature: func NormalizedLegendreP(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
NormalizedLegendreP returns the L2-orthonormal Legendre polynomial
sqrt((2n+1)/2) P_n(x), whose square integrates to 1 over [-1, 1].

funcs.name: RealSphericalHarmonic
funcs.signature: func RealSphericalHarmonic(l, m int, theta, phi float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RealSphericalHarmonic evaluates the real spherical harmonic Y_l^m(theta, phi),
where theta is the polar (colatitude) angle in [0, pi] and phi the azimuthal
angle. The real basis is defined without the Condon-Shortley phase as

	m > 0:  sqrt(2)*N_l^m*cos(m*phi)*P_l^m(cos theta),
	m = 0:  N_l^0*P_l^0(cos theta),
	m < 0:  sqrt(2)*N_l^{|m|}*sin(|m|*phi)*P_l^{|m|}(cos theta),

with N_l^m the SphericalHarmonicNormalization and P_l^m the associated
Legendre function taken without the (-1)^m phase.

funcs.name: ShiftedChebyshevFirst
funcs.signature: func ShiftedChebyshevFirst(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ShiftedChebyshevFirst returns the shifted first-kind Chebyshev polynomial
T_n(2x-1), orthogonal on [0, 1] with weight 1/sqrt(x-x^2). n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: ShiftedLegendreP
funcs.signature: func ShiftedLegendreP(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ShiftedLegendreP returns the shifted Legendre polynomial P_n(2x-1), which is
orthogonal on [0, 1] with unit weight. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: SphericalHarmonicNormalization
funcs.doc:
SphericalHarmonicNormalization returns the normalization constant
sqrt((2l+1)/(4*pi) * (l-|m|)!/(l+|m|)!) used in the real and complex
spherical harmonics of degree l and order m.

funcs.name: Zernike
funcs.signature: func Zernike(n, m int, rho, theta float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Zernike evaluates the (unnormalized) Zernike polynomial Z_n^m(rho, theta) on
the unit disk. Positive m selects the cosine azimuthal factor, negative m the
sine factor, and m == 0 the purely radial polynomial.

funcs.name: ZernikeNormalization
funcs.doc:
ZernikeNormalization returns the normalization factor that makes the Zernike
polynomial orthonormal over the unit disk: sqrt(2*(n+1)) for m != 0 and
sqrt(n+1) for m == 0.

funcs.name: ZernikeRadial
funcs.signature: func ZernikeRadial(n, m int, rho float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ZernikeRadial evaluates the Zernike radial polynomial R_n^m(rho) for
0 <= m <= n on the unit disk. It returns 0 when n-m is odd, per the standard
definition.

funcs.name: ZernikeRadialDerivative
funcs.signature: func ZernikeRadialDerivative(n, m int, rho float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ZernikeRadialDerivative returns the derivative d/drho R_n^m(rho), obtained by
differentiating the explicit power-series definition term by term.

packages.name: physics
packages.synopsis: Package physics is the "science math" layer of the algebra module: a standard-library-only collection of physical constants, unit conversions and common physics formulas expressed in SI units.
packages.doc:
Package physics is the "science math" layer of the algebra module: a
standard-library-only collection of physical constants, unit conversions and
common physics formulas expressed in SI units.

It has three parts:

  - Physical constants. Exported float64 values in SI units, each documented
    with its name, symbol, value and unit. Constants that are exact by
    definition of the SI (2019 redefinition) are marked as such. The full
    set is also available programmatically through [Constants] and [Lookup],
    each entry being a [Constant] record — this powers generated
    documentation.

  - Unit conversions. A compact but useful set of conversions grouped by
    physical dimension (length, mass, time, temperature, energy and angle).
    Use the generic [Convert] for symbol-driven conversion — it returns an
    error when the two units belong to different dimensions — or the typed
    helpers such as [CelsiusToKelvin], [DegToRad] and [EVToJoules].

  - Common formulas. Well-documented functions covering kinematics
    ([KineticEnergy], [Force], [Momentum], [Work], [Power], [FreeFallDistance],
    [ProjectileRange]), waves and optics ([WaveSpeed], [PhotonEnergy],
    [DeBroglieWavelength]), relativity ([LorentzFactor], [MassEnergy]),
    thermodynamics ([IdealGasPressure]) and electromagnetism ([CoulombForce],
    [VoltageOhm], [CurrentOhm], [ResistanceOhm]).

All quantities are SI unless a function's documentation states otherwise.
The package depends only on the math standard-library package.

consts.doc:
Extended CODATA / derived physical constants.

This file augments the core catalogue in constants.go with a second tier of
physical constants in SI units, together with O(1) lookup helpers. It adds
only new identifiers and never modifies the base table.

Each value is annotated as either "exact" — fixed by the 2019 SI
redefinition or derived by closed form from constants that are themselves
exact — or "measured" — an experimentally determined CODATA recommended
value carrying the uncertainty of that recommendation.

Physical constants in SI units.

Values follow the 2019 SI redefinition and CODATA recommended values.
Seven constants are exact by definition of the SI base units and are noted
as "exact (SI 2019)" in their documentation; the remaining values are
experimentally determined (CODATA) and carry the precision of the recommended
value.

vars.doc:
The seven SI base dimensions as unit basis vectors. Each has a single
exponent of one in its own slot and zero elsewhere.
Common composed dimensions, expressed in the SI base dimensions.

vars.signature: var CoulombConstant = physicsCoulombK
vars.doc:
CoulombConstant is the Coulomb constant k_e = 1/(4·π·ε0), in newton square
metres per coulomb squared (N·m²·C⁻²). It is precomputed once from the vacuum
permittivity so the electrostatic helpers avoid recomputing 4·π·ε0 on every
call. Its value is approximately 8.9875×10⁹.

vars.signature: var ErrCenterOfMass = errors.New("physics: invalid masses/positions for centre of mass")
vars.doc:
ErrCenterOfMass is returned by [CenterOfMass1D] when its inputs are unusable:
the masses and positions slices have different lengths, the slices are empty,
or the total mass is zero (so the centre of mass is undefined).

vars.signature: var ReducedPlanck = PlanckConstant / (2 * math.Pi)
vars.doc:
ReducedPlanck is the reduced Planck constant ħ = h/(2π), in joule-seconds
(J·s). Derived from the exact Planck constant.

types.name: Constant
types.doc:
Constant is a self-describing physical constant record. The set of all
records is enumerable through [Constants] and searchable through [Lookup],
which makes the constant table usable for generated documentation and
tooling.

funcs.name: Constants
funcs.doc:
Constants returns a copy of the table of physical constants known to the
package, in a stable order. The returned slice is safe to modify; it does
not alias the internal table.

funcs.name: ExtendedConstants
funcs.doc:
ExtendedConstants returns a copy of the table of additional physical
constants defined in this file, in a stable order. The returned slice is
safe to modify; it does not alias the internal table.

funcs.name: Lookup
funcs.doc:
Lookup returns the [Constant] whose Symbol matches symbol exactly, and true
if found. If no constant has that symbol it returns the zero Constant and
false.

funcs.name: LookupAny
funcs.doc:
LookupAny returns the [Constant] whose Symbol matches symbol exactly, and
true if found, searching both the base and the extended tables. Unlike the
O(n) [Lookup], which scans only the base table, this is an O(1) index lookup
that also spans the extended set. If no constant has that symbol it returns
the zero Constant and false.

funcs.name: LookupByName
funcs.doc:
LookupByName returns the [Constant] whose Name matches name exactly, and true
if found, searching both the base and the extended tables. If no constant has
that name it returns the zero Constant and false. The lookup is O(1).

types.name: Dim
types.signature: type Dim [7]int8
types.doc:
Dim is the exponent vector of the seven SI base dimensions. The array indices
are fixed and given by the exported basis vectors, in the order
{Length, Mass, Time, Current, Temperature, Amount, Luminosity}. A Dim is a
value array, so composing or comparing dimensions never allocates.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the dimension as a middle-dot separated product of base-unit
symbols with Unicode superscript exponents, for example "kg·m·s⁻²" for force.
The dimensionless dimension renders as "1".

types.name: Measurement
types.doc:
Measurement is a physical quantity paired with its standard (1σ)
uncertainty. The arithmetic methods implement first-order (linear) error
propagation: contributions combine in quadrature under the assumption that
the operands are statistically INDEPENDENT (uncorrelated). Any correlation
between operands is ignored, so results for correlated inputs are only
approximate.

funcs.name: NewMeasurement
funcs.doc:
NewMeasurement returns a Measurement with the given value and 1σ standard
uncertainty. The uncertainty is stored as its magnitude, so a negative
argument is treated as |sigma|.

funcs.name: Propagate
funcs.signature: func Propagate(f func([]float64) float64, means, sigmas []float64) (Measurement, error)
funcs.doc:
Propagate propagates uncertainty through an arbitrary multi-variable function
f evaluated at means, with per-input standard uncertainties sigmas. It
returns a Measurement whose value is f(means) and whose uncertainty is
√(Σ (∂f/∂xᵢ)² σᵢ²), each partial derivative estimated by a central finite
difference. The inputs are assumed independent; correlation between them is
ignored. It returns an error if len(means) != len(sigmas).

A single scratch slice (a copy of means) is allocated once per call and
reused across every partial-derivative evaluation, so the derivative loop
performs no additional allocation.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns the sum m + o. The values add and the standard uncertainties of
the (independent) operands combine in quadrature: σ = √(σ₁² + σ₂²).
Correlation between m and o is ignored.

methods.name: Apply
methods.signature: func (m Measurement) Apply(f func(float64) float64) Measurement
methods.doc:
Apply propagates uncertainty through an arbitrary single-variable function f.
The output value is f(Value) and the output uncertainty is |f'(Value)|·Sigma,
where the derivative is estimated by a central finite difference with a
deterministic, adaptively sized step. It assumes f is smooth near Value and
that a linear approximation is adequate over the ±Sigma range.

methods.name: Div
methods.doc:
Div returns the quotient m/o. The values divide and the RELATIVE
uncertainties add in quadrature, giving an absolute uncertainty of
|x/y|·√((σ₁/x)² + (σ₂/y)²). o.Value must be non-zero. The operands are
assumed independent; correlation is ignored.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the product m·o. The values multiply and the RELATIVE
uncertainties add in quadrature, so the absolute uncertainty is
|xy|·√((σ₁/x)² + (σ₂/y)²), evaluated in the numerically robust form
√((y·σ₁)² + (x·σ₂)²). The operands are assumed independent; correlation is
ignored.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns m raised to the exact power n. The value is Valueⁿ and the
relative uncertainty scales by |n|, giving an absolute uncertainty of
|Valueⁿ|·|n|·(Sigma/|Value|).

methods.name: Relative
methods.doc:
Relative returns the fractional (relative) uncertainty Sigma/|Value|. It is
undefined when Value is 0 and follows IEEE-754 semantics there (+Inf for a
non-zero Sigma, NaN when Sigma is also 0).

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc:
Scale returns m multiplied by the exact constant k (a value carrying no
uncertainty of its own). Both the value and the standard uncertainty scale,
the latter by |k|.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String formats the measurement as "value ± sigma" using the shortest decimal
representation that round-trips each component.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc:
Sub returns the difference m - o. The values subtract and the standard
uncertainties of the (independent) operands combine in quadrature:
σ = √(σ₁² + σ₂²). Correlation between m and o is ignored.

types.name: Quantity
types.doc:
Quantity is a physical quantity: a numeric value together with its
dimension. Value is always expressed in coherent SI base units (metre,
kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela), so quantities of the same
[Dim] are directly comparable and additive.

funcs.name: NewQuantity
funcs.signature: func NewQuantity(value float64, dim Dim) Quantity
funcs.doc:
NewQuantity returns a Quantity with the given value (in coherent SI base
units) and dimension.

funcs.name: ParseQuantity
funcs.signature: func ParseQuantity(value float64, unitSymbol string) (Quantity, error)
funcs.doc:
ParseQuantity returns the Quantity corresponding to value expressed in the
unit named by unitSymbol. The result's Value is in coherent SI base units.
Recognised symbols extend those of [Convert] (length, mass, time, energy and
angle) with imperial and derived units:

	length:   yd
	volume:   gal
	force:    lbf
	power:    hp
	pressure: psi, atm, bar
	velocity: mph, knot
	energy:   BTU, therm

It returns an error for an unknown symbol. Affine temperature units (C, F)
are not supported here; use [Convert] or the temperature helpers for those.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns the sum of q and r. It returns an error if the two quantities
have different dimensions.

methods.name: Div
methods.doc:
Div returns the quotient of q and r: values divide and dimension exponents
subtract. It never allocates.

methods.name: IsDimensionless
methods.doc:
IsDimensionless reports whether q has no physical dimension (all base
exponents zero), such as a pure number or an angle in radians.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the product of q and r: values multiply and dimension exponents
add. It never allocates.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns q raised to the integer power n: the value is raised to n and
every dimension exponent is multiplied by n. It never allocates.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc:
Sub returns the difference q-r. It returns an error if the two quantities
have different dimensions.

methods.name: To
methods.doc:
To expresses q in the unit named by unitSymbol and returns the numeric value
in that unit. It returns an error if the symbol is unknown or if its
dimension differs from q's dimension. The operation is a single map lookup
plus one division and allocates nothing.

types.name: Vec3
types.doc:
Vec3 is a three-dimensional Cartesian vector with float64 components. It is a
small value type: all methods take and return Vec3 by value, so ordinary
vector arithmetic performs no heap allocation and the compiler can keep
intermediate vectors in registers. Vec3 is used throughout the package for
positions, velocities, forces and field samples.

funcs.name: Curl
funcs.signature: func Curl(f func(Vec3) Vec3, p Vec3, h float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
Curl returns a numerical approximation of the curl ∇×f of the vector field f
at the point p, using second-order central finite differences with step h.
The components are (∂f_z/∂y − ∂f_y/∂z, ∂fₓ/∂z − ∂f_z/∂x, ∂f_y/∂x − ∂fₓ/∂y).
If h ≤ 0 a sensible default step is used.

funcs.name: Gradient
funcs.signature: func Gradient(f func(Vec3) float64, p Vec3, h float64) Vec3
funcs.doc:
Gradient returns a numerical approximation of the gradient ∇f of the scalar
field f at the point p, using second-order central finite differences with
step h. Each component is (f(p+h·eᵢ) − f(p−h·eᵢ)) / (2h) along the
corresponding axis. If h ≤ 0 a sensible default step is used. Central
differences make the result deterministic and second-order accurate.

funcs.name: NewVec3
funcs.signature: func NewVec3(x, y, z float64) Vec3
funcs.doc: NewVec3 returns the vector with components (x, y, z).

funcs.name: SumSlice
funcs.doc:
SumSlice returns the component-wise sum of every vector in v. It makes a
single pass over the slice accumulating into scalar registers, allocating no
intermediate vectors, and returns the zero vector for an empty or nil slice.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the component-wise sum a + b.

methods.name: Angle
methods.doc:
Angle returns the unsigned angle in radians between a and b, in the range
[0, π]. If either vector is the zero vector the result is 0.

methods.name: AngleBetween
methods.doc:
AngleBetween returns the unsigned angle in radians between a and b, in the
range [0, π]. If either operand is the zero vector the angle is undefined and
0 is returned. The cosine is clamped to [-1, 1] before inversion so that
rounding cannot push [math.Acos] out of its domain.

methods.name: Cross
methods.doc:
Cross returns the vector (cross) product a×b, a vector orthogonal to both a
and b whose magnitude equals the area of the parallelogram they span and
whose direction follows the right-hand rule.

methods.name: Distance
methods.doc: Distance returns the Euclidean distance |a - b| between the points a and b.

methods.name: Dot
methods.doc: Dot returns the scalar (inner) product a·b = aₓbₓ + a_yb_y + a_zb_z.

methods.name: Lerp
methods.signature: func (a Vec3) Lerp(b Vec3, t float64) Vec3
methods.doc:
Lerp returns the linear interpolation (1-t)·a + t·b between a and b. t = 0
yields a and t = 1 yields b; values outside [0, 1] extrapolate.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns the vector pointing opposite to a, that is a scaled by -1.

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc: Norm returns the Euclidean length (magnitude) |a| = √(a·a) of the vector.

methods.name: Norm2
methods.doc:
Norm2 returns the squared Euclidean length a·a. It avoids the square root of
[Vec3.Norm] and is preferred when only comparing magnitudes.

methods.name: Normalize
methods.doc:
Normalize returns the unit vector pointing in the direction of a. If a is the
zero vector it is returned unchanged, since its direction is undefined.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns the vector a with every component multiplied by the scalar s.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns the component-wise difference a - b.

methods.name: Unit
methods.doc:
Unit returns the unit vector (length 1) pointing in the direction of a. If a
is the zero vector its direction is undefined and the zero vector is returned
unchanged. Unit is the allocation-free companion of [Vec3.Normalize] and, like
all Vec3 arithmetic, returns its result by value without touching the heap.

funcs.name: AddInto
funcs.doc:
AddInto stores the component-wise sum a + b into *dst. It is the in-place form
of [Vec3.Add]: hot loops that repeatedly accumulate into an existing vector
can use it to make their intent explicit while performing, like the value
form, no heap allocation. dst may alias a or b.

funcs.name: Beta
funcs.doc:
Beta returns the dimensionless velocity ratio β = v/c for a speed v (m/s),
where c is the speed of light. Values approach ±1 as |v| approaches c.

funcs.name: COPHeatPump
funcs.doc:
COPHeatPump returns the ideal (Carnot) coefficient of performance of a heat
pump, COP = Th/(Th − Tc), delivering heat to a hot reservoir at tHot (K) while
drawing from a cold reservoir at tCold (K). The result is dimensionless. tHot
must differ from tCold.

funcs.name: COPRefrigerator
funcs.doc:
COPRefrigerator returns the ideal (Carnot) coefficient of performance of a
refrigerator, COP = Tc/(Th − Tc), moving heat from a cold reservoir at tCold
(K) to a hot reservoir at tHot (K). The result is dimensionless. tHot must
differ from tCold.

funcs.name: CapacitorCharge
funcs.doc:
CapacitorCharge returns the charge Q = C·V stored on a capacitor of
capacitance c (F) at voltage v (V). The result is in coulombs (C).

funcs.name: CapacitorEnergy
funcs.doc:
CapacitorEnergy returns the energy E = ½·C·V² stored in a capacitor of
capacitance c (F) charged to voltage v (V). The result is in joules (J).

funcs.name: CapacitorsParallel
funcs.doc:
CapacitorsParallel returns the equivalent capacitance of capacitors connected
in parallel, the sum of the individual capacitances c (F). With no arguments
it returns 0. The result is in farads (F).

funcs.name: CapacitorsSeries
funcs.doc:
CapacitorsSeries returns the equivalent capacitance of capacitors connected
in series, 1/Σ(1/cᵢ), for capacitances c (F). With no arguments it returns 0.
A zero capacitance yields 0. The result is in farads (F).

funcs.name: CarnotEfficiency
funcs.doc:
CarnotEfficiency returns the maximum thermal efficiency 1 − Tc/Th of a heat
engine operating between a cold reservoir at tCold (K) and a hot reservoir at
tHot (K). The result is dimensionless (0–1 for tHot > tCold > 0). It performs
no division guard, but tHot must be greater than zero for a physically
meaningful result.

funcs.name: CelsiusToFahrenheit
funcs.doc:
CelsiusToFahrenheit converts a temperature in degrees Celsius to degrees
Fahrenheit.

funcs.name: CelsiusToKelvin
funcs.doc: CelsiusToKelvin converts a temperature in degrees Celsius to kelvin.

funcs.name: CenterOfMass1D
funcs.doc:
CenterOfMass1D returns the mass-weighted mean position (Σ mᵢ·xᵢ)/(Σ mᵢ) of a
system of point masses arranged on a line, where masses[i] (kg) is the mass
at positions[i] (m). The result is in metres (m).

It returns [ErrCenterOfMass] if masses and positions have different lengths,
if they are empty, or if the total mass is zero (the centre of mass is then
undefined). The two slices are read in a single pass and no memory is
allocated.

funcs.name: CentripetalAcceleration
funcs.doc:
CentripetalAcceleration returns the centripetal acceleration a = v²/r
required to keep a body moving at speed v (m/s) on a circle of radius r (m).
The result is in metres per second squared (m/s²) and points toward the
centre. r must be non-zero.

funcs.name: Convert
funcs.signature: func Convert(value float64, fromUnit, toUnit string) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Convert converts value from the unit fromUnit to the unit toUnit and returns
the result. Both symbols must be known and belong to the same physical
dimension (for example "m" and "km", or "C" and "F"); otherwise Convert
returns an error and a zero value. Temperature conversions are affine and
handled correctly. Recognised symbols are:

	length:      m, km, cm, mm, mi, ft, in, nm, Å
	mass:        kg, g, mg, lb, oz
	time:        s, min, hr, day, yr
	temperature: K, C, F
	energy:      J, eV, cal, kWh
	angle:       rad, deg

funcs.name: CoulombForce
funcs.signature: func CoulombForce(q1, q2, r float64) float64
funcs.doc:
CoulombForce returns the magnitude and sign of the electrostatic force
between two point charges q1 and q2 (C) separated by distance r (m):
F = q1·q2/(4·π·ε0·r²). A positive result indicates repulsion (like charges),
a negative result attraction (opposite charges). The result is in newtons
(N). r must be non-zero.

funcs.name: CurrentOhm
funcs.doc:
CurrentOhm returns the current I = V/R through a resistor of resistance r (Ω)
with voltage v (V) across it, per Ohm's law. The result is in amperes (A).
r must be non-zero.

funcs.name: DeBroglieWavelength
funcs.doc:
DeBroglieWavelength returns the de Broglie wavelength h/(m·v) of a particle of
mass m (kg) moving at speed v (m/s), where h is the Planck constant. The
result is in metres (m). m and v must be non-zero.

funcs.name: DegToRad
funcs.doc: DegToRad converts an angle in degrees to radians.

funcs.name: Displacement
funcs.signature: func Displacement(v0, a, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Displacement returns the displacement s = v0·t + ½·a·t² of a body with
initial velocity v0 (m/s) under constant acceleration a (m/s²) after time t
(s). The result is in metres (m).

funcs.name: Divergence
funcs.signature: func Divergence(f func(Vec3) Vec3, p Vec3, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Divergence returns a numerical approximation of the divergence ∇·f of the
vector field f at the point p, using second-order central finite differences
with step h. It sums the diagonal partial derivatives
∂fₓ/∂x + ∂f_y/∂y + ∂f_z/∂z. If h ≤ 0 a sensible default step is used.

funcs.name: EVToJoules
funcs.doc: EVToJoules converts an energy in electronvolts to joules.

funcs.name: ElasticCollision1D
funcs.signature: func ElasticCollision1D(m1, v1, m2, v2 float64) (v1f, v2f float64)
funcs.doc:
ElasticCollision1D returns the final velocities v1f and v2f (m/s) of two
bodies after a one-dimensional perfectly elastic head-on collision, given
masses m1 and m2 (kg) and initial velocities v1 and v2 (m/s). Both linear
momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. The sum m1+m2 must be non-zero.

funcs.name: ElectricField
funcs.doc:
ElectricField returns the magnitude of the electric field of a point charge,
E = q/(4·π·ε0·r²), for a charge q (C) at distance r (m). The result is in
volts per metre (V/m); its sign follows the sign of q, so a positive charge
yields a positive (radially outward) field. r must be non-zero.

funcs.name: ElectricFieldPointCharge
funcs.doc:
ElectricFieldPointCharge returns the magnitude of the electric field
E = k_e·q/r² produced by a point charge q (C) at distance r (m), where k_e is
the Coulomb constant. The result is in volts per metre (V/m); its sign
follows the sign of q (positive q points radially outward). r must be
non-zero.

funcs.name: ElectricPotential
funcs.doc:
ElectricPotential returns the electric potential of a point charge,
V = q/(4·π·ε0·r), for a charge q (C) at distance r (m), taking the potential
to be zero at infinity. The result is in volts (V); its sign follows the sign
of q. r must be non-zero.

funcs.name: ElectricPotentialPointCharge
funcs.doc:
ElectricPotentialPointCharge returns the electric potential V = k_e·q/r of a
point charge q (C) at distance r (m), taking the potential to be zero at
infinity, where k_e is the Coulomb constant. The result is in volts (V). r
must be non-zero.

funcs.name: EnergyMomentumRelation
funcs.doc:
EnergyMomentumRelation returns the total energy E of a body from the
relativistic energy–momentum relation E = √((p·c)² + (m·c²)²), for rest mass
m (kg) and momentum p (kg·m/s), where c is the speed of light. The result is
in joules (J).

funcs.name: EscapeVelocity
funcs.doc:
EscapeVelocity returns the escape speed from the surface of a body of mass m
(kg) and radius r (m): v = √(2·G·m/r), where G is the Newtonian
gravitational constant. The result is in metres per second (m/s). r must be
non-zero.

funcs.name: FahrenheitToCelsius
funcs.doc:
FahrenheitToCelsius converts a temperature in degrees Fahrenheit to degrees
Celsius.

funcs.name: FahrenheitToKelvin
funcs.doc: FahrenheitToKelvin converts a temperature in degrees Fahrenheit to kelvin.

funcs.name: FieldEnergyDensity
funcs.doc:
FieldEnergyDensity returns the energy density of an electric field in vacuum,
u = ½·ε0·E², for a field magnitude E (V/m). The result is in joules per cubic
metre (J/m³) and is always non-negative.

funcs.name: Force
funcs.doc:
Force returns the net force m·a on a body of mass m (kg) undergoing
acceleration a (m/s²), per Newton's second law. The result is in newtons (N).

funcs.name: FreeFallDistance
funcs.doc:
FreeFallDistance returns the distance ½·g·t² fallen from rest under standard
gravity after time t (s), ignoring air resistance. The result is in metres
(m).

funcs.name: GravitationalForce
funcs.signature: func GravitationalForce(m1, m2, r float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GravitationalForce returns the magnitude of the gravitational attraction
F = G·m1·m2/r² between two point masses m1 and m2 (kg) separated by distance
r (m), where G is the Newtonian gravitational constant. The result is in
newtons (N). r must be non-zero.

funcs.name: GravitationalPotentialEnergy
funcs.signature: func GravitationalPotentialEnergy(m1, m2, r float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GravitationalPotentialEnergy returns the gravitational potential energy
U = −G·m1·m2/r of two point masses m1 and m2 (kg) separated by distance r
(m), where G is the Newtonian gravitational constant. The energy is measured
relative to the conventional zero at infinite separation, so it is negative
for the attractive gravitational interaction. The result is in joules (J).
The separation r must be non-zero.

funcs.name: HeatCapacity
funcs.signature: func HeatCapacity(mass, specificHeat, dT float64) float64
funcs.doc:
HeatCapacity returns the heat Q = m·c·ΔT required to change the temperature
of a mass (kg) with specific heat capacity specificHeat (J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹) by dT
(K). The result is in joules (J); it is negative when dT is negative (heat
released).

funcs.name: IdealGasMoles
funcs.signature: func IdealGasMoles(P, V, T float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IdealGasMoles returns the amount of substance of an ideal gas from
n = P·V/(R·T), for pressure P (Pa) in volume V (m³) at temperature T (K),
where R is the molar gas constant. The result is in moles (mol). T must be
non-zero.

funcs.name: IdealGasPressure
funcs.signature: func IdealGasPressure(n, T, V float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IdealGasPressure returns the pressure of an ideal gas from the ideal gas law
P = n·R·T/V, for n moles at temperature T (K) in volume V (m³), where R is
the molar gas constant. The result is in pascals (Pa). V must be non-zero.

funcs.name: IdealGasTemperature
funcs.signature: func IdealGasTemperature(P, V, n float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IdealGasTemperature returns the temperature of an ideal gas from
T = P·V/(n·R), for pressure P (Pa) in volume V (m³) with n moles, where R is
the molar gas constant. The result is in kelvin (K). n must be non-zero.

funcs.name: IdealGasVolume
funcs.signature: func IdealGasVolume(n, T, P float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IdealGasVolume returns the volume of an ideal gas from V = n·R·T/P, for n
moles at temperature T (K) and pressure P (Pa), where R is the molar gas
constant. The result is in cubic metres (m³). P must be non-zero.

funcs.name: ImpulseMomentum
funcs.doc:
ImpulseMomentum returns the impulse J = force·dt delivered by a constant force
(N) acting over a time interval dt (s). By the impulse–momentum theorem the
impulse equals the change in linear momentum it produces, so the result is in
kilogram-metres per second (kg·m/s), equivalently newton-seconds (N·s). It is
pure arithmetic and always well defined; dt is normally positive.

funcs.name: InductorEnergy
funcs.doc:
InductorEnergy returns the energy stored in an inductor, E = ½·L·I², for an
inductance l (H) carrying current i (A). The result is in joules (J).

funcs.name: InelasticCollision1D
funcs.signature: func InelasticCollision1D(m1, v1, m2, v2 float64) float64
funcs.doc:
InelasticCollision1D returns the common final velocity of two bodies that
stick together after a one-dimensional perfectly inelastic collision, given
masses m1 and m2 (kg) and initial velocities v1 and v2 (m/s):
vf = (m1·v1 + m2·v2)/(m1 + m2). Momentum is conserved; kinetic energy is not.
The result is in metres per second (m/s). The sum m1+m2 must be non-zero.

funcs.name: JoulesToEV
funcs.doc: JoulesToEV converts an energy in joules to electronvolts.

funcs.name: KelvinToCelsius
funcs.doc: KelvinToCelsius converts a temperature in kelvin to degrees Celsius.

funcs.name: KelvinToFahrenheit
funcs.doc: KelvinToFahrenheit converts a temperature in kelvin to degrees Fahrenheit.

funcs.name: KilogramsToPounds
funcs.doc: KilogramsToPounds converts a mass in kilograms to pounds (avoirdupois).

funcs.name: KineticEnergy
funcs.doc:
KineticEnergy returns the translational kinetic energy ½·m·v² of a body of
mass m (kg) moving at speed v (m/s). The result is in joules (J).

funcs.name: Laplacian
funcs.signature: func Laplacian(f func(Vec3) float64, p Vec3, h float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Laplacian returns a numerical approximation of the Laplacian ∇²f of the scalar
field f at the point p, using the second-order central second-difference
stencil with step h. It sums the unmixed second partial derivatives
∂²f/∂x² + ∂²f/∂y² + ∂²f/∂z², each evaluated as
(f(p+h·eᵢ) − 2f(p) + f(p−h·eᵢ)) / h². If h ≤ 0 a sensible default step is used.

funcs.name: LatentHeat
funcs.doc:
LatentHeat returns the heat Q = m·L absorbed or released during a phase
change of a mass (kg) with specific latent heat L (J/kg). The result is in
joules (J).

funcs.name: LengthContraction
funcs.doc:
LengthContraction returns the contracted length L = properLength/γ of an
object measured in a frame in which it moves at speed v (m/s), where
properLength (m) is its length in its rest frame and γ the Lorentz factor.
The result is in metres (m). |v| must be less than c.

funcs.name: LorentzFactor
funcs.doc:
LorentzFactor returns the Lorentz factor γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²) for a speed v
(m/s), where c is the speed of light. It is dimensionless and equals 1 at
v = 0 and grows without bound as v approaches c. |v| must be less than c.

funcs.name: LorentzForceMag
funcs.signature: func LorentzForceMag(q, E, v, B, theta float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LorentzForceMag returns the combined electric and magnetic force on a charge
as the scalar sum F = q·(E + v·B·sin(θ)), for a charge q (C) in an electric
field of magnitude E (V/m) and a magnetic field of magnitude B (T), moving at
speed v (m/s) at angle theta (radians) to the magnetic field. This assumes the
electric force q·E and the magnetic force q·v·B·sin(θ) act along the same line
(the collinear case); for non-collinear fields combine the vector components
directly. The result is in newtons (N).

funcs.name: MagneticFieldStraightWire
funcs.doc:
MagneticFieldStraightWire returns the magnitude of the magnetic field around
a long straight wire, B = μ0·I/(2·π·r), for a current current (A) at
perpendicular distance r (m). The result is in teslas (T). r must be
non-zero.

funcs.name: MagneticForceOnCharge
funcs.signature: func MagneticForceOnCharge(q, v, B, theta float64) float64
funcs.doc:
MagneticForceOnCharge returns the magnitude of the magnetic force on a moving
charge, F = q·v·B·sin(θ), for a charge q (C) moving at speed v (m/s) through a
field of magnitude B (T), where theta (radians) is the angle between the
velocity and the field. The result is in newtons (N). The sign follows the
signs of q and sin(θ).

funcs.name: MassEnergy
funcs.doc:
MassEnergy returns the rest energy m·c² of a mass m (kg), where c is the
speed of light. The result is in joules (J).

funcs.name: MeanKineticEnergy
funcs.doc:
MeanKineticEnergy returns the mean translational kinetic energy of a gas
particle, E = 3/2·k_B·T, at temperature T (K), where k_B is the Boltzmann
constant. The result is in joules (J).

funcs.name: MetersToMiles
funcs.doc: MetersToMiles converts a length in metres to miles.

funcs.name: MilesToMeters
funcs.doc: MilesToMeters converts a length in miles to metres.

funcs.name: Momentum
funcs.doc:
Momentum returns the linear momentum m·v of a body of mass m (kg) moving at
velocity v (m/s). The result is in kilogram-metres per second (kg·m/s).

funcs.name: MostProbableSpeed
funcs.doc:
MostProbableSpeed returns the most probable molecular speed of the
Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution, v_p = √(2·R·T/M), for temperature T (K) and
molar mass molarMass (kg/mol), where R is the molar gas constant. The result
is in metres per second (m/s). molarMass must be non-zero.

funcs.name: NumberDensity
funcs.doc:
NumberDensity returns the number of gas particles per unit volume from
n = P/(k_B·T), for pressure P (Pa) at temperature T (K), where k_B is the
Boltzmann constant. The result is in particles per cubic metre (m⁻³). T must
be non-zero.

funcs.name: OrbitalPeriod
funcs.doc:
OrbitalPeriod returns the period of a circular orbit of radius r (m) about a
central mass m (kg), from Kepler's third law: T = 2π·√(r³/(G·m)), where G is
the Newtonian gravitational constant. The result is in seconds (s). m must be
non-zero.

funcs.name: OrbitalVelocity
funcs.doc:
OrbitalVelocity returns the speed of a body in a circular orbit of radius r
(m) about a central mass m (kg): v = √(G·m/r), where G is the Newtonian
gravitational constant. The result is in metres per second (m/s). r must be
non-zero.

funcs.name: ParallelCapacitance
funcs.doc:
ParallelCapacitance returns the equivalent capacitance of capacitors connected
in parallel, the sum of the individual capacitances c (F), accumulated in a
single pass. With no arguments it returns 0. The result is in farads (F).

funcs.name: ParallelPlateCapacitance
funcs.signature: func ParallelPlateCapacitance(area, separation, relPermittivity float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ParallelPlateCapacitance returns the capacitance of a parallel-plate
capacitor, C = ε0·εr·A/d, for plate area (m²), plate separation (m), and
relative permittivity relPermittivity (dimensionless; 1 for vacuum). The
result is in farads (F). separation must be non-zero.

funcs.name: ParallelResistance
funcs.doc:
ParallelResistance returns the equivalent resistance of resistors connected in
parallel, 1/Σ(1/rᵢ), for resistances r (Ω), accumulated in a single pass. It
returns an error if no resistances are supplied or if any resistance is zero
(a zero resistance short-circuits the network). The result is in ohms (Ω).

funcs.name: PhotonEnergy
funcs.doc:
PhotonEnergy returns the energy h·f of a photon of frequency freq (Hz), where
h is the Planck constant. The result is in joules (J).

funcs.name: PlanckSpectralRadiance
funcs.doc:
PlanckSpectralRadiance returns the spectral radiance of a blackbody per unit
wavelength from Planck's law,

	B(λ,T) = (2·h·c²/λ⁵) · 1/(exp(h·c/(λ·k_B·T)) − 1),

for wavelength (m) and absolute temperature T (K), where h is the Planck
constant, c the speed of light and k_B the Boltzmann constant. The result is
in W·sr⁻¹·m⁻³ (watts per steradian per square metre per metre of wavelength).
The grouped constants 2·h·c² and h·c/k_B are precomputed once at package
initialisation, so this call performs no redundant constant products. Both
wavelength and T must be non-zero.

funcs.name: PotentialEnergyGravity
funcs.doc:
PotentialEnergyGravity returns the gravitational potential energy m·g·h of a
body of mass m (kg) at height h (m) near Earth's surface, using the standard
gravity g. The result is in joules (J).

funcs.name: PoundsToKilograms
funcs.doc: PoundsToKilograms converts a mass in pounds (avoirdupois) to kilograms.

funcs.name: Power
funcs.doc:
Power returns the average power work/time for work (J) done in time t (s).
The result is in watts (W). t must be non-zero.

funcs.name: PowerDissipated
funcs.doc:
PowerDissipated returns the electrical power dissipated by a resistor,
P = I²·R, carrying current i (A) with resistance r (Ω). The result is in
watts (W).

funcs.name: ProjectileMaxHeight
funcs.doc:
ProjectileMaxHeight returns the maximum height reached by a projectile
launched from ground level with initial speed v (m/s) at launch angle theta
(radians), under standard gravity: H = (v·sinθ)²/(2·g). The result is in
metres (m).

funcs.name: ProjectilePosition
funcs.signature: func ProjectilePosition(v, theta, t float64) (x, y float64)
funcs.doc:
ProjectilePosition returns the horizontal and vertical position (x, y), in
metres, of a projectile at time t (s), launched from the origin with initial
speed v (m/s) at launch angle theta (radians) under standard gravity:
x = v·cosθ·t, y = v·sinθ·t − ½·g·t².

funcs.name: ProjectileRange
funcs.doc:
ProjectileRange returns the horizontal range of a projectile launched from
and landing at the same height, with initial speed v (m/s) at launch angle
theta (radians), under standard gravity and ignoring air resistance:
R = v²·sin(2θ)/g. The result is in metres (m).

funcs.name: ProjectileTimeOfFlight
funcs.doc:
ProjectileTimeOfFlight returns the total time a projectile launched from and
landing at the same height stays aloft, with initial speed v (m/s) at launch
angle theta (radians), under standard gravity: t = 2·v·sinθ/g. The result is
in seconds (s).

funcs.name: RCChargeVoltage
funcs.signature: func RCChargeVoltage(vSource, r, c, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RCChargeVoltage returns the capacitor voltage while charging through a
resistor, V = Vs·(1 − e^(−t/RC)), for a source voltage vSource (V) applied to
a resistance r (Ω) and capacitance c (F) at time t (s) after the switch
closes. The result is in volts (V). The product r·c must be non-zero.

funcs.name: RCDischargeVoltage
funcs.signature: func RCDischargeVoltage(v0, r, c, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RCDischargeVoltage returns the capacitor voltage while discharging through a
resistor, V = V0·e^(−t/RC), for an initial voltage v0 (V) across a resistance
r (Ω) and capacitance c (F) at time t (s) after discharge begins. The result
is in volts (V). The product r·c must be non-zero.

funcs.name: RCTimeConstant
funcs.doc:
RCTimeConstant returns the time constant τ = R·C of an RC circuit, for a
resistance r (Ω) and capacitance c (F). The result is in seconds (s).

funcs.name: RMSSpeed
funcs.doc:
RMSSpeed returns the root-mean-square molecular speed of an ideal gas,
v_rms = √(3·R·T/M), for temperature T (K) and molar mass molarMass (kg/mol),
where R is the molar gas constant. The result is in metres per second (m/s).
molarMass must be non-zero.

funcs.name: RadToDeg
funcs.doc: RadToDeg converts an angle in radians to degrees.

funcs.name: RelativisticDopplerFactor
funcs.doc:
RelativisticDopplerFactor returns the relativistic Doppler frequency ratio
f_observed/f_source for motion directly along the line of sight at speed v
(m/s). A positive v denotes the source and observer separating (redshift,
factor < 1); a negative v denotes approach (blueshift, factor > 1). The
factor is √((1 − β)/(1 + β)) with β = v/c. |v| must be less than c.

funcs.name: RelativisticEnergy
funcs.doc:
RelativisticEnergy returns the total relativistic energy E = γ·m·c² of a body
of rest mass m (kg) moving at speed v (m/s), where γ is the Lorentz factor
and c the speed of light. The result is in joules (J) and reduces to the rest
energy m·c² at v = 0. |v| must be less than c.

funcs.name: RelativisticKineticEnergy
funcs.doc:
RelativisticKineticEnergy returns the relativistic kinetic energy
K = (γ − 1)·m·c² of a body of rest mass m (kg) moving at speed v (m/s), where
γ is the Lorentz factor and c the speed of light. The result is in joules (J)
and approaches the classical ½·m·v² for v ≪ c. |v| must be less than c.

funcs.name: RelativisticMomentum
funcs.doc:
RelativisticMomentum returns the relativistic momentum p = γ·m·v of a body of
rest mass m (kg) moving at speed v (m/s), where γ is the Lorentz factor. The
result is in kilogram-metres per second (kg·m/s). |v| must be less than c.

funcs.name: RelativisticVelocityAddition
funcs.doc:
RelativisticVelocityAddition returns the combined velocity of two collinear
velocities u and v (m/s) under special relativity: w = (u + v)/(1 + u·v/c²),
where c is the speed of light. The result never exceeds c in magnitude when
the inputs do not. The result is in metres per second (m/s).

funcs.name: ResistanceOhm
funcs.doc:
ResistanceOhm returns the resistance R = V/I of a component with voltage v
(V) across it carrying current i (A), per Ohm's law. The result is in ohms
(Ω). i must be non-zero.

funcs.name: ResistorsParallel
funcs.doc:
ResistorsParallel returns the equivalent resistance of resistors connected in
parallel, 1/Σ(1/rᵢ), for resistances r (Ω). With no arguments it returns 0. A
zero resistance short-circuits the network and yields 0. The result is in
ohms (Ω).

funcs.name: ResistorsSeries
funcs.doc:
ResistorsSeries returns the equivalent resistance of resistors connected in
series, the sum of the individual resistances r (Ω). With no arguments it
returns 0. The result is in ohms (Ω).

funcs.name: ResonantFrequency
funcs.doc:
ResonantFrequency returns the resonant frequency of an LC circuit,
f = 1/(2·π·√(L·C)), for an inductance l (H) and capacitance c (F). The result
is in hertz (Hz). The product l·c must be positive.

funcs.name: ScaleInto
funcs.signature: func ScaleInto(dst *Vec3, a Vec3, s float64)
funcs.doc:
ScaleInto stores a scaled by the scalar s into *dst. It is the in-place form
of [Vec3.Scale] and, like [AddInto], allocates nothing. dst may alias a.

funcs.name: SeriesCapacitance
funcs.doc:
SeriesCapacitance returns the equivalent capacitance of capacitors connected
in series, 1/Σ(1/cᵢ), for capacitances c (F), accumulated in a single pass. It
returns an error if no capacitances are supplied or if any capacitance is
zero. The result is in farads (F).

funcs.name: SeriesResistance
funcs.doc:
SeriesResistance returns the equivalent resistance of resistors connected in
series, the sum of the individual resistances r (Ω), accumulated in a single
pass. With no arguments it returns 0. The result is in ohms (Ω).

funcs.name: StefanBoltzmannPower
funcs.signature: func StefanBoltzmannPower(emissivity, area, T float64) float64
funcs.doc:
StefanBoltzmannPower returns the thermal power radiated by a grey body from
the Stefan–Boltzmann law P = ε·σ·A·T⁴, for emissivity (dimensionless, 0–1),
surface area area (m²) and absolute temperature T (K), where σ is the
Stefan–Boltzmann constant. The result is in watts (W).

funcs.name: ThermalConduction
funcs.signature: func ThermalConduction(k, area, dT, thickness float64) float64
funcs.doc:
ThermalConduction returns the steady-state rate of heat conduction through a
slab, Q̇ = k·A·ΔT/L (Fourier's law), for thermal conductivity k
(W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹), cross-sectional area area (m²), temperature difference dT (K)
across the slab and thickness (m). The result is in watts (W). thickness must
be non-zero.

funcs.name: TimeDilation
funcs.doc:
TimeDilation returns the dilated time interval Δt = γ·properTime measured in a
frame moving at speed v (m/s) relative to the clock, where properTime (s) is
the interval in the clock's rest frame and γ the Lorentz factor. The result
is in seconds (s). |v| must be less than c.

funcs.name: TimeToStop
funcs.doc:
TimeToStop returns the time t = −v0/a for a body with initial velocity v0
(m/s) under constant acceleration a (m/s²) to reach zero velocity. The result
is in seconds (s). The acceleration a must be non-zero; with a = 0 the body
never stops and the result is an infinity whose sign reflects v0. When a
opposes v0 (genuine deceleration) the result is positive.

funcs.name: Velocity
funcs.signature: func Velocity(v0, a, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Velocity returns the velocity v = v0 + a·t of a body with initial velocity
v0 (m/s) under constant acceleration a (m/s²) after time t (s). The result is
in metres per second (m/s). It is pure arithmetic and always well defined.

funcs.name: VelocityFinal
funcs.signature: func VelocityFinal(v0, a, t float64) float64
funcs.doc:
VelocityFinal returns the final velocity v = v0 + a·t of a body with initial
velocity v0 (m/s) under constant acceleration a (m/s²) after time t (s). The
result is in metres per second (m/s).

funcs.name: VelocityFromDistance
funcs.signature: func VelocityFromDistance(v0, a, d float64) float64
funcs.doc:
VelocityFromDistance returns the speed v = √(v0² + 2·a·d) reached by a body
with initial velocity v0 (m/s) under constant acceleration a (m/s²) after
travelling distance d (m), from the time-independent kinematic relation
v² = v0² + 2·a·d. The result is in metres per second (m/s). The caller must
ensure the radicand v0² + 2·a·d is non-negative; a negative radicand (for
example strong deceleration over a distance the body cannot reach) yields
NaN, signalling that the requested state is not physically attained.

funcs.name: VisViva
funcs.signature: func VisViva(mCentral, r, a float64) float64
funcs.doc:
VisViva returns the orbital speed v = √(G·M·(2/r − 1/a)) of a body at
distance r (m) from a central mass mCentral (kg) on an orbit of semi-major
axis a (m), from the vis-viva equation, where G is the Newtonian
gravitational constant. The result is in metres per second (m/s). Both r and
a must be non-zero; for a bound orbit r lies within the orbit so the radicand
is non-negative, while a radicand driven negative by inconsistent inputs
yields NaN.

funcs.name: VoltageOhm
funcs.doc:
VoltageOhm returns the voltage V = I·R across a resistor carrying current i
(A) with resistance r (Ω), per Ohm's law. The result is in volts (V).

funcs.name: WaveSpeed
funcs.doc:
WaveSpeed returns the propagation speed of a wave, freq·wavelength, for a
wave of frequency freq (Hz) and wavelength wavelength (m). The result is in
metres per second (m/s).

funcs.name: WienPeakWavelength
funcs.doc:
WienPeakWavelength returns the wavelength λ_max = b/T at which a blackbody's
spectral radiance peaks (Wien's displacement law), for absolute temperature T
(K), where b is the Wien displacement constant. The result is in metres (m).
T must be non-zero.

funcs.name: Work
funcs.doc:
Work returns the work done by a constant force f (N) acting over a
displacement d (m) parallel to the force: W = f·d. The result is in joules
(J). For a force at angle θ to the displacement, pass f·cos(θ).

examples.name: CelsiusToKelvin
Convert
Convert (temperature)
DegToRad
LorentzFactor
MassEnergy
PhotonEnergy
Lookup

packages.name: plot
packages.synopsis: Package plot is a small, Matplotlib-shaped plotting library that renders natively in pure Go using only the standard library (image, image/png, image/color) plus an in-package SVG string writer.
packages.doc:
Package plot is a small, Matplotlib-shaped plotting library that renders
natively in pure Go using only the standard library (image, image/png,
image/color) plus an in-package SVG string writer. It has no third-party
dependencies. The only non-standard package it touches is the parent
[github.com/malcolmston/algebra] package, and only in [Axes.PlotExpr], which
samples a parsed symbolic expression.

# Model

The API mirrors Matplotlib's Figure/Axes structure. A [Figure] is a fixed
pixel canvas holding a single [Axes]; the axes owns the data limits, labels
and the ordered list of series. Create a figure with [New] or [NewWithSize],
obtain its axes with [Figure.Axes], add series, then render.

	fig := plot.New()
	ax := fig.Axes()
	ax.PlotFunc(math.Sin, 0, 2*math.Pi, 200)
	ax.Title("sine").XLabel("x").YLabel("sin(x)").Grid(true)
	png, _ := fig.RenderPNG()

# Series types

The axes supports the common Matplotlib chart types, each returning a typed
series value whose exported fields can be tweaked after creation:

  - [Axes.Plot]        — a connected poly-line ([LineSeries]).
  - [Axes.Scatter]     — unconnected markers ([ScatterSeries]).
  - [Axes.Bar]         — a vertical bar chart ([BarSeries]).
  - [Axes.Hist]        — a histogram of raw samples ([HistSeries]).
  - [Axes.Step]        — a piecewise-constant step plot ([StepSeries]).
  - [Axes.FillBetween] — a shaded band between two curves ([FillSeries]).
  - [Axes.PlotFunc]    — samples a Go func(float64)float64 over a range.
  - [Axes.PlotExpr]    — samples an algebra expression string over a range.

Series colors are assigned automatically from [DefaultColors] in the order
the series are added, matching Matplotlib's property cycle.

# Configuration

[Axes.Title], [Axes.XLabel], [Axes.YLabel], [Axes.Grid], [Axes.Legend],
[Axes.XLim] and [Axes.YLim] configure the axes and return the receiver so
they can be chained. When no explicit limits are set the axes autoscales to
the data with a small margin, and axis ticks are placed at "nice" round
values.

# Rendering

Two fully native backends are provided. The raster backend
([Figure.RenderPNG], [Figure.SavePNG]) draws into an [image.RGBA] using
Bresenham line rasterization and a built-in 5x7 bitmap font, then encodes the
result with image/png. The vector backend ([Figure.RenderSVG],
[Figure.SaveSVG]) emits an SVG document with real paths and text. Both
backends are deterministic for a given figure.

# Matplotlib export

[Figure.ExportMatplotlib] emits a runnable Python script that reproduces the
figure with the real Matplotlib library. This keeps the Go package
dependency-free while still offering a route to genuine Matplotlib output for
users who have Python and matplotlib available.

vars.doc:
Named opaque colors used throughout the package. They mirror the default
Matplotlib "tab10" style palette so that figures rendered by this package
look familiar next to Matplotlib output.

DefaultColors is the ordered cycle of colors assigned to successive series
when a caller does not set an explicit color. It matches the Matplotlib
default property cycle.

types.name: Axes
types.doc:
Axes is a single set of x and y coordinate axes together with the series
drawn on them, analogous to a Matplotlib Axes. Obtain the axes of a figure
with [Figure.Axes]. All plotting methods hang off this type.

methods.name: Bar
methods.doc:
Bar adds a vertical bar chart with bars centred on xs and the given heights,
rising from y = 0. If width <= 0 a width of 80% of the smallest center
spacing is chosen automatically. It returns the new series.

methods.name: FillBetween
methods.doc:
FillBetween adds a shaded band between the two curves y1 and y2 sampled at
the shared x values and returns the new series. All three slices must have
equal length.

methods.name: Grid
methods.doc:
Grid enables or disables the background grid lines at the tick locations. It
returns the axes for chaining.

methods.name: Hist
methods.doc:
Hist bins the raw samples into the given number of equal-width bins spanning
the sample range and adds the resulting histogram. If bins < 1 it defaults
to 10. It returns the new series; with no finite samples the series is empty.

methods.name: Legend
methods.doc:
Legend enables or disables the legend box, which lists every series that has
a non-empty label. It returns the axes for chaining.

methods.name: Plot
methods.doc:
Plot adds a line connecting the given points and returns the new series for
further customization. The x and y slices must have equal length. The series
is assigned the next color from the default cycle.

methods.name: PlotExpr
methods.signature: func (a *Axes) PlotExpr(src, varName string, xmin, xmax float64, n int) (*LineSeries, error)
methods.doc:
PlotExpr parses the algebra expression src, samples it at n evenly spaced
points on [xmin, xmax] by binding the given variable name to each sample, and
adds the resulting line. It returns the series and any parse error; on a
parse error the returned series is nil. Points that fail to evaluate to a
finite real number are skipped. If varName is empty it defaults to "x", and
if n < 2 it defaults to 200.

This is the only method that uses the parent algebra package; it lets a
figure be driven directly from a symbolic expression such as "sin(x)/x".

methods.name: PlotFunc
methods.signature: func (a *Axes) PlotFunc(f func(float64) float64, xmin, xmax float64, n int) *LineSeries
methods.doc:
PlotFunc samples the Go function f at n evenly spaced points on [xmin, xmax]
and adds the resulting line, returning the new series. Points where f returns
a non-finite value are skipped, breaking the line there. If n < 2 it defaults
to 200.

methods.name: Scatter
methods.doc:
Scatter adds unconnected point markers and returns the new series. The x and
y slices must have equal length.

methods.name: Step
methods.doc:
Step adds a piecewise-constant step plot through the given points and returns
the new series. The x and y slices must have equal length.

methods.name: Title
methods.doc:
Title sets the axes title, drawn centred above the plot. It returns the axes
for chaining.

methods.name: XLabel
methods.doc:
XLabel sets the x-axis label, drawn centred below the plot. It returns the
axes for chaining.

methods.name: XLim
methods.doc:
XLim fixes the x-axis data range to [lo, hi], disabling autoscaling in x. It
returns the axes for chaining.

methods.name: YLabel
methods.doc:
YLabel sets the y-axis label, drawn rotated along the left edge. It returns
the axes for chaining.

methods.name: YLim
methods.doc:
YLim fixes the y-axis data range to [lo, hi], disabling autoscaling in y. It
returns the axes for chaining.

types.name: BarSeries
types.doc:
BarSeries is a vertical bar chart, the output of [Axes.Bar]. Each bar is
centred on Xs[i] with height Heights[i] measured from Baseline.

types.name: Figure
types.doc:
Figure is the top-level drawing surface, analogous to a Matplotlib Figure.
It has a pixel size and a single [Axes] on which series are plotted. Create
one with [New] or [NewWithSize], add series through the axes, then render
with [Figure.RenderPNG], [Figure.RenderSVG], [Figure.SavePNG],
[Figure.SaveSVG] or [Figure.ExportMatplotlib].

funcs.name: New
funcs.doc:
New returns a new [Figure] of the default size (640x480) with a white
background and an empty axes.

funcs.name: NewWithSize
funcs.doc:
NewWithSize returns a new [Figure] of the given pixel size with a white
background and an empty axes. Non-positive dimensions are replaced by the
defaults.

methods.name: Axes
methods.doc: Axes returns the figure's single axes, on which all series are plotted.

methods.name: ExportMatplotlib
methods.doc:
ExportMatplotlib returns a runnable Python script that reproduces the figure
using the real Matplotlib library. The script imports matplotlib, rebuilds
every series with the equivalent pyplot call, applies the title, axis labels,
limits, grid and legend, and finally shows the figure.

This lets the zero-dependency Go renderer act as a drop-in preview while
still offering a path to pixel-perfect Matplotlib output: write the script to
a .py file and run it with a Python interpreter that has matplotlib
installed. The returned string always contains the line "import matplotlib".

methods.name: RenderPNG
methods.doc:
RenderPNG renders the figure and returns the encoded PNG bytes. The output is
deterministic for a given figure.

methods.name: RenderSVG
methods.doc:
RenderSVG renders the figure and returns the SVG document as a string. The
output is deterministic for a given figure.

methods.name: SavePNG
methods.doc: SavePNG renders the figure and writes the PNG to the file at path.

methods.name: SaveSVG
methods.doc: SaveSVG renders the figure and writes the SVG document to the file at path.

types.name: FillSeries
types.doc:
FillSeries is a shaded region between two y curves sharing common x values,
the output of [Axes.FillBetween]. The band at Xs[i] spans Y1[i] to Y2[i].

types.name: HistSeries
types.doc:
HistSeries is a histogram computed from raw samples by [Axes.Hist]. Edges
has one more element than Counts; bin i spans [Edges[i], Edges[i+1]) and has
height Counts[i].

types.name: LineSeries
types.doc:
LineSeries is a poly-line connecting successive (X, Y) data points, the
output of [Axes.Plot], [Axes.PlotFunc] and [Axes.PlotExpr]. Fields may be
adjusted after creation to change the rendered appearance.

types.name: ScatterSeries
types.doc:
ScatterSeries is a set of unconnected point markers, the output of
[Axes.Scatter].

types.name: StepSeries
types.doc:
StepSeries is a piecewise-constant step plot, the output of [Axes.Step]. The
value Ys[i] is held from Xs[i] up to Xs[i+1] (a "post" step).

funcs.name: ColorCycle
funcs.doc:
ColorCycle returns the color at position i in [DefaultColors], wrapping
around modulo the palette length. It is the rule this package uses to assign
automatic colors to new series.

packages.name: polyhedra
packages.synopsis: Package polyhedra provides tools for three-dimensional convex geometry with a focus on polyhedra: the five Platonic solids, the thirteen Archimedean solids, general triangulated and polygonal meshes, and 3-D convex hulls.
packages.doc:
Package polyhedra provides tools for three-dimensional convex geometry with a
focus on polyhedra: the five Platonic solids, the thirteen Archimedean
solids, general triangulated and polygonal meshes, and 3-D convex hulls.

The package is organised around a small number of value types. [Vec3] is the
core three-component vector, used interchangeably as a point or a
displacement. [Polyhedron] is a boundary representation (a set of vertices
together with polygonal faces given as ordered index loops) on top of which
the package computes surface area, enclosed volume, centroid, face normals
and areas, edge enumeration, the Euler characteristic, and the (polar/canonical)
dual polyhedron. [PlatonicSolid] and [ArchimedeanSolid] capture the
combinatorial and metric data of the regular and semiregular convex solids
and can materialise them as [Polyhedron] meshes with exact vertex
coordinates.

Volumes and areas of closed meshes are obtained from the divergence theorem:
the signed volume is one sixth of the sum over outward-oriented triangles of
the scalar triple product of their vertices, and face normals are computed
with Newell's method so that they are well defined even for slightly
non-planar polygons. The convex hull of a point cloud is computed with an
incremental algorithm ([ConvexHull]); a gift-wrapping variant
([GiftWrapHull]) is provided as an independent cross-check.

All computation is performed in float64 using only the Go standard library.
Routines are deterministic. Predicates that must tolerate floating-point
noise take or use an explicit epsilon so that callers control the trade-off
between robustness and strictness.

consts.signature: const Eps = 1e-9
consts.doc:
Eps is the default absolute tolerance used by the approximate predicates in
this package. It sits comfortably above double-precision rounding noise for
coordinates of moderate magnitude.

vars.signature: var ErrDegenerate = errors.New("polyhedra: degenerate input")
vars.doc:
ErrDegenerate is returned by routines that cannot produce a meaningful result
because their input is degenerate (collinear, coincident, or empty).

vars.signature: var ErrEmptyMesh = errors.New("polyhedra: empty mesh")
vars.doc: ErrEmptyMesh is returned when an operation requires a non-empty mesh.

vars.signature: var ErrNotEnoughPoints = errors.New("polyhedra: need at least four non-coplanar points")
vars.doc:
ErrNotEnoughPoints is returned when a hull is requested from fewer than four
points, or from points that are all coplanar so that no 3-D hull exists.

vars.signature: var Phi = (1 + math.Sqrt(5)) / 2
vars.doc:
Phi is the golden ratio (1 + sqrt 5) / 2, which governs the coordinates of the
dodecahedron and icosahedron.

types.name: ArchimedeanSolid
types.signature:
type ArchimedeanSolid struct {
	Name       string     // common name
	VertexFig  string     // vertex configuration, e.g. "3.6.6"
	NumV       int        // number of vertices
	NumE       int        // number of edges
	NumF       int        // number of faces
	Faces      []FaceType // regular faces by polygon type
	VolumeUnit float64    // volume at edge length 1
	CircumUnit float64    // circumradius at edge length 1
}
types.doc:
ArchimedeanSolid captures the combinatorial and metric data of one of the
thirteen semiregular convex polyhedra (uniform, with regular polygon faces and
a single vertex figure but more than one kind of face). Volume and
circumradius are stored as coefficients for unit edge length; surface area is
derived exactly from the regular face polygons.

funcs.name: ArchimedeanSolids
funcs.doc:
ArchimedeanSolids returns all thirteen Archimedean solids in a canonical
order (increasing face count, then vertices).

funcs.name: NewCuboctahedron
funcs.doc: NewCuboctahedron returns the cuboctahedron (vertex figure 3.4.3.4).

funcs.name: NewIcosidodecahedron
funcs.doc: NewIcosidodecahedron returns the icosidodecahedron (vertex figure 3.5.3.5).

funcs.name: NewRhombicosidodecahedron
funcs.doc: NewRhombicosidodecahedron returns the rhombicosidodecahedron (vertex figure 3.4.5.4).

funcs.name: NewRhombicuboctahedron
funcs.doc: NewRhombicuboctahedron returns the rhombicuboctahedron (vertex figure 3.4.4.4).

funcs.name: NewSnubCube
funcs.doc:
NewSnubCube returns the snub cube (vertex figure 3.3.3.3.4). Its volume and
circumradius involve the tribonacci constant and are given numerically.

funcs.name: NewSnubDodecahedron
funcs.doc:
NewSnubDodecahedron returns the snub dodecahedron (vertex figure 3.3.3.3.5).
Its volume and circumradius involve algebraic constants and are given
numerically.

funcs.name: NewTruncatedCube
funcs.doc: NewTruncatedCube returns the truncated cube (vertex figure 3.8.8).

funcs.name: NewTruncatedCuboctahedron
funcs.doc:
NewTruncatedCuboctahedron returns the truncated cuboctahedron, also called the
great rhombicuboctahedron (vertex figure 4.6.8).

funcs.name: NewTruncatedDodecahedron
funcs.doc: NewTruncatedDodecahedron returns the truncated dodecahedron (vertex figure 3.10.10).

funcs.name: NewTruncatedIcosahedron
funcs.doc:
NewTruncatedIcosahedron returns the truncated icosahedron, the classic
football/soccer-ball shape (vertex figure 5.6.6).

funcs.name: NewTruncatedIcosidodecahedron
funcs.doc:
NewTruncatedIcosidodecahedron returns the truncated icosidodecahedron, also
called the great rhombicosidodecahedron (vertex figure 4.6.10).

funcs.name: NewTruncatedOctahedron
funcs.doc: NewTruncatedOctahedron returns the truncated octahedron (vertex figure 4.6.6).

funcs.name: NewTruncatedTetrahedron
funcs.doc: NewTruncatedTetrahedron returns the truncated tetrahedron (vertex figure 3.6.6).

methods.name: Circumradius
methods.doc:
Circumradius returns the circumradius (center-to-vertex distance) at edge
length a.

methods.name: EulerCharacteristic
methods.doc: EulerCharacteristic returns V - E + F, which is 2 for every Archimedean solid.

methods.name: Midradius
methods.doc:
Midradius returns the midradius at edge length a, derived from the
circumradius and edge via ρ = sqrt(R² - a²/4). This holds for the uniform
Archimedean solids, whose every edge subtends the same midsphere.

methods.name: SurfaceArea
methods.doc:
SurfaceArea returns the surface area of the solid at edge length a, summed
exactly over its regular polygonal faces.

methods.name: TotalFaceCount
methods.doc:
TotalFaceCount returns the number of faces implied by the Faces breakdown; it
equals NumF for a consistent record and is useful as a self-check.

methods.name: Volume
methods.doc: Volume returns the volume of the solid at edge length a.

types.name: Edge
types.doc:
Edge is an undirected edge between two vertex indices. Its endpoints are
stored in ascending order (A <= B) so that edges compare and map canonically.

funcs.name: MakeEdge
funcs.doc:
MakeEdge returns the canonical Edge connecting vertex indices i and j, with
endpoints sorted so that A <= B.

types.name: FaceType
types.doc: FaceType records that a solid has Count regular faces each with Sides sides.

types.name: PlatonicSolid
types.doc:
PlatonicSolid captures the combinatorial and metric data of one of the five
regular convex polyhedra at a given edge length.

funcs.name: NewCube
funcs.doc: NewCube returns the cube (regular hexahedron) with the given edge length.

funcs.name: NewDodecahedron
funcs.doc: NewDodecahedron returns the regular dodecahedron with the given edge length.

funcs.name: NewIcosahedron
funcs.doc: NewIcosahedron returns the regular icosahedron with the given edge length.

funcs.name: NewOctahedron
funcs.doc: NewOctahedron returns the regular octahedron with the given edge length.

funcs.name: NewTetrahedron
funcs.doc: NewTetrahedron returns the regular tetrahedron with the given edge length.

funcs.name: PlatonicSolids
funcs.doc:
PlatonicSolids returns all five Platonic solids at the given edge length, in
order of increasing face count.

methods.name: Circumradius
methods.doc:
Circumradius returns the radius of the circumscribed sphere (through all
vertices).

methods.name: DihedralAngle
methods.doc:
DihedralAngle returns the interior dihedral angle in radians between two
adjacent faces, computed from the Schläfli symbol {p, q} as
2*arcsin(cos(π/q)/sin(π/p)).

methods.name: EulerCharacteristic
methods.doc: EulerCharacteristic returns V - E + F, which is 2 for every Platonic solid.

methods.name: Inradius
methods.doc: Inradius returns the radius of the inscribed sphere (tangent to every face).

methods.name: Mesh
methods.doc:
Mesh materialises the solid as a [Polyhedron] with exact vertex coordinates
and outward-oriented polygonal faces at the solid's edge length, centered at
the origin.

methods.name: Midradius
methods.doc:
Midradius returns the radius of the midsphere (tangent to every edge at its
midpoint).

methods.name: SurfaceArea
methods.doc: SurfaceArea returns the total surface area of the solid.

methods.name: Volume
methods.doc: Volume returns the enclosed volume of the solid.

types.name: Polyhedron
types.doc:
Polyhedron is a boundary representation of a polyhedron: a list of vertex
positions together with faces, each face being an ordered loop of vertex
indices. For a closed, consistently oriented mesh every face loop is listed
counter-clockwise when viewed from outside, so that face normals point
outward.

funcs.name: Bipyramid
funcs.signature: func Bipyramid(n int, a, h float64) *Polyhedron
funcs.doc:
Bipyramid returns the n-gonal bipyramid: two right pyramids joined base to
base, with apexes at heights ±h and a regular n-gon of edge a as the shared
equator, centered on the z-axis. It returns nil for n < 3.

funcs.name: ConvexHull
funcs.doc:
ConvexHull returns the convex hull of the given points as a triangulated
[Polyhedron] whose vertices are exactly the hull vertices (interior and
duplicate points are dropped) and whose triangular faces are oriented
counter-clockwise as seen from outside. It uses an incremental algorithm with
a deterministic symbolic-style perturbation so that coplanar inputs (such as
the corners of a box) are handled robustly. It returns ErrNotEnoughPoints when
the input has no three-dimensional hull.

funcs.name: ConvexHullPolygons
funcs.doc:
ConvexHullPolygons returns the convex hull of the given points with coplanar
triangles merged into maximal polygonal faces (so a box yields six
quadrilaterals rather than twelve triangles). It returns ErrNotEnoughPoints
when the input has no three-dimensional hull.

funcs.name: GiftWrapHull
funcs.doc:
GiftWrapHull returns the convex hull of the given points computed with a
gift-wrapping (pivoting) algorithm. It is slower than [ConvexHull] but
follows an independent derivation, which makes it useful as a cross-check. The
result is a triangulated, outward-oriented [Polyhedron]. It returns
ErrNotEnoughPoints when the input has no three-dimensional hull.

funcs.name: MergeCoplanarFaces
funcs.doc:
MergeCoplanarFaces returns a copy of p in which adjacent triangular (or
polygonal) faces that share an edge and lie in a common plane within angular
tolerance tol are merged into single polygonal faces with their boundary
loops ordered. It is intended for convex meshes such as those returned by
[ConvexHull].

funcs.name: NewPolyhedron
funcs.doc:
NewPolyhedron returns a Polyhedron with the given vertices and faces. The
slices are retained (not copied); callers that intend to keep mutating their
inputs should pass copies.

funcs.name: Prism
funcs.signature: func Prism(n int, a, h float64) *Polyhedron
funcs.doc:
Prism returns a right uniform n-gonal prism whose regular n-gon base has edge
length a and whose lateral height is h, centered on the z-axis. It returns nil
for n < 3.

funcs.name: Pyramid
funcs.signature: func Pyramid(n int, a, h float64) *Polyhedron
funcs.doc:
Pyramid returns a right pyramid over a regular n-gon base of edge a with apex
at height h above the base center, centered on the z-axis. It returns nil for
n < 3.

funcs.name: UniformAntiprism
funcs.doc:
UniformAntiprism returns the uniform n-gonal antiprism with edge length a: two
parallel regular n-gons, the top rotated by π/n relative to the bottom, joined
by 2n equilateral triangles, centered on the z-axis. It returns nil for n < 3.

methods.name: BoundingBox
methods.signature: func (p *Polyhedron) BoundingBox() (min, max Vec3, ok bool)
methods.doc:
BoundingBox returns the axis-aligned minimum and maximum corners of the vertex
set and true, or two zero vectors and false when there are no vertices.

methods.name: Centered
methods.doc:
Centered returns a copy of the polyhedron translated so that its solid
centroid lies at the origin.

methods.name: Centroid
methods.doc:
Centroid returns the centroid of the solid region enclosed by the mesh
(the center of mass of a uniform-density body) and true, or the vertex
centroid and true as a fallback for a mesh of negligible volume, or false when
the mesh has no faces. It uses the divergence-theorem decomposition into
signed tetrahedra.

methods.name: Circumradius
methods.doc:
Circumradius returns the maximum distance from the centroid c to any vertex.
It measures how far the outermost vertices reach.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns a deep copy of the polyhedron.

methods.name: Dual
methods.doc:
Dual returns the polar/canonical dual polyhedron. Each face of p becomes a
vertex placed at that face's centroid, and each vertex of p becomes a face
whose vertices are the duals of the faces surrounding it, ordered
consistently around the original vertex. The mesh must be closed and convex
(as produced by the solid constructors and ConvexHull) for the result to be a
valid polyhedron; otherwise ErrEmptyMesh or ErrDegenerate is returned.

methods.name: EdgeLengths
methods.doc: EdgeLengths returns the length of every edge in EdgeList order.

methods.name: EdgeList
methods.doc:
EdgeList returns all distinct undirected edges of the polyhedron, sorted
lexicographically by endpoint indices.

methods.name: EulerCharacteristic
methods.doc:
EulerCharacteristic returns V - E + F. For a simply connected closed
polyhedron (topologically a sphere) it equals 2.

methods.name: FaceArea
methods.doc: FaceArea returns the area of face i.

methods.name: FaceAreas
methods.doc: FaceAreas returns the area of every face.

methods.name: FaceCentroid
methods.doc:
FaceCentroid returns the area-weighted centroid of face i and true, or the
zero vector and false when face i is empty.

methods.name: FaceCentroids
methods.doc:
FaceCentroids returns the centroid of every face (the zero vector for any
empty face).

methods.name: FaceLoop
methods.doc: FaceLoop returns the vertex positions of face i as an ordered slice.

methods.name: FaceNormal
methods.doc:
FaceNormal returns the outward unit normal of face i (via Newell's method) and
true, or the zero vector and false when the face is degenerate.

methods.name: FaceNormals
methods.doc:
FaceNormals returns the unit normal of every face. Degenerate faces yield the
zero vector.

methods.name: FaceSides
methods.doc: FaceSides returns the number of sides (vertices) of every face.

methods.name: FacesAroundVertex
methods.doc:
FacesAroundVertex returns the indices of the faces incident to vertex v, in
ascending face-index order.

methods.name: Inradius
methods.doc:
Inradius returns the minimum distance from the centroid to any face plane,
i.e. the radius of the largest sphere centered at the centroid that fits
inside the mesh (exact for a convex polyhedron).

methods.name: IsClosed
methods.doc:
IsClosed reports whether the mesh is closed, i.e. every undirected edge is
shared by exactly two faces.

methods.name: Midradius
methods.doc:
Midradius returns the mean distance from the centroid to the edge midpoints
(the midradius of a canonical solid, where all such distances coincide).

methods.name: MinVertexRadius
methods.doc: MinVertexRadius returns the minimum distance from the centroid to any vertex.

methods.name: NumEdges
methods.doc: NumEdges returns the number of distinct undirected edges across all faces.

methods.name: NumFaces
methods.doc: NumFaces returns the number of faces.

methods.name: NumVertices
methods.doc: NumVertices returns the number of vertices.

methods.name: OrientOutward
methods.doc:
OrientOutward returns a copy of the mesh in which every face is oriented so
that its normal points away from the mesh centroid. This is correct for convex
polyhedra and any mesh that is star-shaped from its centroid.

methods.name: ScaledBy
methods.doc:
ScaledBy returns a copy of the polyhedron with every vertex scaled about the
origin by the factor s.

methods.name: SignedVolume
methods.doc:
SignedVolume returns the signed enclosed volume computed from the divergence
theorem by fanning each face into triangles from its first vertex and summing
signed tetrahedron volumes to the origin. For a closed mesh whose faces are
