HermiteHe evaluates the probabilists' Hermite polynomial He_n at x using the
recurrence He_{k+1} = x He_k - k He_{k-1}.

funcs.name: IntegrateBoxGaussLegendre
funcs.signature: func IntegrateBoxGaussLegendre(f FuncN, lower, upper []float64, nPerDim int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateBoxGaussLegendre approximates the integral of f over the box given
by lower and upper corners using an nPerDim-point tensor Gauss-Legendre rule
in every dimension. The cost grows as nPerDim^dim, so it is intended for low
dimensions.

funcs.name: IntegrateFejer1
funcs.signature: func IntegrateFejer1(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateFejer1 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with Fejer's
first rule of n points.

funcs.name: IntegrateFejer2
funcs.signature: func IntegrateFejer2(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateFejer2 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with Fejer's
second rule of n points.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussChebyshev1
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussChebyshev1(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussChebyshev1 approximates the integral of f(x)/sqrt(1-x^2) over
[-1, 1] with the n-point first-kind Gauss-Chebyshev rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussChebyshev2
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussChebyshev2(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussChebyshev2 approximates the integral of f(x)*sqrt(1-x^2) over
[-1, 1] with the n-point second-kind Gauss-Chebyshev rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussChebyshev3
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussChebyshev3(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussChebyshev3 approximates the integral of
f(x)*sqrt((1+x)/(1-x)) over [-1, 1] with the n-point third-kind rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussChebyshev4
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussChebyshev4(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussChebyshev4 approximates the integral of
f(x)*sqrt((1-x)/(1+x)) over [-1, 1] with the n-point fourth-kind rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussHermite
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussHermite(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussHermite approximates the integral of f(x)*e^{-x^2} over the
whole real line with the n-point Gauss-Hermite rule (physicists'
convention).

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussHermiteFunc
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussHermiteFunc(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussHermiteFunc approximates the integral of f (without any weight
factor) over the whole real line by folding the Gaussian weight back into
the summand: the summand is f(x_i)*e^{x_i^2}*w_i. It is accurate when f
decays at least as fast as a Gaussian.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussHermiteProb
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussHermiteProb(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussHermiteProb approximates the integral of f(x)*e^{-x^2/2} over
the whole real line with the n-point probabilists' Gauss-Hermite rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussJacobi
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussJacobi(f Func, n int, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussJacobi approximates the integral of f(x)*(1-x)^a*(1+x)^b over
[-1, 1] with the n-point Gauss-Jacobi rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussKronrod
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussKronrod(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussKronrod is an alias for GaussKronrod15Eval returning only the
integral estimate over [a, b].

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussLaguerre
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussLaguerre(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussLaguerre approximates the integral of f(x)*e^{-x} over
[0, inf) with the n-point Gauss-Laguerre rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussLaguerreFunc
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussLaguerreFunc(f Func, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussLaguerreFunc approximates the integral of f (without any
weight factor) over [0, inf) by folding the e^{-x} weight back in: the
summand is f(x_i)*e^{x_i}*w_i.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussLaguerreGen
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussLaguerreGen(f Func, n int, a float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussLaguerreGen approximates the integral of f(x)*x^a*e^{-x} over
[0, inf) with the n-point generalized Gauss-Laguerre rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussLegendre
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussLegendre(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussLegendre approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with the
n-point Gauss-Legendre rule, mapping the canonical nodes onto [a, b].

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussLobatto
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussLobatto(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussLobatto approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with the
n-point Gauss-Lobatto rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateGaussRadau
funcs.signature: func IntegrateGaussRadau(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateGaussRadau approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with the
n-point left Gauss-Radau rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateInfinite
funcs.signature: func IntegrateInfinite(f Func, tol float64) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateInfinite approximates the integral of f over the entire real line
using the substitution x = t/(1-t^2), t in (-1, 1), evaluated with the
tanh-sinh rule. It is a convenient default when f is smooth and decays
polynomially.

funcs.name: IntegrateNewtonCotesClosed
funcs.signature: func IntegrateNewtonCotesClosed(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateNewtonCotesClosed approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with a
single closed (n+1)-point Newton-Cotes rule of order n.

funcs.name: IntegrateNewtonCotesOpen
funcs.signature: func IntegrateNewtonCotesOpen(f Func, a, b float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateNewtonCotesOpen approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with a
single open n-point Newton-Cotes rule.

funcs.name: IntegrateProduct2
funcs.signature: func IntegrateProduct2(f Func2, rx, ry Rule) float64
funcs.doc:
IntegrateProduct2 applies the tensor product of two one-dimensional rules to
a bivariate function, evaluating f at every (node_i, node_j) pair weighted
by the product of the corresponding weights.

funcs.name: IntegrateSamples
funcs.doc:
IntegrateSamples integrates uniformly spaced samples ys with spacing h,
selecting the highest-order composite rule the sample count permits: Boole
when the number of panels is a multiple of four, otherwise Simpson when even,
otherwise the trapezoidal rule.

funcs.name: JacobiP
funcs.signature: func JacobiP(n int, a, b, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
JacobiP evaluates the Jacobi polynomial P_n^{(a,b)} at x using the standard
three-term recurrence. Legendre is a = b = 0, Chebyshev of the first kind is
a = b = -1/2, and so on.

funcs.name: LaguerreL
funcs.signature: func LaguerreL(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LaguerreL evaluates the Laguerre polynomial L_n at x using the recurrence
(k+1) L_{k+1} = (2k+1-x) L_k - k L_{k-1}.

funcs.name: LaguerreLGen
funcs.signature: func LaguerreLGen(n int, a, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LaguerreLGen evaluates the generalized Laguerre polynomial L_n^{(a)} at x
using the recurrence (k+1) L_{k+1} = (2k+1+a-x) L_k - (k+a) L_{k-1}.

funcs.name: LegendreP
funcs.signature: func LegendreP(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LegendreP evaluates the Legendre polynomial of degree n at x using the
stable three-term recurrence. It is exposed because the Legendre polynomials
underlie the Lobatto and Radau rules.

funcs.name: LegendrePDeriv
funcs.signature: func LegendrePDeriv(n int, x float64) float64
funcs.doc:
LegendrePDeriv evaluates the derivative of the Legendre polynomial of degree
n at x.

funcs.name: Midpoint
funcs.signature: func Midpoint(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Midpoint approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with the single-panel
midpoint rule (b-a)*f((a+b)/2), exact for linear functions.

funcs.name: MidpointSamples
funcs.doc:
MidpointSamples integrates samples ys, interpreted as the values of f at the
midpoints of n equal panels of width h, using the midpoint rule.

funcs.name: NewtonCotesClosed
funcs.doc:
NewtonCotesClosed returns the closed (n+1)-point Newton-Cotes weights as
float64 values (see NewtonCotesClosedExact).

funcs.name: NewtonCotesClosedExact
funcs.doc:
NewtonCotesClosedExact returns the exact rational weights of the closed
(n+1)-point Newton-Cotes rule on the reference interval [0, n], scaled so
that the integral over [a, b] is (b-a)/n times the weighted sum of the
samples. The returned weights sum to n. Panics for n < 1.

funcs.name: NewtonCotesOpen
funcs.doc:
NewtonCotesOpen returns the open n-point Newton-Cotes weights as float64
values (see NewtonCotesOpenExact).

funcs.name: NewtonCotesOpenExact
funcs.doc:
NewtonCotesOpenExact returns the exact rational weights of the open n-point
Newton-Cotes rule with interior nodes at 1,2,...,n on the reference interval
[0, n+1], scaled so that the integral over [a, b] is (b-a)/(n+1) times the
weighted sum. Panics for n < 1.

funcs.name: RecurrenceChebyshev1
funcs.doc:
RecurrenceChebyshev1 returns the monic recurrence coefficients for the
Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind (weight 1/sqrt(1-x^2) on [-1, 1]).
beta[0] = pi, beta[1] = 1/2 and beta[k] = 1/4 for k >= 2.

funcs.name: RecurrenceChebyshev2
funcs.doc:
RecurrenceChebyshev2 returns the monic recurrence coefficients for the
Chebyshev polynomials of the second kind (weight sqrt(1-x^2) on [-1, 1]).
beta[0] = pi/2 and beta[k] = 1/4 for k >= 1.

funcs.name: RecurrenceHermite
funcs.doc:
RecurrenceHermite returns the monic recurrence coefficients for the
physicists' Hermite polynomials (weight e^{-x^2} on the whole real line).
beta[0] = sqrt(pi) and beta[k] = k/2.

funcs.name: RecurrenceHermiteProb
funcs.doc:
RecurrenceHermiteProb returns the monic recurrence coefficients for the
probabilists' Hermite polynomials (weight e^{-x^2/2} on the whole real
line). beta[0] = sqrt(2*pi) and beta[k] = k.

funcs.name: RecurrenceJacobi
funcs.signature: func RecurrenceJacobi(n int, a, b float64) (alpha, beta []float64)
funcs.doc:
RecurrenceJacobi returns the monic recurrence coefficients for the Jacobi
polynomials with parameters a and b (weight (1-x)^a * (1+x)^b on [-1, 1]).
It unifies the Legendre (a=b=0), Chebyshev and Gegenbauer families. Both
parameters must be greater than -1.

funcs.name: RecurrenceLaguerre
funcs.signature: func RecurrenceLaguerre(n int, a float64) (alpha, beta []float64)
funcs.doc:
RecurrenceLaguerre returns the monic recurrence coefficients for the
generalized Laguerre polynomials with parameter a (weight x^a * e^{-x} on
[0, inf)). alpha[k] = 2k+a+1, beta[0] = Gamma(a+1) and beta[k] = k*(k+a).
Standard Laguerre corresponds to a = 0.

funcs.name: RecurrenceLegendre
funcs.doc:
RecurrenceLegendre returns the monic three-term recurrence coefficients for
the Legendre polynomials (weight 1 on [-1, 1]) for an n-point rule. The
returned alpha has length n and beta has length n with beta[0] equal to the
zeroth moment mu0 = 2.

funcs.name: Rescale
funcs.signature: func Rescale(nodes, weights []float64, a, b float64) (rn, rw []float64)
funcs.doc:
Rescale maps parallel node/weight slices from the canonical interval [-1, 1]
to [a, b], returning fresh slices. The weights are multiplied by the
Jacobian (b-a)/2.

funcs.name: RichardsonExtrapolate
funcs.signature: func RichardsonExtrapolate(coarse, fine, ratio, order float64) float64
funcs.doc:
RichardsonExtrapolate performs one step of Richardson extrapolation on two
estimates A(h) and A(h/ratio) of the same quantity whose leading error term
is proportional to h^order, returning the higher-order combination
(ratio^order * fine - coarse) / (ratio^order - 1).

funcs.name: RombergTable
funcs.signature: func RombergTable(f Func, a, b float64, k int) [][]float64
funcs.doc:
RombergTable computes the full Romberg extrapolation table for the integral
of f over [a, b] using k levels of trapezoidal refinement (rows 0..k-1). The
returned lower-triangular table has T[i][j] with T[i][0] the composite
trapezoid estimate on 2^i subintervals and T[i][j] the j-th Richardson
extrapolation. The best estimate is T[k-1][k-1].

funcs.name: RombergValue
funcs.signature: func RombergValue(f Func, a, b float64, k int) float64
funcs.doc:
RombergValue approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with the k-level
Romberg table, returning only the best estimate T[k-1][k-1].

funcs.name: Simpson
funcs.signature: func Simpson(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Simpson approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with Simpson's 1/3 rule
using the two endpoints and the midpoint, exact for cubics.

funcs.name: Simpson38
funcs.signature: func Simpson38(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Simpson38 approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with Simpson's 3/8
rule using four equally spaced points, exact for cubics.

funcs.name: SimpsonSamples
funcs.doc:
SimpsonSamples integrates uniformly spaced samples ys with spacing h using
the composite Simpson rule. It requires an odd number of samples (an even
number of subintervals); when given an even count it falls back to the
trapezoidal correction on the final panel.

funcs.name: SinhSinh
funcs.signature: func SinhSinh(f Func, tol float64) float64
funcs.doc:
SinhSinh approximates the integral of f over the entire real line
(-inf, inf) using the sinh-sinh double-exponential transformation
x = sinh((pi/2) sinh t). It is suited to functions that decay
exponentially or faster at both infinities.

funcs.name: TanhSinh
funcs.signature: func TanhSinh(f Func, a, b, tol float64) float64
funcs.doc:
TanhSinh approximates the integral of f over the finite interval [a, b]
using the tanh-sinh (double-exponential) rule. The rule is robust against
integrable singularities at the endpoints because its weights decay
doubly-exponentially there. It returns the estimate to the requested
tolerance.

funcs.name: Trapezoid
funcs.signature: func Trapezoid(f Func, a, b float64) float64
funcs.doc:
Trapezoid approximates the integral of f over [a, b] with the single-panel
trapezoidal rule (b-a)/2*(f(a)+f(b)), exact for linear functions.

funcs.name: TrapezoidNonUniform
funcs.doc:
TrapezoidNonUniform integrates samples ys taken at the (not necessarily
equally spaced) abscissae xs using the trapezoidal rule. The slices must
have equal length of at least two.

funcs.name: TrapezoidSamples
funcs.doc:
TrapezoidSamples integrates uniformly spaced samples ys with spacing h using
the composite trapezoidal rule. It requires at least two samples.

funcs.name: TripleGaussLegendre
funcs.signature: func TripleGaussLegendre(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleGaussLegendre approximates the triple integral of f over the box
[ax, bx] x [ay, by] x [az, bz] using the n-point Gauss-Legendre rule in each
direction.

funcs.name: TripleMidpoint
funcs.signature: func TripleMidpoint(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleMidpoint approximates the triple integral of f over the box using the
composite midpoint rule with nx, ny and nz subintervals.

funcs.name: TripleSimpson
funcs.signature: func TripleSimpson(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleSimpson approximates the triple integral of f over the box using the
composite Simpson rule with nx, ny and nz subintervals.

funcs.name: TripleTrapezoid
funcs.signature: func TripleTrapezoid(f Func3, ax, bx, ay, by, az, bz float64, nx, ny, nz int) float64
funcs.doc:
TripleTrapezoid approximates the triple integral of f over the box using the
composite trapezoidal rule with nx, ny and nz subintervals.

funcs.name: VanDerCorput
funcs.doc:
VanDerCorput returns the i-th element (i >= 0) of the van der Corput
low-discrepancy sequence in the given base, a number in [0, 1) formed by
reflecting the base-b digits of i about the radix point.

examples.name: AdaptiveSimpson
examples.code:
got := AdaptiveSimpson(math.Sin, 0, math.Pi, 1e-10)
fmt.Printf("%.10f\n", got)
examples.doc: ExampleAdaptiveSimpson demonstrates integrating a smooth function.

examples.name: GaussLegendre
examples.code:
nodes, weights := GaussLegendre(2)
fmt.Printf("nodes: %.6f, %.6f\n", nodes[0], nodes[1])
fmt.Printf("weights: %.6f, %.6f\n", weights[0], weights[1])
examples.doc: ExampleGaussLegendre shows generating nodes and weights.

examples.name: TanhSinh
examples.code:
got := TanhSinh(func(x float64) float64 { return 1 / math.Sqrt(x) }, 0, 1, 1e-9)
fmt.Printf("%.5f\n", got)
examples.doc: ExampleTanhSinh integrates a function with an endpoint singularity.

packages.name: quasirandom
packages.synopsis: Package quasirandom implements low-discrepancy (quasi-random) sequences and the numerical machinery that surrounds them.
packages.doc:
Package quasirandom implements low-discrepancy (quasi-random) sequences and
the numerical machinery that surrounds them.

Quasi-random, or quasi-Monte-Carlo (QMC), point sets fill the unit cube
[0,1)^d far more evenly than independent pseudo-random samples. Their
defining quantity is the discrepancy, a measure of how far the empirical
distribution of the points departs from the uniform distribution. The
Koksma–Hlawka inequality bounds the error of an equal-weight cubature rule by
the product of the star discrepancy of the nodes and the variation of the
integrand, so sequences with asymptotically small discrepancy — of order
(log N)^d / N rather than the 1/sqrt(N) of plain Monte Carlo — give faster,
deterministic convergence.

The package provides:

  - The radical inverse and the van der Corput sequence in any base, with
    digit scrambling and permutation-based (Faure–Tezuka style) scrambling.
  - Halton sequences in arbitrary dimension (prime and custom bases, leaped
    and scrambled variants) and the finite Hammersley point sets.
  - Faure sequences built from the generalized Pascal generator matrices over
    a prime base at least as large as the dimension.
  - Sobol sequences generated from primitive polynomials over GF(2) and their
    direction numbers, using the Gray-code recurrence, with a built-in table
    of direction numbers and support for user-supplied ones.
  - Closed-form L2-type discrepancies (Warnock, centered, wrap-around and
    symmetric), the exact one-dimensional star discrepancy and an exact
    grid-based star discrepancy for small point sets.
  - QMC integration helpers over the unit cube together with the
    Koksma–Hlawka error bound.

Every generator is fully deterministic: no source of randomness is consulted
and results depend only on the requested indices, bases and dimensions.
Only the Go standard library is used.

consts.doc:
GoldenRatio is the golden ratio phi = (1+sqrt5)/2, the optimal irrational for
a one-dimensional Kronecker (Weyl) sequence.

GoldenRatioConjugate is 1/phi = phi-1 = 0.6180339887..., the increment of the
canonical golden-ratio Weyl sequence.

vars.signature: var ErrBadBase = errors.New("quasirandom: base must be at least 2")
vars.doc: ErrBadBase is returned when a radix argument is smaller than two.

vars.signature: var ErrDimension = errors.New("quasirandom: dimension out of range")
vars.doc: ErrDimension is returned when a dimension argument is out of range.

vars.signature: var ErrEmptyPointSet = errors.New("quasirandom: empty point set")
vars.doc: ErrEmptyPointSet is returned when a discrepancy routine is given no points.

vars.signature: var ErrNonPositive = errors.New("quasirandom: argument must be positive")
vars.doc:
ErrNonPositive is returned when a strictly positive argument was required but
a value less than one was supplied.

vars.signature: var ErrOutOfUnitCube = errors.New("quasirandom: coordinate outside the unit cube")
vars.doc: ErrOutOfUnitCube is returned when a point coordinate lies outside [0,1].

vars.signature: var ErrRaggedPointSet = errors.New("quasirandom: points have differing dimensions")
vars.doc:
ErrRaggedPointSet is returned when the points of a set do not all share the
same dimension.

types.name: Integrand
types.signature: type Integrand func(x []float64) float64
types.doc:
Integrand is a scalar function of a point in R^d used by the integration
helpers.

types.name: Sobol
types.doc:
Sobol is a stateful generator of the multi-dimensional Sobol sequence. It
yields successive points in Gray-code order via the Antonov–Saleev recurrence
and may also address any point directly. The zero value is not usable; obtain
one from NewSobol.

funcs.name: NewSobol
funcs.doc:
NewSobol returns a Sobol generator for the given dimension using the built-in
direction numbers. The first point produced by Next is the origin. It returns
an error when dim is out of the supported range.

methods.name: Dimension
methods.doc: Dimension returns the number of coordinates the generator produces.

methods.name: Index
methods.doc:
Index returns the zero-based index of the point that the next call to Next
will return.

methods.name: Next
methods.doc:
Next returns the current Sobol point and advances the generator by one index
using the Antonov–Saleev Gray-code recurrence. The returned slice is freshly
allocated and owned by the caller.

methods.name: Point
methods.doc:
Point returns the n-th Sobol point (zero-based) directly, without disturbing
the generator's streaming state, by expanding the Gray code of n.

methods.name: Reset
methods.doc:
Reset returns the generator to its initial state, so the next call to Next
again yields the origin.

methods.name: Skip
methods.doc:
Skip advances the generator by k indices without returning the intervening
points, leaving it positioned so that the next Next returns point Index()+k.

funcs.name: AdditiveRecurrenceSequence
funcs.signature: func AdditiveRecurrenceSequence(seed, alpha float64, count int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
AdditiveRecurrenceSequence returns count terms of the one-dimensional additive
recurrence x_{n} = frac(seed + n*alpha), a shifted Weyl sequence.

funcs.name: AffinePermutation
funcs.doc:
AffinePermutation returns the permutation i -> (a*i + b) mod n over the digit
set {0,...,n-1}. When a is coprime to n this is a bijection and hence a valid
scrambling permutation; otherwise the returned slice is not a permutation.

funcs.name: ApplyPermutation
funcs.doc:
ApplyPermutation returns a new slice with entry i taken from xs[p[i]],
reordering xs by the permutation p. It returns nil when the lengths differ.

funcs.name: AreCoprimeBases
funcs.doc:
AreCoprimeBases reports whether every pair of the supplied bases is coprime,
the condition under which a multi-dimensional Halton construction is
well distributed. An empty or singleton list is trivially coprime.

funcs.name: Bernoulli2
funcs.doc:
Bernoulli2 returns the second Bernoulli polynomial B2(x) = x^2 - x + 1/6,
the building block of the diaphony's reproducing kernel.

funcs.name: BigBinomial
funcs.doc:
BigBinomial returns the binomial coefficient C(n,k) as an exact big.Int. It
returns an error when n or k is negative.

funcs.name: BigFactorial
funcs.doc:
BigFactorial returns n! as a big.Int, a small helper occasionally useful when
working with the exact combinatorics behind Faure generator matrices. It
returns an error when n is negative.

funcs.name: BitLength
funcs.doc:
BitLength returns the number of bits required to represent n, i.e. the index
of the highest set bit plus one; BitLength(0)==0.

funcs.name: BoundingBox
funcs.doc:
BoundingBox returns the per-coordinate minimum and maximum of a point set.
It returns an error for an empty or ragged set.

funcs.name: BoxToUnit
funcs.doc:
BoxToUnit maps a point set from the axis-aligned box [lower,upper] back into
the unit cube, the inverse of ScaleToBox. It returns an error when the shapes
disagree or a side has zero length.

funcs.name: CenteredL2Discrepancy
funcs.doc:
CenteredL2Discrepancy returns Hickernell's centered L2-discrepancy, which is
invariant under reflection of the point set about the center of the cube and
accounts for all lower-dimensional projections. It returns an error for an
invalid point set.

funcs.name: CenteredL2DiscrepancySquared
funcs.doc:
CenteredL2DiscrepancySquared returns the square of the centered
L2-discrepancy. It returns an error for an invalid point set.

funcs.name: Centroid
funcs.doc:
Centroid returns the coordinate-wise mean of a point set. It returns an error
for an empty or ragged set.

funcs.name: ClampUnit
funcs.doc: ClampUnit clamps x into the closed unit interval [0,1].

funcs.name: ComposePermutations
funcs.doc:
ComposePermutations returns the permutation r with r[i] = p[q[i]], the effect
of applying q and then p. It returns nil when the lengths differ or either
argument is not a permutation.

funcs.name: CoordinateMean
funcs.doc:
CoordinateMean returns the mean of coordinate k over the point set. It returns
an error for an empty set or an out-of-range coordinate index.

funcs.name: CoordinateMeans
funcs.doc:
CoordinateMeans returns the vector of per-coordinate means of a point set. It
returns an error for an empty or ragged set.

funcs.name: CoordinateVariance
funcs.doc:
CoordinateVariance returns the population variance of coordinate k over the
point set. It returns an error for an empty set or an out-of-range index.

funcs.name: CoordinateVariances
funcs.doc:
CoordinateVariances returns the vector of per-coordinate population variances
of a point set. It returns an error for an empty or ragged set.

funcs.name: CountInBox
funcs.doc:
CountInBox returns the number of points lying in the half-open anchored box
[0,t) whose upper corner is t. It returns an error when a point and t have
different lengths.

funcs.name: CountPrimesBelow
funcs.doc:
CountPrimesBelow returns the number of primes strictly less than n
(the prime-counting function pi evaluated just below n).

funcs.name: Diaphony
funcs.doc:
Diaphony returns the classical (Zinterhof) diaphony of a point set, a
Fourier-based uniformity measure with the closed form
F_N^2 = (1/N^2) sum_{i,j} [ prod_k (1 + 2 pi^2 B2({x_ik - x_jk})) - 1 ],
where {} denotes the fractional part. Like the L2 discrepancies it vanishes
only in the limit of perfect equidistribution. It returns an error for an
invalid point set.

funcs.name: DigitAt
funcs.signature: func DigitAt(n uint64, base, pos int) (int, error)
funcs.doc:
DigitAt returns the base-b digit of n at position pos (position zero is the
least significant digit). Positions beyond the length of n yield zero. It
returns an error when base < 2 or pos is negative.

funcs.name: DigitCount
funcs.signature: func DigitCount(n uint64, base int) (int, error)
funcs.doc:
DigitCount returns the number of base-b digits required to represent n, with
zero requiring zero digits. It returns an error when base < 2.

funcs.name: DigitScramble
funcs.signature: func DigitScramble(base int, n uint64, perm []int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
DigitScramble returns the value obtained by scrambling the base-b digits of
the radical inverse of n with the single permutation perm applied to every
digit position, equivalent to ScrambledRadicalInverse.

funcs.name: DigitSum
funcs.signature: func DigitSum(n uint64, base int) (int, error)
funcs.doc:
DigitSum returns the sum of the base-b digits of n. It returns an error when
base < 2.

funcs.name: Digits
funcs.signature: func Digits(n uint64, base int) ([]int, error)
funcs.doc:
Digits returns the base-b representation of the non-negative integer n as a
slice of digits ordered from least significant to most significant. The
value zero yields an empty slice. It returns an error when base < 2 or n is
negative.

funcs.name: DigitsPadded
funcs.signature: func DigitsPadded(n uint64, base, width int) ([]int, error)
funcs.doc:
DigitsBig behaves like Digits but always returns a slice of length at least
width, left-padding the most-significant end with zeros. A width of zero is
equivalent to Digits.

funcs.name: DirectionNumbers
funcs.doc:
DirectionNumbers returns the fractional direction numbers m_i / 2^i for the
given Sobol dimension, one value per bit of resolution, as computed from the
recurrence generated by the dimension's primitive polynomial. It returns an
error when dim is out of range.

funcs.name: EuclideanDistance
funcs.doc:
EuclideanDistance returns the Euclidean distance between two equal-length
points. It returns an error when the lengths disagree.

funcs.name: EvaluateSamples
funcs.doc:
EvaluateSamples returns the slice of function values f(p) for each point p,
the raw samples underlying the QMC average and its error estimates.

funcs.name: Faure
funcs.signature: func Faure(dim int, n uint64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Faure returns the n-th point (zero-based) of the Faure sequence in the given
dimension. All coordinates share the base returned by FaureBase and are the
images of n under successive powers of the Pascal generator matrix. The point
lies in [0,1)^dim. It returns an error when dim < 1.

funcs.name: FaureBase
funcs.doc:
FaureBase returns the base used by the Faure sequence in the given dimension:
the smallest prime greater than or equal to the dimension (and at least two).
It returns an error when dim < 1.

funcs.name: FaureGeneratorMatrix
funcs.signature: func FaureGeneratorMatrix(base, power, size int) ([][]int, error)
funcs.doc:
FaureGeneratorMatrix returns the power-th power of the upper-triangular Pascal
generator matrix modulo base, truncated to size rows and columns. Entry (i,j)
equals C(j,i) * power^(j-i) mod base for j >= i and zero below the diagonal,
which is the generating matrix applied to coordinate index power of a Faure
sequence in the given base. It returns an error when base < 2 or size < 0.

funcs.name: FaurePermutation
funcs.doc:
FaurePermutation returns Faure's deterministic scrambling permutation of the
digit set {0,...,base-1}. The permutations are defined recursively — even
bases interleave two copies of the half-base permutation, odd bases splice
the fixed middle digit into the shifted previous permutation — and are the
standard choice for improving the uniformity of scrambled Halton and
van der Corput sequences. For base < 2 the identity of that size is returned.

funcs.name: FaureSequence
funcs.doc:
FaureSequence returns the first count points of the dim-dimensional Faure
sequence beginning at index zero.

funcs.name: FaureSequenceOffset
funcs.signature: func FaureSequenceOffset(dim, start, count int) ([][]float64, error)
funcs.doc: FaureSequenceOffset returns count Faure points starting at index start.

funcs.name: FaureWithBase
funcs.signature: func FaureWithBase(dim, base int, n uint64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
FaureWithBase returns the n-th Faure point using an explicit prime base, which
must be at least as large as dim for the construction to be a genuine
(t,s)-sequence. It returns an error when dim < 1 or base < 2.

funcs.name: Fibonacci
funcs.doc:
Fibonacci returns the n-th Fibonacci number with Fibonacci(0)==0,
Fibonacci(1)==1. It returns zero for negative n.

funcs.name: FibonacciLattice
funcs.doc:
FibonacciLattice returns the two-dimensional Fibonacci lattice with
Fibonacci(m) points, the rank-1 lattice with generating vector
(1, Fibonacci(m-1)); it is the classic optimal two-dimensional lattice rule.
It returns an error when m < 2.

funcs.name: FillDistance
funcs.doc:
FillDistance returns the fill distance (covering radius) of a point set with
respect to a regular test grid of res divisions per dimension: the largest
distance from any grid node to its nearest sample point. It returns an error
for an empty set or res < 1.

funcs.name: FoldedRadicalInverse
funcs.signature: func FoldedRadicalInverse(base int, n uint64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
FoldedRadicalInverse returns the folded (Hammersley–Kronecker) radical
inverse, in which digit position k contributes (d_k + k) mod base rather than
d_k. The folding removes the correlation artefacts of the plain radical
inverse for large bases. It returns an error when base < 2.

funcs.name: Frac
funcs.doc:
Frac returns the fractional part of x in the half-open interval [0,1),
computed as x - floor(x) so that negative inputs also map into [0,1).

funcs.name: FromDigits
funcs.doc:
FromDigits reconstructs the integer whose base-b digits (least significant
first) are given. It returns an error when base < 2 or a digit is out of the
range [0,base).

funcs.name: GCD
funcs.doc: GCD returns the greatest common divisor of a and b, with GCD(0,0)==0.

funcs.name: GoldenRatioSequence
funcs.doc:
GoldenRatioSequence returns count terms of the Weyl sequence generated by the
golden ratio, the best-distributed one-dimensional additive sequence.

funcs.name: GrayCode
funcs.doc: GrayCode returns the reflected binary Gray code of n, namely n XOR (n>>1).

funcs.name: Halton
funcs.signature: func Halton(dim int, n uint64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Halton returns the n-th point (zero-based) of the Halton sequence in the
given dimension, using the first dim prime numbers as the coordinate bases.
The returned point lies in [0,1)^dim. It returns an error when dim < 1.

funcs.name: HaltonBig
funcs.doc:
HaltonBig returns the n-th Halton point for an index given as a big.Int, using
the first dim primes as bases and returning each coordinate as a float64. It
returns an error when dim < 1 or n is negative.

funcs.name: HaltonCoordinate
funcs.signature: func HaltonCoordinate(dim, k int, n uint64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
HaltonCoordinate returns coordinate k (zero-based) of the n-th Halton point in
the given dimension, the radical inverse of n in the (k+1)-th prime. It
returns an error when dim < 1 or k is out of range.

funcs.name: HaltonSequence
funcs.doc:
HaltonSequence returns the first count points of the dim-dimensional Halton
sequence starting from index zero as a slice of points.

funcs.name: HaltonSequenceOffset
funcs.signature: func HaltonSequenceOffset(dim, start, count int) ([][]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
HaltonSequenceOffset returns count Halton points beginning at index start.
Skipping a leading burn-in of a few dozen points is a common way to reduce
the correlation between the low-index terms of high-dimensional Halton
sequences.

funcs.name: HaltonWithBases
funcs.doc:
HaltonWithBases returns the n-th Halton point using the supplied coordinate
bases, one per dimension. The bases should be pairwise coprime for the point
set to be well distributed. It returns an error when no bases are given or a
base is smaller than two.

funcs.name: Hammersley
funcs.signature: func Hammersley(dim, i, total int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
Hammersley returns the i-th point (zero-based, 0 <= i < total) of the
dim-dimensional Hammersley point set of total points. The first coordinate is
i/total and the remaining dim-1 coordinates are radical inverses in the first
dim-1 primes, giving a finite set whose star discrepancy is asymptotically
smaller than the corresponding Halton sequence. It returns an error when
dim < 1, total < 1 or i is out of range.

funcs.name: HammersleySet
funcs.doc:
HammersleySet returns the complete dim-dimensional Hammersley point set of
total points as a slice of points.

funcs.name: HammersleyWithBases
funcs.signature: func HammersleyWithBases(bases []int, i, total int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
HammersleyWithBases returns the i-th Hammersley point whose trailing
coordinates use the supplied bases (one per trailing dimension, so the point
has len(bases)+1 coordinates). The leading coordinate remains i/total.

funcs.name: IdentityPermutation
funcs.doc:
IdentityPermutation returns the identity permutation (0,1,...,n-1). It returns
an empty slice for non-positive n.

funcs.name: InitialDirectionNumbers
funcs.doc:
InitialDirectionNumbers returns the initial direction integers m_1..m_s for
the given Sobol dimension. Dimension one returns the single value one.
It returns an error when dim is out of range.

funcs.name: IntegrateBox
funcs.signature: func IntegrateBox(f Integrand, lower, upper []float64, points [][]float64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
IntegrateBox estimates the integral of f over the axis-aligned box
[lower,upper] by mapping the unit-cube points into the box and multiplying the
average by the box volume. lower and upper must have the same length as each
point. It returns an error when the shapes disagree or no points are given.

funcs.name: IntegrateUnitCube
funcs.doc:
IntegrateUnitCube estimates the integral of f over the unit cube [0,1]^d as
the equal-weight average of f over the supplied quasi-random points. It
returns an error when no points are given.

funcs.name: InverseGrayCode
funcs.doc: InverseGrayCode returns the integer whose Gray code is g, inverting GrayCode.

funcs.name: InvertPermutation
funcs.doc:
InvertPermutation returns the inverse of the permutation p, so that
InvertPermutation(p)[p[i]] == i. It returns nil when p is not a permutation.

funcs.name: IsPermutation
funcs.doc:
IsPermutation reports whether p is a permutation of {0,...,len(p)-1}: each
value appears exactly once and lies in range.

funcs.name: IsPowerOfTwo
funcs.doc: IsPowerOfTwo reports whether n is a positive power of two.

funcs.name: IsPrime
funcs.doc: IsPrime reports whether n is a prime number. Values below two are not prime.

funcs.name: KoksmaHlawkaBound
funcs.doc:
KoksmaHlawkaBound returns the Koksma–Hlawka upper bound on the absolute error
of an equal-weight cubature rule, namely variation * discrepancy, given the
Hardy–Krause variation of the integrand and the star discrepancy of the
nodes. Both arguments must be non-negative.

funcs.name: KorobovGenerator
funcs.signature: func KorobovGenerator(dim, a, total int) ([]int, error)
funcs.doc:
KorobovGenerator returns the Korobov generating vector (1, a, a^2, ..., a^{dim-1})
reduced modulo total. It returns an error when dim < 1 or total < 1.

funcs.name: KorobovLattice
funcs.signature: func KorobovLattice(dim, a, total int) ([][]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
KorobovLattice returns all total points of the Korobov lattice rule of the
given dimension with parameter a. It returns an error when dim < 1 or
total < 1.

funcs.name: KroneckerPoint
funcs.doc:
KroneckerPoint returns the n-th point (zero-based) of the multi-dimensional
Kronecker sequence with generating vector alpha, the fractional part of
n*alpha in each coordinate. It returns an error when alpha is empty.

funcs.name: KroneckerSequence
funcs.doc:
KroneckerSequence returns the first count points of the Kronecker sequence
with generating vector alpha.

funcs.name: L2StarDiscrepancy
funcs.doc:
L2StarDiscrepancy returns the L2 star discrepancy of the point set using
Warnock's closed-form formula, an O(d N^2) exact evaluation of the L2 norm of
the local discrepancy over all anchored boxes [0,t). It returns an error when
the point set is empty, ragged or leaves the unit cube.

funcs.name: L2StarDiscrepancySquared
funcs.doc:
L2StarDiscrepancySquared returns the square of the L2 star discrepancy,
avoiding the final square root when only the squared quantity is needed. It
returns an error for an invalid point set.

funcs.name: LeapedHalton
funcs.signature: func LeapedHalton(dim int, leap, n uint64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
LeapedHalton returns the n-th point of a leaped Halton sequence, which takes
every leap-th point of the ordinary sequence (index n*leap). Leaping by a
value coprime to the product of the bases is a simple decorrelation device.
It returns an error when dim < 1 or leap < 1.

funcs.name: LocalDiscrepancy
funcs.doc:
LocalDiscrepancy returns the signed local discrepancy of a point set at the
anchored box [0,t): the fraction of points inside minus the box volume. It
returns an error for an empty set or a corner of the wrong length.

funcs.name: LowestZeroBit
funcs.doc:
LowestZeroBit returns the position (from zero) of the lowest zero bit of n,
which drives the Antonov–Saleev Gray-code Sobol recurrence.

funcs.name: MaxSobolDimension
funcs.doc:
MaxSobolDimension returns the largest dimension for which built-in direction
numbers are available (dimensions 1 through this value inclusive).

funcs.name: MinimumDistance
funcs.doc:
MinimumDistance returns the smallest pairwise Euclidean distance in a point
set (twice the separation distance). It returns an error for a set with fewer
than two points.

funcs.name: ModifiedL2StarDiscrepancy
funcs.doc:
ModifiedL2StarDiscrepancy returns Hickernell's modified L2-star discrepancy,
which weights the anchored boxes so that low-dimensional projections
contribute. It returns an error for an invalid point set.

funcs.name: MonteCarloStandardError
funcs.doc:
MonteCarloStandardError returns the estimated standard error of the sample
mean, sqrt(SampleVariance/n). It is the classical error estimate for plain
Monte Carlo and an (over-)conservative one for quasi-Monte-Carlo.

funcs.name: NextPowerOfTwo
funcs.doc:
NextPowerOfTwo returns the smallest power of two greater than or equal to n,
with NextPowerOfTwo(0)==1.

funcs.name: NextPrime
funcs.doc: NextPrime returns the smallest prime strictly greater than n.

funcs.name: NextPrimeGE
funcs.doc: NextPrimeGE returns the smallest prime greater than or equal to n.

funcs.name: PlasticConstant
funcs.doc:
PlasticConstant returns the generalized golden ratio for the given dimension:
the unique real root greater than one of x^(dim+1) = x + 1. For dim==1 this is
the golden ratio and for dim==2 the plastic number 1.3247... It returns an
error when dim < 1.

funcs.name: PopCount
funcs.doc: PopCount returns the number of one bits in n.

funcs.name: PrevPrime
funcs.doc:
PrevPrime returns the largest prime strictly smaller than n, or zero when no
such prime exists (n <= 2).

funcs.name: Prime
funcs.doc:
Prime returns the i-th prime number using one-based indexing, so Prime(1)==2,
Prime(2)==3 and so on. It returns an error when i is not positive.

funcs.name: PrimeBases
funcs.doc:
PrimeBases returns the first dim prime numbers, the canonical choice of
pairwise-coprime bases for a Halton sequence of the given dimension.

funcs.name: PrimeIndex
funcs.doc:
PrimeIndex returns the one-based index of the prime p, or zero when p is not
prime.

funcs.name: PrimeSieve
funcs.doc:
PrimeSieve returns all primes less than or equal to limit in increasing
order using the sieve of Eratosthenes. A limit below two yields an empty
slice.

funcs.name: Primes
funcs.doc:
Primes returns the first n prime numbers (2, 3, 5, ...) in increasing order.
It returns an error when n is negative.

funcs.name: PrimitivePolynomialCoefficients
funcs.doc:
PrimitivePolynomialCoefficients returns the full coefficient vector of the
primitive polynomial for the given Sobol dimension, ordered from the leading
x^s term down to the constant term. Both the leading and constant
coefficients are one. It returns an error when dim is out of range.

funcs.name: PrimitivePolynomialDegree
funcs.doc:
PrimitivePolynomialDegree returns the degree of the primitive polynomial used
for the given Sobol dimension. Dimension one uses the trivial degree-zero
polynomial. It returns an error when dim is out of range.

funcs.name: PrimitivePolynomialEncoding
funcs.doc:
PrimitivePolynomialEncoding returns the integer that encodes the interior
coefficients a_1..a_{s-1} of the primitive polynomial for the given Sobol
dimension, with a_1 the most significant bit. It returns an error when dim is
out of range.

funcs.name: Project
funcs.doc:
Project returns the projection of a point set onto the coordinate axes listed
in dims, producing a lower-dimensional point set. It returns an error when a
requested index is out of range.

funcs.name: QMCIntegrateFaure
funcs.signature: func QMCIntegrateFaure(f Integrand, dim, n int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
QMCIntegrateFaure estimates the integral of f over [0,1]^dim using the first
n points of the Faure sequence. It returns an error when dim < 1 or n < 1.

funcs.name: QMCIntegrateHalton
funcs.signature: func QMCIntegrateHalton(f Integrand, dim, n int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
QMCIntegrateHalton estimates the integral of f over [0,1]^dim using the first
n points of the Halton sequence. It returns an error when dim < 1 or n < 1.

funcs.name: QMCIntegrateHammersley
funcs.signature: func QMCIntegrateHammersley(f Integrand, dim, n int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
QMCIntegrateHammersley estimates the integral of f over [0,1]^dim using the
n-point Hammersley set. It returns an error when dim < 1 or n < 1.

funcs.name: QMCIntegrateSobol
funcs.signature: func QMCIntegrateSobol(f Integrand, dim, n int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
QMCIntegrateSobol estimates the integral of f over [0,1]^dim using the first
n Sobol points after skipping the origin. It returns an error when dim is out
of range or n < 1.

funcs.name: RadicalInverse
funcs.signature: func RadicalInverse(base int, n uint64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
RadicalInverse returns the radical inverse phi_base(n): the value obtained by
reflecting the base-b digits of n about the radix point, mapping the integer
with digits d0 d1 d2 ... to the fraction 0.d0 d1 d2 ... in base b. The result
lies in [0,1). It returns an error when base < 2.

funcs.name: RadicalInverseBase2
funcs.doc:
RadicalInverseBase2 returns the radical inverse in base two using pure
bit reversal, faster than the general routine and exact for indices below
2^52.

funcs.name: RadicalInverseBig
funcs.doc:
RadicalInverseBig returns the radical inverse in the given base of a
non-negative index supplied as a big.Int, as an exact big.Rat in [0,1). It
lets the van der Corput construction reach indices far beyond the range of
uint64. It returns an error when base < 2 or n is negative.

funcs.name: RadicalInverseBigFloat
funcs.doc:
RadicalInverseBigFloat returns RadicalInverseBig evaluated to a float64,
convenient when only ordinary precision is needed but the index exceeds
uint64. It returns an error when base < 2 or n is negative.

funcs.name: RadicalInverseDigits
funcs.signature: func RadicalInverseDigits(base int, n uint64) ([]int, error)
funcs.doc:
RadicalInverseDigits returns the reflected base-b digit vector of n, ordered
from the most significant original digit to the least; interpreting the slice
as 0.d0 d1 d2 ... reproduces RadicalInverse. It returns an error when base<2.

funcs.name: RankOneLattice
funcs.doc:
RankOneLattice returns all total points of the rank-1 lattice rule with the
given generating vector. It returns an error when gen is empty or total < 1.

funcs.name: RankOneLatticePoint
funcs.signature: func RankOneLatticePoint(gen []int, total, i int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
RankOneLatticePoint returns the i-th point (zero-based) of the rank-1 lattice
rule with integer generating vector gen and total nodes, namely the
fractional part of i*gen_k/total in each coordinate. It returns an error when
gen is empty or total < 1.

funcs.name: ReflectPoint
funcs.doc:
ReflectPoint returns the reflection of a single point about the center of the
unit cube, mapping each coordinate x to 1-x.

funcs.name: ReflectPoints
funcs.doc:
ReflectPoints returns the reflection of every point of a set about the center
of the unit cube.

funcs.name: ReverseBits
funcs.signature: func ReverseBits(n uint64, width int) uint64
funcs.doc:
ReverseBits returns n with its low width bits reversed, the base-two analogue
of ReverseDigits used implicitly by the base-two radical inverse.

funcs.name: ReverseDigits
funcs.signature: func ReverseDigits(n uint64, base, width int) (uint64, error)
funcs.doc:
ReverseDigits returns the integer obtained by reversing the base-b digit
string of n over exactly width digits. It is the integer that the radical
inverse scales by base^-width. It returns an error when base < 2.

funcs.name: ReverseHalton
funcs.signature: func ReverseHalton(dim int, n uint64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
ReverseHalton returns the n-th point of the reverse (Faure–Tezuka) Halton
sequence, using the deterministic per-base scrambling permutations returned
by FaurePermutation. This variant markedly improves the uniformity of the
early points in moderate to high dimensions. It returns an error when dim<1.

funcs.name: ReversePermutation
funcs.doc:
ReversePermutation returns the permutation (n-1,...,1,0) that reverses digit
order, a simple scrambling of the digits {0,...,n-1}.

funcs.name: RobertsAlpha
funcs.doc:
RobertsAlpha returns the generating vector of Roberts' R_d low-discrepancy
additive sequence in the given dimension: alpha_j = frac(g^-j) where g is the
plastic constant PlasticConstant(dim). It returns an error when dim < 1.

funcs.name: RobertsPoint
funcs.signature: func RobertsPoint(dim int, n uint64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
RobertsPoint returns the n-th point (zero-based) of Roberts' R_d additive
low-discrepancy sequence in the given dimension, using the seed 1/2 so that
point zero sits at the center of the cube. It returns an error when dim < 1.

funcs.name: RobertsSequence
funcs.doc:
RobertsSequence returns the first count points of Roberts' R_d sequence in the
given dimension.

funcs.name: SampleMean
funcs.doc:
SampleMean returns the arithmetic mean of the values, or zero for an empty
slice.

funcs.name: SampleVariance
funcs.doc:
SampleVariance returns the unbiased (Bessel-corrected) sample variance of the
values, or zero when fewer than two values are given.

funcs.name: ScaleToBox
funcs.doc:
ScaleToBox maps a point set from the unit cube into the axis-aligned box
[lower,upper]. It returns an error when the shapes disagree.

funcs.name: ScrambledHalton
funcs.doc:
ScrambledHalton returns the n-th Halton point in which each coordinate is a
permutation-scrambled radical inverse. perms must supply one permutation per
dimension, each a bijection of {0,...,base-1} for the corresponding prime
base. It returns an error when the shapes disagree.

funcs.name: ScrambledRadicalInverse
funcs.signature: func ScrambledRadicalInverse(base int, n uint64, perm []int) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
ScrambledRadicalInverse returns the radical inverse of n in the given base
with each digit d replaced by perm[d] before reflection. The permutation must
be a bijection of {0,...,base-1}; supplying the identity reproduces
RadicalInverse. It returns an error when base < 2 or perm has the wrong
length. A permutation fixing zero keeps the result in [0,1); a permutation
moving zero adds the geometric tail perm[0]/(base-1) so the result still lies
in [0,1).

funcs.name: SeparationDistance
funcs.doc:
SeparationDistance returns half the minimum pairwise distance, the largest
radius of non-overlapping balls centered at the points. It returns an error
for a set with fewer than two points.

funcs.name: ShiftedRankOneLatticePoint
funcs.signature: func ShiftedRankOneLatticePoint(gen []int, shift []float64, total, i int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
ShiftedRankOneLatticePoint returns the i-th point of the rank-1 lattice rule
shifted by the vector shift (each coordinate taken modulo one), a cheap
randomization-free way to move the lattice off the origin. It returns an
error when the shapes disagree or total < 1.

funcs.name: SobolPoint
funcs.signature: func SobolPoint(dim int, n uint64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
SobolPoint returns the n-th point (zero-based) of the dim-dimensional Sobol
sequence directly. It builds the direction numbers on each call; for
streaming use a Sobol generator instead. It returns an error when dim is out
of range.

funcs.name: SobolSequence
funcs.doc:
SobolSequence returns the first count points of the dim-dimensional Sobol
sequence beginning at the origin (index zero).

funcs.name: SobolSequenceSkip
funcs.signature: func SobolSequenceSkip(dim, skip, count int) ([][]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
SobolSequenceSkip returns count Sobol points after first skipping the leading
skip points (a common way to discard the origin and low-index samples).

funcs.name: StarDiscrepancy
funcs.doc:
StarDiscrepancy returns the exact star discrepancy D*_N of the point set,
computed by the coordinate-grid method: the supremum of the local
discrepancy over anchored boxes is attained at a corner whose coordinates are
drawn from the sample coordinates. The cost grows like N^(d+1), so the
routine is intended for small sets in low dimension. It returns an error for
an invalid point set.

funcs.name: StarDiscrepancy1D
funcs.doc:
StarDiscrepancy1D returns the exact star discrepancy of a one-dimensional
point set (each point a length-one slice) using the closed form
1/(2N) + max_i |x_(i) - (2i-1)/(2N)| over the sorted samples. It returns an
error for an invalid point set or one whose points are not one-dimensional.

funcs.name: StarDiscrepancyGrid
funcs.doc:
StarDiscrepancyGrid returns an approximation to the star discrepancy obtained
by sampling the local discrepancy on a regular grid of res divisions per
dimension. It is a cheap lower bound on the true star discrepancy that is
useful in higher dimensions where the exact routine is impractical. It
returns an error for an invalid point set or res < 1.

funcs.name: SymmetricL2Discrepancy
funcs.doc:
SymmetricL2Discrepancy returns Hickernell's symmetric L2-discrepancy, which is
invariant under reflection of any single coordinate. It returns an error for
an invalid point set.

funcs.name: ToroidalDistance
funcs.doc:
ToroidalDistance returns the Euclidean distance between two points on the unit
torus, where each coordinate difference is taken to be at most one half. It
returns an error when the lengths disagree.

funcs.name: TrailingZeroBits
funcs.doc:
TrailingZeroBits returns the number of trailing zero bits of n, i.e. the
index (from zero) of the lowest set bit. For n==0 it returns 64.

funcs.name: VanDerCorput
funcs.signature: func VanDerCorput(base int, n uint64) (float64, error)
funcs.doc:
VanDerCorput returns the n-th term (zero-based) of the van der Corput
sequence in the given base, which is exactly RadicalInverse(base, n).

funcs.name: VanDerCorputSequence
funcs.doc:
VanDerCorputSequence returns the first count terms of the van der Corput
sequence in the given base, starting from index zero (whose value is 0).

funcs.name: VanDerCorputSequenceOffset
funcs.signature: func VanDerCorputSequenceOffset(base, start, count int) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
VanDerCorputSequenceOffset returns count terms of the van der Corput sequence
in the given base beginning at index start rather than zero.

funcs.name: WeylSequence
funcs.signature: func WeylSequence(alpha float64, n uint64) float64
funcs.doc:
WeylSequence returns the n-th term (zero-based) of the one-dimensional Weyl
(Kronecker) sequence with irrational parameter alpha, namely the fractional
part of n*alpha.

funcs.name: WrapAroundL2Discrepancy
funcs.doc:
WrapAroundL2Discrepancy returns Hickernell's wrap-around L2-discrepancy, which
treats the unit cube as a torus and so is invariant under coordinate shifts
modulo one. It returns an error for an invalid point set.

examples.name: Halton
examples.doc: ExampleHalton prints the first three two-dimensional Halton points.

examples.name: RadicalInverse
examples.doc:
ExampleRadicalInverse prints the first four terms of the base-two van der
Corput sequence.

examples.name: Sobol
examples.doc: ExampleSobol prints the first few two-dimensional Sobol points.

packages.name: queueing
packages.synopsis: Package queueing provides queueing-theory models and formulas built entirely on the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package queueing provides queueing-theory models and formulas built entirely
on the Go standard library.

The package covers the classical single- and multi-server Markovian queues
together with several non-Markovian and network extensions.

Markovian queues: steady-state analysis of the M/M/1, M/M/c, M/M/1/K,
M/M/c/K and M/M/∞ systems is provided through the [MM1], [MMc], [MM1K],
[MMcK] and [MMInf] types. Each type exposes utilization, the stationary
state distribution, mean number in system and in queue, mean sojourn and
waiting times, blocking probabilities for the finite-capacity variants and
waiting-time tail probabilities where a closed form exists.

Non-Markovian queues: the [MG1] type implements the Pollaczek–Khinchine
mean-value results for the M/G/1 queue given the mean and variance (or
squared coefficient of variation) of a general service distribution, with
[MD1] specializing to deterministic service. The [GG1] type collects the
Kingman and Allen–Cunneen diffusion approximations for the general G/G/1 and
G/G/c queues.

Loss and delay systems: [ErlangB] and [ErlangC] evaluate the Erlang loss and
delay formulas via numerically stable recurrences, with helpers for carried
load, server sizing and the Erlang-C waiting-time distribution.

Birth–death chains and networks: [BirthDeath] solves a general finite
birth–death chain for its stationary distribution and moments, while
[JacksonNetwork] solves the traffic equations of an open Jackson network and
reports per-node utilization and occupancy together with network-wide
throughput and sojourn time.

Little's law and related identities are available as free functions such as
[LittleL] and [LittleW].

Unless stated otherwise, arrival and service rates must be strictly positive
and probabilities lie in [0,1]; functions return NaN (or an error from a
constructor) when arguments are out of range or when a stability condition
is violated.

vars.doc: Common sentinel errors returned by the constructors in this package.

types.name: BirthDeath
types.doc:
BirthDeath models a finite continuous-time birth–death chain on states
0,1,...,N. Birth[i] is the transition rate from state i to i+1 (for
i=0..N-1) and Death[i] is the rate from state i+1 to i (for i=0..N-1), so
both slices have length N. Many elementary queues are special cases of this
chain.

funcs.name: NewBirthDeath
funcs.doc:
NewBirthDeath constructs a [BirthDeath] chain from equal-length birth and
death rate slices. It returns an error when the slices differ in length, are
empty, or contain a negative rate or a non-positive death rate.

methods.name: DetailedBalance
methods.signature: func (c BirthDeath) DetailedBalance(p []float64, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
DetailedBalance reports whether a candidate distribution p satisfies the
detailed-balance equations p[i]*Birth[i] == p[i+1]*Death[i] within the given
absolute tolerance.

methods.name: MeanNumber
methods.doc:
MeanNumber returns the stationary mean state value sum_n n*p_n, i.e. the mean
number in system.

methods.name: ProbN
methods.doc:
ProbN returns the stationary probability of state n, or 0 when n is outside
the range 0..N.

methods.name: States
methods.doc: States returns the number of states N+1 in the chain.

methods.name: StationaryDistribution
methods.doc:
StationaryDistribution returns the normalized stationary distribution over
states 0..N obtained from the detailed-balance product
p_n = p_0 * prod_{i<n} Birth[i]/Death[i].

methods.name: Throughput
methods.doc:
Throughput returns the stationary birth rate sum_n Birth[n]*p_n, which equals
the long-run rate of upward transitions.

methods.name: VarianceNumber
methods.doc: VarianceNumber returns the variance of the stationary state value.

types.name: GG1
types.doc:
GG1 models a general single-server queue with renewal arrivals and general
service (Kendall notation G/G/1). Interarrival and service times are
summarized by their rates and squared coefficients of variation Ca2 and Cs2.
Exact results are not available, so the type provides the standard heavy-
traffic diffusion approximations (Kingman and Allen–Cunneen).

funcs.name: NewGG1
funcs.signature: func NewGG1(lambda, serviceMean, ca2, cs2 float64) (GG1, error)
funcs.doc:
NewGG1 constructs a [GG1] queue from the arrival rate, mean service time and
the squared coefficients of variation of the interarrival and service times.
It returns an error for non-positive rates, negative SCVs or when the
utilization Lambda*ServiceMean is not below one.

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the approximate mean number in system, Lq + rho.

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc: Lq returns the approximate mean queue length, Lambda*Wq.

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc: Rho returns the utilization rho = Lambda*E[S].

methods.name: W
methods.doc: W returns the approximate mean sojourn time, Wq + E[S].

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc:
Wq returns the Kingman (VUT) approximation of the mean waiting time,
(rho/(1-rho)) * ((Ca2+Cs2)/2) * E[S].

types.name: JacksonNetwork
types.doc:
JacksonNetwork models an open Jackson network of M/M/c queues. Node i
receives external Poisson arrivals at rate External[i], has Servers[i]
identical servers of rate ServiceRate[i], and routes a completed customer to
node j with probability Routing[i][j]; the residual probability
1 - sum_j Routing[i][j] is the probability of leaving the network. Solving
the traffic equations yields per-node arrival rates, from which each node is
analyzed as an independent M/M/c queue (Jackson's theorem).

funcs.name: NewJacksonNetwork
funcs.signature: func NewJacksonNetwork(external []float64, routing [][]float64, serviceRate []float64, servers []int) (JacksonNetwork, error)
funcs.doc:
NewJacksonNetwork constructs an open [JacksonNetwork]. Servers may be nil, in
which case every node is single-server. It validates dimensions, sign
constraints, and that each node's routing probabilities sum to at most one,
returning an error otherwise.

methods.name: ArrivalRates
methods.doc:
ArrivalRates solves the traffic equations lambda_j = gamma_j + sum_i
lambda_i r_ij and returns the vector of per-node total arrival rates. It
returns nil if the linear system is singular.

methods.name: MeanNumberAt
methods.doc:
MeanNumberAt returns the mean number of customers at node i, analyzing it as
an independent M/M/c queue with the arrival rate from the traffic equations.
It returns NaN if the node is unstable or the network is singular.

methods.name: MeanNumbers
methods.doc: MeanNumbers returns the mean number of customers at every node.

methods.name: Nodes
methods.doc: Nodes returns the number of nodes in the network.

methods.name: SojournTime
methods.doc:
SojournTime returns the mean time a customer spends in the network,
TotalMean/Throughput, by Little's law applied to the whole network.

methods.name: Stable
methods.doc:
Stable reports whether every node has utilization strictly below one, the
condition for the network to admit a product-form steady state.

methods.name: Throughput
methods.doc:
Throughput returns the network throughput, the total external arrival rate
(equal in steady state to the total departure rate).

methods.name: TotalMean
methods.doc:
TotalMean returns the mean total number of customers in the network,
summed over all nodes.

methods.name: Utilizations
methods.doc:
Utilizations returns the per-node utilization rho_i = lambda_i/(c_i mu_i). It
returns nil if the traffic equations are singular.

types.name: MD1
types.doc:
MD1 models a single-server queue with Poisson arrivals of rate Lambda and
deterministic (constant) service time 1/Mu (Kendall notation M/D/1). It is
the M/G/1 special case with zero service-time variance, so its queue length
is exactly half that of the corresponding M/M/1 queue.

funcs.name: NewMD1
funcs.doc:
NewMD1 constructs an [MD1] queue, returning an error for non-positive rates
or when the stability condition Lambda < Mu is violated.

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the mean number in system, Lq + rho.

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc: Lq returns the mean number waiting in the queue, rho^2/(2(1-rho)).

methods.name: P0
methods.doc: P0 returns the probability the system is empty, 1-rho.

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc: Rho returns the utilization rho = Lambda/Mu.

methods.name: Stable
methods.doc: Stable reports whether Lambda < Mu.

methods.name: W
methods.doc: W returns the mean sojourn time, Wq + 1/Mu.

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc: Wq returns the mean waiting time in the queue, rho/(2 Mu (1-rho)).

types.name: MG1
types.doc:
MG1 models a single-server queue with Poisson arrivals of rate Lambda and a
general service-time distribution specified by its mean ServiceMean and
variance ServiceVar (Kendall notation M/G/1). Mean-value results follow from
the Pollaczek–Khinchine formula. A steady state exists when the utilization
rho = Lambda*ServiceMean is below one.

funcs.name: NewMG1
funcs.signature: func NewMG1(lambda, serviceMean, serviceVar float64) (MG1, error)
funcs.doc:
NewMG1 constructs an [MG1] queue from an arrival rate and the mean and
variance of the service time. It returns an error for a non-positive arrival
rate or mean service time, a negative variance, or when the stability
condition Lambda*ServiceMean < 1 is violated.

funcs.name: NewMG1FromSCV
funcs.signature: func NewMG1FromSCV(lambda, serviceMean, scv float64) (MG1, error)
funcs.doc:
NewMG1FromSCV constructs an [MG1] queue from the arrival rate, mean service
time and squared coefficient of variation scv = Var[S]/E[S]^2.

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the mean number in system, Lq + rho.

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc:
Lq returns the mean number waiting in the queue from the Pollaczek–Khinchine
formula, (Lambda^2 Var[S] + rho^2) / (2(1-rho)).

methods.name: MeanResidualService
methods.doc:
MeanResidualService returns the mean residual (forward-recurrence) service
time seen by an arrival at a busy instant, E[S^2]/(2 E[S]).

methods.name: P0
methods.doc:
P0 returns the probability that the system is empty, 1-rho, which holds for
any M/G/1 queue.

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc: Rho returns the utilization rho = Lambda*E[S].

methods.name: ServiceRate
methods.doc: ServiceRate returns the nominal service rate Mu = 1/E[S].

methods.name: ServiceSCV
methods.doc:
ServiceSCV returns the squared coefficient of variation of the service time,
Var[S]/E[S]^2.

methods.name: Stable
methods.doc: Stable reports whether the utilization is below one.

methods.name: W
methods.doc: W returns the mean sojourn time, Wq + E[S].

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc: Wq returns the mean waiting time in the queue, Lq/Lambda.

types.name: MM1
types.doc:
MM1 models a single-server queue with Poisson arrivals of rate Lambda,
exponential service of rate Mu, an infinite waiting room and FCFS discipline
(Kendall notation M/M/1). A steady state exists when Lambda < Mu.

funcs.name: NewMM1
funcs.doc:
NewMM1 constructs an [MM1] queue, returning an error for non-positive rates
or when the stability condition Lambda < Mu is violated.

methods.name: IdleProb
methods.doc: IdleProb returns the probability the system is empty, P0 = 1 - rho.

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the mean number of customers in the system, rho/(1-rho).

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc:
Lq returns the mean number of customers waiting in the queue,
rho^2/(1-rho).

methods.name: Ls
methods.doc: Ls returns the mean number of customers in service, equal to rho.

methods.name: MeanBusyCycle
methods.doc:
MeanBusyCycle returns the mean length of a busy cycle (idle period plus busy
period), 1/(Lambda(1-rho)).

methods.name: MeanBusyPeriod
methods.doc: MeanBusyPeriod returns the mean length of a server busy period, 1/(Mu-Lambda).

methods.name: P0
methods.doc: P0 returns the steady-state probability of an empty system, 1 - rho.

methods.name: PAtLeast
methods.doc:
PAtLeast returns the probability of at least n customers in the system,
P(N>=n) = rho^n. It returns 1 for n<=0.

methods.name: Pn
methods.doc:
Pn returns the steady-state probability P(N=n) = (1-rho) rho^n of finding n
customers in the system. It returns 0 for negative n.

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc:
Rho returns the server utilization (traffic intensity) rho = Lambda/Mu, which
also equals the long-run fraction of time the server is busy.

methods.name: Stable
methods.doc: Stable reports whether the queue admits a steady state (Lambda < Mu).

methods.name: Throughput
methods.doc:
Throughput returns the departure rate of the system, which in steady state
equals the arrival rate Lambda.

methods.name: Utilization
methods.doc: Utilization returns the fraction of time the server is busy, equal to Rho.

methods.name: VarianceN
methods.doc: VarianceN returns the variance of the number in system, rho/(1-rho)^2.

methods.name: W
methods.doc:
W returns the mean sojourn time (response time) in the system,
1/(Mu-Lambda).

methods.name: WaitCDF
methods.doc: WaitCDF returns P(W<=t), the sojourn-time distribution 1-exp(-(Mu-Lambda)t).

methods.name: WaitPercentile
methods.doc:
WaitPercentile returns the sojourn-time quantile t such that P(W<=t)=p, i.e.
-ln(1-p)/(Mu-Lambda). It returns NaN for p outside [0,1).

methods.name: WaitTailProb
methods.doc:
WaitTailProb returns P(W>t), the probability the sojourn time exceeds t,
which is exp(-(Mu-Lambda)t) for the M/M/1 FCFS queue. It returns 1 for t<=0.

methods.name: WaitqCDF
methods.doc:
WaitqCDF returns P(Wq<=t) = 1 - rho*exp(-(Mu-Lambda)t), the queueing-delay
distribution, which has an atom 1-rho at zero.

methods.name: WaitqTailProb
methods.doc:
WaitqTailProb returns P(Wq>t), the probability the queueing delay exceeds t,
which is rho*exp(-(Mu-Lambda)t). It returns rho for t<=0 (the probability of
a positive delay).

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc: Wq returns the mean waiting time in the queue, Lambda/(Mu(Mu-Lambda)).

types.name: MM1K
types.doc:
MM1K models a single-server queue with Poisson arrivals of rate Lambda,
exponential service of rate Mu and a finite system capacity K (including the
customer in service). Arrivals that find K customers present are lost
(Kendall notation M/M/1/K). Because the state space is finite the queue is
always stable, even when Lambda >= Mu.

funcs.name: NewMM1K
funcs.signature: func NewMM1K(lambda, mu float64, k int) (MM1K, error)
funcs.doc:
NewMM1K constructs an [MM1K] queue, returning an error for non-positive rates
or a capacity below 1.

methods.name: BlockingProb
methods.doc:
BlockingProb returns the probability P(N=K) that an arriving customer is
blocked and lost. By PASTA this equals the loss probability.

methods.name: EffectiveArrivalRate
methods.doc:
EffectiveArrivalRate returns the rate of accepted customers,
Lambda*(1-BlockingProb).

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the mean number of customers in the system.

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc: Lq returns the mean number of customers waiting in the queue, L-(1-P0).

methods.name: P0
methods.doc: P0 returns the steady-state probability that the system is empty.

methods.name: Pn
methods.doc:
Pn returns the steady-state probability P(N=n) for 0<=n<=K, and 0 outside
that range.

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc:
Rho returns the traffic intensity rho = Lambda/Mu, which may exceed one for a
finite-capacity queue.

methods.name: Throughput
methods.doc: Throughput returns the departure rate, equal to the effective arrival rate.

methods.name: Utilization
methods.doc:
Utilization returns the fraction of time the server is busy, 1-P0, which also
equals EffectiveArrivalRate/Mu.

methods.name: W
methods.doc: W returns the mean sojourn time using the effective arrival rate, L/lambdaEff.

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc: Wq returns the mean waiting time in the queue, Lq/lambdaEff.

types.name: MMInf
types.doc:
MMInf models an infinite-server queue with Poisson arrivals of rate Lambda
and exponential service of rate Mu (Kendall notation M/M/∞). Every customer
enters service immediately, so there is never any waiting. In steady state
the number in system is Poisson with mean a = Lambda/Mu.

funcs.name: NewMMInf
funcs.doc:
NewMMInf constructs an [MMInf] queue, returning an error for non-positive
rates.

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the mean number of customers in the system, a = Lambda/Mu.

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc: Lq returns the mean number waiting in the queue, which is always 0.

methods.name: MeanBusyServers
methods.doc: MeanBusyServers returns the mean number of busy servers, equal to a.

methods.name: OfferedLoad
methods.doc:
OfferedLoad returns the offered load a = Lambda/Mu, which equals the mean
number in system.

methods.name: P0
methods.doc: P0 returns the probability that the system is empty, e^{-a}.

methods.name: Pn
methods.doc:
Pn returns the steady-state probability P(N=n) = e^{-a} a^n/n!, the Poisson
mass with mean a = Lambda/Mu. It returns 0 for negative n.

methods.name: VarianceN
methods.doc:
VarianceN returns the variance of the number in system, equal to the mean a
for the Poisson distribution.

methods.name: W
methods.doc: W returns the mean sojourn time, equal to a single service time 1/Mu.

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc: Wq returns the mean waiting time in the queue, which is always 0.

types.name: MMc
types.doc:
MMc models a multi-server queue with Poisson arrivals of rate Lambda, C
identical exponential servers each of rate Mu, an infinite waiting room and
FCFS discipline (Kendall notation M/M/c). A steady state exists when the
offered load Lambda/Mu is less than C.

funcs.name: NewMMc
funcs.signature: func NewMMc(lambda, mu float64, c int) (MMc, error)
funcs.doc:
NewMMc constructs an [MMc] queue, returning an error for non-positive rates,
a non-positive server count, or when the stability condition Lambda < C*Mu
is violated.

methods.name: ErlangC
methods.doc:
ErlangC returns the Erlang-C delay probability, the steady-state probability
that an arriving customer must wait (finds all C servers busy).

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the mean number of customers in the system, Lq + a.

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc:
Lq returns the mean number of customers waiting in the queue,
ErlangC * rho/(1-rho).

methods.name: MeanBusyServers
methods.doc:
MeanBusyServers returns the mean number of busy servers, equal to the offered
load a = Lambda/Mu.

methods.name: OfferedLoad
methods.doc:
OfferedLoad returns the offered load a = Lambda/Mu in erlangs, the mean
number of busy servers in steady state.

methods.name: P0
methods.doc: P0 returns the steady-state probability that the system is empty.

methods.name: Pn
methods.doc:
Pn returns the steady-state probability P(N=n) of finding n customers in the
system. It returns 0 for negative n.

methods.name: ProbWait
methods.doc:
ProbWait returns the probability that an arriving customer is delayed, equal
to the Erlang-C value.

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc: Rho returns the per-server utilization rho = Lambda/(C*Mu).

methods.name: Stable
methods.doc: Stable reports whether the queue admits a steady state (Lambda < C*Mu).

methods.name: Throughput
methods.doc: Throughput returns the departure rate, which equals Lambda in steady state.

methods.name: Utilization
methods.doc: Utilization returns the per-server utilization, equal to Rho.

methods.name: W
methods.doc: W returns the mean sojourn time in the system, Wq + 1/Mu.

methods.name: WaitqCDF
methods.doc: WaitqCDF returns P(Wq<=t), the queueing-delay distribution.

methods.name: WaitqPercentile
methods.doc:
WaitqPercentile returns the queueing-delay quantile t such that P(Wq<=t)=p.
It returns 0 when p is at or below the no-wait probability 1-ErlangC, and NaN
for p outside [0,1).

methods.name: WaitqTailProb
methods.doc:
WaitqTailProb returns P(Wq>t), the probability the queueing delay exceeds t,
which is ErlangC * exp(-(C*Mu-Lambda) t). It returns the delay probability
for t<=0.

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc: Wq returns the mean waiting time in the queue, Lq/Lambda.

types.name: MMcK
types.doc:
MMcK models a multi-server queue with Poisson arrivals of rate Lambda, C
exponential servers of rate Mu each and a finite system capacity K>=C.
Arrivals that find K customers present are lost (Kendall notation M/M/c/K).
The queue is always stable because the state space is finite.

funcs.name: NewMMcK
funcs.signature: func NewMMcK(lambda, mu float64, c, k int) (MMcK, error)
funcs.doc:
NewMMcK constructs an [MMcK] queue, returning an error for non-positive
rates, a non-positive server count or a capacity smaller than the number of
servers.

methods.name: BlockingProb
methods.doc: BlockingProb returns the loss probability P(N=K).

methods.name: EffectiveArrivalRate
methods.doc:
EffectiveArrivalRate returns the accepted-customer rate
Lambda*(1-BlockingProb).

methods.name: L
methods.doc: L returns the mean number of customers in the system.

methods.name: Lq
methods.doc: Lq returns the mean number of customers waiting in the queue.

methods.name: MeanBusyServers
methods.doc:
MeanBusyServers returns the mean number of busy servers, which equals
EffectiveArrivalRate/Mu.

methods.name: OfferedLoad
methods.doc: OfferedLoad returns the offered load a = Lambda/Mu in erlangs.

methods.name: P0
methods.doc: P0 returns the steady-state probability that the system is empty.

methods.name: Pn
methods.doc: Pn returns the steady-state probability P(N=n) for 0<=n<=K, and 0 otherwise.

methods.name: ProbWait
methods.doc:
ProbWait returns the probability that an accepted arrival must wait, i.e. it
finds at least C but fewer than K customers present, normalized by the
acceptance probability.

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc:
Rho returns the nominal per-server traffic intensity Lambda/(C*Mu). For a
finite-capacity queue it may exceed one.

methods.name: Throughput
methods.doc: Throughput returns the departure rate, equal to the effective arrival rate.

methods.name: Utilization
methods.doc:
Utilization returns the mean fraction of servers busy,
MeanBusyServers / C.

methods.name: W
methods.doc: W returns the mean sojourn time L/lambdaEff.

methods.name: Wq
methods.doc: Wq returns the mean waiting time in the queue Lq/lambdaEff.

types.name: PriorityMM1
types.doc:
PriorityMM1 models a single-server queue with Poisson arrivals in several
priority classes and exponential, class-dependent service. Class 0 has the
highest priority. Lambda[k] and Mu[k] are the arrival and service rates of
class k. The type provides the non-preemptive (head-of-line) priority
results; a steady state exists when the total utilization is below one.

funcs.name: NewPriorityMM1
funcs.doc:
NewPriorityMM1 constructs a [PriorityMM1] queue from per-class arrival and
service rates. It returns an error for mismatched or empty slices,
non-positive rates, or a total utilization at or above one.

methods.name: ClassL
methods.doc: ClassL returns the mean number of class-k customers in system, Lambda[k]*W_k.

methods.name: ClassLq
methods.doc: ClassLq returns the mean number of class-k customers waiting, Lambda[k]*Wq_k.

methods.name: ClassRho
methods.doc: ClassRho returns the per-class utilization rho_k = Lambda[k]/Mu[k].

methods.name: ClassW
methods.doc: ClassW returns the mean sojourn time of class k, ClassWq(k)+1/Mu[k].

methods.name: ClassWq
methods.doc:
ClassWq returns the mean waiting time of class k under non-preemptive
priority, R / ((1-sigma_{k-1})(1-sigma_k)), where sigma_j is the cumulative
utilization through class j. It returns NaN for an invalid class index.

methods.name: Classes
methods.doc: Classes returns the number of priority classes.

methods.name: MeanWait
methods.doc:
MeanWait returns the arrival-weighted overall mean waiting time across all
classes, (sum_k Lambda[k] Wq_k)/(sum_k Lambda[k]).

methods.name: Rho
methods.doc: Rho returns the total utilization sum_k Lambda[k]/Mu[k].

funcs.name: AllenCunneenLq
funcs.signature: func AllenCunneenLq(lambda, mu float64, c int, ca2, cs2 float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AllenCunneenLq returns the Allen–Cunneen approximation of the mean queue
length in a G/G/c queue, lambda times [AllenCunneenWq].

funcs.name: AllenCunneenWq
funcs.signature: func AllenCunneenWq(lambda, mu float64, c int, ca2, cs2 float64) float64
funcs.doc:
AllenCunneenWq returns the Allen–Cunneen approximation of the mean waiting
time in a G/G/c queue with arrival rate lambda, per-server service rate mu, c
servers and interarrival/service SCVs ca2 and cs2. It combines the exact
M/M/c waiting time with the variability factor (ca2+cs2)/2. It returns +Inf
when the queue is unstable and NaN for invalid inputs.

funcs.name: BirthDeathStationary
funcs.doc:
BirthDeathStationary returns the stationary distribution of a birth–death
chain given standalone birth and death rate slices, or nil when the inputs
are invalid (mismatched lengths, empty, or a non-positive death rate).

funcs.name: CarriedLoad
funcs.signature: func CarriedLoad(c int, a float64) float64
funcs.doc:
CarriedLoad returns the load carried by an M/M/c/c loss system, a(1-B), where
