root counts subdivides an interval known to contain every real root until each
piece is isolating. The returned intervals are sorted in increasing order.

funcs.name: IsolateRootsInterval
funcs.signature: func IsolateRootsInterval(p Poly, a, b float64) [][2]float64
funcs.doc:
IsolateRootsInterval returns disjoint isolating intervals for the distinct
real roots of p that lie in (a, b], using recursive Sturm bisection. Each
returned interval contains exactly one root.

funcs.name: KojimaBound
funcs.doc:
KojimaBound returns Kojima's bound on the root moduli, formed from the ratios
of consecutive coefficients: |z| <= 2 * max_i q_i, where q_1 = |a_{n-1}/a_n|
and q_i = |a_{n-i}/a_{n-i+1}| for i>1 (terms with a zero denominator are
skipped).

funcs.name: LagrangeBound
funcs.doc:
LagrangeBound returns Lagrange's bound: |z| <= max(1, sum_{i<n} |a_i/a_n|)
for every root z of p. It is often tighter than [CauchyBound].

funcs.name: Laguerre
funcs.signature: func Laguerre(c CPoly, x0 complex128, tol float64, maxIter int) (complex128, int, error)
funcs.doc:
Laguerre finds a single root of the polynomial c starting from x0 using
Laguerre's method, which has cubic convergence for simple roots and is
famously reliable: it converges to some root from almost any starting point,
even for complex roots of a real polynomial. It operates in complex
arithmetic throughout.

funcs.name: LaguerreRoots
funcs.signature: func LaguerreRoots(c CPoly, tol float64, maxIter int) ([]complex128, error)
funcs.doc:
LaguerreRoots finds all roots of the polynomial c by repeated application of
[Laguerre] followed by deflation: each root found is divided out and the
search continues on the reduced polynomial. Roots are polished on the original
polynomial to undo deflation error. This is the classic reliable driver used
by numerical libraries for arbitrary complex polynomials.

funcs.name: LowerRootBound
funcs.doc:
LowerRootBound returns a positive lower bound on the moduli of the nonzero
roots of p: no nonzero root satisfies |z| < LowerRootBound(p). It is obtained
by applying [CauchyBound] to the reversed polynomial and taking the
reciprocal. It returns 0 when 0 is a root or p is constant.

funcs.name: MaxResidual
funcs.doc:
MaxResidual returns the largest modulus |c(z)| over the given candidate roots,
a scale-free measure of how well a computed root set satisfies the polynomial.

funcs.name: Multiplicity
funcs.signature: func Multiplicity(p Poly, r, tol float64) int
funcs.doc:
Multiplicity returns the multiplicity of the real value r as a root of p,
i.e. the largest k with (x-r)^k dividing p. It uses the derivative test:
r has multiplicity k when p, p', ... , p^(k-1) all vanish at r but p^(k) does
not. Values are compared against a scaled tolerance tol. A return of 0 means r
is not a root.

funcs.name: MultiplicityComplex
funcs.signature: func MultiplicityComplex(c CPoly, r complex128, tol float64) int
funcs.doc:
MultiplicityComplex returns the multiplicity of the complex value r as a root
of the complex polynomial c, via the complex derivative test with tolerance
tol.

funcs.name: NewtonComplex
funcs.signature: func NewtonComplex(c CPoly, x0 complex128, tol float64, maxIter int) (complex128, int, error)
funcs.doc:
NewtonComplex finds a root of the complex polynomial c from the start x0 using
Newton's method in complex arithmetic. It is the complex analogue of [Newton]
and converges quadratically near a simple root.

funcs.name: PolishComplexRoots
funcs.signature: func PolishComplexRoots(c CPoly, roots []complex128, tol float64, steps int) []complex128
funcs.doc:
PolishComplexRoots refines each approximate root in roots with a few Newton
steps on the complex polynomial c, improving accuracy after a global solve or
deflation. The input slice is not modified; a refined copy is returned.

funcs.name: PolyRoots
funcs.doc:
PolyRoots returns all complex roots of the real polynomial p using the
Aberth-Ehrlich method, the recommended general-purpose driver. The result is
sorted by real part then imaginary part. It returns ErrDegreeTooLow for
constant polynomials.

funcs.name: QuadraticRoots
funcs.signature: func QuadraticRoots(a, b, c float64) (complex128, complex128)
funcs.doc:
QuadraticRoots returns the two roots of the quadratic a*x^2 + b*x + c using a
numerically stable formula that avoids catastrophic cancellation. The roots
are returned as complex numbers; real roots have zero imaginary part.

funcs.name: RealRootInterval
funcs.doc:
RealRootInterval returns a symmetric interval [-b, b] that contains all real
roots of p, where b is the Lagrange bound. Every real root x satisfies
-b <= x <= b.

funcs.name: RealRoots
funcs.signature: func RealRoots(p Poly, imagTol float64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
RealRoots returns the real roots of p, that is the roots whose imaginary part
is negligible relative to imagTol. Each is refined by a Newton polish on the
real polynomial. The result is sorted in ascending order.

funcs.name: SeparateRoots
funcs.doc:
SeparateRoots partitions a list of complex roots into those that are
effectively real (|imag| <= imagTol) and those that are genuinely complex.
The real parts of the real roots are returned sorted in ascending order.

funcs.name: SignChange
funcs.signature: func SignChange(f Func, a, b float64) bool
funcs.doc:
SignChange reports whether f(a) and f(b) have strictly opposite signs, which
guarantees a root of a continuous f in (a, b) by the intermediate value
theorem. A zero at an endpoint counts as a sign change.

funcs.name: SignVariations
funcs.doc:
SignVariations returns the number of sign changes in the sequence of values,
skipping zeros. It is the basic count underlying Descartes' rule of signs and
the Budan-Fourier theorem.

funcs.name: SolveCubic
funcs.signature: func SolveCubic(a, b, c, d float64) ([]complex128, error)
funcs.doc:
SolveCubic returns the three roots of a*x^3 + b*x^2 + c*x + d as complex
numbers, using Cardano's method with complex cube roots so the formula is
numerically valid in all cases. Real roots have (near) zero imaginary part.
It returns ErrDegreeTooLow when a is zero.

funcs.name: SolveCubicReal
funcs.signature: func SolveCubicReal(a, b, c, d float64) ([]float64, error)
funcs.doc:
SolveCubicReal returns the real roots of the cubic a*x^3+b*x^2+c*x+d in
ascending order, extracted from [SolveCubic] by keeping roots whose imaginary
part is negligible.

funcs.name: SolveLinear
funcs.doc:
SolveLinear returns the root of the linear equation a*x + b = 0. It returns
ErrDegreeTooLow when a is zero.

funcs.name: SolveQuadraticReal
funcs.doc:
SolveQuadraticReal returns the real roots of a*x^2 + b*x + c in ascending
order: two values when the discriminant is positive, one (repeated) when it is
zero, and none when it is negative.

funcs.name: SortComplex
funcs.doc: SortComplex returns a copy of z sorted by real part then imaginary part.

funcs.name: SturmCountRoots
funcs.signature: func SturmCountRoots(seq []Poly, a, b float64) int
funcs.doc:
SturmCountRoots returns the number of distinct real roots of the polynomial
underlying the Sturm sequence seq that lie in the half-open interval (a, b],
computed as V(a) - V(b). The endpoints must satisfy a < b.

funcs.name: SturmRealRoots
funcs.doc:
SturmRealRoots returns all distinct real roots of p refined to tolerance tol.
It isolates each root with [IsolateRoots] and refines it with Sturm-count
bisection, so it is correct even in the presence of repeated (including
even-multiplicity) roots. The roots are returned in increasing order.

funcs.name: SturmRefine
funcs.signature: func SturmRefine(seq []Poly, a, b, tol float64) float64
funcs.doc:
SturmRefine refines an isolating interval [a, b] known to contain exactly one
distinct real root of p down to width tol, using Sturm-count bisection. Unlike
sign-based bisection it works for roots of even multiplicity, where p does not
change sign. It returns the interval midpoint.

funcs.name: SturmVariations
funcs.doc:
SturmVariations returns the number of sign changes in the Sturm sequence seq
evaluated at x, ignoring terms that evaluate to zero. This is the function
V(x) of Sturm's theorem.

funcs.name: SturmVariationsAtNegInf
funcs.doc:
SturmVariationsAtNegInf returns the sign-variation count of the Sturm sequence
as x -> -infinity, determined from the leading coefficients and degrees. It is
used to count roots on unbounded left intervals.

funcs.name: SturmVariationsAtPosInf
funcs.doc:
SturmVariationsAtPosInf returns the sign-variation count of the Sturm sequence
as x -> +infinity, determined from the signs of the leading coefficients.

funcs.name: TotalRealRoots
funcs.signature: func TotalRealRoots(p Poly, tol float64) int
funcs.doc:
TotalRealRoots returns the number of real roots of p counted with
multiplicity, summing the multiplicities reported by
[RealRootsWithMultiplicity].

examples.name: DescartesRuleOfSigns
examples.code:
p := NewPoly(-1, 0, 1)
pos, neg := DescartesRuleOfSigns(p)
fmt.Println(pos, neg)

examples.name: PolyRoots
SturmRealRoots
Brent

packages.name: satsolver
packages.synopsis: Package satsolver implements Boolean logic and Boolean satisfiability using only the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package satsolver implements Boolean logic and Boolean satisfiability using
only the Go standard library.

The package covers the classical toolbox of propositional reasoning and
automated Boolean decision procedures:

  - Literals and clauses (literal.go): the [Lit] type encoding a signed
    variable reference, together with the [Clause] conjunctive/disjunctive
    term type and its manipulation helpers.
  - Conjunctive and disjunctive normal-form containers (cnf.go, dnf.go): the
    [CNF] and [DNF] formula types with evaluation, simplification, DIMACS
    serialisation ([CNF.DIMACS], [ParseDIMACS]) and unit/pure-literal
    analysis.
  - Boolean expression trees (expr.go): the sealed [Expr] interface with the
    [Variable], [BoolConst], [Not], [And], [Or], [Xor], [Implies], [Iff],
    [Nand], [Nor] and [Xnor] node types, the [Parse] recursive-descent
    parser and structural utilities such as [Substitute] and [ExprEqual].
  - Normal-form conversion (nnf.go, normalform.go): [ToNNF], [ToCNFExpr],
    [ToDNFExpr], the Boolean [Simplify] rewriter and the [ToCNFFormula]
    bridge into the clausal world.
  - Tseitin encoding (tseitin.go): the [Tseitin] equisatisfiable
    transformation and the incremental [TseitinEncoder].
  - Truth tables and semantics (truthtable.go, semantic.go): [TruthTable],
    [Minterms], [Maxterms] and the decision procedures [IsTautology],
    [IsSatisfiable], [Equivalent], [Entails], [FindModel] and [CountModels].
  - A DPLL/CDCL-lite SAT solver (solver.go): [DPLL], [SolveCNF],
    [UnitPropagate], [PureLiteralElimination], resolution ([Resolve]) and
    model enumeration ([AllSolutions], [CountSolutions]).
  - Reduced ordered binary decision diagrams (bdd.go): the [BDD] manager with
    [BDD.Apply], [BDD.Ite], [BDD.SatCount] and [BDD.FromExpr].
  - Two-level minimisation (qm.go): the Quine-McCluskey engine
    [QuineMcCluskey] with [PrimeImplicants] and [EssentialPrimeImplicants],
    the [MinimizeSOP] cover routine and the [KarnaughMap] helper.

Everything is implemented with real, self-contained algorithms and depends
only on the packages math, math/big, sort, errors, fmt and strings.

vars.signature: var ErrEmptyFormula = errors.New("satsolver: empty formula")
vars.doc: ErrEmptyFormula is returned by routines that require at least one clause.

types.name: And
types.signature: type And struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc: And is logical conjunction of two operands.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the conjunction.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the conjunction as "(x & y)".

types.name: BDD
types.doc:
BDD is a reduced ordered binary decision diagram manager. It fixes a variable
ordering at construction and maintains a shared, canonical set of nodes so
that logically equal functions are represented by the identical [Node].

funcs.name: NewBDD
funcs.doc:
NewBDD returns a manager whose variables are tested in the given order, most
significant (closest to the root) first.

methods.name: And
methods.doc: And returns the conjunction of f and g.

methods.name: AnySat
methods.doc:
AnySat returns one satisfying assignment of the function n as a name->value
map and true, or nil and false when n is unsatisfiable. Variables not tested
on the chosen path are set to false.

methods.name: Apply
methods.signature: func (b *BDD) Apply(op string, f, g Node) Node
methods.doc:
Apply combines f and g with the named binary operator ("and", "or", "xor",
"nand", "nor", "iff", "implies"). Unknown names default to conjunction.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether two nodes denote the same Boolean function. Because the
diagram is reduced and ordered, this is exactly node identity.

methods.name: Eval
methods.signature: func (b *BDD) Eval(n Node, env map[string]bool) bool
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the function n under the given environment.

methods.name: FromExpr
methods.doc:
FromExpr builds the BDD node for the expression e, testing its variables in
the manager's ordering. Variables absent from the ordering are appended.

methods.name: Iff
methods.doc: Iff returns the biconditional (equivalence) of f and g.

methods.name: Implies
methods.doc: Implies returns the conditional f -> g.

methods.name: IsContradiction
methods.doc: IsContradiction reports whether n is the false terminal.

methods.name: IsSatisfiable
methods.doc: IsSatisfiable reports whether n is not the false terminal.

methods.name: IsTautology
methods.doc:
IsTautology reports whether n is the true terminal, i.e. the function is
identically true.

methods.name: Ite
methods.signature: func (b *BDD) Ite(f, g, h Node) Node
methods.doc:
Ite computes the if-then-else operator ite(f, g, h) = (f AND g) OR (NOT f AND
h), the universal ternary connective from which all binary Boolean operators
are derived.

methods.name: Nand
methods.doc: Nand returns the negated conjunction of f and g.

methods.name: Nor
methods.doc: Nor returns the negated disjunction of f and g.

methods.name: Not
methods.doc: Not returns the negation of n.

methods.name: NumNodes
methods.doc:
NumNodes returns the total number of distinct nodes allocated, including the
two terminals.

methods.name: Or
methods.doc: Or returns the disjunction of f and g.

methods.name: Order
methods.doc: Order returns the variable ordering used by the manager.

methods.name: Restrict
methods.signature: func (b *BDD) Restrict(n Node, name string, val bool) Node
methods.doc:
Restrict returns the cofactor of n obtained by fixing the variable name to
val (Shannon restriction).

methods.name: SatCount
methods.doc:
SatCount returns the number of assignments over all ordering variables under
which the function represented by n is true.

methods.name: ToExpr
methods.doc:
ToExpr reconstructs a Boolean [Expr] from the node n by Shannon expansion,
using the manager's variable names.

methods.name: Var
methods.doc:
Var returns the node representing the single variable name. The variable must
be part of the manager's ordering.

methods.name: Xor
methods.doc: Xor returns the exclusive-or of f and g.

types.name: BoolConst
types.signature: type BoolConst bool
types.doc: BoolConst is a Boolean literal constant, either true or false.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the constant's value.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns "T" or "F".

types.name: CNF
types.doc:
CNF is a formula in conjunctive normal form: a conjunction (AND) of clauses,
each of which is a disjunction (OR) of literals. An empty clause list is the
valid formula (true); a formula containing an empty clause is unsatisfiable.

funcs.name: NewCNF
funcs.doc: NewCNF returns a CNF formula built from the given clauses.

funcs.name: ParseDIMACS
funcs.doc:
ParseDIMACS parses a formula from DIMACS CNF text. Comment lines beginning
with 'c' and the optional 'p cnf' header are ignored; literals are read as
signed integers with 0 terminating each clause.

funcs.name: PureLiteralElimination
funcs.signature: func PureLiteralElimination(f CNF, assign map[int]bool) (CNF, map[int]bool)
funcs.doc:
PureLiteralElimination assigns every pure literal (one occurring with a single
polarity) to the value that satisfies its clauses, repeating until no pure
literals remain. It returns the simplified formula and the extended
assignment.

funcs.name: TseitinCNF
funcs.doc: TseitinCNF returns just the equisatisfiable CNF from [Tseitin].

funcs.name: UnitPropagate
funcs.signature: func UnitPropagate(f CNF, assign map[int]bool) (CNF, map[int]bool, bool)
funcs.doc:
UnitPropagate repeatedly assigns the literal of every unit clause and
simplifies the formula accordingly. It returns the simplified formula, the
extended assignment, and a boolean that is false when propagation derived a
conflict (an empty clause).

methods.name: AddClause
methods.doc: AddClause returns a new formula with c appended to the conjunction.

methods.name: Assign
methods.doc:
Assign returns the formula conditioned on literal l being true: clauses
satisfied by l are removed and the complement of l is deleted from the
remaining clauses (unit propagation of a single literal).

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns a deep copy of the formula.

methods.name: Concat
methods.doc: Concat returns the conjunction of two CNF formulas.

methods.name: CountLiterals
methods.doc: CountLiterals returns the total number of literal occurrences in the formula.

methods.name: DIMACS
methods.doc:
DIMACS renders the formula in the standard DIMACS CNF text format, with a
"p cnf <vars> <clauses>" header followed by one zero-terminated clause per
line.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval evaluates the formula under the given assignment: true when every clause
is satisfied.

methods.name: HasEmptyClause
methods.doc:
HasEmptyClause reports whether the formula contains an empty clause, which
makes it unsatisfiable.

methods.name: IsEmpty
methods.doc:
IsEmpty reports whether the formula has no clauses, in which case it is
trivially satisfied.

methods.name: Literals
methods.doc:
Literals returns every literal occurrence across all clauses, with
repetition.

methods.name: MaxVar
methods.doc:
MaxVar returns the largest variable index occurring in the formula, or 0 when
there are none.

methods.name: Negate
methods.doc: Negate on a CNF returns a DNF equivalent to its negation by De Morgan's law.

methods.name: NumClauses
methods.doc: NumClauses returns the number of clauses in the formula.

methods.name: NumVars
methods.doc: NumVars returns the count of distinct variables appearing in the formula.

methods.name: PureLiterals
methods.doc:
PureLiterals returns the literals that occur with only one polarity across
the whole formula. Such literals can always be set true without falsifying a
clause.

methods.name: RemoveDuplicateClauses
methods.doc:
RemoveDuplicateClauses returns an equivalent formula with syntactically
duplicate clauses collapsed to a single copy.

methods.name: Simplify
methods.doc:
Simplify returns an equivalent formula with tautological clauses dropped and
duplicate literals removed from each remaining clause.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the formula as a conjunction of parenthesised clauses joined
by " & ".

methods.name: UnitClauses
methods.doc:
UnitClauses returns the literals of every unit (single-literal) clause in the
formula.

methods.name: Vars
methods.doc: Vars returns the sorted set of distinct variable indices in the formula.

types.name: Clause
types.signature: type Clause []Lit
types.doc:
Clause is a disjunction of literals (an OR term) when used inside a [CNF] and
a conjunction of literals (an AND term) when used inside a [DNF]. The slice
order carries no logical meaning.

funcs.name: NewClause
funcs.doc: NewClause returns a clause built from the given literals.

funcs.name: Resolve
funcs.signature: func Resolve(c1, c2 Clause, v int) (Clause, bool)
funcs.doc:
Resolve computes the resolvent of two clauses on the pivot variable v: the
disjunction of all their other literals, provided one clause contains the
positive and the other the negative literal of v. It returns the resolvent
and true, or nil and false when the clauses cannot be resolved on v.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the clause.

methods.name: Contains
methods.doc: Contains reports whether the literal l appears in the clause.

methods.name: ContainsVar
methods.doc:
ContainsVar reports whether variable v appears in the clause with either
polarity.

methods.name: Dedup
methods.doc:
Dedup returns a copy of the clause with duplicate literals removed, order
preserved.

methods.name: EvalAnd
methods.doc:
EvalAnd evaluates the clause as a conjunction under the assignment: true when
every literal is satisfied.

methods.name: EvalOr
methods.doc:
EvalOr evaluates the clause as a disjunction under the assignment: true when
at least one literal is satisfied.

methods.name: IsEmpty
methods.doc:
IsEmpty reports whether the clause has no literals. An empty disjunctive
clause is unsatisfiable (false); an empty conjunctive term is valid (true).

methods.name: IsTautology
methods.doc:
IsTautology reports whether the disjunctive clause contains a literal and its
negation, making it trivially true.

methods.name: IsUnit
methods.doc: IsUnit reports whether the clause consists of a single literal.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of literals in the clause.

methods.name: MaxVar
methods.doc:
MaxVar returns the largest variable index occurring in the clause, or 0 for
the empty clause.

methods.name: Negate
methods.doc:
Negate returns the literal-wise complement of the clause. Interpreting the
input as a disjunction, the result is the conjunction (as a slice of unit
clauses) equivalent to its negation by De Morgan's law.

methods.name: Sorted
methods.doc: Sorted returns a copy of the clause sorted by variable index then sign.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the clause as a parenthesised disjunction such as
"(x1 | ~x2 | x3)".

methods.name: Vars
methods.doc:
Vars returns the sorted set of distinct variable indices occurring in the
clause.

types.name: DNF
types.doc:
DNF is a formula in disjunctive normal form: a disjunction (OR) of terms,
each of which is a conjunction (AND) of literals. An empty term list is the
unsatisfiable formula (false); a formula containing an empty term is valid.

funcs.name: NewDNF
funcs.doc: NewDNF returns a DNF formula built from the given terms.

methods.name: AddTerm
methods.doc: AddTerm returns a new formula with t appended to the disjunction.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns a deep copy of the formula.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc:
Eval evaluates the formula under the assignment: true when at least one term
is fully satisfied.

methods.name: IsEmpty
methods.doc:
IsEmpty reports whether the formula has no terms, in which case it is
unsatisfiable.

methods.name: MaxVar
methods.doc: MaxVar returns the largest variable index occurring in the formula, or 0.

methods.name: Negate
methods.doc:
Negate returns a CNF formula logically equivalent to the negation of the DNF,
applying De Morgan's law term-by-term.

methods.name: NumTerms
methods.doc: NumTerms returns the number of terms in the formula.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the formula as a disjunction of parenthesised conjunctive
terms joined by " | ".

methods.name: Vars
methods.doc: Vars returns the sorted set of distinct variable indices in the formula.

types.name: Expr
types.doc:
Expr is the sealed interface implemented by every node of a Boolean
expression tree. Concrete implementations are [BoolConst], [Variable],
[Not], [And], [Or], [Xor], [Implies], [Iff], [Nand], [Nor] and [Xnor].

vars.signature: var False Expr = BoolConst(false)
vars.doc: False is the constant false expression.

vars.signature: var True Expr = BoolConst(true)
vars.doc: True is the constant true expression.

funcs.name: AndAll
funcs.doc:
AndAll returns the conjunction of all expressions. The empty conjunction is
[True].

funcs.name: AndE
funcs.doc: AndE returns the conjunction of x and y.

funcs.name: CNFToExpr
funcs.doc:
CNFToExpr renders a clausal CNF as an [Expr] tree using the variable names in
vm.

funcs.name: Constant
funcs.doc: Constant returns the constant expression for b.

funcs.name: DNFToExpr
funcs.doc: DNFToExpr renders a clausal DNF as an [Expr] tree using the names in vm.

funcs.name: EliminateImplications
funcs.doc:
EliminateImplications rewrites an expression so that it uses only the And,
Or and Not connectives, expanding conditionals, biconditionals and the
derived gates Xor, Nand, Nor and Xnor into that basis.

funcs.name: IffE
funcs.doc: IffE returns the biconditional x <-> y.

funcs.name: ImpliesE
funcs.doc: ImpliesE returns the conditional x -> y.

funcs.name: MinimizeSOPExpr
funcs.doc:
MinimizeSOPExpr returns the minimised sum-of-products cover of the function as
a Boolean [Expr] built from the given variable names.

funcs.name: MustParse
funcs.doc:
MustParse is like [Parse] but panics on error. It is intended for use with
constant expressions in tests and package-level initialisers.

funcs.name: NandE
funcs.doc: NandE returns the nand of x and y.

funcs.name: NorE
funcs.doc: NorE returns the nor of x and y.

funcs.name: NotE
funcs.doc: NotE returns the negation of x, folding double negations and constants.

funcs.name: OrAll
funcs.doc:
OrAll returns the disjunction of all expressions. The empty disjunction is
[False].

funcs.name: OrE
funcs.doc: OrE returns the disjunction of x and y.

funcs.name: Parse
funcs.doc:
Parse parses a Boolean expression from its textual form and returns the
corresponding [Expr] tree. Supported operators, from lowest to highest
precedence, are the biconditional ("<->" or "=="), the conditional ("->"),
disjunction ("|" or "or"), exclusive-or ("^" or "xor"), conjunction ("&" or
"and") and negation ("~", "!" or "not"). Identifiers are variables and the
words true/false (also T/F, 1/0) are constants.

funcs.name: Simplify
funcs.doc:
Simplify applies Boolean simplification rules — constant folding, negation,
idempotence, complementation, identity, domination and absorption — repeatedly
until the expression reaches a fixed point.

funcs.name: Substitute
funcs.signature: func Substitute(e Expr, name string, repl Expr) Expr
funcs.doc:
Substitute returns a copy of e with every occurrence of the variable named
name replaced by the expression repl.

funcs.name: ToCNFExpr
funcs.doc:
ToCNFExpr converts an arbitrary Boolean expression into an equivalent
expression in conjunctive normal form using De Morgan's laws and distribution
of disjunction over conjunction. The result may be exponentially larger than
the input; use [Tseitin] when only equisatisfiability is required.

funcs.name: ToDNFExpr
funcs.doc:
ToDNFExpr converts an arbitrary Boolean expression into an equivalent
expression in disjunctive normal form.

funcs.name: ToNNF
funcs.doc:
ToNNF converts an expression to negation normal form, in which negations
apply only to variables and the tree uses solely And, Or and Not (over
literals). Implications and biconditionals are first eliminated.

funcs.name: V
funcs.doc: V returns a variable expression with the given name.

funcs.name: XnorE
funcs.doc: XnorE returns the xnor of x and y.

funcs.name: XorAll
funcs.doc:
XorAll returns the exclusive-or of all expressions. The empty case is
[False].

funcs.name: XorE
funcs.doc: XorE returns the exclusive-or of x and y.

types.name: Iff
types.signature: type Iff struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc: Iff is the biconditional X <-> Y (logical equivalence).

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the biconditional.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the biconditional as "(x <-> y)".

types.name: Implicant
types.doc:
Implicant is a Boolean cube: a product term covering a set of minterms. Value
holds the fixed bit pattern (dash positions are zero) and Mask marks the
positions that are "don't care" (combined away), so the cube covers exactly
the assignments x with (x &^ Mask) == Value.

funcs.name: EssentialPrimeImplicants
funcs.doc:
EssentialPrimeImplicants returns the prime implicants that are the sole cover
of at least one required minterm (a minterm in the on-set, ignoring
don't-cares).

funcs.name: MinimizeSOP
funcs.doc:
MinimizeSOP returns a minimal (greedy) sum-of-products cover of the function
as a list of implicants: all essential prime implicants plus a greedy
selection of further primes to cover the remaining minterms.

funcs.name: PrimeImplicants
funcs.doc:
PrimeImplicants returns the prime implicants of the Boolean function defined
by the given on-set minterms and don't-care terms over numVars variables,
computed by iterated Quine-McCluskey combination.

funcs.name: QuineMcCluskey
funcs.doc:
QuineMcCluskey is an alias for [PrimeImplicants], returning all prime
implicants of the function.

methods.name: Covers
methods.doc: Covers reports whether the cube covers the minterm m.

methods.name: Minterms
methods.doc: Minterms returns the sorted list of minterm indices covered by the cube.

methods.name: Size
methods.doc:
Size returns the number of minterms the cube covers, which is 2 raised to the
number of dash positions.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the cube as a pattern of '0', '1' and '-' characters,
most-significant variable first.

methods.name: Term
methods.doc:
Term renders the cube as a product term using the given variable names, most
significant first, e.g. "a & ~c". The all-dash cube renders as "1".

types.name: Implies
types.signature: type Implies struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc: Implies is the material conditional X -> Y.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the conditional.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the conditional as "(x -> y)".

types.name: KarnaughMap
types.doc:
KarnaughMap is a two-dimensional Gray-coded arrangement of the truth table of
a Boolean function, the layout used for manual minimisation.

funcs.name: NewKarnaughMap
funcs.signature: func NewKarnaughMap(minterms, dontcares []int, numVars int) KarnaughMap
funcs.doc:
NewKarnaughMap builds the Karnaugh map of the function defined by minterms and
dontcares over numVars variables. The higher-order half of the variables
index rows and the lower-order half index columns.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the Karnaugh map as a grid of '0', '1' and 'X' (don't-care)
characters with Gray-coded axis labels.

types.name: Lit
types.signature: type Lit int
types.doc:
Lit is a Boolean literal: a signed reference to a variable. A positive value
v denotes the variable v asserted true; a negative value -v denotes the
variable v asserted false. Variables are numbered from 1 upward, following
the DIMACS convention, so the zero value is not a valid literal.

funcs.name: MakeLit
funcs.signature: func MakeLit(v int, negated bool) Lit
funcs.doc:
MakeLit builds the literal for variable v (which must be >= 1). If negated is
true the returned literal is the negation of v, otherwise it is v asserted
positively.

funcs.name: NegLit
funcs.doc: NegLit returns the negative literal for variable v.

funcs.name: PosLit
funcs.doc: PosLit returns the positive literal for variable v.

methods.name: IsNeg
methods.doc: IsNeg reports whether the literal is negated (its variable asserted false).

methods.name: IsPos
methods.doc: IsPos reports whether the literal is positive (its variable asserted true).

methods.name: Negate
methods.doc: Negate returns the complementary literal, flipping its polarity.

methods.name: Satisfied
methods.doc:
Satisfied reports whether the literal evaluates to true under the given
variable assignment. A variable missing from the assignment is treated as
false.

methods.name: Sign
methods.doc: Sign returns +1 for a positive literal and -1 for a negative literal.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the literal in the form "x3" for a positive literal and
"~x3" for a negative literal.

methods.name: Valid
methods.doc: Valid reports whether the literal refers to a real variable (index >= 1).

methods.name: Var
methods.doc:
Var returns the underlying variable index of the literal, always a
non-negative number, discarding the sign.

types.name: Nand
types.signature: type Nand struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc: Nand is the negated conjunction of two operands.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the nand.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the nand as "(x !& y)".

types.name: Node
types.signature: type Node int
types.doc:
Node identifies a vertex of a reduced ordered binary decision diagram managed
by a [BDD]. The constants [BDDFalse] and [BDDTrue] are the two terminals.

types.name: Nor
types.signature: type Nor struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc: Nor is the negated disjunction of two operands.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the nor.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the nor as "(x !| y)".

types.name: Not
types.signature: type Not struct{ X Expr }
types.doc: Not is logical negation of its operand.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the negation.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the negation as "~(x)".

types.name: Or
types.signature: type Or struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc: Or is logical inclusive disjunction of two operands.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the disjunction.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the disjunction as "(x | y)".

types.name: Solver
types.doc:
Solver is a small stateful wrapper around the DPLL search that remembers the
formula and its most recent model.

funcs.name: NewSolver
funcs.doc: NewSolver returns a solver for the given formula.

methods.name: AddClause
methods.doc:
AddClause adds a clause to the solver's formula and invalidates any cached
result.

methods.name: Model
methods.doc:
Model returns the satisfying assignment found by the most recent successful
[Solver.Solve]. It is nil when the formula is unsatisfiable or unsolved.

methods.name: Solve
methods.doc:
Solve runs the search and returns whether the formula is satisfiable, caching
the outcome and model.

types.name: TruthRow
types.doc:
TruthRow is a single line of a [TruthTable]: an assignment to the variables
and the resulting value of the expression.

types.name: TruthTable
types.doc:
TruthTable is the exhaustive truth table of a Boolean expression over its
variables, ordered so that the first variable is the most significant bit of
the row index.

funcs.name: NewTruthTable
funcs.doc:
NewTruthTable builds the complete truth table of e. Variables are taken in
sorted order.

methods.name: Column
methods.doc:
Column returns the output column of the table as a slice of booleans in row
order.

methods.name: Maxterms
methods.doc: Maxterms returns the row indices at which the expression is false.

methods.name: Minterms
methods.doc: Minterms returns the row indices at which the expression is true.

methods.name: NumRows
methods.doc:
NumRows returns the number of rows, which is 2 raised to the number of
variables.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the truth table as fixed-width text with a header row.

types.name: TseitinEncoder
types.doc:
TseitinEncoder builds an equisatisfiable CNF encoding of a Boolean expression
by introducing a fresh auxiliary variable for every compound subexpression.
The resulting formula grows only linearly in the size of the input, unlike
the potentially exponential distribution performed by [ToCNFFormula].

funcs.name: NewTseitinEncoder
funcs.doc: NewTseitinEncoder returns an empty encoder ready to encode expressions.

methods.name: Assert
methods.doc:
Assert records that the encoded expression must be true, by adding a unit
clause on its top-level literal.

methods.name: CNF
methods.doc: CNF returns the accumulated clauses as a [CNF] formula.

methods.name: Clauses
methods.doc: Clauses returns the clauses accumulated so far.

methods.name: Encode
methods.doc:
Encode registers the clauses that define e and returns a literal that is true
exactly when e is true. Repeated identical subexpressions share their
auxiliary variable.

methods.name: VarMap
methods.doc:
VarMap returns the variable map that records both original and auxiliary
variable names introduced during encoding.

types.name: VarMap
types.doc:
VarMap maps variable names to positive integer indices (1-based), the numeric
encoding used by the clausal solver.

funcs.name: NewVarMap
funcs.doc: NewVarMap builds a VarMap assigning indices 1..n to the given names in order.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the index of name, allocating a fresh one if it is new.

methods.name: Index
methods.doc: Index returns the index of name and whether it is present.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of variables in the map.

methods.name: Name
methods.doc:
Name returns the variable name for index i (1-based), or the empty string if
out of range.

types.name: Variable
types.signature: type Variable string
types.doc: Variable is a named Boolean variable.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval looks the variable up in env, defaulting to false.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns the variable name.

types.name: Xnor
types.signature: type Xnor struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc:
Xnor is the negated exclusive disjunction (equivalence) of two operands. It
is logically identical to [Iff] but retained as a distinct gate.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the xnor.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the xnor as "(x ~^ y)".

types.name: Xor
types.signature: type Xor struct{ X, Y Expr }
types.doc: Xor is exclusive disjunction of two operands.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval evaluates the exclusive-or.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the exclusive-or as "(x ^ y)".

funcs.name: AllModels
funcs.doc: AllModels returns every satisfying assignment of e over its variables.

funcs.name: AllSolutions
funcs.doc:
AllSolutions returns every complete satisfying assignment of f over its
variables, enumerated in ascending mask order.

funcs.name: BitString
funcs.doc:
BitString returns the binary representation of x in exactly numVars digits,
most-significant bit first.

funcs.name: CountModels
funcs.doc:
CountModels returns the number of satisfying assignments of e over its
variables.

funcs.name: CountNodesByKind
funcs.doc:
CountNodesByKind returns a map from a short kind name ("var", "const", "and",
...) to the number of nodes of that kind in e.

funcs.name: CountSolutions
funcs.doc:
CountSolutions returns the number of complete satisfying assignments of f
over its variables.

funcs.name: DPLL
funcs.doc:
DPLL solves the satisfiability of a CNF formula with the classic
Davis-Putnam-Logemann-Loveland procedure: unit propagation, pure-literal
elimination and backtracking search. It returns a satisfying partial
assignment and true, or nil and false when the formula is unsatisfiable.

funcs.name: Depth
funcs.doc: Depth returns the height of the expression tree; a leaf has depth 1.

funcs.name: Entails
funcs.doc:
Entails reports whether a semantically entails b (a |= b): every assignment
satisfying a also satisfies b.

funcs.name: EnvToIndex
funcs.doc:
EnvToIndex converts a variable environment to its row index for the given
sorted variable list, first variable most significant.

funcs.name: Equivalent
funcs.doc:
Equivalent reports whether two expressions have identical truth values on
every assignment to their combined variables.

funcs.name: EquivalentCNF
funcs.doc:
EquivalentCNF reports whether two clausal CNF formulas are logically
equivalent by comparing their truth tables over the union of their variables.

funcs.name: Eval
funcs.signature: func Eval(e Expr, env map[string]bool) bool
funcs.doc:
Eval evaluates e under env; a convenience wrapper over the [Expr.Eval]
method.

funcs.name: EvalString
funcs.signature: func EvalString(s string, env map[string]bool) (bool, error)
funcs.doc:
EvalString parses s and evaluates it under env, returning the value and any
parse error.

funcs.name: ExprEqual
funcs.doc: ExprEqual reports whether two expression trees are structurally identical.

funcs.name: FindModel
funcs.doc:
FindModel returns a satisfying assignment for e and true, or nil and false if
e is unsatisfiable.

funcs.name: GrayCode
funcs.doc: GrayCode returns the reflected binary Gray-code sequence for n bits.

funcs.name: GrayDecode
funcs.doc: GrayDecode inverts [GrayEncode].

funcs.name: GrayEncode
funcs.doc: GrayEncode returns the reflected binary Gray code of n, equal to n XOR (n>>1).

funcs.name: Implies2
funcs.doc:
Implies reports the same relation as [Entails]; provided as a readable alias
for semantic implication.

funcs.name: IndexToEnv
funcs.doc:
IndexToEnv converts a row index to a variable environment for the given
sorted variable list, first variable most significant.

funcs.name: IsCNFExpr
funcs.doc:
IsCNFExpr reports whether e is syntactically in conjunctive normal form: a
conjunction of disjunctions of literals.

funcs.name: IsContingent
funcs.doc:
IsContingent reports whether e is neither a tautology nor a contradiction:
true for some assignments and false for others.

funcs.name: IsContradiction
funcs.doc: IsContradiction reports whether e evaluates to false under every assignment.

funcs.name: IsDNFExpr
funcs.doc:
IsDNFExpr reports whether e is syntactically in disjunctive normal form: a
disjunction of conjunctions of literals.

funcs.name: IsNNF
funcs.doc:
IsNNF reports whether e is already in negation normal form: it contains only
And, Or, Not, Variable and BoolConst nodes and every Not wraps a variable.

funcs.name: IsSatisfiable
funcs.doc:
IsSatisfiable reports whether e evaluates to true under at least one
assignment.

funcs.name: IsSatisfiableCNF
funcs.doc:
IsSatisfiableCNF reports whether the CNF formula has at least one satisfying
assignment.

funcs.name: IsTautology
funcs.doc: IsTautology reports whether e evaluates to true under every assignment.

funcs.name: IsTautologyCNF
funcs.doc:
IsTautologyCNF reports whether every assignment satisfies f, i.e. the
negation of f is unsatisfiable. It is decided by enumeration over the
variables of f.

funcs.name: IsUnsatisfiableCNF
funcs.doc:
IsUnsatisfiableCNF reports whether the CNF formula has no satisfying
assignment.

funcs.name: Leaves
funcs.doc:
Leaves returns the sorted distinct variable names, identical to [Vars]; kept
for readability at call sites that view the tree as a term.

funcs.name: Maxterms
funcs.doc: Maxterms returns the maxterm indices of e: the assignments where e is false.

funcs.name: Minterms
funcs.doc:
Minterms returns the set of minterm indices of e: the assignments (as integer
row indices with the first sorted variable most significant) where e is true.

funcs.name: NewBDDFromExpr
funcs.doc:
NewBDDFromExpr is a convenience that builds a manager whose ordering is the
sorted variables of e and returns both the manager and the root node.

funcs.name: PopCount
funcs.doc: PopCount returns the number of one bits (the Hamming weight) of x.

funcs.name: PrettyString
funcs.doc:
PrettyString is an alias for the expression's String rendering, provided for
symmetry with other renderers.

funcs.name: SOPString
funcs.doc:
SOPString renders a list of implicants as a sum-of-products Boolean
expression using the given variable names, most significant first. An empty
cover renders as "0".

funcs.name: Size
funcs.doc: Size returns the number of nodes in the expression tree.

funcs.name: SolveCNF
funcs.doc:
SolveCNF solves f and, on success, returns a complete assignment covering
every variable of f (variables left free by the search are set to false).

funcs.name: ToCNFFormula
funcs.doc:
ToCNFFormula converts an expression to a clausal [CNF] together with the
[VarMap] recording the name-to-index correspondence. The encoding is
equivalence-preserving (via distribution), so it is exact rather than merely
equisatisfiable.

funcs.name: ToDNFFormula
funcs.doc:
ToDNFFormula converts an expression to a clausal [DNF] together with the
[VarMap] recording the name-to-index correspondence.

funcs.name: Tseitin
funcs.doc:
Tseitin returns an equisatisfiable CNF encoding of e that asserts e is true,
together with the variable map used. The original variables of e keep the
lowest indices; auxiliary variables follow.

funcs.name: Vars
funcs.doc: Vars returns the sorted set of distinct variable names occurring in e.

examples.name: IsTautology
examples.code:
e := MustParse("a | ~a")
fmt.Println(IsTautology(e))

examples.name: SolveCNF
examples.code:
f := NewCNF(
	NewClause(PosLit(1), PosLit(2)),
	NewClause(NegLit(1), PosLit(3)),
)
_, sat := SolveCNF(f)
fmt.Println(sat)

examples.name: MinimizeSOPExpr
examples.code:
expr := MinimizeSOPExpr([]int{0, 2, 4, 6}, nil, []string{"a", "b", "c"})
fmt.Println(expr)

examples.name: Parse
examples.code:
e := MustParse("a -> b")
fmt.Println(Equivalent(e, MustParse("~a | b")))

packages.name: seq
packages.synopsis: Package seq implements classical integer sequences and figurate numbers.
packages.doc:
Package seq implements classical integer sequences and figurate numbers.

The package is a self-contained catalogue of the recurrences, closed forms
and iterative procedures that recur throughout elementary number theory and
recreational mathematics. It is organised into three broad families:

  - Linear recurrences and their relatives: the Fibonacci numbers computed by
    the fast-doubling method, together with the Lucas, Pell, Pell-Lucas,
    Jacobsthal, Jacobsthal-Lucas, Tribonacci, Tetranacci, Padovan and Perrin
    sequences, a fully general constant-coefficient LinearRecurrence engine
    and the Zeckendorf representation.

  - Figurate numbers: the polygonal numbers (triangular, square, pentagonal,
    hexagonal, heptagonal, octagonal and the general s-gonal family), the
    centered polygonal numbers, the three-dimensional figurate numbers
    (tetrahedral, square-pyramidal), the four-dimensional pentatope numbers
    and assorted companions (pronic, star, cube and gnomonic numbers), each
    paired where natural with a membership predicate.

  - Digit- and iteration-driven sequences: the Collatz trajectory, happy
    numbers, Kaprekar numbers and the Kaprekar 6174 routine, Recaman's
    sequence and the look-and-say sequence.

Machine-word routines return uint64 (or int64 where negative terms arise) and
document the index beyond which they overflow; arbitrary-precision *big.Int
variants are provided for the Fibonacci-family sequences where exact values
far beyond the 64-bit range are wanted. Every routine is deterministic and
depends only on the Go standard library.

funcs.name: CenteredHexagonal
funcs.doc:
CenteredHexagonal returns the n-th centered hexagonal number 3n(n+1) + 1,
also known as a hex number: 1, 7, 19, 37, 61, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: CenteredPolygonal
funcs.doc:
CenteredPolygonal returns the n-th centered s-gonal number
s·n(n+1)/2 + 1, the count of dots in a centered polygonal arrangement with
n rings around a central dot: for s=4 this is 1, 5, 13, 25, 41, … s must be
at least 3 and n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: CenteredSquare
funcs.doc:
CenteredSquare returns the n-th centered square number 2n(n+1) + 1:
1, 5, 13, 25, 41, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: CenteredTriangular
funcs.doc:
CenteredTriangular returns the n-th centered triangular number
3n(n+1)/2 + 1: 1, 4, 10, 19, 31, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: CollatzMax
funcs.doc:
CollatzMax returns the largest value attained along the Collatz trajectory of
n, including n itself. n must be positive.

funcs.name: CollatzNext
funcs.doc:
CollatzNext returns the successor of n under the Collatz (3n+1) map: n/2 when
n is even and 3n+1 when n is odd. n must be positive.

funcs.name: CollatzSequence
funcs.doc:
CollatzSequence returns the Collatz trajectory of n: the sequence starting at
n and repeatedly applying CollatzNext until it reaches 1, inclusive of both
endpoints. n must be positive. Intermediate values are assumed to stay within
the range of uint64.

funcs.name: CollatzSteps
funcs.doc:
CollatzSteps returns the total stopping time of n: the number of applications
of the Collatz map required to reach 1. CollatzSteps(1) is 0. n must be
positive.

funcs.name: Cube
funcs.doc: Cube returns the n-th cube number n³. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Fibonacci
funcs.doc:
Fibonacci returns the n-th Fibonacci number Fₙ, defined by F₀ = 0, F₁ = 1 and
Fₙ = Fₙ₋₁ + Fₙ₋₂. It is computed by the fast-doubling method in O(log n)
arithmetic operations. n must be non-negative. The result is exact for
n ≤ 93; beyond that Fₙ exceeds the range of uint64 and the value wraps.

funcs.name: FibonacciBig
funcs.doc:
FibonacciBig returns the n-th Fibonacci number Fₙ as an arbitrary-precision
integer, computed by the fast-doubling method in O(log n) big-integer
multiplications. n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: FibonacciSequence
funcs.doc:
FibonacciSequence returns the first n Fibonacci numbers F₀ … Fₙ₋₁. n must be
non-negative; a value of 0 yields an empty slice. Values are exact for
indices up to 93.

funcs.name: GeneralizedPentagonal
funcs.doc:
GeneralizedPentagonal returns n(3n−1)/2 evaluated at the signed index n, so
that ranging n over 0, 1, −1, 2, −2, … yields the generalized pentagonal
numbers 0, 1, 2, 5, 7, 12, 15, … that appear in Euler's pentagonal number
theorem. The result is int64 because the index may be negative.

funcs.name: Gnomonic
funcs.doc:
Gnomonic returns the n-th gnomonic number 2n−1, the n-th odd number and the
difference between consecutive square numbers: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, … n must be at
least 1.

funcs.name: HappyNumbers
funcs.doc:
HappyNumbers returns the first count happy numbers in increasing order,
beginning with 1, 7, 10, 13, 19, 23, 28, … count must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Heptagonal
funcs.doc:
Heptagonal returns the n-th heptagonal number n(5n−3)/2: 0, 1, 7, 18, 34,
55, 81, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Hexagonal
funcs.doc:
Hexagonal returns the n-th hexagonal number Hₙ = n(2n−1): 0, 1, 6, 15, 28,
45, 66, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: IntSqrt
funcs.doc:
IntSqrt returns the integer square root of v, that is the largest integer r
with r*r <= v. It is exact for every uint64 input.

funcs.name: IsFibonacci
funcs.doc:
IsFibonacci reports whether n is a Fibonacci number. Every non-negative
integer that appears in the sequence 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, … qualifies.

funcs.name: IsHappy
funcs.doc:
IsHappy reports whether n is a happy number: starting from n and repeatedly
replacing the value by the sum of the squares of its decimal digits, the
process reaches 1. Unhappy numbers instead enter the cycle that contains 4.
n must be positive.

funcs.name: IsHexagonal
funcs.doc:
IsHexagonal reports whether x is a hexagonal number, that is whether
(1+√(8x+1))/4 is a positive integer.

funcs.name: IsKaprekar
funcs.doc:
IsKaprekar reports whether n is a Kaprekar number: the decimal representation
of n² can be split into a left part and a non-zero right part that add up to
n. For example 45² = 2025 splits as 20+25 = 45, and 4879² = 23804641 splits
as 238+04641 = 4879. The value 1 qualifies by convention. n must be positive.
This is the classical definition (OEIS A006886), which permits the split at
any digit boundary.

funcs.name: IsPentagonal
funcs.doc:
IsPentagonal reports whether x is a (non-negative-index) pentagonal number,
that is whether (1+√(24x+1))/6 is a positive integer.

funcs.name: IsPerfectSquare
funcs.doc: IsPerfectSquare reports whether v is a perfect square.

funcs.name: IsPolygonal
funcs.signature: func IsPolygonal(s int, x uint64) bool
funcs.doc:
IsPolygonal reports whether x is an s-gonal number for some non-negative
index, i.e. whether the equation P(s, n) = x has a non-negative integer
solution n. s must be at least 3.

funcs.name: IsPronic
funcs.doc:
IsPronic reports whether x is a pronic number, that is whether x = n(n+1)
for some non-negative integer n.

funcs.name: IsSquare
funcs.doc:
IsSquare reports whether x is a perfect square. It is an alias of
IsPerfectSquare provided for symmetry with the other figurate predicates.

funcs.name: IsTriangular
funcs.doc:
IsTriangular reports whether x is a triangular number, that is whether
8x+1 is a perfect square.

funcs.name: Jacobsthal
funcs.doc:
Jacobsthal returns the n-th Jacobsthal number Jₙ, defined by J₀ = 0, J₁ = 1
and Jₙ = Jₙ₋₁ + 2·Jₙ₋₂: 0, 1, 1, 3, 5, 11, 21, … equivalently
Jₙ = (2ⁿ − (−1)ⁿ)/3. n must be non-negative. The result is exact for n ≤ 63.

funcs.name: JacobsthalLucas
funcs.doc:
JacobsthalLucas returns the n-th Jacobsthal-Lucas number, defined by the same
recurrence with initial values 2 and 1: 2, 1, 5, 7, 17, 31, … equivalently
2ⁿ + (−1)ⁿ. n must be non-negative. The result is exact for n ≤ 63.

funcs.name: JacobsthalSequence
funcs.doc:
JacobsthalSequence returns the first n Jacobsthal numbers J₀ … Jₙ₋₁. n must
be non-negative.

funcs.name: KaprekarNumbers
funcs.doc:
KaprekarNumbers returns every Kaprekar number in the closed interval
[1, limit], in increasing order: 1, 9, 45, 55, 99, 297, 703, 999, …

funcs.name: KaprekarSequence
funcs.doc:
KaprekarSequence returns the trajectory of the four-digit Kaprekar routine
starting from n, inclusive of n, continuing until it reaches the fixed point
6174, the fixed point 0 (for repdigits such as 1111), or a value that has
already been seen. n must be in the range [0, 9999].

funcs.name: KaprekarStep
funcs.doc:
KaprekarStep applies one step of Kaprekar's routine to a four-digit number:
the digits of n (zero-padded to four places) are arranged into the largest
and smallest numbers they form, and the smaller is subtracted from the
larger. n must be in the range [0, 9999]. Applied repeatedly to any four-digit
number that does not have all identical digits, the routine converges to
Kaprekar's constant 6174.

funcs.name: LinearRecurrence
funcs.signature: func LinearRecurrence(coeffs, init []int64, n int) int64
funcs.doc:
LinearRecurrence evaluates the term aₙ of the constant-coefficient linear
recurrence

	a(i) = coeffs[0]·a(i-1) + coeffs[1]·a(i-2) + … + coeffs[k-1]·a(i-k)

of order k = len(coeffs), whose first k terms a₀ … a₍ₖ₋₁₎ are supplied in
init. coeffs and init must be non-empty and of equal length, and n must be
non-negative. Arithmetic is performed in int64, so negative terms (as in the
Perrin sequence) are handled naturally; the caller is responsible for the
overflow range.

funcs.name: LinearRecurrenceBig
funcs.doc:
LinearRecurrenceBig evaluates term aₙ of the constant-coefficient linear
recurrence described by LinearRecurrence using arbitrary-precision integer
arithmetic, so no overflow can occur. coeffs and init must be non-empty and
of equal length, and n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LinearRecurrenceSequence
funcs.doc:
LinearRecurrenceSequence returns the first m terms a₀ … a₍ₘ₋₁₎ of the linear
recurrence described by LinearRecurrence. coeffs and init must be non-empty
and of equal length, and m must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LookAndSaySequence
funcs.doc:
LookAndSaySequence returns the first n terms of the look-and-say sequence
generated from the seed string, inclusive of the seed. Each subsequent term
is LookAndSayStep applied to the previous one. seed must be a non-empty
decimal digit string and n must be non-negative. With seed "1" the sequence
is "1", "11", "21", "1211", "111221", …

funcs.name: LookAndSayStep
funcs.doc:
LookAndSayStep returns the look-and-say successor of the decimal digit string
s: each maximal run of identical digits is replaced by its length followed by
the digit. For example "1211" becomes "111221". s must be non-empty and
consist only of decimal digits.

funcs.name: Lucas
funcs.doc:
Lucas returns the n-th Lucas number Lₙ, defined by L₀ = 2, L₁ = 1 and
Lₙ = Lₙ₋₁ + Lₙ₋₂. It is derived from the fast-doubling Fibonacci pair via the
identity Lₙ = 2·Fₙ₊₁ − Fₙ. n must be non-negative. The result is exact for
n ≤ 91; beyond that Lₙ exceeds the range of uint64.

funcs.name: LucasBig
funcs.doc:
LucasBig returns the n-th Lucas number Lₙ as an arbitrary-precision integer.
n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: LucasSequence
funcs.doc:
LucasSequence returns the first n Lucas numbers L₀ … Lₙ₋₁. n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: Octagonal
funcs.doc:
Octagonal returns the n-th octagonal number n(3n−2): 0, 1, 8, 21, 40, 65,
96, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Padovan
funcs.doc:
Padovan returns the n-th Padovan number, defined by P₀ = P₁ = P₂ = 1 and
Pₙ = Pₙ₋₂ + Pₙ₋₃: 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 9, 12, 16, … n must be
non-negative. The result is exact for n ≤ 219.

funcs.name: PadovanSequence
funcs.doc:
PadovanSequence returns the first n Padovan numbers P₀ … Pₙ₋₁. n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: Pell
PellBig
funcs.doc:
PellBig returns the n-th Pell number Pₙ as an arbitrary-precision integer.
n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: PellLucas
funcs.doc:
PellLucas returns the n-th Pell-Lucas (companion Pell) number Qₙ, defined by
Q₀ = 2, Q₁ = 2 and Qₙ = 2·Qₙ₋₁ + Qₙ₋₂: 2, 2, 6, 14, 34, 82, … n must be
non-negative. The result is exact for n ≤ 50.

funcs.name: PellSequence
funcs.doc:
PellSequence returns the first n Pell numbers P₀ … Pₙ₋₁. n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: Pentagonal
funcs.doc:
Pentagonal returns the n-th pentagonal number Pₙ = n(3n−1)/2: 0, 1, 5, 12,
22, 35, 51, … n must be non-negative. For the signed indexing used in
Euler's pentagonal number theorem see GeneralizedPentagonal.

funcs.name: Pentatope
funcs.doc:
Pentatope returns the n-th pentatope (4-simplex) number
n(n+1)(n+2)(n+3)/24, the four-dimensional analogue of the triangular and
tetrahedral numbers: 0, 1, 5, 15, 35, 70, 126, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Perrin
funcs.doc:
Perrin returns the n-th Perrin number, defined by P₀ = 3, P₁ = 0, P₂ = 2 and
Pₙ = Pₙ₋₂ + Pₙ₋₃: 3, 0, 2, 3, 2, 5, 5, 7, 10, 12, 17, 22, … n must be
non-negative. The result is exact for n ≤ 218.

funcs.name: PerrinSequence
funcs.doc:
PerrinSequence returns the first n Perrin numbers P₀ … Pₙ₋₁. n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: Polygonal
funcs.doc:
Polygonal returns the n-th s-gonal number

	P(s, n) = ((s−2)·n² − (s−4)·n) / 2,

the generalization of the triangular (s=3), square (s=4), pentagonal (s=5)
and higher polygonal numbers. s must be at least 3 and n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: Pronic
funcs.doc:
Pronic returns the n-th pronic (oblong) number n(n+1), the product of two
consecutive integers: 0, 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: RecamanSequence
funcs.doc:
RecamanSequence returns the first n terms of Recaman's sequence, defined by
a₀ = 0 and, for k ≥ 1,

	a(k) = a(k-1) − k  if that value is positive and not already in the sequence,
	a(k) = a(k-1) + k  otherwise.

The sequence begins 0, 1, 3, 6, 2, 7, 13, 20, 12, 21, 11, … n must be
non-negative.

funcs.name: Square
funcs.doc: Square returns the n-th square number n². n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: SquarePyramidal
funcs.doc:
SquarePyramidal returns the n-th square-pyramidal number
n(n+1)(2n+1)/6, the number of spheres in a pyramid with a square base of
side n: 0, 1, 5, 14, 30, 55, 91, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: StarNumber
funcs.doc:
StarNumber returns the n-th star number (centered 12-gonal number)
6n(n−1) + 1: 1, 13, 37, 73, 121, … n must be at least 1.

funcs.name: Tetrahedral
funcs.doc:
Tetrahedral returns the n-th tetrahedral (triangular-pyramidal) number
n(n+1)(n+2)/6, the number of spheres in a tetrahedral stack of n layers:
0, 1, 4, 10, 20, 35, 56, … n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Tetranacci
funcs.doc:
Tetranacci returns the n-th Tetranacci number, defined by initial values
0, 0, 0, 1 and the four-term sum recurrence: 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 4, 8, 15, 29,
56, … n must be non-negative. The result is exact for n ≤ 88.

funcs.name: Triangular
funcs.doc:
Triangular returns the n-th triangular number Tₙ = n(n+1)/2, the number of
dots in a triangular arrangement with n dots on a side: 0, 1, 3, 6, 10, 15,
… n must be non-negative.

funcs.name: Tribonacci
funcs.doc:
Tribonacci returns the n-th Tribonacci number Tₙ, defined by T₀ = 0, T₁ = 0,
T₂ = 1 and Tₙ = Tₙ₋₁ + Tₙ₋₂ + Tₙ₋₃: 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 4, 7, 13, 24, 44, …
n must be non-negative. The result is exact for n ≤ 91.

funcs.name: TribonacciSequence
funcs.doc:
TribonacciSequence returns the first n Tribonacci numbers T₀ … Tₙ₋₁. n must
be non-negative.

funcs.name: Zeckendorf
funcs.doc:
Zeckendorf returns the Zeckendorf representation of n: the unique set of
non-consecutive Fibonacci numbers (each ≥ 1, drawn from 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, …)
whose sum is n, returned in descending order. The representation of 0 is the
empty slice.

packages.name: settheory
packages.synopsis: Package settheory implements finite set theory, binary relations, order theory and lattices over integer and string domains, built entirely on the Go standard library.
packages.doc:
Package settheory implements finite set theory, binary relations, order
theory and lattices over integer and string domains, built entirely on the
Go standard library.

The package is self-contained and deterministic: identical inputs always
yield identical outputs, iteration-order sensitivity is eliminated by
returning sorted slices from every accessor, and no third-party dependency
is required.

The following families are covered:

  - finite sets: an IntSet and a StringSet backed by hash maps, providing
    membership, cardinality, union, intersection, difference, symmetric
    difference, subset/superset/disjointness tests, Cartesian product and
    power set enumeration;
  - binary relations on an integer domain: composition, inverse, the
    reflexive/symmetric/transitive property tests, and the reflexive,
    symmetric, transitive (via Warshall's algorithm) and equivalence
    closures;
  - partitions: equivalence classes induced by an equivalence relation, the
    relation induced by a partition, refinement testing, and the Bell and
    Stirling-of-the-second-kind counting numbers;
  - partial orders (posets): comparability, covering (Hasse) edges, a
    deterministic topological order (linear extension), minimal/maximal and
    least/greatest elements, upper and lower bounds, chain height;
  - lattices: pairwise meet (greatest lower bound) and join (least upper
    bound), lattice detection, and the divisibility lattice of a natural
    number.

All construction functions validate their inputs and report violations as
errors rather than panicking.

types.name: IntSet
types.signature: type IntSet map[int]struct{}
types.doc:
IntSet is a finite set of int values backed by a hash map. The zero value is
not usable; create sets with NewIntSet. A nil IntSet behaves as an empty set
for read-only operations but must not be mutated.

funcs.name: NewIntSet
funcs.doc:
NewIntSet returns a new IntSet containing the given elements. Duplicate
elements are collapsed, so NewIntSet(1, 1, 2) has cardinality two.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add inserts x into the set. Adding an element already present is a no-op.

methods.name: CartesianProduct
methods.doc:
CartesianProduct returns every ordered pair (a, b) with a in s and b in
other, sorted lexicographically. The result has len(s)*len(other) entries.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc:
Clone returns an independent copy of the set that shares no storage with the
receiver.

methods.name: Contains
methods.doc: Contains reports whether x is a member of the set.

methods.name: Difference
methods.doc:
Difference returns a new set containing every element that is in s but not in
other.

methods.name: Elements
methods.doc:
Elements returns the members of the set in ascending order. The result is a
freshly allocated slice; mutating it does not affect the set.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether s and other contain exactly the same elements.

methods.name: Intersection
methods.doc:
Intersection returns a new set containing every element that is in both s and
other.

methods.name: IsDisjoint
methods.doc: IsDisjoint reports whether s and other share no elements.

methods.name: IsEmpty
methods.doc: IsEmpty reports whether the set has no elements.

methods.name: IsProperSubsetOf
methods.doc:
IsProperSubsetOf reports whether s is a subset of other and the two sets are
not equal.

methods.name: IsSubsetOf
methods.doc:
IsSubsetOf reports whether every element of s is also an element of other.
The empty set is a subset of every set.

methods.name: IsSupersetOf
methods.doc: IsSupersetOf reports whether every element of other is also an element of s.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the cardinality of the set.

methods.name: Max
methods.doc:
Max returns the largest element of the set. The boolean result is false when
the set is empty.

methods.name: Min
methods.doc:
Min returns the smallest element of the set. The boolean result is false when
the set is empty.

methods.name: PowerSet
methods.doc:
PowerSet returns all 2^n subsets of the set, where n is its cardinality. The
subsets are ordered by the binary counter over the ascending element list, so
the empty set is first and the full set is last. PowerSet panics if the set
has more than 20 elements, since the result would exceed one million subsets.

methods.name: Remove
methods.doc: Remove deletes x from the set. Removing an absent element is a no-op.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String returns a canonical textual representation of the set such as
"{1, 2, 3}", with elements in ascending order. The empty set renders as
"{}".

methods.name: Sum
methods.doc: Sum returns the sum of all elements of the set; the empty set sums to zero.

methods.name: SymmetricDifference
methods.doc:
SymmetricDifference returns a new set containing every element that is in
exactly one of s and other.

methods.name: Union
methods.doc: Union returns a new set containing every element that is in s or in other.

types.name: Pair
types.doc:
Pair is an ordered pair of integers (From, To). It is used both as an element
of a binary Relation and as an edge in the Hasse diagram of a Poset.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the pair as "(from, to)".

types.name: Partition
types.signature: type Partition []IntSet
types.doc:
Partition is a partition of a finite set of integers into non-empty,
pairwise-disjoint blocks whose union is the whole set. Blocks are represented
as IntSets. Canonical partitions produced by this package list their blocks in
ascending order of least element.

funcs.name: EquivalenceClasses
funcs.signature: func EquivalenceClasses(rel Relation, set IntSet) Partition
funcs.doc:
EquivalenceClasses returns the partition of set into the equivalence classes
of the relation rel, which must be an equivalence relation on set. The blocks
are canonicalized in ascending order of least element. It panics if rel is not
an equivalence relation on set.

methods.name: Blocks
methods.doc:
Blocks returns the blocks of the partition sorted by ascending least element,
each block itself presented via IntSet.Elements when rendered. The returned
slice is freshly ordered but shares the underlying IntSet values.

methods.name: IsValidPartitionOf
methods.doc:
IsValidPartitionOf reports whether p is a valid partition of set: every block
is non-empty, blocks are pairwise disjoint, and their union equals set.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of blocks in the partition.

methods.name: Refines
methods.doc:
Refines reports whether p is a refinement of q, i.e. every block of p is
contained in some block of q. Both partitions are assumed to cover the same
universe.

methods.name: Universe
methods.doc: Universe returns the union of all blocks, i.e. the set being partitioned.

types.name: Poset
types.doc:
Poset is a finite partially ordered set: a set of integer elements together
with a reflexive, antisymmetric and transitive "less than or equal" relation.
The zero value is not usable; build posets with NewPoset, NewChainPoset or
NewDivisibilityPoset.

funcs.name: NewChainPoset
funcs.doc:
NewChainPoset returns the totally ordered Poset on the given elements ordered
by ascending integer value. Duplicate elements are collapsed.

funcs.name: NewDivisibilityPoset
funcs.doc:
NewDivisibilityPoset returns the divisibility Poset on the positive divisors
of n, where a ≤ b means a divides b. It returns an error for n < 1. This poset
is always a lattice, with meet given by greatest common divisor and join by
least common multiple.

funcs.name: NewPoset
funcs.signature: func NewPoset(set IntSet, leq Relation) (Poset, error)
funcs.doc:
NewPoset returns a Poset over set whose order is leq. It returns an error if
leq relates any value outside set, or if leq is not a partial order on set
(reflexive on set, antisymmetric and transitive).

methods.name: AreComparable
methods.signature: func (p Poset) AreComparable(a, b int) bool
methods.doc: AreComparable reports whether a ≤ b or b ≤ a.

methods.name: Covers
methods.signature: func (p Poset) Covers(lower, upper int) bool
methods.doc:
Covers reports whether upper covers lower: lower < upper and there is no
element c with lower < c < upper.

methods.name: Elements
methods.doc: Elements returns the elements of the poset in ascending order.

methods.name: GreatestElement
methods.doc:
GreatestElement returns the unique element that is ≥ every element of the
poset, if one exists. The boolean result reports existence.

methods.name: HasseEdges
methods.doc:
HasseEdges returns the covering pairs of the poset as Pairs (From = lower,
To = upper) with To covering From. Edges are sorted lexicographically. These
are exactly the edges of the poset's Hasse diagram.

methods.name: Height
methods.doc:
Height returns the number of elements in a longest chain of the poset. The
empty poset has height zero. The height of a poset equals the length of its
longest path in the covering (Hasse) digraph plus one.

methods.name: IsAntichain
methods.doc:
IsAntichain reports whether subset is an antichain: no two distinct elements
of subset are comparable.

methods.name: IsChain
methods.doc:
IsChain reports whether the poset is totally ordered: every two elements are
comparable.

methods.name: IsLattice
methods.doc:
IsLattice reports whether every pair of elements has both a meet and a join,
i.e. the poset is a lattice. The empty poset and any single-element poset are
trivially lattices.

methods.name: Join
methods.signature: func (p Poset) Join(a, b int) (int, bool)
methods.doc:
Join returns the least upper bound (supremum) of a and b, if it exists. The
boolean result reports existence; the join exists when the set of common
upper bounds has a unique least element.

methods.name: LeastElement
methods.doc:
LeastElement returns the unique element that is ≤ every element of the poset,
if one exists. The boolean result reports existence.

methods.name: Leq
methods.signature: func (p Poset) Leq(a, b int) bool
methods.doc: Leq reports whether a ≤ b in the poset order.

methods.name: Less
methods.signature: func (p Poset) Less(a, b int) bool
methods.doc: Less reports whether a < b, i.e. a ≤ b and a ≠ b.

methods.name: LowerBounds
methods.doc:
LowerBounds returns the elements that are ≤ every element of subset, in
ascending order.

methods.name: MaximalElements
methods.doc:
MaximalElements returns the elements that have no strictly larger element, in
ascending order.

methods.name: Meet
methods.signature: func (p Poset) Meet(a, b int) (int, bool)
methods.doc:
Meet returns the greatest lower bound (infimum) of a and b, if it exists. The
boolean result reports existence; the meet exists when the set of common
lower bounds has a unique greatest element.

methods.name: MinimalElements
methods.doc:
MinimalElements returns the elements that have no strictly smaller element,
in ascending order.

methods.name: Size
methods.doc: Size returns the number of elements in the poset.

methods.name: TopologicalOrder
methods.doc:
TopologicalOrder returns a linear extension of the poset: a permutation of the
elements in which a appears before b whenever a < b. The extension is made
deterministic by always selecting the smallest currently-minimal element.

methods.name: UpperBounds
methods.doc:
UpperBounds returns the elements that are ≥ every element of subset, in
ascending order.

types.name: Relation
types.signature: type Relation map[Pair]struct{}
types.doc:
Relation is a binary relation on the integers, represented as a set of
ordered pairs. The zero value is not usable; create relations with
NewRelation.

funcs.name: NewRelation
funcs.doc:
NewRelation returns a new Relation containing the given pairs. Duplicate pairs
are collapsed.

funcs.name: RelationFromPairs
funcs.doc:
RelationFromPairs builds a Relation from ordered pairs supplied as two-element
arrays [from, to]. It is a convenience for table-driven construction.

funcs.name: RelationFromPartition
funcs.doc:
RelationFromPartition returns the equivalence relation induced by p: two
elements are related exactly when they lie in the same block.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add inserts the ordered pair (a, b) into the relation.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the relation.

methods.name: Compose
methods.doc:
Compose returns the composition r∘other defined so that (a, c) is in the
result whenever (a, b) is in other and (b, c) is in r for some b. This is the
standard "apply other, then r" ordering.

methods.name: Domain
methods.doc: Domain returns the set of first coordinates appearing in the relation.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether r and other contain exactly the same pairs.

methods.name: EquivalenceClosureOn
methods.doc:
EquivalenceClosureOn returns the smallest equivalence relation over set that
contains r. It is the reflexive (on set), symmetric and transitive closure.

methods.name: Field
methods.doc:
Field returns the union of the domain and range, i.e. every value that
participates in the relation.

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse returns the converse relation, containing (b, a) for every pair
(a, b) in r.

methods.name: IsAntisymmetric
methods.doc:
IsAntisymmetric reports whether the presence of both (a, b) and (b, a)
implies a equals b.

methods.name: IsEquivalenceOn
methods.doc:
IsEquivalenceOn reports whether the relation is reflexive on set, symmetric
and transitive, i.e. an equivalence relation over set.

methods.name: IsIrreflexiveOn
methods.doc: IsIrreflexiveOn reports whether (x, x) is absent for every x in set.

methods.name: IsPartialOrderOn
methods.doc:
IsPartialOrderOn reports whether the relation is reflexive on set,
antisymmetric and transitive, i.e. a (non-strict) partial order over set.

methods.name: IsReflexiveOn
methods.doc: IsReflexiveOn reports whether (x, x) is in the relation for every x in set.

methods.name: IsSymmetric
methods.doc: IsSymmetric reports whether (b, a) is in the relation whenever (a, b) is.

methods.name: IsTransitive
methods.doc:
IsTransitive reports whether (a, c) is in the relation whenever both (a, b)
and (b, c) are.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of ordered pairs in the relation.

methods.name: Pairs
methods.doc:
Pairs returns the ordered pairs of the relation sorted lexicographically by
(From, To). The result is freshly allocated.

methods.name: Range
methods.doc: Range returns the set of second coordinates appearing in the relation.

methods.name: ReflexiveClosureOn
methods.doc:
ReflexiveClosureOn returns the smallest relation containing r that is
reflexive on set. It adds (x, x) for every x in set.

methods.name: Related
methods.signature: func (r Relation) Related(a, b int) bool
methods.doc: Related reports whether the ordered pair (a, b) is in the relation.

methods.name: Remove
methods.doc: Remove deletes the ordered pair (a, b) from the relation.

methods.name: SymmetricClosure
methods.doc:
SymmetricClosure returns the smallest symmetric relation containing r. It adds
(b, a) for every pair (a, b).

methods.name: TransitiveClosure
methods.doc:
TransitiveClosure returns the smallest transitive relation containing r,
computed with Warshall's algorithm over the field of the relation. The result
is the reachability relation of r viewed as a directed graph.

types.name: StringSet
types.signature: type StringSet map[string]struct{}
types.doc:
StringSet is a finite set of string values backed by a hash map. The zero
value is not usable; create sets with NewStringSet. A nil StringSet behaves
as an empty set for read-only operations but must not be mutated.

funcs.name: NewStringSet
funcs.doc:
NewStringSet returns a new StringSet containing the given elements. Duplicate
elements are collapsed.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add inserts x into the set. Adding an element already present is a no-op.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc:
Clone returns an independent copy of the set that shares no storage with the
receiver.

methods.name: Contains
methods.doc: Contains reports whether x is a member of the set.

methods.name: Difference
methods.doc:
Difference returns a new set containing every element that is in s but not in
other.

methods.name: Elements
methods.doc:
Elements returns the members of the set in ascending lexicographic order. The
result is a freshly allocated slice; mutating it does not affect the set.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether s and other contain exactly the same elements.

methods.name: Intersection
methods.doc:
Intersection returns a new set containing every element that is in both s and
other.

methods.name: IsDisjoint
methods.doc: IsDisjoint reports whether s and other share no elements.

methods.name: IsEmpty
methods.doc: IsEmpty reports whether the set has no elements.

methods.name: IsProperSubsetOf
methods.doc:
IsProperSubsetOf reports whether s is a subset of other and the two sets are
not equal.

methods.name: IsSubsetOf
methods.doc:
IsSubsetOf reports whether every element of s is also an element of other.
The empty set is a subset of every set.

methods.name: IsSupersetOf
methods.doc: IsSupersetOf reports whether every element of other is also an element of s.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the cardinality of the set.

methods.name: PowerSet
methods.doc:
PowerSet returns all 2^n subsets of the set, where n is its cardinality. The
subsets are ordered by the binary counter over the ascending element list.
PowerSet panics if the set has more than 20 elements.

methods.name: Remove
methods.doc: Remove deletes x from the set. Removing an absent element is a no-op.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String returns a canonical textual representation of the set such as
"{a, b, c}", with elements quoted and in ascending order. The empty set
renders as "{}".

methods.name: SymmetricDifference
methods.doc:
SymmetricDifference returns a new set containing every element that is in
exactly one of s and other.

methods.name: Union
methods.doc: Union returns a new set containing every element that is in s or in other.

funcs.name: BellNumber
funcs.doc:
BellNumber returns the nth Bell number, the number of partitions of an n-set,
computed exactly with the Bell triangle. It panics for negative n. Values are
exact for n up to about 25 before int overflow becomes a concern on 64-bit
platforms.

funcs.name: StirlingSecond
funcs.doc:
StirlingSecond returns the Stirling number of the second kind S(n, k), the
number of ways to partition an n-set into exactly k non-empty blocks. It
panics for negative arguments and returns zero when k exceeds n. The recurrence
S(n, k) = k*S(n-1, k) + S(n-1, k-1) is used.

packages.name: signal
packages.synopsis: Package signal is a small, dependency-free digital-signal-processing toolkit written entirely with the Go standard library (only the math package).
packages.doc:
