Package signal is a small, dependency-free digital-signal-processing
toolkit written entirely with the Go standard library (only the math
package). It operates on plain []float64 sample vectors and []complex128
spectra and is completely standalone: it does not import the parent algebra
package.

The package is organised into the following groups.

# Window functions

Fixed-length analysis windows: [Rectangular], [Hann], [Hamming],
[Blackman], [BlackmanHarris], [Nuttall], [FlatTop], [Bartlett], [Welch],
[Cosine], [Gaussian], [Kaiser] and [Tukey], together with the modified
Bessel function [BesselI0], the [KaiserBeta] design helper and the
element-wise [ApplyWindow].

# FIR filter design

Linear-phase windowed-sinc finite-impulse-response filters:
[FIRLowpass], [FIRHighpass], [FIRBandpass] and [FIRBandstop] (each using a
Hamming window by default) plus the explicit-window variants
[FIRLowpassWin], [FIRHighpassWin], [FIRBandpassWin] and [FIRBandstopWin].
Supporting routines are the normalized [Sinc], the reusable [FIRFilter]
state object, [ApplyFIR], [FIRFrequencyResponse] and [FIRGroupDelay].

# IIR (biquad) filter design

Second-order recursive sections following the Robert Bristow-Johnson audio
EQ cookbook: [BiquadLowpass], [BiquadHighpass], [BiquadBandpass],
[BiquadNotch], [BiquadAllpass], [BiquadPeaking], [BiquadLowShelf] and
[BiquadHighShelf]. Higher-order maximally-flat filters are built as cascades
of second-order sections by [ButterworthLowpass] and [ButterworthHighpass]
and applied with [FilterSOS]. The [Biquad] type carries the coefficients and
per-sample state.

# Convolution and correlation

[Convolve], [ConvolveSame], [ConvolveValid], [CrossCorrelate] and
[AutoCorrelate].

# Resampling

[Upsample], [Downsample], [Decimate], [Interpolate] and [ResampleLinear].

# Spectral analysis

The direct [DFT] and [IDFT], the spectral helpers [Magnitude], [Phase] and
[PowerSpectrum], the [Periodogram] and Welch-averaged [WelchPSD] power
spectral density estimators and the [FrequencyBins] helper.

# Time-domain analysis

[MovingAverage], [MovingAverageCentered], [ExponentialMovingAverage],
[CumulativeSum], [Diff], [RMS], [Energy] and [ZeroPad].

Frequencies are expressed throughout as fractions of the Nyquist rate:
a normalized cutoff of 1.0 corresponds to half the sampling rate. Functions
that take an explicit sampling rate fs interpret it in hertz.

types.name: Biquad
types.doc:
Biquad is a second-order recursive (IIR) filter section realising the
transfer function

	H(z) = (B0 + B1·z⁻¹ + B2·z⁻²) / (1 + A1·z⁻¹ + A2·z⁻²).

The leading denominator coefficient is normalized to 1. The Z1 and Z2 fields
hold the two state variables of a transposed direct-form II realisation and
are updated by [Biquad.Process]; call [Biquad.Reset] to clear them. The
design constructors in this package (for example [BiquadLowpass] and
[BiquadPeaking]) return fully-populated, ready-to-run sections.

funcs.name: BiquadAllpass
funcs.signature: func BiquadAllpass(f0, fs, q float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadAllpass returns a second-order all-pass [Biquad] centred on f0 hertz
with sampling rate fs hertz and quality factor q, following the RBJ
cookbook. Its magnitude response is unity at every frequency while its phase
varies, which is useful for phase equalisation and fractional delay.

funcs.name: BiquadBandpass
funcs.signature: func BiquadBandpass(f0, fs, q float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadBandpass returns a second-order band-pass [Biquad] centred on f0 hertz
with sampling rate fs hertz and quality factor q, normalized to unit
(0 dB) peak gain at the centre frequency, following the RBJ cookbook. Larger
q gives a narrower pass band.

funcs.name: BiquadHighShelf
funcs.signature: func BiquadHighShelf(f0, fs, q, gainDB float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadHighShelf returns a second-order high-shelf [Biquad] with shelf
midpoint f0 hertz, sampling rate fs hertz, quality factor q and shelf gain
gainDB decibels, following the RBJ cookbook. Frequencies above f0 are scaled
by the shelf gain while low frequencies pass at unity.

funcs.name: BiquadHighpass
funcs.signature: func BiquadHighpass(f0, fs, q float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadHighpass returns a second-order high-pass [Biquad] with corner
frequency f0 hertz, sampling rate fs hertz and quality factor q, following
the RBJ cookbook. The gain at Nyquist is unity and the gain at DC is zero.

funcs.name: BiquadLowShelf
funcs.signature: func BiquadLowShelf(f0, fs, q, gainDB float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadLowShelf returns a second-order low-shelf [Biquad] with shelf midpoint
f0 hertz, sampling rate fs hertz, quality factor q and shelf gain gainDB
decibels, following the RBJ cookbook. Frequencies below f0 are scaled by the
shelf gain while high frequencies pass at unity.

funcs.name: BiquadLowpass
funcs.signature: func BiquadLowpass(f0, fs, q float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadLowpass returns a second-order low-pass [Biquad] with corner frequency
f0 hertz, sampling rate fs hertz and quality factor q, following the RBJ
audio-EQ cookbook. A q of 1/√2 ≈ 0.7071 gives a maximally-flat (Butterworth)
response. The DC gain is unity.

funcs.name: BiquadNotch
funcs.signature: func BiquadNotch(f0, fs, q float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadNotch returns a second-order band-reject (notch) [Biquad] centred on
f0 hertz with sampling rate fs hertz and quality factor q, following the RBJ
cookbook. The gain is zero at f0 and unity far from it; larger q gives a
narrower notch.

funcs.name: BiquadPeaking
funcs.signature: func BiquadPeaking(f0, fs, q, gainDB float64) Biquad
funcs.doc:
BiquadPeaking returns a second-order peaking-EQ [Biquad] centred on f0 hertz
with sampling rate fs hertz, quality factor q and a boost or cut of gainDB
decibels (positive boosts, negative cuts), following the RBJ cookbook. The
gain far from f0 is unity.

funcs.name: ButterworthHighpass
funcs.signature: func ButterworthHighpass(order int, f0, fs float64) []Biquad
funcs.doc:
ButterworthHighpass designs an order-order Butterworth high-pass filter with
corner frequency f0 hertz and sampling rate fs hertz, returned as a cascade
of second-order [Biquad] sections (plus one first-order section when order is
odd). Apply the cascade with [FilterSOS]. It panics if order < 1. The
response is maximally flat and 3 dB down at f0.

funcs.name: ButterworthLowpass
funcs.signature: func ButterworthLowpass(order int, f0, fs float64) []Biquad
funcs.doc:
ButterworthLowpass designs an order-order Butterworth low-pass filter with
corner frequency f0 hertz and sampling rate fs hertz, returned as a cascade
of second-order [Biquad] sections (plus one first-order section when order is
odd). Apply the cascade with [FilterSOS]. It panics if order < 1. The
response is maximally flat in the pass band and is 3 dB down at f0.

methods.name: FrequencyResponse
methods.doc:
FrequencyResponse evaluates the section's complex frequency response H(ω) at
each normalized frequency in freqs, where 1.0 corresponds to Nyquist
(ω = π rad/sample). The returned slice has the same length as freqs and does
not depend on the state variables.

methods.name: Process
methods.doc:
Process filters one input sample x through the section and returns the output
sample, advancing the internal state. It implements the transposed
direct-form II recurrence, which is numerically well behaved for
fixed-frequency sections.

methods.name: ProcessBlock
methods.doc:
ProcessBlock filters an entire block x through the section, returning a new
slice of the same length, with state carried across calls. The input is not
modified.

methods.name: Reset
methods.doc:
Reset clears the section's internal state variables so that subsequent
filtering starts from rest.

types.name: FIRFilter
types.doc:
FIRFilter is a reusable streaming finite-impulse-response filter. It holds a
copy of the tap coefficients and an internal delay line so that a long
signal can be processed block by block with correct state carried across
calls. The zero value is not usable; construct one with [NewFIRFilter].

funcs.name: NewFIRFilter
funcs.doc:
NewFIRFilter returns a streaming [FIRFilter] initialized with a copy of taps
and a zeroed delay line. The supplied slice is not retained.

methods.name: Process
methods.doc:
Process filters an entire block x through the streaming filter, returning a
new slice of the same length. State is carried across calls, so feeding the
concatenation of two blocks yields the same result as feeding them one after
another. The input is not modified.

methods.name: ProcessSample
methods.doc:
ProcessSample feeds a single input sample through the filter and returns the
corresponding output sample, updating the internal delay line. Successive
calls implement the causal convolution y[k] = Σ h[n]·x[k-n].

methods.name: Reset
methods.doc:
Reset clears the filter's delay line, returning it to the state of a freshly
constructed filter without reallocating.

funcs.name: ApplyFIR
funcs.doc:
ApplyFIR filters the signal x through the FIR filter defined by taps and
returns the full linear convolution, of length len(x)+len(taps)-1. Because
the filter is linear-phase, the useful output is delayed by (len(taps)-1)/2
samples relative to the input. The inputs are not modified.

funcs.name: ApplyWindow
funcs.doc:
ApplyWindow returns the element-wise product of the signal x and the window
w. The two slices must have the same length; if they do not, ApplyWindow
panics. The input x is not modified.

funcs.name: AutoCorrelate
funcs.doc:
AutoCorrelate returns the full autocorrelation of a, equal to
CrossCorrelate(a, a). The result has length 2·len(a)-1 and is symmetric about
its centre element, which holds the signal energy Σ a[n]². The input is not
modified.

funcs.name: Bartlett
funcs.doc:
Bartlett returns the length-n Bartlett (triangular) window with zero-valued
endpoints, w[k] = 1 - |(k - (n-1)/2)/((n-1)/2)|. It is the simplest tapered
window and equals the normalized autoconvolution of two rectangular
windows. For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned; for n == 1 the sample is 1.

funcs.name: BesselI0
funcs.doc:
BesselI0 returns the modified Bessel function of the first kind of order
zero, I0(x), evaluated by its always-convergent power series
Σ ((x/2)^(2m)/(m!)²). It underlies the Kaiser window and is accurate to
full double precision for the arguments encountered in filter design.

funcs.name: Blackman
funcs.doc:
Blackman returns the length-n Blackman window,
w[k] = 0.42 - 0.5·cos(2π·k/(n-1)) + 0.08·cos(4π·k/(n-1)). The three-term
design pushes the peak side lobe down to about −58 dB, trading a wider main
lobe for far lower spectral leakage. For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned.

funcs.name: BlackmanHarris
funcs.doc:
BlackmanHarris returns the length-n four-term Blackman-Harris window using
the coefficients 0.35875, 0.48829, 0.14128 and 0.01168. It reaches a peak
side lobe near −92 dB, one of the lowest of any fixed window, and is well
suited to detecting weak tones near strong ones. For n <= 0 an empty slice
is returned.

funcs.name: Convolve
funcs.doc:
Convolve returns the full discrete linear convolution of a and b,
(a∗b)[k] = Σ a[i]·b[k-i], as a new slice of length len(a)+len(b)-1. If
either input is empty the result is empty. The inputs are not modified.

funcs.name: ConvolveSame
funcs.doc:
ConvolveSame returns the central part of the full convolution of a and b
that is the same length as a. It is the full [Convolve] result trimmed by
(len(b)-1)/2 samples at the front, matching the "same" mode of common
numerical libraries. The inputs are not modified.

funcs.name: ConvolveValid
funcs.doc:
ConvolveValid returns only those elements of the convolution of a and b that
are computed without any zero-padding of the shorter sequence, i.e. where
the two sequences overlap completely. The result has length
max(len(a),len(b)) - min(len(a),len(b)) + 1, or is empty if either input is
empty. The inputs are not modified.

funcs.name: Cosine
funcs.doc:
Cosine returns the length-n cosine (sine) window,
w[k] = sin(π·k/(n-1)). It is a single half-cycle of a sine and provides a
gentle taper with a −23 dB peak side lobe. For n <= 0 an empty slice is
returned; for n == 1 the sample is 1.

funcs.name: CrossCorrelate
funcs.doc:
CrossCorrelate returns the full cross-correlation of a and b as a slice of
length len(a)+len(b)-1. Element index i corresponds to lag ℓ = i-(len(b)-1),
so out[len(b)-1] is the zero-lag correlation Σ a[n]·b[n]. Positive lags shift
b to the right relative to a. The inputs are not modified.

funcs.name: CumulativeSum
funcs.doc:
CumulativeSum returns the running (prefix) sum of x, where out[i] is the sum
of x[0…i]. The result has the same length as x. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: DFT
funcs.doc:
DFT returns the discrete Fourier transform of the real signal x, computed
directly as X[k] = Σ x[n]·exp(-j2πkn/N) for k = 0…N-1. The result has the
same length as x. The algorithm is O(N²) and intended for correctness and
modest sizes rather than speed. An empty input yields an empty result.

funcs.name: Decimate
funcs.doc:
Decimate reduces the sampling rate of x by an integer factor after applying
a linear-phase FIR low-pass anti-aliasing filter with cutoff 1/factor of
Nyquist, then compensating for the filter's group delay so the output stays
time-aligned with the input. The result has length ceil(len(x)/factor). It
panics if factor < 1. For factor == 1 the input is returned unchanged.

funcs.name: Diff
funcs.doc:
Diff returns the first-order forward difference of x,
out[i] = x[i+1] - x[i]. The result has length len(x)-1, or is empty when x
has fewer than two elements. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: Downsample
funcs.doc:
Downsample decreases the sampling rate of x by an integer factor by keeping
every factor-th sample starting from index 0 (decimation without
filtering). The result has length ceil(len(x)/factor). No anti-aliasing
filter is applied; use [Decimate] to suppress aliasing first. It panics if
factor < 1.

funcs.name: Energy
funcs.doc:
Energy returns the total energy of x, Σ x[i]². For an empty input it returns
0.

funcs.name: ExponentialMovingAverage
funcs.doc:
ExponentialMovingAverage returns the exponentially-weighted moving average of
x with smoothing factor alpha in [0, 1]: out[0] = x[0] and
out[i] = alpha·x[i] + (1-alpha)·out[i-1]. Larger alpha weights recent samples
more heavily and reacts faster. It panics if alpha is outside [0, 1]. An
empty input yields an empty result.

funcs.name: FIRBandpass
funcs.signature: func FIRBandpass(numtaps int, low, high float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FIRBandpass designs a length-numtaps linear-phase band-pass FIR filter
passing normalized frequencies between low and high using a Hamming window.
See [FIRBandpassWin].

funcs.name: FIRBandpassWin
funcs.signature: func FIRBandpassWin(numtaps int, low, high float64, w []float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FIRBandpassWin designs a length-numtaps linear-phase band-pass FIR filter
that passes normalized frequencies between low and high (both in fractions
of Nyquist, 0 < low < high < 1) using the supplied window w. It is formed as
the difference of two unit-DC-gain low-pass prototypes and therefore has
zero gain at DC and at Nyquist. It panics on a length mismatch or a
non-positive numtaps.

funcs.name: FIRBandstop
funcs.signature: func FIRBandstop(numtaps int, low, high float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FIRBandstop designs a length-numtaps linear-phase band-stop FIR filter
rejecting normalized frequencies between low and high using a Hamming
window. numtaps must be odd; see [FIRBandstopWin].

funcs.name: FIRBandstopWin
funcs.signature: func FIRBandstopWin(numtaps int, low, high float64, w []float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FIRBandstopWin designs a length-numtaps linear-phase band-stop (notch) FIR
filter that rejects normalized frequencies between low and high using the
supplied window w. It is the spectral complement of the corresponding
band-pass design and requires an odd numtaps so that a single centre tap
exists; an even numtaps panics, as does a window length mismatch. The taps
have unit gain at DC and at Nyquist.

funcs.name: FIRFrequencyResponse
funcs.doc:
FIRFrequencyResponse evaluates the complex frequency response H(ω) of the
FIR filter taps at each normalized frequency in freqs, where a value of 1.0
corresponds to Nyquist (ω = π rad/sample). H(ω) = Σ h[n]·exp(-jωn). The
returned slice has the same length as freqs.

funcs.name: FIRGroupDelay
funcs.doc:
FIRGroupDelay returns the group delay, in samples, of the FIR filter taps at
each normalized frequency in freqs (1.0 corresponds to Nyquist). It is
computed as τ(ω) = Re(Σ n·h[n]·e^{-jωn} / Σ h[n]·e^{-jωn}). For an exactly
linear-phase filter the result is the constant (len(taps)-1)/2 wherever the
response does not vanish. Where the response magnitude is negligible the
group delay is reported as 0.

funcs.name: FIRHighpass
funcs.doc:
FIRHighpass designs a length-numtaps linear-phase high-pass FIR filter with
the given normalized cutoff using a Hamming window. numtaps must be odd; see
[FIRHighpassWin].

funcs.name: FIRHighpassWin
funcs.signature: func FIRHighpassWin(numtaps int, cutoff float64, w []float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FIRHighpassWin designs a length-numtaps linear-phase high-pass FIR filter
with the given normalized cutoff using the supplied window w. The design
uses spectral inversion of a low-pass prototype and therefore requires an
odd numtaps so that a single centre tap exists; an even numtaps panics, as
does a window whose length differs from numtaps. The taps have unit gain at
Nyquist and zero gain at DC.

funcs.name: FIRLowpass
funcs.doc:
FIRLowpass designs a length-numtaps linear-phase low-pass FIR filter with
the given normalized cutoff (in fractions of Nyquist) using a Hamming
window. It is the windowed convenience wrapper over [FIRLowpassWin].

funcs.name: FIRLowpassWin
funcs.signature: func FIRLowpassWin(numtaps int, cutoff float64, w []float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
FIRLowpassWin designs a length-numtaps linear-phase low-pass FIR filter with
the given normalized cutoff (in fractions of Nyquist, 0 < cutoff < 1) using
the supplied window w, whose length must equal numtaps. The returned taps
are scaled to unit gain at DC. It panics on a length mismatch or a
non-positive numtaps.

funcs.name: FilterSOS
funcs.doc:
FilterSOS filters the signal x through a cascade of second-order sections
sos and returns a new slice of the same length. Each section is applied in
turn using a fresh copy so that the caller's sections and their state are
left unmodified. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: FlatTop
funcs.doc:
FlatTop returns the length-n flat-top window using the SR785 five-term
coefficients. Its main lobe is deliberately broadened and flattened so that
the amplitude of a tone is recovered accurately regardless of where it falls
between DFT bins, which makes it the standard choice for calibrated
amplitude measurements. For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned.

funcs.name: FrequencyBins
funcs.doc:
FrequencyBins returns the n bin-centre frequencies of an n-point DFT for a
signal sampled at fs hertz, i.e. k·fs/n for k = 0…n-1. Bins above n/2
correspond to negative frequencies fs·(k-n)/n if reinterpreted as a two-sided
spectrum. An n <= 0 yields an empty slice.

funcs.name: Gaussian
funcs.doc:
Gaussian returns the length-n Gaussian window with normalized standard
deviation sigma (0 < sigma <= 0.5 is typical),
w[k] = exp(-½·((k - (n-1)/2)/(sigma·(n-1)/2))²). Smaller sigma yields a
narrower window and greater tapering. For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned;
for n == 1 the sample is 1.

funcs.name: Hamming
funcs.doc:
Hamming returns the length-n Hamming window,
w[k] = 0.54 - 0.46·cos(2π·k/(n-1)). Its coefficients are optimized to
cancel the nearest side lobe, giving a −43 dB peak side lobe at the cost of
non-zero endpoints. For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned.

funcs.name: Hann
funcs.doc:
Hann returns the length-n Hann (raised-cosine) window,
w[k] = 0.5·(1 - cos(2π·k/(n-1))). It has zero-valued endpoints, a −31 dB
peak side lobe and a side-lobe roll-off of 18 dB/octave, making it a good
general-purpose choice for spectral analysis. For n <= 0 an empty slice is
returned; for n == 1 the single sample is 1.

funcs.name: IDFT
funcs.doc:
IDFT returns the inverse discrete Fourier transform of the spectrum X,
x[n] = (1/N)·Σ X[k]·exp(+j2πkn/N), as a complex slice of the same length. For
a spectrum produced by [DFT] of a real signal the imaginary parts of the
result are zero to within rounding. An empty input yields an empty result.

funcs.name: Interpolate
funcs.doc:
Interpolate increases the sampling rate of x by an integer factor,
zero-stuffing and then applying a linear-phase FIR low-pass anti-imaging
filter of cutoff 1/factor scaled by factor to preserve amplitude. The result
has length len(x)·factor and is time-aligned with the input. It panics if
factor < 1; for factor == 1 the input is returned unchanged.

funcs.name: Kaiser
funcs.doc:
Kaiser returns the length-n Kaiser window with shape parameter beta,
w[k] = I0(beta·√(1 - (2k/(n-1) - 1)²)) / I0(beta), where I0 is [BesselI0].
Larger beta trades a wider main lobe for lower side lobes; beta = 0 reduces
to the rectangular window. For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned; for n == 1
the sample is 1.

funcs.name: KaiserBeta
funcs.doc:
KaiserBeta returns the Kaiser shape parameter beta that achieves a stop-band
attenuation of atten decibels, using Kaiser's empirical formula. For
atten > 50 it returns 0.1102·(atten-8.7); for 21 <= atten <= 50 it returns
0.5842·(atten-21)^0.4 + 0.07886·(atten-21); and for atten < 21 it returns 0.

funcs.name: Magnitude
funcs.doc:
Magnitude returns the element-wise magnitude |X[k]| of a complex spectrum X.
The result has the same length as X.

funcs.name: MovingAverage
funcs.doc:
MovingAverage returns the causal (trailing) simple moving average of x with
window length w: out[i] is the mean of x[max(0,i-w+1) … i]. At the start of
the signal, where fewer than w samples are available, the average is taken
over the samples seen so far. The result has the same length as x. It panics
if w < 1.

funcs.name: MovingAverageCentered
funcs.doc:
MovingAverageCentered returns the centered simple moving average of x with
window length w. Each output is the mean of the w samples centred on the
current index, with the window truncated at the signal boundaries so the
output length matches the input. For an even w the window is biased one
sample towards the past. It panics if w < 1.

funcs.name: Nuttall
funcs.doc:
Nuttall returns the length-n four-term Nuttall window using the coefficients
0.355768, 0.487396, 0.144232 and 0.012604. It has a continuous first
derivative at the endpoints and a peak side lobe near −93 dB. For n <= 0 an
empty slice is returned.

funcs.name: Periodogram
funcs.signature: func Periodogram(x []float64, window []float64, fs float64) (freqs, psd []float64)
funcs.doc:
Periodogram estimates the one-sided power spectral density of the real signal
x sampled at fs hertz. If window is non-nil it is applied element-wise to x
first and must have the same length; if it is nil a rectangular window is
used. It returns the bin frequencies (length len(x)/2+1, from 0 to fs/2) and
the matching PSD values, normalized by the window power so that the estimate
is a density in units of power per hertz. An empty x yields two empty slices.

funcs.name: Phase
funcs.doc:
Phase returns the element-wise phase angle arg(X[k]), in radians in the range
(-π, π], of a complex spectrum X. The result has the same length as X.

funcs.name: PowerSpectrum
funcs.doc:
PowerSpectrum returns the element-wise squared magnitude |X[k]|² of a complex
spectrum X. The result has the same length as X.

funcs.name: RMS
funcs.doc:
RMS returns the root-mean-square value of x, √(Σ x[i]²/N). It is a measure of
the effective amplitude or power of the signal. For an empty input it returns
0.

funcs.name: Rectangular
funcs.doc:
Rectangular returns the length-n rectangular (boxcar) window, whose samples
are all 1. It applies no tapering and is equivalent to not windowing at all.
For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned.

funcs.name: ResampleLinear
funcs.doc:
ResampleLinear resamples x to exactly numOut samples using linear
interpolation over the original sample positions. It is a lightweight,
arbitrary-ratio resampler suitable when a fractional rate change is needed
and full band-limited interpolation is not required. It returns an empty
slice when numOut <= 0 or x is empty, and a length-numOut constant slice when
x has a single sample. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: Sinc
funcs.doc:
Sinc returns the normalized cardinal sine, sinc(x) = sin(πx)/(πx), with the
removable singularity sinc(0) = 1 handled exactly. It is the ideal
continuous-time impulse response of a brick-wall low-pass filter and the
building block of every windowed-sinc design in this package.

funcs.name: Tukey
funcs.doc:
Tukey returns the length-n Tukey (tapered-cosine) window with taper fraction
alpha in [0, 1]. A fraction alpha of the window is a cosine taper split
between the two ends and the remaining centre is flat. alpha = 0 gives the
rectangular window and alpha = 1 gives the Hann window. Values of alpha
outside [0, 1] are clamped. For n <= 0 an empty slice is returned; for
n == 1 the sample is 1.

funcs.name: Upsample
funcs.doc:
Upsample increases the sampling rate of x by an integer factor by inserting
factor-1 zeros between each input sample (zero stuffing). The result has
length len(x)·factor, or is the input unchanged when factor <= 1. No
anti-imaging filter is applied; follow with a low-pass filter or use
[Interpolate] for a smoothed result. It panics if factor < 1.

funcs.name: Welch
funcs.doc:
Welch returns the length-n Welch (parabolic) window,
w[k] = 1 - ((k - (n-1)/2)/((n-1)/2))². Its samples trace a downward
parabola that vanishes at the endpoints. For n <= 0 an empty slice is
returned; for n == 1 the sample is 1.

funcs.name: WelchPSD
funcs.signature: func WelchPSD(x []float64, segLen, overlap int, fs float64) (freqs, psd []float64)
funcs.doc:
WelchPSD estimates the one-sided power spectral density of x sampled at fs
hertz using Welch's method: x is split into overlapping segments of length
segLen with the given overlap (number of shared samples, 0 <= overlap <
segLen), each segment is windowed with a Hann window, its periodogram is
computed, and the periodograms are averaged. Averaging reduces the variance
of the estimate at the cost of frequency resolution. It returns the bin
frequencies (length segLen/2+1) and the averaged PSD. It panics if segLen < 1
or overlap is out of range; if x is shorter than one segment a single
zero-padded segment is used.

funcs.name: ZeroPad
funcs.doc:
ZeroPad returns a copy of x extended with trailing zeros to total length n.
If n <= len(x) the leading n samples of x are returned unchanged (a copy),
so the result always has length max(0, n). The input is not modified.

packages.name: simplicial
packages.synopsis: Package simplicial implements abstract simplicial complexes and the core machinery of computational topology over the Go standard library alone.
packages.doc:
Package simplicial implements abstract simplicial complexes and the core
machinery of computational topology over the Go standard library alone.

The fundamental combinatorial object is a [Simplex]: a finite, non-empty set
of integer vertices, stored in ascending order. A k-simplex has k+1 vertices
and dimension k. Simplices are glued together into an abstract
[Complex], a downward-closed family of simplices — whenever a simplex
belongs to the complex so does every one of its faces. From a complex the
package builds the chain complex of boundary maps

	… → C_{k+1} → C_k → C_{k-1} → … → C_0 → 0

in three flavours of coefficients: the two-element field GF(2) ([GF2Matrix]),
the rationals Q ([RatMatrix]) and the integers Z ([IntMatrix]). Over a field
the ranks of consecutive boundary maps give the Betti numbers

	b_k = dim ker ∂_k − rank ∂_{k+1},

and their alternating sum reproduces the Euler characteristic. Over Z the
Smith normal form ([IntMatrix.SmithNormalForm]) additionally exposes the
torsion of the homology groups: the elementary divisors of ∂_{k+1} greater
than one are exactly the torsion coefficients of H_k. All of this is wrapped
by [Complex.BettiNumbers], [Complex.HomologyZ] and friends.

The package also turns geometry into topology. A [PointCloud] in Euclidean
space induces the [VietorisRips] and [Cech] complexes at a chosen scale,
the latter using an exact minimal-enclosing-ball test ([MinimalEnclosingBall]).
Letting the scale grow produces a [Filtration], whose evolving homology is
summarised by persistent homology: the standard matrix-reduction algorithm
([PersistentHomology]) returns [PersistencePair]s — a birth/death time for
every topological feature — from which barcodes and persistence diagrams are
read off.

A small zoo of named complexes ([SphereComplex], [TorusComplex],
[ProjectivePlaneComplex], [Cone], [Suspension], …) is
provided so that the homology routines can be exercised against spaces with
known invariants.

Everything is deterministic and depends only on the standard library:
integer and rational arithmetic use math/big, so ranks, invariant factors
and torsion coefficients are computed exactly with no floating-point error.

vars.signature: var ErrDimMismatch = errors.New("simplicial: point dimension mismatch")
vars.doc:
ErrDimMismatch is returned when two points, or a point and a metric, have
mismatched ambient dimensions.

vars.signature: var ErrDimension = errors.New("simplicial: invalid dimension")
vars.doc:
ErrDimension is returned when a dimension argument is negative or otherwise
outside the range meaningful for the operand.

vars.signature: var ErrEmptyCloud = errors.New("simplicial: empty point cloud")
vars.doc:
ErrEmptyCloud is returned when a point-cloud operation requires at least one
point but the cloud is empty.

vars.signature: var ErrEmptySimplex = errors.New("simplicial: empty simplex")
vars.doc:
ErrEmptySimplex is returned when an operation requires a non-empty simplex
but receives one with no vertices.

vars.signature: var ErrNotSquare = errors.New("simplicial: matrix is not square")
vars.doc:
ErrNotSquare is returned when a square matrix is required, for example by a
determinant, but a rectangular one is supplied.

vars.signature: var ErrShape = errors.New("simplicial: incompatible matrix shape")
vars.doc:
ErrShape is returned when two matrices have incompatible shapes for the
requested linear-algebra operation.

vars.signature: var ErrSingular = errors.New("simplicial: matrix is singular")
vars.doc:
ErrSingular is returned when an operation requires an invertible matrix but
the operand is singular.

types.name: Bar
types.doc: Bar is a half-open interval [Birth, Death) in a persistence barcode.

methods.name: IsInfinite
methods.doc: IsInfinite reports whether the bar extends to +Inf.

methods.name: Length
methods.doc: Length returns the length of the bar (+Inf for an essential bar).

types.name: BoundaryTerm
types.doc:
BoundaryTerm is one oriented summand of a simplex's boundary: a face together
with the sign (+1 or −1) with which it appears.

types.name: Complex
types.doc:
Complex is an abstract simplicial complex: a finite, downward-closed family
of [Simplex] values. Downward-closed means that whenever a simplex belongs to
the complex, so does every one of its faces; the mutating constructors below
preserve this invariant automatically.

funcs.name: BoundarySphere
funcs.doc:
BoundarySphere returns the boundary of the standard (n+1)-simplex: the
n-skeleton of an (n+1)-simplex, a triangulation of the n-sphere S^n on n+2
vertices. For n ≥ 1 it has the homology of S^n.

funcs.name: Cech
funcs.signature: func Cech(pc *PointCloud, r float64, maxDim int) *Complex
funcs.doc:
Cech builds the Čech complex of a point cloud at radius r up to dimension
maxDim. A set of points spans a simplex exactly when the closed balls of
radius r around them share a common point — equivalently when the minimal
enclosing ball of the points has radius at most r. The Čech complex is a
subcomplex of the Vietoris–Rips complex at scale 2r and, unlike it, is
homotopy equivalent to the union of the balls (the nerve lemma).

funcs.name: CompleteGraphComplex
funcs.doc:
CompleteGraphComplex returns the 1-skeleton of the full simplex on n vertices:
the complete graph K_n with all n(n−1)/2 edges but no higher faces.

funcs.name: Cone
funcs.doc:
Cone returns the cone on c with a new apex vertex: every simplex σ of c is
joined to apex, together with the apex itself. The cone is always
contractible. The apex must differ from every vertex of c.

funcs.name: CycleComplex
funcs.doc:
CycleComplex returns the cycle graph C_n: n vertices 0..n−1 joined in a ring
by n edges. For n ≥ 3 it is a triangulation of the circle S^1.

funcs.name: DiscreteComplex
funcs.doc:
DiscreteComplex returns n isolated vertices 0..n−1 with no edges; its degree-0
homology has rank n and all higher homology vanishes.

funcs.name: DisjointUnion
funcs.doc:
DisjointUnion returns the disjoint union of c and d. The vertices of d are
relabelled by adding the given offset so that the two vertex sets do not
collide; choose an offset larger than any vertex of c.

funcs.name: FromSimplices
funcs.doc:
FromSimplices builds the smallest simplicial complex containing all of the
given simplices, i.e. their union together with every face.

funcs.name: NeighborhoodGraph
funcs.doc:
NeighborhoodGraph returns the 1-skeleton (graph) of the Vietoris–Rips complex
of a point cloud at scale epsilon: vertices for every point and an edge
between points at Euclidean distance at most epsilon.

funcs.name: NewComplex
funcs.doc: NewComplex returns a new, empty simplicial complex.

funcs.name: PathComplex
funcs.doc:
PathComplex returns the path graph on n vertices 0..n−1 with n−1 edges. It is
contractible for n ≥ 1.

funcs.name: PointComplex
funcs.doc:
PointComplex returns the complex consisting of a single vertex {0}: a
contractible space with the homology of a point.

funcs.name: ProjectivePlaneComplex
funcs.doc:
ProjectivePlaneComplex returns the minimal 6-vertex triangulation of the real
projective plane RP^2 (the hemi-icosahedron) on vertices 0..5, with 15 edges
and 10 triangles (Euler characteristic 1). Over Z its homology is H_0 = Z,
H_1 = Z/2, H_2 = 0; over GF(2) the Betti numbers are (1,1,1). It is the
smallest complex in the package with non-trivial torsion.

funcs.name: SphereComplex
funcs.doc:
SphereComplex returns a triangulation of the n-sphere S^n as the boundary of
the standard (n+1)-simplex. SphereComplex(0) is two points; SphereComplex(1)
is a triangle boundary (a circle); SphereComplex(2) is a hollow tetrahedron.

funcs.name: StandardSimplex
funcs.doc:
StandardSimplex returns the full n-simplex on vertices 0..n together with all
of its faces. It is contractible for every n ≥ 0.

funcs.name: Suspension
funcs.doc:
Suspension returns the suspension of c: two cones on c glued along c, using
the two new apex vertices north and south. The suspension of S^n is S^{n+1}.
The apex vertices must differ from each other and from every vertex of c.

funcs.name: TorusComplex
funcs.doc:
TorusComplex returns Möbius's minimal 7-vertex triangulation of the torus
T^2, generated on vertices 0..6 (indices taken mod 7) by the triangles
{i,i+1,i+3} and {i,i+2,i+3}. It has 7 vertices, 21 edges and 14 triangles
(Euler characteristic 0) and its homology is H_0 = Z, H_1 = Z^2, H_2 = Z.

funcs.name: VietorisRips
funcs.signature: func VietorisRips(pc *PointCloud, epsilon float64, maxDim int) *Complex
funcs.doc:
VietorisRips builds the Vietoris–Rips complex of a point cloud at scale
epsilon under the Euclidean metric, up to dimension maxDim.

funcs.name: VietorisRipsFromDistances
funcs.signature: func VietorisRipsFromDistances(dist [][]float64, epsilon float64, maxDim int) *Complex
funcs.doc:
VietorisRipsFromDistances builds the Vietoris–Rips complex at scale epsilon
directly from a symmetric distance matrix, up to dimension maxDim. A set of
vertices spans a simplex exactly when all of its pairwise distances are at
most epsilon (the clique complex of the neighbourhood graph). Every vertex is
always included.

funcs.name: VietorisRipsMetric
funcs.signature: func VietorisRipsMetric(pc *PointCloud, epsilon float64, maxDim int, metric Metric) *Complex
funcs.doc:
VietorisRipsMetric builds the Vietoris–Rips complex of a point cloud at scale
epsilon under an arbitrary metric, up to dimension maxDim.

methods.name: AddEdge
methods.doc: AddEdge inserts the 1-simplex {a,b} and its two endpoints.

methods.name: AddSimplex
methods.doc:
AddSimplex inserts the simplex and every one of its faces into the complex,
preserving downward closure. Adding a simplex already present is a no-op.

methods.name: AddSimplices
methods.doc: AddSimplices inserts several simplices and their faces.

methods.name: AddTriangle
methods.doc: AddTriangle inserts the 2-simplex {a,b,c} and all of its faces.

methods.name: AddVertex
methods.doc: AddVertex inserts the 0-simplex {v}.

methods.name: BettiNumber
methods.doc:
BettiNumber returns the k-th Betti number b_k of the complex, the rank of the
k-th homology group with rational coefficients. It equals dim ker ∂_k −
rank ∂_{k+1} = (n_k − rank ∂_k) − rank ∂_{k+1}, where n_k is the number of
k-simplices. Negative k yields 0.

methods.name: BettiNumberGF2
methods.doc:
BettiNumberGF2 returns the k-th Betti number computed with GF(2) coefficients.
It can differ from the rational Betti number in the presence of 2-torsion —
for example the real projective plane has GF(2) Betti numbers (1,1,1) but
rational Betti numbers (1,0,0).

methods.name: BettiNumbers
methods.doc:
BettiNumbers returns the rational Betti numbers b_0,…,b_d of the complex,
where d is its dimension. The empty complex yields an empty slice.

methods.name: BettiNumbersGF2
methods.doc: BettiNumbersGF2 returns the GF(2) Betti numbers b_0,…,b_d of the complex.

methods.name: BoundaryMatrixGF2
methods.doc:
BoundaryMatrixGF2 returns the matrix of the boundary map ∂_k : C_k → C_{k-1}
over GF(2). Columns are indexed by the k-simplices and rows by the
(k−1)-simplices, both in [CompareSimplices] order. For k ≤ 0 the map is the
zero map into the trivial group and a matrix with zero rows is returned.

methods.name: BoundaryMatrixQ
methods.doc:
BoundaryMatrixQ returns the boundary map ∂_k over the rationals, identical in
content to [Complex.BoundaryMatrixZ] but with rational entries.

methods.name: BoundaryMatrixZ
methods.doc:
BoundaryMatrixZ returns the matrix of the boundary map ∂_k : C_k → C_{k-1}
over the integers, with the standard orientation signs. Columns are indexed
by k-simplices and rows by (k−1)-simplices in [CompareSimplices] order. For
k ≤ 0 a matrix with zero rows is returned.

methods.name: BoundaryRankGF2
methods.doc: BoundaryRankGF2 returns the rank over GF(2) of the boundary map ∂_k.

methods.name: BoundaryRankQ
methods.doc: BoundaryRankQ returns the rank over Q of the boundary map ∂_k.

methods.name: ChainRank
methods.doc:
ChainRank returns the dimension of the chain group C_k, i.e. the number of
k-simplices in the complex.

methods.name: ClosedStar
methods.doc:
ClosedStar returns the closure of the star of s as a subcomplex: the smallest
subcomplex containing every coface of s.

methods.name: Cofaces
methods.doc:
Cofaces returns every simplex of the complex that has s as a face, s itself
included when present.

methods.name: ComponentOf
methods.doc:
ComponentOf returns the sorted vertex list of the connected component
containing vertex v, or nil if v is not a vertex of the complex.

methods.name: ConnectedComponents
methods.doc:
ConnectedComponents partitions the vertices into connected components and
returns, for each component, its sorted vertex list. Components are ordered
by their smallest vertex.

methods.name: Copy
methods.doc: Copy returns an independent copy of the complex.

methods.name: Dimension
methods.doc:
Dimension returns the largest dimension of any simplex in the complex, or −1
for the empty complex.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether c and d contain exactly the same simplices.

methods.name: EulerCharacteristic
methods.doc:
EulerCharacteristic returns the alternating sum Σ_k (−1)^k f_k of the
f-vector, the Euler characteristic of the complex.

methods.name: EulerCharacteristicFromBetti
methods.doc:
EulerCharacteristicFromBetti returns the Euler characteristic computed as the
alternating sum of the Betti numbers, Σ_k (−1)^k b_k. By the Euler–Poincaré
theorem it equals [Complex.EulerCharacteristic].

methods.name: FVector
methods.doc:
FVector returns the f-vector of the complex: entry k is the number of
k-simplices, for k from 0 up to the dimension of the complex. The empty
complex yields an empty slice.

methods.name: Has
methods.doc: Has reports whether the given simplex belongs to the complex.

methods.name: HasVertex
methods.doc: HasVertex reports whether the 0-simplex {v} belongs to the complex.

methods.name: Homology
methods.doc: Homology returns the integral homology groups H_0,…,H_d of the complex.

methods.name: HomologyRank
methods.doc: HomologyRank is an alias for [Complex.BettiNumber]: the free rank of H_k.

methods.name: HomologyZ
methods.doc:
HomologyZ returns the k-th integral homology group H_k(c; Z) as a
[HomologyGroup], combining the Betti number (free rank) with the torsion
coefficients from the Smith normal form of ∂_{k+1}.

methods.name: IsConnected
methods.doc:
IsConnected reports whether the complex has at most one connected component.
The empty complex is considered connected.

methods.name: IsCycleGF2
methods.doc:
IsCycleGF2 reports whether the given GF(2) chain — a 0/1 coefficient vector
indexed like [Complex.SimplicesOfDim](k) — is a cycle, i.e. lies in the
kernel of ∂_k.

methods.name: IsValid
methods.doc:
IsValid reports whether the complex is genuinely downward-closed. The
mutating methods maintain this, so it should always be true; it exists for
testing and for complexes assembled by hand.

methods.name: Link
methods.doc:
Link returns the link of s: the subcomplex of faces τ that are disjoint from
s yet whose join with s lies in the complex. The link of a vertex in a
surface triangulation is a cycle.

methods.name: MaximalSimplices
methods.doc:
MaximalSimplices returns the maximal simplices of the complex — those that
are not a proper face of any other simplex. A complex is determined by its
maximal simplices.

methods.name: NumConnectedComponents
methods.doc:
NumConnectedComponents returns the number of connected components of the
1-skeleton of the complex — equivalently the rank b_0 of degree-0 homology.
The empty complex has zero components.

methods.name: NumSimplices
methods.doc: NumSimplices returns the total number of simplices of every dimension.

methods.name: NumSimplicesOfDim
methods.doc: NumSimplicesOfDim returns the number of k-simplices in the complex.

methods.name: NumVertices
methods.doc: NumVertices returns the number of 0-simplices (vertices) of the complex.

methods.name: RemoveSimplex
methods.doc:
RemoveSimplex deletes s and every coface of s from the complex, keeping it
downward-closed.

methods.name: Simplices
methods.doc: Simplices returns all simplices of the complex sorted by [CompareSimplices].

methods.name: SimplicesOfDim
methods.doc:
SimplicesOfDim returns all k-simplices of the complex sorted by
[CompareSimplices].

methods.name: Skeleton
methods.doc:
Skeleton returns the k-skeleton of the complex: a new complex containing
exactly the simplices of dimension at most k. A negative k yields the empty
complex.

methods.name: Star
methods.doc:
Star returns the open star of s: all simplices of the complex having s as a
face. It is an alias for [Complex.Cofaces].

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String returns a compact multi-line description listing the simplices by
dimension.

methods.name: TorsionCoefficients
methods.doc:
TorsionCoefficients returns the torsion coefficients of the k-th integral
homology group H_k(c; Z): the elementary divisors greater than one of the
boundary map ∂_{k+1}, obtained from its Smith normal form. An empty result
means H_k is free. For example the real projective plane returns [2] for
k = 1.

methods.name: Union
methods.doc: Union returns the simplicial complex whose simplices are those of c or d.

methods.name: Vertices
methods.doc: Vertices returns the sorted list of vertices appearing in the complex.

types.name: FilteredSimplex
types.doc:
FilteredSimplex is a simplex together with the scalar value at which it enters
a filtration.

types.name: Filtration
types.doc:
Filtration is a simplicial complex whose simplices are equipped with
non-decreasing entrance values: every face of a simplex must enter no later
than the simplex itself. Growing the value threshold sweeps out a nested
family of subcomplexes whose evolving homology is captured by persistent
homology.

funcs.name: NewFiltration
funcs.doc: NewFiltration returns an empty filtration.

funcs.name: RipsFiltration
funcs.signature: func RipsFiltration(pc *PointCloud, maxEps float64, maxDim int) *Filtration
funcs.doc:
RipsFiltration builds the Vietoris–Rips filtration of a point cloud up to the
scale maxEps and dimension maxDim. Every simplex enters at its diameter — the
largest pairwise distance among its vertices — with vertices entering at 0.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add inserts a single simplex at the given value. It does not automatically
insert faces; use [Filtration.AddClosure] to keep the filtration valid.
Re-adding a simplex keeps the smaller of the two values.

methods.name: AddClosure
methods.doc:
AddClosure inserts s and every one of its faces at the given value (faces take
the smaller of their existing and the new value), preserving validity.

methods.name: ComplexAt
methods.doc: ComplexAt returns the subcomplex of all simplices with value at most t.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of simplices in the filtration.

methods.name: Simplices
methods.doc:
Simplices returns the filtered simplices in filtration order: ascending by
value, then by dimension, then by [CompareSimplices]. This order lists every
face before the simplices it bounds.

methods.name: Value
methods.doc: Value returns the filtration value of the simplex and whether it is present.

types.name: GF2Matrix
types.doc:
GF2Matrix is a dense matrix over the two-element field GF(2) = {0,1}, with
arithmetic performed modulo 2. Entries are stored as bytes that are always 0
or 1.

funcs.name: GF2Identity
funcs.doc: GF2Identity returns the n×n identity matrix over GF(2).

funcs.name: NewGF2Matrix
funcs.doc:
NewGF2Matrix returns a rows×cols zero matrix over GF(2). It panics if either
dimension is negative.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns the entrywise sum (XOR) m+n over GF(2). The matrices must have the
same shape; otherwise nil and [ErrShape] are returned.

methods.name: AddRow
methods.doc: AddRow adds (XORs) row src into row dst in place.

methods.name: At
methods.doc: At returns the entry in row i, column j as 0 or 1.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the matrix.

methods.name: Col
methods.doc: Col returns a copy of column j as a slice of 0/1 ints.

methods.name: Cols
methods.doc: Cols returns the number of columns.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether m and n have the same shape and entries.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether every entry is zero.

methods.name: KernelBasis
methods.doc:
KernelBasis returns a basis of the null space {x : m·x = 0} over GF(2). Each
basis vector is returned as a length-cols slice of 0/1 ints; the number of
vectors equals the nullity.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the matrix product m·n over GF(2). The inner dimensions must
agree; otherwise nil and [ErrShape] are returned.

methods.name: Nullity
methods.doc:
Nullity returns the dimension of the kernel of the matrix acting on column
vectors, namely cols − rank.

methods.name: Rank
methods.doc: Rank returns the rank of the matrix over GF(2).

methods.name: Row
methods.doc: Row returns a copy of row i as a slice of 0/1 ints.

methods.name: RowEchelon
methods.doc: RowEchelon returns the reduced row-echelon form of the matrix over GF(2).

methods.name: Rows
methods.doc: Rows returns the number of rows.

methods.name: Set
methods.doc: Set stores v (reduced modulo 2) in row i, column j.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns a multi-line 0/1 rendering of the matrix.

methods.name: SwapRows
methods.doc: SwapRows exchanges rows i and j in place.

methods.name: Transpose
methods.doc: Transpose returns the transpose of the matrix.

types.name: HomologyGroup
types.doc:
HomologyGroup describes a finitely generated abelian group H_k = Z^r ⊕
(Z/t_1 ⊕ … ⊕ Z/t_m) by its free rank r and its torsion coefficients
t_1 | t_2 | … | t_m, each greater than one.

methods.name: Betti
methods.doc: Betti returns the free rank of the group.

methods.name: IsFree
methods.doc: IsFree reports whether the group is torsion-free.

methods.name: IsTrivial
methods.doc: IsTrivial reports whether the group is the zero group.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the group in the usual notation, e.g. "Z^2 + Z/2" or "0".

types.name: IntMatrix
types.doc:
IntMatrix is a dense matrix over the integers Z, with entries stored exactly
as *big.Int. It is the setting for the Smith normal form, from which the
torsion of homology groups is read.

funcs.name: IntIdentity
funcs.doc: IntIdentity returns the n×n identity matrix over Z.

funcs.name: IntMatrixFromInt64
funcs.doc:
IntMatrixFromInt64 builds an r×c integer matrix from a row-major slice of
int64 values. It panics if len(vals) != r*c.

funcs.name: NewIntMatrix
funcs.doc: NewIntMatrix returns a rows×cols zero matrix over Z.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns m+n. The shapes must match; otherwise nil and [ErrShape].

methods.name: At
methods.doc: At returns a copy of the entry in row i, column j.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the matrix.

methods.name: Cols
methods.doc: Cols returns the number of columns.

methods.name: Determinant
methods.doc:
Determinant returns the determinant of a square integer matrix. It returns
[ErrNotSquare] for a rectangular matrix.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether m and n have the same shape and entries.

methods.name: InvariantFactors
methods.doc: InvariantFactors returns the non-zero invariant factors of the matrix.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether every entry is zero.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the product m·n. The inner dimensions must agree; otherwise nil
and [ErrShape].

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns −m.

methods.name: Rank
methods.doc:
Rank returns the rank of the integer matrix, computed over Q (equivalently the
number of non-zero invariant factors of its Smith normal form).

methods.name: Rows
methods.doc: Rows returns the number of rows.

methods.name: Set
methods.doc: Set stores a copy of v in row i, column j.

methods.name: SetInt
methods.signature: func (m *IntMatrix) SetInt(i, j int, v int64)
methods.doc: SetInt stores the integer v in row i, column j.

methods.name: SmithNormalForm
methods.doc:
SmithNormalForm computes the Smith normal form of the matrix and returns U, D
and V with U·A·V = D. The original matrix is not modified.

methods.name: SmithNormalFormDiagonal
methods.doc:
SmithNormalFormDiagonal returns just the diagonal (invariant factors) of the
Smith normal form.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns a multi-line rendering of the matrix.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns m−n. The shapes must match; otherwise nil and [ErrShape].

methods.name: ToRat
methods.doc: ToRat returns the matrix reinterpreted over the rationals.

methods.name: Transpose
methods.doc: Transpose returns the transpose of the matrix.

types.name: Metric
types.signature: type Metric func(a, b []float64) float64
types.doc:
Metric is a distance function between two points of equal length. The Rips and
Čech constructors accept a Metric so that complexes can be built from any
dissimilarity, not only the Euclidean one.

types.name: Persistence
types.doc:
Persistence holds the result of a persistent-homology computation: the list of
birth/death pairs across all dimensions.

funcs.name: PersistentHomology
funcs.doc:
PersistentHomology runs the standard matrix-reduction algorithm over GF(2) on
the given filtration and returns the resulting persistence pairs.

methods.name: Barcode
methods.doc: Barcode returns the persistence barcode in dimension k: one [Bar] per feature.

methods.name: BettiAt
methods.signature: func (p *Persistence) BettiAt(k int, t float64) int
methods.doc:
BettiAt returns the k-th Betti number of the subcomplex at threshold t,
counting the features of dimension k that are alive at t (born at or before t
and dying strictly after t).

methods.name: Diagram
methods.doc:
Diagram returns the persistence diagram in dimension k as a slice of
[birth, death] points; essential classes have death +Inf.

methods.name: EssentialClasses
methods.doc:
EssentialClasses returns the pairs that never die (infinite persistence),
whose count in dimension k is the k-th Betti number of the final complex.

methods.name: NumPairs
methods.doc: NumPairs returns the total number of persistence pairs.

methods.name: Pairs
methods.doc: Pairs returns all persistence pairs across every dimension.

methods.name: PairsOfDim
methods.doc: PairsOfDim returns the persistence pairs of homological dimension k.

types.name: PersistencePair
types.doc:
PersistencePair records the birth and death of a single homology class of a
given dimension during a filtration. A death of +Inf marks an essential class
that never dies.

methods.name: IsEssential
methods.doc: IsEssential reports whether the class never dies (infinite persistence).

methods.name: Persistence
methods.doc:
Persistence returns Death − Birth, the lifetime of the feature (+Inf for
essential classes).

types.name: PointCloud
types.doc:
PointCloud is a finite set of points in Euclidean space, all of the same
ambient dimension. It is the geometric input to the Vietoris–Rips and Čech
constructions.

funcs.name: CirclePoints
funcs.doc:
CirclePoints returns n points equally spaced on the circle of the given radius
centred at the origin in the plane.

funcs.name: GridPoints
funcs.doc:
GridPoints returns a point cloud of the integer lattice points of an
n-dimensional box: for dims = [a,b,c] it produces a·b·c points at all integer
coordinates 0..a−1 × 0..b−1 × 0..c−1.

funcs.name: NewPointCloud
funcs.doc:
NewPointCloud returns a point cloud from the given points. Each point is
copied. The points should all have the same length; mixed lengths are allowed
but the metric routines will report NaN distances between mismatched points.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add appends a copy of point p to the cloud and returns its index.

methods.name: Centroid
methods.doc:
Centroid returns the coordinatewise mean of the points, or nil for an empty
cloud.

methods.name: Diameter
methods.doc:
Diameter returns the largest pairwise Euclidean distance in the cloud, or 0
for a cloud with fewer than two points.

methods.name: Dim
methods.doc:
Dim returns the ambient dimension (the length of the first point), or 0 for an
empty cloud.

methods.name: Distance
methods.doc: Distance returns the Euclidean distance between points i and j.

methods.name: DistanceMatrix
methods.doc: DistanceMatrix returns the symmetric matrix of pairwise Euclidean distances.

methods.name: DistanceMatrixWith
methods.doc:
DistanceMatrixWith returns the symmetric matrix of pairwise distances under
the given metric.

methods.name: DistanceWith
methods.signature: func (pc *PointCloud) DistanceWith(i, j int, metric Metric) float64
methods.doc:
DistanceWith returns the distance between points i and j under the given
metric.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of points in the cloud.

methods.name: Point
methods.doc: Point returns a copy of the i-th point.

types.name: RatMatrix
types.doc:
RatMatrix is a dense matrix over the field of rationals Q, with entries stored
exactly as *big.Rat. A nil entry is treated as zero on read but the
constructors always populate every cell.

funcs.name: NewRatMatrix
funcs.doc: NewRatMatrix returns a rows×cols zero matrix over Q.

funcs.name: RatIdentity
funcs.doc: RatIdentity returns the n×n identity matrix over Q.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns m+n. The shapes must match; otherwise nil and [ErrShape].

methods.name: At
methods.doc: At returns a copy of the entry in row i, column j.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the matrix.

methods.name: Cols
methods.doc: Cols returns the number of columns.

methods.name: Determinant
methods.doc:
Determinant returns the determinant of a square matrix over Q. It returns
[ErrNotSquare] for a rectangular matrix.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether m and n have the same shape and equal entries.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether every entry is zero.

methods.name: KernelBasis
methods.doc:
KernelBasis returns a basis of the null space {x : m·x = 0} over Q. Each basis
vector is a length-cols slice of *big.Rat.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the product m·n. The inner dimensions must agree; otherwise nil
and [ErrShape].

methods.name: Nullity
methods.doc: Nullity returns cols − rank, the dimension of the kernel over Q.

methods.name: Rank
methods.doc: Rank returns the rank of the matrix over Q.

methods.name: RowEchelon
methods.doc: RowEchelon returns the reduced row-echelon form of the matrix over Q.

methods.name: Rows
methods.doc: Rows returns the number of rows.

methods.name: Scale
methods.doc: Scale returns the matrix with every entry multiplied by s.

methods.name: Set
methods.doc: Set stores a copy of v in row i, column j.

methods.name: SetInt
methods.signature: func (m *RatMatrix) SetInt(i, j int, v int64)
methods.doc: SetInt stores the integer v in row i, column j.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns a multi-line rendering of the matrix.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns m−n. The shapes must match; otherwise nil and [ErrShape].

methods.name: Transpose
methods.doc: Transpose returns the transpose of the matrix.

types.name: Simplex
types.doc:
Simplex is a finite set of distinct integer vertices, stored in ascending
order. A simplex with k+1 vertices is a k-simplex and has dimension k; the
empty simplex has dimension −1. Simplices are value types: methods never
mutate the receiver, they return fresh simplices.

funcs.name: Edge
funcs.doc:
Edge returns the 1-simplex on vertices a and b. If a equals b the result is
the 0-simplex {a}.

funcs.name: EmptySimplex
funcs.doc: EmptySimplex returns the (−1)-dimensional simplex with no vertices.

funcs.name: NewSimplex
funcs.doc:
NewSimplex returns the simplex on the given vertices. Repeated vertices are
collapsed and the result is sorted ascending, so NewSimplex(2,0,2,1) and
NewSimplex(0,1,2) denote the same 2-simplex.

funcs.name: Tetrahedron
funcs.signature: func Tetrahedron(a, b, c, d int) Simplex
funcs.doc: Tetrahedron returns the 3-simplex on vertices a, b, c and d.

funcs.name: Triangle
funcs.signature: func Triangle(a, b, c int) Simplex
funcs.doc: Triangle returns the 2-simplex on vertices a, b and c.

funcs.name: Vertex
funcs.doc: Vertex returns the 0-simplex on the single vertex v.

methods.name: Boundary
methods.doc:
Boundary returns the oriented algebraic boundary ∂s of the simplex, the
alternating sum Σ_i (−1)^i [v_0,…,v̂_i,…,v_k] of its codimension-1 faces. A
0-simplex has empty boundary.

methods.name: Closure
methods.doc:
Closure returns all faces of the simplex, itself included: every non-empty
subset of its vertices. It is an alias for [Simplex.Subsets].

methods.name: Contains
methods.doc: Contains reports whether v is a vertex of the simplex.

methods.name: ContainsSimplex
methods.doc:
ContainsSimplex reports whether t is a (not necessarily proper) face of s,
that is, whether every vertex of t is a vertex of s.

methods.name: Copy
methods.doc: Copy returns an independent copy of the simplex.

methods.name: Dim
methods.doc:
Dim returns the dimension of the simplex, one less than its number of
vertices. The empty simplex has dimension −1.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether s and t have exactly the same vertex set.

methods.name: Face
methods.doc:
Face returns the codimension-1 face obtained by deleting the i-th vertex
(0-indexed, ascending order) of the simplex.

methods.name: Faces
methods.doc:
Faces returns the codimension-1 faces of the simplex: for a k-simplex the
k+1 sub-simplices obtained by omitting one vertex. A 0-simplex has no faces
(an empty slice is returned).

methods.name: Intersect
methods.doc:
Intersect returns the simplex on the common vertices of s and t. The result
may be the empty simplex.

methods.name: IsEmpty
methods.doc: IsEmpty reports whether the simplex has no vertices.

methods.name: IsFaceOf
methods.doc:
IsFaceOf reports whether s is a face of t (equivalent to
t.ContainsSimplex(s)).

methods.name: IsProperFaceOf
methods.doc: IsProperFaceOf reports whether s is a face of t of strictly lower dimension.

methods.name: Join
methods.doc: Join returns the simplex whose vertex set is the union of those of s and t.

methods.name: Key
methods.doc:
Key returns a canonical string key identifying the simplex, suitable as a map
key. Distinct simplices have distinct keys.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of vertices of the simplex.

methods.name: Max
methods.doc: Max returns the largest vertex, or 0 for the empty simplex.

methods.name: Min
methods.doc: Min returns the smallest vertex, or 0 for the empty simplex.

methods.name: ProperFaces
methods.doc:
ProperFaces returns every proper face of the simplex (all non-empty subsets
except s itself).

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns a human-readable representation such as "{0,1,2}".

methods.name: Subsets
methods.doc:
Subsets returns every non-empty subset of the vertices of s as a simplex,
including s itself. A k-simplex has 2^{k+1}−1 non-empty subsets.

methods.name: Vertex
methods.doc:
Vertex returns the i-th vertex (0-indexed) of the simplex in ascending
order. It panics if i is out of range, matching slice indexing.

methods.name: Vertices
methods.doc: Vertices returns a copy of the simplex's vertex list in ascending order.

types.name: SmithResult
types.doc:
SmithResult holds the Smith normal form decomposition U·A·V = D of an integer
matrix A, where U and V are unimodular (invertible over Z) and D is diagonal
with non-negative entries d_1 | d_2 | … | d_r each dividing the next.

methods.name: Diagonal
methods.doc:
Diagonal returns the diagonal entries of D, i.e. the invariant factors,
padded with nothing beyond the smaller dimension. Trailing zeros indicate the
rank deficiency.

methods.name: InvariantFactors
methods.doc:
InvariantFactors returns the non-zero diagonal entries of D, the invariant
factors of the matrix.

methods.name: Rank
methods.doc: Rank returns the number of non-zero diagonal entries of D.

methods.name: TorsionFactors
methods.doc:
TorsionFactors returns the invariant factors of D that are strictly greater
than one; these are the elementary divisors giving rise to torsion.

funcs.name: ChebyshevDistance
funcs.doc:
ChebyshevDistance returns the L∞ (maximum coordinate) distance. It returns NaN
if the lengths differ.

funcs.name: Circumsphere
funcs.signature: func Circumsphere(points [][]float64) (center []float64, radius float64, ok bool)
funcs.doc:
Circumsphere returns the centre and radius of the unique sphere passing
through all of the given (affinely independent) points, with the centre lying
in their affine hull. For a single point the radius is 0; for two points it is
the sphere with them as a diameter. It returns ok = false when the points are
affinely dependent so that no such sphere is determined.

funcs.name: CompareSimplices
funcs.doc:
CompareSimplices provides a total order on simplices: first by dimension,
then lexicographically by vertex list. It returns −1, 0 or +1.

funcs.name: EnclosingRadius
funcs.doc:
EnclosingRadius returns the radius of the minimal enclosing ball of the
points, a convenience wrapper around [MinimalEnclosingBall].

funcs.name: EuclideanDistance
funcs.doc:
EuclideanDistance returns the L2 distance between two equal-length points. It
returns NaN if the lengths differ.

funcs.name: ManhattanDistance
funcs.doc:
ManhattanDistance returns the L1 (taxicab) distance. It returns NaN if the
lengths differ.

funcs.name: MinimalEnclosingBall
funcs.doc:
MinimalEnclosingBall returns the centre and radius of the smallest ball
containing every one of the given points, computed exactly with Welzl's
algorithm. The empty input yields a zero radius and nil centre.

funcs.name: MinkowskiDistance
funcs.signature: func MinkowskiDistance(a, b []float64, p float64) float64
funcs.doc:
MinkowskiDistance returns the L_p distance for p ≥ 1. For p = 1 it is the
Manhattan distance, for p = 2 the Euclidean distance. It returns NaN if the
lengths differ.

funcs.name: SortSimplices
funcs.doc: SortSimplices sorts a slice of simplices in place by [CompareSimplices].

funcs.name: SquaredEuclideanDistance
funcs.doc:
SquaredEuclideanDistance returns the squared L2 distance, avoiding the square
root. It returns NaN if the lengths differ.

examples.name: VietorisRips
examples.code:
pc := CirclePoints(6, 1)
vr := VietorisRips(pc, 1.01, 2)
fmt.Println(vr.BettiNumbers())

examples.name: Complex (BettiNumbers)
examples.code:
t := TorusComplex()
fmt.Println(t.BettiNumbers())
fmt.Println("Euler:", t.EulerCharacteristic())

examples.name: Complex (HomologyZ)
examples.code:
rp2 := ProjectivePlaneComplex()
fmt.Println("H0 =", rp2.HomologyZ(0))
fmt.Println("H1 =", rp2.HomologyZ(1))
fmt.Println("H2 =", rp2.HomologyZ(2))

packages.name: socialchoice
packages.synopsis: Package socialchoice implements classical social-choice theory: ranked and cardinal voting rules, pairwise/tournament analysis, and seat apportionment.
packages.doc:
Package socialchoice implements classical social-choice theory: ranked and
cardinal voting rules, pairwise/tournament analysis, and seat apportionment.

The central ranked-ballot type is Profile, a weighted collection of Ballot
preference orders over a fixed set of candidates. Ballots may be truncated:
a candidate that a ballot does not list is treated as ranked below every
listed candidate and as tied with the other unlisted candidates. On top of
Profile the package offers:

  - positional rules: Plurality, AntiPlurality, Borda, Dowdall and the
    generic PositionalScores, plus the iterative Borda methods Nanson and
    Baldwin;
  - pairwise analysis: the PairwiseMatrix of majority preferences, Condorcet
    winners and losers, Copeland and MiniMax scores, and the Smith and
    Schwartz sets computed from the majority tournament;
  - Condorcet-consistent completions: the Schulze beatpath method and
    Tideman's ranked pairs;
  - elimination rules: instant-runoff voting, the single transferable vote
    with Droop-quota Gregory surplus transfers, Coombs, Bucklin, the
    contingent and supplementary votes, and the two-round runoff;
  - cardinal rules: approval, range (score), STAR, cumulative and
    majority-judgment voting.

The apportionment layer allocates a fixed number of seats to parties from
their vote totals: the highest-averages (divisor) methods D'Hondt/Jefferson,
Sainte-Laguë/Webster, Adams, Dean and Huntington-Hill, and the
largest-remainder (quota) methods Hamilton and Droop, together with quota-rule
and Alabama-paradox diagnostics.

All routines use only the Go standard library, are deterministic, resolve
ties toward the lowest candidate or party index unless documented otherwise,
and are validated in the accompanying tests against reference values such as
the standard Tennessee-capital voting example.

types.name: ApprovalProfile
types.doc:
ApprovalProfile is a set of approval ballots over Candidates candidates; each
ballot approves the candidates whose entry is true.

funcs.name: ApprovalFromRanked
funcs.doc:
ApprovalFromRanked derives an approval profile from a ranked profile by
approving each ballot's top k candidates. Ballot weights are expanded into
repeated approval ballots.

methods.name: NumBallots
methods.doc: NumBallots returns the number of approval ballots.

methods.name: Ranking
methods.doc: Ranking returns candidates ordered by descending approval.

methods.name: Scores
methods.doc:
Scores returns each candidate's approval tally, the number of ballots
approving it.

methods.name: TotalApprovals
methods.doc: TotalApprovals returns the total number of approvals cast across all ballots.

methods.name: Winner
methods.doc: Winner returns the most approved candidate.

types.name: Ballot
types.signature: type Ballot []int
types.doc:
Ballot is a strict preference order over candidate indices, most preferred
first. A ballot may be truncated: any candidate not listed is considered
ranked below every listed candidate and tied with the other omitted ones.

methods.name: Bottom
methods.doc:
Bottom returns the least preferred listed candidate and whether the ballot is
non-empty. Unlisted candidates, though notionally lower, are not returned.

methods.name: BottomAmong
methods.doc:
BottomAmong returns the least preferred listed candidate for which
continuing[c] is true, or -1 if none is listed.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns a copy of the ballot.

methods.name: Position
methods.doc:
Position returns the zero-based rank of candidate c on the ballot and whether
c appears at all. Rank 0 is the most preferred candidate.

methods.name: Prefers
methods.signature: func (b Ballot) Prefers(a, c int) bool
methods.doc:
Prefers reports whether the ballot strictly prefers candidate a to candidate
b. A listed candidate is preferred to any unlisted one; two unlisted
candidates yield no preference (false in both directions).

methods.name: Ranks
methods.doc: Ranks reports whether candidate c appears anywhere on the ballot.

methods.name: Reversed
methods.doc: Reversed returns a new ballot with the preference order inverted.

methods.name: Top
methods.doc:
Top returns the most preferred candidate on the ballot and whether the ballot
is non-empty.

methods.name: TopAmong
methods.doc:
TopAmong returns the most preferred candidate on the ballot for which
continuing[c] is true, or -1 if the ballot lists no continuing candidate.

types.name: CumulativeProfile
types.doc:
CumulativeProfile is a set of cumulative-voting ballots; Ballots[v][c] is the
number of points voter v allocates to candidate c.

methods.name: Scores
methods.doc: Scores returns the total points each candidate accumulates.

methods.name: Winner
methods.doc: Winner returns the candidate with the most cumulative points.

types.name: DivisorFunc
types.signature: type DivisorFunc func(seatsWon int) float64
types.doc:
DivisorFunc maps the number of seats a party currently holds to the divisor
applied to its votes when computing the next-seat priority in a highest-
averages apportionment. A divisor of zero denotes an infinite priority, giving
every party with positive votes a seat before any second seat is awarded.

types.name: JudgmentProfile

methods.name: MedianGrades
methods.doc: MedianGrades returns each candidate's lower-median grade.

methods.name: Winner
methods.doc:
Winner returns the majority-judgment winner: the highest median grade wins,
and ties are broken by repeatedly removing one median grade from each tied
candidate and recomparing medians. A residual tie resolves toward the lower
index.

types.name: MajorityPair
types.doc:
MajorityPair is a directed pairwise victory of Winner over Loser with the
given majority margin and winning-vote count, used by ranked pairs.

types.name: PairwiseMatrix
types.signature: type PairwiseMatrix [][]int
types.doc:
PairwiseMatrix holds pairwise majority tallies: entry [i][j] is the number of
voters who strictly prefer candidate i to candidate j.

methods.name: Beats
methods.signature: func (m PairwiseMatrix) Beats(i, j int) bool
methods.doc: Beats reports whether a strict majority prefers i to j.

methods.name: CondorcetLoser
methods.doc:
CondorcetLoser returns a candidate that loses to every other candidate
pairwise and true, or 0 and false when none exists.

methods.name: CondorcetWinner
methods.doc:
CondorcetWinner returns a candidate that beats every other candidate pairwise
and true, or 0 and false when none exists.

methods.name: CopelandNet
methods.doc: CopelandNet returns each candidate's net Copeland score, wins minus losses.

methods.name: CopelandScores
methods.doc:
CopelandScores returns each candidate's Copeland score, counting 1 for every
pairwise win and 0.5 for every pairwise tie.

methods.name: Covers
methods.signature: func (m PairwiseMatrix) Covers(i, j int) bool
methods.doc:
Covers reports whether candidate i covers candidate j: i beats j and beats
every candidate that j beats.

methods.name: HasCondorcetCycle
methods.doc:
HasCondorcetCycle reports whether the majority relation contains a cycle,
equivalently whether the Smith set has more than one candidate.

methods.name: HasTie
methods.doc: HasTie reports whether any pair of distinct candidates is pairwise tied.

methods.name: IsTournament
methods.doc:
IsTournament reports whether the majority relation is a strict tournament: no
pair of distinct candidates is tied.

methods.name: KemenyRanking
methods.doc:
KemenyRanking returns a Kemeny–Young optimal ranking, found by exhaustive
search over all orderings (suitable for a modest number of candidates). Ties
are broken toward the lexicographically smallest ranking.

methods.name: KemenyScore
methods.doc:
KemenyScore returns the Kemeny agreement score of a full ranking: the summed
pairwise support for every ordered pair placed in agreement with the ranking.
The Kemeny–Young optimal ranking maximises this score.

methods.name: Losses
methods.doc: Losses returns the number of candidates that beat i pairwise.

methods.name: Margin
methods.signature: func (m PairwiseMatrix) Margin(i, j int) int
methods.doc: Margin returns the signed majority margin of i over j, m[i][j]-m[j][i].

methods.name: MiniMaxScores
methods.doc:
MiniMaxScores returns, for each candidate, the largest margin by which it is
defeated in any pairwise contest (its worst pairwise loss); a value of zero or
below means the candidate is never beaten. This is the margins variant of the
Simpson–Kramer minimax rule.

methods.name: N
methods.doc: N returns the number of candidates covered by the matrix.

methods.name: NetMargins
methods.doc:
NetMargins returns the antisymmetric margin matrix, entry [i][j] = margin of i
over j.

methods.name: Prefer
methods.signature: func (m PairwiseMatrix) Prefer(i, j int) int
methods.doc: Prefer returns the number of voters preferring i to j.

methods.name: RankedPairsLocked
methods.doc:
RankedPairsLocked returns the acyclic set of locked pairwise victories under
Tideman's ranked-pairs method: majorities are considered strongest first and a
pair is locked in unless doing so would create a cycle with the pairs already
locked. locked[i][j] is true when the edge i→j is locked.

methods.name: RankedPairsRanking
methods.doc:
RankedPairsRanking returns the ranked-pairs ordering: a topological order of
the locked-victory graph, most preferred first. Ties are broken toward the
lower index.

methods.name: RankedPairsWinner
methods.doc:
RankedPairsWinner returns the source of the locked-victory graph, the
ranked-pairs winner.

methods.name: SchulzeRanking
methods.doc:
SchulzeRanking returns candidates ordered by descending number of Schulze
pairwise victories, breaking ties toward the lower index.

methods.name: SchulzeWinner
methods.doc:
SchulzeWinner returns a Schulze-method winner: a candidate whose strongest path
to every other candidate is at least as strong as the reverse. Ties are
resolved toward the lower index.

methods.name: SchulzeWins
methods.doc:
SchulzeWins returns each candidate's number of Schulze pairwise victories: the
count of opponents j for which the strongest path from i to j is stronger than
the strongest path from j to i.

methods.name: SchwartzSet
methods.doc:
SchwartzSet returns the Schwartz set: the union of the minimal undominated
sets under the strict beat relation (ties do not dominate). The result is
sorted ascending.

methods.name: SmithSet
methods.doc:
SmithSet returns the Smith set: the smallest non-empty set of candidates such
that every member beats every non-member pairwise. It is computed as the
maximal elements of the transitive closure of the "at least as good as"
relation (i does not lose to j). The result is sorted ascending.

methods.name: SortedMajorities
methods.doc:
SortedMajorities returns every pairwise victory in the matrix ordered by
descending margin; ties in margin are broken by descending winning votes and
then by the (Winner, Loser) index pair to make the order deterministic.

methods.name: StrongestPaths
methods.doc:
StrongestPaths returns the Schulze strongest-beatpath matrix p, where p[i][j]
is the strength of the strongest path from i to j. A direct link i→j has
strength m[i][j] when i beats j pairwise and 0 otherwise; a path's strength is
its weakest link, and the matrix maximises over all paths via Floyd–Warshall.

methods.name: TieCount
methods.doc: TieCount returns the number of candidates pairwise tied with i (excluding i).

methods.name: Ties
methods.signature: func (m PairwiseMatrix) Ties(i, j int) bool
methods.doc: Ties reports whether i and j are pairwise tied.

methods.name: TopCycle
methods.doc:
TopCycle returns the Smith set, also known as the top cycle of the majority
tournament.

methods.name: Transpose
methods.doc: Transpose returns the transpose of the pairwise matrix.

methods.name: UncoveredSet
methods.doc:
UncoveredSet returns the uncovered set: the candidates that no other candidate
covers. The result is sorted ascending.

methods.name: Wins
methods.doc: Wins returns the number of candidates that i beats pairwise.

types.name: Profile
types.doc:
Profile is a weighted collection of ranked ballots over a fixed number of
candidates identified by the indices 0..Candidates-1. Counts[i] is the number
of voters casting Ballots[i]; a nil or short Counts slice treats the missing
weights as 1.

funcs.name: NewProfile
funcs.signature: func NewProfile(candidates int, ballots []Ballot, counts []int) (*Profile, error)
funcs.doc:
NewProfile builds a Profile over the given number of candidates and validates
it. counts may be nil, in which case every ballot has weight 1.

methods.name: AddBallot
methods.doc:
AddBallot appends a ballot with the given weight and returns the updated
profile pointer for chaining. It does not re-validate; call Validate when
building profiles from untrusted input.

methods.name: AntiPluralityRanking
methods.doc:
AntiPluralityRanking returns candidates ordered by descending anti-plurality
score.

methods.name: AntiPluralityScores
methods.doc:
AntiPluralityScores returns each candidate's anti-plurality (veto) score: the
total weight of ballots that do not rank the candidate last. Each ballot
vetoes only its bottom listed candidate.

methods.name: AntiPluralityWinner
methods.doc: AntiPluralityWinner returns the candidate vetoed by the fewest ballots.

methods.name: BaldwinWinner
methods.doc:
BaldwinWinner returns the winner of the Baldwin rule: repeatedly eliminate the
single continuing candidate with the lowest Borda score until one remains
(ties resolved toward the lower index).

methods.name: BlackWinner
methods.doc:
