spectrum X, a complex series of the same length. For a spectrum produced by
[DFT] the imaginary parts are numerically negligible.

funcs.name: IsStationaryADF
funcs.signature: func IsStationaryADF(x []float64, lags int, level float64) bool
funcs.doc:
IsStationaryADF reports whether the augmented Dickey–Fuller test rejects the
unit-root null at the given significance level (typically 0.05), i.e. whether
the series appears stationary. It uses no deterministic trend.

funcs.name: Kurtosis
funcs.doc:
Kurtosis returns the excess kurtosis (fourth standardized moment minus 3) of
the series. It returns NaN if the series has fewer than two observations or
zero variance.

funcs.name: Lag
funcs.doc:
Lag shifts the series forward by k steps, padding the first k positions with
NaN. The result has the same length as x. A negative k delegates to [Lead].

funcs.name: LagMatrix
funcs.doc:
LagMatrix builds a design matrix of lagged values for autoregressive modeling.
Row i (for i from 0) contains [x[t−1], x[t−2], …, x[t−p]] where t = p+i, so the
matrix has len(x)−p rows and p columns. The aligned targets x[t] are returned
as the second result. It returns nil, nil if p < 1 or p ≥ len(x).

funcs.name: Last
funcs.doc: Last returns the final observation of the series, or NaN if empty.

funcs.name: Lead
funcs.doc:
Lead shifts the series backward by k steps, padding the last k positions with
NaN. The result has the same length as x. A negative k delegates to [Lag].

funcs.name: LevinsonDurbin
funcs.doc:
LevinsonDurbin solves the Yule–Walker equations for an AR(p) model from the
autocovariance sequence gamma (which must have length at least p+1). It
returns the p AR coefficients and the innovation variance.

funcs.name: LjungBox
funcs.doc:
LjungBox returns the Ljung–Box Q statistic for the first h autocorrelation
lags, a test for overall autocorrelation. Under the null of no
autocorrelation Q is approximately chi-squared with h degrees of freedom.

funcs.name: LogReturns
funcs.doc:
LogReturns returns the log returns of the series: out[i] = ln(x[i+1]/x[i]).
The result has length len(x)−1.

funcs.name: LogTransform
funcs.doc:
LogTransform returns the natural logarithm of each observation. Non-positive
inputs map to NaN.

funcs.name: Max
funcs.doc: Max returns the largest value in the series, or NaN if it is empty.

funcs.name: Mean
funcs.doc: Mean returns the arithmetic mean of the series, or NaN if it is empty.

funcs.name: MeanAbsoluteDeviation
funcs.doc:
MeanAbsoluteDeviation returns the mean of the absolute deviations from the
series mean.

funcs.name: MeanAbsoluteError
funcs.doc:
MeanAbsoluteError returns the mean of |actual − forecast| over the aligned
pairs. It returns NaN if the lengths differ or are zero.

funcs.name: MeanAbsolutePercentageError
funcs.doc:
MeanAbsolutePercentageError returns the mean of |(actual − forecast)/actual|
as a fraction (multiply by 100 for a percentage). Pairs with a zero actual
value are skipped. It returns NaN if the lengths differ or no valid pair
exists.

funcs.name: MeanAbsoluteScaledError
funcs.signature: func MeanAbsoluteScaledError(actual, forecast, train []float64, m int) float64
funcs.doc:
MeanAbsoluteScaledError returns the MASE of the forecast, the mean absolute
error scaled by the mean absolute error of a naive one-step (seasonal-period
m) forecast computed on the training series train. A value below 1 means the
forecast beats the naive benchmark. It returns NaN on length mismatch or an
undefined scale.

funcs.name: MeanError
funcs.doc:
MeanError returns the mean forecast error (bias) actual − forecast over the
aligned pairs. It returns NaN if the lengths differ or are zero.

funcs.name: MeanSquaredError
funcs.doc:
MeanSquaredError returns the mean of (actual − forecast)² over the aligned
pairs. It returns NaN if the lengths differ or are zero.

funcs.name: Median
funcs.doc:
Median returns the median of the series, or NaN if it is empty. The input is
not modified.

funcs.name: MedianAbsoluteError
funcs.doc:
MedianAbsoluteError returns the median of |actual − forecast| over the
aligned pairs, a robust accuracy measure.

funcs.name: MedianFilter
funcs.doc:
MedianFilter returns the centered running median with window length w,
truncating the window at the boundaries so the output length equals the input
length. It returns nil if w < 1.

funcs.name: Min
funcs.doc: Min returns the smallest value in the series, or NaN if it is empty.

funcs.name: MinMaxNormalize
funcs.doc:
MinMaxNormalize rescales the series linearly to the unit interval [0,1]. If
all values are equal the result is all zeros.

funcs.name: MovingAverage
funcs.doc:
MovingAverage returns the causal (trailing) simple moving average with window
length w: out[i] is the mean of the up-to-w most recent samples ending at i.
At the start, where fewer than w samples exist, the average uses the samples
seen so far. The result has the same length as x. It returns nil if w < 1.

funcs.name: MovingAverageCentered
funcs.doc:
MovingAverageCentered returns the centered simple moving average with window
length w, truncating the window at the boundaries so the output length equals
the input length. For even w the window is biased one sample toward the past.
It returns nil if w < 1.

funcs.name: MovingAverageValid
funcs.doc:
MovingAverageValid returns the centered simple moving average over full
windows only, so the result has length len(x)−w+1. It returns nil if w < 1 or
w > len(x).

funcs.name: NumberOfDifferences
funcs.signature: func NumberOfDifferences(x []float64, lags, maxD int, level float64) int
funcs.doc:
NumberOfDifferences estimates the order of differencing needed to make the
series stationary by repeatedly applying the ADF test (constant, given lags)
and differencing until stationarity is achieved or maxD is reached.

funcs.name: PartialAutoCorrelation
funcs.doc:
PartialAutoCorrelation returns the sample partial autocorrelation function
(PACF) for lags 0,1,…,maxlag computed with the Durbin–Levinson recursion.
Element 0 is 1 by convention and element k is the last coefficient of the
fitted AR(k) model.

funcs.name: Percentile
funcs.doc: Percentile returns the p-th percentile (0 ≤ p ≤ 100) of the series.

funcs.name: Periodogram
funcs.doc:
Periodogram returns the one-sided raw periodogram of the series, an estimate
of the power at each Fourier frequency. The returned slices freqs and power
have length ⌊n/2⌋+1; power[j] = |Σ x_t·e^{−2πi j t/n}|² / n. The mean is
removed before the transform.

funcs.name: PopStdDev
funcs.doc:
PopStdDev returns the population standard deviation (square root of
[PopVariance]).

funcs.name: PopVariance
funcs.doc:
PopVariance returns the population (divide-by-n) variance of the series. It
returns NaN for an empty series.

funcs.name: Quantile
funcs.doc:
Quantile returns the q-quantile (0 ≤ q ≤ 1) of the series using linear
interpolation between order statistics. It returns NaN for an empty series
or an out-of-range q. The input is not modified.

funcs.name: RSquared
funcs.doc:
RSquared returns the coefficient of determination R² = 1 − SS_res/SS_tot of
the forecast relative to the mean of the actual series. It returns NaN on
length mismatch or when the actual series has zero variance.

funcs.name: Range
funcs.doc: Range returns the difference between the maximum and minimum values.

funcs.name: ReflectionCoefficients
funcs.doc:
ReflectionCoefficients returns the partial autocorrelation (reflection /
PARCOR) coefficients k_1,…,k_p produced by the Levinson–Durbin recursion on
the series, one per order from 1 to p.

funcs.name: Rescale
funcs.doc: Rescale linearly maps the series to the interval [a,b].

funcs.name: Reverse
funcs.doc: Reverse returns a new series with the observations in reverse order.

funcs.name: RollingMax
funcs.doc:
RollingMax returns the trailing rolling maximum over full windows of length
w. It returns nil if w < 1 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: RollingMean
funcs.doc:
RollingMean returns the trailing rolling mean over full windows of length w,
producing len(x)−w+1 values. It returns nil if w < 1 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: RollingMedian
funcs.doc:
RollingMedian returns the trailing rolling median over full windows of length
w. It returns nil if w < 1 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: RollingMin
funcs.doc:
RollingMin returns the trailing rolling minimum over full windows of length
w. It returns nil if w < 1 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: RollingStdDev
funcs.doc:
RollingStdDev returns the trailing rolling sample standard deviation over
full windows of length w. It returns nil if w < 2 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: RollingSum
funcs.doc:
RollingSum returns the trailing rolling sum over full windows of length w. It
returns nil if w < 1 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: RollingVariance
funcs.doc:
RollingVariance returns the trailing rolling sample variance over full
windows of length w. It returns nil if w < 2 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: RootMeanSquare
funcs.doc: RootMeanSquare returns sqrt(mean(x²)), the quadratic mean of the series.

funcs.name: RootMeanSquaredError
funcs.doc: RootMeanSquaredError returns the square root of [MeanSquaredError].

funcs.name: SESForecast
funcs.signature: func SESForecast(x []float64, alpha float64, h int) []float64
funcs.doc:
SESForecast returns the h-step-ahead forecast of a simple exponential
smoothing model fitted to x with parameter alpha. Because the model has no
trend, every forecast equals the final smoothed level, so the returned slice
of length h is constant. It returns nil for an out-of-range alpha or empty x.

funcs.name: SeasonalDiff
funcs.doc:
SeasonalDiff returns the seasonal difference at lag s: out[i] = x[i+s] − x[i].
The result has length len(x)−s. It returns an empty slice if s < 1 or s ≥
len(x).

funcs.name: SeasonalIndices
funcs.signature: func SeasonalIndices(x []float64, period int, mult bool) []float64
funcs.doc:
SeasonalIndices returns the per-phase seasonal factors of the series for the
given period (length period): additive deviations that sum to zero when mult
is false, or multiplicative factors that average to one when mult is true. It
returns nil for an invalid period or too-short series.

funcs.name: SeasonalIntegrate
funcs.doc:
SeasonalIntegrate is the inverse of [SeasonalDiff] at lag s: given the
seasonal-difference series d and the s initial values seed (the first season
of the original series), it reconstructs the original series of length
len(d)+s. It returns nil if len(seed) != s.

funcs.name: SeasonallyAdjust
funcs.signature: func SeasonallyAdjust(x []float64, period int, mult bool) []float64
funcs.doc:
SeasonallyAdjust removes the estimated seasonal component from the series,
returning the seasonally adjusted series (trend plus residual for the
additive model, or the series divided by the seasonal factors for the
multiplicative model). It returns nil for an invalid period or too-short
series.

funcs.name: Shift
funcs.signature: func Shift(x []float64, k int, fill float64) []float64
funcs.doc:
Shift shifts the series forward by k steps, filling vacated positions with
fill. A negative k shifts backward. The result has the same length as x.

funcs.name: SimpleExponentialSmoothing
funcs.doc:
SimpleExponentialSmoothing returns the simple exponentially smoothed series
with smoothing parameter alpha in (0,1): s[0] = x[0] and
s[t] = alpha·x[t] + (1−alpha)·s[t−1]. It returns nil for an out-of-range
alpha. This is identical to [ExponentialMovingAverage].

funcs.name: SimpleReturns
funcs.doc:
SimpleReturns returns the simple (arithmetic) returns of the series:
out[i] = x[i+1]/x[i] − 1. The result has length len(x)−1.

funcs.name: Skewness
funcs.doc:
Skewness returns the sample skewness (third standardized moment) using the
population standard deviation in the denominator. It returns NaN if the
series has fewer than two observations or zero variance.

funcs.name: SlidingWindows
funcs.doc:
SlidingWindows returns the list of consecutive full windows of length w over
the series, each stepped by step positions. Each window is a fresh copy. It
returns nil if w < 1, step < 1 or w > len(x).

funcs.name: SpectralEntropy
funcs.doc:
SpectralEntropy returns the normalized spectral (Shannon) entropy of the
series computed from its periodogram, a value in [0,1] that is near 1 for
white noise and near 0 for a pure sinusoid. It returns NaN if the series is
too short.

funcs.name: SqrtTransform
funcs.doc:
SqrtTransform returns the square root of each observation. Negative inputs
map to NaN.

funcs.name: Standardize
funcs.doc:
Standardize returns the z-scored series (x − mean)/stddev using the sample
standard deviation. If the standard deviation is zero the result is all
zeros.

funcs.name: StdDev
funcs.doc: StdDev returns the sample standard deviation (square root of [Variance]).

funcs.name: Sum
funcs.doc: Sum returns the sum of all observations in the series.

funcs.name: SymmetricMAPE
funcs.doc:
SymmetricMAPE returns the symmetric mean absolute percentage error, the mean
of |actual − forecast| / ((|actual| + |forecast|)/2) as a fraction. Pairs
where both values are zero are skipped.

funcs.name: TakensThetaAutoMI
funcs.doc:
TakensThetaAutoMI returns the lag at which the sample autocorrelation of the
series first drops to or below 1/e, a common heuristic for choosing the delay
in a time-delay embedding. It searches lags 1..maxLag and returns 0 if the
threshold is never crossed.

funcs.name: TheilU
funcs.doc:
TheilU returns Theil's U2 forecast-accuracy statistic, the ratio of the RMSE
of the forecast to the RMSE of a naive no-change forecast. Values below 1
indicate the forecast improves on the naive benchmark. The actual and
forecast slices are aligned one-step-ahead predictions; the naive benchmark
uses the previous actual value, so both must have length ≥ 2. It returns NaN
on invalid input.

funcs.name: TimeDelayEmbedding
funcs.doc:
TimeDelayEmbedding is an alias for [Embed] with the conventional argument
order (dimension then delay), returning the delay-coordinate matrix.

funcs.name: ToeplitzMatrix
funcs.doc:
ToeplitzMatrix builds the symmetric Toeplitz matrix whose first row and
column are c: element (i,j) is c[|i−j|]. It returns nil for an empty c.

funcs.name: TrendComponent
funcs.doc:
TrendComponent returns the classical moving-average trend estimate of the
series for the given period, with NaN at boundary positions. It returns nil
if the period is invalid.

funcs.name: TrendLine
funcs.doc: TrendLine returns the fitted trend values a + b·i for the series.

funcs.name: TriangularMovingAverage
funcs.doc:
TriangularMovingAverage returns a trailing weighted moving average whose
weights increase linearly from 1 up to w (giving most weight to the most
recent sample), a smooth double-averaging filter. It returns nil if w < 1.

funcs.name: TripleExponentialMovingAverage
funcs.doc:
TripleExponentialMovingAverage returns the TEMA indicator,
3·EMA − 3·EMA² + EMA³, which further reduces lag. It returns nil for an
out-of-range alpha.

funcs.name: Variance
funcs.doc:
Variance returns the sample (unbiased, divide-by-n−1) variance of the
series. It returns NaN if fewer than two observations are present.

funcs.name: VarianceRatio
funcs.doc:
VarianceRatio returns the Lo–MacKinlay variance ratio of the series at
horizon q: the variance of q-period differences divided by q times the
variance of one-period differences. A value near 1 is consistent with a
random walk; values away from 1 indicate mean reversion (<1) or trending
(>1). It returns NaN for q < 2 or a too-short series.

funcs.name: WeightedMovingAverage
funcs.doc:
WeightedMovingAverage returns the causal weighted moving average whose window
length equals len(weights). out[i] is Σ weights[j]·x[i−len+1+j] divided by the
sum of the weights used; at the boundary the trailing weights are applied to
the available samples. It returns nil for empty weights.

examples.name: AutoCorrelation
HoltLinear

packages.name: transform
packages.synopsis: Package transform implements integral and discrete signal transforms in pure Go using only the standard library.
packages.doc:
Package transform implements integral and discrete signal transforms in
pure Go using only the standard library.

The package collects the transforms most often needed in numerical
analysis, digital-signal processing and applied mathematics and provides
correct, self-contained implementations of each.

The following families are covered:

  - Discrete Fourier transforms: the direct O(n^2) [DFT] and [IDFT], the
    radix-2 Cooley-Tukey [FFT] and [IFFT], the mixed-length [FFTAny] and
    [IFFTAny] (which fall back to Bluestein's algorithm for non-power-of-two
    lengths), the real-input transforms [RFFT] and [IRFFT], two-dimensional
    [FFT2D]/[IFFT2D], the reusable [FFTPlan], and the single-bin [Goertzel]
    algorithm;
  - spectral helpers: [FFTShift], [FFTFreq], [Magnitude], [Phase],
    [PowerSpectrum], [Periodogram] and the analysis windows [Hann],
    [Hamming], [Blackman], [Bartlett] and [Welch];
  - the discrete cosine and sine transforms [DCT], [IDCT], [DCT1], [DCT4],
    [DST] and [IDST];
  - fast linear, circular and cross correlation via [Convolve],
    [ConvolveFFT], [CircularConvolve], [Correlate] and [AutoCorrelate];
  - the numerical Laplace transform [Laplace] together with three classical
    inversion methods: the fixed Talbot method [InverseLaplaceTalbot], the
    Gaver-Stehfest method [InverseLaplaceStehfest] and the Fourier-series
    Euler method [InverseLaplaceEuler];
  - the [ZTransform], its numerical inverse [InverseZTransform], the
    chirp-z transform [ChirpZTransform] and [Bluestein]'s algorithm;
  - the [Hilbert] analytic signal together with the derived [Envelope],
    [InstantaneousPhase] and [InstantaneousFrequency], and the
    discrete-time Fourier transform samplers [DTFT] and [SampleDTFT].

Every routine is deterministic and depends only on the Go standard library.

types.name: FFTPlan
types.doc:
FFTPlan holds precomputed twiddle factors for repeated radix-2 transforms of
a fixed power-of-two length. Reusing a plan avoids recomputing the twiddle
table on every call and is convenient when transforming many blocks of the
same size. A plan is safe for concurrent use because Forward and Inverse do
not mutate it.

funcs.name: NewFFTPlan
funcs.doc:
NewFFTPlan creates a reusable transform plan for signals of length n, which
must be a power of two. NewFFTPlan panics for other lengths.

methods.name: Forward
methods.doc:
Forward computes the discrete Fourier transform of x using the plan. The
length of x must equal the plan length. The input is not modified.

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc:
Inverse computes the inverse discrete Fourier transform of X using the plan,
including the 1/N scaling. The length of X must equal the plan length. The
input is not modified.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the fixed transform length the plan was created for.

funcs.name: ApplyWindow
funcs.doc:
ApplyWindow returns the element-wise product of the signal x and the window
w. It panics if the lengths differ.

funcs.name: AutoCorrelate
funcs.doc:
AutoCorrelate returns the full autocorrelation of x, i.e. the
cross-correlation of x with itself. The result has length 2*len(x)-1 and is
symmetric about its center, whose value equals the signal energy.

funcs.name: Bartlett
funcs.doc:
Bartlett returns the n-point Bartlett (triangular) window, which tapers
linearly to zero at both ends.

funcs.name: Blackman
funcs.doc:
Blackman returns the n-point Blackman window,
w[i] = 0.42 - 0.5*cos(2*pi*i/(n-1)) + 0.08*cos(4*pi*i/(n-1)). It provides
strong side-lobe suppression at the cost of a wider main lobe.

funcs.name: Bluestein
funcs.doc:
Bluestein computes the discrete Fourier transform of x for an arbitrary
length by expressing it as a convolution (the chirp-z transform with a = 1,
w = exp(-2*pi*i/N) and m = N). It runs in O(n log n) time and returns the
same result as [DFT].

funcs.name: ChirpZTransform
funcs.signature: func ChirpZTransform(x []complex128, m int, w, a complex128) []complex128
funcs.doc:
ChirpZTransform computes the chirp-z transform of x, sampling the
Z-transform along a spiral contour. It returns m points

	X[k] = sum_{j=0}^{N-1} x[j] * a^{-j} * w^{j*k},   k = 0 .. m-1,

where a is the complex starting point and w is the ratio between successive
spiral points. Setting a = 1 and w = exp(-2*pi*i/N) with m = N reproduces
the DFT. The transform is evaluated with Bluestein's convolution method in
O((N+m) log(N+m)) time.

funcs.name: CircularConvolve
funcs.doc:
CircularConvolve returns the circular (cyclic) convolution of a and b. The
shorter input is zero-extended to the length N of the longer one and the
result has length N with

	c[k] = sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a[n] * b[(k-n) mod N].

funcs.name: Convolve
funcs.doc:
Convolve returns the full linear convolution of the real sequences a and b,
computed directly. The result has length len(a)+len(b)-1 with

	c[k] = sum_i a[i] * b[k-i].

For long inputs [ConvolveFFT] is asymptotically faster.

funcs.name: ConvolveComplex
funcs.doc:
ConvolveComplex returns the full linear convolution of the complex sequences
a and b, computed directly. The result has length len(a)+len(b)-1.

funcs.name: ConvolveFFT
funcs.doc:
ConvolveFFT returns the full linear convolution of the real sequences a and
b using the FFT (multiplication in the frequency domain). The result has
length len(a)+len(b)-1 and matches [Convolve] to within floating-point
rounding; it is much faster for large inputs.

funcs.name: Correlate
funcs.doc:
Correlate returns the full cross-correlation of a and b, defined as the
convolution of a with the time-reversed b. The result has length
len(a)+len(b)-1; the peak indicates the lag at which the two signals are
best aligned.

funcs.name: CrossCorrelateFFT
funcs.doc:
CrossCorrelateFFT returns the full cross-correlation of a and b computed via
the FFT. It matches [Correlate] to within floating-point rounding.

funcs.name: DCT
funcs.doc:
DCT computes the unnormalized type-II discrete cosine transform of x:

	X[k] = sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] * cos(pi/N * (n + 1/2) * k),   k = 0 .. N-1.

This is the DCT most commonly meant by "the DCT" and used in signal and
image compression. It is inverted by [IDCT].

funcs.name: DCT1
funcs.doc:
DCT1 computes the unnormalized type-I discrete cosine transform of x, which
requires at least two samples:

	X[k] = (x[0] + (-1)^k x[N-1])/2 + sum_{n=1}^{N-2} x[n] cos(pi*n*k/(N-1)).

The type-I transform is symmetric: applying DCT1 twice and scaling by
2/(N-1) recovers the input.

funcs.name: DCT4
funcs.doc:
DCT4 computes the unnormalized type-IV discrete cosine transform of x:

	X[k] = sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] cos(pi/N * (n+1/2) * (k+1/2)).

The type-IV transform is its own inverse up to the scale factor 2/N and is
the building block of the modified DCT used in audio coding.

funcs.name: DFT
funcs.doc:
DFT computes the discrete Fourier transform of x directly in O(n^2) time,
returning X[k] = sum_n x[n] exp(-2*pi*i*k*n/N). It works for any length and
serves as the reference implementation for the faster routines.

funcs.name: DFTMatrix
funcs.doc:
DFTMatrix returns the n-by-n discrete Fourier transform matrix W whose entry
W[j][k] equals exp(-2*pi*i*j*k/n). Multiplying this matrix by a column
vector reproduces [DFT].

funcs.name: DST
funcs.doc:
DST computes the unnormalized type-I discrete sine transform of x:

	X[k] = sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] sin(pi*(n+1)*(k+1)/(N+1)),   k = 0 .. N-1.

It is inverted by [IDST].

funcs.name: DTFT
funcs.doc:
DTFT evaluates the discrete-time Fourier transform of the real sequence x at
the angular frequency omega (radians/sample), returning
sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-i omega n}.

funcs.name: DTFTComplex
funcs.doc:
DTFTComplex evaluates the discrete-time Fourier transform of the complex
sequence x at the angular frequency omega (radians/sample).

funcs.name: DTFTSample
funcs.doc:
DTFTSample evaluates the discrete-time Fourier transform of the real
sequence x at each of the supplied angular frequencies, returning one
complex value per frequency.

funcs.name: Envelope
funcs.doc:
Envelope returns the amplitude envelope of the real sequence x, i.e. the
magnitude of its analytic signal (see [Hilbert]).

funcs.name: FFT
funcs.doc:
FFT computes the discrete Fourier transform of x using the radix-2
Cooley-Tukey algorithm. The length of x must be a power of two; FFT panics
otherwise. Use [FFTAny] to transform sequences of arbitrary length.

funcs.name: FFT2D
funcs.doc:
FFT2D computes the two-dimensional discrete Fourier transform of a matrix by
transforming every row and then every column. The matrix must be
rectangular; each dimension may have any length.

funcs.name: FFTAny
funcs.doc:
FFTAny computes the discrete Fourier transform of x for any length. When the
length is a power of two it uses the radix-2 [FFT]; otherwise it uses
[Bluestein]'s algorithm, which runs in O(n log n) time.

funcs.name: FFTFreq
funcs.doc:
FFTFreq returns the n sample frequencies corresponding to the bins of an
n-point DFT taken with sample spacing d (in seconds). The result follows the
standard convention: bins 0..(n-1)/2 hold the non-negative frequencies and
the remainder hold the negative frequencies.

funcs.name: FFTReal
funcs.doc:
FFTReal computes the discrete Fourier transform of a real-valued signal x,
returning the full complex spectrum of the same length. It accepts any
length.

funcs.name: FFTShift
funcs.doc:
FFTShift rearranges a spectrum so that the zero-frequency component is moved
to the center of the slice, matching the conventional plotting order. It is
inverted by [IFFTShift].

funcs.name: Goertzel
funcs.signature: func Goertzel(x []float64, k, n int) complex128
funcs.doc:
Goertzel computes a single DFT bin X[k] of the length-n signal x using the
Goertzel algorithm, which is more efficient than a full DFT when only a few
bins are required. The result equals DFT(x)[k].

funcs.name: GoertzelPower
funcs.signature: func GoertzelPower(x []float64, targetFreq, sampleRate float64) float64
funcs.doc:
GoertzelPower returns the power (squared magnitude) of the signal x at the
frequency targetFreq given the sampleRate, using the Goertzel recurrence.
Unlike [Goertzel] the target frequency need not fall on an exact DFT bin.

funcs.name: Hamming
funcs.doc:
Hamming returns the n-point Hamming window,
w[i] = 0.54 - 0.46*cos(2*pi*i/(n-1)). It minimizes the nearest side lobe at
the cost of slower far-off side-lobe roll-off compared with the Hann window.

funcs.name: Hann
funcs.doc:
Hann returns the n-point Hann (raised-cosine) window,
w[i] = 0.5*(1 - cos(2*pi*i/(n-1))). The Hann window offers a good balance
between main-lobe width and side-lobe suppression for spectral analysis.

funcs.name: Hilbert
funcs.doc:
Hilbert returns the analytic signal of the real sequence x, whose real part
is x and whose imaginary part is the Hilbert transform of x. It is computed
with the standard FFT method: the negative-frequency components are zeroed
and the positive-frequency components doubled before inverting. The result
has the same length as x.

funcs.name: HilbertTransform
funcs.doc:
HilbertTransform returns the Hilbert transform of the real sequence x, i.e.
the imaginary part of its analytic signal (see [Hilbert]). It applies a
90-degree phase shift to every frequency component.

funcs.name: IDCT
funcs.doc:
IDCT computes the inverse of the type-II transform produced by [DCT] (it is
the scaled type-III transform):

	x[n] = (2/N) * ( X[0]/2 + sum_{k=1}^{N-1} X[k] * cos(pi/N*(n+1/2)*k) ).

Applying IDCT to the output of [DCT] recovers the original signal.

funcs.name: IDFT
funcs.doc:
IDFT computes the inverse discrete Fourier transform of X directly,
returning x[n] = (1/N) sum_k X[k] exp(+2*pi*i*k*n/N). It works for any
length.

funcs.name: IDST
funcs.doc:
IDST computes the inverse of the type-I sine transform produced by [DST].
The type-I DST is orthogonal, so the inverse is the same transform scaled by
2/(N+1).

funcs.name: IFFT
funcs.doc:
IFFT computes the inverse radix-2 FFT of X, including the 1/N scaling. The
length of X must be a power of two; IFFT panics otherwise.

funcs.name: IFFT2D
funcs.doc:
IFFT2D computes the inverse two-dimensional discrete Fourier transform,
including the 1/(rows*cols) scaling. The matrix must be rectangular.

funcs.name: IFFTAny
funcs.doc:
IFFTAny computes the inverse discrete Fourier transform of X for any length,
including the 1/N scaling.

funcs.name: IFFTShift
funcs.doc:
IFFTShift is the inverse of [FFTShift]; it moves the zero-frequency
component from the center back to the start of the slice.

funcs.name: IRFFT
funcs.doc:
IRFFT reconstructs a real signal of length n from the floor(n/2)+1
half-spectrum X produced by [RFFT]. The original length n must be supplied
because it cannot be recovered from the half-spectrum alone.

funcs.name: InstantaneousFrequency
funcs.doc:
InstantaneousFrequency returns the instantaneous frequency, in the same
units as sampleRate (typically Hz), of the real sequence x. It is the time
derivative of the unwrapped [InstantaneousPhase] scaled by
sampleRate/(2*pi), computed with central differences.

funcs.name: InstantaneousPhase
funcs.doc:
InstantaneousPhase returns the unwrapped instantaneous phase, in radians, of
the real sequence x, obtained as the argument of its analytic signal (see
[Hilbert]).

funcs.name: InverseLaplaceEuler
funcs.signature: func InverseLaplaceEuler(F func(complex128) complex128, t float64, m int) float64
funcs.doc:
InverseLaplaceEuler approximates the inverse Laplace transform f(t) using
the Fourier-series method with Euler summation (Abate and Whitt). The
Bromwich integral is written as an alternating series which is accelerated
by averaging partial sums with binomial weights; m sets the number of
Euler-averaged terms (around 15 is a good default). The transform F may be
complex-valued and t must be positive.

funcs.name: InverseLaplaceStehfest
funcs.signature: func InverseLaplaceStehfest(F func(float64) float64, t float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc:
InverseLaplaceStehfest approximates the inverse Laplace transform f(t) using
the Gaver-Stehfest algorithm, which evaluates the real-argument transform F
at n points:

	f(t) ~= (ln 2 / t) * sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k * F(k ln 2 / t).

n must be even (odd values are rounded up). The method needs only
real-valued transform samples and works well for smooth, non-oscillatory
functions; typical choices are n = 10..14.

funcs.name: InverseLaplaceTalbot
funcs.signature: func InverseLaplaceTalbot(F func(complex128) complex128, t float64, m int) float64
funcs.doc:
InverseLaplaceTalbot approximates the inverse Laplace transform f(t) of the
transform F, supplied as a callable, using the fixed Talbot method of Abate
and Valko. The contour is deformed into the left half-plane so that the
integrand decays rapidly; m controls the number of terms (and thus the
accuracy), with values around 20-40 giving several correct digits for
smooth transforms. The argument t must be positive.

funcs.name: InverseZTransform
funcs.signature: func InverseZTransform(X func(complex128) complex128, n int, radius float64, m int) []complex128
funcs.doc:
InverseZTransform recovers the first n samples of a causal sequence from its
Z-transform X(z), which is supplied as a callable. The samples are obtained
by numerically evaluating the inversion contour integral on a circle of the
given radius using m equally spaced points:

	x[j] = (radius^j / m) * sum_{k=0}^{m-1} X(radius*e^{i*theta_k}) * e^{i*j*theta_k}

The radius must lie outside every pole of X(z); m controls accuracy and
should be noticeably larger than n.

funcs.name: IsPow2
funcs.doc: IsPow2 reports whether n is a positive power of two.

funcs.name: Laplace
funcs.signature: func Laplace(f func(float64) float64, s complex128, upper float64, n int) complex128
funcs.doc:
Laplace numerically approximates the Laplace transform

	F(s) = integral_0^upper f(t) e^{-s t} dt

of the real function f at the complex point s, using composite Simpson
quadrature with n subintervals over the truncated interval [0, upper]. For
convergent transforms upper should be large enough that f(t)e^{-s t} has
decayed to negligible size; n must be positive and is rounded up to the next
even number.

funcs.name: Magnitude
funcs.doc:
Magnitude returns the element-wise magnitudes (absolute values) of a complex
spectrum.

funcs.name: NextPow2
funcs.doc:
NextPow2 returns the smallest power of two that is greater than or equal to
n. For n <= 1 it returns 1.

funcs.name: Periodogram
funcs.doc:
Periodogram returns the periodogram power-spectral-density estimate of the
real signal x, defined as (1/N) |DFT(x)[k]|^2 for k = 0 .. N-1.

funcs.name: Phase
funcs.doc:
Phase returns the element-wise phase angles, in radians in the range
(-pi, pi], of a complex spectrum.

funcs.name: PhaseUnwrap
funcs.doc:
PhaseUnwrap returns a copy of the phase sequence with discontinuities larger
than pi removed by adding integer multiples of 2*pi, producing a continuous
phase curve.

funcs.name: PowerSpectrum
funcs.doc:
PowerSpectrum returns the element-wise squared magnitudes |X[k]|^2 of a
complex spectrum.

funcs.name: RFFT
funcs.doc:
RFFT computes the discrete Fourier transform of a real-valued signal x and
returns only the non-redundant first floor(N/2)+1 frequency bins. The
remaining bins are the complex conjugates of these by Hermitian symmetry.
Use [IRFFT] to invert the result.

funcs.name: RFFTFreq
funcs.doc:
RFFTFreq returns the floor(n/2)+1 non-negative sample frequencies
corresponding to the bins produced by [RFFT] for an n-point signal with
sample spacing d (in seconds).

funcs.name: SampleDTFT
funcs.doc:
SampleDTFT samples the discrete-time Fourier transform of the real sequence
x at m frequencies equally spaced over [0, 2*pi), returning

	X[k] = sum_{n} x[n] e^{-2*pi*i*k*n/m},   k = 0 .. m-1.

When m equals len(x) this coincides with the DFT; larger m interpolates the
spectrum and smaller m produces the aliased (folded) samples.

funcs.name: StehfestCoefficients
funcs.doc:
StehfestCoefficients returns the n Gaver-Stehfest weights V_1..V_n (indexed
from zero) used by [InverseLaplaceStehfest]. n must be a positive even
number; the function panics otherwise. The weights alternate in sign and
grow rapidly, which is why the method is best used in the range n = 8..16.

funcs.name: Welch
funcs.doc:
Welch returns the n-point Welch (parabolic) window,
w[i] = 1 - ((i-(n-1)/2)/((n-1)/2))^2.

funcs.name: ZTransform
funcs.doc:
ZTransform evaluates the one-sided Z-transform of the real sequence x at the
complex point z, returning sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] z^{-n}.

funcs.name: ZTransformComplex
funcs.doc:
ZTransformComplex evaluates the one-sided Z-transform of the complex
sequence x at the point z, returning sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] z^{-n}. The sum is
evaluated by Horner's method for numerical stability.

funcs.name: ZeroPad
funcs.doc:
ZeroPad returns a copy of x extended with zeros to length n. If n is smaller
than len(x) the input is truncated.

funcs.name: ZeroPadReal
funcs.doc:
ZeroPadReal returns a copy of the real slice x extended with zeros to length
n. If n is smaller than len(x) the input is truncated.

packages.name: tropical
packages.synopsis: Package tropical implements algebra over the tropical (min-plus and max-plus) semirings and the numerical objects built on top of them.
packages.doc:
Package tropical implements algebra over the tropical (min-plus and
max-plus) semirings and the numerical objects built on top of them.

A semiring replaces ordinary addition and multiplication with two new
operations. In the min-plus semiring the "sum" of two numbers is their
minimum and the "product" is their ordinary sum; its neutral element for
addition (the tropical zero) is +Inf and its neutral element for
multiplication (the tropical one) is 0. The max-plus semiring is the
order-dual: addition is the maximum, the tropical zero is -Inf and the
tropical one is still 0. Both are idempotent, commutative, complete
dioids and the two are exchanged by negating every entry.

Tropical linear algebra is unexpectedly useful. Tropical matrix
multiplication over min-plus is exactly the shortest-path relaxation, so a
tropical matrix power gives shortest walks of a bounded length and the
Kleene star gives all-pairs shortest paths. Over max-plus the same objects
describe the longest paths and the timing behaviour of discrete-event
systems: the unique eigenvalue of an irreducible matrix is the maximum
cycle mean, computed here with Karp's algorithm. A tropical polynomial is a
piecewise-linear convex (min-plus) or concave (max-plus) function whose
corners are its roots; their multiplicities are read off the Newton
polygon. The tropical determinant coincides with the tropical permanent and
solves the optimal assignment problem, and residuation gives the greatest
(max-plus) or least (min-plus) solution of a tropical linear system as well
as the least solution x = A*b of the affine fixed-point equation x = Ax + b.

This package depends only on the Go standard library. Scalars are ordinary
float64 values together with the appropriate infinity for the tropical zero.
Routines are deterministic: identical inputs always produce identical
outputs. Where a computation can diverge (a negative cycle for min-plus, a
positive cycle for max-plus) the corresponding function reports it instead
of returning a meaningless number.

vars.signature: var ErrDim = errors.New("tropical: incompatible dimensions")
vars.doc: ErrDim is returned when two matrices or vectors have incompatible shapes.

vars.signature: var ErrDivergent = errors.New("tropical: closure diverges (bad cycle)")
vars.doc:
ErrDivergent is returned when a tropical closure diverges because of a
negative cycle (min-plus) or a positive cycle (max-plus).

vars.signature: var ErrNotSquare = errors.New("tropical: matrix must be square")
vars.doc: ErrNotSquare is returned by operations that require a square matrix.

vars.signature: var ErrTooLarge = errors.New("tropical: matrix too large for brute-force enumeration")
vars.doc:
ErrTooLarge is returned by brute-force routines when the matrix is larger
than the supported bound.

types.name: Kind
types.signature: type Kind int
types.doc: Kind enumerates the two tropical semirings supported by this package.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns the human-readable name of the semiring kind.

types.name: Matrix
types.doc:
Matrix is a dense tropical matrix stored in row-major order together with the
semiring under which its entries are interpreted.

funcs.name: Constant
funcs.signature: func Constant(sr Semiring, r, c int, value float64) Matrix
funcs.doc: Constant returns an r-by-c matrix with every entry equal to value.

funcs.name: Diag
funcs.doc:
Diag returns a square matrix with the given values on the diagonal and the
tropical zero elsewhere.

funcs.name: Identity
funcs.signature: func Identity(sr Semiring, n int) Matrix
funcs.doc:
Identity returns the n-by-n tropical identity matrix: the tropical one (0) on
the diagonal and the tropical zero elsewhere.

funcs.name: MaxPlusIdentity
funcs.doc: MaxPlusIdentity returns the n-by-n max-plus identity matrix.

funcs.name: MaxPlusMatrix
funcs.doc: MaxPlusMatrix returns a max-plus Matrix holding a copy of data.

funcs.name: MaxPlusZeros
funcs.doc: MaxPlusZeros returns an r-by-c max-plus matrix of tropical zeros (-Inf).

funcs.name: MinPlusIdentity
funcs.doc: MinPlusIdentity returns the n-by-n min-plus identity matrix.

funcs.name: MinPlusMatrix
funcs.doc: MinPlusMatrix returns a min-plus Matrix holding a copy of data.

funcs.name: MinPlusZeros
funcs.doc: MinPlusZeros returns an r-by-c min-plus matrix of tropical zeros (+Inf).

funcs.name: NewMatrix
funcs.doc:
NewMatrix returns a Matrix over sr holding a deep copy of the rectangular
slice data. It panics if data is not rectangular.

funcs.name: Zeros
funcs.signature: func Zeros(sr Semiring, r, c int) Matrix
funcs.doc:
Zeros returns an r-by-c matrix whose entries are all the tropical zero of the
semiring. It panics if r or c is negative.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns the elementwise tropical sum of m and n. It returns an error if
the shapes or semirings differ.

methods.name: AssignmentValue
methods.doc:
AssignmentValue returns the tropical product of the entries m[i][perm[i]] for
a square matrix, the weight of the permutation as an assignment. It panics if
perm is not a length-n slice for an n-by-n matrix.

methods.name: At
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) At(i, j int) float64
methods.doc:
At returns the entry in row i and column j. It panics if the indices are out
of range.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent deep copy of the matrix.

methods.name: Cofactor
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) Cofactor(i, j int) (float64, error)
methods.doc:
Cofactor returns the tropical cofactor of entry (i,j): the tropical permanent
of the minor obtained by deleting row i and column j. It returns
ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: Col
methods.doc: Col returns a fresh copy of column j.

methods.name: ColVector
methods.doc: ColVector returns column j as a Vector.

methods.name: Cols
methods.doc: Cols returns the number of columns.

methods.name: CountFinite
methods.doc: CountFinite returns the number of finite entries.

methods.name: CriticalNodes
methods.doc:
CriticalNodes returns the indices of the nodes that lie on a cycle attaining
the tropical eigenvalue (the critical cycles). The boolean result is false
when the matrix has no cycle. It returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square
matrix.

methods.name: Determinant
methods.doc:
Determinant returns the tropical determinant of a square matrix. In tropical
algebra there are no additive inverses, so the determinant carries no signs
and coincides with the tropical permanent (the optimal assignment value). It
returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: Diagonal
methods.doc:
Diagonal returns a fresh copy of the main diagonal. For a non-square matrix
it returns the leading square diagonal of length min(rows, cols).

methods.name: DirectSum
methods.doc:
DirectSum returns the block-diagonal matrix with m in the top-left and n in
the bottom-right, the off-diagonal blocks filled with tropical zeros. It
returns an error if the semirings differ.

methods.name: Dual
methods.doc:
Dual returns the same matrix reinterpreted in the dual semiring, with every
entry negated. Negating the entries and switching min-plus with max-plus
turns any tropical problem into the equivalent dual problem.

methods.name: Eigenvalue
methods.doc:
Eigenvalue returns the tropical eigenvalue of an irreducible square matrix:
the maximum cycle mean for max-plus and the minimum cycle mean for min-plus.
The boolean result is false when the graph has no cycle. It returns
ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: Eigenvector
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) Eigenvector() (Vector, float64, bool, error)
methods.doc:
Eigenvector returns a tropical eigenvector v of an irreducible square matrix
together with its eigenvalue lambda, so that A (*) v = lambda (*) v. The
vector is obtained as a critical column of the normalised matrix closure. The
boolean result is false when the matrix has no cycle (and hence no finite
eigenvalue). It returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: ElementwiseMul
methods.doc:
ElementwiseMul returns the elementwise (Hadamard) tropical product of m and
n, whose entries are the ordinary sums of the corresponding entries. It
returns an error if the shapes or semirings differ.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether m and n have the same semiring, shape and identical
entries.

methods.name: EqualTol
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) EqualTol(n Matrix, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
EqualTol reports whether m and n have the same semiring and shape and every
pair of entries agrees to within tol (infinities must match exactly).

methods.name: GreatestSubSolution
methods.doc:
GreatestSubSolution returns the greatest x with A (*) x <= b componentwise.
This is the residuation-based principal solution and is defined for both
semirings (for min-plus the inequality is the natural one on the reversed
order). It returns an error if the dimensions or semirings do not match.

methods.name: HStack
methods.doc:
HStack returns the horizontal concatenation [m | n]. It returns an error if
the row counts or semirings differ.

methods.name: HasBadCycle
methods.doc:
HasBadCycle reports whether the matrix has a cycle that makes its tropical
closure diverge: a negative-weight cycle for min-plus or a positive-weight
cycle for max-plus. It returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: IsDiagonal
methods.doc: IsDiagonal reports whether every off-diagonal entry is the tropical zero.

methods.name: IsFinite
methods.doc:
IsFinite reports whether every entry is finite (no tropical zeros or other
infinities).

methods.name: IsIdentity
methods.doc:
IsIdentity reports whether the matrix is square and equals the tropical
identity to within tol.

methods.name: IsSquare
methods.doc: IsSquare reports whether the matrix has equally many rows and columns.

methods.name: IsSymmetric
methods.doc: IsSymmetric reports whether the matrix is square and equal to its transpose.

methods.name: IsTropicallySingular
methods.doc:
IsTropicallySingular reports whether a square matrix is tropically singular:
its optimal assignment value is the tropical zero (no finite matching) or is
attained by at least two distinct permutations, agreeing to within tol. It
enumerates permutations and returns ErrTooLarge above 9-by-9 and
ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether every entry is the tropical zero.

methods.name: KroneckerProduct
methods.doc:
KroneckerProduct returns the tropical Kronecker product of m and n, the
(m.rows*n.rows)-by-(m.cols*n.cols) matrix whose block (i,j) is m[i][j]
tropically scaling n. It returns an error if the semirings differ.

methods.name: LeftResidual
methods.doc:
LeftResidual returns the greatest matrix X with A (*) X <= B, written A \ B.
The (k,j) entry is the dual tropical sum over i of B[i][j] (/) A[i][k]. It
requires A and B to have the same number of rows and semiring and returns an
error otherwise.

methods.name: LongestPaths
methods.doc:
LongestPaths returns the all-pairs longest-path matrix of a max-plus weighted
adjacency matrix, treating the tropical zero (-Inf) as the absence of an
edge. It returns ErrDivergent when a positive cycle is present. It panics if
the matrix is not max-plus.

methods.name: Map
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) Map(f func(float64) float64) Matrix
methods.doc: Map returns a new matrix obtained by applying f to every entry.

methods.name: MaxCycleMean
methods.doc:
MaxCycleMean returns the maximum cycle mean of a square matrix interpreted as
a weighted digraph (entry (i,j) is the weight of the edge i->j; the tropical
zero means no edge). It is computed with Karp's algorithm and equals the
max-plus eigenvalue of an irreducible matrix. The boolean result is false
when the graph contains no cycle at all. It returns ErrNotSquare for a
non-square matrix.

methods.name: MaxEntry
methods.doc:
MaxEntry returns the largest entry in the usual numeric order, or -Inf for an
empty matrix.

methods.name: MinCycleMean
methods.doc:
MinCycleMean returns the minimum cycle mean of a square matrix interpreted as
a weighted digraph, computed with Karp's algorithm; it equals the min-plus
eigenvalue of an irreducible matrix. The boolean result is false when the
graph contains no cycle. It returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: MinEntry
methods.doc:
MinEntry returns the smallest entry in the usual numeric order, or +Inf for
an empty matrix.

methods.name: Minor
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) Minor(p, q int) Matrix
methods.doc:
Minor returns the matrix with row p and column q removed. It panics if the
indices are out of range.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the tropical matrix product m (*) n, where the (i,j) entry is the
tropical sum over k of m[i][k] (*) n[k][j]. Over min-plus this is the
shortest-path relaxation. It returns an error if the inner dimensions or the
semirings do not match.

methods.name: MulVec
methods.doc:
MulVec returns the tropical matrix-vector product m (*) v. It returns an
error if the dimensions or semirings do not match.

methods.name: OptimalAssignment
methods.doc:
OptimalAssignment returns the tropical permanent value and an optimal
permutation for a square matrix. For min-plus the value is the minimum total
weight of a perfect matching and for max-plus it is the maximum; the returned
slice maps each row to its assigned column. The value is the tropical zero
(and the permutation nil) when no finite assignment exists. It uses the
Hungarian algorithm and returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: PathWeight
methods.doc:
PathWeight returns the tropical product of the edge weights along the walk
visiting the given node sequence, that is the sum of m[nodes[k]][nodes[k+1]].
An empty or single-node walk has weight the tropical one (0). It panics if a
node index is out of range.

methods.name: Permanent
methods.doc:
Permanent returns the tropical permanent of a square matrix, the tropical sum
over all permutations sigma of the tropical product of the entries A[i,
sigma(i)]. This equals the optimal assignment value: the minimum-weight
perfect matching for min-plus and the maximum-weight one for max-plus. It
returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: PermanentBrute
methods.doc:
PermanentBrute returns the tropical permanent computed by explicitly
enumerating every permutation. It handles arbitrary entries, including the
tropical zero, and is intended for cross-checking and for matrices whose
forbidden pattern makes the assignment infeasible. It returns ErrTooLarge for
matrices larger than 10-by-10 and ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: PlusClosure
methods.doc:
PlusClosure returns the tropical Kleene plus A^+ = A (+) A^2 (+) A^3 (+) ...,
the tropical sum of all positive powers of a square matrix, computed with a
Floyd-Warshall style relaxation. Over min-plus the (i,j) entry is the weight
of the shortest walk of length at least one from i to j. It returns
ErrDivergent if a bad cycle makes the closure diverge and ErrNotSquare for a
non-square matrix.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns the tropical matrix power m^n by repeated squaring. The zeroth
power is the identity. It returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix and an
error for negative n.

methods.name: PrincipalSolution
methods.doc:
PrincipalSolution returns the principal solution of the tropical linear
system A (*) x = b obtained by residuation. For max-plus it is the greatest x
with A (*) x <= b (componentwise); for min-plus it is the least x with
A (*) x >= b. Its j-th entry is the dual tropical sum over i of the residual
b[i] (/) A[i][j]. It returns an error if the dimensions or semirings do not
match.

methods.name: Raw
methods.doc: Raw returns a fresh deep copy of the entries as a [][]float64.

methods.name: Reachability
methods.doc:
Reachability returns a boolean matrix whose (i,j) entry reports whether j is
reachable from i in at least one step, i.e. whether the plus-closure entry is
not the tropical zero. Bad cycles do not affect reachability, so no error is
returned for divergence.

methods.name: RightResidual
methods.doc:
RightResidual returns the greatest matrix X with X (*) A <= B, written B / A.
The (i,k) entry is the dual tropical sum over j of B[i][j] (/) A[k][j]. It
requires A and B to have the same number of columns and semiring and returns
an error otherwise.

methods.name: Row
methods.doc: Row returns a fresh copy of row i.

methods.name: RowVector
methods.doc: RowVector returns row i as a Vector.

methods.name: Rows
methods.doc: Rows returns the number of rows.

methods.name: ScalarAdd
methods.doc: ScalarAdd returns the matrix obtained by tropically adding c to every entry.

methods.name: ScalarMul
methods.doc:
ScalarMul returns the matrix obtained by tropically multiplying every entry
by c.

methods.name: Semiring
methods.doc: Semiring returns the semiring under which the matrix is interpreted.

methods.name: Set
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) Set(i, j int, value float64)
methods.doc:
Set stores value in row i and column j. It panics if the indices are out of
range.

methods.name: ShortestPaths
methods.doc:
ShortestPaths returns the all-pairs shortest-path matrix of a min-plus
weighted adjacency matrix, treating the tropical zero (+Inf) as the absence
of an edge. Diagonal entries are 0 unless a shorter cyclic walk exists. It
returns ErrDivergent when a negative cycle is present. It panics if the
matrix is not min-plus.

methods.name: SingleSourceShortestPaths
methods.doc:
SingleSourceShortestPaths returns the vector of best walk weights from src to
every node, computed with the Bellman-Ford relaxation: shortest paths for
min-plus and longest paths for max-plus. It returns ErrDivergent when a bad
cycle is reachable and ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: SolveAffine
methods.doc:
SolveAffine returns the least solution of the affine tropical fixed-point
equation x = A (*) x (+) b, which equals A* (*) b where A* is the Kleene
star. It returns ErrDivergent when the star diverges and an error if the
dimensions or semirings do not match.

methods.name: SolveExact
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) SolveExact(b Vector, tol float64) (Vector, bool, error)
methods.doc:
SolveExact returns the principal solution x of A (*) x = b together with a
boolean reporting whether it solves the system exactly to within tol. When
the boolean is true, x is the greatest exact solution for max-plus and the
least exact solution for min-plus. It returns an error if the dimensions or
semirings do not match.

methods.name: Star
methods.doc:
Star returns the tropical Kleene star A* = I (+) A (+) A^2 (+) ..., the
reflexive-transitive closure of a square matrix. Over min-plus it is the
all-pairs shortest-path matrix (empty walks allowed, so the diagonal is at
most 0). It returns ErrDivergent if a bad cycle makes the closure diverge and
ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: StarSeries
methods.doc:
StarSeries returns the bounded tropical closure I (+) A (+) A^2 (+) ... (+)
A^k, the tropical sum of the first k+1 powers of a square matrix. When the
full Kleene star converges this equals it for every k >= n-1. It returns
ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the matrix with one row per line and space-separated entries.

methods.name: SubMatrix
methods.signature: func (m Matrix) SubMatrix(r0, r1, c0, c1 int) Matrix
methods.doc:
SubMatrix returns the submatrix spanning rows [r0, r1) and columns [c0, c1).
It panics if the ranges are invalid.

methods.name: Trace
methods.doc:
Trace returns the tropical trace, the tropical sum of the diagonal entries.
It returns ErrNotSquare for a non-square matrix.

methods.name: Transpose
methods.doc: Transpose returns the transpose of the matrix.

methods.name: VStack
methods.doc:
VStack returns the vertical concatenation of m stacked above n. It returns an
error if the column counts or semirings differ.

methods.name: VecMul
methods.doc:
VecMul returns the tropical row-vector-matrix product v^T (*) m. It returns an
error if the dimensions or semirings do not match.

types.name: Poly
types.doc:
Poly is a univariate tropical polynomial. Coefficient i multiplies x raised
to the tropical power i, so evaluation is the tropical sum over i of
coeff[i] (*) x^i, that is min_i(coeff[i] + i*x) for min-plus and the
corresponding max for max-plus. Trailing tropical-zero coefficients are
trimmed on construction.

funcs.name: FromRoots
funcs.doc:
FromRoots returns the monic tropical polynomial over sr whose tropical roots
are exactly the given values, that is the tropical product of the linear
factors (x (+) r) over all r. The leading coefficient is the tropical one.

funcs.name: MaxPlusFromRoots
funcs.doc: MaxPlusFromRoots returns the monic max-plus polynomial with the given roots.

funcs.name: MaxPlusPoly
funcs.doc:
MaxPlusPoly returns a max-plus tropical polynomial with the given
coefficients.

funcs.name: MinPlusFromRoots
funcs.doc: MinPlusFromRoots returns the monic min-plus polynomial with the given roots.

funcs.name: MinPlusPoly
funcs.doc:
MinPlusPoly returns a min-plus tropical polynomial with the given
coefficients.

funcs.name: NewPoly
funcs.doc:
NewPoly returns the tropical polynomial over sr with the given coefficients,
coeffs[i] belonging to x^i. It stores a trimmed copy.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns the tropical sum of p and q, computed coefficientwise.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the polynomial.

methods.name: Coeff
methods.doc:
Coeff returns the coefficient of x^i, or the tropical zero when i is out of
range.

methods.name: Coeffs
methods.doc:
Coeffs returns a fresh copy of the coefficient slice, index i holding the
coefficient of x^i.

methods.name: ConstantCoeff
methods.doc: ConstantCoeff returns the coefficient of x^0.

methods.name: Degree
methods.doc:
Degree returns the degree of the polynomial: the highest power with a
non-zero coefficient. The tropical-zero polynomial has degree 0.

methods.name: Dual
methods.doc:
Dual returns the polynomial reinterpreted in the dual semiring with every
coefficient negated; its roots are the negatives of the original roots.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether p and q are over the same semiring and have identical
coefficients.

methods.name: Eval
methods.doc: Eval returns the value of the tropical polynomial at x.

methods.name: EvalAll
methods.doc: EvalAll evaluates the polynomial at every x in xs and returns the values.

methods.name: EvalArg
methods.doc:
EvalArg returns the value of the polynomial at x together with the index of a
term attaining it (the active monomial). The index is -1 for the tropical
zero polynomial.

methods.name: IsMonic
methods.doc: IsMonic reports whether the leading coefficient equals the tropical one (0).

methods.name: IsRoot
methods.signature: func (p Poly) IsRoot(x, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
IsRoot reports whether x is a tropical root of the polynomial, i.e. whether
the polynomial tropically vanishes at x to within tol.

methods.name: LeadingCoeff
methods.doc: LeadingCoeff returns the coefficient of the highest power of the polynomial.

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc:
Mul returns the tropical product of p and q, computed as a tropical
convolution of their coefficients.

methods.name: NewtonPolygon
methods.doc:
NewtonPolygon returns the vertices of the Newton polygon of the polynomial:
the lower convex hull of the finite coefficient points (i, coeff[i]) for
min-plus and the upper convex hull for max-plus, ordered by increasing index.
The slopes of its edges are the negatives of the tropical roots and the edge
widths are the root multiplicities.

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc:
Pow returns the tropical power p^n. The zeroth power is the tropical one
polynomial. It panics for negative n.

methods.name: RootValues
methods.doc:
RootValues returns the distinct tropical roots repeated according to their
multiplicity, ordered by increasing value.

methods.name: Roots
methods.doc:
Roots returns the tropical roots of the polynomial with their multiplicities,
read off the Newton polygon and ordered by increasing root value. A
polynomial of degree d whose constant and leading coefficients are both
finite has multiplicities summing to d. Constant and monomial polynomials
have no roots.

methods.name: ScalarMul
methods.doc:
ScalarMul returns the polynomial with every coefficient tropically multiplied
by c.

methods.name: Semiring
methods.doc: Semiring returns the semiring under which the polynomial is interpreted.

methods.name: ShiftUp
methods.doc:
ShiftUp returns the polynomial multiplied by x^k, prepending k tropical-zero
coefficients. It panics for negative k.

methods.name: String
methods.doc:
String renders the polynomial in the form "a0 (+) a1 x (+) a2 x^2 (+) ...",
omitting terms with a tropical-zero coefficient.

methods.name: TropicallyVanishes
methods.signature: func (p Poly) TropicallyVanishes(x, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
TropicallyVanishes reports whether the tropical polynomial vanishes at x,
i.e. whether the optimal monomial value is attained by at least two distinct
terms to within tol. These points are exactly the tropical roots.

types.name: PolygonVertex
types.doc:
PolygonVertex is a vertex of a Newton polygon: a coefficient index paired
with its (finite) coefficient value.

types.name: Root
types.doc:
Root is a tropical root together with its multiplicity (the horizontal width
of the Newton-polygon edge that produced it).

types.name: Semiring
types.doc:
Semiring is a value describing which tropical semiring a computation uses.
It carries no mutable state and is safe to copy and share.

funcs.name: MaxPlusSemiring
funcs.doc: MaxPlusSemiring returns the max-plus semiring.

funcs.name: MinPlusSemiring
funcs.doc: MinPlusSemiring returns the min-plus semiring.

funcs.name: NewSemiring
funcs.doc:
NewSemiring returns the Semiring for the given kind. It panics if kind is
neither MinPlus nor MaxPlus.

methods.name: Add
methods.signature: func (s Semiring) Add(a, b float64) float64
methods.doc:
Add returns the tropical sum a (+) b: the minimum for min-plus and the
maximum for max-plus.

methods.name: AtLeastAsGood
methods.signature: func (s Semiring) AtLeastAsGood(a, b float64) bool
methods.doc:
AtLeastAsGood reports whether a is at least as good as b in the sense of
tropical addition, i.e. whether a (+) b == a. For min-plus this is a <= b and
for max-plus it is a >= b.

methods.name: Better
methods.signature: func (s Semiring) Better(a, b float64) float64
methods.doc:
Better returns whichever of a and b tropical addition selects, identical to
Add but named for readability at call sites that compare candidates.

methods.name: Div
methods.signature: func (s Semiring) Div(a, b float64) float64
methods.doc:
Div returns the tropical quotient a (/) b, the residual of multiplication:
on finite operands it is the ordinary difference a-b. Dividing the tropical
zero by anything yields the tropical zero, and dividing a finite value by
the tropical zero yields the opposite infinity (the tropical "top").

methods.name: Dual
methods.doc:
Dual returns the order-dual semiring: MinPlus for MaxPlus and vice versa.
Negating every entry of a problem and switching to the dual semiring yields
an equivalent problem.

methods.name: EqualScalar
methods.signature: func (s Semiring) EqualScalar(a, b, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
EqualScalar reports whether a and b agree to within tol, with infinities
required to match exactly and in sign.

methods.name: FormatScalar
methods.doc:
FormatScalar renders a tropical scalar, printing the tropical zero as the
mathematical symbol for infinity with the appropriate sign.

methods.name: IsMaxPlus
methods.doc: IsMaxPlus reports whether s is the max-plus semiring.

methods.name: IsMinPlus
methods.doc: IsMinPlus reports whether s is the min-plus semiring.

methods.name: IsOne
methods.doc: IsOne reports whether a equals the tropical one (0).

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether a is the tropical zero of the semiring.

methods.name: Kind
methods.doc: Kind returns the kind of the semiring.

methods.name: Mul
methods.signature: func (s Semiring) Mul(a, b float64) float64
methods.doc:
Mul returns the tropical product a (*) b. On finite operands it is the
ordinary sum a+b; if either operand is the tropical zero the result is the
tropical zero (which absorbs under multiplication).

methods.name: One
methods.doc:
One returns the tropical one (multiplicative identity) of the semiring,
which is 0 for both min-plus and max-plus.

methods.name: Pow
methods.signature: func (s Semiring) Pow(a float64, n int) float64
methods.doc:
Pow returns the tropical power a raised to the integer exponent n, which
equals n*a for finite a. The zeroth power is the tropical one (0). A
positive power of the tropical zero is the tropical zero. Negative exponents
are permitted only for finite a (the semiring is a semifield on finite
values); a negative power of the tropical zero returns the tropical zero.

methods.name: Prod
methods.doc:
Prod returns the tropical product of all arguments, or the tropical one when
no arguments are given.

methods.name: Star
methods.doc:
Star returns the tropical Kleene star a* = 1 (+) a (+) a^2 (+) ... For
min-plus this is 0 when a >= 0 and -Inf (divergent) when a < 0; for max-plus
it is 0 when a <= 0 and +Inf (divergent) when a > 0.

methods.name: StarConverges
methods.doc:
StarConverges reports whether the scalar Kleene star a* is finite: a >= 0 for
min-plus and a <= 0 for max-plus.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns the human-readable name of the semiring.

methods.name: Sum
methods.doc:
Sum returns the tropical sum of all arguments, or the tropical zero when no
arguments are given.

methods.name: Zero
methods.doc:
Zero returns the tropical zero (additive identity) of the semiring: +Inf for
min-plus and -Inf for max-plus.

types.name: Vector
types.doc:
Vector is a dense tropical vector: a slice of scalar entries together with
the semiring under which it is interpreted.

funcs.name: MaxPlusVector
funcs.doc: MaxPlusVector returns a max-plus Vector holding a copy of data.

funcs.name: MinPlusVector
funcs.doc: MinPlusVector returns a min-plus Vector holding a copy of data.

funcs.name: NewVector
funcs.doc: NewVector returns a Vector over the given semiring holding a copy of data.

funcs.name: OneVector
funcs.signature: func OneVector(sr Semiring, n int) Vector
funcs.doc:
OneVector returns a length-n Vector whose entries are all the tropical one
(0) of the semiring.

funcs.name: UnitVector
funcs.signature: func UnitVector(sr Semiring, n, i int) Vector
funcs.doc:
UnitVector returns the length-n tropical unit vector: the tropical one (0) at
index i and the tropical zero elsewhere. It panics if i is out of range.

funcs.name: ZeroVector
funcs.signature: func ZeroVector(sr Semiring, n int) Vector
funcs.doc:
ZeroVector returns a length-n Vector whose entries are all the tropical zero
of the semiring.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc:
Add returns the elementwise tropical sum of v and w. It panics if the lengths
or semirings differ.

methods.name: ArgBest
methods.doc:
ArgBest returns the index of the entry selected by tropical addition (the
index of the minimum for min-plus, of the maximum for max-plus) and its
value. For an empty vector it returns (-1, tropical zero).

methods.name: Argmax
methods.doc:
Argmax returns the index of the numerically largest entry, or -1 for an empty
vector.

methods.name: Argmin
methods.doc:
Argmin returns the index of the numerically smallest entry, or -1 for an
empty vector.

methods.name: At
methods.doc: At returns the i-th entry. It panics if i is out of range.

methods.name: Clone
methods.doc: Clone returns an independent copy of the vector.

methods.name: Dot
methods.doc:
Dot returns the tropical inner product of v and w: the tropical sum over i of
v[i] (*) w[i]. It panics if the lengths or semirings differ.

methods.name: Dual
methods.doc:
Dual returns the same vector reinterpreted in the dual semiring, with every
entry negated.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc:
Equal reports whether v and w have the same semiring, length and identical
entries.

methods.name: EqualTol
methods.signature: func (v Vector) EqualTol(w Vector, tol float64) bool
methods.doc:
EqualTol reports whether v and w have the same semiring and length and every
pair of entries agrees to within tol (with infinities required to match
exactly and in sign).

methods.name: IsZero
methods.doc: IsZero reports whether every entry equals the tropical zero.

methods.name: Len
methods.doc: Len returns the number of entries in the vector.

methods.name: Map
methods.signature: func (v Vector) Map(f func(float64) float64) Vector
methods.doc: Map returns a new vector obtained by applying f to every entry.

methods.name: Max
methods.doc:
Max returns the largest entry in the usual numeric order. For an empty vector
it returns -Inf.

methods.name: Min
methods.doc:
Min returns the smallest entry, ignoring sign of infinities in the usual
numeric order. For an empty vector it returns +Inf.

methods.name: Normalize
methods.doc:
Normalize returns the vector with its tropical-product "length" shifted so
that the best entry becomes the tropical one (0): every entry has the best
entry subtracted. For an all-zero (tropical) vector it returns a copy.

methods.name: Prod
methods.doc: Prod returns the tropical product of all entries (their ordinary sum).

methods.name: ScalarAdd
methods.doc: ScalarAdd returns the vector obtained by tropically adding c to every entry.

methods.name: ScalarMul
methods.doc:
ScalarMul returns the vector obtained by tropically multiplying every entry
by c.

methods.name: Semiring
methods.doc: Semiring returns the semiring under which the vector is interpreted.

methods.name: Set
methods.signature: func (v Vector) Set(i int, value float64)
methods.doc: Set stores value at index i. It panics if i is out of range.

methods.name: Slice
methods.doc: Slice returns a fresh copy of the underlying entries.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the vector as a bracketed, space-separated list.

methods.name: Sum
methods.doc:
Sum returns the tropical sum of all entries (the minimum for min-plus, the
maximum for max-plus).

funcs.name: IsInf
funcs.doc:
IsInf reports whether x is either positive or negative infinity, i.e. a
tropical zero for one of the two semirings.

funcs.name: MaxPlusAdd
funcs.doc: MaxPlusAdd returns the max-plus tropical sum of a and b, that is max(a, b).

funcs.name: MaxPlusDiv
funcs.doc: MaxPlusDiv returns the max-plus tropical quotient a/b (a-b on finite values).

funcs.name: MaxPlusMul
funcs.doc:
MaxPlusMul returns the max-plus tropical product of a and b (a+b, with -Inf
absorbing).

funcs.name: MaxPlusOne
funcs.doc: MaxPlusOne returns the tropical one of the max-plus semiring, 0.

funcs.name: MaxPlusPow
funcs.signature: func MaxPlusPow(a float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc: MaxPlusPow returns the max-plus tropical power a^n (n*a on finite values).

funcs.name: MaxPlusProd
funcs.doc:
MaxPlusProd returns the max-plus tropical product (ordinary sum) of the
arguments, or 0 when none are given.

funcs.name: MaxPlusStar
funcs.doc:
MaxPlusStar returns the max-plus scalar Kleene star: 0 for a <= 0 and +Inf
otherwise.

funcs.name: MaxPlusSum
funcs.doc:
MaxPlusSum returns the max-plus tropical sum (maximum) of the arguments, or
-Inf when none are given.

funcs.name: MaxPlusZero
funcs.doc: MaxPlusZero returns the tropical zero of the max-plus semiring, -Inf.

funcs.name: MinPlusAdd
funcs.doc: MinPlusAdd returns the min-plus tropical sum of a and b, that is min(a, b).

funcs.name: MinPlusDiv
funcs.doc: MinPlusDiv returns the min-plus tropical quotient a/b (a-b on finite values).

funcs.name: MinPlusMul
funcs.doc:
MinPlusMul returns the min-plus tropical product of a and b (a+b, with +Inf
absorbing).

funcs.name: MinPlusOne
funcs.doc: MinPlusOne returns the tropical one of the min-plus semiring, 0.

funcs.name: MinPlusPow
funcs.signature: func MinPlusPow(a float64, n int) float64
funcs.doc: MinPlusPow returns the min-plus tropical power a^n (n*a on finite values).

funcs.name: MinPlusProd
funcs.doc:
MinPlusProd returns the min-plus tropical product (ordinary sum) of the
arguments, or 0 when none are given.

funcs.name: MinPlusStar
funcs.doc:
MinPlusStar returns the min-plus scalar Kleene star: 0 for a >= 0 and -Inf
otherwise.

funcs.name: MinPlusSum
funcs.doc:
MinPlusSum returns the min-plus tropical sum (minimum) of the arguments, or
+Inf when none are given.

funcs.name: MinPlusZero
funcs.doc: MinPlusZero returns the tropical zero of the min-plus semiring, +Inf.

examples.name: Matrix (ShortestPaths)
examples.code:
inf := MinPlusZero()

A := MinPlusMatrix([][]float64{
	{0, 3, inf},
	{inf, 0, 2},
	{1, inf, 0},
})
sp, _ := A.ShortestPaths()

fmt.Println(sp.At(0, 2))

examples.name: Poly (Roots)

packages.name: voronoi
packages.synopsis: Package voronoi implements planar computational geometry centred on the Delaunay triangulation and its dual, the Voronoi diagram.
packages.doc:
Package voronoi implements planar computational geometry centred on the
Delaunay triangulation and its dual, the Voronoi diagram.

The package is self-contained and depends only on the Go standard library.
All coordinates are float64 and every routine is deterministic: identical
inputs yield identical outputs.

The core algorithms are:

  - The incremental Bowyer-Watson algorithm for the Delaunay triangulation
    of a planar point set ([Triangulate]).
  - Construction of the dual Voronoi diagram from that triangulation
    ([Voronoi]), including bounded and unbounded cells.
  - Andrew's monotone-chain convex hull ([ConvexHull]).
  - Alpha shapes and alpha complexes derived from the Delaunay triangulation
    ([AlphaShape]).
  - Nearest-neighbour, k-nearest, closest-pair, largest-empty-circle and
    minimum-enclosing-circle queries.
  - Point location within a triangulation ([Triangulation.Locate]).

Supporting value types include [Point] (a location in the plane), [Circle],
[Rect], [Edge] and [Triangle]. Numerically delicate predicates
(orientation, in-circle) are computed as signed determinants so callers can
interpret the sign directly, and approximate comparisons accept an explicit
epsilon.

consts.signature: const Eps = 1e-9
consts.doc:
Eps is the default absolute tolerance used by the approximate predicates and
comparisons in this package. It is chosen to sit comfortably above
double-precision rounding noise for coordinates of moderate magnitude.

vars.signature: var ErrCollinear = errors.New("voronoi: points are collinear")
vars.doc:
ErrCollinear is returned by circumcircle constructions when the three input
points are collinear and no unique circle exists.

vars.signature: var ErrDegenerate = errors.New("voronoi: all points are collinear")
vars.doc:
ErrDegenerate is returned when all input points are collinear, so no triangle
can be formed.

vars.signature: var ErrEmpty = errors.New("voronoi: empty point set")
vars.doc: ErrEmpty is returned by aggregate constructions when given no points.

vars.signature: var ErrTooFewPoints = errors.New("voronoi: need at least 3 points")
vars.doc:
ErrTooFewPoints is returned when a triangulation is requested for fewer than
three points.

types.name: AlphaShape
types.doc:
AlphaShape is the boundary of an alpha complex: the family of Delaunay
triangles whose circumradius does not exceed the parameter Alpha, together
with the edges bounding their union. As Alpha grows the shape ranges from the
point set (small Alpha) up to the full convex hull (large Alpha).

funcs.name: AlphaShapeFromTriangulation
funcs.doc:
AlphaShapeFromTriangulation computes the alpha shape from an existing
triangulation without retriangulating.

funcs.name: AlphaShapeOf
funcs.doc:
AlphaShapeOf computes the alpha shape of the Delaunay triangulation for the
given alpha radius. A triangle is included when its circumradius is at most
alpha; boundary edges are the edges incident to exactly one included
triangle. It returns the same errors as Triangulate.

methods.name: Area
methods.doc:
Area returns the total area covered by the alpha complex (the sum of its
triangle areas).

methods.name: BoundaryPoints
methods.doc:
BoundaryPoints returns the indices of points that lie on the alpha-shape
boundary, sorted.

methods.name: Edges
methods.doc: Edges returns the boundary edges of the alpha shape.

methods.name: NumEdges
methods.doc: NumEdges returns the number of boundary edges of the alpha shape.

methods.name: NumTriangles
methods.doc: NumTriangles returns the number of triangles in the alpha complex.

methods.name: Perimeter
methods.doc: Perimeter returns the total length of the alpha shape's boundary edges.

methods.name: Triangles
methods.doc:
Triangles returns the indices of the Delaunay triangles that make up the
alpha complex (those with circumradius <= Alpha).

types.name: Circle
types.doc: Circle is a circle defined by its centre and radius.

funcs.name: CircleFromDiameter
funcs.doc: CircleFromDiameter returns the circle whose diameter is the segment ab.

funcs.name: CircleThrough
funcs.signature: func CircleThrough(a, b, c Point) (Circle, error)
funcs.doc:
CircleThrough returns the unique circle passing through the three points.
It returns ErrCollinear if the points are collinear.

funcs.name: Circumcircle
funcs.signature: func Circumcircle(a, b, c Point) (Circle, error)
funcs.doc:
Circumcircle returns the circumscribed circle of the triangle (a, b, c).
It returns ErrCollinear if the points are collinear.

funcs.name: EmptyCircleAt
funcs.doc:
EmptyCircleAt returns the largest circle centred at c that contains no site
in its interior; its radius is the distance from c to the nearest site.

funcs.name: LargestEmptyCircle
funcs.doc:
LargestEmptyCircle returns the largest circle whose centre lies inside the
convex hull of the sites and whose interior contains no site. The centre is
the Delaunay circumcentre (Voronoi vertex) that maximises the distance to its
nearest site, restricted to circumcentres lying within the hull. It returns
ErrTooFewPoints or ErrDegenerate as Triangulate would.

funcs.name: MinimumEnclosingCircle
funcs.doc:
MinimumEnclosingCircle returns the smallest circle that contains all of the
given points. It uses Welzl's randomized-free incremental algorithm with a
deterministic point order, running in expected linear time. It returns a
zero-radius circle for a single point and the empty Circle for no points.

funcs.name: NewCircle
funcs.signature: func NewCircle(center Point, radius float64) Circle
funcs.doc: NewCircle returns the circle with the given centre and radius.

methods.name: Area
methods.doc: Area returns the area enclosed by the circle.

methods.name: BoundingBox
methods.doc: BoundingBox returns the axis-aligned bounding box of the circle.

methods.name: Circumference
methods.doc: Circumference returns the circle's circumference.

methods.name: Contains
methods.signature: func (c Circle) Contains(p Point, eps float64) bool
methods.doc:
Contains reports whether p lies inside or on the circle, within tolerance
eps applied to the radius.

methods.name: ContainsStrict
methods.signature: func (c Circle) ContainsStrict(p Point, eps float64) bool
methods.doc:
ContainsStrict reports whether p lies strictly inside the circle, within
tolerance eps.

methods.name: Diameter
methods.doc: Diameter returns the circle's diameter.

methods.name: Intersects
methods.doc: Intersects reports whether two circles overlap or touch.

methods.name: OnBoundary
methods.signature: func (c Circle) OnBoundary(p Point, eps float64) bool
methods.doc:
OnBoundary reports whether p lies on the circle boundary within tolerance
eps.

methods.name: PointAt
methods.doc:
PointAt returns the point on the circle at angle theta (radians) measured
counterclockwise from the positive X axis.

methods.name: Sample
methods.doc:
Sample returns n points spaced evenly around the circle, starting at angle 0.
It returns nil for n <= 0.

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String returns a human-readable representation of the circle.

types.name: Edge
types.doc:
Edge is an undirected edge between two vertices identified by their indices
into a point slice.

funcs.name: AlphaShapeEdges
funcs.doc:
AlphaShapeEdges is a convenience wrapper returning just the boundary edges of
the alpha shape of pts for the given alpha.

funcs.name: EuclideanMST
funcs.doc:
